First, memory management: at model load time, LlamaWeb computes all required buffer sizes (weight storage, KV-cache, FlashDecoding intermediates, top-k scratch space) and issues a fixed set of WebGPU buffer allocations. A single rotating-slot parameter buffer holds the small per-kernel metadata (matrix dimensions, strides) across a configurable number of in-flight kernels, replacing per-kernel dynamic allocations used by WebLLM and ONNX Runtime. Model weights stream from the browser's Origin Private File System (OPFS) through 4×1 MB transfer buffers directly into GPU buffers, never materializing in the WASM heap (which is grow-only and cannot be freed within a tab's lifetime). Second, the kernel library: a two-layer design separates the runtime dispatcher (tensor shapes, device limits, command buffer scheduling) from the kernel library (kernel variant selection, preprocessing, pipeline caching). A minimal C++ WGSL preprocessor called pre-wgsl generates shader code conditioned on data formats, device-available subgroup features, buffer aliasing constraints, and tiling parameters. Compiled GPU pipelines are cached by a specialization key. For matrix-matrix multiplication (prefill-dominant), the library provides reg_tile (hierarchical shared-memory + register tiling with f32 accumulation) and sg_mat (subgroup matrix instructions, natively only). For matrix-vector multiplication (decode-dominant, ~90% of decode time), a register dequantization strategy is used instead of shared-memory tiling, which the authors found impractical across diverse quantization layouts. Performance-portable tile defaults for matmul were selected by exhaustive sweep of thousands of configurations across 4 GPU vendors over LLM-representative matrix shapes, yielding 41% kernel-level speedup over hand-picked parameters. Third, quantization-aware kernels: all formats are represented as flat u32 buffers; dequantization routines for all three llama.cpp families (legacy 32-weight blocks, K-quants with 256-weight nested scaling, I-quants with codebook-indexed blocks) plus q1_0 and mxfp4 emulation are integrated directly into matmul and matvec kernels and reused for KV-cache dequantization in FlashAttention. Correctness is validated by NMSE < 1e-7 vs. CPU reference (relaxed to 1e-6 for f16 operations on some devices due to rounding differences). Critical design decisions: (1) Static allocation vs. dynamic pool — well-justified by WASM grow-only heap and browser tab memory caps; (2) Register dequantization for matvec — empirically validated that shared-memory tiling (CLBlast strategy) doesn't generalize across llama.cpp's diverse quantization layouts; (3) Flat u32 representation for all quantization formats — motivated by WebGPU struct alignment constraints, acknowledged as leaving performance on the table for formats that could use u16. Key Results Memory (f16 Llama3.2 1B, 4 device×browser configs): LlamaWeb uses 29–33% less peak memory (geometric mean) than WebLLM and Transformers.js. On Safari/Apple M4 Pro, Transformers.js memory climbs to 10 GB until tab kill (memory leak). On Chrome/Windows RTX 5080, WebLLM similarly leaks to ~10 GB. LlamaWeb shows stable, flat memory profiles across all tested configurations. Decode throughput vs. browser frameworks (f16 Llama3.2 1B, geometric mean across 4 GPUs — RTX 5080, RX 7900 XT, Arc B580, M4 Pro): LlamaWeb achieves 54% higher throughput than WebLLM and 69% higher than Transformers.js. Prefill throughput is 51% lower than WebLLM and 21% lower than Transformers.js, attributed to WebLLM's TVM kernel fusion and Transformers.js's subgroup-optimized matmul versus LlamaWeb's portable register-tiling fallback in browser. Performance vs. native llama.cpp backends (same 4 GPUs): CUDA outperforms WebGPU by up to 10× prefill, 2.5× decode; Metal outperforms by 2× prefill, 50% decode. However, LlamaWeb with safety checks disabled beats native SYCL on Intel Arc B580 by 23% prefill and 10% decode (f16 model), and beats native Vulkan on AMD RX 7900 XT by 38% decode (q4_k_m). WebGPU safety checks add 14–23% prefill overhead and 1–5% decode overhead (up to 42% prefill slowdown on RTX 5080). Cross-device portability (10 models, 16 GPUs from 8 vendors, 481 benchmark runs): High-end discrete GPUs reach >3k tok/s prefill and 100+ tok/s decode for small models; low-power mobile GPUs (Mali, PowerVR, Adreno, iPhone) achieve 4–17 tok/s decode on the four smallest models that fit within memory. This is the largest cross-device browser inference dataset published in a single framework. Quantization (llama, 4 formats, high/mid clusters): f16→q8_0 decode speedup: 20% on high cluster, 53% on mid cluster; q8_0→q4_k_m: 8% gain on mid; q4_k_m→q2_k: 17% throughput decrease on mid, meaning aggressive quantization reduces decode throughput rather than increasing it in WebGPU (software dequantization overhead dominates). Quantization in this setting is primarily a memory-reduction tool, not a performance optimizer. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The system delivers a complete, merged, production-quality WebGPU backend for llama.cpp (8,470 lines C++, 44 kernel templates, 72 ML operators, 23 weight formats), genuinely novel in scope and deployment status relative to prior browser inference frameworks. The empirical contribution — 481 runs across 16 devices from 8 vendors including mobile WebGPU, the first such cross-device dataset — is a genuine community resource. The underlying techniques (FlashAttention, static allocation, tiled matmul, quantization-aware kernels) are known; the contribution is their integration and adaptation to WebGPU's specific constraints (no buffer aliasing, no u16 type, mandatory bounds checks, deferred execution model, WASM heap behavior), plus the empirical demonstration of which choices matter and by how much. Rigor & Reproducibility Key ablations are present: empirical tuning vs. hand-picked parameters (41% kernel speedup); with vs. without safety checks (14–42% overhead, per-device); native vs. browser WebGPU prefill (demonstrates prefill gap is a browser API limitation, not a kernel quality issue). Each experiment runs 5 times with averaged results; no standard deviations or confidence intervals are reported, which is a gap for results with potentially high per-run variance on mobile hardware. No statistical significance tests. Code is publicly released and merged into llama.cpp mainline through 100+ PRs; pre-wgsl is separately released. The framework comparison lacks fine-grained benchmarking control over WebLLM and Transformers.js (authors use representative prompts from those frameworks' own demo pages), which is acknowledged and mitigated by using the same prompts consistently. Firefox excluded due to ~52× performance gap vs. Chrome on M3 — a sound methodological choice but limits generalizability. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.20706v1); not yet peer-reviewed. Deployability (SYSTEMS) - Practical: Runs in standard Chrome and Safari browsers on any WebGPU-capable device; the evaluation covers commercially available hardware from 8 GPU vendors including phones and tablets, with no specialized hardware requirements. - Efficient: 29–33% less peak memory than alternatives; decode at 65–109 tok/s for models up to 3B parameters on high-end GPUs; 4–17 tok/s on low-power mobile. Prefill in-browser is 51% slower than the best alternative (WebLLM), a non-trivial regression for prompt-heavy applications. - Effective: Solves all three stated problems: memory crashes are eliminated (stable profiles observed across all tested configurations), decode throughput leads browser-based alternatives by 54–69%, and 23 weight formats are supported vs. 6–7 in competing frameworks. The prefill regression is real and unresolved. - Scalable: Only models up to ~3B parameters (quantized) fit within browser memory caps on most devices; iOS is limited to <500 MB tab budget. No batching, no multi-GPU, no context lengths beyond 2048 KV-cache depth tested. Larger model support depends on quantization reducing memory below browser caps; the paper does not test or claim scalability beyond the tested range. Delivers a software artifact: fully merged into llama.cpp mainline, open-source, with a separately released pre-wgsl preprocessor library. Strengths - Production deployment rather than a prototype: the backend is already in llama.cpp mainline with 100+ merged PRs and external contributors actively building on it, which is a rare and credibility-elevating property for a systems paper. - Empirical kernel tuning methodology: the exhaustive sweep yielding 41% speedup over hand-tuned parameters, combined with data-driven selection of performance-portable defaults, provides a replicable methodology for WebGPU performance portability that could be extended to more devices. - Breadth of hardware evaluation (16 devices, 8 vendors, including mobile WebGPU) is the most comprehensive cross-platform browser inference evaluation in the literature for this workload class and constitutes a valuable standalone dataset. - Honest, quantified disclosure of the prefill weakness with mechanistic attribution (browser's absence of subgroup matrix feature) and mitigation path (native LlamaWeb already competitive; feature pending GA rollout). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) prefill lags WebLLM by ~51% in-browser due to the subgroup matrix feature not being in stable browser releases; (2) single-token GPU sampling currently provides no benefit, leaving sampling CPU-bound; (3) f16 accumulation causes incoherent output on Apple M-series for certain models (Qwen2.5), requiring fallback to f32; (4) Firefox excluded due to ~52× performance gap vs. Chrome; (5) iOS devices limited to models fitting in <500 MB; (6) empirical tuning only covers 4 GPUs; (7) hardware-native formats (nvfp4, bf16) not supported for inference. The authors confront limitations directly and with quantitative specificity, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The framework performance comparison uses exclusively f16 Llama3.2 1B; it is unknown whether the 54–69% decode advantage holds for quantized models (where WebLLM and Transformers.js also run quantized paths) or for different architectures. A table across 3–4 model×format combinations would substantially strengthen the claim. - No latency metrics (time-to-first-token, p50/p99 per-token latency) are reported — only aggregate throughput — despite latency being the primary user-facing metric for interactive browser applications. - The comparison omits wllama in CPU-only mode, which would establish the GPU acceleration baseline and quantify the actual benefit of WebGPU vs. WASM-CPU for browser inference. - Memory savings are demonstrated only for f16 (the most memory-intensive format); memory comparisons under quantized weights would better characterize the practical advantage for models that actually run on constrained devices. - The k-means inertia curve shows no elbow (358→272→223→173), so the three-cluster structure is analytically chosen, not data-driven; performance comparisons across clusters should be interpreted cautiously. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying LLMs in the browser gains immediate, deployable value: the code is in llama.cpp mainline, the memory improvements are real and prevent crashes, the decode speedup is substantial, and the honest disclosure of the prefill regression — with a clear path to closing it once subgroup matrix instructions reach stable browsers — makes the paper's claims trustworthy.
Search Results 2605.20706 PDF
#12 — Highly Innovative (75)

S $^ 3$ GNN: Efficient Global Mixing and Local Message Passing for Long-Range Graph Learning

Dai Shi, Luke Thompson, Linhan Luo, Lequan Lin, Andi Han, Junbin Gao, José Miguel Hernández Lobato · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces S³GNN, a graph neural network that mitigates oversquashing by combining a low-rank global mixing term (projection onto the 0-eigenspace of the graph Laplacian) with standard sparse message passing and antisymmetric feature transforms, achieving GCN-comparable per-layer complexity without eigendecomposition. - Why it's important: Long-range dependencies are a persistent failure mode of message-passing GNNs, and existing spectral approaches that provide theoretical guarantees either require O(N³) eigendecomposition or rely on conditions that empirical experiments in this paper show are rarely satisfied in practice. - How it does it: The global mixing operator collapses to a per-connected-component mean aggregation scaled by a learned scalar α, implementable without computing eigenvectors, and the antisymmetric weight constraint enforces O(ε²) Jacobian stability per layer. - The good: On the synthetic Eccentricity task S³GNN achieves log₁₀(MSE) = −2.4801 ± 0.0239, an order-of-magnitude improvement over S²GNN (−1.4622) and Stable-ChebNet (−2.1043), using 50% fewer parameters and 4 layers versus order-10 polynomials. - The missing: The global mixing coefficient α is a single scalar per connected component per layer — no attention to which nodes within a component are informationally relevant — and scalability beyond ogbn-proteins (132,534 nodes) is claimed but not measured. - Read it or not: Worth reading for GNN researchers working on long-range tasks or oversquashing theory — the theory-practice gap diagnosis is a concrete, honest contribution and the architecture is simple enough to serve as a drop-in replacement backbone.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (78) is the main driver: fully-proved propositions, multiple-seed results with standard deviations, a direct Jacobian-distribution analysis substantiating the theory-practice gap, and public code — tempered by the unenforced alpha-positivity assumption that Prop. 1 depends on and the missing ablation of the global-mixing term itself. Non-Obviousness (78) is high: collapsing 0-eigenspace projection to per-component mean pooling (no eigendecomposition) is a non-obvious algebraic insight. Novelty (72) is NEW METHOD — a clever simplification/recombination of spectral GNN ideas, not a new formulation. Completeness (72) and Resilience (72) are solid: breadth across 6 task domains and an honest critique of prior work, but pulled down by the missing component-isolation ablation, untested scalability beyond 132K nodes, and some absent transformer baselines. Impact (75) reflects a simple drop-in spectral-spatial backbone with released research code. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*75 = 75.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 72 Resilience 72 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Message-passing GNNs suffer from oversquashing: Jacobian sensitivity ||∂hᵢ(ℓ)/∂xₛ|| decays exponentially with graph distance, making tasks that require integrating information from distant nodes (graph property prediction, KGQA, physical simulation on meshes) unreliable. Rewiring approaches enrich spatial connectivity but add edges heuristically. Recent spectral methods — specifically S²GNN (Geisler et al., 2024) — derive a positive lower bound on Jacobian sensitivity under three simplifying assumptions (identity feature transform, zeroed spatial term, filter concentrated on the 0-eigenvalue) but the actual implementation of S²GNN violates all three: it uses non-linear gating for features, retains a spatial aggregation term, and parameterizes the spectral filter with Gaussian smearing over 70% of the spectrum. This mismatch between the theoretical model and the implemented model is the gap this paper targets. Key Idea: Restricting the spectral filter to the 0-eigenspace of the graph Laplacian (which captures connected-component membership) produces a propagation term that equals a scalar-weighted, fully connected subgraph within each connected component — implementable as a mean aggregation per component scaled by a learned α, with no eigendecomposition required. Adding this cheap global mixing term on top of standard sparse message passing recovers a distance-independent non-vanishing Jacobian lower bound, while an antisymmetric weight constraint stabilizes Jacobian energy across depth. Methodology: S³GNN's layer update is H(ℓ+1) = H(ℓ) + ε(Pθ(ℓ)H(ℓ)Ŵ(ℓ) + ÂH(ℓ)Ŵ(ℓ)), where Ŵ is antisymmetric, Pθ = Σᵣ αθ,ᵣ v^(r)v^(r)ᵀ is the 0-eigenspace projection (computable from connected component indicators alone), and is the normalized adjacency. The forward Euler discretization (step size ε) of a continuous-time ODE motivates the residual form. The two critical design decisions are: (1) zeroing spectral filter coefficients for all non-zero eigenvalues, which simplifies Pθ to block-constant per component and eliminates the need for eigendecomposition — well-justified by the theory-practice gap experiment; (2) constraining W to be antisymmetric rather than free or orthogonal — justified by an ablation on the Barbell synthetic benchmark showing antisymmetry provides better stability-expressivity tradeoff than orthogonality (which over-constrains optimization) or free W (which destabilizes Jacobians). Per-layer complexity is O(|E|d + Nd²), matching GCN and a factor K better than ChebNet/Stable-ChebNet. Two propositions are proved: Proposition 1 establishes a distance-independent lower bound ||∂hᵢ(ℓ)/∂xₛ|| ≥ ∏ₚ ε·αₚ/nᵣ · σmin(W(p)); Proposition 2 shows ||J(ℓ)||₂ = 1 + O(ε²) due to antisymmetry of the total propagation operator. Key Results: On the graph property prediction benchmark (Corso et al., 2020), S³GNN achieves log₁₀(MSE) of −0.3301 ± 0.0342 (Diameter), −2.6311 ± 0.0171 (SSSP), and −2.4801 ± 0.0239 (Eccentricity), uniformly outperforming GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, GIN, ChebNet, Stable-ChebNet, S²GNN, and DIFFormer, with a ~1 log₁₀ unit gain on Eccentricity over S²GNN. On OGB datasets: ogbn-arxiv accuracy 76.38 ± 0.32 (vs. Stable-ChebNet 75.42, S²GCN 73.89); ogbn-proteins 81.90 ± 0.12 (vs. SPEXPHORMER 80.65, S²GCN 80.58). On LRGB peptides-func S³GNN achieves 73.20 ± 0.14 AP and peptides-struct 0.2429 ± 0.0014 MAE, competitive with or better than GRIT, Graph ViT, and S²GNN. For KGQA on WebQSP multi-hop F1, S³GNN improves over base reasoning GNN by +10.8 points (69.6 vs. 58.8). On cylinder-flow fluid dynamics (mesh simulation), S³MGN reduces min test loss from 0.173 to 0.102 (41% reduction) and velocity RMSE from 0.000589 to 0.000333 vs. MeshGraphNet. On heterophily benchmarks (Table 10), S³GNN achieves 91.52 Roman-empire accuracy and 97.58 Minesweeper AUC, competitive with the best methods. Multiple seeds and standard deviations are reported throughout. The learned α values are empirically confirmed to be positive and increasing with layer depth across all tested tasks — a necessary condition for the lower bound to hold. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The core novelty is the observation that restricting spectral filtering to the 0-eigenspace produces a computationally cheap global mixing operator equivalent to per-component mean pooling with learnable scalar weights, enabling theory-backed OSQ mitigation at GCN complexity. The antisymmetric stability constraint is adopted from Stable-ChebNet (Hariri et al., 2025). The theory-practice gap diagnosis of S²GNN's lower bound is a genuine empirical and analytic contribution. Overall, this is a clever recombination and simplification of existing spectral GNN ideas, not an entirely new formulation. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are provided for the weight constraint type (free vs. antisymmetric vs. orthogonal) on the Barbell benchmark; α dynamics are visualized across layers and datasets. Results are reported with standard deviations over multiple seeds (5 runs for graph property prediction, 4 for heterophily, not always specified for LRGB/OGB). The Jacobian norm distribution analysis (11 test-set graphs) provides direct empirical support for the theory-practice gap claim and for S³GNN's superiority. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/EEthanShi/S3-GNN.git. One methodological concern: the α positivity assumption required for Proposition 1 is not enforced during training; the paper relies on empirical observation that it holds, without providing a theoretical explanation for why training favors positive α. The lower bound in Prop 1 could collapse to near-zero in practice if α is small or σmin(W) < 1, which the paper does not quantify across experiments. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (methods and systems evaluated via experiments). - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA H200 GPU (HPC cluster); all experiments are feasible on commercially available high-end hardware, with complexity comparable to GCN. - Efficient: Per-layer cost O(|E|d + Nd²) matches GCN and is K× cheaper than ChebNet/Stable-ChebNet per layer; eliminates the O(N³) eigendecomposition of S²GNN; achieves leading results with 4 layers vs. 4-layer K=10 polynomials in ChebNet. - Effective: Evidence across 6 task domains (synthetic graph properties, OGB node classification, LRGB, KGQA, brain signal interpolation, mesh fluid dynamics) consistently demonstrates strong performance on the stated long-range interaction problems. - Scalable: Tested up to ogbn-proteins (132,534 nodes, 79M edges) and ogbn-arxiv (169,343 nodes); the O(Nd) global mixing term scales linearly in nodes per component, but behavior on truly massive graphs (billions of edges) is not evaluated and the connected-component structure must be precomputed. Code is released as a research artifact, not a production library. Strengths: - The theory-practice gap analysis is concrete and honest: the paper runs the idealized S²GNN dynamics (Eq. 7) and the practical S²GNN (Eq. 5) side-by-side and shows empirically that the theoretical lower bound is frequently violated by the real model — a methodologically sound critique of a prior ICML/NeurIPS contribution. - The key algebraic observation that the 0-eigenspace projection collapses to a per-component mean (implementable without eigenvectors) is non-obvious and practically impactful, reducing S²GNN's eigendecomposition bottleneck to a simple aggregation. - Propositions 1 and 2 are fully proved in the appendix with complete, correct proofs — the Kronecker product argument for stability is clean and follows directly from antisymmetry. - Breadth of experimental validation (6 task types across 4 distinct domains) substantially strengthens the generalization claim compared to papers that evaluate on LRGB alone. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that the choice of weight constraint (antisymmetric vs. orthogonal) depends on the propagation mechanism and task, and they frame this as an open direction for future work. They acknowledge that orthogonal W can more strongly tighten the Jacobian lower bound but may destabilize optimization. The paper does not explicitly acknowledge the α positivity gap (the bound requires α > 0 but this is not enforced), nor does it confront the upper limit on graph scale tested. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The global mixing coefficient α is a single learnable scalar per connected component per layer — it provides uniform weighting within a component and cannot capture which nodes are informationally relevant. On large, heterogeneous components this is a significant representational limitation that the paper does not discuss. - The α positivity assumption (required for Prop. 1) is empirically satisfied on the tested benchmarks but is never explained theoretically. If training dynamics on a different task push α negative, the non-vanishing guarantee collapses entirely; no experiment probes this failure mode. - Scalability claim beyond ogbn-proteins (132K nodes) is stated but not tested. The global mixing term requires identifying connected components (cheap) but the paper provides no wall-clock or memory benchmarks at >1M node scale. - The ablation only varies the weight constraint form on the Barbell graph. Missing: ablation of the global mixing term itself (what happens if Pθ is removed, leaving only the antisymmetric spatial MPNN?), which would isolate how much of the gain comes from global mixing vs. the stability constraint vs. the combination. - Several highly relevant transformer baselines (Graphormer, GPS++, NodeFormer on protein tasks) are absent from some tables without explanation. Bottom Line: A practitioner working on long-range GNN tasks gains a concretely useful architecture: S³GNN is simpler to implement than S²GNN (no eigendecomposition), faster per layer than ChebNet variants, theoretically grounded, and empirically dominant across diverse benchmarks — it should be the new default spectral-spatial baseline for oversquashing experiments.
Search Results 2605.23467 PDF
#13 — Highly Innovative (75)

SpatialBench: Is Your Spatial Foundation Model an All-Round Player? (56 ▲ )

Haosong Peng, Hao Li, Jiaqi Chen, Yuhao Pan, Runmao Yao, Yalun Dai, Fushuo Huo, Fangzhou Hong, Zhaoxi Chen, Haozhao Wang, Dingwen Zhang, Ziwei Liu, Wenchao Xu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SpatialBench, a cross-paradigm benchmark spanning 19 datasets, 546 scenes, and 41 model variants across 6 reconstruction paradigms, plus DA-Next-5M (5.5M-frame egocentric/wrist-view dataset) and DA-Next (a trained baseline model), for evaluating spatial foundation models under deterministic multi-density sampling. - Why it's important: Existing evaluations of 3D spatial models are siloed by paradigm, use non-reproducible frame sampling, and ignore egocentric/wrist-view domains critical for embodied robotics — making it impossible to know whether any model generalizes across real-world deployment conditions. - How it does it: A deterministic multi-density scene-index protocol (SINGLE/SPARSE/MEDIUM/DENSE regimes derived from voxel-coverage set-cover) ensures every method sees identical inputs, enabling fair cross-paradigm comparison; domain-tagged metadata supports over 100 distinct evaluation configurations. - The good: DA-Next trained on the new egocentric/wrist-view DA-Next-5M dataset achieves +47%/+59% AbsRel improvement over DA3-Giant on sparse/medium depth estimation, concretely demonstrating that the OOD gap is domain-data-driven, not architectural. - The missing: DA-Next itself is only evaluated at sparse/medium density (goes OOM on dense inputs), and there is no ablation isolating the contribution of domain data versus the added scale-prediction head — the performance gain from in-domain data and the architectural change are conflated. - Read it or not: Researchers building or evaluating spatial/3D foundation models for robotics or embodied AI should read it; it establishes the most rigorous cross-paradigm evaluation protocol to date and exposes concrete, quantified failure modes.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (benchmark infrastructure + trained DA-Next model). Practical Impact (85) is the dominant weighted term — a released cross-paradigm benchmark (code, DA-Next-5M dataset, model weights) that lowers the cost of all comparable future work and exposes a field-level, quantified OOD failure mode (wrist/ego-view). Novelty (75) is real but lives in scope and protocol design, not method — the deterministic voxel-set-cover multi-density sampling is a genuine, reusable reproducibility contribution. Rigor (70) is dragged down by single-run point estimates with no significance tests, an unstated DENSE frame cap, and a DA-Next ablation that conflates the scale head with in-domain data. Non-Obviousness (70) and Resilience (70) are solid: the OOD finding is aggregated over 41 models so it survives scrutiny, though DA-Next's own gains are challengeable. Completeness (70) — limitations are acknowledged but the scope-vs-framing gap ('spatial intelligence' but only metric depth/geometry) is real. Weighted: 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.30*85 = 75.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: 3D spatial foundation models — systems that infer depth, camera pose, point clouds, and scene geometry from image sequences — are now deployed in robotics, autonomous driving, and AR/VR. However, the field lacks a standardized benchmark: existing evaluations cover only a narrow paradigm slice (e.g., optimization-based only, or feed-forward only), use arbitrary and non-reproducible frame sampling, and do not cover the egocentric and robot wrist-view domains that matter most for embodied AI. As a result, it is not possible to determine whether any model genuinely generalizes across paradigm, domain, and input density — the critical question for production deployment. Key Idea: The central insight is that reproducible, fair cross-paradigm comparison requires decoupling scene selection from inference: precompute deterministic per-scene frame indices (stored as JSON records) across four density regimes derived via a voxel-coverage set-cover objective. This separates the question of "which frames" from the question of "how does a model process them," allowing methods with entirely different operating assumptions (online streaming vs. full-context attention vs. TTT) to be evaluated on identical inputs. Methodology: SpatialBench unifies 19 publicly available datasets (static-real, static-synthetic, dynamic-real, dynamic-synthetic) into a shared per-scene representation of RGB frames, metric depth, camera-to-world poses, and intrinsics. Frame indices for four density regimes are precomputed: SINGLE (one deterministic frame), SPARSE (greedy voxel-coverage set-cover, ~4–15 frames), MEDIUM (overlap-favoring coverage, moderate budget), and DENSE (near-full trajectory, capped for feasibility). Five task suites are evaluated: camera pose estimation (RRA/RTA/AUC), camera trajectory estimation (ATE/RPE), depth estimation (AbsRel/δ thresholds), dense reconstruction (F-score/Accuracy/Completeness), and prior-enhanced prediction (GT depth or pose injected). Unified adapter code wraps 31 methods across 6 paradigms. A dedicated stereo-depth pipeline (S2M2 + MapAnything pose estimation + SAM3 dynamic-region masking + bundle adjustment) curates metric depth for the DROID wrist-view sequences. DA-Next extends the DA3-Giant architecture with added scale tokens that regress a per-scene metric scale scalar, trained with a 5-component multi-task loss (depth, gradient, ray, pointmap, scale) on DA-Next-5M plus 11 general 3D datasets on 4 H200 GPUs over 7 days. Critical design decisions: (1) voxel set-cover for SPARSE/MEDIUM is well-justified — prevents naive temporal subsampling from producing degenerate overlapping views; (2) GT-camera token injection at training time with p=20% is practically motivated but not ablated for sensitivity; (3) joint training on general datasets alongside DA-Next-5M is described but the mixing ratio's effect is not ablated. Key Results: Across 41 model variants on SpatialBench's main table: full-context feed-forward models (DA3-Giant, VGGT-Omega, π3-X) define the accuracy upper bound at sparse/medium density — DA3-Giant achieves AbsRel 0.095 (sparse) and VGGT-Omega reaches AUC@30 0.803 (sparse). Most full-context models go OOM on dense inputs (>140 GB). Streaming/chunk-wise/TTT models survive dense inputs: LingbotMap achieves AUC@30 0.641 (average) with ATE 1.989 at dense. TTT methods show clear density-dependent trade-offs: TTT3R nearly doubles AUC@30 at dense (0.165→0.321), but degrades on sparse vs. its CUT3R base. DA-Next achieves AbsRel 0.050 (sparse, −47% vs. DA3-Giant 0.095) and 0.035 (medium, −59% vs. 0.086), with AUC@30 improvements of +3.1%/+5.5%; it goes OOM on dense inputs. Domain analysis shows wrist-view and ego-view are the strongest OOD failure modes across all 41 models. Prior injection ablations show that providing GT camera poses to DA3-Giant collapses ATE to 0.000 (medium), bounding the headroom available to pose-prediction modules. Baselines are current and strong (evaluated models include 2025–2026 preprints). The benchmark was tested at standard scale (up to ~1000 frames per dense scene) with no stress-scale experiment beyond that. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark design (deterministic multi-density voxel-set-cover protocol, cross-paradigm unified adapters, domain-tagged metadata) is genuinely novel in scope and rigor for the 3D spatial modeling field. DA-Next-5M is a new egocentric/wrist-view dataset. The empirical finding that wrist/ego-view OOD failure is field-level (not model-specific) and addressable by targeted data curation is substantiated and non-obvious. DA-Next itself is a standard extension of DA3 (adds a scale head and in-domain data) and is framed as a baseline, not a primary contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: The benchmark's deterministic protocol is a direct reproducibility contribution — all frame indices are precomputed and shared, eliminating sampling variance. Unified adapter code is released at github.com/Ropedia/SpatialBench, and the dataset is at ropedia-ai/DA-Next-5M (HuggingFace). No ablation is provided on the voxel-cover budget parameters or on the SPARSE/MEDIUM frame-count sensitivity. DA-Next lacks an ablation separating the scale-head architectural contribution from the domain-data contribution; the joint dataset mixing ratio is not ablated. Error bars and significance tests are absent — the benchmark results are single-run point estimates. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.27367v1), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (benchmark infrastructure + trained DA-Next model). - Practical: The benchmark can run on commercially available H100/A100 GPUs; many full-context models require >40 GB and go OOM on dense at 140 GB, while bounded-memory models run on consumer-tier GPUs. - Efficient: DA-Next inference time is 0.50 s/sequence (sparse), comparable to DA3-Giant (0.47 s); training required 4× H200 GPUs for 7 days, which is accessible but not cheap. - Effective: The benchmark demonstrates that no current model is an all-round player; DA-Next effectively closes the wrist/ego-view depth gap (+47–59% AbsRel) but does not close the dense-regime OOM limitation. - Scalable: Dense evaluation is capped at an unspecified maximum frame count to keep all methods feasible; actual long-horizon behavior (10,000+ frames) is not tested. The software artifact (benchmark code + DA-Next model weights + DA-Next-5M) is released. Strengths: - The deterministic voxel-set-cover sampling is a principled and reusable solution to the reproducibility problem in visual geometry evaluation; it separates scene difficulty from density-regime difficulty in a way no prior benchmark does. - Evaluating 41 model variants across 6 paradigms under a single unified protocol at this scale is a genuine engineering and organizational contribution that directly enables cross-paradigm insight (e.g., the TTT density-dependence finding, which required comparing the same base model with and without TTT across four density regimes). - The domain-level OOD analysis is backed by aggregating results over all 41 models, making the wrist/ego-view failure finding a field-level claim rather than an artifact of any single model's weakness. - The GT-prior injection ablation (Table 3) cleanly quantifies the ceiling on what better pose or depth prediction could contribute, providing a roadmap for future model development. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that DA-Next goes OOM on dense inputs (same memory profile as its DA3-Giant base), explicitly stating it is a baseline for the identified domain gap rather than a complete solution. They acknowledge that SpatialBench is a snapshot benchmark and that new models need to implement new adapters to be included. No broader failure modes or evaluation biases are explicitly discussed. Analyst-Identified Gaps: DA-Next's improvements conflate two changes — added scale head and in-domain DA-Next-5M data — with no ablation separating them; the scale improvement claim could be entirely data-driven. The dense-regime OOM for DA-Next (same as DA3-Giant) limits the benchmark's usefulness as a guide for the most practically demanding deployment scenario for embodied AI (continuous long-horizon operation). The maximum frame count for the DENSE cap is not stated in the main text, making it unclear how "dense" the dense evaluation actually is. The benchmark covers only metric-scale depth and camera geometry — no semantic understanding, no novel-view synthesis, no surface normal prediction — so its scope as a proxy for "spatial intelligence" is narrower than the framing implies. No statistical significance tests accompany any of the head-to-head comparisons. Bottom Line: Practitioners evaluating or training 3D spatial foundation models should adopt this benchmark as a reference protocol — the deterministic multi-density design solves a real reproducibility problem, the 41-model cross-paradigm comparison surfaces actionable findings (full-context vs. bounded-memory trade-off, TTT density dependence, domain gap quantification), and the released code and dataset directly lower the cost of comparable future work.
Recommended 2605.27367 PDF
#14 — Innovative (74)

CATS: Cascaded Adaptive Tree Speculation for Memory-Limited LLM Inference Acceleration

Yuning Han, Yangchenchen Jin, Dylan Zhao, Jingwei Sun · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CATS, a self-speculative decoding framework that partitions a transformer LLM into three cascaded stages—draft, shallow verify, and target verify—whose layer boundaries adapt to the device's DRAM budget, achieving up to 5.08× wall-clock speedup on edge hardware without increasing peak memory footprint beyond the target model alone. - Why it's important: Flash-to-DRAM bandwidth (~2 GB/s) is the binding bottleneck for LLM inference on edge devices where even a 7B model barely fits in DRAM, yet every existing speculative decoding method assumes memory-resident weights—an assumption that causes their speedups to degrade by 20–25% when moved from server to edge hardware. - How it does it: A shallow verifier (DRAM-resident intermediate layers) catches and corrects draft errors at zero additional flash-transfer cost, folding those correction candidates into a tree-masked single final pass over the remaining layers streamed from flash—so more tokens are accepted per flash-load cycle without widening the bandwidth envelope. - The good: On real Jetson AGX Orin hardware (8 GB budget), CATS achieves 3.16× speedup on Vicuna-7B vs. 1.93× for Kangaroo and 1.90× for EAGLE-self, while reducing Bytes Per Token from 8.37 to 5.84 GB/tok—consistent gains across all five benchmarks, four models, and three memory budgets tested. - The missing: No ablation compares the Reduced KL Loss against standard full-vocabulary KL distillation, leaving its contribution to acceptance-rate improvement unquantified; and no baseline tests non-speculative flash-I/O-optimized inference (e.g., PowerInfer) to isolate how much of the gain is from speculative decoding versus smarter flash scheduling. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone deploying transformer LLMs on DRAM-constrained edge devices; of limited relevance for server-side GPU inference where HBM capacity is not the constraint.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (weights 15/15/10/15/15/30). Practical Impact (80) dominates and is well-earned: code is released, speedups are measured end-to-end on real Jetson AGX Orin hardware (3.16–5.08×), and the artifact is directly deployable on commodity edge devices. Non-Obviousness (78) and Novelty (75) are high because reframing speculative decoding as a flash↔DRAM bandwidth-constrained problem — and minimizing Bytes-Per-Token rather than maximizing acceptance — is a genuinely fresh problem formulation absent from prior spec-decoding work, with a clean BPT analytical decomposition. Rigor (68) reflects consistent dominance across 4 models × 5 benchmarks × 3 memory budgets and released code, but no seeds/error bars, preprint status, and a missing isolation of the Reduced-KL loss. Completeness (68) and Resilience (70) are held back by the absent non-speculative flash-I/O baseline (e.g., PowerInfer), leaving the speculative-vs-scheduling attribution open, and untested long-context KV pressure. Composite: 0.15*75+0.15*68+0.10*78+0.15*68+0.15*70+0.30*80 ≈ 74.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 68 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Autoregressive LLM decoding is memory-bound: each generation step loads all model weights and intermediate activations from memory. On server-class GPUs (e.g., B200 with ~180 GB HBM at ~8 TB/s), weights reside persistently in HBM and the binding cost is HBM↔SRAM bandwidth. On edge devices with unified DRAM architectures (e.g., Jetson AGX Orin with ≤16 GB shared DRAM at ~60 GB/s and flash at only ~2 GB/s), the situation is categorically different: even a 7B model (~14 GB) barely fits in DRAM, let alone a second draft model, so weights must be streamed from flash on every forward pass. The per-token latency breakdown on Jetson AGX Orin is 6.81s for flash loading vs. 225ms DRAM-resident compute and 29ms on-chip compute—flash dominates by 30×. All existing speculative decoding methods—auxiliary-draft (Kangaroo, Medusa, EAGLE) and self-speculative (LayerSkip, SWIFT)—assume memory-resident weights, and their design choices (draft depth, verification strategy, extra adapter parameters) all incur additional flash traffic that compounds the bottleneck they were meant to relieve. The paper measures this directly: Kangaroo's speedup falls 19.8% and EAGLE's falls 24.5% when moved from B200 to Jetson AGX Orin. Key Idea The core insight is that the verification pass need not be monolithic: by splitting the full model into a shallow verifier (intermediate layers, kept DRAM-resident alongside the draft model) and a target-verify tail (remaining layers, streamed from flash), the shallow verifier can catch draft errors and generate corrections at zero additional flash-transfer cost. Those corrections are then folded as side branches into a tree-masked single batched pass over the target tail, increasing the number of tokens accepted per flash-load cycle without widening the Bytes Per Token (BPT) footprint. The draft and shallow verifier layer depths are free parameters set to exactly consume the available DRAM budget, making the framework automatically adaptive to any memory configuration. Methodology CATS organizes each decoding cycle into three stages. In the draft stage, a compact draft model (layers 1 to L_DM with adapter A_DM) runs γ sequential autoregressive steps, all DRAM-resident; L_DM is chosen shallow (default 3) to balance DRAM capacity, repeated compute cost, and bandwidth. In the shallow verification stage, the intermediate layers (L_DM+1 to L_SV, loaded once per cycle alongside the draft model) forward all γ draft candidates in parallel; the SV adapter (A_SV) predicts verification tokens at each position and flags mismatches, generating correction candidates. Mismatches and corrections are assembled into a verification tree with tree-masked attention. Critically, re-forwarding correction tokens through layers 1 to L_SV reuses DRAM-resident weights at zero additional flash cost. In the target verification stage, the remaining layers (L_SV+1 to L_total) are streamed chunk-by-chunk from flash; the assembled tree is processed in a single batched forward pass, and the longest accepted prefix is selected. L_SV is a free parameter set to consume whatever remaining DRAM budget exists after loading the draft model. The Bytes Per Token formula makes the design principle precise: BPT_CATS = (γ·B_draft + B_SV + B_target) / (β+1), where B_SV + B_target = B_verify and B_SV is amortized across a single flash load per cycle (not repeated γ times like the draft), so enlarging L_SV increases verifier capacity without inflating the repeated-draft bandwidth term. Adapter training uses a Reduced KL Loss that restricts distillation supervision to the top-K tokens under the target distribution at each position (renormalized), rather than the full vocabulary. Formally: L_top-K = -(1/|M|) Σ_{t∈M} Σ_{v∈T_K(t)} p̃_target(v|t) · log q_draft(v|t), where T_K(t) is the top-K support. The rationale is that only tokens in T_K(t) have non-negligible acceptance probability; spreading gradient signal over the full vocabulary wastes capacity of the compact sub-networks. Adapters are trained on 68,000 ShareGPT samples in 13 hours on 2 B200 GPUs. Three critical design decisions: (1) keeping L_DM shallow (3 layers)—justified empirically via BPT analysis showing that deepening the draft model (Kangaroo L_DM=3→15) raises BPT from 8.37 to 11.10 GB/tok while the speedup falls from 1.93× to 1.53× on the same 8 GB budget; (2) setting L_SV adaptively to the DRAM ceiling—validated across 2, 6, and 8 GB budgets with monotonically increasing speedup; (3) single flash load per cycle for SV layers—the shallow verification is effectively free from a bandwidth perspective. Key Results On Vicuna-7B with greedy decoding on Jetson AGX Orin: CATS achieves 3.18× mean end-to-end speedup and 3.06 mean accepted tokens per target call, vs. 1.92× / 2.27 tokens for Kangaroo, 1.76× / 2.48 tokens for Medusa, and 1.74× / 2.20 tokens for EAGLE-self chain. CATS+EAGLE tree decoding reaches 3.71×. Under relaxed decoding (τ=0.7): CATS achieves 3.94× vs. 2.28× (Kangaroo) and 2.94× (EAGLE-self+tree). On LLaMA2-13B: CATS achieves 4.65× speedup (4.35 accepted tokens) under greedy vs. EAGLE-self at 3.88× and Kangaroo at 3.60×; CATS+EAGLE reaches 5.08×. Memory budget ablation (Vicuna-7B, Table 3): 2 GB → 2.82× (Kangaroo: 2.27×), 6 GB → 2.96× (Kangaroo: 2.12×), 8 GB → 3.16× (Kangaroo: 1.93×)—CATS leads across all budgets. BPT (Table 4): CATS at 5.84 GB/tok vs. 8.37–11.10 GB/tok for Kangaroo variants with equivalent or higher acceptance. Quality-speed Pareto analysis (MT-Bench, GPT-4o judge): under matched relaxation schedules, CATS occupies the upper-right Pareto frontier, meaning higher accepted length without lower quality scores. Baselines (Kangaroo ICML 2024, Medusa ICML 2024, EAGLE ICML 2024, REST NAACL 2024, Lookahead arXiv 2024) are current and cover the relevant self-speculative landscape; auxiliary-model methods are excluded because they cannot fit in edge DRAM—a justified exclusion. Scale tested: 7B and 13B models; not tested above 13B. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM + NEW PROBLEM FORMULATION. The cascaded three-stage verification architecture that explicitly reasons about flash↔DRAM transfer costs and partitions model layers across draft/shallow-verify/target-verify based on a DRAM budget parameter is not present in prior speculative decoding work. Framing speculative decoding as a flash-memory-constrained problem is itself novel—prior work uniformly assumes HBM/DRAM residency. The Reduced KL distillation loss is a new training objective. The EAGLE-compatible tree extension is a straightforward integration of an existing idea. Overall, the novelty is in the systems problem identification and the architectural response, not in fundamental algorithmic primitives. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover drafting horizon γ (Table 5, showing diminishing returns beyond γ=5), memory budget (Tables 3–4 with BPT decomposition), and the full cross-product of models × benchmarks × decoding modes (Tables 1–2). The BPT analytical framework provides a principled explanation for why deeper drafters hurt edge speedup despite improving acceptance. Code is released at https://github.com/ElizaFuLan/CATS.git. Wall-clock speedup is measured on real Jetson AGX Orin hardware (not simulated). Training details (dataset, learning rates, compute) are sufficient for reproduction. No error bars or multiple-seed variance reported. No ablation isolates the Reduced KL Loss contribution vs. standard KL—the paper argues the focused loss matters for compact sub-networks but provides no ablation to confirm this. This is a preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built framework evaluated on real deployed hardware. - Practical: Runs on commercially available NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin (≤16 GB DRAM); adapter training requires 2 B200 GPUs for 13 hours—accessible to academic labs but not single-device deployment. - Efficient: CATS reduces per-cycle compute time from 0.098s (Kangaroo) to 0.062s and BPT from 8.37 to 5.84 GB/tok on Vicuna-7B 8 GB budget—a ~30% bandwidth reduction per token alongside a 64% acceptance improvement. - Effective: Wall-clock speedup measured end-to-end on real edge hardware with quality validated via GPT-4o MT-Bench scoring; Pareto analysis confirms gains are not achieved by relaxing the output distribution. - Scalable: Tested up to 13B parameters; framework is model-agnostic for transformer architectures; no evaluation at 30B+ or at long context lengths where KV-cache could saturate the DRAM budget and alter the L_SV optimization—a gap the paper does not address. Code and a complete algorithm listing (Algorithm 1) are released. Strengths - Correctly identifies flash↔DRAM bandwidth as the structurally different binding constraint on edge devices and builds the entire design around minimizing BPT rather than maximizing acceptance rate in isolation—a systems insight entirely absent from prior speculative decoding literature. - The BPT analytical decomposition (Equations 3–4) cleanly exposes why deepening the draft model degrades edge speedup even when it improves acceptance, and the Table 4 ablation confirms this experimentally with five configurations. - Consistent dominance across all 5 benchmarks, 4 models (Vicuna-7B/13B and LLaMA2-7B/13B), 3 memory budgets, and both greedy and relaxed decoding modes—not a cherry-picked configuration. - Orthogonality to tree-based candidate expansion (EAGLE integration) is demonstrated rather than claimed, adding a practical pathway for further speedup without framework redesign. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge in the Conclusion: (1) evaluation is restricted to 7B–13B models; (2) the draft sub-network and SV adapter require distilled adapter training, incurring an upfront cost; (3) the three-stage design presumes transformer-style layer-aligned representations and does not directly apply to state-space architectures (Mamba, etc.). These limitations are stated directly and not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The Reduced KL Loss is presented as essential for compact sub-networks, but no ablation compares it to standard full-vocabulary KL distillation on acceptance rate or speedup—its independent contribution is unquantified. - No baseline from non-speculative flash-I/O-optimized inference (e.g., PowerInfer, LLM in a Flash with row-column bundling) is included; it is unclear how much of CATS's wall-clock advantage over vanilla inference is attributable to speculative decoding logic vs. efficient flash scheduling, or whether CATS could be combined with those I/O optimizations. - KV-cache pressure at long sequence lengths is dismissed as non-dominant at small batch sizes, but no experiment tests sequences beyond typical chat lengths; at long contexts the DRAM budget available for L_SV could shrink significantly, and the framework provides no guidance for dynamic L_SV adjustment. - GPT-4o-as-judge quality evaluation on MT-Bench uses a single temperature sweep with no variance estimates—the quality-speed Pareto claim rests on a thin statistical basis. - Adapter training requires the target model and 68,000-sample distillation; practitioners wanting to apply CATS to fine-tuned or private models must repeat this process, a non-trivial prerequisite not analyzed in terms of sample efficiency or sensitivity. Bottom Line Practitioners deploying transformer-based LLMs (7B–13B) on DRAM-constrained edge hardware should read this paper—it is the first speculative decoding method designed around the flash-memory regime and delivers hardware-validated, benchmark-consistent speedups that existing self-speculative methods demonstrably cannot achieve in this setting.
Recommended 2605.11186 PDF
#15 — Innovative (74)

Conceptual Schema Inference for Tabular Datasets using Large Language Models

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces two LLM-based methods — GeSI (generative, decoder-based) and EmSI (embedding, encoder-based) — for automatically deriving conceptual schemas (entity types, type hierarchies, attributes, and inter-type relationships) from raw tabular datasets using only column headers and cell values, without external ontologies or task-specific training. - Why it's important: Data lakes and open data portals contain thousands of heterogeneous tables that cannot be navigated or queried without a global conceptual map; existing dataset discovery tools operate at the individual table or attribute level and provide no repository-wide semantic understanding. - How it does it: GeSI chains per-table LLM type-path inference → weighted merging → LLM-verified pruning → column-type hierarchy construction → bottom-up attribute inheritance → attribute-value-guided relationship and cardinality inference; EmSI clusters subjectness-weighted table embeddings into top-level types, then clusters columns into attributes, builds sub-hierarchies from Jaccard-similarity dendrograms with silhouette-based pruning, and identifies relationships via column-embedding similarity thresholds. - The good: GeSI achieves RI > 0.978 and Purity > 0.89 for conceptual attribute inference across all three evaluated benchmarks, and F1 scores of 0.675–0.967 for relationship inference, outperforming the embedding-based EmSI (best F1 ≤ 0.359) on every relationship metric. - The missing: No downstream task evaluation demonstrates that an inferred schema actually improves dataset search, data integration, or annotation quality; the only baselines for type inference and attributes are prior works from the same author group. - Read it or not: Database researchers working on data lake management, ontology learning, or LLM-assisted data integration will find the formal problem definition and the generative vs. embedding trade-off analysis valuable; practitioners should note that GeSI's attribute inference dominates runtime (>90% of total) and reaches ~2000 minutes at 10K tables with a local 14B model.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (80) is high — the paper formally defines repository-level conceptual schema inference (NEW PROBLEM) covering types, hierarchies, attributes, and relationships jointly, and GeSI's intermediate column-type hierarchy for attribute disambiguation is a genuinely new mechanism. Rigor (72) is solid — public code/prompts/datasets, ablations of prompt constraints and LLM verification, four benchmarks averaged over 5 runs — but is held back by missing standard deviations, Schema.org-derived ground truth that biases toward LLM-aligned outputs, and baselines drawn only from the same author group. Non-Obviousness (75) reflects the hierarchy-first insight that prevents premature semantic collapse. Completeness (72) and Resilience (70) are good with honestly confronted limitations, though the absence of any downstream task evaluation and of non-LLM comparators leaves the practical value unestablished. Impact (70) credits the released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*80 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*70 ≈ 74.
empirical Novelty 80 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Data scientists working with data lakes, web tables, and open data portals face repositories of hundreds to thousands of tables from heterogeneous producers. These repositories exhibit both schema-level heterogeneity (the same attribute represented under different column names) and granularity variation (tables mixing entity records at different hierarchical levels). Existing methods for dataset discovery — keyword search, join discovery, table union search — operate at the level of individual tables or attribute pairs and cannot provide a global conceptual understanding: which entity types are present, what attributes characterize them, and what semantic relationships recur across types. Prior schema inference work targets semi-structured formats (XML, JSON, RDF) using syntactic similarity and is unsuitable for raw tabular data. Data profiling and join discovery enrich the logical schema but do not reduce complexity or resolve semantic inconsistencies. The gap is a method that automatically constructs an Entity-Relationship-style conceptual schema from arbitrary tabular repositories, without curated taxonomies, external ontologies, or task-specific labeled training data. Key Idea The central insight is that LLMs — both generative and embedding variants — contain sufficient world knowledge to map raw table columns to semantic entity types and to reason about hierarchical and relational structure, provided that inference is decomposed into tractable per-table or per-column subtasks and the outputs are merged, verified, and propagated bottom-up. For GeSI, the key trick is introducing an intermediate column type hierarchy as a shared semantic backbone: each column is anchored to its lowest-common-ancestor in this hierarchy, so columns that share a semantic domain (e.g., both Composer and Performer are typed as Musician) are clustered together before being resolved into distinct attributes via LLM role-naming. This prevents premature semantic collapse and is the direct source of GeSI's superior Purity in attribute inference. For EmSI, the key trick is replacing column identity by embedding similarity, using subjectness-weighted table embeddings to cluster tables that describe the same real-world concept regardless of their surface column names. Methodology GeSI is a three-step sequential pipeline. Step 1 infers a global type hierarchy: each table receives one or more type paths (e.g., Thing→Event→MusicEvent) via few-shot LLM prompts with optional constraints (ABS blacklists abstract top-level types; SLC forces second-level convergence; FET pre-extracts leaf types then generates full paths; FULL combines SLC and FET). Per-table paths are merged by edge frequency weighting and pruned via self-loop removal, inverse-edge resolution, and LLM-based is-a verification (each candidate edge A→B is validated by asking the LLM "Is A the parent type of B?"). Step 2 constructs a parallel column type hierarchy, maps each column to its lowest-common-ancestor semantic type, clusters co-typed columns per entity type, names attributes via LLM using column name + sampled values + table context, deduplicates synonymous attribute names (Fig. 7 prompt), and promotes attributes appearing in ≥θ=0.9 of child types up the hierarchy. Step 3 infers relationships by identifying attributes whose sampled values are named entities of other inferred types (two-pass top-K then refined selection), generating a predicate label, and inferring cardinality from three column statistics (null ratio ρ_null, multi-value ratio ρ_multi, uniqueness ratio ρ_uniq) plus sampled rows. LLM backbones evaluated: Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-14B, GPT-3.5, GPT-4. EmSI proceeds in four steps. Step 1 computes subjectness scores (via TableMiner+) for each column, constructs weighted table embeddings as weighted aggregations of column embeddings, and clusters tables via agglomerative clustering. Step 2 clusters all columns within each top-level type by column-embedding similarity; each cluster becomes a conceptual attribute; subject attributes are identified by named-entity membership and subjectness score ≥ 50%. Step 3 runs hierarchical clustering on tables within each top-level type using Jaccard similarity of their conceptual attribute sets, selects dendrogram slices with silhouette score within Δ=0.15 of the maximum, and promotes retained clusters to subtypes. Step 4 establishes a relationship between subject attribute cs_ai of type t_i and non-subject attribute ca_j of type t_j when more than δ_2 of ca_j's columns are within distance δ_1 of cs_ai's columns. Embedding backbones: Starmie, DeepJoin, SwAV (fine-tuned), SBERT, Unicorn (pretrained). Critical design decisions: (a) the hierarchy-first ordering in GeSI is well-justified as type context disambiguates attribute roles; (b) the frequency-weighted edge merging is reasonable but can produce overlapping type clusters (a Book table assigned to both Product and CreativeWork paths) that reduce RI; (c) the silhouette-based dendrogram pruning in EmSI is principled but Δ=0.15 was tuned on WDC and applied across all datasets, which risks overfitting to WDC structure. Key Results All experiments run on a single NVIDIA A100 (80 GB), 5 repetitions averaged. Four benchmarks: WDC (602 tables / 43 types), GDS (660 tables / 53 types), OD Small (158 tables / 24 types), OD Large (10,361 tables / 53 types — type/hierarchy only). Top-level types: GeSI achieves highest Purity on all benchmarks (0.963–0.993 best-variant), outperforming GeTT baseline by 2.8–8.1% and EmSI by 1.6–9.8%. EmSI achieves highest RI on 3/4 benchmarks (SwAV: 0.887 WDC, 0.894 GDS, 0.952 OD Small, 0.892 OD Large). GeSI RI lags primarily because its multi-path assignment creates overlapping clusters. Type hierarchy (PTCS): EmSI dominates (0.800–1.000 best-variant) vs. GeSI (0.633–0.868); EmSI outperforms GeSI's best by 9.3–15.3%. GeSI generates 24–213 more distinct types than EmSI but hierarchies are 0.5–1.0 levels shallower on three datasets. LLM-based verification improves GeSI's PTCS by +0.053 (GDS) to +0.153 (WDC). Prompt constraints (FET/FULL) improve RI by ~7% on WDC; ABS/SLC improve PTCS by 8–12% on WDC. Conceptual attributes: GeSI substantially outperforms EmSI and SI-LLM baseline: GeSI RI 0.978–0.995 / Purity 0.867–0.933 vs. EmSI best RI 0.933/Purity 0.803, SI-LLM best RI 0.966/Purity 0.742 (on WDC). GeSI Purity advantage over EmSI is up to +0.209. Relationships: GeSI best F1 0.729 (WDC), 0.807 (GDS), 0.967 (OD Small). EmSI best F1 0.359 (WDC), 0.177 (GDS), 0.222 (OD Small) — primarily recall-constrained (recall ≤ 0.243). GeSI worst variant outperforms EmSI best by +0.316 (WDC), +0.523 (GDS), +0.563 (OD Small). Cardinality: GeSI GPT-4 achieves 0.804–0.857 accuracy; Qwen2.5-14B peaks at 0.870 on WDC but drops to 0.712 on GDS. GPT-4 is most consistent across datasets. Scalability: Near-O(n) scaling for both methods on OD Large (1K–10K tables). GeSI (GPT-3.5) is ~1.4× slower than EmSI (SwAV) at 10K tables; ~20× slower at 1K tables. GeSI attribute inference accounts for >90% of GeSI runtime. EmSI attribute clustering is O(m²) in columns, becoming the primary bottleneck at large scale. Cost: GeSI with GPT-3.5 costs $1.23–$3.77 per benchmark dataset. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM: The paper formally defines repository-level conceptual schema inference from raw tabular data — distinguishing it from prior work on schema matching, logical schema profiling, and dataset discovery — and is the first to jointly target entity types, type hierarchies, conceptual attributes, and inter-type relationships under minimal metadata assumptions. NEW METHOD: GeSI's use of an intermediate column type hierarchy for attribute disambiguation is genuinely novel; EmSI's subjectness-weighted embedding clustering with silhouette-based hierarchical type inference is a new adaptation of known clustering methods to this problem. NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING: The paper establishes a quantified complementarity between generative and embedding approaches — generative dominates on attribute and relationship inference while embedding dominates on type hierarchy structural consistency — which was not previously characterized. The individual components (LLM prompting, embedding clustering, agglomerative clustering, silhouette pruning) are standard; the contribution lies in their composition and evaluation for this specific problem. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation studies are present and informative: prompt constraint variants are evaluated individually on WDC (Figs. 14–15), and LLM-based verification is ablated on all four datasets (Fig. 16), confirming both design choices contribute meaningfully. Results are averaged over 5 runs, but standard deviations are not reported, making it impossible to assess statistical significance of small differences. Code, prompts, and datasets are publicly released at https://github.com/PierreWoL/SILM/. Ground truth for WDC and GDS is based on Schema.org type annotations with manual attribute and relationship labeling; OD Small/Large ground truth derives Schema.org type paths by upward traversal, not by independent domain-expert labeling, which systematically biases evaluation toward Schema.org vocabulary and may advantage GeSI (which generates Schema.org-like type names from LLM priors). The PTCS metric is novel and defined in this paper without external validation that it correlates with human-judged schema utility. The only baselines for type/hierarchy and attribute inference are GeTT and SI-LLM, both prior works from the same author group; no comparison to non-LLM schema discovery systems (e.g., ALITE, Abstra, classical ontology learning) is included. EmSI's Δ and (δ₁, δ₂) thresholds are selected on WDC and applied to all other datasets, including WDC in the evaluation, which is a minor but real source of optimism in WDC results for EmSI. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: GeSI can run using OpenAI API or local open-weight models (Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-14B) on a single A100 (80 GB); EmSI requires the same hardware for embedding models. Both paths use commercially available hardware, though 80 GB VRAM is high-end. - Efficient: GeSI attribute inference is the dominant cost (>90% of total) with ~2000 minutes at 10K tables using Qwen2.5-14B; EmSI's O(m²) column clustering becomes the bottleneck beyond ~350K columns (currently ~2000 minutes at 10K tables). Neither is near real-time for large repositories. - Effective: GeSI demonstrably solves attribute inference (RI > 0.978, Purity > 0.89) and relationship inference (F1 up to 0.967) on benchmark data; EmSI solves type hierarchy structural consistency (PTCS up to 1.0). No downstream task evaluation confirms utility in actual data lake management. - Scalable: Near-linear scaling demonstrated up to 10,361 tables / 354,666 columns; EmSI's quadratic column complexity would become prohibitive at 10× this column count; GeSI's linear LLM-call count scales better but API cost and latency become significant. The work delivers a software artifact (code + prompts + datasets at GitHub), but no pre-trained models or CLI tool; integration requires engineering effort. Strengths - The formal problem definition (Definition 1–7) cleanly separates conceptual schema inference from related tasks and is the most precise treatment of this problem in the literature, enabling future comparisons. - GeSI's intermediate column type hierarchy construction for attribute disambiguation is a technically sound and empirically validated design choice: it prevents premature semantic collapse (correctly separating Composer and Performer as distinct roles vs. a broad Person attribute) in a way that neither SI-LLM nor EmSI achieves. - The PTCS metric addresses a genuine evaluation problem — multiple valid hierarchies exist for the same data — by measuring ancestor-descendant consistency rather than exact edge match, and is better suited to this task than standard clustering metrics alone. - The efficiency analysis at scale (1K–10K tables, Figs. 17–19) is unusually thorough for a methods paper of this type and provides actionable guidance on when to use each approach. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) GeSI fails on numeric and acronym-heavy columns lacking informative names (e.g., FIFA World Cup position codes "rcm", "st" incorrectly merged into a generic Number cluster); (2) GeSI sometimes produces near-synonymous sibling types (e.g., Musictrack and Song); (3) EmSI's relationship inference captures instance-level rather than conceptual-level relationships, limiting recall; (4) both methods may confuse observed cardinality in sampled data with true conceptual cardinality; (5) EmSI (Unicorn) failed to scale to GDS due to GPU memory limits from quadratic pairwise computation. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The most significant gap is the absence of any downstream task evaluation. The paper does not show that an inferred schema improves dataset search precision, reduces integration effort, or enables better annotation — which is the stated motivation. Schema quality measured against Schema.org ground truth is a proxy, not the target. - All baselines for type/hierarchy inference (GeTT) and attribute inference (SI-LLM) are prior works by the same group. No classical schema inference methods (ALITE, Abstra, schema summarization methods) or non-LLM ontology learning tools are included as comparators, making it impossible to assess how much improvement LLMs actually provide over simpler approaches. - The Schema.org-derived ground truth for OD Small/Large is systematically biased: paths are constructed by upward traversal of Schema.org, not by independent annotation. GeSI's LLM outputs are naturally aligned with Schema.org vocabulary (as it is in LLM pretraining data), which may inflate GeSI's scores relative to methods that do not share this bias. - The 5-run averaging without standard deviations prevents any statistical significance assessment, particularly relevant for closely competing configurations. - EmSI's O(m²) column clustering complexity is mentioned but not addressed; no approximation (e.g., ANN-based similarity) is proposed, which caps EmSI's practical applicability to repositories well below the scale of major data lakes. - The paper claims near-O(n) scaling for GeSI but does not evaluate attribute inference or relationship inference on OD Large (only type/hierarchy), so the claimed scalability is incomplete. Bottom Line Database researchers designing LLM-assisted data lake management systems will find this paper a rigorous and well-executed reference for both the formal problem of conceptual schema inference and the complementary strengths of generative versus embedding approaches; however, the absence of non-LLM baselines and downstream task validation means the practical value of the inferred schemas relative to simpler alternatives remains unestablished.
Search Results 2605.23105 PDF
#16 — Innovative (74)

From Raw Experience to Skill Consumption: A Systematic Study of Model-Generated Agent Skills (22 ▲ )

Zisu Huang, Jingwen Xu, Yifan Yang, Ziyang Gong, Qihao Yang, Muzhao Tian, Xiaohua Wang, Changze Lv, Xuemei Gao, Qi Dai, Bei Liu, Kai Qiu, Xue Yang, Dongdong Chen, Xiaoqing Zheng, Chong Luo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a utility-grounded evaluation framework that systematically studies the full three-stage lifecycle of model-generated agent skills (experience generation → skill extraction → skill consumption) across five agentic task domains, six target models, and five extractor models, and derives a meta-skill prior that consistently improves skill quality at extraction time. - Why it's important: Model-generated skills are increasingly deployed in commercial agent platforms as a scalable alternative to hand-crafted skills, but practitioners have had no principled basis for choosing extractors or predicting when skills will help versus hurt — the study finds that 25% of extractor-target pairings exhibit negative transfer. - How it does it: By holding the target model fixed while varying only the extractor across a controlled cross-matrix experiment, then using automated contrastive analysis of high-gap skill pairs to identify three validated rubric dimensions (Failure Mechanism Encoding, Actionable Specificity, High-Risk Action Blacklist) that predict downstream utility, and operationalizing them as a system-prompt insertion at extraction time. - The good: The validated 3-dimension meta-skill improves downstream performance in all nine evaluated cells (+1.55 pp average) and raises LLM judge pairwise accuracy on skill pairs from 46.4% to 73.8%, while the intuitive plausibility rubric hurts performance in 6 of 9 cells (−0.59 pp average). - The missing: The validated rubric was derived from and tested on the same experimental cells (3 domains × 3 targets), with no held-out domain to confirm generalizability; the study also does not test multi-skill libraries, skill retrieval, or cross-domain transfer, which are the primary deployment scenarios. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners building agent skill libraries or researchers studying LLM agent self-improvement, as it provides the first comprehensive lifecycle analysis and a concrete, drop-in extraction improvement backed by systematic experiments.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (80) and Rigor (78) are the top contributors: the finding that LLM quality judgment inversely predicts utility at high gaps (15.8% accuracy at δ≥5pp) is genuinely surprising, and the design is disciplined — 3-run averaging, Friedman tests with effect sizes, clean single-variable composition study, and released code (aka.ms/SkillLens). Novelty (70) is moderate because the meta-skill itself is a prompt-engineering intervention; the contribution is empirical breadth, not a new algorithm. Resilience (70) is the main drag: the validated rubric was derived and tested on the same 3-domain/3-target cells with no held-out domain, Table 1 lacks CIs, and effect sizes are modest (+1.55pp). Completeness (78) is helped by transparent scoping of what is excluded (retrieval, multi-skill libraries). Composite: 0.20*70 + 0.25*78 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*65 = 74.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 78 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 78 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Language agents increasingly rely on reusable procedural artifacts called "skills" — distilled from past execution trajectories — to improve performance without retraining. Domain-level, model-generated skills are particularly attractive because they scale beyond labor-intensive hand-crafting and are now standard in several commercial agent platforms. However, prior evaluation work has studied only isolated stages of this lifecycle: SkillsBench, SWE-Skills-Bench, and Skills-in-the-Wild measure skill consumption with pre-authored or repository-sourced skills; SkillCraft studies extraction but restricts skills to executable function compositions not generalizable to broader domains. No prior work traverses the full experience-generation → extraction → consumption pipeline and asks whether model-generated skills reliably work, when they fail, and what structural properties determine utility. The result is that practitioners deploying skill libraries have no principled basis for choosing an extractor model, managing experience pool composition, or screening skills before deployment. Key Idea The core insight is that skill extraction capability is distinct from and uncorrelated with a model's task-solving capability — the same model can be a strong executor and a weak extractor, or vice versa — and that downstream skill utility is not predictable from surface textual properties such as fluency, format, or completeness. Instead, utility is governed by three specific content properties: encoding why agents fail with named mechanisms (not just that they fail), providing step-level procedures tied to domain objects and tools (not abstract process advice), and explicitly blacklisting high-risk action patterns. These properties can be operationalized as a compact meta-skill injected into the extractor's system prompt without modifying the extraction pipeline. Methodology The pipeline has three formal stages. Stage 1: a target model M executes tasks from a training split to produce an experience pool T_{M,D} containing both successful and failed trajectories. Stage 2: an extractor model E distills the pool into a single domain-level skill S_{E,M,D} via a two-stage framework — per-trajectory pattern extraction (up to K=3 success or failure patterns per trajectory, fully parallelizable) followed by tree-structured hierarchical consolidation (merging in groups of G=10 until a single consolidated pattern set remains, then converting to a schema-conformant skill via structured tool calls). Stage 3: the skill is inlined into M's system prompt and evaluated on held-out test tasks, with the metric being performance delta Δ(E,M,D) = Perf(M|skill,test) − Perf(M|test). Two aggregate metrics disentangle the two roles: Extraction Efficacy (EE) = average Δ across all targets for a fixed extractor, and Target Evolvability (TE) = average Δ across all extractors for a fixed target. The extraction framework intentionally uses minimal structure with no domain heuristics, leaving all abstraction decisions to the extractor model itself so that performance differences are attributable to extractor capability rather than pipeline engineering. Critical design decisions: (1) consolidating all experience into a single domain-level skill — well-justified for causal attribution but explicitly limits ecological validity; (2) minimal extraction scaffold — appropriate for isolating extractor differences but means the pipeline may underperform more engineered alternatives; (3) downstream Δ as the sole quality metric — well-justified by the finding that text-based quality signals are unreliable. The rubric discovery pipeline analyzes 17 high-gap skill pairs (same target, same domain, |Δ_A − Δ_B| ≥ 0.5pp), extracts per-pair differences via GPT-5.4, consolidates them into 7 candidate dimensions, and validates each by better-rate (proportion of pairs where the higher-Δ skill scores better on that dimension); three dimensions with better-rates 64–66% form the validated rubric. Key Results Across 5 domains, 6 targets, and 5 extractors, model-generated skills improve performance in 75% of extractor-target pairings and degrade it in 25%, with domain-level negative transfer rates ranging from 13% (SpreadsheetBench, SWE-bench-Verified) to 47% (ALFWorld). Extraction Efficacy is uncorrelated with model capability: on SpreadsheetBench, Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite achieves the highest EE (+5.86 pp) while GPT-5.4 — the strongest task-performer — ranks last (+1.67 pp). Target Evolvability varies sharply within domains: on ALFWorld, GPT-5.4 reaches TE = +4.93 while Qwen3.5-9B reaches TE = −1.69 from the same set of extractors. Experience composition studies (3 domains, 5 success ratios) show pure-failure pools are consistently worst; the optimal mix is domain-specific (ALFWorld peaks at failure-heavy pools due to informative dead-end states; SpreadsheetBench peaks near all-success). Format normalization (Friedman test) yields p > 0.34 for all 4 formats on all 6 targets (no detectable effect), while switching extractors yields p < 0.005 for 5/6 targets. LLM pairwise judgment without rubric achieves 46.4% accuracy overall, declining to 15.8% on high-gap pairs (δ ≥ 5pp) — the fluent skill is the worse performer. The validated meta-skill improves all 9 evaluation cells (+1.55 pp average, largest +3.7 pp on SpreadsheetBench); the 7-dimension plausibility rubric hurts 6/9 cells (−0.59 pp average). Validated rubric raises pairwise judge accuracy from 46.4% to 73.8%.
