Alternative harness validation (Claude Code, Codex) on SpreadsheetBench yields consistent directional results (+0.4 pp average), confirming results are not artifacts of the Python-script harness. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW METHOD. The three-stage lifecycle analysis spanning extractors, targets, and domains is genuinely novel in scope — no prior benchmark crosses all three stages with domain-level, model-generated skills. The finding that LLM judgment of skill quality inversely predicts actual utility at high performance gaps (15.8% accuracy at δ ≥ 5pp) is a striking empirical result with direct practical consequences. The automated rubric-discovery and validation pipeline that closes the loop from diagnostic analysis to a concrete extraction improvement is a methodologically novel contribution. The meta-skill itself is a straightforward prompt-engineering intervention, not a novel algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility All main table entries are averaged over 3 independent evaluation runs. The format experiment uses the Friedman test with σ-ratio effect size, providing a principled statistical basis. The experience composition study holds the extractor fixed and manipulates only the success-failure ratio, cleanly isolating one variable. Pairwise skill evaluation uses 9-vote majority voting with randomized presentation order to mitigate position bias, on 151 pairs exceeding a 0.5 pp gap threshold. Code is released at https://aka.ms/SkillLens. However, no confidence intervals or error bars appear in Table 1, making it difficult to determine whether individual small Δ values (e.g., ±0.5 pp) exceed run-to-run noise. The validated rubric was derived using pairs from the same experimental matrix it is subsequently tested on (the better-rate evaluation and the meta-skill evaluation draw from the same 9 (domain, target) cells), raising the risk of overfitting the rubric to the specific experimental distribution. No held-out domain is used to confirm generalizability of the rubric. This is an arXiv preprint (May 2026) without peer review; results should be treated with the corresponding confidence level. Prompt templates and hyperparameters are fully documented in appendices (B–H). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The extraction pipeline runs on API-accessible frontier models or locally served open-source models (Qwen3.5-35B, Qwen3.5-9B on 8 NVIDIA B200 GPUs via vLLM); the meta-skill is a prompt insertion requiring no additional infrastructure. - Efficient: Per-trajectory extraction is embarrassingly parallel; hierarchical consolidation scales as O(n log n) in trajectory count with fixed group size; the meta-skill adds zero inference-time compute overhead. - Effective: The meta-skill improves downstream performance in all 9 evaluated cells, but absolute effect sizes are modest (+1.55 pp average); the core finding that 25% of pairings exhibit negative transfer is robustly replicated across two alternative harnesses (Claude Code, Codex). - Scalable: The study consolidates all experience into a single domain-level skill and evaluates only in-distribution held-out tasks; performance with multi-skill libraries, larger experience pools, or cross-domain transfer is not evaluated and is explicitly deferred to future work — scalability claims are not substantiated experimentally. Software artifact: code available at https://aka.ms/SkillLens. Strengths - The controlled cross-matrix design (6 targets × 5 extractors × 5 domains) is the most comprehensive evaluation of the skill lifecycle to date and surfaces non-obvious empirical patterns — particularly that extraction capability is a distinct, non-monotone function of model capability across all domains tested. - The quantified finding that LLM judgment of skill quality inversely predicts actual utility at high performance gaps (15.8% accuracy at δ ≥ 5pp) is concrete, surprising, and practically consequential: it invalidates the common assumption that a capable LLM can screen a skill library reliably. - The automated rubric-discovery pipeline (contrastive pair analysis → candidate dimensions → better-rate validation → end-to-end meta-skill test) is methodologically coherent and avoids purely post-hoc rationalization by testing the rubric in downstream extraction rather than only in pairwise judgment. - The experience composition study (Section 5.1) cleanly isolates the effect of success-failure pool composition, yielding directly actionable guidance: all-failure pools are universally worst, and the optimal mix is domain-specific for identifiable reasons (ALFWorld failures reveal dead-end states; SpreadsheetBench successes encode stable procedural strategies). Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly and directly acknowledge that consolidating all experience into a single domain-level skill injected via system prompt is a deliberate interpretability choice that sacrifices coverage — it excludes retrieval, multi-skill libraries, planning scaffolds, and skill interference. They frame scaling to richer harnesses and larger skill libraries as future work rather than a gap, and acknowledge that skills extracted from imperfect experience pools may carry biases or unsafe shortcuts that become harder to audit at scale. This transparent scoping is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The validated rubric was derived from pairs drawn from the same 3-domain, 3-target cells used for the meta-skill evaluation; no held-out domain tests whether the three dimensions generalize to BFCL or SEAL-0, where experience pool dynamics (tool-calling, multi-hop retrieval) differ substantially from the derivation domains. - No statistical significance tests appear for the main Δ matrix (Table 1); with only 3 evaluation runs per cell, many small positive and negative Δ values could fall within run-to-run noise, and the reported 75%/25% positive/negative split may shift under more runs. - The study does not report experience pool sizes per (target, domain) pair or investigate sensitivity to pool size — a critical practical variable, since experience collection is the most expensive step and pool sizes presumably vary widely across domains. - The experience generator and skill consumer are the same model M, confounding pool quality with target capability; a stronger design would decouple them (e.g., generate experience with a fixed strong model and vary only the extractor and consumer). - The behavioral impact analysis (Appendix D) characterizing why skill consumption helps GPT-5.4 and hurts Qwen3.5-9B is qualitative trajectory inspection on one domain (SpreadsheetBench); it is descriptive rather than causal and is not replicated across other domains or targets. Bottom Line Practitioners building model-generated skill libraries should read this paper — it provides the first rigorous evidence that extraction capability is model-specific and uncorrelated with task strength, that textual plausibility is a misleading quality signal, and that inserting a validated 3-dimension rubric into any extractor's system prompt measurably reduces negative transfer without modifying the underlying pipeline.
Recommended 2605.23899 PDF
#17 — Innovative (74)

Your Eyes Won't Lie: Snooping Online Voting Privacy from User Webcam

par.nsf.gov · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces an end-to-end attack system that infers a user's secret online voting choice from standard webcam video streams by analyzing eye-movement behaviors — specifically hand-eye coordination patterns — rather than estimating on-screen gaze points. - Why it's important: Online voting in video conferences relies on continuous webcam streaming to authenticate voters and prevent coercion, yet this very stream can be exploited by any co-participant to silently break ballot anonymity without malware, special hardware, or victim-specific training data. - How it does it: The system bypasses the fundamental resolution barrier of gaze-point inference (choices separated by ~1px at webcam distance) by instead detecting the distinct eye-motion signatures — variance spikes, fixation transitions, eyelid dynamics — that arise when hand-eye coordination locks onto a specific checkbox and confirm button. - The good: The attacker's model (zero victim training data, trained only on other participants) still achieves 90.2% accuracy on binary horizontal-layout polls, rising to 94.2% with a handful of victim sessions added, demonstrating that voting-related hand-eye coordination is genuinely cross-user generalizable. - The missing: The study uses 19 participants in a narrow age band (20–35), all university-affiliated, and the train/test split is within the same small homogeneous pool, leaving the cross-demographic generalizability of the behavioral signal entirely untested. - Read it or not: Security and privacy researchers working on webcam side-channels, online voting systems, or behavioral biometrics should read this; it opens a credible new attack class, though practitioners should note the limited participant pool before drawing deployment conclusions.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (82) and Non-Obviousness (85) are the top contributors: the work opens a NEW PROBLEM (semantic vote leakage from face-video) and makes the genuinely surprising pivot from physically-impossible gaze-point inference to cross-user hand-eye-coordination signatures, with the zero-victim-data 90.2% result falsifying the intuition that behavioral variation blocks generalization. Rigor (68) is held down by 19 homogeneous participants, no error bars or significance tests, and no released code, though the leave-one-out design, broad ablation sweep, IRB handling, and IEEE S&P 2026 peer review are real positives. Completeness (72) benefits from directly-confronted limitations. Composite: 0.20*82 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*85 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*65 ≈ 74.
empirical Novelty 82 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 85 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Organizations increasingly conduct votes — faculty hiring, tenure decisions, policy ballots — inside Zoom meetings using either built-in polling (Zoom Polls) or external web forms (SurveyMonkey). Platforms stream the voter's face throughout to provide continuous authentication and coercion resistance. The implicit assumption is that the camera captures only the face while the screen remains private. The gap this paper closes is the realization that the face-video stream leaks a second signal: the eye-motion pattern that is causally tied to which checkbox the user selects and confirms. Prior webcam side-channel work exploited eyeglass reflections (requiring strict optical alignment, affecting only 22.4% of sessions) or arm-motion vibrations (barely above random for mouse clicks). Gaze-point estimation is categorically ruled out at webcam resolution: choices separated by 1.47 cm on a 1080p display correspond to less than 1 pixel of eye displacement in a 720p webcam frame at 55 cm eye-camera distance. The paper identifies and exploits the gap between where users look (too coarse to resolve) and how they look (hand-eye coordination signatures that are choice-specific and cross-user consistent). Key Idea: The core insight is that selecting a specific checkbox forces a stereotyped sequence of eye motions — scan to find the choice, move with the cursor toward the checkbox, dwell and confirm — that produces a characteristic temporal signature in the moving variance of pupil position, not in the absolute gaze location. Because these kinematics are driven by the physical layout of the form (which the attacker knows, since the form is shared on screen) and by universal hand-eye coordination mechanics, they are consistent enough across people that a model trained on one set of individuals predicts another's choices without calibration. Methodology: The pipeline has four stages. First, MediaPipe extracts 479 facial landmarks per frame, and MPIIFaceGaze estimates 3D gaze angle and head orientation; landmarks are normalized by centering on the midpoint between inner eye corners and scaling by the inter-eye distance to remove head-movement noise. Second, voting-action segmentation: peaks in the root-mean-square moving variance of pupil position detect the two critical gaze-shift moments (instruction-to-choices, choices-to-confirm-button), dividing each voting session into five overlapping temporal parts. Third, a 39-dimensional feature vector is derived per frame: statistical eye motion (mean, median, variance, SD over a sliding window), fixation-point-of-eye-position (FPOEP, dispersion-threshold gaze fixations), 5D gaze angle + head orientation, eyelid open height, and eye-open area. Fourth, a dedicated Transformer encoder (time-series projection → multi-head self-attention → residual + batch norm → RELU → dense) processes each of the five temporal parts independently, and their softmax outputs are fused via probability-based integration (summation or a lightweight SVM). Three attack models cover the spectrum of victim-data availability: Victim Model (victim-only data, 85.6%), General Model (other users + some victim data, 94.2%), and Attacker's Model (no victim data, 90.2%). Design decisions: (1) Behavioral features over gaze points — well-justified by the physics of the resolution barrier and confirmed empirically by t-SNE separation. (2) Multi-part temporal segmentation — justified by ablation showing Part 2–5 fusion (96.1%) outperforms the whole-session baseline (94.2%). (3) Transformer over CNN/SVM/LightGBM — justified by ablation comparing all four architectures, though differences are moderate (transformer leads by 1–12 points depending on layout). Key Results: Baseline accuracy is 96.6% on SurveyMonkey horizontal layout and 94.2% on Zoom polling horizontal layout, against a 50% random baseline (binary Yes/No). Vertical layouts degrade to 87.6% (web) and 75.9% (Zoom). Five-choice vertical top-1 accuracy falls to 47.6%, but top-3 candidate accuracy recovers to 88.4%. The attacker's model (zero victim data) achieves 90.2%, rising to 92.1% with one victim session. Laptop setup (shorter eye-camera distance) yields 95.8% vs 94.2% for desktop. Accuracy is nearly invariant to lighting (94.2% office vs 94.1% dark room) and video quality (94.2% at 720p60fps vs 94.0% at 360p30fps). Eyeglasses reduce accuracy by ~5–13 points but do not break the attack. These results are from 19 participants (6F/13M, ages 20–35, 9 with glasses), split 50/50 train/test within the same pool. The study is peer-reviewed and published at IEEE S&P 2026. Novelty Classification: NEW PROBLEM + NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The novel problem is semantic privacy leakage from natural eye motions during online voting via standard webcam streams. The novel method is the shift from gaze-point inference to behavioral hand-eye-coordination inference, including the temporal segmentation heuristic and multi-part transformer ensemble. The novel empirical finding is that hand-eye coordination during checkbox selection is sufficiently stereotyped across users to enable cross-person generalization without calibration. The underlying components (MediaPipe, MPIIFaceGaze, Transformers for time series) are established tools applied in a new attack context. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: the five temporal parts are individually evaluated, three attack models are compared, four classifiers are compared, and the effect of choices, layouts, lighting, resolution, camera height, eyeglasses, and device type are each tested. No error bars, confidence intervals, or statistical significance tests are reported for any result — all outcomes are single-point estimates. With only 19 participants split 50/50, the effective per-class training sample counts are small. The train/test split is within-pool: models are trained on some participants and tested on others from the same 19-person cohort, making the generalization claim dependent on a homogeneous sample. No code, dataset, or model weights are released. IRB approval is stated and raw videos are deleted post-feature extraction. Vulnerability disclosure was made to Zoom, Teams, Google Meet, and Webex; Microsoft Teams and Cisco Webex responded. A key methodological concern: the Already-Know condition (participants told which choice to make before the question appears) is designed to produce minimal cognitive variance; real voting decisions involve deliberation that the Need-to-Think proxy (two-decimal math) only partially captures, and the 2% performance gap between the two suggests cognitive noise is real but modest in this setup. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (end-to-end attack system evaluated on real Zoom sessions). - Practical: The attack requires only the video stream already available to every meeting participant; no special hardware, calibration, or software installation on the victim's machine is needed. - Efficient: Inference runs on the attacker's own machine post-hoc on recorded video streams; MediaPipe and MPIIFaceGaze are real-time capable on commodity hardware, though the paper does not report specific inference latency or compute cost. - Effective: The evidence shows the attack works on real Zoom and SurveyMonkey sessions at 720p under typical office conditions, but effectiveness degrades substantially for vertical layouts with more than two choices, and the participant pool is too narrow to claim broad effectiveness. - Scalable: The attack requires knowing the form layout in advance and that the form pops up synchronously for all participants — conditions satisfied by video-conference polling but not by asynchronous web forms; the paper does not test beyond 5 choices or beyond 19 demographically similar participants. The work delivers an attack pipeline (code not released), not a library or tool. Strengths: - The fundamental framing pivot — from gaze-point localization (physically impossible at webcam resolution) to behavioral hand-eye-coordination classification — is the right insight and is rigorously justified with both optical analysis and empirical comparisons to three state-of-the-art eye trackers. - The attacker's model result (90.2% with zero victim data) is the strongest and most surprising finding; it falsifies the intuition that individual behavioral variation would block cross-person generalization and has direct practical implications. - The ablation sweep is unusually broad for a security paper: lighting, resolution, camera height, device type, eyeglasses, layout, number of choices, cognitive load, and three attack training regimes are all evaluated empirically rather than just discussed. - Responsible disclosure to four major video conferencing vendors is documented, and the IRB-approved data handling (raw video deletion, hashed anonymization) reflects appropriate ethics practice. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly state three scope boundaries: (1) attacker access to video streams requires either conference participation or malware (honestly characterized); (2) the participant pool is limited to ages 20–35 with university education, and generalization to older or more demographically diverse populations requires further study; (3) complex form types (multi-select, scroll, matrix) are not studied. These are confronted directly rather than minimized, which is a credibility positive. The authors also acknowledge that the malware scenario loses the coarse synchronization that video conferencing provides, requiring further temporal disambiguation work. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The train/test split draws from the same 19-person pool. The Attacker's Model is trained on N-1 participants and tested on the held-out participant, which is the correct leave-one-out design, but 19 subjects is too few to characterize the tails of behavioral variation. The reported accuracy numbers should be treated as upper bounds for a similarly homogeneous population. - No error bars or significance tests appear anywhere in the paper. With 90 voting sessions per participant per layout and only 19 participants, session-level variance across participants could be substantial, and the single-point accuracy figures conceal this. - The paper assumes the attacker knows the exact form layout and choice ordering. While this is justified for synchronized video-conference polls, it is a non-trivial prerequisite; the paper does not study the attack's robustness when the attacker has only approximate layout knowledge. - The choice randomization procedure eliminates some bias, but the Already-Know condition still uses choices labeled "AAA", "BBB", "CCC" (not real candidates) — the paper provides no evidence that the eye-motion patterns transfer to real voting decisions involving meaningful content and variable deliberation time. - The defense of randomizing choice order is proposed but never attacked: the authors do not evaluate whether the proposed transformer, trained on one ordering, degrades when choices are shuffled per session. This is the primary recommended defense, yet its efficacy is unquantified. - The paper reports no cross-cultural or cross-language generalization data; hand-eye coordination during mouse-click tasks may differ across input device conventions (e.g., touchpad vs mouse), and the paper does not test touchpad users separately despite testing laptop vs desktop. Bottom Line: This is a solid and well-motivated security paper that credibly demonstrates a previously unrecognized privacy threat in video-conference voting; security researchers studying webcam side-channels should read it, but the 19-participant homogeneous sample and absent statistical testing mean the headline accuracy numbers should not yet be cited as deployment-grade ground truth.
Search Results
#18 — Innovative (74)

TriSplat: Simulation-Ready Feed-Forward 3D Scene Reconstruction (34 ▲ )

Weijie Wang, Zimu Li, Jinchuan Shi, Zeyu Zhang, Botao Ye, Marc Pollefeys, Donny Y. Chen, Bohan Zhuang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TriSplat, a feed-forward 3D reconstruction network that represents scenes as oriented triangle primitives and directly exports simulation-ready triangle meshes from sparse, unposed images in a single forward pass. - Why it's important: Existing Gaussian splatting feed-forward methods cannot deliver meshes directly to physics engines — they require expensive post-hoc TSDF fusion that degrades geometry and adds latency, blocking practical use in robotics, simulation, and embodied AI. - How it does it: Triangle orientation is anchored to dense point-map geometry (refined by a U-Net) rather than learned as a free latent, and a mono-normal bootstrap schedule warm-starts training before the point maps converge, together with opacity and blur scheduling that transitions from soft to crisp primitives. - The good: On DL3DV (6 views) TriSplat achieves mesh-rendering PSNR of 20.84 dB versus 18.88 dB for the strongest Gaussian baseline, and end-to-end mesh production time of 0.57 s versus 18.7 s for the fastest competitor (33× speedup), with the advantage growing structurally as view count increases. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to three indoor/real-estate datasets; there is no test on outdoor unbounded scenes or autonomous-driving scenarios where simulation-readiness is equally critical, and the non-manifold triangle-soup output is not usable for finite-element analysis or watertight mesh applications. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone building feed-forward 3D reconstruction systems that need to interface with physics engines or standard graphics pipelines; skippable for those only interested in novel-view synthesis quality.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (a new method with a strong systems/deployment angle, but the core contribution is methodological). Non-Obviousness (80) and Novelty (78) drive the score — lifting triangle splatting from per-scene optimization into a pose-free feed-forward regime via geometry-anchored normals plus a mono-normal bootstrap operating in the forward pass is a non-obvious solution to the orientation-brittleness problem, and mesh export becomes identity-trivial. Rigor (70) is held back by single-run results with no error bars, unconfirmed code/weight release, and a fair-but-asymmetric mesh-rendering protocol that structurally favors triangle-native output over TSDF-fused Gaussian baselines. Practical Impact (78) is strong: 33x faster time-to-mesh with demonstrated loading into Unity and Isaac Sim. Completeness (72) and Resilience (70) are good — limitations (non-manifold triangle soup, resolution-tied topology) are confronted honestly, but indoor/real-estate-only evaluation and the TSDF asymmetry leave open challenges. Weighted: 0.20*78 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*78 = 74.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Robotics, augmented reality, and embodied AI require 3D scene representations that support physics simulation, collision detection, and contact-rich planning — all of which depend on explicit triangle meshes consumable by engines like NVIDIA Isaac Sim, Unity, and Unreal. Current feed-forward reconstruction models based on Gaussian splatting produce high-quality novel-view synthesis but represent surfaces only implicitly. Extracting a usable mesh requires TSDF fusion or Poisson reconstruction — per-scene post-processing that is slow, discards the actual rendering primitives, and introduces surface degradation. Pose-free methods compound this by requiring joint estimation of geometry and cameras, with no existing approach producing meshes natively in a single forward pass from unposed sparse inputs. Key Idea The core insight is that triangle splatting — a differentiable rasterizer that operates on oriented planar triangle primitives rather than volumetric Gaussians — can be lifted from per-scene optimization into a feed-forward, pose-free regime if (and only if) triangle orientation is constrained to local surface geometry rather than treated as an unconstrained latent variable. A triangle 1° misoriented creates hard-edged rasterization artifacts proportional to that error; a Gaussian at the same angle produces a plausible soft footprint. Anchoring orientation to finite-difference normals derived from predicted point maps, then refining them with an image-conditioned U-Net and bootstrapping from a monocular teacher during early training, converts an inherently brittle primitive into a trainable one — and because rendering already produces oriented triangles in world space, mesh export is identity-level trivial. Methodology TriSplat builds on a DINOv2 ViT-L/14 encoder with 2D rotary position embeddings and a transformer decoder that alternates intra-view self-attention and cross-view joint attention. Three parallel heads decode to (i) dense point maps per pixel (depth as exp(z') for strict positivity, lateral coordinates unconstrained), (ii) SE(3) camera poses per view via SVD-orthogonalized regression with scheduled sampling to prevent distribution shift, and (iii) per-pixel triangle attributes (density logit, 3 scale logits, quaternion, spherical-harmonic appearance, blur). Triangle orientation is derived by: computing finite-difference geometry normals from the point map; smoothing with an orientation-aware box filter; refining via a lightweight 4-scale U-Net conditioned on raw/smoothed normals, RGB, depth, and validity mask (zero-initialized for identity start); blending the result with offline monocular teacher normals (Omnidata) via a cosine-decaying schedule across three phases (takeover → blend → release); and converting the blended normal to an orthonormal tangent frame for triangle parameterization. Progressive sharpening schedules opacity exponent and blur magnitude together from soft to crisp over training. The loss is a sum of photometric (MSE + LPIPS), pairwise relative pose (Huber translation + angular rotation), and normal cosine-similarity terms. Critical design decisions: (a) zero-initialization of the normal refinement U-Net — justified explicitly to prevent early-stage orientation destruction; (b) mono-normal bootstrap operating on the forward-pass representation rather than as a loss term, so teacher normals shape all downstream gradients; (c) pairwise rather than absolute pose loss for frame-invariance. All three are validated by ablation. Key Results On DL3DV (6/12/24 input views), TriSplat achieves surface F1 of 0.287/0.279/0.277 versus 0.145/0.152/0.156 for DepthSplat (next best at 6 views), and mesh-rendering PSNR of 20.84/18.71/18.06 dB versus 18.88/16.90/16.71 dB for YoNoSplat (next best). On RealEstate10K (6 views), mesh-rendering PSNR reaches 24.69 dB against 21.94 dB for the strongest Gaussian baseline (+2.75 dB). Zero-shot on ScanNet: AbsRel 0.188, AbsDiff 0.341 (best), mean normal error 27.9° and <30° accuracy 71.7% (versus 54.1° and 41.0% for the next best). End-to-end time-to-mesh is 0.57/0.62/1.23 s at 6/12/24 views on a single H100, versus 18.7/28.6/33.0 s for AnySplat (33× to 27× faster) and up to 306 s for DepthSplat. Baselines are current and appropriate: MVSplat, DepthSplat, AnySplat, YoNoSplat, MeshSplat, SurfelSplat. The paper qualitatively demonstrates mesh loading into Unity and NVIDIA Isaac Sim for locomotion, grasping, and rigid-body simulation. Ablations on RE10K show each component contributes roughly 1–1.6 dB PSNR and 0.057–0.065 F1; the mono-normal bootstrap produces the largest surface gain (F1 −0.065) and normal refinement the largest rendering gain (PSNR −1.58 dB). Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel contribution is lifting triangle splatting from per-scene optimization into a pose-free feed-forward regime via geometry-anchored normal construction, mono-normal bootstrapping, and progressive sharpening. The DINOv2 backbone, transformer decoder, and spherical-harmonic appearance model are standard components from the Gaussian feed-forward literature (YoNoSplat architecture); the triangle primitive renderer is adopted from Triangle Splatting (CVPR, cited as [30]). The novelty is in making those primitives trainable in this setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and meaningful: four components ablated individually on RE10K with both geometry (CD, F1) and rendering (PSNR, LPIPS) metrics, showing separable contributions. No error bars or significance tests are reported. The paper states a project page (lhmd.top/trisplat) exists but does not explicitly confirm code or model weight release. Evaluation protocol is detailed in appendices (J for surface metrics). The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.26115v1, May 25, 2026); results have not undergone peer review. The comparison uses the same mesh-rendering protocol for all methods, which is a fair and non-trivial methodological choice that exposes a structural disadvantage of Gaussian baselines (TSDF discretization loss). One potential concern: TriSplat's native renderer and export are both triangle-based, while baselines use their native Gaussian renderer for primitive-mode but incur TSDF loss in mesh-mode — the performance gap may partly reflect this asymmetry rather than purely reconstruction quality. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (and SYSTEMS — a built model evaluated in simulation engines). - Practical: Training requires H100-class hardware (gradient checkpointing, bfloat16 mixed precision noted); inference at 0.57–1.23 s per scene on a single H100 is feasible for real-time robotics pipelines, though consumer GPU performance is not reported. - Efficient: 33×–249× faster time-to-mesh than Gaussian baselines at equivalent input sizes, and the speedup is structural (no TSDF step); memory cost of the DINOv2 ViT-L backbone plus three heads plus a U-Net is not quantified. - Effective: Evidence shows the exported mesh directly loads into Unity and Isaac Sim and supports locomotion and grasping tasks without manual cleanup; rendering quality on exported meshes substantially outperforms TSDF-fused baselines. Zero-shot generalization to ScanNet without fine-tuning confirms task-level robustness. - Scalable: Evaluated up to 24 input views; inference time scales near-linearly (0.57 to 1.23 s from 6 to 24 views). No test on scene scales beyond RE10K/DL3DV (no outdoor unbounded, autonomous-driving, or city-scale scenes). The non-manifold triangle-soup output topology is not scalable to watertight-mesh applications without additional processing. Delivers a trained model and a project page; code/weight availability not explicitly confirmed. Strengths - Structural elimination of TSDF fusion: the quality advantage in mesh rendering is not incidental but follows directly from the representation choice — triangles are the mesh, so no information is lost at export. - The mono-normal bootstrap is an unusually careful solution to the chicken-and-egg problem of geometry-driven orientation during early training, operating in the forward pass rather than as a loss term. - Comprehensive evaluation across three axes (surface quality, NVS under mesh rendering, depth/normal accuracy) with zero-shot cross-dataset generalization and real simulation-engine demonstration. - Ablation study is honest: each component's individual contribution is measured at both geometry and rendering quality, and no component is redundant. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (i) the output is a non-manifold triangle soup, not a watertight mesh, making it unsuitable for finite-element analysis; (ii) per-pixel triangle density is tied to input resolution, limiting topology control. They identify topology-aware export and adaptive tessellation as future directions. These are confronted directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All three evaluation datasets (RE10K, DL3DV, ScanNet) are indoor or real-estate scenes. The simulation-readiness claim is equally relevant for outdoor robotics (autonomous driving, drone navigation) but no such evaluation is provided. - Primitive-rendering quality (Gaussian-native rasterization vs. triangle-native rasterization, prior to mesh export) is relegated to an appendix; the main tables focus on mesh-rendering mode, which structurally favors TriSplat. A reader must check the appendix to understand how much TriSplat trades raw NVS quality for mesh quality in its native mode. - No ablation over training dataset size or backbone scale; it is unclear whether the gains persist with smaller backbones suitable for edge deployment. - Performance on single-GPU consumer hardware (A100, 4090) is not reported; all timing is on H100. - The non-manifold output is downplayed: for physics simulation, many engines do handle triangle soups, but robustness to degenerate triangles and winding-order errors in practice is not characterized quantitatively. Bottom Line TriSplat makes a clear and well-supported case that replacing Gaussian with triangle primitives in a feed-forward reconstruction network yields simultaneous gains in surface quality, mesh-rendering quality, and simulation readiness — a rare win across all three axes — and practitioners building embodied AI or robotics pipelines that need rapid feed-forward mesh reconstruction should read this work.
Recommended 2605.26115 PDF
#19 — Innovative (74)

Right-Sizing Communication and Recommendation Set Size in AI-Assisted Search

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a theoretical framework that jointly characterizes the optimal user message precision and AI recommendation set size in a high-dimensional product search model under mutual-information-based communication and search costs. - Why it's important: AI shopping assistants lack a principled theory for balancing how much preference detail to elicit from users versus how many options to return — misjudging this either overloads users with irrelevant choices or imposes excessive cognitive effort to specify preferences. - How it does it: A Large Deviation Principle (LDP) for the max-utility statistic over n i.i.d. von Mises-Fisher samples on S^{d-1} reduces the stochastic optimization to a deterministic problem as d→∞, yielding closed-form characterizations of optimal policy parameters and phase transitions. - The good: Under optimally tilted sampling, the paper proves a sharp, analytically exact phase transition: pure communication (single recommendation) is optimal when search cost exceeds communication cost, and pure search (no preference query) is optimal otherwise, with no hybrid regime — a clean, actionable result. - The missing: The model assumes uniformly distributed user preferences on a hypersphere, von Mises-Fisher messages, and perfect user identification of the best item in the recommendation set — none of which are validated against real user behavior or empirical recommendation logs. - Read it or not: Researchers in information-theoretic human-AI interaction, rational inattention economics, or recommendation systems theory should read this; ML practitioners seeking deployment guidance will find the stylized assumptions too far from practice to apply directly.
Why this rank: Scored using Theoretical profile. Novelty (78) and Non-Obviousness (80) carry the score — the first formal joint characterization of elicitation depth and recommendation-set size, with an elegant LDP reduction of an infinite-dimensional problem to a scalar tilt and a sharp, analytically exact phase transition. Rigor (70) reflects complete proofs via standard concentration tools but is reduced for preprint status and explicitly deferred tilted-sampling convergence results. Completeness (70) and Practical Impact (55) are limited because there is no empirical grounding and the work points to, but does not deliver, a deployable design lever. Weighted: 0.25*78 + 0.20*70 + 0.25*80 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.05*55 = 74.
theoretical Novelty 78 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation E-commerce platforms and AI shopping assistants face a structural design problem: the AI lacks direct access to user preferences, so users must communicate them (at cognitive cost), and the AI must return a recommendation set large enough to cover residual uncertainty (at search cost). Prior recommendation system research — collaborative filtering, LLM-based recommenders, RAG architectures — focuses on algorithmic quality but provides no principled theory for the joint design of elicitation depth and recommendation set size. The rational inattention literature (Sims 2003) models costly information acquisition in single-agent settings but has not been extended to two-sided human-AI interactions where the AI mediates between noisy user signals and a vast product space. The specific gap is: given the relative cognitive costs of communicating preferences versus searching a recommendation list, how should both be co-calibrated? Key Idea The core technical insight is that the maximum utility achievable by the best item among n i.i.d. draws from a von Mises-Fisher posterior on S^{d-1} satisfies a Large Deviation Principle as d→∞. When message precision scales as κ = ρ/(1−ρ²)·(d−3) and set size as n = exp(αd), the stochastic max-utility concentrates at a deterministic value f(ρ,α) defined by a constrained optimization with an explicit rate function I_ρ(w,x). This concentration converts the original stochastic optimization over (κ,n) into a clean deterministic 2D problem OPT_Joint over (ρ,α), enabling exact analytical characterization of optimal regimes. A secondary insight for the tilted sampling extension is that the infinite-dimensional optimization over sampling distributions reduces to a single scalar tilt parameter v, computable in closed form. Methodology The model places both user preferences h and products θ on the unit hypersphere S^{d-1} in R^d; utility is the dot product u(θ,h) = ⟨h,θ⟩. User preferences are uniform over S^{d-1}. The three-stage interaction is: (1) user sends message m ~ vMF(h,κ) — von Mises-Fisher with concentration κ — incurring communication cost λ_c·D_KL(posterior||prior), which simplifies to λ_c·(κ·E[W] − log C_d(0)/C_d(κ)); (2) agent draws n products i.i.d. from posterior (or a tilted distribution Q^Tilt = {θ = vm + √(1−v²)Y, Y ~ Unif(S(m))}); (3) user selects the best item at search cost λ_s·log n. The objective is P_d(κ,n) = E[max_i⟨h,θ_i⟩] − λ_s·log n − λ_c·KL. The LDP analysis decomposes each utility term into a parallel component W·W_i (fidelity to message) and an orthogonal component √(1−W²)·√(1−W_i²)·X_i (uncommunicated diversity), then exploits concentration of the maximum over exponentially many samples. Three design decisions are well-justified: (a) log(n) search cost via entropy of uniform prior over n items; (b) KL divergence communication cost from rational inattention; (c) i.i.d. posterior sampling as a model of autoregressive LLM generation. The asymptotic approximation error is Õ(d^{−1/2}), proved via Bakry-Émery theory and Herbst's argument. Key Results Under posterior sampling (Theorem 4, d→∞, costs λ_s = c_s/d, λ_c = c_c/d): a switching curve c̄_c(c_s) divides the cost-parameter plane into a hybrid regime (c_c < c̄_c(c_s)) requiring joint optimization of both ρ and α, and a pure-search regime (c_c > c̄_c(c_s)) where ρ = 0. Monotonicity holds: higher communication cost reduces ρ and increases α, and vice versa for search cost. No communication-only regime exists in the asymptotic limit except at boundary conditions; finite-d discretization (Proposition 6) introduces a communication-only regime (n=1) when λ_c is small due to the discrete jump in search cost from n=1 to n=2. Under tilted sampling (Theorem 8): optimal tilt v(ρ,α) = ρ/√(1−(1−ρ²)e^{−2α}), strictly greater than ρ for any finite n; sharp phase transition at c_c = c_s — pure communication (α=0, v=1) when c_s > c_c, pure search (ρ=0, v*=0) when c_s < c_c, with no hybrid regime. Tilted sampling strictly outperforms posterior sampling, with substantial payoff gains visible when c_s > c_c. Concrete numerical example: d=15, α=0.15 yields n=15 recommendations. Asymptotic policy achieves performance within O(√(log d/d)) of the true finite-d optimum (Theorem 5). Baselines compared: pure communication (n=1, optimize κ) and pure search (κ=0, optimize n) — both shown to be strictly suboptimal in the hybrid regime, with maximum incremental gains concentrated in a narrow band where c_c is smaller than but comparable to c_s. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION, NEW METHOD (theoretical). Genuinely novel contributions are: (1) the first formal model jointly characterizing optimal preference elicitation depth and recommendation set size under information-theoretic costs in high dimensions; (2) the LDP analysis applied to max-utility statistics of posterior samples on a hypersphere; (3) the closed-form optimal tilt parameter reducing an infinite-dimensional sampling optimization to a scalar design lever. The ingredients — vMF distributions, rational inattention KL costs, LDP theory, Bakry-Émery concentration — are individually known; the synthesis and the resulting structural phase-transition results are new. Rigor & Reproducibility Full proofs are provided in a 30-page appendix (Appendices D–G) covering all main theorems, using Bakry-Émery theory, Herbst sub-Gaussian argument, LDP rate function analysis, and Cramér's rule for monotonicity. Error bounds are explicit and proved. Monte Carlo simulations for d ∈ {5, 10, 15, 50} validate asymptotic convergence (Figure 6), with appropriate acknowledgment of sampling variability. No code or data released; this is a purely theoretical paper. No empirical user study or real-system validation. The three key modeling assumptions — uniform preferences, vMF messages, and perfect best-item identification by the user — are stated clearly but not empirically grounded. For the tilted distribution, the authors explicitly defer a full convergence result and performance-gap bound "in the interest of space," a gap relative to the posterior-sampling analysis. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.23944v1); peer-reviewed confidence is lower. Deployability (THEORETICAL) - Sound: Assumptions are explicit and stated upfront; proofs in the appendix appear complete and technically correct, using standard concentration tools correctly applied. The key assumption of i.i.d. posterior sampling as a model of LLM generation is a simplification acknowledged by the authors. - General: Results hold under d→∞ asymptotics with costs scaling as 1/d; the authors argue d=15 represents "effective preference complexity" for many product categories, but this claim is asserted, not validated — the actual effective dimensionality of user preferences in deployed systems is unknown. - Tight: The Õ(d^{−1/2}) approximation error bound is proved but no matching lower bound is given; the switching curve c̄_c(c_s) under posterior sampling is not given in closed form (only the c_c > c_s condition for the search-only regime has a clean characterization). - Implementable: The tilted distribution result directly suggests a practical design: a single scalar temperature-like parameter v calibrated jointly to message precision and recommendation set size. No software artifact is produced; the paper stops at formal results and numerical illustrations. Strengths - The closed-form optimal tilt v*(ρ,α) = ρ/√(1−(1−ρ²)e^{−2α}) and the exact phase transition boundary c_c = c_s under tilted sampling are analytically exact, non-trivial results that directly inform AI system design (calibrate temperature jointly with query quality and set size). - The LDP reduction technique is technically elegant and non-trivial: it converts an infinite-dimensional stochastic optimization to a 2D deterministic problem with an explicit rate function, yielding insights unavailable from simulation alone. - The asymmetric comparison of posterior sampling (hybrid regime exists) vs. tilted sampling (pure policies only) reveals a qualitative architectural distinction between black-box and optimized agents, not merely a quantitative performance gap. - The weighted preferences extension (Appendix C) generalizes to heterogeneous feature subspaces with distinct per-group communication costs, showing that the framework can accommodate asymmetric feature importance — communication effort concentrates on describable dimensions while search covers latent ones. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge the model is "stylized" with a non-strategic, non-autonomous AI agent. They note that realizing tilted-sampling gains requires "a more sophisticated training and tuning pipeline." They explicitly defer the full convergence and performance-gap results for the tilted distribution case "in the interest of space." They note the large-d asymptotic nature of the results and provide d=15 as a numerical illustration without claiming it matches any specific real system. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No empirical validation: the cost structure (log search cost, KL communication cost) and utility function (dot-product alignment) are theoretically motivated but not calibrated to or validated against real user behavior, recommendation logs, or A/B test data. - The uniform preference distribution on S^{d-1} imposes rotational symmetry, meaning all product directions are a priori equally likely to be preferred — a strong idealization that eliminates preference heterogeneity across product categories. - Perfect user identification of the best item in a recommendation set (step iii of the model) ignores satisficing, cognitive overload, and noisy comparison — effects central to the behavioral economics literature the paper cites as motivation (Iyengar and Lepper 2000, Scheibehenne et al. 2010). - The i.i.d. sampling assumption precludes modeling deliberate diversity or coverage in recommendation sets, which practitioners engineer specifically to hedge against uncertain preferences. - The paper claims the tilt parameter is "analogous to temperature or retrieval-weighting in RAG pipelines" but establishes no formal connection between the mathematical tilting mechanism and the sampling mechanisms of actual deployed LLMs. - The switching curve c̄_c(c_s) under posterior sampling is characterized only via existence (Theorem 4) and illustrated numerically; no closed form is given, making it difficult to use quantitatively without running the asymptotic optimization. Bottom Line A technically rigorous theoretical contribution that delivers clean, directly actionable design prescriptions for AI recommendation systems — specifically, that an optimized agent should use a single context-dependent tilt parameter calibrated jointly to message quality and set size, with a sharp phase transition determining whether to elicit preferences or serve many options — but which requires empirical grounding before practitioners can trust its quantitative predictions.
Search Results 2605.23944 PDF
#20 — Innovative (73)

LocateAnything: Fast and High-Quality Vision-Language Grounding with Parallel Box Decoding (92 ▲ )

Shihao Wang, Shilong Liu, Yuanguo Kuang, Xinyu Wei, Yangzhou Liu, Zhiqi Li, Yunze Man, Guo Chen, Andrew Tao, Guilin Liu, Jan Kautz, Lei Zhang, Zhiding Yu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LocateAnything, a unified VLM-based visual detection and grounding framework that treats each bounding box as an atomic multi-token unit decoded in one parallel step, rather than serializing coordinates into a sequential 1D token stream. - Why it's important: Token-by-token coordinate generation is both a throughput bottleneck at inference time and a structural mismatch that prevents the model from learning the coupled geometry of a bounding box, limiting both speed and localization accuracy in latency-sensitive applications like robotics and embodied agents. - How it does it: A dual-formulation training strategy jointly optimizes a standard NTP loss and a box-aligned MTP loss on the same sequence using a specialized attention mask — causal for the NTP stream, block-causal across boxes with bidirectional attention inside each block — so the model learns to emit all four coordinates of a box simultaneously. - The good: A 3B model achieves 12.7 boxes/second (BPS) under hybrid mode on a single H100 — 2.5× the throughput of Rex-Omni (5.0 BPS) and 10× that of Qwen3-VL (1.1 BPS) — while simultaneously improving mean F1 over both on LVIS, COCO, VisDrone, DocLayNet, M6Doc, RefCOCOg, and ScreenSpot-Pro. - The missing: All throughput measurements use batch size 1 on a single H100; the throughput advantage of parallel decoding relative to sequential NTP methods at higher batch sizes (where KV-cache reuse patterns differ) is not evaluated, and the two hybrid-fallback thresholds (probability < 0.7, coordinate spread > 80) are presented without ablation. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners designing real-time grounding or detection pipelines on VLMs, and for researchers interested in the intersection of structured multi-token prediction and spatial coordinate generation; the ideas transfer to any task where the output has a known fixed-length structured unit.
Why this rank: Empirical profile (systems-leaning; a trained, deployable artifact). Rigor (75) and Impact (82) are the top contributors: the COCO-only ablation cleanly isolates the box-aligned MTP contribution from the 138M-sample data advantage, baselines are current (Qwen3-VL, Rex-Omni, DeepSeek-VL2), and code + models + dataset are released. Novelty (72) is a sharp NEW METHOD — aligning MTP block boundaries to the bounding box as the atomic unit — not a new formulation. The composite is held below 75 by batch-size-1-only throughput measurement (the central speed claim is untested where sequential NTP can overlap decode steps), the two unablated hybrid-fallback thresholds, and the bundling of data-scale and architecture effects in the headline results. Strongest paper in the cluster and the one most resembling the rigorous, deployable positive-calibration set.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 75 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 82
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-language models formulate visual grounding and detection as autoregressive token generation: a bounding box is serialized as either textual digits ("1", "0", "2", "4") or quantized coordinate tokens (x1→y1→x2→y2), each decoded one token at a time. This has two compounding costs. First, strictly sequential decoding creates an inference bottleneck that scales linearly with the total number of coordinate tokens: at 300 predicted boxes with 4 tokens each, the sequential overhead dominates latency. Second, the 1D serialization artificially decouples x1, y1, x2, y2, which are geometrically tightly coupled — the model must learn their joint distribution through co-occurrence statistics rather than direct structural supervision. General-purpose Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) methods exist but group tokens into arbitrary fixed-length chunks that cross box boundaries, introducing spurious correlations (e.g., learning dependencies between coordinate tokens from different objects or categories). The result is accuracy degradation plus only modest throughput gains from unaligned block sizes. The gap is: no method correctly aligns the MTP block boundary to the natural semantic unit of the bounding box. Key Idea: The core insight is that a bounding box is a natural atomic unit for multi-token prediction: its four coordinates are tightly coupled and their joint dimensionality is small and fixed (4 tokens + 2 structural delimiters = block size 6). By aligning MTP blocks exactly with box boundaries — what the authors call Parallel Box Decoding (PBD) — the model is trained to predict the full coordinate set simultaneously using bidirectional intra-block attention, while maintaining causal dependencies across boxes. This box-aligned supervision improves localization accuracy even in Slow (NTP) mode versus standard quantized NTP, not just throughput, because it enforces better geometric coherence in the learned representations. Methodology: LocateAnything builds on a Moon-ViT vision encoder and Qwen2.5 language decoder bridged by an MLP projector. Continuous coordinates are normalized to [0, 1000], discretized into tokens, and organized into fixed-length blocks B = (b1,...,bN) of length L=6. Four block types are defined: Semantic (natural-language identity), Box (four quantized coordinates), Negative (absent object), and End (termination). Unoccupied positions within a block use a <null> padding token. Training constructs a single concatenated input: x_vis ⊕ x_q ⊕ x_ntp ⊕ x_blk, where x_ntp is the standard token sequence and x_blk is the block-aligned reformulation of the same ground truth. Within each block in x_blk, the first token is retained as prediction context and all remaining positions are masked; the model predicts all masked positions simultaneously. Three attention regimes operate in parallel: (1) causal attention over x_vis, x_q, and x_ntp; (2) block-causal attention across blocks in x_blk (tokens in block i attend to all prior blocks but not future ones); (3) bidirectional attention within each block. The two streams are isolated to prevent data leakage. The joint objective is L = L_ntp + L_mtp with cross-entropy for both. This dual-loss design is well-justified by ablation: removing L_mtp from joint training drops Slow Mode from 52.1 to 50.1 F1 on COCO, and removing L_ntp drops Fast Mode F1 from 49.6 to 47.2. Three on-demand inference modes are provided: Slow (NTP, 3.9 BPS, 52.1 F1), Fast (MTP, 16.9 BPS, 49.6 F1), and Hybrid (MTP with NTP fallback, 13.2 BPS, 51.6 F1). The Hybrid fallback triggers on Format Irregularity (malformed block syntax, e.g., mixed structural and coordinate tokens) or Spatial Ambiguity (top-1 coordinate token probability < 0.7 AND max-min span of top-5 token IDs > 80 in the normalized coordinate space). When triggered, the offending block is discarded, and NTP regenerates it before resuming MTP. Training uses a 4-stage pipeline (world knowledge injection in 2 stages, then detection/grounding SFT in 2 stages), using up to 256 H100 GPUs. Stage 3 trains on 138M grounding/detection queries for 25,000 steps; Stage 4 upsamples dense-scene data for 5,000 additional steps. Stream Packing (best-fit decreasing heuristic for variable-length sequences, >95% packing efficiency) and MagiAttention (a distributed attention framework supporting heterogeneous masks) handle the engineering complexity of the dual-formulation training. The training dataset, LocateAnything-Data, comprises 12M unique images, 138M natural language queries, and 785M annotated bounding boxes. It spans: detection (66.9%), GUI/UI (16.5%), referring expression (7.3%), OCR (3.6%), document layout (3.5%), and pointing (2.2%). Key Results: All main results use the 3B parameter model in Hybrid Mode on a single H100 with batch size 1. The closest comparison throughout is Rex-Omni-3B, a recent VLM-based detection/grounding specialist that is the most direct prior work. General detection: LVIS mean F1 = 50.7 vs Rex-Omni 46.9 (+3.8); COCO mean F1 = 54.7 vs Rex-Omni 52.9 (+1.8). Note LVIS@IoU=0.95: 31.1 vs Rex-Omni 20.7 — PBD's geometric coherence substantially improves tight-IoU localization. Dense detection: VisDrone mean F1 = 39.9 vs Rex-Omni 35.8; Dense200 mean F1 = 58.7 vs Rex-Omni 58.3 (marginal). GUI grounding (ScreenSpot-Pro): 60.3 mean F1, exceeding GUI-Owl-32B (58.0, a 10× larger model), Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B (53.7), and Rex-Omni-3B (36.8). Icon localization (e.g., OS Icons: 43.8) appears strongest relative to prior work. Document layout: DocLayNet mean F1 = 76.8 vs Rex-Omni 70.7; M6Doc mean F1 = 70.1 vs Rex-Omni 55.6 (+14.5, largest single-task margin). Referring expression: RefCOCOg val mean F1 = 76.7 vs Rex-Omni 73.6; HumanRef mean F1 = 78.7 vs Rex-Omni 79.9 (slightly below). Throughput: Fast Mode at 300 predicted boxes achieves ~25 BPS vs ~4 BPS for quantized NTP — a 6× speedup in the dense case. Throughput gain is scene-density-dependent: approximately 2× at 20 boxes, 6× at 300 boxes. Baselines are current and include Qwen3-VL-4B/8B, DeepSeek-VL2, OVIS2.5, MiMo-VL, SEED1.5-VL, and Rex-Omni, all from 2024-2025. The throughput comparison includes only BPS under batch size 1 with no comparison at larger batch sizes or lower-end hardware. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuine novelty is PBD: aligning MTP block boundaries with the natural geometric unit (bounding box) rather than using arbitrary fixed-size token blocks. The architecture (Moon-ViT + Qwen2.5), training objective form (NTP + MTP joint loss), and inference fallback concept are all combinations of existing ideas; the key contribution is that where the block boundary falls matters for both throughput and accuracy. LocateAnything-Data is a new large-scale dataset, though it is an aggregation of existing datasets with new curation/filtering rather than novel data collection. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and targeted: coordinate representation comparison (Textual vs Quantized vs PBD Slow/Fast/Hybrid), MTP formulation comparison (SDLM-B4/B6/B8, Block Diffusion-B6 vs PBD Fast), decoding mode × loss design (3×3 ablation), and box output ordering (4 strategies). All ablations are conducted exclusively on COCO with models trained only on COCO, which correctly isolates PBD's architectural contribution from the 138M-sample data advantage. Error bars and significance tests are not reported; single-run results are standard practice in this subfield but limit statistical confidence. Code and models appear to be publicly available (GitHub, HuggingFace links on the paper cover page, though URLs are not transcribed in the PDF text layer). The two hybrid-fallback thresholds (0.7 and 80) are presented as design choices without ablation. Throughput measurement methodology (batch size, hardware, precision) is stated (BF16, H100, batch 1) which enables reproducibility of the speed claims, though only at that specific configuration. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (the paper trains and evaluates a system). Practical: The 3B model runs on a single H100 GPU in BF16; smaller configurations are not evaluated, and resource requirements for the custom MagiAttention training infrastructure are substantial (256 GPUs for 25,000 steps). Efficient: Hybrid Mode achieves 12.7 BPS — 2.5× the throughput of the nearest VLM-based specialist (Rex-Omni) — at similar parameter count; the dual-formulation training increases memory and sequence length overhead compared to standard NTP-only training, but this is a training-time cost not an inference cost. Effective: The evidence shows improvement on 7 of 8 benchmarks over Rex-Omni and SOTA on ScreenSpot-Pro against both specialists and much-larger generalist models; effectiveness for truly open-world or out-of-distribution scenarios beyond the evaluated domains is not directly tested. Scalable: The throughput advantage grows with box count (2× at 20 boxes, 6× at 300), directly addressing the scaling bottleneck of sequential decoding. Model scaling beyond 3B parameters is not evaluated; dataset scaling is demonstrated (138M queries versus much smaller prior datasets) but its independent contribution is not disentangled from PBD in the main results. The work delivers a trained model and code (GitHub + HuggingFace), making it a deployable artifact. Strengths: - The box-aligned MTP formulation is a precise, well-motivated fix to an identified structural mismatch in existing VLM grounding approaches; the ablation that PBD Slow Mode (52.1 F1) outperforms both Textual (49.1) and Quantized NTP (50.1) on COCO cleanly shows the supervision benefit is independent of throughput. - The hybrid fallback mechanism is practically grounded: it targets two specific failure modes (format irregularity and spatial ambiguity) with concrete runtime checks, achieving 51.6 F1 at 13.2 BPS — recovering most of the accuracy gap between Fast and Slow mode while retaining 78% of the throughput gain. - ScreenSpot-Pro result (60.3 mean F1, beating GUI-Owl-32B at 58.0 and Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B at 53.7) with a 3B model is a strong result in a competitive, practically relevant benchmark. - The ablation design — training all component comparisons solely on COCO — correctly separates the architectural contribution from the data-scale contribution, which is a methodological strength in a paper where both are introduced simultaneously. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note that the model is trained exclusively with supervised fine-tuning, and that reinforcement learning would be an important next step to optimize the block-level decoding policy, reduce fallback frequency in hard cases (dense/long-tail), and improve worst-case decoding speed. This is a substantive limitation confronted directly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - Throughput measurements use batch size 1 only. At batch sizes >1, sequential NTP can overlap decode steps across examples, potentially closing the gap with parallel block decoding; the practical throughput advantage in production batch-inference settings is unknown. - The two hybrid fallback thresholds (top-1 probability < 0.7, coordinate span > 80) are presented as hyperparameters without ablation. The sensitivity of Hybrid Mode F1 and BPS to these values is unclear, and the thresholds were presumably tuned on some evaluation data. - No comparison against strong non-generative detectors at the same 3B parameter scale with equivalent training data. The paper compares against Grounding DINO-Swin-T but not against modern DINO variants (e.g., DINO-Swin-L) or RT-DETR, which would clarify the accuracy cost of the generative paradigm. - The dataset scale contribution is not disentangled from PBD in the main results: LocateAnything-3B is trained on 138M queries while Rex-Omni is trained on substantially fewer. The COCO-only ablation isolates PBD's architecture, but the full-scale results bundle both effects. - This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.27365v2, May 2026); results have not undergone peer review. Bottom Line: LocateAnything makes a clean, well-ablated contribution — box-aligned MTP is a better-motivated and empirically stronger alternative to both standard NTP and structure-agnostic MTP for VLM-based grounding — and any practitioner deploying VLMs for real-time object detection, GUI grounding, or document layout should read this work.
Recommended 2605.27365 PDF
#21 — Innovative (73)

Ablate-to-Validate: Are Vision-Language Models Really Using Continuous Thought Tokens?

Tianyi Zhang, Mahtab Bigverdi, Ranjay Krishna · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Token Replacement Test (TRT), a standardized inference-time diagnostic that measures whether accuracy gains from continuous thought tokens in VLMs stem from actual token content or from incidental confounds like span position and token budget. - Why it's important: Many VLM papers report accuracy improvements from continuous visual tokens without distinguishing whether gains arise from reasoning over token content versus fixed span anchoring, extra context capacity, or training-time regularization — a gap that risks the field building on unvalidated assumptions. - How it does it: TRT replaces the intermediate token span with zero, random, distribution-matched random, first-repeat, and oracle vectors while holding prompt, image, token budget, and decoding procedure fixed, then measures downstream performance change. - The good: Across five diverse systems (LLaVA-13B and Qwen2.5-VL-3B trained from scratch, plus Mirage, Mull-Tokens, and CoVT off-the-shelf), continuous tokens are consistently insensitive to content replacement — random replacement changes accuracy by ≤1–2 points and oracle injection provides no headroom — while a parallel discrete-token control shows strong content dependence (oracle +9.4 points, random −19.9 points for Qwen), validating TRT's ability to detect real bottlenecks. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to relative depth and spatial reasoning tasks on small benchmarks (372–700 questions) with no confidence intervals; whether continuous tokens could carry genuinely useful content for richer semantic tasks remains untested. - Read it or not: Essential for anyone proposing or evaluating continuous thought tokens in VLMs — the TRT protocol is a reusable, low-cost diagnostic that should be standard practice, and the null result for continuous tokens is striking and credibly supported.
Why this rank: Empirical profile (weights 20/25/15/15/15/10). Non-Obviousness (80) and Novelty (75) are the engine: a standardized counterfactual diagnostic (TRT) plus the striking, content-insensitive null result for continuous thought tokens challenges an implicit assumption in the visual-thinking literature, and the discrete-token positive control (oracle +9.4, random −19.9) is genuine internal validation. Rigor (72) is held back from the top band by the absence of any confidence intervals or significance tests on benchmarks of only 372–700 questions where 1–2 point differences are 3–7 questions, plus preprint status (no peer review) and code-release still pending. Resilience (68) is the main drag: several conclusions rest on differences plausibly within noise, and utilization-vs-prediction of the auxiliary target is conflated, so a tough reviewer could press the 'within noise' objection hard. Impact (70) reflects a reusable, low-cost protocol and checkpoints but no released code yet. Composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*70 = 73.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation VLMs are increasingly augmented with auxiliary continuous or latent non-textual tokens intended to support visual reasoning. Systems like Mirage, CoVT, and Mull-Tokens report accuracy improvements from such tokens, but improved end-task accuracy is a weak signal: it cannot distinguish genuine information utilization from (1) span-position anchoring — the model learns that a fixed span location signals something regardless of content, (2) token-budget expansion — extra tokens increase effective context or compute capacity, or (3) training-time regularization — auxiliary objectives improve representations even if tokens are ignored at inference. No standardized diagnostic exists to distinguish these cases, leaving open whether continuous visual tokens are genuine information bottlenecks or merely structural scaffolding. Key Idea A model genuinely relying on visual token content should exhibit monotone sensitivity to content quality: corrupting token content should hurt performance, and injecting ground-truth oracle tokens should help. If performance is flat across zero, random, first-repeat, and oracle replacements while holding span position and budget constant, the model exploits incidental aspects of the span rather than its information content. This counterfactual intervention logic — systematically perturbing the proposed causal variable while holding everything else fixed — is the diagnostic core of the method. Methodology TRT operates at inference time: it intercepts the visual token span u_{1:K} immediately before consumption by the language backbone, replaces it with a chosen substitute, and measures task performance. Seven replacement types are defined: identity (sanity check), zero, random i.i.d. Gaussian, distribution-matched random (matching empirical mean and variance of actual tokens to control for scale), first-repeat (copies the first token K times to separate token presence from diversity), count-matched (for models without fixed budgets), and oracle/ground-truth injection. For a controlled testbed, the authors insert explicit depth-token spans into two trained VLM backbones (LLaVA-13B and Qwen2.5-VL-3B) and train them to both predict and consume depth embeddings extracted from frozen encoders (SigLIP2, CLIP, DINOv2). A learned depth projector P maps depth embeddings into LLM hidden size; a depth head H_d maps hidden states back to depth feature space. The joint loss is L = L_LM + λ_depth · L_depth, supervised only at depth span positions. A discrete baseline (VQ-VAE codebook indices, K=100, cross-entropy) serves as a positive control. TRT is also applied post-hoc to Mirage, Mull-Tokens, and CoVT by intercepting their latent token streams. Critical design decisions: strictly fixing K across all TRT ablations (required to isolate content from budget), using depth reasoning because it admits ground-truth oracle injection, and including distribution-matched random replacement to rule out scale confounds. Key Results On HardBLINK (372 questions, 3/4/5-point relative depth), auxiliary tokens generally match or slightly improve over no-aux baselines, but continuous tokens do not consistently outperform discrete: LLaVA-13B continuous best is 74.46% vs. no-aux 76.68% and discrete 77.69%; Qwen2.5-VL-3B continuous best is 68.55% vs. no-aux 58.87% and discrete 71.24%. The central TRT results: for continuous LLaVA-13B (SigLIP2, K=64), oracle injection gives 74.73% vs. identity 74.46% (+0.27), random gives 72.58% (−1.88), first-repeat gives 74.46% (unchanged). For continuous Qwen2.5-VL-3B (DINOv2, K=4), oracle gives 67.74% vs. identity 68.55% (oracle is slightly worse), random gives 67.20% (−1.35). By contrast, discrete Qwen2.5-VL-3B shows oracle 80.64% vs. identity 71.24% (+9.40) and random drops to 51.34% (−19.90). Off-the-shelf models: Mull-Tokens first-repeat exactly matches baseline on BLINK (63.71%) and SAT (76.33%); random changes ≤0.57 points. CoVT distribution-matched random gives 74.65% vs. identity 76.26% on CV-Bench-2D, while pure random noise collapses to 21.39%. Mirage shows content sensitivity only under complete zeroing (spatial planning 76.25% → 51.50%; depth 26.08% → 8.06%), with distribution-matched random recovering to 76.25% and 22.85% respectively — suggesting reliance on distributional statistics rather than fine-grained content. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD (diagnostic protocol) + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The Ablate-to-Validate principle and TRT are genuinely new as a standardized diagnostic toolset for continuous thought tokens — causal intervention logic exists in interpretability and probing literature, but applying it as a fixed, reproducible protocol with matched budget controls and oracle injection for visual token mechanisms is novel. The empirical finding that continuous tokens are broadly insensitive to content replacement across five diverse systems is a concrete, reproducible result that challenges implicit assumptions in the visual-thinking literature. The secondary finding that discrete tokens do act as meaningful information bottlenecks is also non-obvious. Rigor & Reproducibility The experimental design is strong: multiple replacement types address distinct alternative hypotheses, and the discrete-token positive control validates that TRT can detect genuine content utilization when it exists. A dedicated KV-cache ablation (Table 11) proactively rules out the objection that cached computation masks content sensitivity — content insensitivity holds under full recompute. Intermediate layer supervision and learning rate sensitivity ablations are included. Baseline reproductions (Table 10) verify published numbers within tolerance for all third-party systems. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported on any result, despite evaluation set sizes (372–700 questions) where 1–2% accuracy differences correspond to 3–7 questions and may be within noise. All results are point estimates. Code is promised at a project page whose URL is not provided in the paper. The paper is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2605.21642v1, May 2026) and has not been peer-reviewed. Deployability EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: All experiments run on commodity hardware (1 node, 8× L40s GPUs, 300GB RAM); inference-time TRT adds only extra forward passes with modified embeddings and requires no architectural changes. - Efficient: Training depth-span models is modest (10 epochs on 19k ADE20K images, ~10 wall-clock hours on 8 L40s); evaluation cost scales linearly with number of replacement types (~7× the cost of a standard eval run). - Effective: TRT correctly discriminates between systems that use content (discrete tokens) and systems that do not (continuous tokens across all tested setups), establishing face validity as a diagnostic. - Scalable: Experiments cover 3B–13B parameter models; behavior at 70B+ scale is untested and not claimed. The consistency of null results across architectures provides breadth, but production-scale VLMs may differ in ways not examined. The paper delivers a reusable diagnostic protocol and trained model checkpoints; code release is pending. Strengths - The controlled intervention design — fixing span position, budget, image, and decoding while varying only content — is methodologically correct for isolating the causal role of token content, and the first-repeat/count-matched variants thoughtfully disentangle budget from diversity effects. - The discrete-token positive control is the most important element: by showing TRT detects strong content dependence when discrete tokens are used (oracle +9.4 points, random −19.9 points for Qwen), the paper provides internal validation that the null result for continuous tokens is informative and not a methodological artifact. - Breadth of application across five models, two trained backbone families, six benchmarks, and four training paradigms substantially strengthens the generalizability claim compared to a single-system study. - The KV-cache ablation proactively addresses a non-obvious confound that a reviewer would naturally raise, and eliminates it cleanly. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge that the testbed is limited to depth reasoning and spatial tasks, that the discrete vs. continuous comparison is not a literal same-K comparison, and that continuous token insensitivity may arise from training dynamics or information-bottleneck mismatch rather than a fundamental incapacity of continuous representations. They explicitly do not claim that continuous tokens categorically cannot carry information, only that tested systems fail to use them as bottlenecks. These acknowledgments are direct and credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No confidence intervals or significance tests are reported despite evaluation sets small enough (372 questions) that 1–2 point differences represent 3–7 questions. Several conclusions are drawn from differences that may be within noise. - Depth reasoning is a narrow testbed: the correct answer to "which point is closer" may require only coarse ordinal structure, and a positional or distributional cue may suffice. It is untested whether continuous tokens would carry useful content for tasks requiring richer semantic reasoning (compositional scene understanding, visual math). Off-the-shelf model evaluations (BLINK, VSP, CV-Bench) also target spatial/perceptual skills rather than diverse semantic domains. - The discrete vs. continuous comparison conflates representation type with token budget: discrete uses K=100 fixed tokens while continuous uses K∈{4,16,64,full}. The stronger content sensitivity of discrete tokens may partly reflect richer budget (100 structured symbols) rather than an intrinsic advantage of discretization. - The "no depth loss" ablation (LLaVA 72.85% with no auxiliary supervision at all vs. continuous best 74.46%) suggests the continuous margin over unaugmented training is ~1.6 points, which is within noise without confidence intervals — this proximity is not analyzed. - No analysis of whether the auxiliary training objective was successfully learned (i.e., whether predicted continuous tokens correlate with ground-truth depth) — failure to utilize content and failure to predict accurate content are conflated in the framing. Bottom Line This preprint delivers a genuinely useful diagnostic protocol backed by a striking and credibly replicated empirical finding — continuous visual tokens are largely ignored at inference even when trained with explicit depth supervision — and the TRT framework should become standard practice for evaluating any new latent-token VLM mechanism.
Search Results 2605.21642 PDF
#22 — Innovative (73)

LoMo: Local Modality Substitution for Deeper Vision-Language Fusion (17 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 29 May 2026
