TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LoMo, a data curation method that converts text-only SFT samples into text–image–text interleaved sequences by rendering a middle span of the text prompt as an image, injecting implicit cross-modal alignment supervision into standard VLM fine-tuning without any architectural change. - Why it's important: Current VLMs drop 8.70–11.68 accuracy points when identical semantic content is delivered as a rendered image rather than text tokens, a fragility that undermines real-world robustness in document understanding, mixed-modality interfaces, and any deployment where linguistic content arrives through visual channels. - How it does it: By forcing the model to predict the same answer from a sequence where the semantically critical middle segment is a rendered image bracketed by text tokens, the standard NLL loss implicitly minimizes a KL divergence between the model's predictive distributions under textual and visual carriers — alignment becomes a task-level requirement with no extra objective. - The good: Under a rendered evaluation protocol (entire text question delivered as a pixel image), LoMo raises LLaVA-OV1.5-8B average accuracy across 13 benchmarks by +18.86 points (15.24→34.10), with DocVQA gaining +43.51, and a controlled ablation confirms that the gains are not attributable to simply having more multimodal training samples. - The missing: Evaluation is restricted to 8B–9B models trained for one epoch; whether gains persist or grow at 70B+ scale or after RL-based post-training, and whether the rendered test distribution matching the training renderer inflates rendered evaluation numbers, are not examined. - Read it or not: VLM practitioners who fine-tune on SFT corpora should read this — the method is plug-in compatible, introduces zero inference cost, and is supported by thorough ablations; the core idea is simple enough to implement in a weekend.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (78) and Completeness (75) lead: the clean KL-divergence decomposition showing NLL on substituted spans implicitly aligns text- and visual-carrier distributions is a non-obvious mechanistic insight, the counter-intuitive finding that Standard SFT actually pushes paired representations apart (0.52→0.57) is well-evidenced, and the controlled 1:1 image-to-text ratio ablation explicitly rules out the most obvious confound. Rigor (73) is strong on targeted ablations across two architectures but stops short of the top band: single-run, no error bars, and no comparison against existing cross-modal alignment baselines (VCD, HA-DPO). Resilience (70) is the chief drag — the rendered-eval gains share font/size/renderer between train and test, so distribution matching may inflate the dramatic +18.86 rendered improvement, an objection a reviewer would press. Composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*73 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*65 = 73.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 73 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Vision-language models (VLMs) treat text and image as modality-specific inputs — text as the linguistic query, images as the visual scene — because this is how virtually all standard training corpora (image captioning, VQA, OCR, web-interleaved data) are structured. This asymmetry means the model never needs to equate semantically identical content delivered as text tokens versus as pixel images. The practical symptom is severe: rendering the text question as an image while keeping all other inputs identical causes LLaVA-OV1.5-8B to drop 11.68 points, InternVL3.5-8B to drop 8.70 points, GLM-4.1V-9B to drop 10.09 points, and Qwen3.5-9B to drop 9.25 points across standard multimodal benchmarks. The authors measure this more precisely by extracting hidden states of text tokens and their rendered-image counterparts and computing cosine distances; accuracy drop correlates strictly monotonically with this cross-carrier distance, growing from 7.75% in the closest distance bin to 21.23% in the farthest, establishing a mechanistic link between representational distance and performance degradation. Key Idea The core insight is that the standard NLL fine-tuning objective, applied to an instance where a text span has been replaced by its rendered-image counterpart, implicitly decomposes into the standard SFT loss plus a KL divergence term between the model's predictive distribution conditioned on the original text and its distribution conditioned on the image-substituted version. No new loss function, no auxiliary head, and no contrastive pairs are needed; by merely reformatting the input while keeping the supervision target unchanged, cross-carrier representational alignment becomes a first-class task-level requirement. Methodology LoMo transforms a text-only instance (x, a) through three successive stages. (1) Structure-Aware Span Localization: For short inputs (≤3 sentences), the entire text is the target span. For longer inputs, a formula-aware chunker treats LaTeX expressions and common math commands as atomic units and extracts the middle one-third by character length, ensuring equation boundaries are never split. This produces (x_pre, x_mid, x_suf). (2) Visual Rendering: x_mid is routed to a LaTeX renderer if it contains mathematical expressions, otherwise to a standard text renderer (font size 20, Latin Modern Math at size 26); LaTeX failures automatically fall back to the text renderer. Margin trimming removes large whitespace. (3) Perceptual Distortion: One operation is sampled uniformly from {clean, rotate, blur, shadow/stain, wave} and applied to the rendered image, simulating real-world document capture noise. The final instance (x_pre, I', x_suf) replaces x while preserving the original answer a. Three critical design decisions are well-justified: the middle-span placement (ablation in Table 4 confirms +2.68 vs. +1.56 for prefix placement), the 50% rewrite ratio (Table 3 shows gains peak at 50% and fall to +1.80 at 100%), and formula-aware chunking (full-text rendering without span localization yields only +1.19 average gain, Table 2). Training uses 4M examples total (2M multimodal + 2M text-only, 50% of text-only rendered), LLaMA-Factory, FlashAttention 2, bf16, DeepSpeed ZeRO Stage 1, 8×H200 GPUs, AdamW with cosine schedule (4e-5 to 1e-6), 1 epoch, vision tower frozen. Key Results Under Standard Evaluation (text question delivered as text): LoMo improves LLaVA-OV1.5-8B by +2.68 average (40.88→43.56) and Qwen3.5-9B by +2.82 average (54.43→57.25) across 13 benchmarks. Largest single-task gains on LLaVA: CountBench +8.15 (42.97→51.12), V* +3.99 (47.71→51.70), MM-IFEval +3.21 (58.40→61.61). Largest on Qwen: MMMU-Pro +6.35 (39.83→46.18), MM-IFEval +5.49 (51.74→57.23), DocVQA +6.10 (85.89→91.99). Improvements appear in 23 of 26 comparisons; three marginal regressions (MMMU −0.56 on LLaVA; MathVista −0.60, CC-OCR −0.30 on Qwen). Under Rendered Evaluation (text question delivered as pixel image): gains expand to +18.86 on LLaVA (15.24→34.10) and +11.92 on Qwen (43.26→55.18), roughly 7× and 4× the standard gains. DocVQA: +43.51/+44.49; CC-OCR: +25.40/+16.54; CountBench: +34.82/+12.32. On Qwen, the Standard→Rendered accuracy gap compresses from 11.17 points (Standard SFT) to 2.07 points (LoMo). Alignment metrics at 4M scale: LoMo reduces MIR by 0.122 below Standard SFT and reduces pairwise cross-modal distance from 0.52 to 0.49, while Standard SFT increases it from 0.52 to 0.57. Pure-text benchmarks (MMLU-Pro, GSM8K, HumanEval, LiveCodeBench, IFEval): LoMo matches or slightly improves Standard SFT (+0.28 and +0.58 average), confirming no pure-text regression. The baseline — Standard SFT on the identical 4M-instance corpus — is well-matched; no comparison against other cross-modal alignment methods (VCD, HA-DPO) is provided. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The use of in-context rendered-text insertion as an implicit cross-carrier alignment mechanism during standard SFT is genuinely novel; prior text-as-pixels work (Pix2Struct, Glyph, DeepSeek-OCR) treats pixel rendering as an efficiency substitute for tokens, not as a complementary alignment signal within a single training instance. The KL-divergence interpretation of the implicit loss (Eq. 5–6) is a clean and non-obvious theoretical contribution. The monotonic correlation between pairwise cross-carrier representational distance and accuracy drop is a new empirical finding, though the general modality gap concept originates in CLIP-era work. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and well-targeted: component ablation ruling out full-text rendering and isolating the contribution of perceptual distortion (Table 2); rewrite ratio sweep over 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% (Table 3); rendering position comparison of prefix/middle/suffix/multi-span (Table 4); controlled image-to-text ratio comparison at matched 1:1 ratio (Table 5, +2.45 gain confirms the gains are not merely from more multimodal samples); data scale ablation from 1M to 4M (Figure 4). No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple seeds are reported anywhere — all results are single-run, which is standard in VLM fine-tuning but leaves borderline gains (1–2 points) unverified statistically. Two architecturally different backbones mitigate cherry-picking. No code, data, or model weights are stated to be released in the paper (a project page is listed but no explicit open-source statement). Evaluation uses deterministic decoding (T=0) via EvalScope, aiding reproducibility. No Goodhart's Law concern — benchmarks are standard held-out sets not used in training. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Data construction requires only CPU servers (two 128-core machines, ~20 hours for 4M examples); training runs on 8×H200 for one epoch — within reach of well-resourced labs and cloud budgets. Zero inference overhead. - Efficient: The rendering preprocessing is a one-time CPU cost; LoMo adds no new model parameters, no additional forward passes at inference, and minimal training compute overhead (interleaved tokens are a modest increase over text-only samples). - Effective: The controlled ratio ablation (Table 5) and component ablation (Table 2) together show that the gains are attributable to the interleaved cross-carrier formulation specifically, not to increased multimodal sample count or simple pixel exposure; 23/26 positive comparisons across two architectures is credible evidence. - Scalable: Tested only at 8B–9B scale; the authors explicitly disclaim verification at larger scales. Data scaling from 1M to 4M shows monotonically increasing benefit, but extrapolation beyond the tested range is unsupported by evidence. The work delivers a data curation procedure (reproducible from the paper's algorithm and hyperparameter appendix) but no software artifact, trained model weights, or released dataset. Strengths - The theoretical decomposition (Eq. 5–6) showing that NLL on LoMo-transformed data implicitly minimizes a KL divergence between text-carrier and visual-carrier predictive distributions is a clean, non-obvious insight that elevates this beyond a pure engineering contribution. - The controlled image-to-text ratio ablation (Table 5) explicitly anticipates and rules out the most obvious confound, demonstrating intellectual rigor that many data augmentation papers omit. - Architecture agnosticism and zero inference overhead make LoMo immediately applicable to any SFT pipeline; the data construction cost (~20 CPU-hours per 1M samples) is low enough for routine use. - Measuring both set-level (MIR) and pair-level (pairwise cosine distance) alignment metrics provides complementary evidence that the mechanism is doing what the theory claims — the finding that Standard SFT actually pushes paired text/image representations apart during training (0.52→0.57) is a striking and counter-intuitive result. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors provide a dedicated Limitations section and directly confront three scope boundaries: (1) LoMo is applied only during SFT, with pre-training and RL post-training integration left to future work; (2) the middle-span heuristic is not difficulty-aware or curriculum-style, leaving potential gains on the table; (3) validation is restricted to 8B–9B models due to compute constraints. This direct engagement with limitations is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No direct comparison against other cross-modal alignment methods (VCD, HA-DPO) on downstream task accuracy — the paper claims to address the same modality gap from the data side, making accuracy comparisons with these approaches a natural and missing baseline. - The rendered evaluation protocol uses the same font, size (20pt), and rendering pipeline for both LoMo training (rendered spans) and test-time question rendering, which may inflate rendered evaluation improvements through distribution matching; varying the test-time renderer (different fonts, resolutions) would disentangle genuine alignment from renderer-specific overfitting. - No breakdown of which SFT data categories (math, natural language, OCR) benefit most from rendering — this would guide practitioners on where to concentrate the rewrite budget and explain why visual perception and instruction following gain more than math reasoning. - Single-run results with no error bars; gains of 1–2 points on individual benchmarks (e.g., CC-OCR +2.21, DocVQA +1.72 on LLaVA standard evaluation) cannot be distinguished from run-to-run variance without multiple seeds. - No code, dataset, or model release stated, reducing immediate independent reproducibility. Bottom Line A practitioner fine-tuning VLMs on standard SFT corpora gains a concrete, zero-inference-cost technique with solid mechanistic grounding and thorough ablations; the method is immediately applicable and the evidence — particularly the dramatic rendered evaluation gains and the controlled ratio experiment — is credible enough to justify adoption pending replication at larger model scales.
Recommended 2605.30265 PDF
#23 — Innovative (73)

Prism: Private Relational Data Synthesis with Language Models

dl.acm.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Prism, an end-to-end framework that adapts the PATE (Private Aggregation of Teacher Ensembles) framework to autoregressive LLM generation for differentially private synthesis of relational tabular data via a novel tuple distillation mechanism. - Why it's important: Existing DP synthesis methods either apply DP-SGD to LLMs — which corrupts pretrained representations and degrades utility — or rely on lower-capacity models (graphical models, GANs) that fail to capture complex attribute correlations under a tight privacy budget; Prism sidesteps both problems. - How it does it: An ensemble of teacher LLMs fine-tuned on disjoint data partitions vote token-by-token during autoregressive generation; trie-guided pruning constrains votes to schema-valid tokens and short-circuits deterministic completions, data-dependent adaptive noise scales Gaussian perturbation to histogram range, and probabilistic sampling with threshold filtering replaces argmax to preserve diversity. - The good: Prism outperforms eight DP synthesis baselines across five benchmark datasets at all tested DP budgets (ε ∈ {1.0, 3.2, 6.8, 10.0}), with a formal end-to-end RDP-based privacy proof that correctly handles the adaptive (data-dependent) noise mechanism. - The missing: Evaluation is limited to small-to-medium tables (up to 236K rows, 22 columns) with entirely enumerable attribute domains; the method's behavior on truly high-cardinality continuous attributes — which require pre-discretization that the paper never discusses — is unaddressed. - Read it or not: Database and privacy researchers working on DP data synthesis should read this — it delivers a clean, formally sound PATE-to-LLM adaptation with strong empirical results; practitioners needing fast synthesis should be aware the 8.8h runtime on a dataset of 32K rows using 8× A100s is a real deployment barrier.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (system-flavored but evaluated empirically). Rigor (80) leads: peer-reviewed SIGMOD paper, a technically non-trivial and correct end-to-end RDP proof handling data-dependent noise, eight current/strong baselines, error bars, and public code. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (75) reflect a genuinely non-obvious PATE-to-LLM combination (trie short-circuiting + adaptive noise composition), though individual components have prior art. Pulled down by the unenumerated continuous-attribute gap, ablations only on Adult, and absence of statistical-fidelity metrics and empirical privacy auditing (Completeness 70, Resilience 72), plus a steep 8×A100/8.8h deployment cost capping Impact (65). Composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*80 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*65 ≈ 73.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 80 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 72 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Organizations store sensitive personal data in relational tables and need to share it for analytics and ML without violating privacy regulations (GDPR, HIPAA). The standard approach — privately training a generative model from scratch — forces a harsh privacy-utility trade-off: injecting DP noise into LLM weights (DP-SGD) distorts the pretrained representations that make LLMs useful for capturing complex attribute correlations, yielding poor utility. Non-LLM alternatives (Bayesian networks, GANs, autoencoders, diffusion models) lack the representational capacity to model high-dimensional joint distributions under a tight privacy budget. The specific gap: how to leverage LLMs' pretrained knowledge for relational synthesis while enforcing formal, provable DP without corrupting pretrained representations. Key Idea Perturb LLM outputs rather than weights: treat the problem as PATE-style knowledge distillation where k teacher LLMs vote at the token level during autoregressive generation, and add calibrated Gaussian noise to the token vote histogram before selection. The critical enabling trick is a token trie built per attribute domain that simultaneously enforces schema validity (pruning out-of-domain tokens), reduces the number of privacy-budget-consuming teacher queries (short-circuiting deterministic completions when a trie node has a single child), and compacts the vote histogram to improve the signal-to-noise ratio of the DP mechanism. Methodology Prism is a three-step pipeline. Step 1 (teacher training): partition the private table D into k disjoint equal subsets; serialize each tuple as "A1 is v1, A2 is v2, ..." and fine-tune k copies of a pretrained LLM using causal language modeling (CLM) cross-entropy loss. Step 2 (tuple distillation): for each token position in an autoregressive generation, query all k teachers for their top-token predictions; aggregate into a count histogram; apply three innovations — (a) Trie-guided pruning: for each attribute, build a token trie from the attribute domain; prune out-of-domain tokens from the histogram; short-circuit single-child trie nodes without a teacher query; (b) Data-dependent DP noise: compute histogram range r = max(c[τ]) − min(c[τ]), perturb r with Gaussian noise N(0,(σr)²) (sensitivity Δr = 2, DP-accounted), then linearly interpolate a noise scale σh ∈ [σh_min, σh_max] where high-range (strong consensus) histograms get larger σh (saves budget) and low-range gets smaller σh (preserves signal); add N(0,(σh)²) to each count (sensitivity Δh = √2); (c) Probabilistic sampling with threshold filtering: zero out noisy counts below 2σh, normalize the remaining histogram, and sample rather than taking the argmax. Repeat until the RDP accountant signals budget exhaustion, yielding differentially private distilled dataset D̂. Step 3 (student training): fine-tune a student LLM on D̂ (no additional privacy cost by post-processing property), then sample the final synthetic dataset D̃ by autoregressive generation from the student. Privacy analysis uses Rényi DP (RDP) composition. Sensitivity of the range query Δr = 2 (Lemma 2: disjoint partitions mean one tuple change affects at most one teacher, shifting max and min by at most 1). Histogram L2 sensitivity Δh = √2 (Lemma 3: worst case is a valid→valid token vote change). Per-round RDP: εr(α) = 2α/(σr)² for range release; εh_t(α|H_t) = α/(σh_t)² for histogram release conditioned on privatized history. The adaptive noise σh_t is a deterministic function of privatized history H_t, so RDP composes additively across rounds. Standard RDP-to-(ε,δ)-DP conversion completes the proof (Theorems 1–2). Three critical design decisions: (1) Token-level voting, not value-level — well-justified: LLM knowledge is encoded at the token level; treating multi-token categorical values (e.g., "Bachelors" → ["ba","che","lors"]) as atomic would require new vocabulary entries without pretrained representations. (2) Trie short-circuiting — well-justified: reduces queries from one-per-token to approximately one-per-value for categorical attributes, delivering substantial budget savings. (3) Probabilistic rather than deterministic argmax selection — well-justified and empirically validated: argmax induces mode collapse toward high-frequency values, artificially inflating recall while collapsing ranking metrics (AUROC, AUPRC). Key Results Experiment 1 (main comparison): Prism outperforms 8 baselines across 5 datasets (Abalone 4,177; Tax 30,000; Adult 32,561; Cardio 70,000; Diabetes 236,378 rows) at ε = 6.8, δ = 1e-5. Representative results — Adult F1: Prism 0.8222 vs. best baseline DP-LLM Full 0.8036; Adult AUROC: Prism 0.6941 vs. best baseline AIM 0.6916; Tax F1: Prism 0.5776 vs. next-best DP-LLM LoRA 0.5555; Diabetes F1: Prism 0.7939 vs. next-best AIM 0.7910. Prism consistently achieves highest AUROC and AUPRC across almost all datasets. Baseline set is current and strong: includes both DP-SGD LLM methods, GAN-based, diffusion-based, and graphical-model-based methods. Experiment 2 (ablation on Adult): All three components contribute. Removing probabilistic sampling + threshold filtering causes the largest degradation in ranking metrics (AUROC drops from 0.6941 to 0.6726), with spuriously high recall (0.9192) confirming mode collapse. Removing data-dependent DP and token pruning each cause smaller but consistent drops. Experiment 5 (varying ε on Adult): Prism is best or near-best at all four tested budgets ε ∈ {1.0, 3.2, 6.8, 10.0}; even at ε = 1.0 (strongest privacy), Prism outperforms all baselines. Experiment 7 (impact of noise): Prism w/o DP achieves AUROC 0.7098 vs. Prism 0.6941 — the noise slightly reduces ranking quality as expected, but F1 difference is small (0.8169 vs. 0.8222), suggesting the noise acts as mild regularization at the chosen scale. Experiment 11 (runtime): 8.8h total for Prism with 1800 teachers on Adult (8× A100s, 96 vCPUs, 1TB RAM), dominated by teacher fine-tuning (linear in k). 600 teachers achieves 4.1h with near-identical utility. Lightweight baselines run in < 1h; P3GM takes 8.4h. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD, NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is the first adaptation of PATE to autoregressive LLM-based tabular data synthesis, with the trie-guided pruning + data-dependent adaptive noise + probabilistic sampling mechanism enabling tractable budget use. Each individual component (tries, data-dependent DP, probabilistic sampling) has prior art in the database literature; the contribution is the non-obvious combination needed to make token-level PATE work for this setting. The student-teacher LLM framing and LLMs-for-tabular-data are both established; what is new is the mechanism connecting them under formal DP. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present (Exp 2) and cover all three components, but only on the Adult dataset — the paper asserts "consistent trends across datasets" without showing ablation tables for other datasets, which is a reproducibility gap given that Cardio and Abalone show narrower advantages over baselines. Error bars (mean ± std of 3 runs) are reported throughout. The RDP-based privacy proof is complete and technically sound, correctly handling the data-dependent adaptive noise via the conditioning argument that σh_t is a deterministic function of privatized history H_t. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/golden-eggs-lab/prism. One methodological concern: the paper uses only downstream predictive utility (Train-on-Synthetic, Test-on-Real with 9 classifiers) as the evaluation metric; statistical fidelity metrics (marginal distributions, pairwise correlations, Jensen-Shannon divergences) are entirely absent, making it impossible to assess whether Prism preserves data structure beyond what classifiers capture. No empirical privacy auditing (membership inference, attribute inference attacks) is performed to validate that the formal DP guarantee translates to empirical resilience. Published as a peer-reviewed SIGMOD paper (Proc. ACM Manag. Data, received Oct 2025, accepted Feb 2026). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (builds and evaluates a complete end-to-end synthesis system). - Practical: Requires 8× A100 GPUs and 1TB RAM as tested; a reduced configuration (600 teachers) achieves nearly identical utility in 4.1h, lowering the hardware bar, but the memory footprint of 1800 fine-tuned LLM copies is still substantial and not characterized separately. - Efficient: Teacher fine-tuning dominates runtime and scales linearly with k; at 1800 teachers, Prism takes 8.8h vs. < 1h for lightweight alternatives — a 10–40× overhead that is a real deployment barrier for iterative workflows. - Effective: The TSTR evaluation consistently shows Prism synthetic data preserves downstream classification utility better than all 8 baselines at all tested DP budgets, directly solving the stated problem; the student model generalizes substantially better than direct release of the distilled set (29,035 unique tuples vs. 501 in teacher-sampling on Adult). - Scalable: Tested up to 236K rows and 22 attributes — moderate tabular scale, not stress-scale. The trie-based approach requires fully enumerable attribute domains, which is structurally incompatible with high-cardinality continuous attributes without pre-discretization; this discretization step and its privacy/utility implications are not addressed. Single-table scope only; multi-table and foreign key scenarios are acknowledged as out of scope. The paper delivers an open-source prototype at https://github.com/golden-eggs-lab/prism. Strengths - The RDP-based end-to-end privacy proof is technically non-trivial and correctly handled: the key step — showing that σh_t is a deterministic function of the privatized history H_t, enabling additive RDP composition — is not obvious and is cleanly formalized. - Trie-guided pruning simultaneously solves three problems: schema validity enforcement, vocabulary restriction to schema-plausible tokens, and dramatic reduction in teacher query count (often one query per multi-token categorical value), making the approach budget-efficient in a way that would not be apparent from the PATE starting point alone. - The mode-collapse hypothesis for argmax selection is empirically confirmed in Exp 2 with the characteristic signature: highest recall (0.9192) but worst AUROC/AUPRC — a crisp, interpretable result that validates a key design choice. - The evaluation at ε = 1.0 (very high privacy) is particularly informative: Prism still leads all baselines, suggesting the pretrained LLM's prior knowledge provides a floor of utility that other methods cannot match under strong noise. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly limit scope to single-table synthesis, noting multi-table and foreign key handling as "orthogonal." They acknowledge the runtime disadvantage vs. lightweight baselines and discuss it as a utility-cost trade-off. They note the consistent LLM architecture choice (same model for teachers and students) as a simplification. They address the pretraining exposure concern (§7.1) with an argument that DistilGPT2 was trained on unstructured web text unlikely to contain the benchmark tables in CSV form, and cite the Diabetes dataset (postdates OpenWebText) as additional evidence — though this argument is intuitive rather than rigorous. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The trie-based approach requires each attribute domain to be explicitly enumerated. For continuous numerical attributes (floating-point income, continuous age), this requires pre-discretization — but the paper never discusses how discretization is performed, what discretization granularity is used, or how granularity affects the privacy-utility trade-off. The benchmark datasets happen to have largely categorical attributes and low-cardinality integers; this structural dependency is a serious unacknowledged gap for real-world tables with continuous-valued columns. - Ablation study (Exp 2) is presented only for Adult, not for the other four datasets. The claim of "consistent trends across datasets" is unverified and particularly important for datasets where Prism's margin over baselines is smaller (e.g., Cardio, where PATE-CTGAN achieves F1 0.7332 vs. Prism 0.7533). - No empirical privacy audit is conducted (no membership inference or attribute inference attacks). The formal DP guarantee is sound, but the gap between provable and empirical privacy can be large depending on calibration; its absence is a standard omission in the DP synthesis literature but notable given the paper's emphasis on formal guarantees over empirical heuristics. - Statistical fidelity metrics (marginal distributions, pairwise correlations) are absent. Using only downstream classification utility as the sole metric is a proxy that can miss structural data quality issues — a synthetic dataset that inflates certain attribute combinations might score well on TSTR while being useless for other downstream tasks. - Experiment 7 shows Prism w/o DP achieves slightly lower F1 (0.8169) than Prism (0.8222), meaning adding DP noise marginally improves the primary metric. This counterintuitive result is not explained and could indicate that hyperparameters (noise scales) were tuned to implicitly benefit the Adult benchmark. Bottom Line Prism delivers a technically sound and empirically well-validated contribution — the first clean PATE-LLM integration for DP tabular synthesis with a rigorous privacy proof and consistent gains over eight baselines — but the steep compute cost, unenumerated continuous-attribute limitation, and absence of statistical fidelity metrics and empirical privacy auditing mean a practitioner should treat the results as promising rather than deployment-ready for arbitrary real-world tables.
Recommended
#24 — Innovative (73)

SMDD-Bench: Can LLMs Solve Real-World Small Molecule Drug Design Tasks?

Kevin Han, Renfei Zhang, Kathy Wei, Hamed Mahdavi, Niloofar Mireshghallah, Amir Barati Farimani · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SMDD-Bench, a 502-task multi-turn agentic benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on five realistic small molecule drug design task types: 2D Pharmacophore Identification, Interaction Point Discovery, Scaffold Hopping, Lead Optimization, and Fragment Assembly. - Why it's important: Existing chemistry benchmarks restrict evaluation to single-turn QA on simple tasks, leaving no standardized testbed for the long-horizon, multi-step reasoning, 3D spatial intuition, and constrained optimization that real medicinal chemistry demands. - How it does it: A witness-aware procedural generation pipeline simultaneously constructs each task instance and a hidden witness molecule that satisfies all evaluation criteria, guaranteeing solvability without human expert review; agents operate in sandboxed environments with limited oracle calls (8 Boltz2, 15 ADMET-AI). - The good: Even the best model (GPT-5.4) solves only 40.2% of tasks, with near-zero success on tasks requiring 3D geometry reasoning (Interaction Point Discovery 0%, Fragment Assembly ≤1.7%), revealing concrete capability gaps rather than benchmark saturation. - The missing: All evaluation is against Boltz2 and ADMET-AI predicted distributions, not wet lab data; the benchmark rewards optimizing against oracle biases that may not reflect real structure-activity relationships, and no specialized molecular design baselines (e.g., diffusion/flow models) are included for comparison. - Read it or not: Required reading for anyone building or evaluating LLM agents for drug discovery or scientific tool use; less essential for researchers focused on generative molecular design methods.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (a benchmark deployed by running agent systems; software artifact + public leaderboard). Impact (80) and Novelty (80) carry the score: witness-aware procedural generation guaranteeing solvable instances at scale is a genuine NEW FORMULATION, and the released benchmark + Lite subset + leaderboard give real practitioner value. Rigor (60) is the main drag — single stochastic run per (model,task) leaves variance unreported, the entire pipeline treats Boltz2/ADMET-AI as ground truth, witness-recovery rates raise memorization questions, and it is a preprint. Resilience (65) and Non-Obviousness (75) are solid but the oracle-world-only caveat bounds how far the claims extend. Weighted: 0.15*80 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.30*80 = 73.
systems Novelty 80 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Computational drug discovery requires medicinal chemists to perform multi-step workflows combining 3D protein-ligand reasoning, structure-activity relationship (SAR) synthesis across many oracle calls, cheminformatics tool programming, and constrained multi-objective optimization — all under sparse experimental feedback. Existing LLM benchmarks (ChemBench, SmolInstruct, MolecularIQ, QCBench) restrict evaluation to single-turn question answering on simple chemistry knowledge. Prior agentic drug design work either reports hand-selected successes or uses ad hoc non-standardized setups. The field has no standardized, scalable testbed for evaluating long-horizon agentic performance across diverse chemistries and protein targets. Key Idea: Witness-aware task generation: each task instance is constructed simultaneously with a known valid solution (the witness molecule). For lead optimization, a dominating molecule whose ADMET/affinity profile strictly improves the reference is identified from a pre-validated pool, defining objectives from the measured property delta; the dominating molecule becomes the witness. For scaffold hopping and fragment assembly, experimentally known actives that satisfy all evaluation gates when co-folded by Boltz2 serve as witnesses. This approach enables procedural generation at scale without per-instance human expert validation, and guarantees every task has at least one computable solution. Methodology: Five task types with purely computational evaluation using RDKit, PLIP, Boltz2, and ADMET-AI. (1) 2D Pharmacophore Identification: agent writes a Python `check_pharmacophore(smiles) -> bool` function given 10 ChEMBL actives + 10 inactives; evaluated on held-out experimentally validated sets by recall ≥0.8 and specificity ≥0.8. (2) Interaction Point Discovery: given apo-protein PDB + pocket center, predict 3D coordinates of 3 conserved pharmacophoric points; ground truth derived from DBSCAN clustering (ε=1.0Å, ≥15% conservation) of thousands of PDB co-crystal interaction features; Hungarian algorithm matching at 2.5Å cutoff. (3) Scaffold Hopping: propose molecule with Tanimoto <0.5 and scaffold MCS ratio <0.65 versus reference, but Boltz2 interaction fingerprint Tanimoto >0.75 and binding probability >0.7. (4) Lead Optimization: optimize reference molecule on up to 5 ADMET/affinity objectives while holding 4 others constant within tolerance bands; maintain Tanimoto ≥0.7 to reference; hard drug-likeness constraints (MW <600, logP ∈[-1,5], TPSA <140, SA <4.5, PAINS/Brenk free). (5) Fragment Assembly: incorporate 1-2 BRICS-derived 3D fragments into drug-like molecule with fragment RMSD <2.0Å after Boltz2 co-folding, binding probability >0.7, and affinity ≤0.1 log units below witness baseline. Critical design decisions: oracle budget (8 Boltz2, 15 ADMET-AI) calibrated by a sweep on a 25-task stratified subset; target identifiers obfuscated; internet access prohibited; minimalist ReAct loop with ≤100 turns and ≤10M cumulative tokens. Key Results: GPT-5.4 achieves 40.2% overall (best), Claude Sonnet 4.6 38.0%, Gemini 3.1 Pro 39.0%; open-source models lag substantially (Kimi K2.5 Thinking 30.3%, DeepSeek V3.2 24.3%, Qwen3.5 27.5%, MiniMax M2.7 19.3%). Headline rates are driven by Lead Optimization: GPT-5.4 reaches 57.6%, Claude 53.5%, Gemini 55.6% there, while Interaction Point Discovery is 0% for all models except Gemini (4%), Fragment Assembly ≤1.7%, and Scaffold Hopping ≤3.8%. Enumeration-vs-selection analysis recovers Claude Sonnet 4.6 Scaffold Hopping from 3.8% to 32.7% if the best SMILES ever mentioned in the reasoning trace is submitted — indicating a selection/reasoning failure, not an enumeration failure. On SMDD-Bench Diversity (20 hardest Lead Optimization instances, 10 runs each), Claude Sonnet 4.6 achieves highest success rate (8.40/10) but lowest unique diversity (3.70 unique, Tanimoto similarity 0.823); MiniMax M2.7 achieves the most unique successful molecules (4.05, similarity 0.763). Chemical novelty of submissions: 62–94% of molecules not found in ChEMBL36, SureChEMBL, PubChem, or BindingDB. Benchmark scale: 502 tasks, 102 protein targets, 855 unique small molecules, median pairwise Tanimoto between reference molecules 0.102. Evaluated on A800/A6000 GPUs; average wall clock 15–60 min per task depending on model. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW PROBLEM FORMULATION. The five task types correspond to established medicinal chemistry workflows; the genuinely novel contribution is (1) the witness-aware procedural generation algorithm enabling guaranteed-solvable task instances at scale, (2) the standardized multi-turn agentic evaluation framework with computational oracle-based scoring, and (3) the enumeration-vs-selection diagnostic decomposition. The approach is not a new molecular design method. Rigor & Reproducibility: No model ablations (appropriate for a benchmark paper). Success rates are point estimates from single runs per model per task — variance is unknown for the full benchmark; diversity subset provides 10 runs on 20 tasks. Stochasticity in Boltz2 predictions is mitigated by averaging over 10 diffusion samples. Oracle budget was empirically calibrated on a 25-task stratified subset via performance-vs-budget sweeps. Code and data are available via GitHub (URL not stated in paper but smddbench.com hosts a public leaderboard). Major methodological concern: the entire evaluation pipeline treats Boltz2 and ADMET-AI as ground truth — tasks are solvable within the oracle world by construction, but a molecule that satisfies all oracle criteria may not exhibit those properties in vitro. Witness molecule recovery rates (14–22% on Lead Optimization) indicate LLMs sometimes directly propose the witness, raising memorization questions given limited target diversity despite identifier obfuscation. The enumeration extraction uses GPT-5.4-mini, which may introduce SMILES extraction errors that inflate recovered success rates. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.21740v2). Deployability (SYSTEMS): Profile: SYSTEMS — a benchmark evaluated by deploying agent systems. - Practical: Runs on A800/A6000 GPUs with Boltz2 for protein-ligand co-folding; each task requires 15–60 min wall clock at standard GPU availability. - Efficient: Computationally expensive per evaluation; SMDD-Bench Lite (100 tasks, stratified representative subset) is provided for iterative development and reduces cost ~5×. - Effective: The benchmark exposes genuine capability gaps (near-zero 3D task rates, enumeration/selection gap) and provides actionable failure mode analyses; it measures agentic drug design skill more faithfully than prior single-turn QA benchmarks. - Scalable: 502 tasks is a practical evaluation scale; expansion to additional protein families or task types would require additional Boltz2 compute for generation. The oracle-dependency means the benchmark cannot directly transfer to real wet lab settings without retraining agents under the true SAR distribution. Software artifact: benchmark task files + evaluation pipeline + public leaderboard. Strengths: - Witness-aware generation cleanly solves the task-feasibility problem without requiring human expert annotation of each instance, enabling procedural scaling to 502 diverse tasks across 102 protein targets. - The enumeration-vs-selection decomposition and annotated failure mode analyses (SAR non-synthesis, candidate recycling, memorization substituting for design) are more diagnostically useful than raw pass rates and directly motivate training interventions. - Obfuscating PDB codes, ligand identifiers, and target names, combined with prohibiting internet access, meaningfully reduces data-leakage shortcuts while preserving a realistic computational environment. - Chemical diversity of the benchmark is quantitatively documented: median pairwise reference-molecule Tanimoto of 0.102 across 855 unique molecules spanning 102 protein targets across kinases, proteases, GPCRs, epigenetic regulators, and nuclear receptors. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge that Boltz2 and ADMET-AI are imperfect oracles and frame this explicitly, arguing that treating them as ground truth (analogous to wet lab assays) is a principled choice enabling a fully computational benchmark. They note that agents trained on SMDD-Bench can be re-interfaced with actual automated wet labs. They do not minimize this limitation. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) No specialized molecular design baseline (e.g., diffusion or flow matching models such as DiffSBDD, Pocket2Mol, or TargetDiff) is benchmarked; comparing LLM agents only against other LLM agents leaves unclear whether any approach outperforms specialized SMDD tools on the same tasks. (2) Lead Optimization constitutes 68% of the 502 tasks, and overall success rates are dominated by performance there; the headline "40.2%" understates failure on 3D reasoning tasks. (3) Variance of success rates is unreported for the full benchmark; a single stochastic run per (model, task) pair means reported percentages carry unknown confidence intervals. (4) The SMILES extraction from agent traces using GPT-5.4-mini to perform the enumeration-vs-selection analysis is itself a model-dependent step whose accuracy is unvalidated, potentially inflating recovered success rates. (5) No analysis of how different oracle thresholds (e.g., binding probability >0.7, interaction fingerprint Tanimoto >0.75) affect task difficulty distribution or which thresholds are rate-limiting for failures. Bottom Line: SMDD-Bench is a well-engineered benchmark that fills a real gap; its witness-aware generation and failure mode analyses are the most useful contributions, and any group building LLM-based computational drug discovery pipelines should read it — with the caveat that all reported results measure oracle-world performance, not real chemistry.
Citations 2605.21740 PDF
#25 — Innovative (72)

Teaching Language Models to Think in Code

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces THINC (Thinking in Code), a training framework that restructures tool-integrated reasoning so that code itself serves as the primary reasoner — replacing the interleaved natural-language/code paradigm — through trajectory distillation, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning, producing THINC-4B which achieves 78.1% avg@16 across five competition-level math benchmarks. - Why it's important: Interleaved tool-integrated reasoning systems routinely let NL arithmetic errors propagate silently into code, use code only as a post-hoc verifier rather than a solver, and duplicate work between NL and code; these structural flaws corrupt final answers and degrade robustly under execution failures, limiting the ceiling of LLM mathematical reasoning. - How it does it: THINC constrains every trajectory to a single NL planning block followed by code-only reasoning blocks connected solely through interpreter execution outputs, then trains this format via 12.2k distilled trajectories (SFT) and three-stage GRPO/DAPO reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. - The good: THINC-4B achieves 78.1% avg@16 across five competition benchmarks, outperforming all TIR baselines including the much larger rStar2-Agent-14B and ReTool-32B, surpassing Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking on four of five benchmarks, and maintaining 64–69% recovery@3 under consecutive code failures versus near-collapse for interleaved baselines. - The missing: All experiments are confined to 1.7B and 4B parameter models and competition-level mathematics; whether the code-centric format transfers to larger scales or other tool-integrated domains (e.g., web search, multi-tool agentic tasks) is entirely untested. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on mathematical reasoning, tool-integrated LLM training, or RL post-training — the structural diagnosis of interleaved TIR is sharp and the ablation against ASTER-4B (matched base, teacher, and RL pipeline) isolates the trajectory format as the source of gain.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (training framework + trained models on benchmarks). Rigor (72) and Non-Obviousness (75) lead: the matched ASTER-4B ablation (same base, teacher, RL pipeline, differing only in trajectory format) credibly isolates the code-centric format as the causal variable, and the Recovery@k / 99.2%-code-grounding behavioral analyses reveal robustness invisible to accuracy alone. Novelty (75) is solid — the format design is new, but the training components (distillation, SFT, GRPO/DAPO) are established. Rigor is capped by the preprint status, code/models only 'pending release,' un-ablated SFT filtering thresholds, and un-analyzed train/test contamination across AIME/HMMT. Completeness (72), Resilience (70), and Impact (60, math-only, 1.7B–4B scale untested) round it out. Composite: 0.20*75 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*60 ≈ 72.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 72 Resilience 70 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mathematical reasoning in LLMs has increasingly relied on tool-integrated reasoning (TIR), where NL thought blocks alternate with code execution across multiple turns. Practitioners in LLM post-training and competition-math solving face three recurring failure modes: (1) the model completes a derivation in NL, then runs code only to confirm — code adds no new computation; (2) when NL performs arithmetic, errors propagate as hard-coded constants into subsequent code blocks without detection; (3) NL and code duplicate each other's reasoning roles, with NL laying out the algorithm step-by-step and code merely transcribing it. These structural problems are intrinsic to the interleaved TIR format rather than being model-specific failures, and prior RL or prompting interventions have not addressed them at the trajectory-format level. Key Idea: The key insight is that code is already a symbolic reasoning system equivalent to mathematics in expressive power, so NL reasoning between code blocks is structurally redundant and error-prone. THINC eliminates NL from the reasoning chain entirely after an initial strategic planning step: the first block (t1) expresses only a high-level solution strategy, and every subsequent block is code whose inputs are exclusively the execution outputs of prior code blocks. This means every intermediate value is produced and verified by the interpreter by construction, NL errors cannot propagate, and the roles of planning (NL) and computation (code) are cleanly separated. Methodology: The pipeline has three stages. First, trajectory distillation: using a 3-shot prompt demonstrating the THINC format, the authors sample one trajectory per problem from Qwen3.5-27B on Skywork-OR1 and OpenMathReasoning (English, positive-integer-answer problems). Trajectories are retained only if correct, error-free in every code block, contain ≥3 code blocks, and spend <50% of tokens in the planning thought block (|t1|/|τ| < 0.5). This yields 12,200 THINC-SFT trajectories. Second, supervised fine-tuning: Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 are fine-tuned on THINC-SFT using the standard next-token prediction objective (m_k = 1 for all tokens), context length 32K, learning rate 7×10⁻⁶ with cosine schedule, batch size 16, 3 epochs. Third, reinforcement learning: starting from SFT checkpoints, GRPO is applied on DAPO-Math-17k using the DAPO variant with token-level normalization and asymmetric clipping (ε_low = 0.20, ε_high = 0.28), no KL penalty, rollout batch size 128, G = 8 trajectories per prompt. Training runs three curriculum stages of increasing context budget and tool call limit (Stage 1: 280 steps, 16K context, 20 tool calls; Stage 2: 120 additional steps filtering trivial problems; Stage 3: from step 400, 32K context, 40 tool calls). The reward function is binary exact-match. Key design decisions: (1) retaining all tokens in the SFT loss (including execution outputs) — authors find no significant difference from masking them; (2) strict filtering criteria that enforce the code-centric format at distillation time rather than relying on RL alone; (3) the per-code-block stationless design where variables do not persist between turns, forcing re-imports and hardcoded values from prior results. Key Results: THINC-4B achieves 78.1% ± 4.4% avg@16 across AIME 2024 (88.3%), AIME 2025 (85.8%), AIME 2026 (86.0%), HMMT 2025 February (74.0%), and BeyondAIME (56.1%). This is the best result in the comparison table, outperforming all TIR baselines: ASTER-4B (73.8%), rStar2-Agent-14B (61.5%), ReTool-32B (50.7%), DemyAgent-4B (57.3%), CoRT-1.5B (57.0%). It also surpasses the NL-only Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking (75.2%) on four of five benchmarks, and outperforms its own distillation teacher Qwen3.5-27B (64.7% with 3-shot) by 13.4 points despite being 7× smaller. The most controlled ablation compares THINC-4B against ASTER-4B, which shares the same base model, teacher capacity, and RL pipeline but uses an interleaved trajectory structure: THINC-4B exceeds ASTER-4B by 4.1 points on average with fewer tool calls (6.1 vs. 11.1) and shorter responses (13.5k vs. 15.4k tokens). Code-grounding analysis shows 99.2% of THINC-4B final answers appear in at least one code block's execution output, versus 88.4% for ReTool and 74.3% for rStar2. Recovery@k (fraction reaching correct answer after k consecutive initial code failures) shows THINC-4B maintains 64–69% recovery for k = 1,2,3, dropping to 54.5% at k = 4 and 33.3% at k = 5, while ASTER drops from 52.1% to 18.5% and rStar2-Agent collapses to 0% at k = 5. OOD evaluation on GPQA-Diamond shows THINC-4B at 66.48% avg@16 and 91.41% best@16, compared to ASTER-4B at 63.42/90.40 and base Qwen3-4B-Thinking at 66.32/83.84. THINC-1.7B reaches 42.8% avg@16, outperforming Qwen3-1.7B (32.2%), the tool-prompted base (29.8%), and CoRT-1.5B (25.7%). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuinely novel contribution is the trajectory format design: restricting NL to a single strategic planning step and routing all subsequent reasoning through code-only blocks connected by execution outputs. The individual training components — trajectory distillation from a teacher model, SFT, GRPO/DAPO RL with verifiable rewards — are established techniques applied from prior TIR and post-training literature. The THINC-SFT dataset of 12,200 trajectories is a new artifact. The combination of format design + the empirical demonstration that this structural change explains the performance gap (via the ASTER-4B ablation) constitutes a substantive contribution beyond applying known methods to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper's key ablation — comparing THINC-4B against ASTER-4B under matched conditions (same base model, same teacher, same RL pipeline, differing only in trajectory format) — is the strongest piece of evidence isolating the format as the causal variable; this is methodologically sound. Error bars (95% confidence intervals) are reported throughout. The code-grounded answer rate (99.2%) and Recovery@k metric are well-designed behavioral analyses that go beyond accuracy numbers. No significant ablation is provided to show which of the three SFT filtering criteria (correctness, error-free execution, ≥3 code blocks, planning token ratio) individually matter — the filtering criteria are justified by intuition but not ablated. The claim that m_k = 1 vs. m_k = 0 (masking execution tokens) produces no significant difference is stated but not shown in detail. Code and models are declared as "will be released soon" — not yet publicly available at submission time. Training used 8× NVIDIA H200 GPUs. Multiple random seeds for RL training are not mentioned explicitly. This is a preprint; results have not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (training framework + trained models evaluated on benchmarks). - Practical: Training on 8× NVIDIA H200 GPUs is commercially accessible in cloud settings; inference runs within a 32K token budget using standard Python interpreter access (numpy, scipy, sympy). - Efficient: THINC-4B uses fewer tool calls (6.1 vs. 11.1 for ASTER) and generates shorter responses (13.5k vs. 15.4k tokens), giving lower inference cost than comparable interleaved baselines at the same model size. - Effective: 78.1% avg@16 on five competition-level benchmarks with 99.2% code-grounded answers provides strong evidence the method solves the stated problem of unreliable NL-mediated computation. - Scalable: Experiments are confined to 1.7B and 4B parameter models; whether the format and training recipe hold at 7B, 14B, or 70B+ is not tested and constitutes the single most important open question. The paper delivers two trained model checkpoints (pending release) and a 12.2k SFT dataset; no public library or CLI tool is provided. Strengths: - The three-failure-mode diagnosis of interleaved TIR (post-hoc verification, NL error propagation, role confusion) is precise and falsifiable, not merely motivational. - The ASTER-4B ablation is unusually clean: matching base model, teacher, and RL pipeline isolates trajectory format as the variable, providing stronger causal evidence than most TIR papers. - Recovery@k is a novel and informative metric that reveals robustness properties invisible to accuracy-only evaluation; the result that THINC maintains 64–69% recovery while interleaved systems degrade to near-zero is a concrete behavioral characterization. - The OOD evaluation on GPQA-Diamond shows the code-centric format generalizes beyond mathematical training distribution, which partially addresses the scope concern. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state two limitations: (1) experiments are restricted to 1.7B and 4B parameter models due to computational constraints; (2) evaluation is confined to competition-level mathematics. They name extension to larger models and other tool-integrated domains as natural future directions. They confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The SFT dataset is drawn from Skywork-OR1 and OpenMathReasoning, which overlap significantly with competition math benchmarks — the degree of train/test contamination across AIME and HMMT problems is not analyzed. - THINC's stateless code execution (each block is a fresh Python process, variables do not persist) is an unusual design choice that forces models to hardcode prior results; this may harm performance on problems requiring long stateful derivations, but no analysis of failure cases where statefulness would have helped is provided. - The SFT filtering criteria (particularly the 50% planning-token ratio threshold) are engineering choices presented without ablation; the sensitivity of downstream RL performance to these thresholds is unknown. - The comparison includes Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking, a much larger NL-only model, but does not compare against any large TIR system (e.g., a 70B TIR model); the ceiling of the interleaved TIR paradigm at large scale is therefore not established. - Code and models are not yet released, making independent replication impossible at time of publication. Bottom Line: Any practitioner building or training tool-integrated mathematical reasoning systems should read this paper — the ASTER-4B ablation credibly demonstrates that the trajectory format alone (not model size or RL recipe) drives a meaningful accuracy gain, and the Recovery@k analysis reveals a robustness advantage that standard evaluation metrics miss entirely.
Recommended 2605.07237 PDF
#26 — Innovative (72)

MobileGym: A Verifiable and Highly Parallel Simulation Platform for Mobile GUI Agent Research (48 ▲ )

Dingbang Wu, Rui Hao, Haiyang Wang, Shuzhe Wu, Han Xiao, Zhenghong Li, Bojiang Zhou, Zheng Ju, Zichen Liu, Lue Fan, Zhaoxiang Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces MOBILEGYM, a browser-hosted simulation platform that represents everyday mobile app state as structured JSON, enabling deterministic outcome verification and scalable parallel online RL training for GUI agents. - Why it's important: Existing mobile agent environments force a choice between heavyweight Android emulators (~4.5–6 GB RAM, ~78s cold start, limited parallelism) covering only system utilities, or real devices covering everyday apps but blocking reproducible evaluation and online RL due to irreversible side effects and unresettable state. - How it does it: Full environment state is layered as read-only World Data + mutable per-environment Runtime Overlay + OS Runtime, all serializable to JSON, enabling millisecond-level snapshot/reset/fork and deterministic state-diff judging without any VLM in the loop. - The good: GRPO training on Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct for 10 steps gains +12.8 pt on the 256-task simulation benchmark, and 95.1% of that gain transfers to a real Redmi device on the 59-task signal subset, with absolute sim–real gaps of only 1.7 pt (base) and 3.8 pt (trained). - The missing: The sim-to-real study covers only one 4B model trained for just 10 GRPO steps on a cherry-picked 59-task signal subset; whether the ~95% transfer rate holds for larger models, longer training, or a random test sample is undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building mobile GUI agents or online RL training pipelines targeting everyday apps; less relevant for those focused on pure VLM capability scaling without an interactive environment component.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile (Deployability tag SYSTEMS). Practical Impact (75) dominates under systems weighting (0.30): MOBILEGYM delivers a working browser-hosted platform plus benchmark with large, measured efficiency wins (10x memory, 25x cold start, $0 judging) and the novel Unexpected-Side-Effects metric that no prior programmatic mobile benchmark provides. Novelty (72) is strong as a new system+benchmark, anchored by the interaction-fidelity-only insight. Rigor (70) is credible — std devs for open models, a 118-trajectory judge audit — but loses ground to absent reward-component ablations, single runs for proprietary models, and an explicit 'existence proof' sim-to-real study (one 4B model, 10 GRPO steps, favorable 59-task subset). Resilience (68) and Completeness (72) benefit from unusually candid scope disclosure, though the headline 95.1% transfer figure is fragile and Chinese-centric. Code is implied but not confirmed open-source. Weighted: 0.15*72 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*70 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*68 + 0.30*75 = 72.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Researchers training and evaluating mobile GUI agents face a structural trilemma. Android emulator environments (AndroidWorld, AndroidLab) support deterministic verification for system utilities and open-source apps but require 4.5–6 GB RAM per instance, ~78s cold starts, and do not support state forking needed for group-based RL methods like GRPO. Real-device benchmarks (MobileBench-OL) cover the everyday apps—WeChat, Alipay, booking services—that agents are ultimately intended to operate, but live accounts, backend state drift, irreversible operations, and the cost of maintaining device farms make episodes impossible to reproducibly reset, parallelize, or verify without brittle XPath rules or unreliable VLM judges. VLM-based judging itself carries a demonstrated 10.2% error rate on the signal-bucket trajectories in this paper, replicated with two independent judge models. The result is that online RL—now an important capability driver for GUI agents—is practically blocked for the most important app category. The core gap is that existing approaches conflate interaction fidelity (what agents need: realistic screens responding to actions) with full backend replication (what makes reproducibility hard). Key Idea The central insight is that GUI agents observe only screenshots and act through discrete touch/type/swipe actions, so a simulator only needs interaction fidelity—realistic screen responses to agent actions—rather than real backend replication. By representing all app state as structured JSON layered over read-only world data, every agent-induced state change becomes simultaneously readable for deterministic judging, writable for reset and configuration, forkable for parallel GRPO rollouts, and diff-able for side-effect detection. The same JSON mechanism that enables cheap parallelism (browser instances at ~400 MB, ~3s cold start) also enables the only metric in the benchmark that captures off-target state mutations: the Unexpected Side Effects metric, which no existing programmatic mobile benchmark provides. Methodology MOBILEGYM is implemented as a browser-hosted React application that mirrors Android OS semantics (task stack via TaskManager, intent routing, back-key priority dispatch, permissions, notifications, cross-app ContentProvider) over structured local state. Three layers compose the rendered UI: read-only World Data containing 190K+ synthetic entities (contacts, tickets, transactions, products, posts), a per-environment Runtime Overlay holding mutable agent-writable state (carts, drafts, messages, settings), and OS Runtime state (task stack, system hardware state). Only the Runtime Overlay is snapshotted and compared, keeping snapshot size small while capturing all agent-induced changes. Each app's UI navigation is formalized as an EFSM tuple (S, Σ, Δ, s₀, D, G, U) with guard-conditional transitions and data-driven state expansion; the same specification file drives runtime navigation and static BFS task-trajectory enumeration. The benchmark layer maps agent outputs to 17 standardized actions executed via Playwright with coordinates normalized to [0, 1000]. Task judging compares JSON state diffs programmatically against a per-task declarative judge specification. Query tasks use a typed AnswerSheet GUI form with field-specific matchers (exact text, numeric tolerance, choice) to replace brittle string-similarity heuristics. The RL experiment uses GRPO (Shao et al., 2024) on Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct with group size k=8, lr=1e-6, KL=0.01, DAPO asymmetric clip-higher (0.2/0.28), 96 parallel environment instances on a single node with 3 RTX Pro 6000s, 10 training steps. The reward is PR-shaped with multiplicative penalties for side effects (×0.8), false completion (×0.8), post-success abort (×0.5), and overdue termination (×0.5). Critical design decisions: (1) layering only the mutable runtime overlay in snapshots is well-justified for efficiency but means backend-only phenomena are not captured; (2) the AnswerSheet protocol is well-justified by concrete false-accept/reject failure examples from string-similarity heuristics; (3) the EFSM navigation formalization enables automatic task generation but requires ~3–4 person-days per everyday app, totaling ~60 person-days for 28 apps. Key Results Benchmark: 9 agents evaluated on the 256-task test set achieve SR spanning 9.4% (Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct) to 58.8% (Gemini 3.1 Pro ±1.4), with proprietary models substantially outperforming open-source GUI specialists (best: AutoGLM-Phone-9B at 20.0% ±1.3). On L4 (hardest) tasks only Gemini 3.1 Pro achieves meaningful SR (21.9%); all other proprietary models reach ≤6.2% and all open-source GUI specialists ≤1.9%, indicating genuine frontier discrimination. Unexpected Side Effects range from 4.7% to 14.5% across models and do not correlate monotonically with overall SR, demonstrating independent diagnostic value. RL training: GRPO raises Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct SR from 9.4% to 22.2% (+12.8 pt) on the 256-task test set; the trained 4B model surpasses AutoGLM-Phone-9B (9B) on L1–L3. Sim-to-Real: on the 59-task signal subset, real-device SR rises from 32.2% to 72.9% (+40.7 pt), matching simulation gains of +42.8 pt (95.1% retention). Trajectory lengths on paired operate tasks are comparable between simulation and real device (trained: 10.08 vs. 12.20 steps; base: 5.00 vs. 6.03 steps). Efficiency: 256 parallel instances on one server use <10% CPU and ~100 GB RAM, completing a full evaluation in ~6 minutes; this compares to 10 bare-metal servers (960 vCPUs, 3840 GB RAM) needed for 512 Android emulator instances in prior work. VLM judge audit: 10.2% error rate on 118 signal-bucket trajectories, replicated at the same aggregate rate (12/118) with GPT-5.4 on the same saved trajectories. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The browser-hosted JSON-state simulation approach for everyday mobile apps is the core novel system contribution—prior lightweight simulation work (GUI-Genesis, OpenApps) covered only single-trajectory or simple-app settings. The AnswerSheet protocol and structured state-diff Unexpected Side Effects metric are novel mechanisms within the system. MOBILEGYM-BENCH is a novel benchmark with a principled 4-axis task taxonomy and model-calibrated difficulty strata. The GRPO training algorithm, underlying VLM architectures, and sim-to-real transfer concept are existing ideas applied in this new environment. Rigor & Reproducibility This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.26114v2, May 2026); peer review status not stated. The project page (mobilegym.github.io) is listed but the paper does not explicitly confirm open-source code release. Open-source model results use 4 trials with standard deviations reported; proprietary models use single runs (marked †), with one additional run for the strongest model (Gemini 3.1 Pro) to estimate variance. The sim-to-real training study is explicitly described as an "existence proof" rather than a comprehensive evaluation—10 GRPO steps, one model, one device (Redmi Note 12 Turbo). The 59-task signal subset is specifically drawn from tasks where simulation-side improvement was observed (Uplift, Mid, Stable-pass buckets), not a random sample; the 95.1% retention figure does not apply to the full 256-task test. No ablations of individual reward components or the AnswerSheet protocol vs. free-text baseline are provided (the AnswerSheet ablation is explicitly deferred to future work). Difficulty stratification sensitivity is tested with a 4-model vs. 8-model calibration set, finding robust qualitative conclusions. The VLM judge misjudgment analysis covers 118 manually audited trajectories, a credible but not scalable methodology. Deployability (4-property check) SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: Runs in a browser on commodity hardware; 256 parallel instances demonstrated on a single server with ~100 GB RAM and <10% CPU, accessible without cloud infrastructure or device farms. - Efficient: ~400 MB per instance vs. ~4.5–6 GB for Android emulators (10× reduction); ~3s cold start vs. ~78s; ~50 MB core disk vs. ~20 GB; code-level judging costs $0 at any scale vs. $2.6–$23 per 256-task VLM-judge evaluation run or $240 per RL step in real-device configurations. - Effective: Deterministic state-diff judging eliminates the 10.2% VLM error rate; the 95.1% sim-to-real gain retention on the 59-task signal subset provides concrete evidence that the simulated environment trains policies with behavioral transfer value, but this is demonstrated for only one 4B model trained for 10 steps. - Scalable: Single-server capacity demonstrated at 256 parallel instances; architecture supports more via additional browser contexts with no OS-image duplication overhead. No stress test beyond 256 instances reported. App development at ~60 person-days for 28 apps is a non-trivial scaling cost for expanding benchmark coverage. The work delivers a software platform (MOBILEGYM) and benchmark (MOBILEGYM-BENCH); code availability is implied by the project page but not explicitly confirmed as open-source in the paper. Strengths - The layered JSON state model (World Data + Runtime Overlay + OS Runtime) elegantly solves readable/writable/forkable/consequence-free state in a single design decision, and the interaction-fidelity-only target is a principled justification for why lightweight simulation is sufficient for agents that observe only screenshots. - The Unexpected Side Effects metric, uniquely enabled by full-environment state comparison, captures a safety-critical property (agent completing a task while also sending unintended messages or deleting unrelated records) that no existing programmatic mobile benchmark provides and that VLM judges cannot reliably detect from screenshots—directly demonstrated in this paper. - The AnswerSheet protocol addresses a concrete, demonstrated failure mode of string-similarity judging with a mechanism that preserves GUI-interaction realism for agents while enabling type-specific deterministic checking; the conservative-gate analysis showing that AnswerSheet pass rate lower-bounds free-text success rate adds non-obvious rigor. - Efficiency advantages are large, concrete, and measured rather than estimated: 10× memory, 25× cold-start time, ~100× disk footprint, and $0 judging cost vs. $100–$850 per 100 GRPO steps with VLM judges. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly and honestly confront three scope boundaries: (1) Visual appearance differences from real apps—subtle layout details, animations, app-specific icons—may impair transfer for tasks requiring exact icon recognition. (2) Backend and dynamic-content modeling is absent: ads, recommendation feeds, fraud checks, latency spikes, and real-time messages are represented as controllable JSON state, not replicated; stochastic backend phenomena are outside the current scope. (3) Each simulated app implements main everyday-use scenarios rather than the full feature surface, and expanding within-app coverage is deferred to future work. The authors also explicitly frame the sim-to-real study as an existence proof rather than a comprehensive transfer analysis, which is a credibility-positive disclosure. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The training gain of +12.8 pt comes from 10 GRPO steps with no learning curve reported; it is unknown whether gains continue, plateau, or degrade with further training, making the result difficult to extrapolate. - The sim-to-real transfer result (95.1%) applies only to the 59-task signal subset, which by construction excludes tasks where neither base nor trained model shows improvement (189 Stable-fail tasks). The transfer rate on the full 256-task test set cannot be inferred from the reported numbers. - Transfer is tested with a single 4B model; whether larger models (8B, 70B) or models with stronger base capability exhibit the same transfer fidelity is untested and likely model-dependent. - The benchmark has a Chinese-centric app composition (WeChat, Alipay, 12306, RedNote, Bilibili, Tencent Meeting dominate the everyday-app set); the RL training experiments use this corpus exclusively, and generalization to Western everyday-app tasks is not evaluated. - No ablation of reward components (side effect penalty, false completion penalty, overdue penalty) is provided; it is unknown which components drive the +12.8 pt gain. - App development cost (~60 person-days for 28 apps) is substantial and poses a genuine bottleneck for expanding app or task coverage; no tooling or automation pipeline for accelerating new app development is described beyond high-level mention of LLM-assisted coding. Bottom Line MOBILEGYM solves a structurally real bottleneck in mobile GUI agent research with a well-engineered and efficiency-verified system; the 95.1% sim-to-real gain retention is a compelling result, but it rests on a single small model trained for 10 steps on a favorable 59-task subset, so practitioners should treat it as a promising proof-of-concept rather than an established engineering specification.
Recommended 2605.26114 PDF
#27 — Innovative (72)

ParaVT: Taming the Tool Prior Paradox for Parallel Tool Use in Agentic Video Reinforcement Learning (29 ▲ )

Zuhao Yang, Kaichen Zhang, Sudong Wang, Keming Wu, Zhongyu Yang, Bo Li, Xiaojuan Qi, Shijian Lu, Xingxuan Li, Lidong Bing · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ParaVT, a multi-agent RL-trained framework that dispatches multiple `crop_video` tool calls in a single turn rather than sequentially, paired with PARA-GRPO, a modified GRPO algorithm that stabilizes format compliance and eliminates the tool-skip reward shortcut during training. - Why it's important: Sequential tool calling in long-video understanding causes error propagation (no peer correction), context bloat (O(K) visual-token re-encoding), and linear inference-cost scaling — problems that grow as video understanding tasks demand more evidence-gathering steps. - How it does it: A main agent emits K parallel `crop_video` calls dispatched to K weight-shared sub-agents that return text summaries; PARA-GRPO adds a selective structural-boundary format reward (Exploration Anchoring) and per-prompt frame-budget randomization (nFrames Gating) to prevent format collapse and tool-skipping reward hacking simultaneously. - The good: The ablation in Table 2 clearly isolates each PARA-GRPO component — Exploration Anchoring alone raises format reward from 0.13 to 0.35, nFrames Gating alone pushes tool-call rate to 1.36 but leaves format stuck at 0.10, and only the full recipe stabilizes both axes — making the necessity of each component empirically clear. - The missing: The Tool Prior Paradox hypothesis rests on a single cross-model pair (Qwen2.5-VL vs. Qwen3-VL), the frame-budget gating set {4,8,16,32,64} is never ablated, and only the `crop_video` tool is evaluated, leaving generalizability to other tool types and model families unverified. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers designing tool-augmented RL post-training pipelines for video LMMs; less relevant for those focused on text-only reasoning or architecture changes.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. This is the most reproducible paper in the cluster: code, data, and model weights are publicly released, driving Rigor (72) up despite no multi-seed runs and cross-protocol GPT-4o comparisons. Non-Obviousness (78) is the high point — the Tool Prior Paradox diagnosis, with formally separated failure modes and a bidirectional tag-reversion experiment that rules out the obvious fix, is a genuinely surprising mechanistic finding. Novelty (72) reflects a new method plus a named empirical finding. Completeness (75) and Resilience (70) are bolstered by an unusually complete negative-results section, tempered by the single Qwen2.5/Qwen3 model pair behind the paradox and the unmeasured sub-agent summary faithfulness. Practical Impact (65) reflects a released artifact but moderate absolute gains (+7.9%) at only 8B scale. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*65 = 72.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Long-video understanding requires identifying the relevant evidence within potentially hours of footage. Tool-augmented LMMs address this by calling `crop_video(start, end)` to inspect temporal segments, but all prior systems dispatch these calls sequentially — one crop per turn, with each turn's visual output accumulated in the running context. This creates three compounding problems: a single miscropped segment propagates errors down a serial chain with no peer to correct it; re-including the full visual context each turn inflates token count as O(K) where K is the number of crops; and inference latency grows linearly with K. The additional gap closed by this work: no prior open-source method dispatches multiple crops in one turn via end-to-end RL training, and no prior work has diagnosed or addressed the instability that pretrained tool priors create when GRPO is applied to a tool-native model. Key Idea The central insight is the Tool Prior Paradox: pretrained tool priors play a paradoxical dual role during RL fine-tuning. They are necessary for tool exploration — a weaker-prior model (Qwen2.5-VL) stays format-perfect under GRPO but emits zero tool calls — but they also cause structural format collapse under temperature sampling, with the policy reverting to the pretrained `<tool_code>` tag rather than the SFT-learned `<tool_call>`, dropping closing tags, and stopping answer generation. The fix is to target structural-boundary tokens specifically with a reward anchoring mechanism (not content tokens), while simultaneously randomizing the visual-frame budget per prompt to ensure that tool calls yield a measurably higher reward than skipping them — creating the gradient signal that vanilla GRPO otherwise averages to zero. Methodology ParaVT uses a two-stage SFT→RL pipeline. SFT cold-start: 97K-sample multi-task corpus (50K general video QA from LLaVA-Video-178K, 22.5K self-curated parallel-tool traces, 12K temporal grounding, 5K long-video reasoning, 5K Gemini-2.5-Flash distillations), with tool-bearing samples comprising 30% of the mix. Sequential LongVT traces are converted to parallel by merging crops on non-overlapping, non-cross-referencing windows and replacing visual responses with text summaries, aligning SFT data with the RL sub-agent's text output format. The step-500 checkpoint is selected as the RL initialization based on format stability under τ=0.7. RL stage: 4,406 disjoint samples across open-ended QA (1,606), MCQ (1,600), and temporal grounding (1,200), pre-filtered using a DAPO-style zero-gradient filter. GRPO samples G=8 rollouts per prompt.
