Practical deployments (persistent surveillance, multi-week missions) involve much longer horizons and likely larger task pools where the behavior of greedy exploration and re-exploitation would differ significantly. - No experiment exceeds n=240 tasks; the O(nd/ρm log n) coverage time implies that at larger n (e.g., n=10,000 tasks), a team of 30 robots would need T_rec ≈ 1,600 rounds before recovery — far exceeding the T=50 shown — yet scalability to realistic task-pool sizes is not tested. - The guessed rank d̂ is drawn from [d, 2d] in all experiments, implicitly assuming the true rank is known to within 2×. In practice, for novel task domains, even an order-of-magnitude estimate of d may be unavailable; the rank-adaptive variant removing this assumption is deferred to follow-up. Bottom Line A researcher building communication-denied multi-robot systems gains a theoretically grounded, open-source method with a clean categorical result, but a practitioner should note that all quantitative evidence is from a synthetic simulator at toy scale, the binding coordination problem under contention is unsolved, and the method's advantage depends on three scope conditions that must be verified before deployment.
Search Results 2605.25584 PDF
#33 — Innovative (71)

The Silent Hyperparameter: Quantifying the Impact of Inference Backends on LLM Reproducibility

David Pape, Jonathan Evertz, Lea Schönherr · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates empirically that the choice of inference backend (vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp, LMDeploy, Ollama) constitutes an undocumented hyperparameter that shifts LLM benchmark scores by up to 16.6 percentage points on identical model weights, hardware, and decoding settings. - Why it's important: LLM benchmarking increasingly separates SOTA claims by fractions of a percent, yet backend-induced variance can dwarf those margins, potentially invalidating comparative rankings and safety guarantees — a blind spot affecting the entire community. - How it does it: By holding model weights, hardware (H100), precision (FP16), decoding (greedy, T=0), batch size (1), and chat templates constant across five inference engines and five models on four benchmarks, then tracing divergence to specific system-level root causes via targeted ablations. - The good: The root cause taxonomy cleanly separates correctable hidden defaults (Ollama's undocumented repetition penalty of 1.1 alone accounts for 11.67pp of DeepSeek R1's performance deficit) from irreducible optimization-induced numerical drift, giving practitioners an actionable remediation path. - The missing: All tested models are ≤30B parameters on single-GPU setups; tensor-parallel inference for 70B+ models introduces additional numerical variance sources (activation partitioning across GPUs) that are entirely absent from the analysis. - Read it or not: Essential reading for anyone who publishes LLM benchmark results, operates a leaderboard, or deploys a model whose evaluation backend differs from its serving backend.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Strongest paper in the cluster on execution quality. Rigor (72) is high — 12-seed averaging, systematic root-cause ablations (BOS injection, repetition penalty, prefix caching, CUDA graphs, kernel tie-breaking), and four robustness ablations spanning hardware (L40), batching, sampling, and safety (JailbreakBench); held back only by code-not-yet-released and the unverified treatment of HF transformers as ground truth. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (72) are genuine — isolating inference backend as a consequential, systematically unreported hyperparameter (up to 16.6pp swing) and cleanly decomposing it into correctable defaults vs. irreducible numerical drift was not predicted by prior reproducibility work. Completeness (75) is a positive: limitations (batch-size-1 lower bound, greedy-decoding caveat for reasoning models) are confronted directly. Resilience (72) is strong because the robustness ablations pre-empt the obvious objections; Impact (60) reflects no released software artifact, though the actionable fix is zero-cost. Composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*60 ≈ 71.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 75 Resilience 72 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM progress is measured through standardized benchmarks where SOTA claims routinely differ by fractions of a percent. Simultaneously, computational cost has driven widespread adoption of specialized inference engines (vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp, etc.) for both research evaluation and production deployment. These engines employ hardware-level optimizations — custom CUDA kernels, paged attention, prefix caching, reduced-precision arithmetic, and engine-specific preprocessing defaults — that can alter token log-probabilities. Prior reproducibility work examined prompt sensitivity, quantization effects, and decoding strategies, but the inference engine itself remained an undocumented variable. The concrete threat is that a claimed benchmark improvement may reflect backend-specific numerical behavior rather than architectural or training superiority, and that a model validated for safety on a reference implementation may behave differently — including less safely — when deployed on a high-throughput engine. Key Idea The core insight is that backend-induced variance decomposes into two mechanistically distinct classes: (1) correctable systematic defaults — undocumented engine behaviors applied before generation (silent BOS token injection, hidden repetition penalties) that directly alter the prompt or token distribution and account for the largest observed performance gaps; and (2) irreducible optimization-induced numerical drift — floating-point non-associativity from prefix caching, CUDA graphs, and custom kernels that compound layer-by-layer and cascade into divergent token selections. The first class can be fixed by standardizing configurations; the second is architecturally intrinsic to high-throughput serving and cannot be eliminated without abandoning the optimizations. Methodology The experimental framework holds model weights, hardware (single NVIDIA H100, later confirmed on L40), model precision (FP16; GGUF-FP16 for llama.cpp/Ollama), decoding strategy (greedy, T=0), batch size (1), and chat templates (Jinja2, applied externally) constant across six backends: HuggingFace transformers (reference), vLLM, SGLang, LMDeploy, llama.cpp, and Ollama. Five models span standard (Llama 3.1 8B, Qwen3 4B, Qwen3 30B) and reasoning (DeepSeek R1 Distill Qwen 7B, Qwen3 Thinking 30B) categories. Four benchmarks cover math (GSM8K), science (GPQA Diamond), factuality (SimpleQA Verified), and code (LiveCodeBench v6). Results are averaged over 12 seeds. Metrics include benchmark accuracy (Max-Min spread across backends), disagreement rate (fraction of inputs yielding a different prediction than the transformers reference), token-level divergence index (position of first mismatched token, normalized), logprob RMSE of the top-1 token on the matching prefix, and Top-5 token Jaccard similarity. Root cause ablations systematically disable prefix caching, CUDA graphs, BOS tokens, repetition penalties, and patch kernel tie-breaking logic. A parallel literature survey applies an LLM-as-judge pipeline (Qwen3-235B-AWQ via vLLM) to 35,000 papers from NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, ACL, and EMNLP (2023–2025) to quantify inference stack reporting frequency. Three critical design decisions: greedy decoding to isolate backend effects (well-justified, acknowledged to disadvantage reasoning models); batch size 1 to prevent batching-induced instability from confounding backend effects (well-justified); external chat template application to prevent template-handling differences (well-justified and necessary). Key Results Backend choice alone shifts GSM8K accuracy by up to 16.60 percentage points (DeepSeek R1 Distill Qwen 7B: 61.87% on Ollama vs. 78.47% on transformers). Llama 3.1 8B on Ollama drops 10.24pp on GSM8K. DeepSeek R1 on Ollama shows a 27.37% disagreement rate on GSM8K — 360 of 1,319 questions answered differently than the reference — even though aggregate accuracy difference appears smaller. Reasoning models diverge earlier: DeepSeek R1 on Ollama first diverges at token position 9 on GPQA (normalized score near 0). Root cause ablations attribute the most extreme Ollama gaps to: hidden BOS token injection (+8.34pp for Llama 3.1, +7.35pp for DeepSeek R1 when removed) and a hidden default repetition penalty of 1.1 (+11.67pp for DeepSeek R1 when disabled). After correcting these defaults, residual optimization-induced variance is <1pp but remains directionally unpredictable and exceeds the margins used to claim SOTA. The safety ablation using JailbreakBench shows DeepSeek R1's attack success rate varies 8.9 percentage points (42.90% to 51.80%) purely from backend switching. Hardware independence is confirmed on L40 GPUs (up to 17.2pp Max-Min). The literature survey of 9,018 relevant papers finds that among the 2,257 papers with no code repository, only 36% name the inference backend; among 6,098 accessible GitHub repositories, 37% (1,778) lack dependency specifications. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW EMPIRICAL METHOD. The identification of inference backend as a consequential and systematically unreported hyperparameter is genuinely novel — no prior work had isolated this specific source of variance at this scale or traced it to specific root causes. The landscape survey (200 engines catalogued) and the LLM-judge pipeline over 35,000 papers contribute new empirical datasets. The individual metrics (RMSE, Jaccard similarity, disagreement rate) are standard; what is novel is their coordinated application to diagnose backend-level numerical effects across a controlled experimental matrix. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are systematic and cover the main candidate root causes (prefix caching, CUDA graphs, BOS injection, repetition penalty, kernel tie-breaking). Averaging over 12 seeds mitigates sampling randomness. Four robustness ablations confirm findings generalize: hardware (L40), batching (batch size 4), stochastic sampling (T=0.7), and safety (JailbreakBench). The LLM judge pipeline was manually verified on 150 papers with 88–96% agreement, and failure modes are characterized. Code and experimental artifacts are promised upon publication but not yet released (preprint status). Methodological concern: the controlled setup (batch size 1, greedy decoding, single GPU) represents a lower bound on real-world variance, which the authors acknowledge. The literature survey uses a single LLM judge with an 88% relevance-filtering agreement rate; the resulting 12% noise propagates into the tier distribution statistics unreported. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: All experiments run on a single NVIDIA H100 or L40 GPU using commercially available hardware and publicly released software. - Efficient: The study itself is computationally expensive (5 backends × 5 models × 4 benchmarks × 12 seeds on an H100), but it produces no new inference method — it diagnoses existing systems. The actionable finding (disable hidden defaults) has zero additional compute cost. - Effective: The evidence is strong for the specific models and benchmarks tested; the root cause ablations confirm that identified mechanisms account for the majority of observed variance in the most extreme cases. - Scalable: Tested only on models up to 30B parameters in single-GPU configurations; behavior at 70B+ under tensor parallelism is untested and is a known gap. The paper does not assess variance across backend version updates within the same engine. The work delivers a controlled experimental methodology, a landscape survey dataset, and concrete reporting recommendations — it does not deliver a software artifact, though code release is planned. Strengths - The dual root-cause taxonomy (correctable defaults vs. irreducible drift) is precise and directly actionable: it tells practitioners what they can eliminate vs. what they must report and accept. - The literature survey at scale (35,000 papers, automated LLM-judge pipeline with manual verification) grounds the claim of widespread underreporting in data rather than anecdote. - The multi-layer diagnostic framework (accuracy, disagreement rate, divergence index, logprob RMSE, Jaccard similarity) reveals that backends with identical aggregate accuracy can answer entirely different subsets of questions correctly — a qualitatively distinct failure mode invisible to accuracy-only comparisons. - The jailbreak safety finding (8.9pp attack success rate variance) is an unexpected and important extension that elevates the paper's stakes beyond academic benchmarking to deployment safety. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The controlled setup (batch size 1, greedy decoding) does not reflect production environments; observed variance is likely a lower bound. Greedy decoding is suboptimal for reasoning models and may artificially inflate their divergence relative to sampling-based evaluation. Ablating every custom kernel is infeasible; completely deterministic execution is often unachievable without abandoning the engine. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All tested models are ≤30B on single-GPU setups; tensor-parallel inference for 70B+ models introduces additional non-associative reduction patterns from activation partitioning across GPUs — potentially the most important unexplored dimension given that many production deployments use large multi-GPU models. - The literature survey statistics carry unquantified noise from the 12% relevance-filtering disagreement rate; error bars on the tier distribution are absent. - Top-p and min-p sampling (common in production) are not tested in the stochastic ablation, only T=0.7 with default nucleus settings. - No analysis of intra-engine version-to-version variance (e.g., vLLM 0.8 vs. 0.10) — arguably the most practically relevant reproducibility threat for longitudinal research, since benchmarks published months apart may use different engine versions. - The paper implicitly frames HuggingFace transformers as the ground-truth reference without establishing its own numerical stability across versions or hardware, which is an unverified assumption underlying the disagreement rate metric. Bottom Line Any researcher who publishes LLM benchmark numbers, operates a leaderboard, or deploys a model whose evaluation stack differs from its serving stack should read this paper — the evidence that backend choice alone produces variance larger than most reported SOTA margins is direct and hard to dismiss.
Recommended 2605.19537 PDF
#34 — Innovative (71)

Neural Scalable Symbolic Search Framework for Complex Logical Queries with Multiple Free Variables

arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces NS3 (Neural Scalable Symbolic Search), a budgeted framework that enables direct joint tuple ranking for existential first-order logic queries with k free variables (EFOk) over incomplete knowledge graphs, without enumerating the full Cartesian product E^k. - Why it's important: All prior CQA benchmarks and methods evaluate EFOk queries via per-variable marginal rankings, which cannot eliminate spurious tuple combinations — e.g., true answers {(1,3),(2,4)} yield marginals {1,2} and {3,4}, which cannot rule out invalid tuple (1,2) — making existing benchmarks a poor proxy for the actual task. - How it does it: NS3 first solves marginalized sub-queries to prune each variable's candidate domain, then merges free variables into hypernodes with a Cartesian product domain bounded by a budget B, and applies a greedy progressive merge ordering to reduce any EFOk query to an EFO1 query solved via existing neural symbolic search. - The good: On the new joint-ranking benchmark, NS3(J) achieves HIT@10 of 47.2% on FB15K vs. FIT's 14.1% — approximately 3× improvement — while running 2–4× faster and avoiding the out-of-memory failures that prevent FIT from running on larger KGs. - The missing: Experiments are capped at k=3; the geometric-mean budget allocation heuristic lacks theoretical justification; and the new benchmark was constructed by the same group proposing NS3, creating mild evaluation circularity. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers in KG reasoning and complex query answering, as it cleanly exposes the marginal-vs-joint evaluation gap, provides a practical remedy with strong empirical support, and releases both code and a new benchmark.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (78) is the strongest dimension — cleanly exposing the marginal-vs-joint evaluation gap for EFOk queries is close to a new problem formulation, and the marginalize-merge-budget reduction with a commutativity proof (Prop D.1) is a non-obvious (75) algorithmic contribution. Rigor (68) is the limiter: single runs with no error bars, only HIT@10 (no MRR), inclusion of leakage-prone FB15K, and a benchmark constructed by the same group create mild circularity. Completeness (68) and Resilience (68) are held back by the unjustified geometric-mean budget heuristic and the greedy merge order lacking approximation bounds; Impact (65) reflects released code + a new benchmark in a niche KG-reasoning domain. Composite: 0.20*78 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*65 ≈ 71.
empirical Novelty 78 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 68 Resilience 68 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge graphs are widely used for structured reasoning in web and enterprise applications, but large-scale KGs are substantially incomplete. Complex Query Answering (CQA) trains models to predict answers to first-order logical queries over these incomplete graphs. Prior work almost exclusively addressed EFO1 queries (one free variable), where the answer is a single entity. EFOk queries with k free variables return entity tuples — enabling applications such as fraud ring detection (find pairs of entities jointly satisfying a financial-transaction pattern). The core difficulty is that the candidate answer space grows as |E|^k: for FB15K-237 (~15K entities) and k=2, this is ~225M candidate pairs. The only existing EFOk benchmark, EFOk-CQA, sidesteps joint ranking by evaluating each variable marginally, but this conflates good marginal ranking with good tuple ranking — a pair of independently good entities may form an invalid joint answer. Key Idea: Any valid joint answer tuple must project onto valid marginal answers: marginalization is a necessary but not sufficient condition for joint validity. This allows safe candidate pruning — by solving k marginalized EFO1 sub-queries, one can retain only the top-B entities per variable without losing gold tuples (99% recall at B=4000 on FB15K-237). Merging these pruned per-variable domains into a single hypernode with a bounded joint domain converts the EFOk query into an EFO1 query, which existing neural symbolic search can solve exactly over the reduced domain. Methodology: NS3 operates in three algorithmic phases layered on top of a neural link predictor (NLISA is used as the backbone). (1) Marginalization: For each free variable yi, an EFO1 marginal query is constructed by existentially quantifying all other free variables. These are solved via fuzzy vector propagation over the query graph (product norm conjunction/disjunction/negation), producing per-variable soft entity scores. A formal commutativity result (Proposition D.1) guarantees that these marginal answer sets correctly project from the true joint answer set. (2) Merge Transformation: Two variables y1, y2 with fuzzy counts C1 = Σ Cy1[i] and C2 = Σ Cy2[i] are merged into a hypernode γ. Budget allocation uses the geometric mean heuristic: λ = √(2B/(C1·C2)), b1 = λC1, b2 = λC2, so b1·b2 ≈ 2B. The top-b1 and top-b2 entities form the reduced joint domain of size ≤ 2B. Edges between merged variables become self-loop edges on γ; constraint propagation to γ uses element-wise masking over the joint domain (constraint slicing), avoiding explicit enumeration of E². (3) Progressive Budgeting for k > 2: A greedy algorithm merges variable pairs in order of smallest estimated marginal domain product (most constrained pair first), progressively reducing EFOk → EFOk-1 → ... → EFO1. Budget for a hypernode of nγ variables is nγ·B, ensuring linear budget growth with k. Overall time complexity is O(nk³B²) — polynomial in k for fixed B. Critical design decisions: the geometric mean budget allocation is not theoretically justified but empirically sound; the greedy merge order is a heuristic without approximation bounds; zero-scoring of pruned tuples introduces systematic false negatives for gold tuples dropped during marginal pruning. Key Results: On the existing EFOk-CQA benchmark (FB15K-237, k=2, marginal evaluation): NS3(M) achieves 42.8% average HIT@10 on the Marginal metric vs. FIT's 41.2%, and 6.6% on the Joint metric vs. FIT's 5.9% — modest but consistent improvements. On the new scalable EFOk benchmark (14 query types, k=2 and k=3, three KGs): NS3(J) outperforms all baselines on nearly all 14 query types and all 3 KGs. Avg HIT@10 across all query types: FB15K-237 — NS3(J) 17.6% vs. FIT 6.4%; FB15K — NS3(J) 47.2% vs. FIT 14.1%; NELL — NS3(J) 28.0% vs. FIT 16.6%. On k=3 queries specifically, NS3(J) achieves 13.8% (FB15K-237), 36.2% (FB15K), and 23.8% (NELL) vs. FIT at 5.0%, 10.7%, and 16.5%. Runtime: NS3 completes in 16–40 minutes per dataset vs. FIT's 22–164 minutes; FIT OOMs on V100 32GB for NELL and FB15K. At B=4000, gold tuple recall is 99.0% (FB15K-237), 98.8% (FB15K), and 93.1% (NELL) — coverage that uses only ~4% of the full EFO2 search space for FB15K-237. Progressive budgeting outperforms simultaneous merge at k=3: 13.72 vs. 11.58 (FB15K-237), 40.22 vs. 27.56 (FB15K), 27.34 vs. 23.96 (NELL). Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The novel contribution is the marginalize-merge-budget pipeline that, for the first time, enables direct joint ranking for EFOk queries with polynomial complexity in k; the paper also releases a benchmark extending EFOk-CQA to k=3 with 14 query types. The fuzzy logic framework and the neural link predictor backbone (NLISA) are existing components; NS3 wraps them in a genuinely new search reduction strategy. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present on two key components: budget size (Figure 5, Tables 4/7) and progressive vs. simultaneous merge order (Table 5/9). Gold tuple recall as a function of B is explicitly measured (Table 6), which is an appropriate diagnostic for the pruning step. No error bars or multiple random seeds are reported; all results appear to be single runs. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/HKUST-KnowComp/NS3_KDD2026 and archived at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20355345. Experiments run on a single V100 32GB GPU — a standard research configuration. FB15K is included despite its known test-set leakage issues, though results are consistent across FB15K-237 and NELL, which do not have this problem. FIT is excluded from NELL and FB15K results due to OOM, which the paper discloses transparently. The new benchmark is authored by the same group proposing NS3, creating a potential Goodhart's Law concern — although query types are structurally enumerated rather than tuned to NS3's specific strengths. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Runs on a single commodity V100 32GB GPU; no distributed infrastructure required. - Efficient: 2–4× faster than FIT (the only prior method supporting joint reasoning) while avoiding FIT's OOM failures on larger KGs; ConE is 8–10× faster but only performs marginal inference. - Effective: Joint HIT@10 improvements of 2–4× over FIT on the joint-ranking benchmark are consistent across three KGs and 14 query types; the evidence is credible, not marginal. - Scalable: Complexity is O(nk³B²) — polynomial in k for fixed B — but the experiments cover only k≤3 on KGs with at most 63K entities; no evidence is provided for k=5+ or entity sets of 10^6+. The paper delivers a software artifact (code + new benchmark dataset) alongside the method description. Strengths: - The commutativity proof (Proposition D.1) makes the pruning step formally sound: any gold tuple excluded from the marginal candidate sets cannot be a valid joint answer, so no false negatives are introduced by the pruning step itself (only by the approximation of truncating marginal scores at top-B). - The new benchmark fills a concrete gap: EFOk-CQA previously supported only k=2 and forced marginal evaluation; the 14-query extension with explicit joint-ranking evaluation creates a meaningful test bed for future work. - The budgeted approach is practically efficient: at B=4000 on FB15K-237, 99% of gold tuples are retained while searching only 4% of the full EFO2 space — a favorable recall-efficiency tradeoff. - Progressive merge ordering beats simultaneous merge by up to 31% relative on FB15K at k=3, demonstrating that merge order is a non-trivial algorithmic choice. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that experiments are limited to k≤3 due to benchmark availability, and explicitly state that extending to larger k is future work. They acknowledge that marginal ranking remains suboptimal relative to direct joint ranking and that pruned tuples are assigned score zero, introducing false negatives for borderline candidates. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The geometric mean budget allocation heuristic (bi proportional to Ci via √(2B/(C1·C2))) has no theoretical justification beyond the constraint b1·b2 ≈ 2B. No comparison with uniform split, entropy-weighted, or learned allocation is provided. - The greedy progressive merge order lacks approximation guarantees — there is no bound on how far the greedy solution diverges from the optimal merge ordering that minimizes intermediate domain size. - The new benchmark is built and evaluated by the same group proposing NS3. While query types are structurally motivated, an independently constructed evaluation set would provide stronger claims. - Evaluation on the new benchmark uses only HIT@10; MRR is not reported, making it impossible to assess ranking quality for answers outside the top-10. - The ~6.9% recall loss at B=4000 on NELL (Table 6) is not analyzed further — it is unclear whether this loss is concentrated in specific query types (e.g., sparse cyclic queries) or is broadly distributed. Bottom Line: A KG reasoning practitioner gains concrete, actionable value from this paper: it identifies and quantifies a real evaluation gap, delivers a 2–4× accuracy improvement on joint ranking with faster runtime than the prior symbolic baseline, and ships code and a new benchmark — this is a credible engineering and research contribution, not just an incremental score bump.
Search Results 2605.25985 PDF
#35 — Innovative (71)

B-cos GNNs: Faithful Explanations through Dynamic Linearity

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces B-cos GNNs, a class of inherently explainable graph neural networks that yield exact per-node, per-feature prediction decompositions from a single forward-backward pass by replacing all ReLU-MLPs in the GIN framework with B-cos transforms. - Why it's important: Post-hoc GNN explainability methods are slow and provably unfaithful; existing inherently interpretable GNNs add architectural complexity (auxiliary subgraph generators, modified objectives) that B-cos GNNs eliminate, making faithful explanations free at inference time. - How it does it: B-cos transforms satisfy "dynamic linearity" — their output equals an input-dependent linear map W̃(x)·x — and sum-based aggregation preserves this property end-to-end, so the full GNN prediction decomposes exactly into per-node contributions without any approximation or auxiliary network. - The good: B-cos GINs achieve Jaccard@k of 0.84, 0.96, and 0.91 on BA-2Motif, Di-Halo-Benzene, and MNIST-75sp respectively, outperforming all baselines including GSAT (0.57, 0.48, 0.61), while explaining at ~0.6–1.2 ms/graph versus 290–327 ms for GNNExplainer. - The missing: Evaluation is restricted to small graphs (24–119 average nodes); the required materialization of the full Jacobian W*(X,A) scales quadratically with graph size, and no evidence is provided that the method is tractable on graphs with thousands of nodes. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on GNN explainability or inherently interpretable models — the theoretical foundation is sound and the results are the strongest reported for this setting — but hold off on deploying it if your graphs are large or if you need mean/max/attention aggregation or edge-level attributions.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (78) and Rigor (70) drive the composite. The dynamic-linearity insight — that B-cos transforms compose into a single input-dependent linear map and that GIN's sum aggregation preserves this — is genuinely non-obvious and yields exact, by-construction faithfulness rather than an approximation (Lemma 1 + Proposition 1 are real formal development). Rigor is strong for a preprint: five seeds with std devs, a B-sensitivity sweep, and disciplined RINGS-filtered benchmark selection with a purpose-built Di-Halo-Benzene dataset; it is pulled down by no released code, the author-constructed dataset not yet being public, an unreproducible GSAT baseline, and no ablation on aggregation/depth. Novelty (72) is a principled extension of a known CV technique to graphs rather than a new problem class, and Completeness (75) benefits from honest, specific limitation acknowledgment. Resilience (70) is high on the faithfulness claim but capped by untested quadratic-scaling tractability and the unknown-k operationalization gap; Impact (60) reflects architectural principles with no shipped artifact. Composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*60 = 71.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 75 Resilience 70 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation GNN deployment in drug discovery, scientific reasoning, and safety-critical systems requires explanations that faithfully reflect the model's computation, not post-hoc approximations. Three families of methods exist: gradient-based post-hoc attribution (Saliency Maps, Integrated Gradients, GradCAM, LRP), which approximate the model; optimization-based post-hoc methods (GNNExplainer, PGExplainer, SubgraphX), which are slow and fragile and have been shown to perform no better than random edge ranking despite strong AUROC; and inherently interpretable GNNs (IB-subgraph, DIR, GSAT), which build explanation into the model but require auxiliary subgraph generators, rationale modules, or information-bottleneck objectives, adding architectural complexity and modified training objectives. No existing method achieves exact, faithful per-node decomposition of GNN predictions without auxiliary machinery. Key Idea The B-cos transform's output can be written exactly as W̃(x)·x, where W̃ depends only on the current input and the (normalized) weights — a property called dynamic linearity. Compositions of dynamically linear functions are themselves dynamically linear (with the composed weight matrix being the product of the per-layer dynamic weights). Because GIN's sum aggregation and the affine skip connection (1+ε)x_i + Σ_j x_j are constant linear operations that preserve dynamic linearity, replacing every ReLU-MLP in GIN with a B-cos network means the entire model's prediction is a single input-dependent linear map of the original node features, making per-node contributions directly readable from the Jacobian in one backward pass. Methodology The B-cos transform replaces a standard (bias-free) linear layer W with f_{(W,B)}(x)_j = ||ŵ_j|| ||x|| |cos(x, ŵ_j)|^B · sgn(cos(x, ŵ_j)), where B ∈ [1,∞) controls alignment pressure and ŵ_j is the unit-norm version of the j-th row. For B=1 the transform is linear; B>1 introduces non-linearity that suppresses low-cosine-similarity activations sharply. The transform's dynamic weight matrix is W̃(x) = diag(|c(x,Ŵ)|^{B-1})·Ŵ. The architectural recipe has three stages: (1) Replace each MLP in GIN with a stack of B-cos transforms. (2) Keep sum aggregation and the affine skip connection unchanged — both are constant linear operations that preserve dynamic linearity (Lemma 1, proved by induction in Appendix B). (3) Use a B-cos readout module. The composition of all components produces ŷ = W(X,A)·x, and the contribution map M(X,A) = W*(X,A) ⊙ x gives per-node, per-feature contributions summing exactly to the model output; top-k nodes by aggregated contribution form the explanatory subgraph. Edge features are handled as additive offsets (x_j + e_{ji}) before aggregation, preserving dynamic linearity but producing node-level (not edge-level) attributions. Three critical design decisions: (1) Sum aggregation is required — mean, max, and attention aggregation would break dynamic linearity and are left to future work. (2) B=2 is selected as the default after sensitivity analysis over B ∈ {1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0}; B=1 (effectively linear) underperforms on predictive accuracy, and B>2.5 begins to hurt on real-world chemistry datasets while continuing to improve synthetic explanation accuracy. (3) Edge features as additive offsets is the simplest dynamic-linearity-preserving extension; the authors acknowledge it sacrifices edge-level attribution. Key Results All results are from five independent random seeds. Explainability (Jaccard@k, k=|G*|): BA-2Motif — B-cos 0.84±0.02 vs. GNNExplainer 0.57, IG 0.54, GSAT 0.57; Di-Halo-Benzene — B-cos 0.96±0.01 vs. GNNExplainer 0.36, IG 0.83, GSAT 0.48; MNIST-75sp — B-cos 0.91±0.01 vs. GNNExplainer 0.34, IG 0.63, GSAT 0.61. AUROC gains are consistent. Explanation speed: B-cos requires ~0.62–1.17 ms/graph (one forward+backward pass) versus GNNExplainer at 290–327 ms (~470× slower) and IG at 62–78 ms (~70× slower); GSAT is marginally faster at 0.44–0.72 ms (single forward pass). Predictive accuracy (F1): modest losses on NCI1 (0.80 vs. 0.72) and OGB-MolHIV (0.79 vs. 0.73), a gain on MNIST-75sp (0.86 vs. 0.93), and a tie on PATTERN (0.69 vs. 0.69). The GSAT baseline is from retrained models because the published GSAT performance could not be reproduced under the shared backbone. All benchmarks involve small graphs (average 24–119 nodes); Di-Halo-Benzene is a custom-constructed dataset provided as supplementary material. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The B-cos transform (Böhle et al., CVPR 2022) and GIN (Xu et al., ICLR 2019) are both prior work; the novel contribution is the formal characterization of the conditions under which GNNs inherit dynamic linearity (Lemma 1 + Proposition 1), the architectural instantiation in GIN/GINE, and the empirical demonstration that this yields substantially better structural alignment with ground-truth rationales than competing methods. This is a principled extension of an existing interpretability technique to graphs, with non-trivial formal development, rather than a straightforward combination of known ideas. Rigor & Reproducibility Five independent seeds are used throughout with standard deviations reported — adequate for this scale. Sensitivity analysis over B is provided (Fig. 2). No code is released: the paper states "We do not release code but provide a reproducibility statement in Appendix G" and commits to public release upon acceptance. The Di-Halo-Benzene dataset is provided as supplementary material but is not yet publicly accessible. GSAT baselines are retrained because the published numbers could not be reproduced under the shared backbone — the paper is transparent about this but it raises questions about whether GSAT was optimally configured. Ablations on the B hyperparameter are thorough; however, there are no ablations on aggregation method, number of layers, or hidden dimension. The metrics (Jaccard@k, AUROC) measure plausibility — alignment with a known ground-truth rationale — not faithfulness to the model's computation. For B-cos GNNs, faithfulness is guaranteed by construction; for all baselines, it is not, and recent work (Amara et al., GInX-Eval) suggests popular post-hoc methods can be no better than random ranking despite high AUROC. The paper explicitly acknowledges this distinction and cites GInX-Eval. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — GNN architecture evaluated on graph classification benchmarks. - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A100-PCIE-40GB; given the small graph sizes (24–119 average nodes) and compact architecture (64–128 hidden channels, 3–4 layers), a consumer GPU would likely suffice for inference. - Efficient: Explanation cost is one forward+backward pass (~0.6–1.2 ms/graph), essentially identical to standard GIN inference plus one backprop; the B-cos transform adds no trainable parameters beyond removing bias terms and fixing ε=0. - Effective: Contribution maps are exactly faithful to the model's computation by construction (not an approximation), and achieve the highest Jaccard@k on all three benchmarks with ground-truth rationales; predictive accuracy is within single-digit F1 points of vanilla GIN on most benchmarks. - Scalable: Untested beyond graphs with ~119 average nodes; computing W*(X,A) requires forming an n×(n·p₀) matrix, which grows quadratically with node count, and the paper makes no claim about or evidence for tractability on graphs with thousands of nodes. The work delivers architectural design principles; no software artifact is released with this submission. Strengths - The formal proof (Lemma 1 + Proposition 1 by induction) that sum-aggregation GNNs composed of B-cos transforms preserve dynamic linearity is rigorous and provides a genuine theoretical grounding — the exact faithfulness guarantee is not a claim but a mathematical consequence of the construction. - Faithfulness is established by construction rather than measured or approximated, closing the gap that makes post-hoc methods unreliable in high-stakes applications. - The Jaccard@k improvements over GSAT are large and consistent (0.84 vs. 0.57, 0.96 vs. 0.48, 0.91 vs. 0.61) despite GSAT using a strictly more complex architecture; this is a strong empirical signal. - Benchmark selection is disciplined: the authors apply the RINGS framework to filter out trivially solvable datasets, construct Di-Halo-Benzene to require genuine structure-feature interdependence, and select four distinct graph domains, reducing the risk of cherry-picked evaluation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront four limitations: (1) the method is restricted to sum aggregation — mean, max, and attention-based aggregators are incompatible with the current dynamic linearity argument; (2) edge features produce node-level but not edge-level contribution maps; (3) a moderate predictive cost is incurred on NCI1 and OGB-MolHIV; (4) GSAT's published predictive performance could not be reproduced under the shared backbone. These are acknowledged clearly and with appropriate specificity, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The approach is evaluated only on graphs averaging 24–119 nodes. Materializing W*(X,A) as an n×(n·p₀) matrix and computing its backward pass grow quadratically in the number of nodes; no computational complexity analysis or empirical runtime curve at scale is provided, and there is no evidence the method is tractable on graphs with thousands of nodes typical in molecular property prediction at scale or social networks. - LRP (layer-wise relevance propagation) is not included as a baseline despite being a well-established gradient-based attribution method applied to GNNs. Only IG is included as the "primary attribution baseline," which may not represent the full gradient-based frontier. - The Di-Halo-Benzene dataset is author-constructed and not yet publicly available for independent verification; claims on this benchmark cannot currently be reproduced by third parties. - In deployment, k (the explanation size) is unknown because |G| is not available without ground-truth labels. The paper evaluates only at k = |G|, giving no guidance on how to select k in practice, which is a significant operationalization gap. - B-cos GINs outperform GSAT consistently, but GSAT could not be reproduced at its published accuracy; if GSAT had been optimally tuned, the margin might be smaller. The comparison is fair given the authors' transparency, but the absolute position of GSAT in the ranking is uncertain. - No evaluation on molecular graph regression tasks or node-level tasks beyond PATTERN, limiting the generalizability claim to classification. Bottom Line A clean, theoretically grounded, and empirically convincing paper that establishes B-cos GNNs as the current best method for faithful GNN explanation without auxiliary machinery; practitioners who need mean/max/attention aggregation, edge-level attributions, or large-graph scalability should track the follow-up extensions promised in the conclusion before adopting the method.
Search Results 2605.19778 PDF
#36 — Innovative (71)

PiD: Fast and High-Resolution Latent Decoding with Pixel Diffusion (22 ▲ )

Yifan Lu, Qi Wu, Jay Zhangjie Wu, Zian Wang, Huan Ling, Sanja Fidler, Xuanchi Ren · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PiD, a pixel diffusion decoder that replaces reconstruction-oriented VAE decoding with latent-conditioned pixel-space diffusion, unifying latent decoding and super-resolution upsampling into a single 4-step generative module. - Why it's important: Conventional VAE decoders are designed for reconstruction, not synthesis — they cannot recover fine-grained details lost during encoding or correct artifacts in generated latents, and they run out of memory at megapixel resolution, requiring expensive cascaded SR pipelines that add 700–2000 ms of latency. - How it does it: PiD conditions a pretrained high-resolution pixel-space diffusion transformer (PixelDiT, 1.3B parameters) on noise-corrupted latents via a lightweight ControlNet-style adapter with sigma-aware gating, then distills the multi-step model into a 4-step student via DMD2, eliminating the decode-then-upsample cascade entirely. - The good: PiD decodes a 512×512 latent to a 2048×2048 image in 211 ms on a GB200 GPU with 13 GB peak memory, compared to 724–2155 ms for diffusion-based SR cascades, while scoring best or second-best on all eight no-reference IQA metrics and achieving 79–99% win rates against all baselines as judged by three closed-source MLLMs (Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3 Flash, GPT 5.5). - The missing: There is no user study with human raters — quality assessment relies entirely on no-reference IQA metrics and MLLM judges, which are themselves potential proxies rather than ground truth for perceptual quality; alignment/fidelity to the original prompt at 4K scale is not quantified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners in high-resolution image synthesis who want a practical, production-ready replacement for decode-then-upsample cascades, particularly those using FLUX or RAE-based architectures.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Practical Impact (80) leads: PiD is a production-ready, drop-in replacement for decode-then-upsample cascades with a 5–14× latency win, fixed ~13 GB memory, and demonstrated cross-latent-space generality (FLUX VAEs, SD3, DINOv2, SigLIP) — a genuinely large deployment footprint, though weights/code are not released. Rigor (72) is the strongest in the cluster: a three-judge order-swapped MLLM protocol with consistency reporting, ablations isolating the two critical components (T2I prior, sigma-aware gate), and a thorough step sweep — tempered by no error bars/seeds, no-reference IQA only, and vendor (NVIDIA) self-report on GB200. Non-Obviousness (72) reflects the unifying insight that latent decoding and super-resolution are one conditional-generation problem, plus the sigma-aware noisy-latent gate enabling early-exit. Novelty (65) is a strong composition of prior components rather than a new formulation; Completeness (70) and Resilience (70) are solid but dinged by 4K results being only qualitative, no content-fidelity anchor, and the apples-to-oranges 14.3× claim vs a 32B model. Composite: 0.20*65 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*80 = 71.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 70 Resilience 70 Impact 80
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Latent diffusion models generate images in a compressed latent space and rely on a fixed VAE decoder to recover pixel-space images. This decoder is reconstruction-optimized: it inverts the encoder rather than synthesizing detail, propagates or amplifies latent artifacts, and scales poorly to megapixel resolution (FLUX.1 VAE runs out of memory on a standard 80 GB GPU beyond ~2500×2500 with tiling disabled). High-resolution pipelines therefore bolt on a separate diffusion super-resolution stage (e.g., SeedVR2, TSD-SR, InvSR), adding 700–2000 ms of latency and increasing complexity. Representation autoencoders (RAEs) that use semantic encoders like DINOv2 or SigLIP compound the problem: their latents capture semantics but under-specify fine texture, requiring a decoder with genuine generative capacity — something no reconstruction-oriented decoder provides. The gap being closed is the absence of a unified, efficient, generative latent-to-pixel interface that works across both VAE and RAE latent spaces. Key Idea: The core insight is that latent decoding and spatial upsampling are both conditional generation problems, and a pretrained pixel-space diffusion model already has a strong prior for high-resolution texture synthesis; conditioning it on a (possibly noisy) latent via a lightweight adapter converts it into a decoder that simultaneously reconstructs global structure from the latent and synthesizes missing high-frequency detail from its generative prior. By training with noise-corrupted latents (σ sampled uniformly over [0, 0.8]) and a sigma-aware gate that modulates injection strength based on latent reliability, the decoder naturally handles both fully denoised and partially denoised latents, enabling early exit from the base LDM without a separate pipeline branch. Methodology: PiD is built on PixelDiT, an MMDiT-style pixel-space diffusion transformer (1.3B parameters, patch size 16, hidden size 1536, 14 MM-DiT blocks + 2 PiT pixel blocks). Training proceeds in three stages. Stage 1: fine-tune PixelDiT from its 1024×1024 checkpoint on 2.6M high-quality images (MultiAspect-4K-1M + rendered PDFs + internal data, Q-Align filtered) at 2K resolution with NTK-aware RoPE for spatial extrapolation, using a standard rectified-flow objective (batch 128, LR 2×10⁻⁵, 20,000 iterations, 128 H100 GPUs × 1 day). Stage 2: attach a ControlNet-style convolutional adapter (Conv2d + 4 residual blocks, 512 channels) that spatially upscales the noisy latent z̃_σ = (1−σ)z + σξ to the patch grid, flattens to tokens, and injects them every two DiT blocks via a sigma-aware gate g = sigmoid(Linear([h,l]) − α·σ). The gate is zero-initialized so training starts from the pretrained text-to-image behavior. Joint fine-tuning uses batch 64, LR 5×10⁻⁵, 30,000 iterations, 64 H100 GPUs × 0.5 day, with 10% caption and 10% latent-condition dropout for CFG training. For vision-encoder (RAE) latents, the backbone is kept frozen to prevent color drift. Stage 3: DMD2 distillation into a 4-step student with a DiT discriminator (26 blocks, hidden 1536), using the sigma schedule {0.999, 0.866, 0.634, 0.342} and projected GAN regularization. Critical design decisions: (1) noisy latent conditioning — justified by ablation (sigma-aware gate removal raises NIQE from 5.43 to 5.84 and lowers PSNR from 25.00 to 24.28 in the pre-distillation model); (2) the T2I pixel prior — justified by ablation (removing it collapses NIQE from 5.43 to 7.79 and VQ-R1 from 4.649 to 2.587); (3) per-block injection every two blocks rather than all blocks — not explicitly ablated. The 4K variant uses 96 GB200 GPUs with context parallelism and the same recipe. Key Results: Across six distinct latent spaces (FLUX.1 VAE, SD3 VAE, FLUX.2 VAE, Z-Image FLUX.1 VAE, DINOv2 RAE from DiTDH, SigLIP RAE from Scale-RAE), PiD at 24–50 inference steps (teacher) achieves the best or tied-best NIQE score in every configuration and competitive MUSIQ, DEQA, MANIQA, QAlign, Unipercept IAA/IQA, and VQ-R1 scores. The 4-step distilled student (PiD 24/28 FLUX.1) scores MUSIQ 73.26, NIQE 3.50, DEQA 4.31, MANIQA 0.54, QAlign 4.74, Uni-IAA 66.21, Uni-IQA 75.21, VQ-R1 4.68 — matching or beating the 50-step teacher on every perceptual metric while running at 211 ms (compiled, GB200) versus 2084–2155 ms for SeedVR2-3B and TSD-SR. The distilled student outperforms all multi-step teacher variants on IQA metrics but achieves lower PSNR/SSIM for small-text reconstruction (24.19 vs. 25.00 PSNR), indicating a perceptual-fidelity tradeoff. MLLM win rates (Claude Opus 4.6 / Gemini 3 Flash / GPT 5.5): 85.4–88.8% / 85.1–90.1% / 79.0–80.5% against the strongest diffusion SR methods (InvSR, TSD-SR), with 95.4–99.9% wins against Real-ESRGAN and LUA. Memory: PiD uses 13.0 GB (compiled, 2048×2048) versus the FLUX.1 VAE which exceeds 16.7 GB at the same resolution and OOMs at 4K on 80 GB GPUs; PiD stays under 22.5 GB at 4096×4096. End-to-end: FLUX.2(512) + PiD = 7.1s per 2K sample on GB200, versus native FLUX.2 2K at 102.2s (14.3×) and PixelDiT at 13.3s (1.87×). Baselines include the current strongest diffusion SR models (SeedVR2-3B, TSD-SR, InvSR-1), LUA, SSDD, and Real-ESRGAN — a reasonably strong and current set. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The key novelty is treating latent decoding as a unified conditional pixel diffusion problem and the sigma-aware noisy latent conditioning that allows a single trained model to handle both fully denoised and partially denoised latents. The individual components (ControlNet-style adapters, DMD2 distillation, rectified flow, PixelDiT, NTK-aware RoPE) are all prior work; the genuine contribution is their composition into an end-to-end decode-and-upsample generative module and the sigma-aware gating mechanism that bridges base LDM noise levels to decoder conditioning. Applying pixel-space diffusion as a decoder is not new (ε-VAE, SSDD), but scaling it to 4× and 8× upsampling with cross-latent-space generality and achieving sub-second latency is. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are provided for the two most critical components (T2I prior and sigma-aware gate) on the FLUX.1 decoder task and small-text reconstruction, with results across 11 metrics — adequate for the two main design choices, though the injection frequency (every-2-blocks vs. every-block vs. every-4-blocks) and σ_max are not ablated. The decoding step sweep is thorough (50/25/12/8/4 steps for teacher, student). Error bars, multiple seeds, and statistical significance tests are absent throughout. IQA evaluation metrics are no-reference measures computed over a test set of unstated size; no ground truth distribution is available at 2K. The MLLM judgment protocol is well-specified (two-round order-swap, consistency rate, three independent judge models) and substantially stronger than single-model evaluation. No code, model weights, or dataset release is mentioned. The paper is an arXiv preprint from NVIDIA (May 2026); results are vendor self-reports on hardware (GB200) not universally available. The training data includes internally procured images with no disclosure of licensing or content filtering beyond Q-Align quality scoring. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL/SYSTEMS — a trained model evaluated by deployment latency and quality. - Practical: Runs on an RTX 5090 with 13 GB VRAM at 2048×2048 output; at 4096×4096 requires 22.5 GB with torch.compile, which fits on current high-end consumer and data-center GPUs. - Efficient: 211 ms (compiled, GB200) and 979 ms (compiled, RTX 5090) for 2048×2048 decoding — approximately 5–10× faster than diffusion SR cascades at a model size of 1.3B parameters, with fixed ~13 GB peak memory regardless of resolution up to 2K (VAE OOMs at comparable scales). - Effective: Evidence across 8 no-reference IQA metrics and MLLM pairwise judgments consistently favors PiD over all baselines; small-text PSNR/SSIM lags the multi-step teacher, indicating slightly reduced pixel-level fidelity in detail-critical regions. - Scalable: Tested up to 4096×4096 on GB200 GPUs with qualitative results only — no quantitative IQA or latency numbers are reported at 4K; context parallelism across 96 GPUs is needed for 4K training, which is not a consumer-accessible scale. Delivers a trained model system; no software library, CLI, or public checkpoint is announced. Strengths: - The sigma-aware noisy latent conditioning is a practically motivated and well-ablated design choice that elegantly solves the early-termination use case without any architectural modification at inference time. - The cross-latent-space generality — the same architecture and training recipe works for FLUX.1 VAE, SD3 VAE, DINOv2, SigLIP, and Z-Image latents — substantially increases the deployment footprint beyond a single base model. - The MLLM evaluation protocol (three independent judge models, two-round order swap, consistency rate reporting) is more methodologically rigorous than standard FID-based comparisons and mitigates single-judge bias. - Memory efficiency is a concrete engineering win: under 22.5 GB at 4096×4096 versus the VAE OOMing at approximately 2500×2500 on 80 GB GPUs, enabling 4K in a single GPU budget. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that the distilled 4-step student achieves slightly lower PSNR/SSIM than the multi-step teacher on small-text reconstruction, indicating that the distillation trades pixel-level fidelity for perceptual quality. They also note that decoding partially denoised latents introduces an optimal termination step (last 3–5 steps of FLUX.1's 28-step process); terminating too early degrades quality. Beyond these, the paper does not discuss content control limitations, prompt-following degradation at high upscaling factors, or the absence of public weights and code. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The evaluation relies entirely on no-reference IQA metrics (MUSIQ, NIQE, DEQA, MANIQA, QAlign) and MLLM judges — both of which can be gamed by sharpening or texture hallucination that looks vivid but is factually wrong. No content-fidelity measure (e.g., DINO-based content similarity, LPIPS against the VAE-decoded image as a fidelity anchor) is reported for the generation case (only for reconstruction). The test set size is not stated. At 4K, only qualitative results are presented with no IQA scores or latency profiling beyond Table 3, making the 4K claim largely anecdotal. The memory numbers for VAE at 4K (OOM on 80 GB) compare against an un-tiled implementation; tiled VAE is a standard workaround that would reduce the memory advantage claim. The 14.3× speedup against native FLUX.2 2K is measured against a 32B parameter model running 50 steps — comparing a 1.3B parameter decoder add-on against a full 32B native model is not an apples-to-apples comparison for quality. No ablation on injection frequency or σ_max is provided. Bottom Line: PiD delivers a practically significant speedup and memory improvement for high-resolution latent decoding with credibly better perceptual quality, making it a strong candidate for adoption in production text-to-image pipelines — though the lack of code/weight release limits immediate reproducibility.
Recommended 2605.23902 PDF
#37 — Innovative (71)

Your Embedding Model is SMARTer Than You Think (17 ▲ )

Jianrui Zhang, Hyun Jung Lee, Sukanta Ganguly, Tae-Eui Kam, Donghyun Kim, Yong Jae Lee · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SMART (Single-to-Multi Adaptation for Retrieval Transformers), a framework that converts existing single-vector multimodal embedding models into multi-vector retrievers by applying MAXSIM late-interaction over pre-pooling hidden states, either training-free at inference time or via lightweight post-training. - Why it's important: Single-vector retrieval models are theoretically capacity-bounded by embedding dimensionality and fail on queries requiring localized matching (specific chart labels, regional bindings, distinctive visual features), but deploying purpose-built multi-vector models requires expensive full-scale retraining. - How it does it: Contrastive training on the pooled `<eot>` token propagates gradients through the transformer's attention and residual pathways to all non-pooling hidden states, implicitly organizing them into a cosine-compatible retrieval geometry; SMART exploits this by adding a MAXSIM score over these frozen final-layer hidden states combined additively with the original pooled score. - The good: The training-free variant improves all five tested multimodal backbones on MMEB-V2 with zero training cost, and a 1h50m lightweight adapter (8× A6000s) lets a 2B model (average visdoc 81.25) outperform the 4B jina-embeddings-v4 multi-vector model (80.91). - The missing: No latency or index-size cost analysis is provided despite MAXSIM scoring scaling as O(|M_q| × |M_c|) per query-candidate pair — a critical omission for a retrieval paper targeting production systems. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building multimodal RAG or document retrieval pipelines and for IR researchers studying implicit geometry in transformer hidden states; those deploying at million-document scale should treat the efficiency claims with caution until latency numbers appear.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (80) and Novelty (75) drive the score: the gradient-flow argument that contrastive training on the pooled token implicitly shapes non-pooling hidden states into a cosine-compatible geometry is a genuinely surprising, testable insight, and the finding that single-vector models already contain latent multi-vector capability is a NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING rather than a ColBERT transplant. Rigor (65) is solid but capped — meaningful ablations (layer-wise, hybrid, controlled toy benchmark) and open-sourced code plus model weights are real positives, but all results are single runs with no error bars and training is confined to the Colpali visdoc set, and the work is an un-peer-reviewed preprint. The headline gap that prevents a higher composite is the complete absence of latency/storage analysis (Impact 75 reflects easy adoption but unquantified production cost), since MAXSIM over all non-pooling tokens is a known O(|M_q|×|M_c|) bottleneck. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*80 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.10*75 = 71.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 80 Completeness 65 Resilience 70 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multimodal dense retrievers for text, images, visual documents, and video compress entire sequences into a single pooled embedding (the `<eot>` token in most MLLM-based systems like Qwen3-VL-Embedding). While efficient for ANN search, this compression is theoretically capacity-bounded by embedding dimensionality: the number of distinct subset rankings a single-vector model can reliably return is bounded by the embedding dimension. As a result, fine-grained queries requiring local matching — a specific chart legend entry, a regional code-marker binding, a distinctive architectural detail — frequently fail because the localized evidence gets averaged away during pooling. Multi-vector architectures (ColBERT, ColPali, jina-embeddings-v4) address this but require full-scale task-specific training, with self-attention cost scaling quadratically with sequence length. The question the paper addresses is whether existing single-vector models can be converted to multi-vector retrievers without the expense of retraining from scratch. Key Idea The single key insight is that contrastive training on the pooled embedding implicitly shapes all non-pooling hidden states into a cosine-compatible retrieval geometry via gradient backpropagation through the transformer's attention and residual pathways (Eq. 3: ∂L/∂h^l_{q,i} = (∂z_q/∂h^l_{q,i})^T ∂L/∂z_q). Because the contrastive objective is itself defined by cosine similarity, this indirect supervision encourages non-pooling hidden states to organize in a way that supports token-level cosine retrieval, even though they were never explicitly trained as standalone retrieval vectors. Once this is accepted, MAXSIM late-interaction over these frozen hidden states becomes a zero-cost unlock rather than a new capability requiring training. Methodology SMART has two operating modes. In the inference-only mode, the hybrid score is s_hybrid(q,c) = s_single(q,c) + s_late(q,c), where s_single is the standard normalized eot dot-product (Eq. 2) and s_late is the MAXSIM late-interaction score (Eq. 4): s_late(q,c) = (1/|M_q|) Σ_{i∈M_q} max_{j∈M_c} h̃^L_{q,i}^T h̃^L_{c,j}, computed over normalized final-layer non-pooling token hidden states. The unit weight combination is hyperparameter-free by design, justified by the claim that both terms operate in the same final-layer cosine geometry. In the post-training mode, two variants are explored: (1) Adapter-only: freeze the backbone, train a token-wise LayerNorm + Linear projection r_i = normalize(Linear(LN(h^L_i))) on top of final-layer hidden states, optimized via contrastive loss on s_late only; trained on the Colpali dataset with global batch size 512, taking ~1h50m on 8× A6000s for the 2B model. (2) Full LoRA conversion (r=128, α=256, cosine annealing, lr=1e-4): start from a single-vector checkpoint and finetune one extra epoch using s_hybrid as the objective. Three critical design decisions: (1) Using final-layer rather than earlier-layer hidden states — justified by geometric compatibility with the pooled score, validated by layer-wise ablation (Table 4). (2) Additive combination with unit weights rather than learned weighting — justified empirically but without sensitivity analysis. (3) Preserving the global pooled signal rather than replacing it — motivated by its continued utility for global query-candidate compatibility, empirically confirmed by the hybrid vs. late-only ablation in Appendix D. Key Results Inference-only on MMEB-V2 (Table 1): VLM2Vec-V2.0 improves from 64.50 to 67.04 (+2.54 avg); GME-2B from 69.00 to 70.00 (+1.00); GME-7B from 72.26 to 72.56 (+0.30); Qwen3-VL-Embed-2B from 74.87 to 75.77 (+0.90); Qwen3-VL-Embed-8B from 78.83 to 79.34 (+0.51). Small negative results appear for GME Video RET (26.73 → 26.50) and Qwen3-8B Video RET (73.27 → 73.21), consistent with the authors' acknowledgment that SMART does not help all task types. Adapter post-training on visdoc subset (Table 2): Qwen3-2B reaches 81.25 average (vs. baseline 79.27), outperforming jina-embeddings-v4 4B model at 80.91; Qwen3-8B reaches 83.89 (vs. baseline 82.33). LoRA conversion (Table 3): LamRA-Single-Convert (9.5h total) achieves 77.68 average vs. LamRA-Multi from scratch (12h) at 78.31 — roughly 79% of training time for 99% of performance. Hybrid training ablation (Table 5): LamRA-Hybrid achieves 79.10, matching Qwen3-VL-Embedding-2B at 79.27 despite the latter having presumably much larger training data. Controlled toy benchmark: single-vector accuracy 31.9%, late-interaction only 56.8%, native multi-vector baselines (jina at 50.9%, ColPali at 48.7%) — all near chance on this adversarially constructed local-binding task. No error bars or multi-seed results reported anywhere. All baselines (Qwen3-VL-Embedding, jina-embeddings-v4, ColPali-1.3) are current. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.24938v1, May 2026), not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The methodological contribution — applying MAXSIM late-interaction over pre-pooling hidden states of a single-vector model as a plug-and-play adapter — is genuinely novel and not a straightforward transplant of ColBERT-style late interaction, because the key claim (gradient flow from contrastive training implicitly aligns hidden states for cosine retrieval) is non-obvious and motivates the training-free regime. The empirical finding that single-vector models already contain latent multi-vector capability without any multi-vector supervision is new. The hybrid scoring objective and lightweight conversion are technically straightforward once the core observation is established. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and meaningful: layer-wise analysis (Table 4) validates the final-layer choice; hybrid vs. non-hybrid training ablation (Table 5/Appendix D) validates the hybrid design; the toy benchmark (Section 4.1) isolates the local-evidence bottleneck with controlled hard negatives. No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported anywhere; all results are single runs. Code and model weights are open-sourced at https://github.com/HanSolo9682/SMART. Methodological concern: the training experiments are restricted to the Colpali visdoc training set (acknowledged as a compute limitation) and evaluated on the visdoc subset, meaning the training results cannot be compared on equal footing to the full inference-only results across MMEB-V2. The unit-weight hybrid combination is asserted to be robust across backbones but no sensitivity analysis is provided. Potential Goodhart-adjacent concern: the LamRA models are trained and evaluated on visdoc, though the test split is held out. Deployability (4-property check) EMPIRICAL profile. - Practical: Yes — inference-only SMART requires no additional hardware and runs on any GPU that can host the existing single-vector model; the adapter trains in under 2 hours on academic resources (8× A6000s). - Efficient: Not quantified. The paper provides no latency, throughput, or index-size numbers. MAXSIM over all non-pooling tokens scales as O(|M_q| × |M_c|) per query-candidate pair at inference time; for large corpora, this requires precomputing and storing all per-document token vectors (a substantial storage overhead vs. single-vector indexing), and the reranking cost at query time is not characterized. - Effective: Yes for fine-grained dense retrieval tasks (image, visual document, video retrieval); explicitly ineffective or harmful for classification, VQA, visual grounding, and video moment retrieval. - Scalable: Not evaluated or claimed. All experiments use fixed MMEB-V2 corpus sizes; behavior at 10×–100× scale is not discussed. The quadratic MAXSIM cost is a known bottleneck for production-scale deployment that is not addressed. Delivers a software artifact: open-source code and trained model weights. Strengths - The gradient-flow argument (Eq. 3) is a precise and testable theoretical motivation for why hidden states of single-vector models should be cosine-compatible for local matching, elevating the work above pure empirical observation. - The controlled toy benchmark (code-marker binding) cleanly isolates the pooling bottleneck from confounding factors in natural benchmarks, providing independent evidence for the core claim before presenting MMEB-V2 numbers. - The inference-only results are evaluated across five diverse backbones of different scales (2B–8B parameters, different training recipes) and show consistent directional improvement — not a cherry-picked single model. - The efficiency story for the lightweight conversion path (save ~20% training time, recover ~99% of multi-vector performance) is validated by a controlled comparison using the same training recipe and starting checkpoint. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) SMART is not beneficial as inference-only for global tasks like classification, and can actively hurt performance there; (2) training experiments are limited to the visdoc subset due to compute constraints; (3) video moment retrieval is excluded because SMART's token-level matching lacks the temporal reasoning needed for dynamic action queries. These are stated directly and credibly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No latency or storage analysis: MAXSIM requires materializing all per-document token vectors at retrieval time, which multiplies storage costs by sequence length and imposes per-query O(|M_q| × |M_c|) computation. For a 1M-document corpus with 1K tokens per document, this is not the same workload as single-vector ANN search, and the paper's silence on this point is a significant omission. - No error bars: Several gains are small in absolute terms (e.g., +0.51 average for Qwen3-8B, GME Video RET showing a drop), and without variance estimates it is unclear whether these are reliable or noise. - The unit-weight hybrid rule lacks sensitivity analysis; it is plausible that different tasks or modalities require different weighting, and an ablation sweeping weights would strengthen the design choice. - The gradient-flow argument (Eq. 3) is a necessary condition for gradient propagation, not a sufficient mechanistic explanation for the observed cosine-geometric alignment. The paper does not verify whether this effect is specific to architectures with causal attention pooling into a single `<eot>` token vs. mean-pooled models. - All training experiments use the Colpali training set only, making it unclear whether the SMART training benefit generalizes to other training distributions or whether the visdoc evaluation advantage is partly due to in-distribution optimization. Bottom Line SMART makes a clean, well-motivated, and practically useful contribution: the training-free upgrade is genuinely zero-cost and consistently beneficial for fine-grained dense retrieval, the lightweight adapter outperforms dedicated multi-vector baselines with minimal training, and the open-sourced implementation makes immediate adoption easy — the missing latency characterization is the one gap practitioners must independently resolve before production deployment.
Recommended 2605.24938 PDF
#38 — Innovative (70)

BLINKG: A benchmark for LLM- integrated knowledge graph generation

Carla Castedo, Enrique Iglesias, Manuel Lama, Alberto Bugarin-Diz, Maria-Esther Vidal, David Chaves-Fraga · arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces BLINKG, a benchmark that evaluates LLMs' ability to generate declarative semantic mappings from heterogeneous data sources (CSV, JSON, XML) to ontology terms during knowledge graph construction, decomposed into eight independently scorable atomic subtasks. - Why it's important: KG construction mapping phases routinely exceed six person-months in large real-world projects, yet no standardized evaluation framework exists for comparing LLM-based mapping approaches — each prior proposal uses different tasks, datasets, and metrics, making cross-study comparison impossible. - How it does it: BLINKG defines three scenarios of increasing schema-to-ontology lexical distance (Basic / Schema-Aligned using LinkedGTFS / Schema-Distant using eProcurement Ontology + CODICE XML), evaluates six LLMs using task-level precision/recall/F1 scored via a combined Levenshtein + embedding similarity metric calibrated against expert annotations, and releases all gold standards and evaluation code openly. - The good: The benchmark exposes a diagnostic capability cliff: LLMs achieve near-perfect entity class identification (similarity > 0.95) in simple scenarios but score F1 = 0.00 on join conditions and transformation functions in schema-distant Scenario 3 — across all six models and all prompting strategies — providing actionable granularity no end-to-end benchmark delivers. - The missing: The benchmark uses real-world ontologies (GTFS, ePO) that may overlap with LLM training corpora, and the similarity threshold is calibrated on the same expert annotations used for evaluation — creating a circularity that no held-out validation corrects. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners working on LLM-assisted knowledge engineering or KG construction pipelines; less relevant outside the Semantic Web community, and the small gold standards (72 and 26 instances in the two realistic scenarios) limit statistical confidence in task-level findings.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (68) reflect the task decomposition into 8 atomic, independently scorable subtasks and the diagnostic finding that join conditions and transformation functions are a universal LLM failure mode (F1=0.00 across six models and all prompting strategies in the schema-distant scenario) — a non-obvious, actionable result end-to-end benchmarks would obscure. Rigor (70) is helped by peer-review acceptance (TGDK) and a fully released artifact, but pulled down by threshold τ calibrated on the same expert annotations it evaluates (Goodhart concern), no inter-annotator agreement, only 3 runs without significance tests, and acknowledged data-leakage risk. Completeness (72), Resilience (68), and Impact (72) are good but the small gold standards (72 and 26 instances) and absence of non-LLM baselines keep results preliminary. Weighted: 0.20*70 + 0.25*70 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*72 ≈ 70.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 72 Resilience 68 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Knowledge engineers must map heterogeneous data source schemas — CSV columns, JSON paths, XML XPath expressions — to ontology terms in order to build knowledge graphs. In large-scale real-world projects this mapping phase alone regularly exceeds six person-months. Declarative mapping languages (RML, R2RML, SPARQL-Anything) reduce repetition but do not eliminate the semantic alignment work. LLMs have been proposed as accelerators, but existing proposals (Hofer et al. for RML from JSON, Schmidt et al. for YARRRML in manufacturing, Höltgen et al. for infrastructure RDF, Freund et al.'s ReMap) each define their own tasks, datasets, and success criteria, making cross-study comparison impossible. Prior benchmarks are inadequate: RODI targets RDB-only settings and evaluates mappings indirectly through SPARQL query accuracy; SemTab evaluates annotation of tabular data against pre-existing KGs, not ontology alignment for new graph construction. The result is a fragmented landscape with no agreed-upon tasks, gold standards, or metrics for LLM mapping evaluation. Key Idea The core insight is to decompose the monolithic KG mapping problem into eight independently scorable atomic subtasks — Ontology Class Identification, Subject URI Generation, Ontology Property Identification, Data Reference Identification, Related Entity Class and Joins, Language Annotation, Datatype Annotation, and Transformation Functions — and to evaluate LLMs on these subtasks across scenarios that vary in schema-to-ontology lexical distance, rather than measuring end-to-end pipeline quality or syntactic mapping language correctness. This decomposition exposes precisely where LLMs succeed or fail rather than collapsing all failure modes into a single end-to-end metric. Methodology BLINKG defines three scenarios. Scenario 1 (Basic) comprises eight atomic test cases over a minimal three-class toy ontology (Person, Sport, City) with artificial CSV/JSON/XML data derived from RML test cases; it tests each of the eight subtasks in isolation with 1–7 gold-standard instances per case. Scenario 2 (Schema-Aligned) uses the LinkedGTFS ontology (17 classes, 49 datatype properties, 10 object properties, 10 SKOS controlled vocabularies) with real Madrid Metro GTFS CSV feeds (13 files, 72 total gold instances); source and ontology have high lexical alignment since the ontology was derived bottom-up from the GTFS specification. Scenario 3 (Schema-Distant) uses a subset of the eProcurement Ontology (11 classes from the pan-European epo-core module) with CODICE XML procurement entries from Spain (26 gold instances); CODICE and ePO were developed independently, introducing significant structural and lexical divergence requiring deep domain expertise to bridge. Each LLM is prompted with a standardized natural-language template requesting a structured table output listing data references, ontology properties, entity classes, subject templates, joins, datatypes, language tags, and transformation functions. Temperature is fixed at 0.3 based on preliminary runs. Each prompt runs three times; results are macro-averaged. To fit context windows, Scenario 2 is split into 6 sub-scenarios and Scenario 3 into 2. Three evaluation modes are reported: expert (manual annotation), raw (automatic on raw output), and post-processed (automatic after heuristic cleaning — whitespace removal, discarding incoherent fragments, dropping misaligned columns). Correctness is binary: an annotation is correct if max(Levenshtein similarity, raw SBERT cosine, verbalized-ontology SBERT cosine) ≥ τ = 0.8, where τ was chosen by minimizing MAE against expert annotations. Zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot prompting strategies are compared on Scenario 3 for DeepSeek-R1. Six LLMs are evaluated: DeepSeek-R1, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o, OpenAI o3, LLaMA 3.3 70B Instruct, and Mixtral 8x22B Instruct. Three critical design decisions: (1) Tabular output rather than raw RML/R2RML — well justified, as LLMs are known to produce syntactically invalid mapping language files. (2) Threshold τ = 0.8 calibrated against expert annotations — problematic because it is selected on the same data it evaluates (no held-out calibration set). (3) Sub-scenario splitting for context-window limitations — pragmatic but introduces potential coherence loss not ablated. Key Results Scenario 1: Entity class identification similarity ≥ 0.95 for most models; several reach 1.00. Ontology property identification reliably high. Join condition is the hardest task, with similarity ranging 0.37 (LLaMA) to 0.69 (Gemini 2.5 Pro). Language annotation shows high variance: DeepSeek/Gemini > 0.83; Mixtral/LLaMA < 0.53. MAE between expert and post-processed F1 ranges 0.106 (DeepSeek) to 0.236 (Mixtral), indicating the automatic metric is a reasonable but imperfect proxy in simple settings. Scenario 2 (72 gold instances): Entity class and data reference post-processed similarity > 0.90 for top models (GPT-4o data reference: 0.91; DeepSeek entity class: 0.95). Ontology property F1 approaches 1.00 (post-processed) for DeepSeek and Gemini. Join condition: no model exceeds 0.85 F1 (post-processed) and most fall below 0.65. Function Name: all models below 0.20 similarity raw; post-processed jumps to 0.20 (a flat heuristic artifact). Function Output: below 0.41 post-processed for most. MAE between expert and post-processed F1 ranges 0.13 (GPT-4o) to 0.22 (Gemini, Mixtral). Scenario 3 (26 gold instances): No task exceeds mean similarity 0.53 post-processed for any model. Entity class best: DeepSeek 0.53, GPT-4o 0.42. Join condition F1 = 0.00 for all models in all evaluation modes. Function Output F1 = 0.00 for all models except o3 (0.25 post-processed). Subject Generation F1 = 0.00 for all models. Prompting strategy experiment (DeepSeek): few-shot raises data reference F1 from 0.38 to 0.86 and subject generation from 0.00 to 0.71, but join and function tasks remain at 0.00 regardless of strategy.
