MAE between expert and post-processed ranges 0.32 (o3) to 0.42 (DeepSeek), indicating the automatic metric substantially underestimates LLM performance in complex schema-distant settings. No non-LLM baselines are included. Baselines are limited to cross-model comparison within the six LLMs. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The primary contribution is a structured, openly released benchmark with gold standards across three scenarios. The finding that join conditions and transformation functions are a universal failure mode across six diverse LLMs — persistent even under few-shot prompting in schema-distant settings — is a genuine empirical finding. The evaluation methodology (task decomposition + multi-similarity scoring) is an incremental methodological contribution. The paper does not introduce a new method, algorithm, or system for KG construction itself. Rigor & Reproducibility All resources (ontologies, datasets, gold standards, evaluation library) are released at https://github.com/citiususc/blinkg under Apache 2.0. Only 3 prompt runs per scenario — standard deviations across runs are not reported, and significance tests are absent. No inter-annotator agreement is reported for the expert evaluation used as the gold calibration reference. The similarity threshold τ = 0.8 is selected by minimizing MAE against the same expert annotations it evaluates — this is a Goodhart's Law concern: the metric is tuned to its own benchmark. Context-window splitting into sub-scenarios is not evaluated as a confound. The benchmark uses real-world ontologies (GTFS, ePO) publicly available on the Web and potentially in LLM training corpora; the authors explicitly acknowledge data leakage as a limitation and recommend synthetic ontologies for future versions but do not control for it here. The paper is peer-reviewed, accepted to Transactions on Graph Data and Knowledge (received July 2025, accepted March 2026). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (evaluation of LLM systems against gold standards; the paper reports no deployment of a new system, only evaluation of existing LLMs). - Practical: The benchmark runs against API-accessible commercial and open-source LLMs with no specialized hardware beyond API access; any practitioner can reproduce the evaluation. - Efficient: Scenario 2 requires splitting into 6 sub-scenarios to fit LLM context windows, and running 6 models × 3 prompt repetitions over 72+26 instances is modest in compute but non-trivial in API cost; the evaluation library lowers the barrier for reuse. - Effective: The benchmark successfully differentiates model capabilities across difficulty levels and identifies specific failure subtasks (join conditions, transformation functions) that end-to-end evaluation would obscure; expert-vs-automatic MAE confirms the metric is a reasonable proxy in simpler scenarios. - Scalable: Gold standards are small (72 and 26 instances in the two realistic scenarios), limiting statistical confidence; the framework architecture is extensible through community contributions, but current coverage is insufficient to claim generalization to the full diversity of real-world KG mapping challenges. The work delivers a software artifact (benchmark suite with ontologies, datasets, gold standards, evaluation library) at the stated GitHub URL. Strengths - Task decomposition is the primary contribution: separating KG mapping into 8 independently scorable subtasks enables precise failure-mode diagnosis that RODI's SPARQL-based or SemTab's annotation-level evaluation cannot provide. - The three-scenario difficulty ladder with real-world ontologies (LinkedGTFS, ePO) and data sources (Madrid Metro GTFS, Spanish procurement CODICE XML) provides external validity beyond toy examples and covers both CSV and deeply nested XML. - The three-mode evaluation design (expert, raw, post-processed) reveals that raw automatic scoring systematically underestimates LLM performance — a finding with practical implications for how future LLM evaluations in this domain should be designed. - The prompting strategy ablation on Scenario 3 demonstrates concretely that few-shot prompting helps local selection tasks but fails entirely for compositional tasks (joins, transformations), providing actionable guidance for future research. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) data leakage risk from publicly available GTFS and ePO ontologies that may overlap with LLM training corpora, with a commitment to synthetic ontologies in future versions; (2) reliance on manual inspection or simplified metrics for semantic equivalence; (3) exclusion of advanced mapping constructs (RML-CC list generation, RML-Star, RML-IO) deferred to future versions; (4) fragility of proprietary LLM APIs — OpenAI o1 and Gemini 2.0 Pro were removed or restricted mid-evaluation, requiring repetition. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No non-LLM baselines: existing automated mapping tools (MIRROR, AutoMap4OBDA, BootOX, D2RQ) are not compared, leaving unanswered whether LLMs even outperform classical heuristic-based tools on Scenario 1. - Threshold circularity: τ = 0.8 is calibrated by minimizing MAE against the same expert annotations used for evaluation — this overfits the metric to the benchmark and should be validated on held-out data or via cross-validation. - Scenario 3 gold standard instability: with only 26 instances spread across 9 task types, individual task scores (e.g., Join Condition: 16 instances) are computed over counts too small for stable estimates; confidence intervals are absent. - Context-window splitting as an uncontrolled confound: decomposing Scenario 2 into 6 sub-scenarios removes global ontology context an LLM would otherwise use; the effect of this decomposition on results is never ablated. - Single temperature: only temperature 0.3 is reported in final experiments despite the claim that preliminary runs were conducted; no sensitivity analysis is included. - No inter-annotator agreement: the expert evaluation serves as the calibration anchor for τ, but no agreement metric (Cohen's κ, Fleiss's κ) is reported, so the reliability of the gold calibration reference is unknown. Bottom Line BLINKG is a useful, openly released benchmark that fills a genuine gap by providing the first task-decomposed, format-agnostic, multi-scenario evaluation framework for LLM-assisted KG mapping; knowledge graph construction researchers should read it for its diagnostic task decomposition and the finding that compositional mapping tasks (joins, transformation functions) are a universal current LLM failure mode, while keeping in mind that absent non-LLM baselines, small gold standards, and an uncorrected threshold circularity make the quantitative results preliminary rather than definitive.
Search Results 2605.19518 PDF
#39 — Innovative (70)

QUEST: Training Frontier Deep Research Agents with Fully Synthetic Tasks (28 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Quest, a family of open-weight deep research agents (2B–35B parameters) trained using a fully synthetic data pipeline based on hierarchical rubric trees, a structured context management mechanism, and a staged MT→SFT→RL training recipe. - Why it's important: Frontier deep research agents (OpenAI DeepResearch, Gemini) remain proprietary and opaque; existing open alternatives cover only narrow capability slices (fact-seeking or report synthesis, not both), leaving the field without a reproducible general-purpose baseline. - How it does it: The core mechanism is a rubric tree — a hierarchically decomposed, auto-verifiable specification of correctness — that enables generating 8K synthetic training tasks with fine-grained, automated rewards covering fact-seeking, citation grounding, and open-ended report synthesis jointly. - The good: Quest-35B achieves 80.8% on GAIA-Text (vs. GPT-5 at 76.4%), 48.2% on DeepResearch Bench (vs. OpenAI-DR at 47.0%), and 30.7% on Mind2Web 2 (vs. OpenAI-DR at 28.0%), narrowing or closing the open-weight/proprietary gap across three distinct capability dimensions simultaneously. - The missing: The training and inference pipelines depend on proprietary models (GPT-5-mini as context condenser and visit summarizer, Claude Sonnet 4.5 for data generation, Tongyi-DR for trajectory distillation), which means "fully open" overstates reproducibility; no contamination analysis is provided despite training data being crawled from live web content that may overlap with evaluated benchmarks. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or studying autonomous web agents — the rubric-tree synthesis and structured context-state mechanism are genuinely novel components and the full release (models, data, training scripts) provides a credible public reference for future work.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (78) drives the composite — a complete open release (models 2B–35B, Quest-8K dataset, training scripts) delivering the strongest reproducible deep-research baseline, with results that meet or beat proprietary agents on GAIA, DeepResearch Bench, and Mind2Web 2. Novelty (75) is high: the rubric-tree unification of objective and open-ended supervision and the trusted/untrusted/uncertain Context State are genuinely new, not recombinations. Rigor (62) and Resilience (62) cap the score below the Highly-Innovative band: no error bars, small ablation subsets (130 examples), no contamination analysis despite web-crawled training data overlapping the evaluated benchmarks, and embedded proprietary API dependencies (GPT-5-mini, Claude Sonnet 4.5) that make 'fully open' an overstatement. Composite: 0.15*75 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*72 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*62 + 0.30*78 = 70.
systems Novelty 75 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Web-scale information-seeking tasks — locating obscure facts, synthesizing multi-source reports, and grounding claims in verifiable citations — require autonomous agents that can plan, search, verify, and revise over long horizons. Existing proprietary systems (OpenAI DeepResearch, Gemini Deep Research) handle these tasks well but share neither models nor training recipes. Open-weight alternatives (Tongyi-DR, OpenResearcher, DR Tulu) each address a narrow slice: Tongyi-DR focuses on fact-seeking via single-answer supervision; OpenResearcher concentrates on citation grounding; DR Tulu on report synthesis. None provides a unified recipe covering all three capabilities, and none releases a fully reproducible training pipeline. The deeper problem is that synthesizing training data with verifiable rewards for open-ended research tasks (where there is no single correct answer) has resisted clean solutions — prior single-answer approaches provide binary rewards that are insufficient to guide multi-aspect report quality. Key Idea: The central insight is that any deep research task — whether it has a unique factual answer or requires a multi-page synthesized report — can be formalized as a rubric tree: a hierarchical decomposition of correctness criteria where leaf nodes are directly auto-verifiable (factual match, URL presence, source attribution) and internal nodes aggregate their children's scores. Once this representation exists, a single synthetic pipeline can generate diverse tasks, evaluate agent responses with fine-grained partial credit, and provide rubric-tree-shaped rewards for reinforcement learning — all without human annotation. Methodology: The pipeline has four components. (1) Data synthesis: trending Google keywords seed autonomous web exploration by Claude Sonnet 4.5 (Gsyn), which generates queries, derives verifiable constraints, and organizes them into rubric trees. Objective tasks receive Python evaluation scripts (generated by GPT-5) that programmatically verify each leaf node; open-ended tasks fix four top-level criteria (instruction following, comprehensiveness, readability, insight) and use pairwise scoring against a reference report generated by Gsyn, with the final score defined as J(rcand)/(J(rcand)+J(rref)) to avoid reference-bias inflation. Starting from ~20K raw tasks, strict filtering yields 8,228 high-quality SFT/RL instances. (2) Context management: a Context Condenser (GPT-5-mini) triggers once the active context exceeds a threshold, compressing history into a structured JSON "Context State" with three typed buckets — Trusted (verified facts with source URLs), Untrusted (contradicted claims with reason), and Uncertain (pending hypotheses with follow-up actions). Sessions restart from this state. (3) Training: mid-training (MT) on 300K context summarization examples and 1M+ HTML extraction examples adapts the base model to structured context formats. SFT trains on trajectories from Tongyi-DR distillation (session-level decomposition enables training on 200K-token trajectories on 16 H100 GPUs). RL uses GRPO with a composite reward R = 0.75·s_rubric + 0.25·min(s_fact, s_rubric), where s_fact measures the fraction of inline citations verified as supported by GPT-5-mini. (4) Infrastructure: dual-cache (exact + FAISS semantic fallback) for search/visit calls and a fully asynchronous RL pipeline built on VERL decouple rollout, evaluation, and training, accommodating rubric-tree evaluations that average 4 minutes and spike to 30 minutes. Critical design decisions: (a) session-level training instead of full-trajectory training is well-justified by GPU memory constraints (200K-token trajectories become tractable); (b) pairwise normalization for open-ended scoring over pointwise or ternary win/tie/lose is well-justified by empirical failure modes documented in Section 7.4; (c) excluding KL penalty from GRPO is stated but not ablated. Key Results: Quest-35B (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B base) on the full test sets: BrowseComp 64.6% (with discard-all, 45.5% otherwise) vs. OpenAI-DR 51.5%, Claude Opus 4.5 67.8%; BrowseComp-Plus 69.5% vs. OpenAI-DR not reported, Tongyi-DR 44.5%; Mind2Web 2 30.7% vs. OpenAI-DR 28.0%, Tongyi-DR 16.7%; WideSearch 60.6% vs. GPT-5 54.0%, Tongyi-DR 37.3%; HLE 37.2% vs. Claude Opus 4.5 43.2%, GPT-5 35.2%; GAIA 80.8% vs. GPT-5 76.4%; DeepResearch Bench 48.2% vs. OpenAI-DR 47.0%, Tongyi-DR 40.5%; LiveResearchBench 68.2% vs. GPT-5 73.1%, Tongyi-DR 56.3%. At equivalent 30B scale, Quest-30B beats Tongyi-DR and OpenResearcher on 4/8 benchmarks. Quest-2B-SFT scores 30.3% on HLE and 72.8% on GAIA, competitive with or exceeding OpenAI o3 (24.9% HLE, 70.5% GAIA). Ablations show SFT alone degrades open-ended tasks; MT recovers them; RL provides the largest boost for report synthesis but mildly hurts HLE and GAIA (alignment tax). Baselines are current and strong for open-weight comparisons; however, frontier proprietary agents (GPT-5, Claude Opus 4.5) have missing values across several benchmarks, making head-to-head comparisons incomplete. Ablations use sampled subsets (e.g., BrowseComp: 130 of 1,266), which may inflate variance. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The rubric-tree synthesis framework is genuinely novel as a unified mechanism for generating verifiably-graded training data across both objective and open-ended research tasks without human annotation. The structured three-bucket Context State as an explicit epistemic encoding of an agent's research state extends prior context management work (discard-all, keep-last-N) in a principled direction. The full open release (Quest-8K dataset, models 2B–35B, training scripts) is a practical contribution given that no prior open deep research agent released all three components together. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover the effect of each training stage (Vanilla → SFT → MT+SFT → MT+SFT+RL) and model size (2B, 4B, 9B, 35B). No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported for any benchmark result; most ablations use subsets rather than full test sets, and subset sizes (130 examples for BrowseComp) are small enough for noise to influence comparisons. Code, data (Quest-8K), and model weights (Quest-35B-RL at HuggingFace) are publicly released. A notable methodological concern: the training data is generated by web crawling and autonomous exploration, and the evaluated benchmarks (BrowseComp, HLE, GAIA) also draw from public web sources; no contamination analysis is presented. A second concern: BrowseComp results are reported under two different protocols (discard-all giving 64.6% vs. standard giving 45.5%); the headline Figure 1 number uses discard-all while the main table reports both, which requires careful reading to avoid conflation. The manual examination of evaluation scripts (50 samples, 4 annotators) found 6/50 rubric-related errors and 2/50 non-executable scripts — a non-trivial error rate that could introduce noisy rewards. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a built open-weight model family evaluated by deployment on benchmarks). - Practical: Quest-2B and Quest-4B run on commodity GPUs; Quest-35B uses a MoE architecture (3B active parameters out of 35B total) so inference fits on hardware that can serve a standard 7B-dense model, making deployment practical. - Efficient: Training Quest-35B requires 32 H100 GPUs for ~5 days (MT) + ~1 day (SFT) + RL; inference relies on GPT-5-mini (via API) for context condensation and page visit summarization, introducing ongoing API cost and a proprietary external dependency that is not fully replaceable without retraining. - Effective: Evidence across 8 diverse benchmarks shows the method solves all three claimed capability dimensions; the clearest margin over open-weight baselines is on citation grounding (Mind2Web 2: 30.7% vs. 16.7% for Tongyi-DR) and report synthesis (DeepResearch Bench: 48.2% vs. 35.4% for OpenResearcher). - Scalable: The context management mechanism is designed to extrapolate to arbitrarily long horizons (demonstrated at >200 turns during inference despite training on ≤100-turn trajectories), but no benchmark in the evaluation explicitly stress-tests very-long-horizon tasks (hours-long research sessions); scaling to larger model sizes is explored only through SFT, not the full MT+SFT+RL recipe. The work delivers trained model weights (HuggingFace), training scripts (GitHub), and a synthetic dataset (Quest-8K) — a complete software artifact. Strengths: - The rubric-tree formalism is a clean unification of objective and open-ended task supervision; the hierarchical partial-credit score provides a richer RL signal than binary exact-match while remaining fully automated. - The structured Context State (trusted/untrusted/uncertain) goes beyond prior discard-all and keep-last-N strategies by preserving epistemic distinctions — a genuinely useful inductive bias for long-horizon research agents. - Section 7 documents four categories of unsuccessful attempts (search result prediction in MT, rubric-based error identification, DPO for report comparison, evaluation design failures) with hypotheses, which is unusually candid and provides actionable guidance for the field. - Full open release of models, data, and training code substantially lowers the barrier for follow-on research in a space dominated by proprietary systems. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors note that RL slightly sacrifices HLE and GAIA performance (alignment tax from specialization toward report synthesis behaviors). They acknowledge that small models (2B) underperform on open-ended tasks. The section on unsuccessful attempts acknowledges instability in DPO training for long-form comparisons and the failure of pointwise scoring. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The training pipeline depends on GPT-5-mini (context condensation, visit summarization, fact-checking reward computation, citation insertion) and Claude Sonnet 4.5 (data generation) at multiple stages. Despite being called "fully open," these proprietary API dependencies mean training cannot be reproduced without incurring API costs to external vendors; the fallback models (DeepSeek V3.2) are noted only for RL-stage API rate-limit handling, not as full replacements. - No contamination analysis: the web-crawled training data is temporally overlapping with several benchmarks, and the paper does not analyze whether benchmark-relevant content appeared in training queries or visited URLs. - The BrowseComp headline score (64.6%) is produced under the discard-all context strategy (not Quest's Context Condenser), chosen to match the evaluation protocol used by proprietary competitors — but this means the primary advertised result for the most prominent benchmark does not use Quest's own claimed contribution of structured context management. - Ablation subsets are small (130 examples) and no variance estimates are reported, making it difficult to assess whether differences of 1–3 percentage points are meaningful. - The base model (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B) is a mixture-of-experts architecture; comparisons with dense 30B models conflate different computational classes without discussion. Bottom Line: Quest is the strongest open-weight deep research agent published to date and provides the field's most complete reproducible training recipe; a researcher building or benchmarking web agents should read this paper, with the caveat that "fully open" overstates the case given the proprietary API dependencies embedded throughout the training pipeline.
Recommended 2605.24218 PDF
#40 — Innovative (70)

SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills (155 ▲ )

Yifan Yang, Ziyang Gong, Weiquan Huang, Qihao Yang, Ziwei Zhou, Zisu Huang, Yan Li, Xuemei Gao, Qi Dai, Bei Liu, Kai Qiu, Yuqing Yang, Dongdong Chen, Xue Yang, Chong Luo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SkillOpt, a text-space optimizer that trains a frozen agent's natural-language skill document using rollout batches, bounded add/delete/replace edits, a held-out validation gate, a rejected-edit buffer, and an epoch-wise slow/meta update — applying deep-learning training discipline to a portable text artifact. - Why it's important: Weight fine-tuning is unavailable for closed frontier models and expensive for open ones, yet hand-crafted or one-shot-generated skills are brittle; agents operating on procedural tasks (spreadsheet manipulation, embodied navigation, enterprise QA) need reliable iterative domain adaptation without touching model weights. - How it does it: A separate optimizer model reads scored trajectory batches and proposes structured edits; a textual learning-rate budget caps how many edits are applied per step; a held-out selection gate accepts only candidates that strictly improve validation score; rejected edits feed back as negative evidence in the same epoch. - The good: SkillOpt is best or tied-best across all 52 evaluated (model, benchmark, harness) cells, achieving average +23.5 absolute points over no-skill on GPT-5.5 direct chat and demonstrating Codex→Claude Code cross-harness transfer of +59.7 on SpreadsheetBench — the strongest transfer result in the paper and the clearest evidence that the artifact encodes transferable procedural knowledge. - The missing: No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals are reported anywhere; several "wins" in Table 1 are margins of ≤1 percentage point, and the 52/52 best-or-tied claim counts effective ties at that precision — the paper does not distinguish genuinely decisive wins from marginal ones. - Read it or not: Essential reading for practitioners building task-specific agents on closed frontier models who want a reproducible, no-weight-update adaptation procedure; less useful for those seeking theoretical guarantees or working outside the procedural-task regime where the method's gains are largest.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Practical Impact (78) is the strongest dimension — public code (aka.ms/SkillOpt), a zero-inference-overhead static skill artifact, large absolute gains (+23.5 avg), and a striking +59.7 Codex→Claude Code transfer that evidences harness-independent procedural knowledge. The dimensions pulling the composite down are Rigor (65) and Resilience (65): no error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests appear anywhere, several Table 1 'wins' are ≤1pp so the 52/52 best-or-tied claim partly reflects measurement precision, and GPT-5.5 serving as both target and optimizer is a same-family confound only partially addressed. Novelty (72) credits the coherent training-style formulation (bounded edit budget, held-out gate, rejected-edit buffer, protected slow update), though it builds on prior reflection/prompt-optimization work. Composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*78 = 70.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 70 Resilience 65 Impact 78
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Frontier LLMs are increasingly deployed as agents inside tool-use harnesses (Codex, Claude Code) and multi-turn execution loops where success requires domain-specific procedures: correct spreadsheet manipulation idioms, structured answer formatting, search-frontier discipline in embodied environments, and precise evidence binding in document QA. None of these procedures are reliably captured by model weights alone, and fine-tuning closed models is infeasible. Hand-crafted and one-shot LLM-generated skills are brittle under harness changes and cannot correct themselves after observing failures. Prior skill-evolution systems (Trace2Skill, EvoSkill) mine trajectories or evolve skill folders but lack validation gates, bounded update controls, and negative-feedback memory, leaving the central question open: if skills are the domain-adaptation layer, how should they be optimized? Key Idea: The core insight is that a natural-language skill document can be treated as the external trainable state of a frozen agent, with the optimizer analogized to weight-space gradient descent: rollout batch size controls evidence noise, a bounded edit budget controls step size, a held-out selection gate plays the role of validation, rejected edits carry negative feedback analogous to gradient history, and an epoch-wise slow/meta update captures long-horizon regularities. The key move is that bounded, validation-gated updates keep adjacent skill versions close enough that the optimizer's history of accepted and rejected edits remains informative — unlike unbounded rewriting, which erases continuity. Methodology: The pipeline operates on three fixed splits (train, selection, test) and two model roles (frozen target, separate optimizer). Each optimization step proceeds as: (1) the target model executes a rollout batch of size B=40 with the current skill; (2) the optimizer model partitions trajectories into failures and successes, analyzes each in minibatches of size Bm=8, and proposes add/delete/replace patch edits; (3) edits from multiple minibatches are merged hierarchically (failure-first priority) and ranked by expected utility; (4) at most Lt edits (the textual learning rate, default 4, cosine-decayed) are applied to form a candidate skill; (5) the candidate is evaluated on the selection split — accepted only if strictly greater than the current selection score, otherwise rejected and logged to an epoch-local buffer; (6) at epoch boundaries, the slow/meta update compares the same tasks under adjacent epoch skills, writes longitudinal guidance into a markup-protected section of the skill document (also validated through the selection gate), and updates an optimizer-side meta skill that guides future reflection calls but is never shipped with the deployed artifact. The deployed output is a single best_skill.md of 300–2,000 tokens. Critical design decisions: (i) bounded edit budget vs. unconstrained rewriting — well-justified, ablations confirm +2.5–22.5 point degradation without it; (ii) strict greater-than acceptance criterion (ties rejected) — well-justified to prevent silent drift; (iii) separate slow-update protected region preventing fast edits from overwriting epoch-level lessons — well-justified, ablation shows −22.5 on SpreadsheetBench when removed. Key Results: On GPT-5.5 direct chat across six benchmarks, SkillOpt lifts SearchQA 77.7→87.3, SpreadsheetBench 41.8→80.7, OfficeQA 33.1→72.1, DocVQA 78.8→91.2, LiveMathematicianBench 37.6→66.9, ALFWorld 83.6→95.5, for a +23.5 average gain over no skill and +5.4 over an oracle that picks the strongest of six competing methods per cell. On GPT-5.5 inside the Codex harness the average gain over no skill is +24.8, and inside Claude Code +19.1. Across all seven direct-chat target models the average per-model improvement is approximately +17.6 points. The cross-harness transfer result is the most striking: a SpreadsheetBench skill trained inside Codex transfers to Claude Code with +59.7 absolute gain over the Claude Code no-skill baseline (22.1→81.8), slightly exceeding the in-domain Claude Code SkillOpt reference of 80.4. All cross-model and cross-benchmark transfer rows are positive. Training cost ranges from 20.8M tokens total (OfficeQA, 1 accepted edit, +39.0 points) to 213.8M tokens (SearchQA, 4 accepted edits, +9.6 points), with 1–4 median accepted edits across benchmarks — evidence that the validation gate filters aggressively. The strongest baselines (GEPA, EvoSkill) are strong competitors: GEPA reaches 73.6 on SpreadsheetBench for GPT-5.5 vs. SkillOpt's 80.7; EvoSkill reaches 75.0 on Codex SpreadsheetBench vs. SkillOpt's 85.0. All evaluated benchmarks are held-out test splits disjoint from the validation split used to gate edits. No error bars or significance tests are reported anywhere in the paper. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The genuine novelty is the training-style formulation of skill optimization: the bounded edit budget, strict held-out gate, rejected-edit buffer as negative feedback, and protected slow/meta update are a coherent system that has no prior counterpart for text-artifact training. The empirical finding that 1–4 accepted edits suffice for large gains, and that artifacts transfer across harnesses and model scales, is also new. What is not novel: trajectory-driven reflection (Reflexion, Self-Refine), prompt optimization (TextGrad, GEPA), and skill construction from experience (Trace2Skill, EvoSkill) are all prior work that SkillOpt explicitly positions against. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present and informative: learning-rate form, rejected buffer, slow/meta update, training set size, batch size, and schedule are each swept. The ablations are run on three benchmarks (SearchQA, SpreadsheetBench, LiveMath) using GPT-5.5 as both target and optimizer, which is adequate breadth but not comprehensive. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported on any result. Several margins in Table 1 are ≤1 percentage point (e.g., SkillOpt 47.1 vs. LLM skill 46.5 on Qwen3.6-35B-A3B OfficeQA) making the "52/52 best or tied" claim partly a consequence of measurement precision rather than robust superiority. Code is publicly released at https://aka.ms/SkillOpt. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.23904v2, May 2026), not peer reviewed. Several baselines (OfficeQA Pro, LiveMathematicianBench) are themselves 2026 preprints, making the benchmark landscape less settled. The use of GPT-5.5 as both target and optimizer in the primary results introduces a potential same-family confound, though Table 5 partially addresses this with target-matched optimizer experiments. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs entirely via standard LLM API calls on commercially available models (GPT, Qwen); no specialized hardware required for either training or deployment. - Efficient: Training costs 20.8M–213.8M tokens per benchmark per model (a one-time expense); the deployed artifact is a static text file adding zero inference-time overhead, which is a material deployment advantage over methods that require optimizer calls at serve time. - Effective: Held-out test scores confirm that gains generalize beyond the validation split (Figure 3 shows validation checkpoint selection correlates with test performance); the largest absolute gains (+39 points on OfficeQA) appear on procedural benchmarks with strict format requirements where the method's learned rules are most directly applicable. - Scalable: The method is tested on benchmark-sized training pools (35–200 examples per epoch); behavior at 10×–100× scale is not evaluated, and the paper's own limitation note acknowledges that a single skill may be insufficient for highly heterogeneous domains requiring many disjoint procedures. The work delivers a software artifact (released code at https://aka.ms/SkillOpt) plus benchmark-specific best_skill.md artifacts described qualitatively in the paper. Strengths: - The deep-learning analogy is operationally precise rather than decorative: each component (edit budget, selection gate, rejected buffer, slow update) maps to a training-loop primitive with a clear justification and an ablation confirming its contribution. - Transfer experiments across three axes (model scale, harness, benchmark) are the paper's most original empirical contribution; the +59.7 Codex→Claude Code SpreadsheetBench transfer is striking and suggests the learned rules encode domain knowledge that is not harness-specific. - The "edit economy" finding — that +39 points on OfficeQA arises from a single accepted edit — is a surprising and credible result that directly supports the validation-gate design and distinguishes SkillOpt from prompt-inflation approaches. - The qualitative skill inspection (Figure 4, Section 4.5) is unusually informative: verbatim learned rules are procedural, generalizable, and interpretable, providing a sanity check that the optimizer is not simply overfitting to benchmark quirks. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge in Appendix B: (1) dependence on automatic verifiers — the method is less applicable to open-ended tasks without reliable feedback; (2) non-trivial training cost despite being amortized at deployment; (3) single-skill design may be insufficient for highly heterogeneous domains; (4) optimized skills can encode training-distribution heuristics that require careful evaluation before transferring to substantially different settings. These are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The 52/52 best-or-tied claim does not distinguish decisive wins from marginal ones (≤1 percentage point differences appear in multiple cells) and no statistical tests are provided — a reviewer would reasonably push back on this as a headline claim. The evaluation is conducted entirely with GPT and Qwen model families in 2026; the method's behavior with open-weight models at the scale of Llama-3 70B or Mistral families is untested. The paper benchmarks against EvoSkill only in the harness settings, not in direct chat (presumably because EvoSkill is specifically a harness-side system), which means the strongest evolution baseline is missing from the largest portion of the main table. Training token costs for SearchQA (213.8M tokens total) and DocVQA (188.2M tokens total) are non-trivial at current API pricing and are not discussed in the context of cost-effectiveness relative to simply fine-tuning a smaller open model. The GPT-5.5 optimizer optimizing GPT-5.5 target creates a potential self-referential advantage that the Table 5 target-matched experiment only partially addresses — a cross-family optimizer experiment (e.g., Claude as optimizer, GPT as target) would be more diagnostic. Bottom Line: A practitioner deploying frontier agents on procedural tasks should read this paper: the method is immediately actionable, the code is public, and the empirical scope is unusually broad, with the cross-harness transfer result being the most practically significant finding — though the absence of error bars and the reliance on 2026-vintage closed models means independent replication will be needed before treating the 52/52 claim as settled.
Recommended 2605.23904 PDF
#41 — Innovative (69)

Frontier: Towards Comprehensive and Accurate LLM Inference Simulation

Yicheng Feng, Xin Tan, Yangtao Deng, Yimin Jiang, Yibo Zhu, Hong Xu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Frontier, a discrete-event simulator for modern LLM inference serving that models disaggregated architectures (Prefill-Decode Disaggregation and Attention-FFN Disaggregation), runtime optimizations (CUDA Graph, speculative decoding, prefix caching), and stateful workloads (multi-round reasoning, agentic tool calls, RL rollouts). - Why it's important: Existing simulators make configuration recommendations that violate real SLAs in practice — average-case models produce up to 51.7% end-to-end latency error and can select configurations that fail SLA constraints on real hardware, undermining the core purpose of simulation-driven capacity planning. - How it does it: The simulator replaces the monolithic-replica abstraction with role-specific cluster workers whose cross-cluster dependencies (KV-cache transfer, activation ping-pong) are materialized as explicit discrete events, and replaces token-count proxies with class-specific ML predictors (random forests over distributional batch features for attention, load-balance statistics for MoE GroupedGEMM). - The good: Frontier reduces E2E latency error from 51.7% to 2.6% under PDD disaggregation and from 44.9% to 6.4% under co-location, compared to the prior state-of-the-art simulators, validated on a real 16-GPU H800 testbed with vLLM v0.10.2. - The missing: Use-case results — including the 30.4% aTTFT reduction from the phase-aware H2Q-BR scheduler and the 2.04× RL rollout throughput gain from dynamic layout switching — are demonstrated entirely in simulation and never validated on actual hardware, which is a significant credibility gap for a tool whose primary value is replacing expensive hardware experiments. - Read it or not: Systems researchers and infrastructure engineers who configure, evaluate, or build disaggregated LLM serving stacks should read this; practitioners looking for validated scheduling algorithms should treat the §6 use cases as design hypotheses, not confirmed results.
Why this rank: Systems profile. The most technically substantial artifact here. Novelty (78) is the top score in the cluster — compiling serving specs into role-specific cluster workers with explicit event-graph dependencies for PDD/AFD/MoE-EP, plus class-specific operator predictors (distributional batch features for attention, load statistics for MoE routing), is a genuinely new simulator architecture. Non-Obviousness (75) is high: correctly identifying that disaggregation is not expressible by adding latency terms to a monolithic-replica model. Impact (72, weighted 30%) reflects decision-grade fidelity (51.7%→2.6% E2E error on a real 16-GPU H800 testbed) but is capped because no public code release is announced. Rigor, Completeness, and Resilience (62 each) are pulled down by the absence of error bars throughout, the §6 use-case results (30.4% aTTFT reduction, 2.04× RL throughput) being validated only inside the simulator whose own fidelity is the subject of the paper — a circularity the paper itself flags risk for in its motivation — and a 16-GPU testbed backing 1K-GPU claims. Composite: 0.15*78 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*75 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*62 + 0.30*72 ≈ 69.
systems Novelty 78 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 62 Resilience 62 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM inference has become the dominant operational cost for service providers, and the configuration space for modern serving systems is enormous — over 1,000 combinations for a 200B-parameter MoE model, with sweeping 100 configurations on a 64-GPU H100 cluster costing $128,000–$180,000. Simulation is the natural tool for design-space exploration, but existing simulators (Vidur, AIConfigurator, APEX, LLMServingSim2.0) fail in two ways that make them unreliable for production decisions. First, they assume a homogeneous set of monolithic replicas, which cannot represent disaggregated serving architectures (PDD, AFD) where different GPU pools play distinct roles connected by explicit KV-cache or activation transfers. Second, they smooth serving dynamics into average-case models: token-count proxies cause 32.6% mean FlashAttention latency error and 21.0% mean MoE GroupedGEMM error, while "total minus weights" KV-cache budget estimation overestimates the admissible budget by 8.1%–39.73% across configurations — errors large enough to shift TTFT/TPOT estimates across SLA boundaries and recommend configurations that fail on real systems. Key Idea The core insight is that LLM serving is a closed-loop system in which operator runtime errors, KV-cache budget errors, and scheduler admission decisions compound: a 0.1 ms per-operator error propagates across hundreds of decode steps, the scheduler uses the KV-cache budget to bound concurrent decode slots, and batch composition is determined online, making the execution space infeasible to profile exhaustively. The key technical move is to resolve all three sources of error together — materializing disaggregated dependencies as explicit events in the DES graph (so the event ordering enforces correctness), and classifying operators by what actually determines their runtime (token count, per-request length distribution, or expert routing load) so that the right ML predictor feature set is applied to each class. Methodology Frontier is organized into four modules. The Control Plane compiles a serving specification into role-specific Cluster Workers: co-location yields a monolithic cluster C; PDD yields prefill cluster P and decode cluster D; AFD yields P plus separate attention cluster A and FFN cluster F. Parallelism is expressed via a two-domain decomposition with attention sharding tuple (tp_attn, dp_attn) and FFN sharding tuple (tp_ffn, ep_ffn). Five dependency classes are materialized as explicit events: (i) pipeline stage chaining, (ii) PDD KV-cache transfer (KVCacheTransferStart/End causal edges), (iii) AFD per-layer M2N activation transfer ping-pong between A and F, (iv) MoE EP synchronization (AllToAllCombineReady fires only after the slowest rank), and (v) stateful reasoning requeue (ThinkingRequeue event re-admits the request with session affinity for prefix-cache continuity). The Execution Plane lifts production scheduler logic from vLLM and SGLang unchanged, rewiring only the I/O layer; Runtime Adapters inject CUDA Graph (padding to next bin, switching between kernel-only and launch-inclusive timing families), speculative decoding MTP (per-request planned/verified/accepted/committed token tracking), prefix caching (block-hash index), and chunked prefill. The Fidelity Plane applies three operator classes with distinct predictors: token-count operators (linear ops) use linear regression or small random forest on num_tokens; sequence-dependent operators (attention) use random forest over aggregate counts plus distributional statistics of per-request lengths (min, max, percentiles of prefill and decode lengths); routing-dependent operators (MoE GroupedGEMM) use random forest over load-balance statistics (variance and max of token-to-expert counts, expert selection ratio). Memory capacity is estimated via a vLLM-style dummy profile run on a single GPU, measuring weight memory, torch peak increase, and non-torch residency (NCCL workspace, CUDA Graph capture regions); the KV-cache block budget subtracts all three from the requested GPU budget. The Comm. Backend is pluggable (ASTRA-Sim or HTSim), selected by domain scale. The system is implemented in approximately 70K lines of Python, with 808 typed CLI options. Key Results All fidelity experiments use vLLM v0.10.2 (V1 engine) as ground truth on two 8×H800-SXM GPU servers. Operator-level: BF16 p50/p95 relative error is 3.5%/14.2% for attention, 3.3%/6.4% for linear ops, 1.4%/5.3% for MoE GroupedGEMM; Vidur's token-only predictor reaches 55.4%/376.1% for attention and is undefined for GroupedGEMM. FP8 shifts attention p95 to 8.8%. Memory: Initial KV-cache block budget is within 1.89% of vLLM across all four (PP,TP,DP,EP) configurations tested; the analytical baseline overestimates by 14.10%–39.73%. Runtime optimizations: CUDA Graph decode speedup is matched within 1.7% (co-location) and 6.1% (PDD); prefix-cache final hit ratios match exactly (36.98% and 37.11%); MTP speculative decoding p95 errors stay within 11.28% across six configurations (verify lengths {2,8,32} × acceptance ratios {0.3,0.7}). End-to-end: Under co-location, Frontier tracks vLLM within 9.37% across 32 cases (dense and MoE, four workload patterns), with TPOT-p95 and E2E inside 6.4% in all but one; AIConfigurator produces E2E makespan errors up to 170% on dense decode-heavy. Under PDD, all 32 entries are within 10.99% with 20 of 32 inside 3%; AIConfigurator reaches 200% E2E error on both dense and MoE decode-heavy (architectural failure due to missing KV-cache transfer dependency). Under AFD (Step3-316B, 16 H800s), TPOT is within 6.4% and throughput within 7.0% for both FFN-TP and FFN-EP layouts. Frontier scales to 1K+ GPU simulations on commodity CPU hardware (wall-clock time vs. cluster size is not benchmarked). Four use cases are presented using ex-situ simulation only: SLA-aware Pareto frontier comparison shows PDD outperforms at loose TTFT ≤3000ms (137.4K tok/s) while AFD is better at tight TTFT ≤500ms (116.2K tok/s vs. PDD 100.7K tok/s); heterogeneous GPU allocation shows H20 decode pool in PDD at P:D=1:1 passes SLA (CE=1.33) but P:D=2:6 fails SLA despite SR=1.69; a phase-aware H2Q-BR scheduler achieves 30.4% p95 aTTFT reduction over vLLM default; dynamic RL rollout reconfiguration halves makespan from 528.8 s to 259.1 s with 2.04× decode throughput. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW METHOD. The disaggregated abstraction — compiling serving specifications into role-specific cluster workers with explicit event-graph dependencies for PDD/AFD/MoE-EP — is genuinely novel as a simulator architecture. The class-specific operator predictor design (distributional batch features for attention, load statistics for MoE routing) is a real methodological contribution to simulation fidelity. Individual components (DES simulation, random forests, vLLM scheduler logic) are not new, but the specific co-design of architectural completeness and decision-grade fidelity with quantified evidence for each failure mode is original and practically significant. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are partial: each fidelity component (operator accuracy, memory budget, CUDA Graph, prefix cache, speculative decoding) is evaluated separately before the end-to-end comparison, which supports attribution. However, error bars and confidence intervals are absent throughout — all numbers are point estimates from single runs. The testbed is 16 H800 GPUs; the claimed 1K+ GPU scalability is stated but not benchmarked (simulation wall-clock as a function of cluster size is not reported). AFD calibration uses an in-house Step3-316B implementation with no publicly available ground truth, limiting independent verification. Code is not announced as publicly released; the paper describes 70K LoC Python but provides no repository. The use-case experiments (§6) are simulation-only with no hardware validation — the H2Q-BR scheduler (30.4% aTTFT reduction) and RL reconfiguration (2.04× throughput) are presented as findings of the simulator, but the simulator itself is the artifact being evaluated, creating a circularity. Comparisons are against current open-source simulator versions as of April 2026, with discrepancies resolved against repositories rather than papers, which is methodologically reasonable but creates an asymmetric characterization of competitors. Deployability (SYSTEMS) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built simulation tool evaluated through deployment fidelity comparisons. - Practical: Frontier can simulate 1K+ GPU clusters on commodity CPUs; single-GPU profiling is sufficient to generate operator and memory predictors for any cluster scale. - Efficient: Single-GPU dummy profile runs replace full-cluster hardware sweeps; the 70K LoC Python implementation may impose non-trivial simulation wall-clock for very large-scale or long-horizon traces, but this is uncharacterized. - Effective: Evidence of solving the stated problem is strong — sub-10% TTFT/TPOT errors across co-location and PDD on real hardware for two model families and four workload patterns; the SLA decision drift experiment (Figure 5) concretely demonstrates that prior simulators recommend configurations that violate SLA on real hardware. - Scalable: 1K+ GPU simulation on commodity CPU is claimed but not measured; the single-representative-rank PP profiling approach is acknowledged to contribute residual errors in pipeline-parallel configurations, and no results above 256 GPU equivalents are presented. The work delivers a software artifact (Python simulator), though no public release is announced. Strengths - The motivation section (§2.2) establishes each failure mode of existing simulators with real vLLM measurements — CUDA Graph padding inflation (22.6%–57.2% extra tokens), KV budget overestimation (up to 39.73%), and MTP sign-mismatches (4 of 5 low-acceptance batches predicted to slow down when they actually speed up) — grounding the design choices in quantified evidence rather than intuition. - The operator classification into token-count / sequence-dependent / routing-dependent classes, each with appropriate ML predictor feature sets, is well-motivated: Vidur's single token-count regressor has 376.1% p95 attention error precisely because attention runtime is shaped by per-request length distribution inside a batch, not aggregate token count, and the paper correctly identifies and fixes this. - The disaggregated abstraction correctly identifies that PDD/AFD are not expressible by adding latency terms to a monolithic-replica model — the event graph changes fundamentally, and AIConfigurator's 200% PDD error is a direct consequence of the missing KV-cache transfer causal edge, not parameter miscalibration. - The four use cases demonstrate analysis that is not reproducible with any prior simulator: the SLA-conditional Pareto frontier reversal (PDD preferred at loose TTFT, AFD preferred at tight TTFT) is a decision that would be made incorrectly with prior tools, and the gate-based heterogeneous GPU analysis (H20 on FFN fails, H20 on attention passes) is a role-level finding that requires the disaggregated cost model. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) Frontier is primarily calibrated around vLLM, with SGLang and TensorRT-LLM support deferred to future work; (2) CPU overhead modeling may lack robustness as it fluctuates with system scale and architectural updates, and CUDA API interception is discussed as a more accurate alternative but debated for scalability across diverse CUDA versions; (3) profiling tools introduce intrusive overhead, requiring strict isolation of fine-grained metrics from system-level metrics; (4) AFD calibration uses an in-house implementation because no public AFD implementation exists. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The use-case scheduling and reconfiguration results (§6.3 H2Q-BR: 30.4% aTTFT reduction; §6.4 layout switch: 2.04× throughput) are validated entirely within the simulator whose fidelity is the subject of the paper — no hardware deployment validates these policies. The paper's own motivation (Figure 5) shows that prior simulators recommend configurations that fail on real hardware; the same risk applies here, yet the use cases present simulation outcomes as findings without this caveat. - The testbed (16 H800 GPUs) is small relative to the claimed use cases (256-GPU and 1K-GPU scenarios), meaning all large-scale results are produced by a tool validated only at 16-GPU scale — extrapolation risk is substantial and unquantified. - Simulation wall-clock time as a function of cluster size, trace length, and workload complexity is not reported, making it impossible to assess whether 1K-GPU simulation is practically feasible for realistic production traces. - The H2Q-BR scheduler comparison is thin: only vLLM default and skip-join MLFQ are compared; no other stateful scheduling baselines are evaluated. - Operator predictor training data size and train/test split methodology are not reported; it is unclear whether predictors generalize to operators and batch compositions outside the profiling distribution. - Results are limited to two MoE model families and one dense model; generalizability to other architectures (different expert counts, routing strategies, dense models at scale) is assumed. Bottom Line A practitioner designing disaggregated LLM serving infrastructure or trying to understand why their simulator's configuration recommendations fail on real hardware will get substantial, quantified value from this paper — but should treat the §6 use-case policy results as simulation-validated hypotheses requiring hardware confirmation before deployment.
Search Results 2605.21312 PDF
#42 — Innovative (69)

Evolutionary Multi-Task Optimization for LLM-Guided Program Discovery

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EMO-STA (Evolutionary Multi-Task Optimization — Shared-Then-Adapt), a two-stage framework that first evolves a shared archive of executable programs across a related task family and then initializes task-specific adaptation from that archive using one of three variants. - Why it's important: LLM-guided evolutionary search (e.g., FunSearch, AlphaEvolve) is expensive — often requiring tens of iterations — and wastes compute by treating structurally related tasks as entirely independent, re-discovering common program structure from scratch each time. - How it does it: A unified program interface forces the LLM to write one parameterized function evaluable across all tasks in a family; shared evolution optimizes the average family-level score, producing an archive of reusable candidates that warm-start task-local adaptation under a compute-matched budget. - The good: At least one EMO-STA variant beats matched-compute single-task evolution in 38/40 task-family/model cells, with the largest gains on Heilbronn triangle (0.547→0.650 for Haiku-4.5, 0.744→0.877 for Opus-4.6); the overfitting-mitigation finding in ARC and time-series settings is a non-obvious and potentially high-value secondary contribution. - The missing: All eight task families are author-constructed from OpenEvolve examples with hand-crafted parameter ranges; experiments use only K=2–4 tasks per family; and the only baseline is matched-compute independent single-task evolution — no comparison to warm-starting heuristics, multi-task Bayesian optimization, or other transfer approaches. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers and practitioners building LLM-guided program search pipelines over related task families; skip if you need evidence of generality beyond hand-constructed benchmarks.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (75) is the highlight — the finding that shared cross-task evolution acts as a regularizer that recovers ARC overfitting failures and flips COVID-Deaths test MASE from -1.12% to +13.24% is a striking, non-obvious secondary contribution. Rigor (68) is respectable for evolutionary search (5 models × 5 runs, an explicit S+KA=KB compute-matching protocol, released code) but is capped by author-constructed task families, K=2–4 only, a single matched-compute baseline with no transfer-learning comparators, and a 20-case ARC / single-dataset time-series evaluation. Novelty (70), Completeness (68), Resilience (68), and Impact (65, niche) keep it in the Innovative band. Composite: 0.20*70 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*68 + 0.10*65 ≈ 69.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 68 Resilience 68 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM-guided evolutionary search methods (FunSearch, AlphaEvolve, OpenEvolve) discover strong algorithms by iteratively proposing and evaluating candidate programs, but they run independent evolutionary processes for each target task even when multiple tasks share structural similarities — for example, circle-packing with n=20, 22, 24, 26 all require a geometric placement strategy parameterized by circle count. This wastes the family-level compute budget: structural knowledge useful across tasks must be rediscovered independently for each. The research question is a sample-efficiency one: can a fixed total iteration budget be allocated to a shared phase plus task-specific adaptation phases to yield better average task quality than K independent single-task runs of the same total budget? Key Idea If related tasks can be expressed through a shared executable interface — the same function signature evaluated by task-specific evaluators — then a shared evolutionary phase can build an archive of programs biased toward reusable general-purpose structure (by optimizing the average family-level score), and task-local adaptation can then specialize selected archive candidates. The shared archive is not a final solution but a warm-start population; the structural bias it encodes is the mechanism behind both the compute-efficiency gain and the overfitting-mitigation effect. Methodology EMO-STA has two phases. In the shared phase (S iterations), an LLM proposes and mutates candidate programs evaluated across all K tasks simultaneously; the aggregate score is s_shared(p) = (1/K)∑s_i(p), favoring programs that generalize. All candidates with per-task scores are stored in a shared archive A_shared. In the adaptation phase (A iterations per task), task-local archives are initialized from A_shared via one of three variants: (i) STA Warmstart transfers the entire shared archive; (ii) STA Best-Shared seeds from the program with the highest average family-level score; (iii) STA Best-Local seeds from the shared program with the highest score specifically on the target task. Each variant then continues local evolution under the task-specific objective s_i. Budget matching is rigorous: S + K·A = K·B, equating total LLM call counts between EMO-STA and K independent single-task runs of B iterations each. The system is built on OpenEvolve (island-based population management, MAP-Elites context selection). Score normalization by known reference values is applied in geometric families to prevent the aggregate from being dominated by tasks with larger numerical ranges. The unified program interface is enforced through prompt design, a critical design decision the paper validates through its exclusion of tasks that cannot be parameterized this way. Key Results Experiments span 8 task families across 5 models (Haiku-4.5, Sonnet-4.5, Sonnet-4.6, Opus-4.5, Opus-4.6) with 5 independent runs each. At least one EMO-STA variant exceeds single-task baseline in 38/40 task-family/model cells. The strongest gains appear in Heilbronn triangle: +0.103 for Haiku-4.5 (0.547→0.650), +0.119 for Opus-4.5 (0.622→0.741), +0.133 for Opus-4.6 (0.744→0.877). On nearly-saturated tasks, EMO-STA still improves: circle packing Sonnet-4.6 goes from 0.957 to 0.997. STA Best-Local is strongest in-distribution (23/40 cells), while STA Best-Shared yields better OOD transfer to held-out task sizes. Compute-allocation experiments show that a balanced split (S ≈ K·A) is consistently near-optimal; any nonzero shared budget outperforms pure single-task. The ARC overfitting finding is striking: 19/20 Gemini and 12/20 Opus-4.6 single-task failures were overfitting (programs fit training examples but fail held-out test); EMO-STA (STA Best-Shared/Warmstart) recovers 13/20 Gemini failures and 8/20 Opus-4.6 failures. In time-series feature engineering on COVID Deaths (266 univariate series), single-task per-series evolution improves validation MASE by 12.32% but worsens test MASE by 1.12%; STA Best-Shared improves test MASE by 13.24% and test wQL by 32.56%. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.22613v1, May 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The novelty is the application of evolutionary multitasking to open-ended LLM-generated program search via a shared-then-adapt architecture with a unified program interface. The component ideas — evolutionary multitasking (Gupta et al. 2016), warm-starting evolutionary search, LLM-guided program evolution (FunSearch, AlphaEvolve) — are individually established; the synthesis for text-based executable-program evolution is new. The overfitting-mitigation interpretation of shared evolution as a regularizer is a secondary empirical finding with modest theoretical framing. Rigor & Reproducibility Rigor is solid for an empirical systems paper. Five independent runs with mean ± std are reported across five LLM models, which is more thorough than typical evolutionary search papers. The compute-matching protocol is explicit and enforced (S + KA = KB). Code is released at https://github.com/alperengozeten/emo. Three ablation variants (Warmstart/Best-Shared/Best-Local) and multiple compute-allocation splits are evaluated. No significance tests or confidence intervals beyond standard deviation. Methodological concerns: all task families are author-constructed from OpenEvolve examples, with no evaluation on independently established program synthesis benchmarks except ARC (where the evaluation protocol is non-standard — only specifically selected failed-run cases are tested, not a random or systematic split). The ARC evaluation with 20 failed cases per model is too small to draw strong conclusions. The time-series experiment covers only one dataset. Equating iteration counts as compute may overstate fairness for tasks with heterogeneous evaluation costs (Rust adaptive sort compiles code; geometric tasks call scipy). Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on commercially available LLM APIs (Claude via Amazon Bedrock, Gemini via Google AI Studio); no specialized hardware required beyond API credits. - Efficient: The adaptation initialization adds negligible overhead — archive transfer reuses cached per-task scores from shared evolution — so the marginal cost of EMO-STA over matched single-task runs is essentially zero in LLM calls. - Effective: Evidence for in-distribution improvement is strong (38/40 cells); the overfitting-mitigation finding on ARC and COVID Deaths is compelling but rests on a single time-series dataset and 20 ARC failure cases. - Scalable: Only K=2–4 tasks per family were evaluated; behavior with K>>4, with diverse tasks that risk negative transfer, or with automatically discovered task groupings is untested and only claimed. The paper delivers a software artifact (code released on GitHub on top of OpenEvolve), not just paper form. Strengths - The compute-matching protocol is principled and transparent: total LLM iteration counts are equalized, directly isolating the value of shared structure over additional search budget. - The overfitting-mitigation finding is non-obvious and practically valuable: shared evolution acts as a regularizer by selecting programs that must generalize across related tasks, recovering 60-65% of Gemini's overfitting failures on ARC and flipping the COVID Deaths test-set improvement from negative to +13.24%. - The three-variant ablation provides concrete, actionable guidance: Best-Local for in-distribution adaptation, Best-Shared for OOD generalization; this is not a post-hoc rationalization but consistently supported across families. - Multi-model evaluation (5 models, 5 runs) provides substantially more evidence than typical evolutionary search papers that report single-model, single-run results. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state in the Discussion and Appendix C.4 that EMO-STA requires tasks to be expressible through a shared executable interface, limiting it to task families with compatible program signatures. They call for extension to arbitrary task sets via automatic grouping and for understanding when the LLM can infer reusable structure. The COVID Deaths appendix notes that task-local adaptation after the shared phase could reintroduce overfitting, which is why only STA Best-Shared (no adaptation) is reported for that case study. These limitations are stated directly and without minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The sole baseline is matched-compute independent single-task evolution. Missing comparisons: warm-starting one task's final program as the seed for related tasks, multi-task Bayesian optimization (Swersky et al. 2013), or simply running a single-task search for twice as many iterations and picking the best. Without these, it is unclear how much of the gain comes from the shared-archive structure versus the population-seeding effect alone. - All 8 task families are author-constructed with hand-selected task parameters that guarantee relatedness. The method is never tested on independently established multi-task program synthesis benchmarks (beyond ARC in a non-standard protocol). - K=2–4 is very small. With K=20 tasks spanning heterogeneous subtasks, the average-score objective may induce negative transfer; this regime is entirely unexplored. - Equating iteration counts as compute is inconsistent across families: Rust adaptive sort requires compilation per candidate (seconds per evaluation), while circle packing runs numpy/scipy (milliseconds). Wall-clock compute parity would tell a different story. - There is no analysis of what properties shared-archive programs have that make them better seeds than random initializations — the structural-bias claim is asserted but not characterized (e.g., no analysis of code similarity, algorithm class distribution, or coverage of the program space). Bottom Line A well-executed empirical paper with a principled compute-matching protocol, consistent gains across diverse tasks and models, and a compelling non-obvious finding on overfitting mitigation; practitioners building LLM-guided program search over related task families should read it, though the hand-constructed task families and missing transfer-learning baselines leave the generality of the method open.
Search Results 2605.22613 PDF
#43 — Innovative (69)

Rethinking Cross-Layer Information Routing in Diffusion Transformers (92 ▲ )

Chao Xu, Maohua Li, Qirui Li, Yixuan Xu, Yanke Zhou, Yunhe Li, Cuifeng Shen, Hanlin Tang, Kan Liu, Tao Lan, Lin Qu, Shao-Qun Zhang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Diffusion-Adaptive Routing (DAR), a drop-in replacement for the standard residual stream in Diffusion Transformers that performs learnable, timestep-adaptive, softmax-weighted aggregation over all preceding sublayer outputs instead of uniform accumulation. - Why it's important: The residual pathway is the one component of DiTs that has been directly inherited from vanilla Transformers without redesign, yet it governs how information accumulates across layers throughout the time-varying denoising process — a mismatch that wastes model capacity and slows convergence. - How it does it: At each sublayer, the running residual sum is replaced by a softmax attention over cached prior sublayer outputs, where the query is derived from the current adaLN-modulated hidden state, so timestep information from DiT's existing conditioning pathway automatically modulates which historical representations are retrieved. - The good: On ImageNet 256×256, DAR-static reaches the converged quality of vanilla SiT-XL/2 (≈9.67 FID) in 8.75× fewer training iterations, achieves a final FID of 6.92 (SDE) vs. 8.61 for the baseline, and stacking DAR with REPA further cuts FID from 9.89 to 7.09 at 100K iterations. - The missing: All primary experiments use a single 675M–751M parameter model on 256×256 ImageNet; no systematic evaluation at billion-scale MM-DiT or video-DiT backbones is presented, leaving the scaling behavior of the core claim unvalidated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on DiT architecture, training efficiency, or residual stream design; practitioners fine-tuning large T2I/T2V models should note that the large-scale evidence is qualitative only and the method has not been peer-reviewed.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (74) and the diagnostic contribution lift this — the quantified PreNorm-dilution symptoms (magnitude inflation, gradient collapse, >0.9 cosine redundancy) and the SiT-Plus parameter-matched control are methodologically clean and independently valuable. Rigor (67) and Resilience (66) are the limiting dimensions: no code released, no seeds/error bars, single SiT-XL/2 scale, comparisons not always at matched iterations, the large-scale DMD result is qualitative only, and the chunk-size formula's α is fitted post-hoc. Impact (62) credits the delivered Triton kernel and 8.75× convergence speedup, discounted for unproven billion-parameter generalization.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 67 Non-Obv 74 Completeness 73 Resilience 66 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have had nearly every design axis revisited — tokenization, attention, conditioning, training objectives, latent autoencoders — yet the pre-normalized residual stream that governs cross-layer information accumulation has been inherited wholesale from the original Transformer. This is architecturally problematic in the diffusion setting because the denoising process is inherently time-varying: at high noise, coarse structure dominates, and at low noise, fine details matter. A fixed, time-agnostic, unit-coefficient residual accumulation cannot express this shift. Practitioners building DiTs face a convergence tax and representational redundancy that existing techniques (U-Net skip connections, REPA) address only partially. Key Idea The single core insight is that the denoising timestep — absent in LLMs, central to DiTs — should drive which prior sublayer outputs are aggregated into the current hidden state. Rather than accumulating all prior sublayer outputs with equal weight, each layer should learn a timestep-conditioned softmax attention over its history of predecessors, allowing the model to dynamically shift from emphasizing coarse-structure sources at high noise to fine-detail sources at low noise. Plugging this into DiT's existing adaLN conditioning pathway makes timestep injection implicit and parameter-free. Methodology Standard DiT residual routing unrolls as h_l = h_0 + Σ_{i<l} f_i(h_i; t) with unit coefficients. DAR replaces this with a softmax-weighted sum: h_l = Σ_{i<l} α_{i→l}(t) v_i, where v_i = f_i(h_i; t) is each sublayer's output, k_i = RMSNorm(v_i) is the key, and the query q_l(t) is either a learnable vector w_l (static), an explicit w_l + e(t) injection reusing DiT's timestep embedder, or a projection W_q^(l) v_{l-1} (dynamic). The dynamic variant inherits timestep awareness implicitly because the adaLN pathway encodes t throughout the network. To control the O(Ld) activation cost of retaining all sources, a chunked aggregation scheme partitions the L sublayers into chunks of size S; each chunk is summarized by its last sublayer output, and the source set for any sublayer within chunk n contains all prior chunk summaries plus all intra-chunk raw outputs. A rate-distortion model predicts the optimal S = sqrt(L · (1−α)/(1+α)), placing S* ≈ 3.7–4.9 for SiT-XL/2 (L=56), matching the empirically selected S=4. Three critical design decisions are: (1) using adaLN-modulated features as queries so no new conditioning pathway is needed — well-justified by linear-probe diagnostics showing t is linearly decodable from these features with R² > 0.95; (2) exposing raw sublayer outputs in the final chunk's aggregator rather than compressed summaries — justified empirically by a ~2 FID gain at 200K iterations; (3) fusing the entire aggregation into a custom Triton kernel to avoid O(N) HBM reads per source — reduces forward latency 11.5× (22.5ms → 1.96ms). Key Results All experiments are on ImageNet 256×256 class-conditional generation with SiT-XL/2 (675M parameters). Baselines are re-run under identical conditions. (1) DAR-static-c4 at 600K iterations achieves FID-ODE 7.56, vs. SiT-XL/2 baseline 9.67 at 1.75M iterations — 2.11 FID gain at 8.75× fewer iterations. SDE FID is 6.92 vs. 8.61. (2) DAR-dynamic-c4 at 500K iterations achieves FID-ODE-CFG 2.05, vs. SiT-XL/2 2.15. (3) SiT-Plus, a widened SiT matched to the 752M parameter count of dynamic-c4 and trained for 1M iterations (2× the budget), achieves FID 10.02–10.85 and still trails DAR by a large margin, ruling out parameter count as explanation. (4) U-DiT-L (810M, 250K iters) achieves SDE-CFG FID 3.00; DAR-static-c4 achieves 2.23, outperforming it with fewer parameters. (5) Timestep ablation (Table 2): static without timestep injection reaches FID 11.51 at 400K, dynamic reaches 8.10, static with explicit injection reaches 7.97 — both timestep-aware variants substantially outperform the blind baseline. (6) DAR + REPA at 100K iterations achieves FID 7.09 vs. REPA alone 9.89; DAR+REPA at 100K surpasses REPA-alone at 200K (6.89). (7) Diagnostic measurements (Fig. 2): hidden-state magnitude inflates ~100× from block 1 to block 28 under standard residuals; gradient magnitude drops more than an order of magnitude after the first five blocks; adjacent-block cosine similarity stays above 0.9 throughout the deep stack. All three symptoms tighten substantially under DAR. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The method adapts Attention Residuals (AttnRes, a concurrent LLM-side technique) to the diffusion setting, with the genuinely novel contribution being timestep-conditioned query parameterization and the diagnostic framework revealing PreNorm dilution in DiTs along both depth and denoising timestep — a structure invisible to LLM-side analyses. The chunked aggregation is an extension of AttnRes's chunking with a theoretically motivated chunk-size formula. The diagnostic contribution (three symptoms, counterfactual source-importance probing of the standard residual stream) is independently valuable. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and targeted: timestep injection variants (Table 2), chunk size sweep (Table 4), REPA compatibility (Table 3), SiT-Plus parameter-matched control (Table 1). No error bars or multiple seeds are reported across any experiment — a notable gap for a paper whose main result is a 2.11 FID improvement. The linear-probe diagnostic (Fig. 5, R² across 28 blocks) is a credible mechanistic check that the dynamic query has access to timestep information. Baselines are re-run from scratch under identical conditions rather than copied from prior work — a credibility positive. Code is not released at submission time; no repository URL is mentioned. The paper is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2605.20708v1, May 20, 2026) and has not been peer-reviewed. The rate-distortion proposition (Prop. 1) has a complete proof and quantitative agreement with the empirical chunk-size sweep. The large-scale T2I application (DMD on Qwen-Image) is qualitative only — no FID numbers reported for that setting. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (image generation system with a built infrastructure component). - Practical: Runs on NVIDIA H20 GPUs with the custom Triton kernel; the kernel reduces per-step time to ~0.59 s for static-c4, roughly comparable to the SiT baseline. Commercially available hardware is sufficient. - Efficient: The naive aggregation is O(Ld) memory and O(N) HBM reads per source, making it training-prohibitive at depth; the Triton-fused kernel reduces forward latency 11.5×, backward 8.5×, and activation memory 75–82%, making the chunked variant viable. The static variant adds no parameters; the dynamic variant adds ~76M (10% overhead). - Effective: Evidence is strong for the specific setting tested: 2.11 FID improvement on SiT-XL/2 at 256×256 at matched compute, with ablations ruling out parameter count and U-Net skip topology as confounds. DMD high-frequency preservation is demonstrated only qualitatively. - Scalable: Not tested at scale beyond SiT-XL/2 (675M parameters, 256×256). The authors conjecture that PreNorm dilution worsens at greater depth, and the rate-distortion formula predicts chunk size grows as √L — but these are extrapolations, not experiments. Multi-billion-parameter MM-DiT or video-DiT validation is absent. The paper delivers a Triton kernel implementation alongside the architectural proposal; no weights or training code repository is linked. Strengths - The diagnostic contribution — quantifying forward magnitude inflation (15.5→1576 RMS, 100× over 28 blocks), gradient magnitude collapse (>10× drop after block 5), and cosine similarity redundancy (>0.9) jointly across depth and denoising timestep — is concrete, well-instrumented, and independently valuable regardless of whether DAR is adopted. - The counterfactual source-importance probe for the standard residual baseline (attaching scalar gates and reading loss gradients) is a methodologically clean way to reveal latent timestep-dependent routing preferences in a model with no explicit router. - The SiT-Plus parameter-matched control experiment directly rules out capacity as the driver of DAR's gains, a comparison that is often omitted in architecture papers. - The Triton-fused kernel delivering 11.5× latency and 78.7% memory reduction moves DAR from a conceptually interesting but impractical idea to something deployable in real training runs. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly defer large-scale validation to future work, noting that state-of-the-art T2I/T2V backbones (MM-DiT, FLUX, HunyuanVideo) operate at billions of parameters with substantially deeper stacks where the diagnosed symptoms should be more severe — but they have not yet tested this claim. They acknowledge that the optimal chunk size should grow as √L for deeper backbones and that the post-training DMD result is preliminary. The authors are direct about these scope boundaries without minimizing them.
