Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: hyperparameter tuning is problem-dependent and does not generalize across datasets with the same distributional assumptions; tuning improves wall-clock time more reliably than solution accuracy; GCP-FedAdam fails to find the MLE basin of attraction when synchronization is very infrequent; data heterogeneity across processors (Amazon dataset) causes GCP-FedAdam to require more computation to reconcile adversarial local progress. The authors directly confront the convergence risk of asynchrony and frame this study as examining both performance and convergence, not just performance — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - GCP's defining feature is flexible loss functions, yet all real-dataset experiments use Poisson loss exclusively; Bernoulli (binary data), Gamma (continuous positive), and Rayleigh (magnitude) losses — which motivated GCP's original development — are untested under any parallel setting, leaving a critical use-case gap. - The 172× speedup on Amazon uses parameters tuned on arXiv/synthetic data; the paper calls this a "cautionary tale" but does not ablate how much speedup remains with Amazon-tuned or default parameters, making the headline number difficult to interpret. - No convergence guarantees or even convergence conditions are provided for GCP-FedAdam in the nonconvex setting; for practitioners who need convergence assurances (e.g., scientific computing applications), the method is a black box. - Strong scaling is tested over only a 3× node range (6–18); weak scaling experiments are absent, and scalability claims beyond 18 nodes are unsupported by data. - The prior asynchronous GCP work of Lewis & Phipps [21] (ElasticAveraging and LocalSGD in GenTen) is implemented in the same software but does not appear in the performance comparison experiments, preventing a direct quantitative comparison against the proposed GCP-FedAdam. - The two-sided approach's per-iteration communication pattern setup is identified as dominant overhead in Figure 4, but no mitigation (e.g., pre-computed patterns, pattern caching) is proposed or evaluated, leaving a known bottleneck unaddressed. Bottom Line Practitioners at HPC sites who need to compute GCP decompositions of billion-nonzero sparse tensors under non-Gaussian loss gain a concrete, open-source implementation and actionable guidance on distributed strategy selection, but the restriction to Poisson loss in experiments and the absence of convergence guarantees for GCP-FedAdam limit confidence in the broader claimed generality.
Search Results 2605.20353 PDF
#55 — Innovative (68)

Geometry-Aware Representation Denoising for Robust Multi-view 3D Reconstruction (34 ▲ )

Jin Hyeon Kim, Jaeeun Lee, Claire Kim, Kyoungjin Oh, Paul Hyunbin Cho, Jaewon Min, Yeji Choi, Jihye Park, Hyunhee Park, Minkyu Park, Seungryong Kim · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces GARD (Geometry-Aware Representation Denoising), a framework that inserts a diffusion-based multi-view denoiser into the intermediate feature space of a frozen feed-forward 3D reconstruction model, simultaneously recovering accurate scene geometry and high-quality RGB images from motion-blurred multi-view inputs. - Why it's important: Feed-forward 3D reconstructors collapse catastrophically under real-world motion blur — AUC5 for pose estimation on HiRoom drops from 87.20 (clean) to 4.10 (blurred) — blocking their use in robotics, autonomous navigation, and AR where handheld capture is unavoidable. - How it does it: A multi-view latent diffusion denoiser (DiTDH architecture augmented with global cross-view attention) is trained with an interpolated flow matching loss — using the degraded intermediate feature as the source distribution rather than Gaussian noise — plus an attention alignment loss that supervises cross-view correspondences against ground-truth point-cloud maps. - The good: GARD achieves AUC30 of 92.37 on DTU vs. 85.91 for the best single-view baseline and 60.50 for the VAE-space multi-view baseline (VAEMVD), consistently ranking first across five benchmarks and four task metrics, with the VAEMVD ablation isolating the geometry-aware feature space as the decisive factor. - The missing: The entire evaluation uses motion blur only; generalizability to noise, haze, rain, or combined degradations is entirely undemonstrated, and inference latency under the 50-step ODE solver is never reported. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building robust 3D perception systems or working at the intersection of image restoration and geometric reconstruction — the feature-space denoising framing is the key transferable idea.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (78) and Novelty (75) are the high points — feature-space diffusion denoising inside a frozen multi-view reconstructor, with degraded-feature-initialized flow matching plus attention-correspondence alignment, is genuinely novel, and the interaction finding (attention alignment hurts without interpolated FM) is non-trivial. Rigor (68) is the main limiter: ablations are clean and the VAEMVD controlled comparison is excellent, but results are single-run with no error bars, training data is synthetic-only with no domain-gap analysis, K=18 insertion is unswept, and the headline SIR-Diff margin rests on an acknowledged-incomplete reproduction. Completeness (62) and Practical Impact (55) pull the composite down — the entire evaluation uses only motion blur despite a general-robustness framing, no inference latency is reported anywhere for a 50-step ODE solver on a 1.1B backbone, and generality to other backbones (DUSt3R/VGGT) is untested. Weighted: 0.20*75 + 0.25*68 + 0.15*78 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*55 = 68.
empirical Novelty 75 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 62 Resilience 65 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Feed-forward 3D reconstruction models (DUSt3R, MASt3R, VGGT, Depth Anything 3) achieve strong accuracy under clean imaging but are trained exclusively on degradation-free data. In practice, motion blur from handheld cameras or dynamic scenes distorts textures and structural edges that transformers rely on for cross-view feature matching. The degradation causes catastrophic failure: HiRoom AUC5 collapses from 87.20 to 4.10 and F-score from 84.05 to 11.74. Existing fixes are insufficient for three reasons: single-view restoration methods (Restormer, InstructIR) cannot enforce cross-view consistency and sometimes worsen geometry metrics; video restoration models (VRT, FMA-Net) require temporal coherence absent in sparse multi-view settings; and the one multi-view diffusion restoration method (SIR-Diff) operates in a VAE latent space whose compression discards fine-grained spatial structure critical for correspondence estimation. Key Idea The intermediate feature space of a transformer-based feed-forward 3D reconstructor is inherently geometry-aware — cross-view attention layers have already encoded multi-view correspondences — and is higher-dimensional than a VAE latent, preserving structural detail that VAEs compress away. Inserting a lightweight multi-view diffusion denoiser at an intermediate encoder layer to refine degraded features before they propagate deeper arrests degradation accumulation at the right abstraction level, making the geometry already present in the features do the work of guiding restoration rather than trying to recover geometry from restored pixels. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. First, V degraded input images pass through the first K=18 layers of the frozen DA3-GIANT-1.1 encoder (40 transformer layers, C=1536, 1.10B parameters), producing degraded intermediate representations z^K_deg. Second, the GARD denoiser — a DiTDH encoder-decoder architecture (encoder depth 8, wide decoder depth 6) with global cross-view attention layers interleaved between frame-level attention layers — maps z^K_deg → z^K_res. Training uses interpolated flow matching: the source is a noise-perturbed degraded feature ẑ^K_deg = z^K_deg + αϵ (α=0.3) rather than pure Gaussian, providing a structural prior. The objective is L_flow = E[||v(z_t, t) − v(z_t, t)||²₂] along the interpolated path. An attention alignment loss L_attn = −E[A log A_J] (cross-entropy between the denoiser's J=9th decoder global attention and ground-truth correspondence maps from clean-input point clouds) regularizes cross-view correspondence learning. Total loss: L = L_flow + λ_attn · L_attn with λ_attn=1.0. Third, restored features z^K_res propagate through the remaining encoder layers; four feature levels M={20,28,34,40} feed the frozen geometry decoder (depth, pose) and a fine-tuned ViT RGB decoder (adapted from GLD) in a single forward pass. Three critical design decisions: (1) using degraded feature as source distribution — well-justified by ablation (Tab. 4: interpolated FM alone raises ETH3D AUC30 from 67.30 to 73.85); (2) attention alignment effective only when combined with interpolated FM (alone it degrades performance) — an insightful interaction finding; (3) insertion at K=18 — motivated by feature similarity analysis but not empirically swept. Training: Hypersim + TartanAir synthetic data, 10 epochs, batch 8, H100 GPUs, AdamW lr=2e-4. Inference: 50-step Euler ODE solver. Key Results Evaluated on five DA3 benchmarks (HiRoom, ETH3D, DTU, 7Scenes, ScanNet++) under severe motion blur with 10 input views. On pose estimation (AUC30↑): GARD achieves 67.22 (HiRoom), 74.68 (ETH3D), 92.37 (DTU), 84.73 (7Scenes), 87.45 (ScanNet++), versus the best prior baselines of 30.17, 63.25, 85.91, 76.50, 76.12 respectively — margins of +123%, +18%, +7.5%, +10.8%, +14.8%. On 3D reconstruction (F-score↑): 18.25 / 45.79 / 36.08 / 35.77 vs. best baselines 12.41 / 39.91 / 29.80 / 32.40. On image restoration (PSNR↑): 21.89 / 21.88 / 21.25 / 22.67 / 22.19 vs. VAEMVD second-best 19.76 / 21.37 / 20.54 / 21.74 / 21.19. Counterintuitive finding: single-view restoration methods (HI-Diff, Restormer) frequently degrade pose estimation below the raw blurred-input baseline (HI-Diff HiRoom AUC30: 16.02 vs. 32.90 raw). The VAEMVD comparison — same denoiser architecture in VAE space — is definitively weaker on all metrics, isolating representation space choice. View-count ablation shows scaling to 50 views yields further gains (DTU AUC30: 84.25 → 92.37 → 98.00 at 4, 10, 50 views). Baselines are recent and strong for single-view tasks; the SIR-Diff comparison is weaker due to an unofficial reproduction. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The specific contribution — diffusion-based restoration in the intermediate feature space of a frozen multi-view 3D reconstructor, trained with degraded-feature-initialized flow matching plus attention-correspondence alignment — is genuinely novel in the multi-view reconstruction context. Representation Autoencoders (RAE) have previously explored high-dimensional feature spaces for 2D image diffusion, but their extension to geometry-aware multi-view reconstruction is new. The interpolated flow matching formulation (degraded-feature source distribution) is a meaningful training-side innovation. The combination is not a straightforward pipeline assembly; the interaction between interpolated FM and attention alignment is non-obvious. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations (Tab. 4) cleanly isolate interpolated flow matching and attention alignment, demonstrating their individual and joint effects across three representative benchmarks. The interaction result (attention alignment hurts without interpolated FM) is a substantive, non-trivial finding. No error bars or multiple-seed runs are reported — results are single-run. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/cvlab-kaist/GARD. Training and evaluation rely on publicly available datasets and checkpoints. Methodological concern: the SIR-Diff comparison uses an unofficial, acknowledged-incomplete reproduction — the 5-7× margin is likely overstated and should be treated with caution. Ablation over insertion layer K is absent; K=18 is justified only by qualitative feature similarity plots, not a quantitative sweep. Evaluation is confined to a single synthetic-to-real train/test split (trained on Hypersim + TartanAir, tested on real benchmarks); domain gap analysis is not performed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: GARD runs on top of DA3-GIANT-1.1 (1.10B parameters); inference requires H100-class GPU for training and likely a high-end GPU at test time, but the backbone already exists and the denoiser adds a compact head. No quantitative inference cost is reported. - Efficient: 50-step Euler ODE solver adds meaningful latency beyond the already-large backbone; authors acknowledge this as a limitation. No runtime numbers (ms/image or views/second) are provided anywhere in the paper, which is a critical omission for a method targeting real-world deployment. - Effective: Evidence across five diverse benchmarks on four task metrics consistently shows the method closes most of the gap between clean-input and degraded-input performance; the evidence is genuine and not cherry-picked. - Scalable: View-count scaling tested up to 50 views with monotonic improvement; large-scene (many objects, km-scale) or real-time video behavior is not addressed. Delivers a software artifact (code on GitHub); no trained checkpoint release is mentioned. Strengths - The VAEMVD controlled comparison — same denoiser capacity, same training data, same task, only the representation space differs — is the strongest experiment in the paper and provides unusually clean empirical support for the core claim. - The interpolated flow matching formulation is a principled, non-trivial design: initializing the diffusion from the degraded feature (which already carries partial structural information) rather than Gaussian noise is well-motivated and ablated, and the interaction with attention alignment is carefully characterized. - Simultaneous geometry and RGB recovery from a single denoised representation avoids the cascade of errors in sequential restore-then-reconstruct pipelines, and the evaluation separately measures pose, 3D reconstruction, depth, and image quality, demonstrating all four improve together. - Breadth of evaluation across five diverse benchmarks (indoor, outdoor, object-centric) and four quantitative metrics, combined with supplementary qualitative visualizations and view-count scaling experiments, gives a thorough picture of the method's behavior. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: (1) the diffusion-based denoiser requires iterative sampling steps, limiting efficiency in latency-sensitive settings; (2) future work will explore more efficient denoiser designs and multi-layer denoising to reduce error accumulation. They confront these squarely in the Conclusion. They do not acknowledge the single-degradation-type evaluation scope, the absence of inference latency benchmarks, or the lack of an insertion-layer ablation. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single degradation type throughout: motion blur is the only degradation evaluated, despite the paper's framing as a general robustness solution. Performance under noise, haze, rain, or combined degradations is entirely unknown. - No insertion-layer (K) ablation: K=18 is fixed, supported only by qualitative feature similarity plots. The optimal insertion depth is a critical hyperparameter and its sensitivity is uncharacterized. - Inference latency unreported: a diffusion model with 50 ODE steps on top of a 1.1B-parameter backbone likely has non-trivial runtime; its absence is a significant gap for practitioners who must deploy in real time. - SIR-Diff comparison is compromised by incomplete reproduction: the 5-7× margin over SIR-Diff is potentially inflated; a faithful reproduction or official checkpoint comparison is needed. - Synthetic-only training data: Hypersim and TartanAir are both synthetic; no analysis of domain gap or experiments with real degraded training data are presented. - Backbone specificity untested: GARD is evaluated only on DA3-GIANT and DA3-BASE variants; applicability to DUSt3R, MASt3R, VGGT — which the introduction frames as equally relevant — is not shown. Bottom Line Practitioners building motion-robust multi-view 3D perception pipelines will find GARD directly useful and well-supported; however, the missing inference latency report and single-degradation evaluation scope mean it cannot be adopted without additional characterization for deployment-critical applications.
Recommended 2605.26230 PDF
#56 — Innovative (68)

An overview of reaction outcome prediction with physics-based and data-driven methods

pubs.rsc.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper surveys reaction outcome prediction methods across data-driven (graph-based and sequence-generation models) and physics-based (PES exploration, reactive MD) paradigms, organized by whether candidate outcomes and their likelihoods are predicted jointly or in two decoupled stages. - Why it's important: Predicting which products form, in what amounts, and under what conditions is central to synthetic planning and mechanistic understanding, yet no prior review has organized the field by this fundamental task-decomposition axis, leaving researchers unable to clearly reason about the assumptions each method makes. - How it does it: The review imposes a unifying framework — simultaneous vs. decoupled (enumerate-then-score) prediction — across ~484 references spanning rule-based expert systems, graph neural networks, transformer sequence models, DFT-based TS searches, and machine-learned interatomic potentials, supplemented by detailed tables of benchmark performance. - The good: The explicit framing of what "likelihood" means differently across paradigms (statistical token probability vs. Gibbs free energy of activation) and the candid diagnosis that top-1 accuracy plateauing at ~90% on USPTO-480k reflects benchmark underspecification rather than model saturation are among the most intellectually useful contributions in recent reaction prediction literature. - The missing: The review does not substantively cover large language model (LLM) or foundation model approaches to reaction prediction — a significant and growing paradigm as of 2025 that fits awkwardly into neither the data-driven ML nor physics-based categories covered here. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers designing new reaction prediction methods or seeking to understand when existing tools can be trusted; also valuable for experimental chemists evaluating which computational tool to deploy for a specific task.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (a peer-reviewed synthesis review of empirically tested methods, Chemical Society Reviews). Non-Obviousness (75) and Resilience (72) lead: the one-step/two-step task-decomposition axis and the 'what does likelihood mean' framing reveal structural kinship between algorithmically dissimilar methods, and the candid diagnosis that ~90% USPTO-480k plateau reflects benchmark underspecification rather than saturation is well-bounded and anticipates obvious objections. Impact (55) is capped because the deliverable is a survey artifact with no released code, and Completeness (70) is pulled down by the absent LLM/foundation-model paradigm — already a notable gap at publication — plus self-citation concentration toward the Coley group's own FlowER/Graph2SMILES work. Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*72 + 0.10*55 = 68.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 70 Resilience 72 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Predicting chemical reaction outcomes — which products form, in what distributions, under specified conditions — is one of chemistry's most practically consequential and theoretically revealing challenges. The problem is compounded by context-dependence on solvent, temperature, catalyst, stoichiometry, and non-standard activation modes (photochemistry, electrochemistry, high pressure) that are rarely captured in available training data. A deeper issue is that the prediction task itself is under-specified: datasets derived from patent literature report only the experimentally convenient major product, omit reaction conditions, and systematically exclude failed reactions, meaning that models are learning to reproduce a distorted and incomplete view of chemical reactivity. Existing literature reviews typically organize methods by algorithmic paradigm (ML vs. QM) rather than by the structure of the prediction task, obscuring the most meaningful axis of variation: whether a method proposes and evaluates outcomes simultaneously, or treats enumeration and scoring as separable problems with potentially different fidelity requirements. Key Idea The core organizing insight is that reaction outcome prediction methods differ most fundamentally along two axes: (1) whether candidate outcomes and their likelihoods are generated in a single integrated pass or as explicit, separable stages; and (2) what notion of "likelihood" is used — statistical patterns from historical data (token probabilities, graph edit scores), or physically grounded quantities (activation barriers, Gibbs free energies). This framing reveals structural kinship between methods that appear algorithmically dissimilar: a sequence-to-sequence transformer and a nudged elastic band calculation followed by TST are both making one-step joint predictions, while template enumeration + a feasibility classifier and WLDN + WLDN scoring are both two-step frameworks. The distinction also clarifies why physics-based methods, despite their mechanistic rigor, are hard to apply at scale: their scoring oracle is expensive precisely because it is physically meaningful. Methodology This is a comprehensive synthesis review, not an empirical study. The review's architecture follows the organizing framework described above. Section 2 covers the data substrate (USPTO-derived experimental datasets up to 1.1M reactions, high-throughput experimental datasets, mechanistic datasets including RMechDB/PMechDB/FlowER/Reactron at 100k–2.85M elementary steps, and computationally generated datasets RGD-1/Grambow et al./Transition1x) and the physical substrate (transition state theory with its assumptions and failure modes, conformer generation from systematic to ML-based approaches, and energetic evaluation from ab initio through DFT, semi-empirical, force fields, and neural network potentials mapped on a cost-accuracy spectrum). Section 3 covers one-step simultaneous prediction: graph-based models (GTPN via RL, MEGAN via autoregressive editing, NERF/ReactionSink via non-autoregressive electron redistribution, FlowER via flow matching on bond-electron matrices) and sequence models (LSTM→Transformer progression, SMILES augmentation strategies, Chemformer pretraining, T5Chem multi-task, Graph2SMILES hybrid architecture), as well as physics-based PES methods (directed: ADDF, AFIR, SE-GSM, TSSCDS; undirected: metadynamics, bias potential MD, ab initio nanoreactor). Section 4 covers two-step prediction: exhaustive enumeration (bond-electron matrix, b2f2 graph edits), rule-based enumeration (symbolic: LHASA, SECS, RMG, BNICE; mechanistic: CAMEO, EROS, ROBIA), template-based enumeration from data (specificity-generality tradeoff in RDChiral/local/generalized templates), ML-based enumeration (source-sink pair prediction: ReactionPredictor, ELECTRO, Reactron; bond change prediction: WLDN), and scoring (kinetic: TS search via NEB/GSM/CINEB, ML TS generation via OA-ReactDiff/TSDiff/flow matching, activation energy prediction via CGR-GNN/EquiReact; thermodynamic: group additivity, delta-ML; general likelihood: feasibility classifiers, template ranking, candidate ranking via edit vectors/WLDN/LocalTransform). Section 5 covers regioselectivity (ranking-based vs. site-wise regression, hybrid ML+QM) and stereoselectivity (descriptor-based regression on er/DDG‡, data scarcity constraints) and yield prediction (HTE-trained models, global multi-reaction models, and the fundamental challenge of missing experimental context). Critical design decisions that the review identifies as most impactful: the separated vs. mixed reagent formulation for SMILES datasets, atom mapping quality, the document-level vs. random train/test split distinction (Bradshaw et al. 2025 showing substantial accuracy inflation from data leakage), and the generality-specificity tradeoff in template definition. Key Results Since this is a review, the reported results are field-wide empirical findings synthesized from primary sources. On the USPTO-480k separated benchmark, top-1 accuracy for data-driven models spans 82.4% (GTPN 2019) to 92.5% (Chemformer-Large 2022) for sequence models and 89.3–91.5% for graph models — a plateau suggesting benchmark saturation rather than model ceiling. On the more realistic mixed setting (reagents combined with reactants), top-1 accuracy is generally 2–4 points lower. Reactron (2025) achieves 96.4% top-1 on its own Reactron dataset (mixed), and FlowER (2025) achieves 88.4% top-1 but 96.3% top-2 on its FlowER dataset. Transfer learning yields dramatic gains: Heck reactions improve from 66.3% to 94.9% top-1 with pretraining on 370k general reactions; carbohydrate prediction improves from no meaningful baseline to 72.3%; Pfizer ELN models achieve 97.0% top-1 when trained internally vs. 82.9% top-3 when transferring from USPTO. Critically, Bradshaw et al. (ACS Cent. Sci. 2025) show that switching from random to document-level to author-level splits produces substantial accuracy drops, quantifying data leakage as a confound in reported benchmarks — the authors describe this as showing models struggle to generalize to reactions dissimilar from training. For yield prediction, even within-domain HTE models are often matched by one-hot encodings (no chemical information), indicating interpolation within combinatorial spaces rather than genuine chemical generalization. Physics-based methods are not benchmarked in a unified way; applications (RMG modeling n-hexane steam cracking with 1178 steps matching pilot plant yields; YARP exploring 31,000 glucose pyrolysis steps identifying 7,000 kinetically relevant below 45 kcal/mol; GRRM/AFIR computationally predicting difluorocarbene cycloaddition products confirmed experimentally) demonstrate mechanistic insight but not coverage or accuracy at scale. Novelty Classification NEW SURVEY / NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The contribution is a synthesis review with a genuinely novel organizing framework (one-step vs. two-step, and what notion of likelihood each method employs) rather than a new method or dataset. What is genuinely novel versus prior reviews: (1) the task-decomposition axis as the primary organizing principle rather than algorithm type; (2) the explicit treatment of how "likelihood" means different things across paradigms; (3) the candid assessment of benchmark validity, including the Bradshaw et al. data leakage finding; and (4) the concrete taxonomy of what reaction prediction tools are actually useful for versus aspirational. The comprehensive performance tables (Table 3 with ~30 models across 5+ datasets) and mechanistic dataset inventory (Table 2) constitute useful reference artifacts. Rigor & Reproducibility As a review, primary standards are comprehensiveness, accuracy of representation, and intellectual honesty. Table 3 explicitly warns that same-named datasets may differ in preprocessing, making cross-table comparisons unreliable — this is good epistemic practice. The paper is peer-reviewed in Chemical Society Reviews (a high-impact review journal). No original code or data was produced (stated explicitly in the Data Availability section). A significant concern: the corresponding author (Coley) and co-authors (Joung, Casetti) have multiple primary works cited prominently — FlowER (ref 49, published in Nature 2025), Graph2SMILES (ref 155), mechanistic dataset (ref 48), and Reactron citation (ref 224 from Casetti group) — raising a standard concern about selection bias inherent to expert reviews. The FlowER framework receives extended treatment that a disinterested reviewer might consider disproportionate. The conclusion section engages honestly with fundamental limitations including experimental irreproducibility as a ceiling on model learning, which is unusually candid for the genre. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL (reviews and evaluates empirically tested methods) - Practical: Data-driven models run on standard GPU hardware and several are available as web services or open-source packages; physics-based methods require HPC for DFT-based workflows but MLIPs (AIMNet2, OMol25-trained models) reduce hardware requirements substantially. - Efficient: Data-driven model inference takes milliseconds to seconds; template-based enumeration is near-instantaneous; DFT TS searches require hours to days per elementary step; the review maps this cost-accuracy landscape explicitly (Fig. 3) but does not provide standardized compute comparisons across paradigms. - Effective: Within-distribution benchmark performance is strong (90%+ top-1); real-world out-of-distribution performance degrades substantially as shown by document-level splits; yield prediction remains reliably poor outside narrow HTE interpolation settings. - Scalable: Data-driven models scale to millions of training reactions and are fast at inference; physics-based CRN exploration faces combinatorial explosion (bimolecular reactions scale as species count squared) and stiff ODE integration; MLIP-based approaches offer partial relief but inherit training distribution limitations. The paper delivers a survey artifact; described software systems (RMG, YARP, GRRM/AFIR, LocalTransform, Graph2SMILES, etc.) are separately available with varying packaging quality, noted as an accessibility barrier. Strengths - The one-step vs. two-step organizing axis is the most clarifying framework for this literature and is not prominent in prior reviews; it reveals non-obvious structural kinship between template+classifier approaches and end-to-end DL models, and between physics-based PES exploration and generative ML models. - The explicit acknowledgment that ~90% benchmark plateauing reflects data underspecification rather than model saturation is an important corrective to the field's tendency to interpret this as progress stagnation or achievement of human-level performance. - Table 3 compiling ~30 models across diverse datasets, with explicit caveats about comparability, is a reference artifact that will be widely used — the warnings about dataset naming inconsistencies (USPTO-Full size discrepancies between papers) reflect careful scholarship. - The conclusion's distinction between tasks where current models have clear demonstrated utility (parallel chemistry go/no-go decisions, C–H functionalization selectivity guidance, combustion kinetic modeling) versus aspirational but out-of-reach goals (in silico condition optimization, digital twin reaction flasks) is rare in review papers and provides actionable guidance for research prioritization. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly confront several fundamental limitations: (1) training data is not merely sparse but structurally incomplete — reaction product prediction is "ill-posed" because missing experimental context (concentration, order of addition, scale, workup) causally influences outcomes that models cannot recover; (2) physics-based methods degrade for complex systems due to incomplete treatments of electron correlation, solvation, and anharmonicity, and cannot accommodate procedural details like rate of addition; (3) reproducibility challenges in experimental chemistry (substantial fraction of reactions cannot be reproduced even with published procedures) set a ceiling on what any model can learn from literature data; (4) accessibility and usability barriers limit adoption even where performance is strong. The authors present these as substantive intellectual challenges rather than routine caveats, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - LLM/foundation model paradigm absent: The review does not cover LLM-based reaction prediction (GPT-style models fine-tuned on reaction SMILES or text, retrieval-augmented chemistry models, or multimodal models incorporating spectral data). As of 2025 this is an active and distinct paradigm; its omission means the review's coverage of "data-driven methods" is already partially obsolete at publication. - Condition recommendation not covered: Solvent, catalyst, and reagent recommendation given reactants and desired product is a practically important and closely related task addressed by several published models (Parrot, condition-prediction Transformers); it receives only passing mention in the conclusion. - Retrosynthesis integration underserved: The review focuses on forward prediction; how forward models integrate into or constrain retrosynthetic planning systems (ASKCOS, MCTS-based planners) is mentioned only tangentially despite being a stated primary motivation. - Self-citation concentration: The Coley group's own work (FlowER in Nature 2025, Graph2SMILES, mechanistic dataset, Reactron co-citation) receives extended treatment; an independent reviewer would likely apply more uniform depth across competing work (e.g., LocalTransform, BiG2S, T5Chem receive thinner treatment). - Stereochemistry integration: The review acknowledges stereochemical handling gaps in data-driven models but does not systematically address how stereochemical information should flow through the full enumeration-scoring-yield pipeline; the stereoselectivity section is largely disconnected from the main product prediction taxonomy. Bottom Line This is the most conceptually rigorous and honestly critical survey of reaction outcome prediction currently available — the one-step/two-step organizing framework and the candid benchmark critique alone justify reading it, though practitioners should note the missing LLM paradigm and the self-citation concentration that moderately skews coverage toward the authors' own methodological contributions.
Search Results
#57 — Innovative (67)

IndexMem: Learned KV-Cache Eviction with Latent Memory for Long-Context LLM Inference

Xintong Yang, Hao Gu, Binxing Xu, Lujun Li, Bei Liu, Jiacheng Liu, Qiyuan Zhu, Sirui Han, Yike Guo · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces IndexMem, a method that combines a lightweight learned indexer for KV-cache token importance prediction with a fast-weight latent memory module that compresses evicted tokens into an online-updated residual compensation for long-context LLM inference. - Why it's important: KV caches for long-context LLMs consume prohibitive GPU memory (≈320 GB for a 70B model at 1M tokens), and heuristic eviction policies degrade catastrophically at high compression ratios, making deployment of long-context inference infeasible on commodity hardware. - How it does it: A distilled MQA-style indexer learns to predict per-token importance by matching the backbone's max-aggregated attention distribution via streaming KL divergence, while evicted tokens are folded into a fixed-size fast-weight state (outer-product accumulation with decay) that is read back as a gated residual on the attention output. - The good: On RULER-16K at 90% eviction, IndexMem achieves 56.0 on Llama-3.1-8B versus 25.6 for SnapKV and 24.1 for PyramidKV — a margin that demonstrates the learned approach avoids the catastrophic collapse of heuristic methods under aggressive budgets. - The missing: All experiments use 7–8B models and at most 32K prefill; the 1M-token motivation is never evaluated, so the scalability claim beyond the tested regime is entirely unverified. - Read it or not: Worth reading for LLM inference engineers and researchers working on KV compression, particularly for the residual-compensation memory design and the streaming KL distillation trick; less useful for those focused on very large models or multi-million-token contexts.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (weights 20/25/15/15/15/10). Rigor (68) carries the most weight and is helped by peer-review (ICML 2026) plus informative component ablations (indexer-only vs. full, SnapKV+memory), but capped by no seeds/error bars, no code or weight release, a missing H2O baseline, and absent hyperparameter ablations. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (75) are solid: the residual-as-compensation latent memory (avoiding attention-sink collapse) and streaming KL distillation are non-obvious, demonstrated across three model families. Completeness (62) and Resilience (65) are dragged by the central scope gap — the 1M-token motivation is never evaluated, with all experiments at 7–8B and ≤32K — though the authors confront this honestly, a completeness positive. Practical Impact (55) is modest: a training recipe within KVPRESS, no released library or add-on weights. Composite: 0.20*72+0.25*68+0.15*75+0.15*62+0.15*65+0.10*55 ≈ 67.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 68 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 62 Resilience 65 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Autoregressive LLM inference accumulates a KV cache that grows linearly with sequence length, becoming the dominant memory bottleneck at long contexts. Existing KV eviction methods (SnapKV, TOVA, PyramidKV, KeyDiff, ExpectedAttention) are training-free and rely on heuristic proxy scores — local attention windows, key norms, or corpus-level query statistics — that fail to capture context-dependent token importance. More critically, all existing methods permanently discard evicted tokens, which causes irreversible information loss when those tokens become relevant during later decoding steps. The problem is acute for holistic long-context tasks (multi-hop QA, summarization) where useful information is spread across many low-salience tokens, not concentrated in a retrievable needle. Key Idea The core insight is to decouple the two failure modes of heuristic eviction: (1) inaccurate importance estimation and (2) permanent information loss. The learned indexer addresses (1) by distilling backbone attention behavior into a lightweight parallel module that can make better eviction decisions before or during prefill. The latent memory addresses (2) not by storing or offloading evicted tokens (which introduces latency) but by compressing them into a fixed-size fast-weight state via outer-product accumulation, then reading them back as a residual correction to the attention output. The residual-as-compensation framing — rather than injecting memory tokens into softmax — avoids attention-sink collapse and softmax amplification artifacts. Methodology The pipeline has two learned add-on modules inserted per transformer layer, trained with the backbone frozen on LongAlpaca via DDP. Indexer: An MQA-style lightweight attention module with H_index = H/4 heads and d_index = d_head/8 per-head dimension. It takes pre-RoPE queries (downprojected and QK-Norm normalized) and hidden-state-derived keys (shared across heads), computes gated per-head similarities, and aggregates via max over the query set to produce per-token importance scores. During training, it is distilled from the backbone's max-aggregated attention logits via KL divergence; the O(L²) teacher matrix is never materialized — importance is computed in a streaming FlashAttention-style chunk pass, maintaining an O(L) running-max vector. Sink tokens are masked from the KL loss to prevent gradient domination. Latent Memory Module: A fixed-size fast-weight state (M ∈ R^{d_mem × d_model}, b ∈ R^{d_mem}, d_mem = d_model/8), shared across heads. Evicted tokens update M and b via outer-product accumulation with exponential decay (λ, η hyperparameters). Reads are normalized dot-products: m(q) = Linear_θ(q)^T M / (Linear_θ(q)^⊙2^T b + ε). Slow weights (Linear_θ and gate g) are trained with an MSE objective matching the evicted-attention residual (o − o_attn). Training uses a two-stage schedule: indexer first (KL loss), then joint training with memory (MSE on residual). Three critical design decisions: (1) Residual compensation rather than memory-as-tokens avoids attention-sink collapse and is well-justified empirically. (2) Streaming KL distillation is a necessary engineering choice to make training feasible on long sequences, well-justified. (3) Shared memory across heads (rather than per-head memory) reduces parameter count to 0.52M but sacrifices per-head granularity — the trade-off is asserted but not ablated. Key Results All experiments run on a single NVIDIA H800 GPU. Backbone models: Qwen3-8B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. Evaluated via KVPRESS framework for consistent comparison. RULER (4K/16K): At r=0.90 on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, IndexMem scores 55.2/56.0 versus SnapKV 43.4/25.6, PyramidKV 23.1/24.1, TOVA 37.5/59.2, ExpectedAttention 30.6/25.5. At r=0.50 on Qwen3-8B-16K, IndexMem 80.0 vs SnapKV 62.8 vs PyramidKV 47.8. The gains are most pronounced at r≥0.50 and in 16K settings. NIAH: Under 50% compression on Llama-3.1-8B, IndexMem shows stable retrieval across context lengths 1K–12.5K and depths 15%–95%. Heuristic baselines show recency-biased failure patterns. The ablation (Indexer only vs. IndexMem) demonstrates that the memory module eliminates rare catastrophic misses. LongBench: All-task average at 50% compression: IndexMem 55.26 vs ExpectedAttention 42.09, PyramidKV 42.35, SnapKV 41.44, TOVA 42.10. At 75%: IndexMem 42.18 vs ~39 for heuristic methods. An anomalous positive finding: triviaqa performance can improve under moderate compression, attributed to removal of distracting tokens — not a fabricated result but not deeply investigated. Decoding compression (AIME25/Math500): IndexMem consistently leads across Bmax ∈ {512, 1024, 1536, 2048} on Qwen3-8B, with the gap narrowing at larger budgets. Efficiency: Indexer adds 19.92M parameters; memory module adds 0.52M. Cache memory decreases from 7.68 GB (r=0) to 4.08 GB (r=0.9) at 32K prefill on Llama-3.1-8B. Decoding latency with the memory module is competitive with (slightly above) the full-cache baseline across all compression ratios. Appendix (IndexMem+IndexCache): On Mistral-7B at 16K-50%, IndexMem+IndexCache scores 79.3 versus AdaKV 52.1, SnapKV 36.5, TOVA 70.1 — larger gaps than in the main table, though this variant uses a different training schedule, making direct comparison to Table 1 inappropriate. Baselines are current and reasonably strong (SnapKV, PyramidKV, TOVA, ExpectedAttention, KeyDiff). H2O, which is widely cited in the field, is absent from the main tables. Locret and xKV appear only in the appendix. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The combination of a distillation-trained indexer with a fast-weight latent memory used as residual compensation is not a direct prior. The indexer architecture adapts the DeepSeek lightning indexer concept to KV eviction; the memory module draws on Titans/fast-weight literature but applies it specifically to compensate eviction residuals rather than extending context. The streaming KL distillation training procedure is an engineering contribution. The paper does not claim a new problem or new benchmark. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: Indexer-only vs. IndexMem clearly shows the contribution of each component, and SnapKV+Memory shows the memory module generalizes to other eviction policies. However, ablations of shared-vs-per-head memory, d_mem sensitivity, λ and η values, and the two-stage training schedule are absent. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple training seeds are reported — a concern for the 7–8B-scale results, though differences at high compression are large enough to be robust. No code or model weights are released (not mentioned). The paper is peer-reviewed at ICML 2026 (43rd ICML, Seoul). Training is on LongAlpaca only; generalization to other training distributions is not evaluated. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL (methods and systems evaluated experimentally). - Practical: The method runs on a single NVIDIA H800 GPU with 32K prefill; given that it adds only 20.4M parameters and has no hardware-specific kernels beyond those in KVPRESS, it should run on any H100-class GPU. - Efficient: Cache memory at r=0.9 is 4.08 GB versus 7.68 GB at r=0 for 32K prefill on Llama-3.1-8B; decoding latency with the memory module is close to (but slightly above) the indexer-only variant due to online write overhead. The indexer-only variant is faster than the full-cache baseline at all compression ratios. - Effective: Evidence on RULER, NIAH, and LongBench across three model families demonstrates genuine task performance improvements, not just proxy metric gains. The memory module's contribution is specifically validated on tasks where holistic reasoning matters (multi-news, qasper, trec). - Scalable: Tested only on 7–8B models with up to 32K prefill and 1K decode tokens. The 1M-token motivation from the introduction is never experimentally realized. Behavior at 70B+ or true long-context workloads (128K+) is unknown. The work delivers a training recipe and evaluation framework (within KVPRESS); no standalone library or pretrained add-on module weights are released. Strengths - The residual-compensation memory design solves a real limitation of memory-as-tokens approaches (attention-sink collapse, softmax fragility) and is backed by ablation evidence. - Streaming KL distillation training is a practically important contribution that makes the method trainable on long sequences without O(L²) memory. - Cross-model generalizability (Qwen3, Mistral, Llama) with consistent improvements reduces the risk that results are model-specific. - The appendix cross-layer score redundancy analysis (Appendix B) provides useful diagnostic insight into why token importance is partially shared across layers, even if it is not the main method. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state: (1) training objectives for the indexer and memory may be suboptimal, especially at extreme compression; (2) training is conducted at limited token budgets and modest model scales, with no large-scale evaluation; (3) the backbone is frozen — end-to-end joint training is proposed as future work. These limitations are stated clearly and credibly in Appendix A. The authors do not, however, acknowledge the absence of code release or the gap between the 1M-token motivation and the 32K experimental scope. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 1M-token deployment scenario is cited to motivate the work but never evaluated; all experiments use 4K–32K contexts, making claims about ultra-long-context applicability speculative. - H2O (heavy-hitter oracle) is a widely-used and often-stronger baseline in the eviction literature and is absent from all main tables. - No batch-throughput or multi-sequence serving benchmarks are reported; decoding latency is measured in isolation, which understates real serving costs at batch size > 1. - The memory module's hyperparameters (λ, η, d_mem) are fixed without sensitivity analysis; at extreme compression, these likely have significant impact. - Shared memory across heads is an architectural choice that trades granularity for efficiency, but no ablation justifies it. - Training exclusively on LongAlpaca (instruction following) may not generalize to code or scientific document tasks, which often dominate real long-context workloads. - The 50% LongBench peak (all-task average peaks at 50% compression for IndexMem) is anomalous and deserves deeper investigation rather than a one-sentence attribution to information-density effects. Bottom Line A practitioner working on long-context inference efficiency gains concrete, deployable techniques from this paper — the residual-compensation memory design and streaming distillation training are non-obvious and demonstrated across three model families — but should treat the large-scale applicability claims with caution given the 32K context ceiling in all experiments.
Search Results 2605.25475 PDF
#58 — Innovative (67)

LLaVA-OneVision-2: Towards Next-Generation Perceptual Intelligence (18 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces LLaVA-OneVision-2 (LLaVA-OV-2), an 8B-parameter vision-language model that replaces uniform frame sampling with codec-stream tokenization — using H.264/H.265 P/B-frame packet bit-cost to partition video into adaptive temporal groups and motion-residual saliency to select compact spatial patches, together with a new jump-rope cycle-localization benchmark (JumpScore). - Why it's important: Video MLLMs waste token budget on temporally uniform but semantically redundant frames, causing them to miss rare but event-critical transitions in long or rapidly repetitive video — the core bottleneck for temporal grounding and long-form understanding under fixed compute. - How it does it: The codec front-end accumulates P/B-frame packet bytes into temporal bins, triggers adaptive GOP boundaries at bit-cost valleys, scores 2×2 patch blocks by fused motion-vector magnitude and luma residual, and packs the top-ranked blocks into compact I/P visual canvases assigned under a shared 3D RoPE spatiotemporal coordinate system. - The good: Under matched visual-token budgets on JumpScore (189 jump-rope clips, sub-second cycle localization), codec-stream inputs recover 82 of 85 cycle starts on a single illustrative clip (mIoU 0.894) versus 14 of 85 for uniform 128-frame sampling (mIoU 0.116), and the model outperforms Qwen3-VL-8B by +44.8 JumpScore mAP (74.9 vs. 30.1). - The missing: JumpScore is a small, single-lab-created benchmark of 189 clips designed precisely around the regime where codec-stream has a structural advantage; there is no independently constructed evaluation of the claimed temporal-grounding benefit, and the model trails Qwen3-VL-8B on image/document tasks (79.7 vs. 80.7 average) and on several general video QA tasks. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on video tokenization or efficient MLLM input representations; the codec-stream formalism is technically precise and the per-skill ablation in Figure 8 is genuinely informative about when the approach helps and when it does not.
Why this rank: Systems-flavored Empirical profile. Practical Impact (75) is the standout: code, data, and trained weights are fully open-sourced, delivering a genuine drop-in input-side mechanism. Rigor (65) is the strongest of the cluster — the codec-vs-uniform ablation holds backbone, connector, and protocol fixed to isolate the input representation, and Figure 8 honestly documents where codec hurts (future-event −11.7, dyadic −5.6) — though no error bars and the self-constructed JumpScore (where the method has a structural home-field advantage) and the Stage-4 spatial-corpus confound cap it. Completeness (72) and Non-Obviousness (72) are high: using codec bit-cost as a temporal information-density prior is a non-obvious, well-specified idea, and the paper confronts its task-class limits directly. Composite: 0.20*65 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*72 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*75 = 67.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 72 Resilience 60 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Video MLLMs serving robotics, temporal grounding, and long-form event understanding require allocating a fixed visual-token budget over videos that can span tens of minutes and contain hundreds of semantically similar frames. The prevailing paradigm — uniform frame sampling or mixed-resolution key-frame selection — allocates tokens by elapsed time rather than by information content: perceptual transitions, motion peaks, and event boundaries receive no preferential coverage. This is especially damaging for high-frequency repetitive motion (e.g., jump-rope cycles at 0.4–0.7 s spacing) where the decisive signal is a sub-second state change among visually near-identical frames, and for temporal grounding at low frame budgets where event boundaries fall between sampled slots. Prior token-compression work addressed this at the model side (merging, KV-cache sparsification, learned routing) but left the input side unchanged; adaptive frame samplers select frames but not patches, and cannot reduce the quadratic attention cost for high-motion frames. Key Idea: The core insight is that compressed video codecs (H.264/H.265) already produce a continuous signal of temporal information density: P/B-frame packet size (bit-cost) is high precisely when inter-frame prediction is difficult — at motion peaks, scene cuts, and perceptual transitions — and low during predictable, static intervals. Treating this bit-cost stream as a grouping prior replaces the fixed-GOP assumption with a content-adaptive one: the video is partitioned so each group accumulates roughly equal total prediction cost, naturally placing boundaries at low-cost (predictable) moments between high-motion bursts. Within each group, motion vectors and luma residuals then identify which 2×2 patch blocks carry spatial evidence, and stratified canvas packing prevents single-frame dominance. Methodology: The pipeline has four stages at the input side. (1) GOP Partition: the video is divided into B temporal bins of duration Δ; P/B packet bytes are summed per bin (I-frame packets excluded); a greedy boundary-trigger fires when cumulative bit-cost since the last boundary reaches a quota θ = Σe_b / K_tar after a minimum span L_min, subject to a maximum span L_max; each boundary is refined by a local valley search (lexicographic: lowest bit-cost bin, then closest to trigger). (2) Saliency Scoring: for each predicted frame, codec motion vectors are densified to the pixel grid and luma residuals are centered at their zero point 128; both are percentile-normalized and summed into a dense map S_t(x) = M_t(x) + R_t(x). (3) 2×2 Block Selection: candidate blocks are scored by summing saliency over their 4 constituent patches plus a normalized bit-cost prior; same-frame attenuation A̲_z = A_{t,i,j} / sqrt(1 + λρ_t(z)) prevents dominance by a single frame. (4) Canvas Packing: a cumulative allocation curve F_k(ℓ) over frame-level allocation masses assigns P-canvas slots proportionally to saliency mass, with fallback to neighboring frames when a temporal interval has too few candidates. The shared 3D RoPE assigns each token a (x, y, t) coordinate regardless of whether it came from a codec canvas, a uniform frame, or a static image. Group-visible attention masks expose tokens from the same variable-length GOP to one another but not across GOP boundaries. The LLM backbone is Qwen3-8B; the vision-language connector is a two-layer MLP. Training uses a four-stage progressive recipe scaling from 30s/30-frame to 10–15 min/768-frame videos; codec-stream tokenization is introduced only in Stage 4 (on the 10–15 min caption corpus at 384 and 768 frames), with 50% codec / 37.5% uniform / 12.5% image batches across stages 1–4. Critical design decisions: (a) bit-cost at bin level rather than per-frame (better temporal smoothing, justified empirically); (b) 2×2 block granularity aligned to the encoder's merge operation (avoids incoherent token merging, reasonable); (c) codec training introduced only in Stage 4 (sequential rather than concurrent with instruction tuning — design decision not ablated). Key Results: All results are for LLaVA-OV-2-8B versus Qwen3-VL-8B, Keye-VL-1.5-8B, InternVL-3.5-8B, PLM-8B, and LLaVA-OV-1.5-8B using LMMs-Eval under matched token budgets. Video understanding (18 tasks): LLaVA-OV-2 achieves 62.5 average vs. Qwen3-VL-8B 58.2 (+4.3). Largest absolute gains are on temporal grounding: Charades-STA +5.2, ActivityNet +7.0, QVHighlights +7.0 (all over Qwen3-VL-8B). JumpScore mAP: 74.9 vs. Qwen3-VL-8B 30.1 (+44.8). VSI-Bench +11.8, ReVSI +8.7. However, LLaVA-OV-2 trails Qwen3-VL-8B on MV-Bench (66.2 vs. 69.0), NextQA (82.5 vs. 83.4), MLVU-dev (76.6 vs. 78.1), LVBench (55.5 vs. 58.0), MMVU-val (56.2 vs. 58.7), and MMOU (39.5 vs. 40.6). Spatial reasoning (11 tasks): 63.5 vs. 58.2 (+5.3); largest gains on CrossPoint (+35.0) and TraceSpatial-3D (31.0 vs. 8.0). Image/document (11 tasks): 79.7 vs. 80.7 (−1.0); LLaVA-OV-2 trails on InfoVQA, OCRBench, MMStar, AI2D. Tracking (4 tasks, J&F): 48.0 vs. 32.4 (+15.6); all gains consistent. Codec vs. uniform ablation (matched model, same Stage-4 setting): 9.7-point average gain on 3 temporal grounding benchmarks; 17.3-point average on JumpScore across 6 budgets; modest 1.2–2.6 points on general long-video QA. Codec loses to uniform on VideoMME-v2 skills: future event prediction (−11.7), dyadic interaction (−5.6), motion properties (−5.1), motion trajectory (−3.1). The codec advantage on temporal grounding narrows significantly at higher frame budgets (Charades-STA codec actually falls below uniform at 32+ frames). Baselines are current (including Qwen3-VL, released 2025–2026) and tested under identical protocols. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The codec-stream tokenization pipeline (GOP partitioning by bit-cost, motion-residual spatial scoring, stratified canvas packing) is the primary technical contribution; related ideas appear in Video-LaVIT, EMA, and the predecessor OV-Encoder, so this is an incremental but coherent extension that unifies bit-cost temporal grouping with motion-residual spatial selection into an operationally complete training-and-inference pipeline. The JumpScore benchmark is a new evaluation artifact targeting a genuinely underrepresented regime. The empirical finding that codec advantages are task-class-specific (event-level vs. detail-sensitive) is a meaningful diagnostic contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility: The codec-vs.-uniform ablation (§8) holds model weights, backbone, connector, and evaluation protocol fixed, varying only the input representation — this is the paper's strongest methodological control. Numerical tables (Tables 4–5) back the scaling curves, and Figure 8 provides per-skill breakdown on VideoMME-v2, including cases where codec loses. No error bars or significance tests are reported on any benchmark; given that the average gains on general video QA are 1.2–4.3 points and codec advantage narrows or reverses at higher frame budgets for some tasks, confidence intervals matter. Code, data, and model weights are released (GitHub, HuggingFace), enabling replication. However, two methodological concerns are notable: (1) JumpScore was constructed by the same lab that designed the codec-stream method, and the benchmark targets exactly the regime (sub-second repeated cycles with high bit-cost at transitions) where the method has a theoretical and empirical advantage — the +44.8-point margin should be verified on an independently constructed benchmark; (2) Stage 4 simultaneously introduces codec tokenization and a new 4M spatial QA corpus, so the spatial task gains (+5.3 on 11 tasks, +35.0 on CrossPoint) cannot be cleanly attributed to codec tokenization alone versus the spatial data. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (systems + experiments). - Practical: The codec preprocessing pipeline (ffprobe/ffmpeg for packet metadata, standard motion-vector extraction) runs on commodity hardware; the 8B model fits on a single A100-class GPU. - Efficient: Codec preprocessing adds a one-time per-video extraction step but reduces token count by up to 87.5% versus dense frame encoding (demonstrated on a 30-min example); the downstream encoder and LLM are unmodified, so inference cost per token is unchanged. - Effective: Evidence is strong for temporal grounding and densely repeated motion tasks; for general long-video QA the gains are marginal (1.2–2.6 points) and non-monotonic, suggesting codec tokenization is not universally beneficial. - Scalable: Training goes up to 768 visible frames; the paper explicitly states that "denser or hour-scale inputs remain an extrapolation regime" and are not evaluated, so scalability to hour-length video is a claim, not a measured result. The work delivers trained model weights, code, and data as open-source artifacts. Strengths: - The codec-stream temporal grouping mechanism is technically well-specified (Equations 2–6), with explicit algorithmic choices (valley search, stratified allocation, same-frame attenuation) that are principled and individually motivated. - The per-skill ablation on VideoMME-v2 (Figure 8) honestly documents where codec hurts (future event prediction: −11.7, dyadic interaction: −5.6) rather than reporting only aggregate averages, providing a genuine coverage-vs.-resolution characterization. - Releasing the full training data stack (8M video captions, 4M spatial QA, plus data for all four stages) at this scale is a substantive open-source contribution that enables follow-on work. - The tracking results (+15.6 J&F average over Qwen3-VL-8B) are large and consistent across all four RVOS splits, and the point-track-to-SAM2 pipeline is a clean engineering decision that avoids adding a segmentation decoder. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that codec-stream inputs are less suitable for tasks requiring "dense trajectory continuity, such as future event prediction, dyadic interaction, motion properties, and motion trajectory estimation" where frame sampling preserves better local texture and frame-to-frame continuity. They also note that codec-stream and frame sampling advantages converge at high frame budgets. The conclusion acknowledges that hour-scale streaming is future work. The authors do not explicitly flag the self-referential nature of JumpScore. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - JumpScore was designed by the same team as the method it primarily evaluates, targeting exactly the regime (high-frequency repeated cycles with bit-cost spikes at boundaries) where codec-stream has a structural advantage. The +44.8-point lead over Qwen3-VL-8B is credible evidence of a real difference, but the magnitude cannot be interpreted without an independently constructed repetitive-motion benchmark. - Stage 4 co-introduces codec tokenization and the 4M-sample spatial corpus; there is no ablation holding spatial data constant while varying codec vs. uniform, so the spatial benchmark gains (+5.3 average, +35.0 CrossPoint) are not attributable to either factor independently. - The codec advantage on temporal grounding benchmarks (Charades-STA, ActivityNet) reverses or flattens at 32–64 frames (Table 5), yet the headline +9.7-point average is computed over all budget levels including low-budget regimes that may not reflect practical inference settings. - No evaluation beyond 8B scale; whether codec-stream tokenization provides complementary benefit to larger models or those already trained on dense frame sequences is unknown. - The paper is a preprint with no peer review; vendor-side benchmark numbers should be weighted accordingly. Bottom Line: A practitioner building temporal grounding or long-video understanding systems at the 8B scale gains a concrete, open-source, drop-in input-side mechanism with demonstrated benefits — but should treat the JumpScore margin as an upper-bound estimate on a lab-designed benchmark and verify codec-stream benefit on their own distribution before committing to the preprocessing overhead.
Recommended 2605.25979 PDF
#59 — Innovative (67)

Why Far Looks Up: Probing Spatial Representation in Vision-Language Models (18 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 29 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a representation-level analysis framework and the SpatialTunnel synthetic benchmark that together expose and quantify vertical-distance entanglement in VLMs — the systematic conflation of 2D vertical image position with 3D depth in model internal representations. - Why it's important: VLMs deployed in robotics and embodied AI that appear to pass spatial reasoning benchmarks may be relying on perspective-driven shortcuts rather than genuine 3D understanding, making them brittle under distribution shift in ways that aggregate accuracy scores cannot reveal. - How it does it: Minimal contrastive question pairs (swapping queried object order to flip ground-truth labels) are used to extract delta vectors from intermediate VLM layers, and two metrics — axis coherence (CohD) and VD-Entanglement Index (VD-EI) — quantify whether depth and vertical axes occupy separable directions in representation space. - The good: Distance coherence (CohD) computed on the synthetic SpatialTunnel correlates strongly with counter-heuristic accuracy on two independent real-image benchmarks (ρ = 0.759 on EmbSpatial-Bench, ρ = 0.804 on CV-Bench-3D, both p < 10⁻³), establishing that the representation metric captures genuine generalization signal. - The missing: The paper diagnoses the bias comprehensively but proposes no remedy, and the comparison model RoboRefer — which shows the strongest disentanglement — uses RGB-D training data, 20M+ samples, and a different training recipe, so the causal driver of its lower entanglement remains confounded. - Read it or not: Researchers building or evaluating spatially-capable VLMs for robotics or embodied AI should read this; practitioners hoping for a training fix will find the diagnosis useful but will need to look elsewhere for remediation.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (78) and Novelty (72) drive the score: the finding that vertical-distance entanglement is model-intrinsic and is sometimes *amplified* by spatial fine-tuning is genuinely counter-intuitive, and SpatialTunnel plus the probing pipeline are real artifacts (code released). The cross-domain CohD correlation (ρ = 0.759–0.804, p < 10⁻³) between a synthetic benchmark and two real-image benchmarks is strong diagnostic evidence. Impact (58) is capped because the paper diagnoses without proposing any fix, and Resilience (62) is pulled down by small counter-example samples (n = 65–129) with no significance tests on the primary results and a confounded RoboRefer reference. A well-executed diagnostic that resembles the rigorous positive-calibration set.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 78 Completeness 68 Resilience 62 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation VLMs are increasingly deployed in robotics and embodied AI where they must reason about 3D spatial structure from 2D monocular images. Existing spatial benchmarks measure output accuracy, but perspective projection in natural photographs creates a systematic statistical shortcut: objects farther from the camera on a ground plane appear higher in the image. Models trained on such data can learn to treat above ≈ far and below ≈ close, achieving high aggregate benchmark accuracy while failing systematically on counter-heuristic examples where the farther object appears lower. Prior probing work either detects isolated spatial primitives or examines local attention mechanisms; no prior work directly measures the global geometric organization — specifically whether depth and vertical axes are separable directions — within VLM embedding space. The result is that benchmark accuracy overestimates genuine 3D spatial understanding, and two models with identical benchmark scores may differ substantially in underlying representational structure. Key Idea The key insight is that perspective bias in natural photographs causes VLMs to conflate the vertical image axis with the depth axis in representation space, and this entanglement is detectable by examining the geometry of delta vectors — differences between hidden states for minimally contrastive question pairs — rather than model outputs. By swapping queried object order in a spatial VQA question (which flips the ground-truth label while holding visual context fixed), per-axis encoding consistency can be measured directly in embedding space, revealing whether depth is encoded as a stable, separable direction or parasitically rides the vertical axis. Methodology The framework has two components. First, the contrastive probing pipeline: given a spatial VQA question, construct a minimal question pair by swapping object order (e.g., "Is A to the left of B?" ↔ "Is B to the left of A?"), extract the final-token hidden state h_q at a fixed intermediate layer L* per model family, and define the delta vector δ = h_q2 − h_q1. Aggregating across many images yields per-axis delta vector sets. Two metrics are derived: axis coherence Coh_axis (mean pairwise cosine similarity among sign-corrected delta vectors; higher = more stable direction encoding), and VD-Entanglement Index (VD-EI; cosine similarity of perspective-aligned mean category vectors above↔far and below↔close minus perspective-opposing pairs; positive = entanglement). Layer selection follows a principled protocol prioritizing axis coherence plateau and VD-EI stability, with robustness verified across 1K random within-range layer samples (Spearman ρ = 0.928 for CohD ranking). Second, SpatialTunnel: a Blender-rendered tunnel-shaped environment where objects are parameterized by depth z and angular position θ on the tunnel cross-section. Holding z fixed while sweeping θ moves objects in the image plane without changing depth, decoupling the vertical image position cue. A 16×16 grid of angular configurations × 12 randomized scene instances × 4 question templates yields 12,288 question-image pairs. Responses are scored via logit-based probability p = σ(ℓ_Yes − ℓ_No) rather than discrete accuracy, enabling gradient comparisons between consistent and counter subsets. Models evaluated include Molmo-7B-O-0924, NVILA-Lite-2B, and Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct, each fine-tuned at four scales (80k, 400k, 800k, 2M samples) on a uniform mix of SAT, RoboSpatial, SPAR-7M, RefSpatial, and PRISM; plus RoboRefer-2B-SFT and Qwen3-VL-235B as reference models. Critical design decisions: the logit-based scoring is well-motivated and follows established practice; the tunnel geometry is analytically derived (Appendix A) and clean; the choice to evaluate the distance swap pair using randomly sampled distractors from multiple-choice format (rather than direct relational queries) is a pragmatic adaptation to EmbSpatial-Bench's annotation format, though it introduces mild noise. Key Results On existing benchmarks: consistent examples account for 80.9% of EmbSpatial-Bench depth questions and 60.5% of CV-Bench-3D depth questions; counter examples constitute only ~10% in each — a previously undocumented evaluation-set skew. Across all tested models and training scales, consistent accuracy exceeds counter accuracy: Qwen2.5-VL fine-tuned on 2M samples achieves 60.9% consistent vs. 24.0% counter on EmbSpatial-Bench (Δ = 36.9 pp); even Qwen3-VL-235B shows Δ = −7.3 pp on CV-Bench-3D. On SpatialTunnel: base Qwen2.5-VL-3B achieves v_cons = 0.776 vs. v_ctr = 0.360 (Δ = +0.416); scaling to 2M widens the gap to Δ = +0.295. RoboRefer-2B (>20M training samples, RGB-D supervision) achieves Δ = +0.046 and Qwen3-VL-235B achieves v = 0.908 with Δ = +0.068 — the only models to substantially reduce the gap. Representation analysis: CohD is the weakest axis across all models; Molmo-7B at 2M reaches CohD = 0.112 vs. CohV = 0.574; Qwen2.5-VL-3B CohD remains essentially flat across all training scales (0.043 → 0.052). Cross-domain: CohD computed on SpatialTunnel correlates with counter accuracy on EmbSpatial-Bench (ρ = 0.759) and CV-Bench-3D (ρ = 0.804), both p < 10⁻³. Notably surprising: for Qwen2.5-VL, more spatial training amplifies the consistent-counter gap, indicating that scaled fine-tuning on natural-image-derived spatial data deepens rather than resolves the bias. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD (probing framework). The empirical finding that vertical-distance entanglement is model-intrinsic, universal across architecture families, and sometimes intensified by spatial fine-tuning is genuinely non-obvious and challenges the naive expectation that more spatial training data resolves shortcut reliance. SpatialTunnel is a new benchmark artifact. The contrastive probing framework adapts established representation probing techniques (delta vectors, cosine similarity metrics) to the specific structure of spatial reasoning VQA — a methodologically clean application rather than a fundamentally new technique, but well-executed. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations are presented on the specific functional form of VD-EI, the ∆y < 5% threshold for ambiguous examples, or the data mix composition for fine-tuning. Counter-example sample sizes are small (n = 129 for EmbSpatial-Bench, n = 65 for CV-Bench-3D), and no significance tests are reported for the Table 2 accuracy gaps, leaving statistical reliability of smaller differences unclear. Wilson 95% confidence intervals are provided for BLINK results (Table 7) but not for the primary EmbSpatial-Bench or SpatialTunnel analyses. Multiple training seeds are not reported. The layer selection robustness check (Spearman ρ = 0.928 across 1K random layer samples) is a positive methodological addition. Code, SpatialTunnel benchmark, and probing pipeline are stated as publicly released on the project website. This is an arXiv preprint; results have not been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: All analysis runs on commercially available hardware; inference requires standard GPU access for model hidden-state extraction, and SpatialTunnel scenes are generated with Blender on consumer hardware. - Efficient: The probing pipeline requires only forward passes with no training; 12,288 SpatialTunnel queries per model is modest; fine-tuning experiments at 2M scale are within academic compute reach. - Effective: The diagnostic evidence is strong — CohD predicts robustness cross-domain (ρ = 0.759–0.804) — but the paper delivers a characterization tool rather than a fix; it does not demonstrate that optimizing CohD during training reduces real-world deployment failures. - Scalable: The probing framework is architecture-agnostic and was applied to a 235B-parameter MoE model (Qwen3-VL-235B); SpatialTunnel can generate arbitrary numbers of scenes parametrically. The paper delivers a synthetic benchmark (SpatialTunnel, Blender-rendered), a probing pipeline (code released), and empirical characterization of existing models. It stops at paper-plus-artifacts form with no trained model weights. Strengths - The consistent/counter analysis on real benchmarks quantifies benchmark-skew for the first time: showing 80.9% of EmbSpatial-Bench depth questions favor the perspective heuristic is a concrete finding with immediate implications for benchmark design. - SpatialTunnel's tunnel geometry analytically decouples vertical position from depth (rigorously derived in Appendix A) and the 16×16 angular grid enables cell-level heatmap diagnostics that reveal position-specific biases invisible to aggregate accuracy. - The cross-domain CohD correlation (ρ = 0.759/0.804) between a purely synthetic benchmark and two real-image benchmarks is the strongest evidence in the paper that the representation metric captures something beyond dataset-specific artifacts. - The counter-intuitive and practically important finding that spatial fine-tuning at intermediate scales sometimes increases entanglement (Qwen2.5-VL gap widens from 22.1 pp at base to 36.9 pp at 2M) directly challenges the prevailing assumption that more spatial data improves spatial reasoning quality uniformly. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly note that RoboRefer reflects a different training regime — including RGB-D inputs and substantially larger training scale — and treat it as an illustrative reference rather than attributing its gains to a single factor. Layer selection for Qwen3-VL-235B at 93% depth (versus 71–78% for smaller models) is acknowledged as architecturally specific and complicated by VD-EI oscillation in upper layers. The paper acknowledges it does not propose a mitigation strategy. These limitations are confronted directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Counter-example sample sizes (n = 129 / n = 65) are small and no significance tests appear in the main results; some of the smaller per-model accuracy gaps in Table 2 may not be statistically reliable. - The probing layer L* is selected by inspecting axis coherence across all layers of each model family before cross-model comparison; while the 1K random-sample robustness check is reassuring, the absence of a fully held-out layer protocol means the reported CohD values may represent the best available layer rather than an unbiased estimate. - The paper does not disentangle whether entanglement originates in the visual encoder, language decoder, or cross-modal fusion — a distinction that matters for designing targeted fixes. - No ablation tests which training data sources within the 5-dataset mix most drive entanglement reduction or amplification, leaving practitioners without actionable guidance on data curation. - The most striking results for proprietary models (GPT-5.2 reasoning mode achieving Δ = +0.058; Gemini-2.5-Pro achieving Δ = −0.028) and the size-bias variant are relegated to appendices, despite being substantively informative about the generality of the findings. Bottom Line This is a well-executed diagnostic paper with genuinely useful artifacts — the SpatialTunnel benchmark and probing pipeline — and a concrete, non-obvious empirical finding that practitioners designing spatially-capable VLMs should read; it does not, however, deliver a training-side fix, and the small counter-example evaluation sets call for significance testing that the paper omits.
Recommended 2605.30161 PDF
#60 — Innovative (67)

Self-Improving Language Models with Bidirectional Evolutionary Search (44 ▲ )

Guowei Xu, Zhenting Qi, Huangyuan Su, Weirui Ye, Himabindu Lakkaraju, Sham M. Kakade, Yilun Du · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 28 May 2026
