When backtracking makes an evicted block re-enter Path*, its KV state is rebuilt via a single prefill (no generation) before the next decoding step — yielding the same conditioning as full retention on the active path. Key Results All experiments run on a single RTX 4090 (24 GB, FP16) with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct. Development subsets of 200 instances per benchmark are used for rapid iteration; full-split evaluations are promised but not in this paper. Config-S (budget sweep, B=3, D=6, Nexpand=64): At ρ=0.50 on GSM8K, ArborKV (Llama) reaches 77.8% vs. ThinKV 75.3%, StreamingLLM 76.1%, FullKV 80.1% at roughly half the peak memory (11.0 vs. 21.8 GiB). At ρ=0.25 the gap widens: ArborKV 70.2% vs. ThinKV 67.1%, H2O 65.6%. The advantage is largest on the backtracking-heavy Game of 24: Qwen at ρ=0.25, ArborKV 52.6% vs. ThinKV 49.3%, H2O 42.4%, against FullKV 64.2%. Config-L (fixed-memory scaling, B=5, D=8, Nexpand=256): FullKV OOMs at Nexpand=256; ArborKV achieves 78.4% accuracy at 5.6 GiB and 38.2s latency on MATH500 with Llama-3.1-8B — matching FullKV's 79.8% at Nexpand=64 (5.9 GiB). H2O reaches 69.0% and StreamingLLM 67.1% at the same expansion. Ablations (ρ=0.25): Removing the distance term Δᵢ (MSVE-only, no TAE topology) causes the largest single drop: 70.2% → 59.2% on GSM8K while average rehydrations rise from 2.7 to 4.8 — the strongest evidence for the paper's central claim. Removing individual MSVE signals each causes 1.4–2.7% drops; removing all MSVE (TAE-only) causes 4.7%. Removing lazy rehydration while keeping MSVE+TAE: ArborKV drops to 68.6% (vs. full 70.2%), still above ThinKV 67.1%, confirming both allocation and recovery contribute. Intra-block heavy-hitter retention adds 2.3% over sink+tail at the same memory cap. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW FORMULATION. Formulating KV-cache management for tree-structured inference as a topological budget allocation problem, and solving it via a distance-decayed KKT allocation with reversible eviction, is genuinely novel. The individual components — attention heavy hitters, entropy-based uncertainty, convex allocation — are drawn from prior work; their synthesis around tree search topology, and the lazy rehydration mechanism enabling non-monotonic eviction, constitute the original contribution. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and well-structured, isolating MSVE signals, TAE distance term, sibling coordination, lazy rehydration, and intra-block selection independently — this is a credibility positive. However, no error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported; all accuracy figures appear to be single-run on 200-instance development subsets, making 1-3% differences between methods difficult to interpret statistically. The MSVE calibration protocol (leave-one-out block masking on 100 trajectories) is methodologically transparent. The hyperparameter space (α, γ, λd, λΔ, η, Kmin, Ltail) is not analyzed for sensitivity. No code repository or model release is mentioned in the paper. The paper is peer-reviewed (ICML 2026), which provides higher baseline confidence than a preprint, but the arXiv version analyzed here is v1. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: Runs on a single RTX 4090 (24 GB) with 7B-8B FP16 models using only commodity hardware; no specialized accelerators required. - Efficient: Policy control adds <1.2% wall-clock overhead; ArborKV total inference time is comparable to or below baseline KV-compressed methods (e.g., 21.5s vs. 23.6s for ThinKV at ρ=0.25 on GSM8K with Llama) because a smaller KV cache speeds up attention; rehydration averages 2–5 events per episode at ρ=0.25. - Effective: Within the 7B-8B range, ArborKV demonstrably preserves near-FullKV accuracy at ρ=0.50 and enables search configurations (Nexpand=256) that are infeasible under FullKV — evidence is limited to four math reasoning benchmarks on two models. - Scalable: Tested only at 7B-8B scale on a single GPU with batch size 1; behavior at 70B+, multi-GPU tree search, or larger batch sizes is untested; rehydration frequency at deeper/wider trees (B=10, D=12) is not characterized. The work delivers algorithmic pseudocode (Algorithms 1–2) and an inference-time framework, but no released software library is mentioned. Strengths - The tree-distance decay term is the paper's most defensible contribution: the ablation shows removing it causes the largest single accuracy drop (11 points) with the highest rehydration cost, providing direct causal evidence for the central hypothesis that topological proximity governs short-term KV reuse in tree search. - Lazy rehydration cleanly solves the irreversibility problem of all prior sequence-centric methods — when a thought block is evicted but later needs to re-enter the active path, a single prefill suffices, with average overhead of 2–5 events per episode. - The KKT derivation of the TAE formula gives a principled interpretation to what would otherwise be an ad hoc heuristic, and shows the multiplicative form arises from diminishing-return marginal utility of additional retained tokens. - The fixed-memory scaling result (Config-L) is the most compelling empirical finding: ArborKV is the only method that completes the Nexpand=256 configuration, demonstrating a categorical capability difference rather than a marginal accuracy improvement. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not provide an explicit limitations section. The experimental section implicitly acknowledges that development subsets of 200 instances are used and that full-split evaluations will appear in "final release materials" — an acknowledgment that current results are not on full benchmarks. The Impact Statement briefly notes risks of misuse and over-reliance on efficient deliberative search but does not engage with technical limitations. The authors note that GiB peak memory may vary slightly across methods due to allocator effects and transient rehydration buffers. No other limitations are confronted directly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-run, subset evaluation: All accuracy results are single runs on 200-instance subsets with stochastic decoding (temperature=0.7); differences of 1–3% between methods are within plausible run-to-run variance and cannot be taken as statistically significant without error bars or multiple seeds. - No CPU-offload baseline: The natural competitor to eviction-with-rehydration is offloading inactive subtree KVs to CPU and prefetching on backtracking — this requires no semantic scoring and is fully reversible; the paper provides no comparison or justification for why eviction is preferred over offloading. - Hyperparameter sensitivity absent: Five TAE parameters (α, γ, λd, λΔ, η) plus two stability floors (Kmin, Ltail) are tuned per configuration; their sensitivity surfaces and default-setting rationale are not reported, making it unclear how much tuning effort the reported results required. - Batch size 1 only: Production ToT serving would multiplex many users on one GPU; the interaction between per-user tree structures and a shared KV pool is not addressed. - MSVE calibration scope: The transferability result (Table 8) is limited to within-mathematical-reasoning tasks; whether MSVE signals calibrated on math transfer to code generation, logical puzzles, or agentic task trees is untested. - Rehydration at larger trees: Average rehydration counts of 2–5 per episode are reported at the tested configurations, but the growth rate of rehydration frequency with branching factor and depth is uncharacterized — this is the most important missing analysis for practitioners considering deeper tree search. Bottom Line A practitioner building Tree-of-Thoughts inference systems under GPU memory constraints will find the topology-aware allocation principle directly actionable and the fixed-memory scaling experiment genuinely compelling — but should replicate the key accuracy comparisons with multiple seeds before trusting the precise margins, and should benchmark against a CPU-offload baseline before committing to this approach.

#66 — Innovative (66)

Chronicle: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Joint Language and Time Series Understanding

Paul Quinlan, Jeremy Levasseur, Qingguo Li, Xiaodan Zhu · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Chronicle, a 324M-parameter decoder-only transformer trained from scratch jointly on natural language and time series, with both modalities sharing the same transformer backbone and only modality-specific input/output interfaces separated. - Why it's important: All prior multimodal text+time-series models adapt pretrained LLMs post-hoc, forcing temporal data to fit representations never shaped by it; Chronicle tests whether joint training from scratch avoids this fundamental mismatch and whether such models actually outperform unimodal specialists. - How it does it: Text tokens and time series patches occupy positions in a single autoregressive sequence; pretraining uses unimodal-only micro-batches (92% text, 8% time series) so cross-modal capability emerges from shared parameter updates without explicit cross-modal losses, followed by a short alignment stage with 5% interleaved data. - The good: On 24 UCR/UEA datasets with a frozen linear probe, Stage 1 achieves 0.736 accuracy surpassing Moirai-2 (0.714) and all per-dataset supervised baselines except FEDformer; on Time-MMD multimodal forecasting, Stage 2 (LP) achieves NMAE 0.514 vs. the best MM-TSFlib baseline at 0.621. - The missing: The paper never ablates the most critical counterfactual — a Chronicle-architecture model trained on 100% time series data at the same compute budget — so the cost or benefit of joint training for TS quality cannot be isolated from architecture and objective differences. - Read it or not: Researchers building multimodal time series systems should read it for its principled evaluation protocol and the no-catastrophic-interference finding; those seeking production-quality zero-shot forecasting should note it trails dedicated specialists like TimesFM-2.5 by a wide margin on GIFT-Eval.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (76) is the driver — a decoder-only transformer trained on text and time series jointly from scratch is a genuinely new paradigm (NEW SYSTEM + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING), not a post-hoc LLM adaptation. Non-Obviousness (72) is high: the no-catastrophic-interference finding at 8% TS compute is surprising and refutes a standing assumption. Rigor (60) is held back by single-run GIFT-Eval/NLU numbers, placeholder code URLs, and preprint status. Completeness (65) and Resilience (62) are solid thanks to the unusually candid limitations and the field's most rigorous evaluation protocol, but the missing 100%-TS counterfactual ablation prevents isolating the cost of joint training. Impact (55) reflects compact checkpoints not yet matching dedicated specialists. Weighted: 0.20*76+0.25*60+0.15*72+0.15*65+0.15*62+0.10*55 = 66.
empirical Novelty 76 Rigor 60 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Real-world time series almost always arrive with accompanying text — metadata, domain reports, anomaly descriptions, news — yet time series foundation models process numerical sequences in complete isolation. A growing body of work attempts to bridge this by attaching language models to time series, but every existing approach begins with a pretrained LLM and adapts it post-hoc. Because those backbones were shaped entirely by text before ever seeing temporal data, the time series modality must contort itself into a representational space not designed for it. Compounding the problem, these multimodal models are evaluated almost exclusively against other multimodal or task-specific baselines, never against strong unimodal foundation models in each domain, leaving open whether joint modeling adds value at all. Evidence from Tan et al. (2024) — who found LLM pretraining does not transfer to forecasting — and Merrill et al. (2024) — who found LLMs struggle with text-encoded series — motivates a fundamentally different approach. Key Idea: The core insight is that a decoder-only transformer trained from scratch with modality-specific input/output interfaces but a fully shared backbone, combined with a text-heavy unimodal batch curriculum (92/8), can develop representations suitable for both modalities simultaneously without catastrophic interference. Cross-modal capability emerges passively from shared parameter updates across modalities rather than from architecturally engineered cross-attention or early explicit alignment supervision. Methodology: The architecture is a 16-layer decoder-only transformer (d=1024, 8 GQA query heads, 4 KV heads, SwiGLU, pre-norm RMSNorm, RoPE, 324M parameters total). Time series input: each series is arsinh-normalized (statistics computed over visible values only), segmented into non-overlapping patches of length P=32, and each patch represented by a 4P-dimensional feature vector [time ramp r; normalized values v; binary validity mask m; channel ramp c], projected to d via a single bias-free linear layer + RMSNorm. Text input uses a 131,072-entry BPE vocabulary with tied embedding/LM-head. Output heads are modality-specific: a tied LM head with logit soft-capping (α=15) for text (cross-entropy loss LCE); a 21-quantile head over τ∈[0.05, 0.95] for time series (masked quantile loss LQL). Combined objective: L = LCE + 2.5·LQL (asymmetric weighting to balance gradient magnitudes). Stage 1 (47,683 steps, seq len 2048): 92% text-only batches (FineWeb-Edu + Dolmino-mix), 8% time-series-only batches (GiftEvalPretrain + KernelSynth augmentation); ~150B total tokens. Stage 2 (extended seq len 4096): injects 5% interleaved alignment data from ChatTS synthetic pairs and Merrill et al. descriptions into the TS stream. Optimizer: Muon for transformer block weight matrices; AdamW for embeddings, heads, and norms. Critical design decisions: (1) unimodal batches during bulk pretraining rather than interleaved from the start — justified as preventing modality interference early; (2) 92/8 text-heavy mix — acknowledged as a compute constraint rather than an optimal ratio; (3) channel ramp encoding for multivariate identity — ablated in Appendix E.2 and shown to improve UEA classification by +0.039 accuracy on average. Key Results: NLU (19 tasks, DCLM suite, zero/few-shot): Chronicle Stage 2 achieves 0.406 average, matching Gemma-3-270M-PT (0.406) at comparable scale despite seeing only ~138B text tokens vs. Gemma-3's 6T. On ARC-Easy, Stage 2 scores 0.651 vs. LLaMA-3.2-1B's 0.678 (a model 4× larger trained exclusively on text). GIFT-Eval zero-shot forecasting (97 tasks): Stage 1 = 0.978 MASE / 0.690 CRPS, below all dedicated TSFMs (best: TimesFM-2.5 at 0.705 MASE, PatchTST-FM-r1 at 0.483 CRPS) but above N-BEATS (0.938 MASE), DLinear (1.061), and Auto-ARIMA (0.912 CRPS). Stage 2 regresses to 1.053 MASE. UCR/UEA classification (24 datasets, frozen linear probe, 5 seeds): Stage 1 = 0.736 accuracy / 0.712 F1 vs. Moirai-2 at 0.714/0.692, TimesFM at 0.611/0.563, Chronos-2 at 0.376/0.230. Exceeds all per-dataset supervised DL baselines except FEDformer (0.723). Time-MMD multimodal forecasting (9 domains, LP head on frozen backbone): Stage 2 (LP) = 0.514 NMAE / avg rank 2.56, outperforming BERT+TNet (0.621 NMAE) and GPT2+Moi2 (0.588 NMAE). TimeCAP multimodal classification: Stage 2 LoRA (r=64) = 0.613 F1; Stage 1 LoRA (r=64) = 0.763 AUC — both best across all baselines over 3 seeds. Baselines on GIFT-Eval and UCR are drawn from the current public leaderboard and are strong and up-to-date. Evaluation scale: standard benchmarks, 324M parameters; no stress-scale experiments at larger model sizes. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The from-scratch joint training paradigm is genuinely novel — no prior work has trained a single model on both modalities from random initialization. The cross-domain evaluation protocol against dedicated specialists in each modality is also new to the multimodal TS literature. The architecture itself (decoder-only transformer with patch embedding, quantile output head) is a straightforward combination of PatchTST-FM and GPT-2 style components; the novelty is in the training strategy and evaluation discipline, not architectural invention. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present but incomplete: the Stage 1 vs. Stage 2 trade-off is cleanly documented across all benchmarks; the TS-token repetition sweep (Figure 3) is thorough; the channel-aware vs. mean-channel pooling ablation (Figure 4) covers all 10 UEA datasets. However, the critical missing ablation is a Chronicle-architecture model trained on 100% time series — without it, the cost of joint training for TS quality cannot be isolated from architecture and objective differences relative to PatchTST-FM or TimesFM. Error bars are reported for TimeCAP (3 seeds) and UCR/UEA (5 seeds) but not for GIFT-Eval or NLU (single runs). Code and checkpoints are stated as publicly available on Hugging Face and GitHub, but URLs in the preprint are placeholder strings ([HUGGINGFACE_LINK], [GITHUB_LINK]) — not accessible at paper submission time. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.20268v1); results have not undergone peer review. Training data (FineWeb-Edu, Dolmino-mix) and evaluation suite (DCLM) partially overlap in distribution, which is standard practice but may inflate NLU comparisons against models trained on less curated text. Deployability (EMPIRICAL): - Practical: Stage 1+2 training runs on 2×H100 80GB GPUs in roughly one week; inference is standard autoregressive decoding runnable on a single GPU. - Efficient: 324M parameters is compact relative to the 7–14B baselines it surpasses on multimodal tasks; however, autoregressive next-patch forecasting is slower than one-shot bidirectional TSFMs for long-horizon prediction. - Effective: Evidence is strong for frozen-embedding classification and head-trained multimodal forecasting; zero-shot forecasting is substantially weaker than dedicated specialists, a limitation the paper explicitly measures and explains. - Scalable: No scaling experiments beyond 324M parameters; the paper asserts the forecasting gap is addressable with more compute or a curriculum strategy but provides no evidence at larger scale. The paper delivers stated model checkpoints (URLs pending release) and evaluation code as software artifacts. Strengths: - The finding that 8% time series compute in a 324M joint model does not cause catastrophic interference — quantified against scale-matched LLMs across 19 NLU tasks — is the first clean empirical proof of this claim and directly refutes the implicit assumption underlying all prior post-hoc LLM adaptation work. - The evaluation protocol is the most rigorous in the multimodal TS literature: evaluating against the public GIFT-Eval leaderboard, dedicated TSFM frozen probes, and scale-matched LLMs simultaneously closes a longstanding gap that prior work had not addressed. - The frozen-embedding UCR/UEA classification result (0.736 accuracy, beating Moirai-2 at 0.714 and per-dataset supervised DL baselines with a single backbone) is a substantive empirical contribution, not just a tie. - The TS-token repetition trick (Section 5.4) is a simple, architecture-agnostic solution to the modality-imbalance problem in shared autoregressive backbones, with a clean ablation showing ~3 accuracy and ~4 AUC points of headroom — practically useful for downstream classification tasks with short time series. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly confront the forecasting gap to dedicated TSFMs, attributing it to two principled design choices: the 8% compute allocation and the causal next-patch objective vs. contiguous patch masking used by PatchTST-FM. They explicitly suggest curriculum strategies (TS-only pretraining followed by joint continued pretraining) and hybrid attention objectives as remedies, and note that Stage 2 covers only 5% of TS tokens with cross-modal supervision — a larger alignment stage is called out as the most promising direction. They also acknowledge the scope restriction to frozen representation learning vs. conversational agents. Overall the limitations section is unusually candid. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most consequential missing ablation is a Chronicle-architecture model trained on 100% time series at the same compute budget as Stage 1 — without this, it is impossible to determine whether the gap to PatchTST-FM is due to (a) joint training hurting TS quality, (b) the causal next-patch objective, (c) the lower TS compute, or some combination; the paper attributes it to (b) and (c) but cannot isolate them. The Time-MMD LP comparison is partially apples-to-oranges: Chronicle's LP head is trained with joint text+TS input (backbone sees both), while FM Fusion baselines freeze both encoders and only train the head — Chronicle thus benefits from cross-modal conditioning during fine-tuning in a way the baselines do not. The NLU comparison favors Chronicle on data quality: Chronicle uses FineWeb-Edu (highly filtered) while GPT-2's ~10B tokens are much lower quality Common Crawl — the 43× fewer token count comparison is accurate but potentially misleading about the actual information advantage. No per-domain analysis on Time-MMD breaks down which domains benefit from text conditioning vs. not, which would be critical for understanding when joint training actually helps rather than just matching well-tuned unimodal baselines. The paper does not evaluate retrieval quality of the time series embeddings or robustness to domain shift. Bottom Line: Chronicle establishes the right evaluation methodology for multimodal time series models and produces the first credible evidence that joint from-scratch training is feasible at compact scale without catastrophic interference; practitioners building systems where time series and text co-occur should read it, but the large zero-shot forecasting gap to dedicated specialists means it is not yet a viable replacement for production forecasting pipelines.
Citations 2605.20268 PDF
#67 — Innovative (66)

Combining On-Policy Optimization and Distillation for Long-Context Reasoning in Large Language Models

Miguel Moura Ramos, Duarte M. Alves, André F. T. Martins · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces dGRPO (Distilled Group Relative Policy Optimization), a post-training method that augments on-policy GRPO with dense token-level teacher guidance, plus LONGBLOCKS, a 193K-sample multilingual synthetic long-context dataset, forming a two-stage recipe for long-context alignment of LLMs. - Why it's important: Existing post-training methods for long-context fail in complementary ways — off-policy methods suffer from exposure bias over long horizons while on-policy RL with sparse rewards is unstable — leaving practitioners without a reliable recipe for long-context alignment. - How it does it: dGRPO jointly optimizes sparse outcome-based GRPO rewards and a reverse-KL on-policy distillation term toward a stronger teacher (Qwen3-32B) along the model's own sampled trajectories, providing dense token-level regularization that stabilizes reward optimization. - The good: RULER performance at 128K tokens improves from 11.0 to 44.5 (a 4× gain) while short-context benchmarks are preserved or slightly improved, and the final 1.7B model is competitive with 3B–4B baselines including a YaRN-based context-extension variant. - The missing: The entire empirical study is conducted at 1.7B parameters; no evidence is provided that the recipe scales to 7B, 14B, or 70B models, where reward-hacking dynamics and teacher-capacity gaps may differ substantially. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on post-training alignment for long-context LLMs — the recipe is concrete, reproducible, and the ablations are unusually thorough; skip it if you need scale evidence beyond 1.7B.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Rigor (70) is the lead contributor — unusually thorough ablations (β sweep, teacher source, init strategy, data ratio), 5 runs with reported std — but is capped by in-family circularity (Qwen3-generated LONGBLOCKS verified by Qwen3, Qwen3-family LM judge) and preprint status. Completeness (70) is helped by the clean stage-isolation analysis and dramatic held-out RULER-128K jump (11.0→44.5), yet pulled down by the absent limitations section and unacknowledged single-scale (1.7B-only) generalizability. Novelty (65) is moderate: a principled but linear combination of established GRPO + on-policy distillation. Composite 0.20*65+0.25*70+0.15*62+0.15*70+0.15*62+0.10*60 ≈ 66.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 70 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 70 Resilience 62 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Real-world applications requiring LLMs to reason over code repositories, multi-document collections, and long conversations expose a fundamental limitation: post-training methods that work well at short context fail to generalize. Off-policy SFT and knowledge distillation suffer from exposure bias — they optimize only on expert-generated prefixes, so the model never learns to recover from its own long-horizon errors. On-policy GRPO addresses exposure bias but relies on sparse trajectory-level rewards that produce noisy gradient estimates, unstable training, and degradation of short-context skills when applied at long context. On-policy distillation (OPD) provides dense token-level guidance on model-generated rollouts but cannot directly optimize arbitrary reward signals. The gap is a principled method that combines on-policy reward optimization with dense supervision, applied to a sufficiently diverse long-context training set. Key Idea The core insight is that GRPO's sparse reward signal and OPD's dense teacher signal are complementary rather than competing: by adding a reverse-KL divergence term between the student policy and a stronger teacher model (evaluated along the student's own sampled trajectories) directly to the GRPO objective, the teacher provides per-token learning signal that stabilizes credit assignment over long sequences while the GRPO term preserves the ability to optimize non-differentiable task rewards. The teacher's KL term acts as a learned regularizer tied to the student's current distribution, avoiding the train-inference mismatch of standard KD. Methodology The training pipeline has two stages. Stage 1 is an off-policy SFT warm-up on a 10/90 mix of short-context (Nemotron-Post-Training-Dataset-v2) and long-context (LONGBLOCKS) data, trained for 2 epochs with AdamW (lr=1×10⁻⁵, batch size 4M tokens, bfloat16, sequence packing). This stage stabilizes rollout coherence before on-policy RL. Stage 2 applies dGRPO for 1 epoch on the same data mixture. The dGRPO objective is: J_dGRPO(θ) = E[GRPO_objective − β·D_KL(π_θ(·|p,o_{<t}) ∥ π_teacher(·|p,o_{<t}))], where β=0.5 (swept over {0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.4, 0.5}), and the KL is computed over student-sampled completions. Three critical design decisions: (1) setting β_ref=0 (eliminating the KL term to the SFT reference policy, following prior reasoning-model work), well-justified by evidence that reasoning models can safely diverge from their initialization; (2) using Qwen3-32B as the external teacher rather than self-distillation, justified empirically by Figure 5 showing self-distillation is consistently inferior; (3) the 10/90 short/long token ratio, justified by ablation in Appendix C.4 showing long-context performance is sensitive to this ratio while short-context is not. Rewards are verifiable (SymPy for math, unit tests for coding) or LM-judge-based (Qwen3-4B as binary judge for chat and long-context tasks). LONGBLOCKS is constructed by prompting Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking to generate QA pairs from raw documents, then filtering with an LLM-as-judge step that rejects locally answerable, weakly grounded, or malformed examples. The base model is Qwen3-1.7B extended to 128K context by fixing the RoPE base frequency to 1×10⁶ without modifying positional parameterization. Key Results All evaluations use lm-eval-harness with temperature 0.6, top-p=0.95, top-k=20, 32,768 max generation tokens, averaged over 5 runs. On RULER (128K context), average score improves from 64.5 to 74.8 (Qwen3-1.7B base → ours); the 128K-specific score improves from 11.0 to 44.5. On LongBench the model improves from 34.4 to 39.6; on ∞Bench from 6.6 to 22.7. Short-context benchmarks show preservation or slight gains: GSM8K 89.8→89.8, MATH-500 60.4→65.3, HumanEval 67.7→69.5. The final model is competitive with Llama-3.2-3B, Gemma-3-4B, and Qwen3-1.7B+YaRN while using a 1.7B parameter base. The dGRPO ablation (Figure 4) shows GRPO alone produces noisy reward curves and lower long-context accuracy, OPD produces stable but smaller gains, and dGRPO achieves the highest reward and best long-context performance. The β=0 (GRPO-only) case is strictly dominated across all β>0 settings tested. One notable finding: KD cold-start provides only marginal gains over SFT (e.g., RULER 28.4 vs 28.9) at higher computational cost, making SFT the preferred initialization. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD (dGRPO) + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (LONGBLOCKS). The combination of on-policy reward optimization with on-policy distillation in a single objective is genuinely novel for long-context post-training; individual components (GRPO from Shao et al. 2024, OPD from Agarwal et al. 2024) are established, and the integration follows a straightforward linear combination, but its application to the long-context stability problem and the systematic recipe are new contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are thorough and directly address the main design choices: β sweep, teacher source (external vs. self), SFT vs. KD cold start, and short/long data ratio. Each benchmark is run 5 times and standard deviations are reported in Appendix C.6; deviations are broadly comparable between the base model and the proposed method, confirming the training procedure does not inflate variance. LONGBLOCKS data generation prompts are fully reproduced in Appendix A. The LM-judge prompt for reward computation is shown in Appendix B. Methodological concern: the LM-judge reward (Qwen3-4B) is the same model family as the base model (Qwen3), and LONGBLOCKS is generated by Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking and verified by Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Thinking — creating potential in-family circular quality validation. Code and trained models are stated to be available on Hugging Face (no explicit URL provided in the paper text, but footnote 1 indicates Hugging Face). This is a preprint under review. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The full pipeline runs on consumer-accessible GPU hardware; the base model is 1.7B parameters and the teacher (Qwen3-32B) is available commercially, though RL training at 128K context requires significant GPU memory not quantified in the paper. - Efficient: dGRPO adds a teacher forward pass per training step versus plain GRPO; the relative compute overhead is not reported, which is a missing practical detail. - Effective: The evidence shows genuine long-context capability gains (RULER 128K: 11.0→44.5) on held-out benchmarks with distinct task formats, not just the training distribution; the LM-judge reward for open-ended tasks introduces some Goodhart risk that is not analyzed. - Scalable: The paper provides no evidence beyond the 1.7B scale; the claim that the recipe generalizes to larger models is asserted but untested. The paper delivers a trained model and dataset (Hugging Face), not a general-purpose library. Strengths - The dGRPO objective is well-motivated by a precise diagnosis of what GRPO and OPD each lack, and the combination is principled rather than ad hoc. - The RULER 128K result (11.0→44.5) is a dramatic capability jump on a held-out diagnostic benchmark specifically designed to test long-context retention and reasoning. - Ablations are unusually complete for a preprint: β sensitivity, teacher quality, initialization strategy, and data mix ratio are each independently evaluated with appropriate controls. - The short/long-context frontier visualization (Figure 3) cleanly isolates the contribution of each training stage and provides a compelling comparison against external baselines. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not include an explicit limitations section, but they acknowledge in the results that very long-context tasks (>100K tokens) remain difficult, particularly for a 1.7B model (∞Bench scores remain low despite improvement). They note the β hyperparameter is task-dependent, citing prior work. They do not acknowledge scale generalizability as a limitation, which a reviewer would flag. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The entire study is conducted at 1.7B parameters; there is no evidence the recipe transfers to 7B+ models where sparse-reward dynamics and teacher-capacity gaps may behave differently. - The LM-judge reward for long-context tasks (Qwen3-4B evaluating Qwen3-1.7B outputs) is an in-family evaluation; reward hacking or systematic bias cannot be ruled out without cross-family judge evaluation. - LONGBLOCKS is generated and quality-verified exclusively by Qwen3-family models, creating a potential distribution-narrow training set that may inflate Qwen3-family model evaluations. - Retrieve.KV on ∞Bench scores 0.0 for both the baseline and the proposed model, suggesting a systematic failure mode not discussed. - Compute cost of dGRPO relative to GRPO (teacher forward pass overhead) is not reported, which is relevant to practitioners deciding between methods. - No analysis of catastrophic forgetting dynamics during RL at long context beyond the aggregate short-context accuracy metric. Bottom Line Practitioners doing long-context post-training on sub-3B models will find a concrete, well-ablated recipe with substantial empirical support; the single-scale limitation (1.7B only) means the recipe should be validated before applying it to larger models.
Recommended 2605.12227 PDF
#68 — Innovative (66)

Agent Explorative Policy Optimization for Multimodal Agentic Reasoning (71 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 28 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces AXPO (Agent eXplorative Policy Optimization), an RL training algorithm that recovers learning signal on tool calls in agentic VLM training by anchoring the thinking prefix of failed tool-using rollouts and resampling only the tool call and its continuation. - Why it's important: Standard GRPO-based RL leaves tool-call tokens with zero or negative gradient on ~40% of questions where tools are attempted, precisely the questions where tool use would matter most, blocking the VLM from learning to invoke tools reliably. - How it does it: For all-wrong tool-using subgroups, AXPO fixes the thinking prefix up to the `<tool_call>` boundary, draws K=4 new continuations conditioned on that prefix prioritized by token-level uncertainty, and decomposes the advantage so prefix tokens receive a binary recovery reward while continuation tokens get a per-prefix GRPO advantage, avoiding gradient conflicts. - The good: At 8B parameters, SFT+AXPO achieves 75.8 Pass@4 versus 75.1 for the inference-only 32B base, using 4× fewer parameters, and +25% extra resampling budget outperforms +100% extra GRPO rollout budget, directly confirming the gain comes from where compute is spent rather than how much. - The missing: Trainable experiments are capped at 8B; whether the Thinking-Acting Gap persists and AXPO recovers it at 32B+ trainable scale—where base models approach the tool-use ceiling—is explicitly left open and untested. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone building agentic RL training pipelines for VLMs; the diagnostic framing of the Thinking-Acting Gap is empirically precise and the fix is practically deployable with modest compute overhead.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (72) and Novelty (70) lead — the precisely measured Thinking-Acting Gap diagnosis plus tool-call-boundary resampling is a targeted, mechanistically-motivated fix, and the '+25% resampling beats +100% rollout' result is unusually clean structural evidence. Rigor (65) is solid (all 5 components ablated, eval variance reported, training dynamics measured) but constrained by single model family, unreleased code, and a soft VisualProbe circularity. Impact (48) is the main drag — no artifact released and the headline '8B beats 32B' holds only against an untrained 32B baseline. Composite ≈ 66.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 68 Resilience 64 Impact 48
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Vision-language models with extended reasoning perform well on self-contained math and science benchmarks but fail on tasks requiring external tools (web search, code execution, image inspection). When standard group-based RL (GRPO) is applied to agentic VLMs that interleave thinking and tool calls, tool use remains severely under-trained. The root cause is a structural asymmetry: thinking is the model's default mode under full token control, while tool use is an optional, high-variance boundary that triggers external execution the model cannot predict. As a consequence, tool-using rollouts represent only ~20–35% of the rollout group across training steps, and when tool use is attempted, the entire tool-using subgroup fails together on ~40% of questions (vs. ~25% for no-tool subgroups). Under group-normalized advantage, this yields zero advantage in all-wrong groups and negative advantage when no-tool rollouts succeed, suppressing the gradient signal on tool-call tokens on exactly the questions where tool use is needed. Key Idea The core insight is that when a tool-using trajectory fails, the thinking prefix is often sound—the failure is in the specific tool call generated from it. Because the tool call is a real divergence point (16 resamples from a fixed prefix produce 2.9–3.4 distinct semantic clusters on average, not a single collapsed class), resampling at the tool-call boundary—rather than re-deriving the full trajectory from scratch—concentrates exploration budget where the Thinking-Acting Gap manifests. Anchoring the prefix and varying only the tool call and its continuation guarantees every resampled trajectory is tool-using by construction, eliminating the (1−q) waste factor inherent in raw rollout sampling. Methodology AXPO adds three design choices on top of the standard SFT→GRPO pipeline, with a branching budget capped at r=0.25 of the standard rollout budget: (1) Trigger and selection: Resampling is activated only on groups where the tool-using subgroup is non-empty and entirely wrong. Within those groups, candidate prefixes are ranked by the mean policy probability over tool-call tokens (confidence as a proxy for entropy; justified post-hoc by Pearson ρ=0.843 with exact entropy). The lowest-confidence prefixes are prioritized breadth-first across triggered questions. (2) Tool-call resampling (Proposition 1): From the selected prefix t1^src (which already includes the opening `<tool_call>` tag), K=4 continuations are drawn from π_θ(·|x, t1^src), each tool call is executed, and trajectories are rolled forward to completion. Every resampled trajectory is tool-using by construction, so the per-branch success probability p(t1^src) must only exceed q·p^tool (strictly below the average per-tool-using-rollout success p^tool) for resampling to dominate raw sampling—a condition provably satisfied by all prefixes at or above the conditional mean success rate. (3) Advantage decomposition: Continuation tokens of the K resampled trajectories receive a per-prefix GRPO advantage computed only over their K outcomes. The source prefix tokens receive a binary recovery reward r^prefix = 1[any resample succeeds], inserted into the source group's GRPO normalization, applied only to prefix tokens while the source continuation is masked. This avoids the gradient conflict of propagating contradictory mixed-sign continuation advantages back to the shared prefix. The full AXPO objective is the standard GRPO loss for non-triggered groups plus, per selected prefix, the PPO-clip surrogate on prefix tokens (with recovery reward) plus the sum of K continuation clip losses. Training uses Qwen3-VL-Thinking at 2B/4B/8B; SFT uses 64K trajectories generated by the 32B teacher; RL uses ~37K filtered problems. Asymmetric DAPO clipping (ε_low=0.2, ε_high=0.4), KL coefficient β=1×10⁻³, N=8 rollouts per question, 3 turns maximum, 8× A100 80GB GPUs. Key Results SFT+AXPO vs. SFT+GRPO on 9 multimodal benchmarks across three scales (averaged): - Pass@1 gains: +1.1 pp (2B), +1.4 pp (4B), +1.8 pp (8B) - Pass@4 gains: +2.8 pp (2B), +2.3 pp (4B), +1.8 pp (8B) - At 8B: 62.3% Pass@1 (vs. 60.5% SFT+GRPO, vs. 62.9% inference-only 32B Base) and 75.8% Pass@4 (vs. 74.0% SFT+GRPO, surpassing 75.1% inference-only 32B Base) - Gains concentrate in Perception (+2.9 pp at 8B Pass@1) where image-tool use is load-bearing; Reasoning gains are smallest (+0.6 pp), consistent with reasoning bottlenecks residing in thinking quality, not tool calls - Training dynamics: tool-use rate climbs +28 pp under AXPO (vs. flat ~30% under GRPO); all-wrong rate sits ~17 pp below GRPO at every step; ~12% of all-wrong tool-using subgroups per step are recovered by resampling - Compute efficiency: +25% resampling budget (AXPO) beats +100% rollout budget (GRPO) by +1.1 pp, confirming the gain is structural rather than budgetary - Ablation (Table 2, 8B): Removing prefix fixing drops to 52.1 avg Pass@1 (vs. 53.9 full AXPO); removing uncertainty ranking drops to 51.0 (−2.9 pp); removing prefix credit drops to 51.4 (−2.5 pp); all components are necessary - Comparison (Table 3): AXPO (53.9 avg) outperforms ARPO (51.3), CISPO (47.8), RLTF (43.3), tool bonus (50.8), tool penalty (46.0), and 2× rollout budget (52.4) - Per-benchmark Pass@1 shows some negative deltas at specific benchmarks (e.g., V* −1.3 pp at 4B, VisualProbe −1.0 pp at 2B), indicating AXPO does not improve uniformly across all tasks - Cross-model comparison (Table 9): SFT+AXPO at 8B achieves 76.6% average over 5 shared benchmarks vs. 66.3% for the strongest prior system (PyVision-RL on Qwen2.5-VL-7B), though base models differ Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The empirical diagnosis of the Thinking-Acting Gap (tool-use rate ~30%, all-wrong subgroup rate ~40%) is a concrete finding not previously characterized this precisely. The tool-call resampling mechanism with its decomposed advantage streams is genuinely novel versus prior work: it resamples at the tool-call boundary (not post-observation like ARPO), targets all-wrong subgroups specifically (not general rollout scaling), and modifies rollout distribution rather than reward shaping. The Proposition 1 theoretical motivation is a straightforward application of monotone function theory, not a deep mathematical contribution, but it grounds the practical design choice. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover all 5 design choices with clear magnitude estimates (Table 2); each component contributes 0.7–2.9 pp, avoiding the problem of individually insignificant ablations. Variance across 4 independent evaluation rollouts is reported (Table 6, std 0.4–3.6 pp per cell, column-averaged 1.2–1.4 pp), below headline gains. Training dynamics (Figure 4) directly measures the proposed diagnostic symptoms over 50 RL steps, providing mechanistic confirmation. Confidence-entropy correlation (Figure 6, ρ=0.843) justifies the proxy choice. Benchmarks are standard public sets; training data sources are credited. Code is not released in this preprint (project page link is present but leads to a placeholder). One mild concern: the VisualProbe hard subset is defined as questions the base 8B model fails without tools, creating a soft circularity when the trained 8B variant is evaluated on the same subset. The MMSearch judge uses "gpt-5-mini," a non-standard model, which may introduce variance not quantified in the variance analysis. All experiments use a single model family (Qwen3-VL-Thinking); cross-architecture generalization is claimed but untested. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Training runs on 8× A100 80GB GPUs using standard RL frameworks (verl, rllm); inference uses vLLM; both are commercially available and broadly accessible to ML labs. - Efficient: AXPO adds only 25% extra rollout compute (r=0.25) over GRPO, a 1.25× multiplier that outperforms a 2× multiplier with GRPO; memory overhead from branched rollouts is bounded by the same cap. - Effective: Gains are observed across all 3 scales on both Pass@1 and Pass@4; training dynamics confirm the mechanism acts as described; per-tool matched-tool analysis (Figure 5b) shows improvement across all 3 tool families, ruling out pure frequency effects. - Scalable: Tested only up to 8B trainable parameters; the 32B reference is inference-only. Whether the all-wrong subgroup rate shrinks at larger scale (making AXPO's trigger fire less often) or the tool-call quality gap closes is open. No software artifact (library, trained weights, CLI) is released in this preprint. Strengths - The diagnostic characterization of the Thinking-Acting Gap is empirically precise: all three measurements (tool-use rate, all-wrong rate, semantic cluster diversity under fixed prefix) are collected during actual GRPO training and directly motivate each design choice. - The method is narrowly targeted: rather than adding reward channels or scaling rollouts uniformly, AXPO intervenes exactly where GRPO fails. The result that +25% resampling budget beats +100% rollout budget is unusually clean evidence that the gain is structural. - Advantage decomposition handles the gradient conflict between failed source prefix and successful resampled continuation formally, with a monotone recovery indicator that is both theoretically sound and empirically justified by the prefix-credit ablation (−2.5 pp without it). - The unseen-tool generalization experiment (Table 8) probes whether AXPO's training transfers beyond the three tool families used in training; the Pass@1 asymmetry (+4.6 pp for AXPO vs. +2.8 pp for GRPO on image-search addition) provides preliminary evidence beyond the training distribution. Author-Acknowledged Limitations - AXPO requires binary, automatically verifiable outcome rewards; tasks with partial verifiability (open-ended generation, subjective evaluation) require a different trigger definition before the method applies. - Trainable scale is bounded at 8B by compute availability; whether the gap and fix generalize to 32B+ trainable scale—where the base policy is closer to the tool-use ceiling—is explicitly left open. - Tool inventory covers 3 families; image-search was excluded from training due to API cost, and the unseen-tool study uses a GPT-5.4 proxy rather than a real retrieval endpoint. - All experiments use Qwen3-VL-Thinking; replication on other architectures is stated as future work. The authors confront these limitations directly rather than minimizing them. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 32B ceiling is inference-only and never trained with AXPO. The headline claim that "8B AXPO surpasses 32B Base" holds against an untrained model; SFT+AXPO at 32B would be the informative comparison and is absent. - Several per-benchmark deltas are negative at specific scales (V* −1.3 pp Pass@1 at 4B, −1.6 pp Pass@4 at 8B; VisualProbe −1.0 pp at 2B), but the paper's discussion focuses on averages and does not analyze conditions under which AXPO degrades performance. - The unseen-tool experiment uses a GPT-5.4 prompt proxy for image search—the extent to which this proxy inflates the generalization signal compared to a real visual-retrieval API is unquantified. - AXPO resamples only at the first tool-call boundary; for multi-hop search questions (where the load-bearing decision may be whether to issue a second query after the first hop returns), it is unclear whether the method's coverage gain propagates to later turns. - All SFT training data is generated by a single teacher (Qwen3-VL-32B-Thinking); whether teacher diversity affects the Thinking-Acting Gap or AXPO's recovery is not investigated. Bottom Line A practitioner building agentic VLM training pipelines gains a compute-efficient, well-ablated improvement over GRPO that targets a real and measurable training failure, backed by mechanistic training dynamics and clean compute-efficiency evidence—this is directly actionable for the agentic RL community.
Recommended 2605.28774 PDF
#69 — Innovative (66)

Native Audio-Visual Alignment for Generation (18 ▲ )

Longbin Ji, Guan Wang, Xuan Wei, Chenye Yang, Xiangrui Liu, Zhenyu Zhang, Shuohuan Wang, Yu Sun, Jingzhou He · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 29 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces NAVA, a 6.3B-parameter joint audio-video generation framework built on an Align-then-Fuse MMDiT architecture that first establishes audio-video correspondence in a dedicated self-attention space before injecting text and timbre as external cross-attention conditioning. - Why it's important: Existing open-source joint audio-video generators either use dual-tower designs with weak late-stage cross-modal alignment or fully unified tri-modal spaces that entangle high-level semantic control with low-level synchronization, both limiting fine-grained temporal coherence at a time when joint audio-video generation is a critical unsolved problem for media, avatars, and conversational AI. - How it does it: Audio and video tokens co-evolve through joint self-attention in modality-aware Hierarchical Alignment Layers before transitioning to modality-shared Unified Fusion Layers for collaborative denoising, with timbre cues embedded as span-bound context tokens for per-speaker control. - The good: NAVA achieves the best Sync-C (7.791) and Sync-D (7.566) on Verse-Bench using only 6.3B parameters versus competitors at 10–32B, and achieves speaker similarity of 66.7 on Seed-TTS, exceeding dedicated audio-only TTS systems like CosyVoice (60.9). - The missing: All evaluations are conducted on clips averaging ~7 seconds; there is no testing at longer durations, no reporting of inference latency or memory footprint, and the Gemini-3-Flash prompt rewriting applied to all test prompts is a potential confound given NAVA's own training pipeline also uses Gemini captioning. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on multimodal generation who want a principled, ablation-backed alternative to dual-tower and fully-unified architectures, particularly for the Align-then-Fuse design and Timbre-in-Context Conditioning mechanisms.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Novelty (68) and Non-Obviousness (70) carry the score: the Align-then-Fuse ordering — forcing audio-video synchronization to form in a dedicated self-attention space before semantic context is injected via cross-attention — is a non-obvious departure from both dual-tower and fully-unified paradigms, and the ablation (alignment CFG lifting Sync-C from 6.170 to 7.791) gives causal support. Rigor (65) is helped by current baselines evaluated on released versions and two informative ablations, but held back by no error bars/seeds, unreleased weights, an uncontrolled Gemini-rewriting confound, and missing latency/memory numbers despite an efficiency-centered claim. Completeness (65) and Resilience (63) are pulled down by the ~7-second-clip-only evaluation, an unablated rate-aware RoPE that may be doing hidden synchronization work, the IB-Score deficit vs LTX-2.3, and a user study with marginal per-comparison power. Composite: 0.20*68 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*63 + 0.10*62 = 66.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 63 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Joint audio-video generation — synthesizing temporally synchronized and semantically coherent audio and video in a single generative process — matters for media production, virtual avatars, video dubbing, and interactive AI systems. Open-source systems dominate reproducible research here because commercial systems (Seedance, Kling, Veo) are proprietary. Existing open-source methods split into two families: dual-tower designs (Ovi, LTX, MoVA) that keep audio and video in separate feature streams and introduce cross-modal interaction only after separate conditioning — weakening fine-grained co-evolution — and fully unified tri-modal designs (daVinci-MagiHuman) that place text, audio, and video tokens into one shared self-attention space, entangling high-level semantic control with low-level synchronization and preventing a dedicated alignment structure from forming. Neither approach provides a representation space whose sole purpose is audio-video temporal correspondence, independent of the semantic conditioning signal. Key Idea The core insight is a strict ordering of operations: audio and video tokens first interact via joint self-attention without text tokens present (context-conditioned native audio-visual alignment), establishing event-level temporal correspondences natively; context — text and timbre embeddings — is then injected only via cross-attention after this correspondence has been formed. Materializing this ordering progressively, with modality-specific projections in early layers giving way to shared projections in later layers, is the Align-then-Fuse MMDiT architecture. The consequence is that synchronization and semantic guidance are learned in separate parts of the computational graph rather than competing in the same representation space. Methodology NAVA is a 30-block MMDiT with 6.3B parameters: the first 10 blocks are Hierarchical Alignment Layers (HAL) and the remaining 20 are Unified Fusion Layers (UFL). Video is encoded by Wan2.2-VAE (4×16×16 compression) and audio by LTX2.3-VAE into multi-channel spectrogram latents; both are fed as separate token sequences. In HAL blocks, modality-decoupled projections map audio and video tokens into a shared interaction space, then Audio-Video Joint Self-Attention & FFNs perform repeated cross-modal interaction during denoising. To handle token-rate mismatch, RoPE for audio tokens is rescaled by θ_rope = TR_v / TR_a, placing both modalities on a comparable temporal grid. Context is injected separately in all blocks via Context-Guided Cross-Attention & FFNs, keeping text and timbre out of the audio-video self-attention space. In UFL blocks, modality-shared projections replace the decoupled ones, encouraging compact collaborative denoising after alignment has been established. Timbre-in-Context Conditioning encodes reference utterances into context-space timbre tokens s_i = E_tim(R_i) and inserts them into the prompt at the corresponding speech span boundaries (<S> s_i text(S_i) <E>), binding timbre cues to spans through the existing cross-attention pathway without any auxiliary speaker branch. Training follows a three-stage progressive multi-task schedule (T2AV, TI2AV, T2A, T2V, TIA2AV) on 15M clips with AdamW at lr=5e-5 on 128 H100 GPUs for 70K steps (~107,520 H100 GPU-hours total). Structured dropout — 20% random cross-modality attention masking and 20% timbre-token dropout — enables condition-factorized classifier-free guidance with three independent guidance scales (s_text, s_align, s_timbre) at inference. Critical design decisions: (1) HAL-before-UFL ordering — well-justified by ablation; (2) rate-aware RoPE rescaling — justified by token-rate mismatch argument but not ablated independently; (3) context as cross-attention only — justified by contrast with fully unified approach but isolated contribution not separately measured. Key Results On Verse-Bench (speech, sound effects, musical instruments): NAVA achieves Sync-C 7.791 and Sync-D 7.566 (best among all models), video quality 0.659 (best), WER 0.099 (best). IB-Score 0.313 is competitive but below LTX-2.3 (0.337). Audio PQ 6.861 and FD 0.833 are mid-range. All competing models are 10B–32B; NAVA uses 6.3B. On Seed-TTS (EN subset, timbre controllability): NAVA achieves speaker similarity 66.7 and WER 4.20 — exceeding CosyVoice (60.9/4.29) and Qwen2.5-Omni (63.2/2.72) on similarity while DreamID-Omni (the only other audio-video model) scores 35.7/31.76. User study (250 cases, GSB): T2AV win rates of 67.5%, 60.0%, 80.0% over Ovi-1.1, LTX-2.3, daVinci on overall quality; alignment win rates 62.5%, 65.0%, 72.5%. On TI2AV, LTX-2.3 remains competitive (NAVA wins only 26.2% overall quality vs LTX-2.3). Ablation: alignment CFG alone lifts Sync-C from 6.170 to 7.791 — the largest single-component gain in the paper, indicating that the factorized guidance mechanism is as important as the architecture. Baselines are current (all 2025–2026 preprints), evaluated on their released base versions without post-processing; this is methodologically sound. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. What is genuinely novel: the Align-then-Fuse MMDiT formulation that enforces a learned separation between audio-video synchronization and semantic context conditioning, and the Timbre-in-Context Conditioning mechanism that achieves per-span speaker control purely through the context pathway. What is standard combination: MMDiT architecture, classifier-free guidance, progressive multi-task training, VAE-based latent diffusion — all are established components applied in a new structural arrangement. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: Table 3 (HAL vs. UFL vs. both) and Table 4 (alignment CFG and timbre CFG on/off) both isolate meaningful components. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere. Code and model weights are not stated as publicly released (a project page is cited but no code repository). The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.30073v1, May 2026); results are not peer-reviewed. Training cost (~107,520 H100 GPU-hours) makes independent reproduction practically impossible for academic groups. One methodological concern: Gemini-3-Flash prompt rewriting is applied to all test prompts to match each model's inference format — if NAVA's training data pipeline uses Gemini-3-Flash and Gemini-3-Pro for captioning, the rewriting may disproportionately favor NAVA's learned input distribution, introducing a subtle evaluation confound that is disclosed but not controlled for. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL (builds and evaluates a trained 6.3B-parameter generation model). - Practical: The 6.3B model is smaller than all compared baselines; deployment on 1–4 A100/H100 GPUs is plausible by analogy with comparable open-source models, but inference hardware requirements and latency are not reported. - Efficient: NAVA uses 2–5× fewer parameters than its best competitors while achieving superior synchronization and video quality, but per-inference compute, memory footprint, and generation speed are not stated. - Effective: Achieves best synchronization (Sync-C, Sync-D), video quality, and timbre controllability on their benchmarks; semantic audio-visual consistency (IB-Score) is below LTX-2.3, and audio fidelity (PQ, FD) is mid-range — the method does not dominate on all dimensions. - Scalable: All evaluations use clips averaging ~7 seconds; behavior at longer durations, higher speaker counts, or more complex acoustic scenes is not evaluated — the paper only claims and demonstrates performance at short-clip scale. The work delivers a trained model (weights not released) and a paper-form architectural specification; no software library or inference codebase is provided. Strengths - The ablation in Table 3 provides clean causal evidence for both architectural components: HAL-only degrades IB-Score and video quality (0.269→0.313 IB, 30.91→34.34 video), while UFL-only degrades synchronization (Sync-C 7.643 vs. 7.684 combined) — this is a genuine contribution to understanding what drives audio-visual alignment. - Timbre-in-Context Conditioning is architecturally elegant: achieving per-span speaker control through the existing cross-attention pathway without any backbone modification or auxiliary branch, and doing so at speaker similarity (66.7) competitive with or exceeding dedicated audio-only TTS systems. - The efficiency result is noteworthy: 6.3B parameters outperforming 10B–32B baselines on the primary claimed dimension (synchronization) suggests the architectural prior matters more than model scale in this regime. - The condition-factorized CFG formulation with three independently controllable guidance directions is practically useful, enabling inference-time trade-offs between synchronization, text adherence, and timbre without retraining. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge that NAVA struggles with long-tail and compositionally rich audio events — rare animal sounds, music, singing, complex sound mixtures — attributing this to data scarcity in these categories. They propose future exploration of earlier fusion mechanisms (joint audio-visual tokenizers, unified representation models) as a next direction. The acknowledgment is direct and credible; the authors do not minimize these gaps. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Inference latency and memory footprint are entirely absent — for a paper whose efficiency claim (6.3B vs. 10–32B) is central, this omission prevents practitioners from assessing deployment cost. - The Gemini-3-Flash prompt rewriting applied to all benchmarks is a potential systematic bias: NAVA's training captions are generated by the same Gemini models, so the model may be better calibrated to Gemini-rewritten prompts than competitors trained on differently distributed captions — this is disclosed but not controlled with a no-rewriting ablation. - Rate-aware RoPE rescaling (Eq. 6) is presented as a key solution to the token-rate mismatch problem but is never ablated independently; it may be doing significant synchronization work that is conflated with the HAL architecture. - The IB-Score gap (NAVA 0.313 vs. LTX-2.3 0.337) is underemphasized given that semantic audio-visual consistency is arguably as important as temporal synchronization for downstream utility. - No evaluation on clips longer than ~7 seconds; the approach's behavior with multi-minute video, more than two speakers, or shot sequences longer than those shown in Fig. 3 is unknown. - The user study uses 250 cases for T2AV + TI2AV combined across 4 baselines — approximately 30–60 cases per pairwise comparison — which is marginal statistical power for the win-rate differences reported. Bottom Line Practitioners building joint audio-video generation systems should read this paper — the Align-then-Fuse architecture is a well-motivated, ablation-backed departure from the field's dominant paradigms, and Timbre-in-Context Conditioning offers an immediately applicable mechanism for controllable multi-speaker video generation without model surgery.
Recommended 2605.30073 PDF
#70 — Innovative (65)

Quant. npu: Enabling Efficient Mobile NPU Inference for on-device LLMs via Fully Static Quantization

Jinghe Zhang, Daliang Xu, Chenghua Wang, Weikai Xie, Tao Qi, Yun Ma, Mengwei Xu, Gang Huang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Quant.npu, a fully static integer-only post-training quantization framework that jointly optimizes learnable rotation matrices and static quantization parameters for LLM inference on mobile NPUs, without requiring runtime quantization parameter recomputation. - Why it's important: Mobile NPUs (e.g., Qualcomm Hexagon) require fully static quantization for hardware efficiency, but all competitive rotation-based PTQ methods (SpinQuant, QuaRot) use dynamic quantization simulation during optimization — causing 15%+ accuracy collapse when converted to static deployment mode. - How it does it: The core move is recognizing that rotated activations follow Gaussian distributions (stable to gradient-optimize) while unrotated activations are heavy-tailed (unstable gradient landscape due to competing clipping vs. rounding error), then treating them asymmetrically: gradient-optimize the former with mean-based initialization, statically calibrate the latter at higher bit-widths with Max-Min initialization. - The good: Under W4A4 per-channel quantization on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, all baselines collapse entirely (SpinQuant PPL 528.97, QuaRot PPL 481.82) while Quant.npu achieves PPL 21.76 and 55.25% zero-shot accuracy — only 6% below FP32, which is the single most credible result in the paper. - The missing: No error bars or multi-seed runs are reported, the calibration set is 16 WikiText-2 samples with no ablation on size or composition despite acknowledged sensitivity, and the primary latency baseline (ExecuTorch W4A16 per-block) uses less NPU-efficient granularity than Quant.npu's per-channel configuration — potentially inflating the 15.1% speedup claim. - Read it or not: Essential for anyone deploying 1–3B LLMs on Qualcomm Hexagon NPUs; the hardware-measured latency and energy results are rare in quantization papers and the W4A4 results are genuinely striking; skip if you work exclusively on server-side inference.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile — although the Deployability tag reads SYSTEMS, the paper's primary value is the algorithmic contribution (distribution-aware asymmetric treatment of rotated Gaussian vs. unrotated heavy-tailed activations), so novelty and rigor dominate. Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (70) are the lead drivers: identifying the train-static/deploy-static mismatch as the core NPU failure mode, plus the rounding/clipping-error decomposition, is a genuine and non-obvious insight rather than a known-technique recombination. Rigor (58) is held back by no code release, no error bars or multi-seed runs, and a 16-sample calibration set with no size ablation; the latency baseline also confounds per-channel vs. per-block granularity with the algorithmic gain. Completeness (65) and Resilience (65) are buoyed by genuine progressive ablations, real Qualcomm-hardware latency/energy measurement, and honest limitation acknowledgment, while the striking W4A4 result (PPL 21.76 where SpinQuant/QuaRot collapse to 481–529) survives the missing-error-bar critique because the margin is enormous. Composite: 0.20*70 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.10*65 = 65.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 65 Resilience 65 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Mobile NPU hardware architectures impose three hard constraints that conventional GPU-oriented quantization methods violate: (1) integer-only arithmetic is required for matrix multiplication, (2) static compilation rejects dynamic per-tensor min/max recomputation at inference time (a tree reduction on 128-element INT8 costs 7+ vector instruction cycles), and (3) coarse-grained per-tensor activation quantization is required by systolic array cores (per-block granularity incurs frequent de-quantization, yielding ≈20% slower throughput than per-tensor W4A8). All leading rotation-based PTQ methods (SpinQuant, QuaRot, FlatQuant) assume dynamic activation quantization during optimization, meaning the quantization scale is computed at runtime from input statistics. Converting these models to the static NPU setting causes catastrophic accuracy degradation — SpinQuant on SmolLM2-1.7B-Instruct degrades by 15.61% absolute accuracy. Naively fixing quantization parameters before optimization prevents this mismatch but severely hinders convergence because the optimal quantization range shifts as rotation matrices evolve during training (Figure 1b shows loss variance of 0.3 with fixed scales vs. stable convergence with learnable scales). Key Idea The core technical insight is a statistical asymmetry between activation tensor types: activations that pass through offline-fusable rotation matrices (R1, R2) develop approximately Gaussian distributions, for which mean-based initialization (µ ± 3σ clipping) is stable and gradient optimization of scale/zero-point converges reliably; activations that bypass rotation (output activations, KV tensors, down_proj inputs after removing costly online R4) remain heavy-tailed, for which gradient optimization creates a rugged loss landscape because minimizing clipping error requires enlarging scale (which collapses rounding precision on the dominant near-zero mass) and vice versa — producing many local minima that gradient descent cannot escape. The implication is that the two activation populations should never be jointly optimized; the heavy-tailed group should be handled purely by static calibration at sufficient bit-width (8-bit or 16-bit Max-Min), while only the Gaussian group undergoes gradient training. Methodology The pipeline has three sequential components, all operating within a post-training regime using 16 WikiText-2 calibration samples and 512 training steps on 4× NVIDIA A40 GPUs. Stage 1 — Gradient-based optimization jointly updates: (a) rotation matrices R1, R2 via Cayley optimization to maintain orthogonality; (b) scale and zero-point parameters for input activations of all linear layers, using straight-through estimator (STE) gradients with gradient scaling factor g=1/√(N·Qmax) to balance gradient magnitudes between rotation matrices and quantization parameters; and (c) a local quantization error loss min||DQ(Q(x))−x||₂² applied for the first 128 steps to anchor quantization parameters before they become coupled to the rotating weight space. LM-head and output activations remain in BF16 during this stage. Learning rates: 0.1 for rotation, 0.01 for quantization parameters, SGD with cosine decay. Stage 2 — Static calibration applies Max-Min initialization directly to output activations of linear layers, KV tensors, and SiLU activations, without gradient optimization. Per-tensor symmetric quantization for activations; per-channel symmetric for weights. Adaptive mixed-precision addresses down_proj input activations (which cannot be smoothed by online R4 rotation without floating-point overhead): compute per-layer relative quantization error ratio = (1/N)Σ|dequantized_i − original_i| / (|original_i| + ε), then promote the top 10% most sensitive down_proj layers from 8-bit to 16-bit (3 out of 28 layers in Llama-3.2-3B). Critical design decisions: (1) decoupling Stage 1 and Stage 2 optimization is well-justified theoretically via rounding/clipping error decomposition; (2) using only offline-fusable rotations (R1, R2) is well-justified as R3/R4 add ~2× compute overhead; (3) the 10% threshold for mixed-precision is empirically tuned with one model and may not generalize. Key Results All evaluations run on Qualcomm SM8650 real hardware. Baselines are ExecuTorch (edge runtime with LPBQ per-block W4A16), QuaRot (fixed Hadamard rotations), and SpinQuant (learnable rotations with dynamic quantization) — current and strong baselines for the mobile NPU setting, though AWQ and GPTQ are absent. On Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct (Table 1): W8A8 per-channel — PPL(C4) 16.42 vs FP32 16.85 and SpinQuant 17.18; W4A8 per-channel — PPL 19.16 vs SpinQuant 28.78, zero-shot avg 58.27% vs SpinQuant 46.23% (+12.04%); W4A4 per-channel — PPL 21.76 vs SpinQuant 528.97 and QuaRot 481.82, zero-shot avg 55.25% vs near-random baselines (~30%). Latency: up to 15.1% speedup over ExecuTorch-W4A16 with average 2.58% accuracy drop and 1.23 PPL increase across all tasks and models. On Llama3-8B W4A8: PPL 11.88 vs SpinQuant 22.37. Additional experiments on Qwen2.5-3B, SmolLM2-1.7B, Qwen3-1.7B show consistent gains; on Qwen2.5-3B W4A8, improvement over baselines reaches 30.41%. AlpacaEval 2.0 W8A8 achieves 48.82% length-controlled win rate vs the FP16 reference model. SM8750 hardware: peak memory 1876–1985 MB for 3B models; prefill throughput up to 1612 tokens/s for 1.7B models. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The combination of jointly learning static quantization parameters and rotation matrices specifically to close the train-time/deploy-time quantization mode gap for NPU hardware is novel. The distribution-aware asymmetric treatment of rotated vs. unrotated tensors is the primary intellectual contribution, backed by theoretical analysis. Individual components (STE, Hadamard rotations, learnable quantization, Cayley optimization) are drawn from prior work; the contribution is their principled combination and the identification of the train-static/deploy-static mismatch as the core failure mode of existing methods. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations in Table 2 progressively build each component and validate contribution: removing initialization degrades PPL from 36.42 to 69.65; removing selective optimization from 30.89 to 36.42; removing adaptive mixed-precision from 22.09 to 30.89. This is genuine and informative ablation. The theoretical analyses in Appendices D and E for initialization rules and gradient instability are more suggestive than rigorous: Proposition D.3 assumes independence between rotation matrix rows and input components (which does not hold for learned, data-dependent rotations), and the proof of optimization failure for heavy-tailed tensors identifies the qualitative problem without bounding the expected error. No error bars, confidence intervals, or multiple seeds are reported for any table. No code or model weights are publicly released (no repository URL appears in the paper). Calibration uses 16 WikiText-2 samples — unusually small and untested for variance. Evaluation benchmarks (PIQA, Winogrande, HellaSwag, ARC, LAMBADA) are standard but measure only commonsense reasoning; no math, coding, or generation quality benchmarks beyond AlpacaEval appear in the main evaluation. This is a preprint; results have not been peer reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built quantization framework evaluated on real hardware. - Practical: The full pipeline (training + inference) runs on commercially available hardware: training on NVIDIA A40 GPUs (~2.5–8 hours), inference on Qualcomm SM8650/SM8750 consumer SoCs available in flagship Android devices. - Efficient: W4A8 per-tensor activation per-channel weight achieves 2.0× matrix multiplication speedup over FP (Table 3); peak memory 994–1985 MB for 1.7B–3B models; a residual 16-bit overhead remains for SiLU and ~10% of down_proj activations, which the authors acknowledge as a latency bottleneck. - Effective: Directly measured on-device latency (15.1% faster than ExecuTorch-W4A16), memory, and energy consumption on SM8650/SM8750 confirm the framework operates in the actual NPU deployment configuration, not simulation — the problem it addresses is real and the metric is the right one. - Scalable: Tested on 1B–3B models (full evaluation) and 8B (one table, Winogrande only); no experiments beyond 8B; authors explicitly bound target deployment to mobile devices with ~4GB memory budgets; no claim of scaling to larger models. The paper delivers a quantization methodology and hardware deployment demonstration; no public code release is mentioned. Strengths - The W4A4 results are the strongest evidence in the paper: a configuration where every baseline system fails completely (PPL 481–6950) but Quant.npu achieves near-FP32 quality (PPL 21.76, 55.25% accuracy), demonstrating the method solves a regime the field had not previously addressed for NPUs. - Real hardware evaluation with latency, memory, and energy consumption numbers on SM8650 and SM8750 is rare in quantization papers and makes the 15.1% speedup claim independently verifiable rather than simulated. - The theoretical analysis of gradient failure on heavy-tailed distributions (Appendix E: rounding/clipping error trade-off creates multi-modal loss landscape) is more principled than the typical empirical design choice and provides a transferable insight. - Cross-architecture evaluation across Llama, Qwen2.5, Qwen3, and SmolLM2 families, plus a size sweep to 8B, provides meaningful generalizability evidence given that NPU quantization can be architecture-sensitive. Author-Acknowledged Limitations 1. A minor subset of operations (SiLU activations and ~10% of down_proj activations) remain at 16-bit precision, creating a latency bottleneck compared to a hypothetical fully low-bit pipeline; reducing these without accuracy cost is stated as future work. 2. No principled calibration dataset selection strategy is employed; the authors explicitly acknowledge that the parameter optimization is sensitive to activation distributions, so the quality of 16 randomly drawn WikiText-2 samples could be consequential. The authors confront both limitations directly, which is credibility-positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The primary latency comparison uses ExecuTorch-W4A16 per-block as the reference; Quant.npu uses per-channel weight quantization, which delivers a hardware speedup independent of any algorithmic contribution (Table 3 shows per-channel W4 achieves 2.0× vs per-block W4's 1.67×). The paper does not isolate what fraction of the 15.1% latency improvement comes from granularity switching vs. the quantization optimization itself. - No multi-seed or variance analysis on any result; with only 16 calibration samples and models in the 1–3B range, run-to-run variance in PPL and accuracy could be non-trivial and is unreported. - The AlpacaEval evaluation uses FP16 Llama-3.2-3B as the reference rather than the standard GPT-4-Turbo reference, with a stated rationale (small models score poorly vs GPT-4-Turbo), but this prevents comparison with published AlpacaEval numbers from other quantization work. - No ablation on calibration set size (why 16 samples? how does performance change at 32, 64, 128 samples?) despite the acknowledged sensitivity — this would directly address the most important stated limitation. - The paper does not compare against AWQ or GPTQ under static NPU constraints, nor against OmniQuant; MobileQuant comparison is relegated to the appendix and uses a different model (TinyLlama) that does not appear in any other table, making it difficult to assess relative positioning on the main evaluation suite. Bottom Line Any practitioner deploying small LLMs on Qualcomm NPUs should read this paper: it identifies and solves a real deployment-breaking problem that prior methods ignore, demonstrates it on actual hardware, and the W4A4 results — where all competing methods fail and Quant.npu succeeds — are strong enough that the missing error bars and calibration ablations do not undermine the core contribution.
Search Results 2605.20295 PDF
#71 — Innovative (65)

TIDE: Efficient and Lossless MoE Diffusion LLM Inference with I/O-aware Expert Offload

arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
