TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces TIDE, an I/O-aware inference system for MoE-based diffusion LLMs that exploits temporal stability of expert activations across denoising steps to skip redundant GPU-CPU expert migrations. - Why it's important: MoE diffusion LLMs like LLaDA2.0-flash (100B parameters) exceed single-GPU VRAM, yet existing AR-MoE offloading strategies create prohibitive I/O or CPU bottlenecks when applied to dLLMs because each denoising step activates experts for an entire token block simultaneously — far larger and more fragmented than a single AR token routing. - How it does it: Adjacent denoising steps share cosine similarity ~0.985 in expert routing, so TIDE refreshes the GPU-resident expert set only every τ steps (determined offline by minimizing a closed-form I/O + CPU cost model via greedy search) and reuses expert placement in between, with asynchronous PCIe overlap masking CPU-resident expert compute. - The good: TIDE achieves up to 1.5× throughput over Mixtral-Offloading on LLaDA2.0-flash with zero accuracy degradation — a lossless guarantee proven by construction, not just claimed empirically. - The missing: Evaluation uses only a single coding benchmark (MBPP) on two LLaDA2.0 model variants in a single GPU-CPU setting; no language understanding, reasoning, or summarization benchmarks are tested, and multi-GPU or batch > 1 scenarios are not evaluated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems engineers targeting edge or resource-constrained deployment of large MoE or diffusion LLMs; skip if your interest is model quality, AR inference, or multi-GPU serving.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (68) and Novelty (70) carry the score: TIDE is a released, training-free scheduler that exploits a genuinely new, diffusion-specific empirical finding (~0.985 cross-step expert-routing cosine similarity) and the lossless-by-construction guarantee is mathematically rigorous, lifting Resilience (62). Rigor (58) is held back by single-benchmark evaluation (MBPP only), no error bars, and ablations that isolate only τ (not the async overlap or hit-count selection policy), and Completeness (58) by the batch-1, single-GPU scope. Weighted: 0.15*70 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*72 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*62 + 0.30*68 = 65.
systems Novelty 70 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 58 Resilience 62 Impact 68
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Large MoE diffusion LLMs (e.g., LLaDA2.0-flash at 100B parameters) cannot fit in a single GPU's VRAM. Inference requires expert offloading between GPU HBM and CPU host memory, but existing AR-MoE offloading strategies are poorly matched to the dLLM execution pattern. In dLLMs, each denoising step activates experts for all tokens in a block simultaneously (e.g., 32 tokens × top-8 experts = up to ~256 expert calls per step vs. a single token × k in AR), creating a far wider and more fragmented expert footprint. Naive per-step expert swapping (Mixtral-Offloading) generates excessive PCIe I/O traffic proportional to this large activated set. Routing tokens to statically CPU-resident experts (Fiddler) avoids I/O but becomes CPU-bound because dense GEMM on CPU is orders of magnitude slower than GPU. The unsolved problem is an orchestration strategy that simultaneously minimizes I/O overhead and maximizes GPU compute utilization for MoE-dLLM inference on single GPU-CPU systems. Key Idea The core insight is that expert routing in MoE-dLLMs exhibits strong temporal locality across consecutive denoising steps within a block: adjacent steps share cosine similarity ~0.985, and steps 5 apart retain >0.95. This quasi-static property allows the GPU-resident expert set to be refreshed only once every τ steps rather than every step, amortizing PCIe I/O cost across the interval while keeping the CPU miss rate low. The optimal τ is derived by formulating I/O latency as a decreasing function of τ and CPU latency as a monotonically increasing function of τ, finding their sum's minimum via hardware-profiled constants and greedy search. Methodology TIDE splits dLLM block decoding into refresh steps and skipped steps. At each refresh step (every τ denoising steps), TIDE ranks all experts by their token hit counts accumulated over the prior interval, promotes the top-B highest-demand CPU-resident experts to GPU memory via async PCIe transfer, and evicts low-demand GPU experts to CPU. During the τ−1 intervening skipped steps, the expert placement is frozen: GPU-hit tokens are processed normally, CPU-miss tokens are dispatched asynchronously over PCIe, and results are re-synchronized at the end of each FFN block — overlapping CPU and GPU computation and preventing GPU stall. The optimal τ is determined offline: (1) hardware profiling estimates I/O bandwidth constant C^IO and CPU computation constant C^CPU for the target platform; (2) greedy search minimizes Lat^IO(τ) ≈ C^IO·(B·T/τ)·(1−(1−d)^τ) + Lat^CPU(τ) ≈ C^CPU·T·B·f(τ), where d is the per-step expert drift rate approximated as constant. Critical design decisions: (a) asynchronous CPU-GPU overlap is well-justified as it directly prevents GPU idling; (b) using token hit frequency as expert selection criterion is pragmatic but not proven optimal — alternatives like LRU or learned predictors are not compared; (c) approximating drift rate d as a constant is an acknowledged simplification — Figure 2(a) shows unique expert count grows through the block, meaning drift is not uniform, though Figure 2(b) shows cosine similarity remains high throughout. Key Results On LLaDA2.0-mini (16BA1B, NVIDIA A100 40GB): TIDE achieves 1.89–2.44 token/s across GPU budget and generation length configurations, vs. Fiddler's 1.74–1.81 and Mixtral-Offload's 1.45–1.91 — representing 1.2–1.4× speedup. On LLaDA2.0-flash (100BA6B, NVIDIA H100 80GB): TIDE achieves 1.24–1.73 token/s vs. Fiddler's 0.89–1.14 and Mixtral-Offload's 1.01–1.35 — representing up to 1.5× speedup. The optimal τ outperforms random τ selection by up to 1.4×, validating the analytical scheduling approach. Gains are consistent across block sizes 32–128, GPU expert budgets 32–128, and confidence thresholds 0.7–0.95. Accuracy on MBPP is claimed identical to unconstrained GPU execution by construction. Baselines are the only two existing AR-MoE offloading methods applied to dLLMs; no dLLM-native baselines exist, which the authors acknowledge. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The empirical discovery that MoE-dLLM expert routing exhibits ~0.985 cosine similarity between adjacent denoising steps is new and specific to the diffusion paradigm — it cannot be assumed from AR-MoE behavior. The TIDE system operationalizes this finding in a training-free, lossless inference scheduler. The interval-based refresh concept draws on skip/reuse ideas from dense-dLLM KV caching work (Fast-dLLM, dKV-Cache) but is genuinely novel in its application to the MoE expert placement problem and in the accompanying analytical optimization framework. Rigor & Reproducibility The lossless guarantee is proven by construction (expert routing decisions and model weights unchanged, only placement differs), which is mathematically rigorous. The throughput claims are supported by experiments across multiple memory budgets and generation lengths. However, no ablations isolate the individual contributions of (a) asynchronous CPU-GPU overlap, (b) hit-count-based expert selection vs. alternative heuristics, and (c) optimized τ — only the last is ablated via Table 2. No error bars or run-to-run variance reported. Evaluation is restricted to a single benchmark (MBPP, sanitized coding subset), which limits generalization of throughput claims to other task types. Hardware setup is specified precisely. Code is referenced on the project page linked in the paper. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built inference scheduler evaluated by deployment throughput on real hardware. - Practical: Runs on commodity server hardware (A100 40GB or H100 80GB + Intel CPU); commercially available, though the 100B flash model requires an H100 and 55GB GPU expert budget. - Efficient: At optimal τ, I/O migrations scale as 1/τ of the full-refresh cost; throughput improves 1.2–1.5× over best existing baseline with no additional memory overhead beyond the existing CPU-GPU hierarchy. - Effective: The lossless claim is proven by construction, not just measured; throughput gains on MBPP are concrete, though output quality on diverse generation tasks is not validated. - Scalable: Tested only at single-batch, single GPU-CPU scale on models up to 100B; multi-GPU, multi-node, and large-batch regimes are explicitly not addressed and left as future work. The system delivers a software artifact implemented on HuggingFace Transformers + dInfer (PyTorch 2.9, CUDA 12.8), with code available via the paper's project page. Strengths - The temporal locality finding is empirically well-characterized: the cosine similarity heatmap quantitatively establishes the phenomenon and bounds the safe interval range — this is the empirical foundation the entire system rests on, and it is solid. - The analytical model for optimal τ is principled: it derives a closed-form I/O cost function with known asymptotic behavior (1/τ decay with diminishing returns), profiles hardware constants offline, and adds zero runtime overhead. - The lossless guarantee is mathematically rigorous by construction, removing the need for accuracy benchmarking across every possible task. - Asynchronous GPU-CPU computation overlap is a concrete systems contribution that prevents GPU stall on CPU-resident expert misses, and its necessity is well-motivated by the latency formulation in Section 3.1. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge three limitations in an appendix: (1) expert activation pattern analysis is restricted to within-block; cross-block similarity analysis could yield further gains; (2) evaluation is on NVIDIA GPUs + Intel CPU only, with no AMD or ARM coverage; (3) the work is limited to single GPU-CPU systems and does not extend to multi-GPU or distributed expert parallelism. The authors confront these directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-benchmark evaluation on MBPP (coding, short outputs) does not support generalization of throughput gains to tasks with longer or more diverse generation; throughput may differ significantly on tasks that trigger different expert activation distributions. - Batch size 1 assumed throughout; throughput comparisons at batch > 1 are absent, which is more relevant for production server deployment and may change the I/O vs. compute balance substantially. - The constant drift rate d approximation is used in the analytical model, but Figure 2(a) shows unique expert count increases monotonically across the block — meaning drift accelerates toward block end, and the model likely underestimates CPU latency for larger τ at later denoising steps. - No comparison to quantization-based memory reduction approaches (e.g., QuantdLLM, also cited) that might achieve similar VRAM footprint reduction without any I/O complexity. - Token hit frequency as the expert selection policy is not ablated against alternatives (e.g., LRU, learned predictors, or random selection), leaving open whether a better selection policy would yield additional gains. Bottom Line This is a focused, technically sound systems paper that correctly identifies a real bottleneck, provides a new empirical finding to justify the approach, and delivers measurable lossless improvement — practitioners building resource-constrained MoE-dLLM pipelines on single GPU-CPU nodes should read it, but should validate throughput claims on their own task distributions and batch sizes before deploying.
Search Results 2605.20179 PDF
#72 — Innovative (65)

Evaluation of Pipelines for Data Integration into Knowledge Graphs

Marvin Hofer, Erhard Rahm · arxiv.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces KGI-Bench, a benchmark framework for end-to-end evaluation of knowledge graph (KG) integration pipelines, together with KGI-Bench-Movie, a publicly released dataset instantiation covering 10,000 films in RDF, JSON, and text formats with a DBpedia-derived reference KG. - Why it's important: Existing benchmarks evaluate isolated KG construction tasks (entity resolution, ontology matching) but no benchmark evaluates the quality of the integrated KG produced by complete pipelines, leaving practitioners unable to compare pipeline designs on holistic quality. - How it does it: Pipelines are evaluated by comparing the output KG against a reference KG via three orthogonal metrics — coverage (recall of reference entities/triples), correctness (precision of produced entities/triples), and consistency (ontology constraint violation rates) — after entity alignment using sentence-transformer embeddings. - The good: RDF_llm achieves F1_E=0.973 and F1_T=0.901 and ranks first across all 66 weight permutations of coverage/correctness/consistency, demonstrating that LLM-assisted ontology matching propagates measurably into end-to-end KG quality. - The missing: The text sources are DBpedia abstracts while the reference KG is derived from DBpedia structured data, creating a circularity that makes the text pipeline results (F1_E=0.037–0.074) an artifact of this particular construction rather than a general finding about unstructured source integration. - Read it or not: Worth reading for practitioners building or comparing KG integration pipelines who need a principled evaluation harness; the metric formalism is reusable even beyond the movie domain, though the benchmark itself is narrow.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (62) and Resilience (60) pull the composite down: results are single-run point estimates with no significance tests or component ablations, it is an unreviewed arXiv preprint, and — most damaging — the DBpedia-abstract text source is circular with a DBpedia-derived reference KG, making the catastrophic text-pipeline results (F1_T=0.017) partly an artifact of construction rather than a general finding; all 12 pipelines also come from the authors' own tool. Novelty (68) and Impact (70) credit a genuine gap (end-to-end pipeline evaluation) filled with a publicly released benchmark, dataset, and the orthogonal coverage/correctness/consistency decomposition. Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*70 ≈ 65.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 65 Completeness 65 Resilience 60 Impact 70
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Constructing and continuously updating knowledge graphs requires chaining multiple heterogeneous tasks — extraction, schema alignment, entity resolution, fusion, cleaning — into pipelines. These tasks are developed independently and evaluated in isolation. The missing capability is holistic pipeline evaluation: given two competing pipelines that produce different updated KGs, there is no established methodology to determine which is better. Incremental integration compounds the problem because errors in early stages propagate through downstream steps, making task-level metrics misleading. The field has matured benchmarks for individual subtasks (OAEI for ontology matching, OpenEA for entity alignment, Text2KGBench for extraction) but nothing that assesses the combined output of a complete, multi-stage pipeline. Key Idea: Bypass the intractability of evaluating individual pipeline internals by evaluating the artifact produced — the updated KG — against a reference KG. The key insight is decomposing output quality into three orthogonal dimensions (coverage/correctness/consistency) that individually diagnose different failure modes: low coverage exposes extraction or mapping incompleteness; low correctness exposes fusion or entity resolution errors; low consistency exposes ontology-level defects. This triage structure lets the benchmark serve as a diagnostic tool, not just a ranking mechanism. Methodology: The benchmark assumes a seed KG (KG₀), one or more input sources (S₁…Sₙ) to integrate sequentially, and a reference KG (KG_R) as ground truth. A pipeline φ is modeled as a DAG of task implementations. Evaluation proceeds in two stages: (1) establish an entity alignment A_n ⊆ E(KGₙ) × E(KG_R) using sentence-transformer similarity over entity labels; (2) compute coverage (recall of reference entities with correct types and recall of reference triples via triple matching), correctness (precision of produced entities/triples against aligned reference, penalizing duplicates), and consistency (six ontology violation ratios: disjointness, domain, range, relation direction, datatype, format). Metrics are aggregated into a weighted composite score with tunable weights per quality dimension. The benchmark instantiation (KGI-Bench-Movie) constructs a reference KG by recursively crawling 10,000 DBpedia film entities with their related persons and companies (~336,000 triples), then partitions into a seed (25%) and three source splits (25% each) with controlled ~5% pairwise overlap. RDF sources are generated by namespace renaming; JSON by flattening subgraphs into nested records; text by including DBpedia abstracts. Twelve pipelines are evaluated: six single-source-type (two per format — baseline and LLM-enhanced) and six multi-source-type (all permutations of base pipelines across formats). The LLM-based pipelines use OpenAI's gpt-5-mini-2025-10 at zero-shot inference. Critical design decisions: (a) evaluation-restricted sets exclude seed content from metrics to avoid crediting prior knowledge — well-justified; (b) duplicate-penalized entity correctness counts reference entities in the numerator while counting produced entities in the denominator — sound choice that prevents gaming via duplication; (c) fixed PARIS similarity thresholds (0.95 for entities, 0.8 for relations) across all pipelines — limits threshold sensitivity analysis. Key Results: Among single-source pipelines on the 10k benchmark, RDF_llm achieves the best performance: CovE=0.959, CorrE=0.988, F1_E=0.973, CovT=0.884, CorrT=0.917, F1_T=0.901, near-perfect consistency. RDF_base performs second: F1_E=0.866, F1_T=0.676. JSON_base reaches F1_E=0.807, F1_T=0.600 with notable relation-direction violations (OR=0.842). Text pipelines fail severely: TEXT_base achieves F1_E=0.037, F1_T=0.017; TEXT_llm (1k benchmark only) achieves F1_E=0.074, F1_T=0.069. JSON_llm and TEXT_llm are evaluated only on the 1k version, limiting direct comparability. Multi-source pipelines cluster tightly (F1_E ~0.57–0.59, F1_T ~0.41–0.43) with integration order having minimal effect. In comparative ranking across 66 weight permutations, RDF_llm holds rank 1 stably, RDF_base holds rank 2; JSON pipelines show wider rank ranges (3–11). The key mechanistic finding is that improved ontology matching recall in RDF_llm (OM_r=0.83 vs. 0.63 for RDF_base) propagates into higher fact coverage and F1. Entity linking recall for text sources is 43% for films and lower for persons/companies, constituting the primary bottleneck. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK/DATASET + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark framework and KGI-Bench-Movie dataset are the primary contributions. The individual metrics borrow from existing KG quality literature (SHACL-style consistency checking, precision/recall for entity resolution); their novelty lies in the combination and application to end-to-end pipeline evaluation rather than isolated task evaluation. The empirical finding that LLM assistance benefits structured (RDF) pipelines but not unstructured (text) pipelines is new at this level of systematic specificity, though directionally anticipated. Rigor & Reproducibility: All code and datasets are publicly available (GitHub: https://github.com/ScaDS/KGI-Bench; Zenodo: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17246357). Ablations in the traditional sense are absent — no component-level ablations within a single pipeline to isolate which task drives quality. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported; all results are point estimates from single runs. The paper is an arXiv preprint; results are not peer-reviewed. A methodological concern: the LLM-based JSON and text pipelines are evaluated only on the 1k benchmark (not 10k), making direct comparison with RDF pipelines problematic. Thresholds (PARIS at 0.95/0.8, EmbRL at 0.8) were set via preliminary experiments but not systematically swept in the main evaluation. The evaluation alignment itself — sentence-transformer over entity labels — is imperfect and could systematically misattribute errors. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (benchmark construction and pipeline execution). Practical: The benchmark runs on a single workstation (AMD Ryzen 9 9850HX, 64GB RAM, NVIDIA RTX 4070 GPU); text pipelines consume up to 19.8GB RAM, which is within reach of standard research machines. Efficient: LLM-based JSON pipelines are prohibitively slow (7,025 seconds) and expensive (€9.9 per 1k-film run), limiting practical deployment; RDF-based pipelines are fast (65–222 seconds). Effective: The metrics successfully distinguish pipeline quality across format types and detect concrete integration defects (duplicate entities, ontology violations) that correlate with known failure modes. Scalable: The benchmark is tested up to 10,000 films; the full DBpedia corpus contains ~150,000 films and is not tested, leaving scalability of both the evaluated pipelines and the evaluation framework unverified. The paper delivers a software artifact (KGpipe tool, benchmark datasets) and a reusable evaluation methodology. Strengths: - Fills a genuine and well-characterized gap: no prior benchmark evaluates complete KG integration pipeline quality end-to-end against a ground truth, and the paper makes this case clearly with a thorough related work survey. - The three-metric decomposition (coverage/correctness/consistency) is orthogonal by construction and provides interpretable diagnostic signals — each metric points to a distinct failure class, which is more useful for pipeline debugging than a single aggregated score. - The duplicate-penalized entity correctness formulation is a non-trivial and correct design choice that prevents pipelines from gaming precision by under-merging entities. - Public release of all code, datasets, and three benchmark sizes (100/1k/10k films) enables immediate reproduction and incremental adoption. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors limit the scope to a fixed ontology (no dynamic schema evolution). They note that the benchmark is domain-specific to movies and call for extension to other domains. They acknowledge that JSON_llm and TEXT_llm could only be executed on the smaller 1k benchmark, limiting comparability. They note the evaluation alignment is imperfect and that the benchmark is not intended to be exhaustive in consistency constraint types. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The most significant unacknowledged gap is the circularity in benchmark construction: DBpedia abstracts serve as the text source while the reference KG is derived from DBpedia's structured data. This means the text pipeline's entity linker (DBpedia Spotlight) has structural affinity with the ground truth that would not exist in real-world unstructured data integration. The catastrophic text-pipeline results (F1_T=0.017) partly reflect this design choice rather than a general property of LLM or OpenIE extraction. Second, all 12 pipelines are generated by the authors' own tool KGpipe, which means no independent or third-party pipeline designs are evaluated — the benchmark demonstrates usefulness with the authors' own pipelines, not with independently developed ones. Third, the entity alignment procedure (sentence-transformer over labels) used in evaluation is itself imperfect and introduces noise into all downstream metrics; no analysis quantifies this evaluation-alignment error. Fourth, there are no significance tests: with single-run results and no variance estimates, the differences between, e.g., multi-source pipelines (F1_E ~0.566–0.585) may not be statistically meaningful. Bottom Line: A practitioner who needs to compare KG integration pipelines holistically will find the KGI-Bench framework and dataset immediately useful, but should treat the movie-domain results as a proof of concept rather than a general characterization of pipeline behavior, and should be aware that the text-source findings are partly artifacts of the benchmark's DBpedia-derived construction.
Search Results 2605.22304 PDF
#73 — Innovative (65)

Toto 2.0: Time Series Forecasting Enters the Scaling Era

arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Toto 2.0, a family of five open-weights time series foundation models (4m to 2.5B parameters) built under a single training recipe that produces monotonically improving forecast quality with scale, the first such demonstration for time series foundation models. - Why it's important: Every prior time series foundation model family has exhibited erratic scaling where larger versions sometimes underperform smaller ones, making parameter-count investment unreliable; Toto 2.0 closes this gap and establishes a reproducible scaling template. - How it does it: The key moves are (1) replacing autoregressive decoding with contiguous patch masking for single-pass parallel inference, (2) adopting u-µP to transfer hyperparameters from a cheap 10m proxy to all five target sizes without retuning, and (3) training exclusively on proprietary observability telemetry and synthetic data rather than public time series corpora. - The good: Toto 2.0 takes the top three spots on the contamination-resistant TIME benchmark (CRPS ranks 3.43, 3.51, 3.86) ahead of every external model, without having seen any public forecasting data at pretraining — a strong test of cross-domain generalization. - The missing: No component-level ablations are reported; it is impossible to attribute gains independently to CPM, the quantile head, NorMuon, u-µP, or the data recipe, and the hyperparameter sweep optimized against the GIFT-Eval validation set before GIFT-Eval test evaluation, raising a Goodhart's Law concern. - Read it or not: Practitioners building or deploying time series foundation models should read this — it supplies a concrete, reproducible scaling recipe and open weights; researchers seeking fundamental algorithmic novelty will find the contributions are mostly first-class engineering of recently published building blocks.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (releases five open-weight checkpoints and a distributed u-µP training library). Practical Impact (85) dominates the weighting and is the strongest dimension — leading benchmarks plus immediately usable artifacts. Rigor (52) is the major weakness: zero component ablations despite attribution claims, no error bars/seeds, a GIFT-Eval validation-set Goodhart concern, and a self-controlled BOOM benchmark. Novelty (63) and Non-Obviousness (55) are moderate — first µP application to TSFMs and a real monotonic-scaling finding, but built from published components. Completeness (58) is helped by honest limitations though hurt by missing ablations; the clean TIME benchmark result supports Resilience (55). Weighted: 0.15*63+0.15*52+0.10*55+0.15*58+0.15*55+0.30*85 = 65.
systems Novelty 63 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 58 Resilience 55 Impact 85
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Time series foundation models (TSFMs) have begun to match or exceed tuned statistical baselines across heterogeneous domains, but they have not replicated one of the defining achievements of NLP: reliable scaling where a single training recipe produces predictable improvements as parameters increase. Competing TSFM families such as Chronos, TimesFM, and Moirai exhibit uneven scaling — larger checkpoints sometimes score worse than smaller ones on the same benchmark — making investment in compute unpredictable. Infrastructure operators (e.g., SRE teams, cloud monitoring platforms) need forecasts over CPU utilization, memory, latency, and error rates that are both accurate and fast at inference; prior models' autoregressive decoding grows linearly slow with horizon length. The gap this paper targets is thus twofold: establish monotonic scaling across the full 4m–2.5B range and deliver the speed improvements that make long-horizon probabilistic forecasting practical in production. Key Idea: The central technical insight enabling reliable scaling is u-µP (unit-scaled maximal update parametrization): by reparametrizing hidden weights so that the optimal learning rate is width-invariant, a single hyperparameter sweep on a cheap 10m proxy model transfers zero-shot to all five target sizes. Without this, each of the five sizes would require independent multi-day sweeps, making a coordinated family infeasible. The secondary architectural insight is contiguous patch masking, which converts Toto 1.0's K-step autoregressive decoder into a single-pass decoder — collapsing K forward passes to one and eliminating compounding error across horizon steps. Neither idea is new in isolation (u-µP from Blake et al. 2025; CPM adapted from TiRex/Auer et al. 2025), but their joint application to TSFM training appears to be new. Methodology: The backbone is a decoder-only patched transformer (retained from Toto 1.0) with alternating time-axis causal attention and variate-axis full attention. Patch size is 32 (down from 64), doubling sequence length per context window. Input is normalized via a causal arcsinh transform that preserves small fluctuations and logarithmically compresses large excursions. Linear patch projections are replaced by two-layer SiLU residual MLPs at both input and output ends. The output head predicts nine quantiles {0.1, …, 0.9} trained with the pinball loss averaged over all quantile levels. The optimizer is NorMuon for matrix-shaped parameters and AdamW for input/output projections, biases, and norms; NorMuon extends Muon (momentum + Newton-Schulz orthogonalization) by row-normalizing the update against an EMA of per-row squared magnitudes, reinstating an effective per-neuron β₂ variance mechanism discarded by vanilla Muon. The choice of NorMuon is argued (but not ablated) to pair better with sign-valued pinball gradients than AdamW does. Training data is 42.5% proprietary Datadog observability metrics and 57.5% TempoPFN synthetic data; public time series data is excluded from pretraining. Hyperparameter search uses four sequential Optuna TPE rounds (architecture, data mixture, optimizer, decay schedule) optimized against GIFT-Eval validation MASE and CRPS on the 10m proxy; the same configuration is then applied to all five targets. The five sizes range from 4m (d=256, L=4) to 2.5B (d=2048, L=48), all trained on 4,096-step contexts with patch 32 and 32 variates per sample. Key Results: On BOOM (observability benchmark), all five Toto 2.0 sizes sit on the Pareto frontier: CRPS ranks are 3.88 (2.5B), 3.96 (1B), 4.26 (313m), 5.53 (22m), 7.17 (4m) versus Toto 1.0's 6.94 and the next-best external model Chronos-2 at 7.39. On GIFT-Eval (97 tasks across 23 datasets, zero-shot foundation-model tier), the top three CRPS ranks are 20.3 (2.5B), 21.1 (1B), 21.4 (313m), a 1.7-point gap above the next-best foundation model PatchTST-FM r1 at 23.1. On TIME (98 tasks from 50 fresh datasets designed to resist training contamination), the top three CRPS ranks are 3.43 (2.5B), 3.51 (313m), 3.86 (1B), with Chronos-2 fourth at 4.03. A minor inversion (313m outperforming 1B on TIME MASE and rank metrics) is the one departure from monotonic scaling. The 22m achieves Toto 1.0's (151m parameters) quality with 7× fewer parameters. Inference: a 1,024-step forecast requires 16 forward passes for Toto 1.0 versus 1 for Toto 2.0 single-pass; at 4,096 steps, Toto 2.0 2.5B single-pass remains faster than Chronos-2. The finetuned 2.5B-FT and FnF ensemble reach CRPS ranks 13.4 and 12.8 on the full GIFT-Eval leaderboard (including agentic/ensemble entries). These are vendor self-reported numbers on benchmarks the vendor optimized against (GIFT-Eval validation); the TIME result is the cleanest independent test. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL, NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The system contribution is a five-checkpoint model family with open weights and a distributed u-µP training library (dd_unit_scaling). The empirical finding is that time series foundation models exhibit reliable scaling when trained under this recipe. The individual components — u-µP, CPM, NorMuon, quantile heads, TempoPFN synthetic data — are all published prior work; the novelty is their combination, the first application of µP to TSFMs, and the engineering required to make u-µP work under FSDP2/torch.compile at scale. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are reported isolating the contribution of individual components (CPM vs. autoregressive, quantile head vs. SMM, NorMuon vs. AdamW, u-µP vs. per-model tuning, or synthetic vs. observability data). The paper provides qualitative reasoning for design choices (e.g., why pinball gradients favor NorMuon) but no ablation experiments to confirm them. Error bars, multiple seeds, and significance tests are absent throughout; rank metrics aggregate over many tasks which provides some noise averaging, but individual dataset results are not analyzed. A material Goodhart's Law concern: the four-round hyperparameter sweep uses the GIFT-Eval validation set as the optimization objective, and Toto 2.0 is then evaluated on GIFT-Eval test — the model's data mixture and all architectural choices were selected to minimize a proxy of this target. BOOM is Datadog's own proprietary benchmark, making the top-of-BOOM result effectively self-reported on a benchmark the authors control. Code is released (github.com/DataDog/toto, dd_unit_scaling under Apache 2.0) and weights are on HuggingFace; however, the proprietary observability training data is not released, so full pretraining reproducibility is impossible. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL and SYSTEMS — the paper trains, evaluates, and releases a deployed forecasting system. - Practical: The five base models (4m through 2.5B) are released under Apache 2.0 with documented inference code; the 4m is positioned for edge deployment and the 313m runs at roughly the same latency as Chronos-2 on standard GPU hardware. - Efficient: Single-pass CPM reduces long-horizon inference from O(K) to O(1) forward passes; at a 1,024-step horizon, Toto 2.0 sizes are dramatically faster than Toto 1.0, and at a 4,096-step horizon the 2.5B remains faster than Chronos-2, though absolute latency numbers are reported only on unspecified hardware in Figure 9. - Effective: Evidence on three benchmarks — including the contamination-resistant TIME benchmark — indicates the models solve the stated forecasting task, not merely a benchmark proxy, though BOOM is a proprietary benchmark and GIFT-Eval validation was the hyperparameter optimization target. - Scalable: Scaling is validated from 4m to 2.5B parameters (roughly 600×); behavior at 10×–100× the tested scale (25B+) is not studied and not claimed, and the one non-monotonicity at 313m vs. 1B on TIME suggests scaling is not perfectly smooth even within the tested range. The paper delivers a software artifact (five model checkpoints, a training library) alongside the paper. Strengths: - Demonstrating monotonic scaling across a 600× parameter range is a genuine empirical contribution to the TSFM field; prior families consistently exhibit inversions that this work avoids. - The u-µP transfer pipeline is practically impactful: tuning hyperparameters once on a 10m proxy and applying them to a 2.5B model without retuning is a meaningful efficiency gain that the community can adopt. - The TIME benchmark results — top three ranks on a dataset the model was never trained on and that was specifically constructed to avoid training contamination — provide the strongest independent evidence of generalization quality. - The engineering of u-µP for distributed training (FSDP2, torch.compile, data parallelism, sequence-length invariance) addresses real production friction and is released as a general-purpose library. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly note that (1) the 4m loses long-horizon coherence past its training context, confirming small models have limited extrapolation; (2) even the 2.5B loses structure at 8,192 steps where a properly-fitted seasonal model would extrapolate cleanly; (3) data curation in TSFMs (including their own) remains ad hoc rather than principled; (4) the model treats observability metrics as generic time series, losing structure from histogram/distribution metric types, heterogeneous sampling frequencies, and high-dimensional variate selection problems. These are confronted directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The absence of component ablations is the most consequential gap: the paper asserts NorMuon is better suited to pinball loss than AdamW with a theoretical argument but presents no experiment confirming this. Similarly, the benefit of CPM over autoregressive decoding for quality (not just speed) is unquantified. The GIFT-Eval sweep/test circularity is not flagged by the authors: the hyperparameter selection (data mixture ratios, masking parameters, optimizer settings) was optimized on the GIFT-Eval validation set, so GIFT-Eval test performance is not fully held-out. BOOM is controlled by the authors; an independently administered benchmark where Datadog has no influence would be more convincing. The long-horizon stability experiment uses only synthetic sinusoidal mixtures (three superimposed periods) — real observability signals exhibit regime changes, seasonality drift, and discontinuities that sinusoidal mixtures do not capture, making the stability claim harder to generalize. Finally, the paper compares against models that used public data for pretraining, but does not compare against a Toto 2.0 variant that also used public pretraining data, leaving open whether the "no public data" choice is actually optimal or just matched the sweep. Bottom Line: Practitioners building time series forecasting infrastructure gain two concrete, immediately usable things from this work — five open-weight checkpoints that lead current benchmarks and a distributed u-µP training library — making it worth reading for anyone who trains or deploys TSFMs at scale, despite the absence of component ablations and the self-evaluation concerns around BOOM and GIFT-Eval.
Citations 2605.20119 PDF
#74 — Innovative (65)

WBench: A Comprehensive Multi-turn Benchmark for Interactive Video World Model Evaluation (83 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces WBENCH, a multi-turn benchmark of 289 test cases and 1,058 interaction turns for evaluating interactive video world models across five dimensions (video quality, setting adherence, interaction adherence, consistency, and physics compliance) using 22 automatic sub-metrics. - Why it's important: As interactive video world models proliferate across gaming, embodied AI, and autonomous driving, the field lacks a unified evaluation standard covering controllability, memory, and physical realism together, making fair comparison and systematic failure diagnosis impossible. - How it does it: WBENCH defines test cases via a world-setting specification (scene, style, subject, perspective) paired with multi-turn interaction sequences across four types (navigation, subject action, event editing, perspective switching), evaluated through specialist vision models and VLMs whose scores are validated against 400 crowdsourced human pairwise comparisons. - The good: All 10 automated sub-metrics achieve Spearman ρ ≥ 0.94 against human win rates (4 reach ρ = 1.00), providing unusually strong evidence that the automated pipeline reliably reflects human judgment at model-ranking granularity. - The missing: The benchmark covers only discrete action sequences and relies partly on LMM-based evaluation whose reliability may degrade for subtle physical effects; there is no continuous control track, and most closed-source models evaluated (Genie 3, Happy Oyster) are assessed via a separate web-based protocol that may introduce evaluation artifacts compared with the API-accessible models. - Read it or not: Researchers building or evaluating interactive video world models should read this; the benchmark establishes actionable diagnostic baselines across 20 models and surfaces non-obvious findings (navigation independence, camera-control vs. subject-control decoupling) that directly inform architecture choices.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile (benchmark + released evaluation pipeline). Practical Impact (72) and Rigor (62) anchor the score upward: public code and data on GitHub, 20 current models across three paradigms, and unusually strong human-alignment validation (Spearman ρ ≥ 0.94 across all 10 aspects, 400 annotators) that most benchmark papers lack. Non-Obviousness (62) and Completeness (65) are helped by the actionable cross-dimension findings (navigation independence, camera/subject-control decoupling) and directly confronted limitations. The composite is held below the Highly-Innovative band by residual rigor gaps — a single commercial VLM judge concentrating systematic bias, no error bars on a small 289-case set, and two closed models evaluated via an inconsistent web-UI protocol. Weighted: 0.15*58 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*62 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*62 + 0.30*72 = 65.
systems Novelty 58 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 72
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Interactive video world models are advancing rapidly across games, autonomous driving, and embodied interaction, yet evaluation remains fragmented. Most works rely on qualitative demos or narrow task-specific protocols. Existing benchmarks cover only subsets of required competencies: VBench measures perceptual quality without control, WorldMark and MIND cover navigation and memory but omit semantic interactions, Omni-WorldBench adds causal interaction but restricts to first-person view, and WorldLens is limited to autonomous driving. No existing benchmark jointly spans open-domain scenes, both first- and third-person perspectives, all four interaction types (navigation, subject action, event editing, perspective switching), and all five evaluation dimensions (quality, setting adherence, interaction adherence, consistency, physics). This gap prevents fair cross-model comparison and makes failure diagnosis—distinguishing rendering failures from controllability failures from memory failures—infeasible. Key Idea: The core insight is to separate what the world is (world setting: scene, style, subject, perspective) from what the user requests (interaction sequence), and evaluate both independently with dimension-specific metrics. This decomposition lets analysts localize failure modes—a model may render the initial scene correctly but fail later actions, or execute a single instruction correctly but lose identity across turns. A second key design decision is unifying three navigation control paradigms (text, 6-DoF camera pose, discrete keyboard actions) into a single benchmark track so that models with different native interfaces can be compared fairly without penalizing the control modality itself. Methodology: Dataset construction follows a setting-first principle: annotators design a world setting then derive multi-turn interaction sequences that are physically executable and semantically coherent within that setting. Stratified sampling across scene, style, perspective, subject, and interaction type ensures coverage; all cases undergo manual review. Initial frames are generated by Nano Banana 2 and GPT-Image-1.5, supplemented by web-collected images. The evaluation suite comprises 22 sub-metrics grouped into five dimensions. Video Quality uses five VBench sub-metrics (aesthetic quality, imaging quality, temporal flickering, dynamic degree, motion smoothness) plus HPSv3-Norm. Setting Adherence uses VLM scoring for scene adherence (visible vs. off-screen elements) and subject adherence (appearance vs. motion priors). Interaction Adherence uses MegaSaM-estimated camera pose comparison against synthetic ground-truth trajectories for navigation (yielding a normalized Absolute Trajectory Error-based NavScore), and structured VLM 5-binary-check scoring per turn for event editing and subject action, and a 3-binary-check categorical protocol for perspective switching. Consistency metrics combine MegaSaM pose estimates, DreamSim perceptual similarity (spatial/gated spatial), TransNetV2 scene-cut detection (segment continuity), SAM2 subject tracking (perspective and subject consistency), and Depth Anything 3 reprojection (geometric and photometric consistency). Physical compliance uses VLM-based causal fidelity and visual plausibility scoring. Critical design decisions: (1) using per-case synthetic ground-truth navigation trajectories rather than real-world recordings, which is practical but assumes the synthetic trajectory faithfully represents intended motion—partially justified; (2) using doubao-seed-2-0-lite-260215 as the primary VLM judge for most interaction and adherence metrics, a single-model choice that concentrates systematic bias—not deeply justified; (3) the dual-track evaluation protocol (full 289-case benchmark for text-driven models, 158-case navigation split for all 20 models) accommodates heterogeneous control interfaces but limits cross-paradigm comparison on semantic interaction dimensions. Key Results: Experiments cover 20 models across three paradigms: 9 text-driven (Seedance 1.5, Wan 2.7, Kling 3.0, YUME 1.5, HY-Video 1.5, LTX 2.3, LongCat-Video, Kairos 3.0, Cosmos 2.5), 6 camera-controlled (LingBot-World, HY-World 1.5, Fantasy-World, InSpatio-World, Astra, and others), and 6 action-conditioned (Happy Oyster, Matrix-Game 3.0, Genie 3, Matrix-Game 2.0, HY-GameCraft, Infinite-World). Key headline findings: (1) No model dominates all five dimensions. (2) Navigation is nearly independent of other dimensions: Pearson r = −0.12 with quality, r = −0.05 with consistency, r = −0.15 with physics across 20 models. (3) HY-World 1.5 achieves the highest navigation score (87.5) while ranking 8th of 11 world models on perspective consistency, showing camera control and subject control are separate capabilities. (4) Physical correctness correlates strongly with video quality (r = 0.84) and consistency (r = 0.72) but not navigation (r = −0.15), implying physics plausibility is inherited from generative priors rather than control training. (5) Navigation degrades −33 points from turn 1 to turn 4+, the sharpest drop of any interaction type; event editing degrades −13 and subject action −9. (6) Perspective switching is the hardest task: average score 30.7 across text-driven models, with Kling 3.0 achieving only 55.0. (7) Open-source world models are competitive: HY-World 1.5 leads all navigation, LingBot-World leads consistency (89.9). Automated scores validated against human preference: Spearman ρ ≥ 0.94 across all 10 tested aspects. Baselines are current (models from 2025–2026); the benchmark was not available to model developers, reducing evaluation gaming risk. Novelty Classification: NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK. The genuine novelty is the combination of (a) multi-turn evaluation across four interaction types simultaneously, (b) unified navigation interface enabling cross-paradigm comparison, (c) 22 validated sub-metrics spanning all five world-model competencies, and (d) empirical findings about inter-dimension relationships that are non-obvious (navigation independence, physics-quality coupling). Individual metric components (DreamSim, MegaSaM, SAM2, VBench sub-metrics) are borrowed from existing work and assembled into a new evaluation framework. Rigor & Reproducibility: No ablations are reported on metric choices (e.g., why 5 binary checks rather than 3 or 7, or sensitivity to the VLM judge). Human validation is conducted with 400 annotators and Spearman ρ is reported, which is a positive credibility signal. No error bars or confidence intervals are reported for the automated scores themselves—given the small benchmark size (289 cases, 158 navigation cases), score differences of a few points may not be statistically meaningful. Code and data are publicly released at https://github.com/meituan-longcat/WBench. Two models (Genie 3, Happy Oyster) are evaluated via web UI rather than the same API pipeline, introducing a methodological inconsistency that the paper acknowledges but does not quantify. The primary VLM judge (doubao-seed-2-0-lite) is a closed model from a commercial provider; results could shift with a different judge. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS (a benchmark and evaluation pipeline). - Practical: The evaluation pipeline runs on commercially available hardware using a combination of open-source specialist models (MegaSaM, SAM2, TransNetV2, Depth Anything 3) plus a commercial VLM API; the pipeline is publicly released. - Efficient: Per-case evaluation cost is not stated; the reliance on a VLM API call for most adherence metrics means evaluation cost scales with test set size and API pricing, and evaluation of 20 models on 1,058 turns represents a non-trivial budget. - Effective: The benchmark measures what it claims to measure—human validation confirms ρ ≥ 0.94 for all tested aspects—but covers only 289 cases, which may provide insufficient statistical power to distinguish models separated by small score margins. - Scalable: Test set expansion is straightforward in principle (the construction pipeline is documented), but the MegaSaM-based navigation evaluation requires synthetic GT trajectory generation for each new case, and the web-based protocol for closed-source models will remain a bottleneck. The paper delivers a software artifact (benchmark data + evaluation code) and dataset. Strengths: - The unified navigation interface (text / 6-DoF pose / discrete keys) is a concrete and non-obvious engineering contribution that enables fair cross-paradigm comparison without penalizing the control modality, filling a genuine gap in prior benchmarks. - The human-alignment validation (Spearman ρ ≥ 0.94 across all 10 aspects, 400 annotators) is more rigorous than most benchmark papers, providing strong evidence the automated metrics are proxy-valid. - The cross-dimension correlation analysis yields non-obvious, actionable findings—particularly the near-zero correlation between navigation and all other dimensions, and the decoupling of camera control from subject control—that have direct implications for world model architecture design. - Comprehensive coverage of 20 current models from three paradigms, including both open-source and closed-source systems, makes this a useful empirical snapshot of the state of the field. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly state three limitations: (1) the benchmark covers only discrete action sequences, not continuous control; (2) the physical compliance dimension relies partly on LMM-based evaluation whose reliability may degrade for subtle physical effects; (3) the test set is limited in scale and diversity, with promising extensions to additional domains and real-time evaluation. These are confronted directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps: (1) The benchmark has 289 cases and 158 navigation cases—given the number of metrics and models, statistical significance of score differences is not assessed; a 1-2 point difference in the main table may be noise. (2) Using a single commercial VLM (doubao-seed-2-0-lite) as the primary judge for most adherence and setting metrics concentrates systematic bias; ablations over alternative judges (GPT-4o, Gemini, open-source VLMs) are absent. (3) Genie 3 and Happy Oyster are evaluated via web UI rather than a controlled API pipeline; the paper does not quantify the reliability penalty this introduces. (4) The benchmark construction relies on AI-generated initial frames (Nano Banana 2, GPT-Image-1.5), which may introduce distributional artifacts favoring models trained on similar generated-image distributions. (5) The navigation ground-truth trajectory is synthetic and perspective-dependent, but the validity of that GT (e.g., whether a 30° yaw rotation in a synthetic trajectory accurately models what models should produce) is not empirically validated beyond the human correlation study. Bottom Line: WBENCH is the most comprehensive interactive world model benchmark currently available, and practitioners evaluating or building world models will find the 20-model diagnostic and the inter-dimension correlation findings directly actionable—read it.
Recommended 2605.25874 PDF
#75 — Innovative (65)

$D^2$-Monitor: Dynamic Safety Monitoring for Diffusion LLMs via Hesitation-Aware Routing (13 ▲ )

Aoxi Liu, Yupeng Chen, James Oldfield, Guanzhe Hong, Junchi Yu, Baoyuan Wu, Philip Torr, Adel Bibi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 27 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces D²-Monitor, a bi-level safety probe cascade for diffusion LLMs that activates a heavier second-stage probe only when a lightweight linear probe's denoising trajectory shows repeated near-boundary uncertainty, called "hesitation." - Why it's important: Diffusion LLMs are entering commercial deployment at speeds exceeding 1000 tokens/second, yet no external safety monitor exploits their unique multi-step hidden-state trajectory — leaving a gap that alignment-only defenses cannot close against adversarial attacks. - How it does it: The count of denoising steps where the linear probe's signed margin |d_s| falls below a threshold τ (hesitation severity n_τ) predicts probe failure far better than token-level entropy or confidence, enabling a router to activate a focused second-stage probe only on the minimal contiguous hesitation window of hard trajectories. - The good: D²-MLP achieves 89.9% accuracy and 89.7% F1 on WildGuardMix with LLaDA-8B-Instruct using ≤0.21M expected parameters, beating the best baseline (LSTM at 87.8% Acc, 87.4% F1 with 2.57M parameters) while running at 0.56ms post-generation inference and 0.68 MFLOPs per sample versus 163 MFLOPs for LSTM. - The missing: The paper tests only the LLaDA model family (≤16B parameters) and never evaluates against an adaptive adversary who deliberately suppresses hesitation steps — the single most important threat model the paper itself identifies. - Read it or not: Read it if you are building safety infrastructure for diffusion LLMs or studying probe cascade design; the mechanistic insight on hesitation as an adversarial signal is substantive and practically actionable.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The best-executed paper here on methodological care. Non-Obviousness (75) leads: defining hesitation severity (count of near-boundary denoising steps) as a probe-intrinsic routing signal that also acts as an implicit adversarial detector is a genuine, mechanistically grounded insight, not an off-the-shelf trick. Rigor (65) is high — extensive ablations (routing signal, threshold, step/generation length, remasking strategy), leakage-free out-of-fold scoring, 5-seed robustness, and FLOPs verified against wall-clock — though it is confined to one LLaDA model family with no released code. Novelty (68) reflects a NEW METHOD + EMPIRICAL FINDING adapted from AR-LLM cascades. Completeness (62) benefits from honestly confronted limitations (≤16B scale; adaptive-adversary vulnerability), but Resilience (58) and Impact (55) are limited by the untested adaptive attack (the paper's own top threat), missing LLM-as-monitor baselines, narrow cross-dataset margins, and no released artifact. Weighted: 0.20*68 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*55 = 65.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 62 Resilience 58 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Diffusion LLMs (D-LLMs) reformulate text generation as iterative masked-token denoising with bidirectional attention across the full sequence. Unlike autoregressive LLMs, they expose a trajectory of intermediate hidden states at each denoising step. Safety monitoring for D-LLMs is almost entirely unexplored: prior defenses are either alignment fine-tuning (expensive, hurts utility) or decoding-time intervention (requires regeneration, reduces throughput). External probe-based monitors — the lightweight, non-intrusive alternative used in production AR-LLM systems like Google Gemini — have not been adapted to the multi-step D-LLM setting. The specific problem is that standard single-step probing discards the trajectory, missing safety-relevant signal, while naive full-trajectory methods (mean pooling, majority vote, LSTM, attention over all steps) either waste compute or do not reliably outperform single-step probes. The gap is: how to extract the incremental safety information embedded in the denoising trajectory without incurring the cost of processing every step for every sample. Key Idea The core insight is that a linear safety probe applied step-by-step along the D-LLM's denoising trajectory will repeatedly cross or approach its decision boundary on hard samples — particularly adversarially crafted prompts — while easy samples produce stable, high-margin trajectories from early steps onward. Counting the number of steps where the signed margin |d_s| < τ (hesitation severity n_τ) is a fine-grained, monotonic predictor of per-sample probe failure: F1 drops from ~90% at n_τ=0 to 55–77% at the largest n_τ buckets, a ~30-point spread. Critically, this probe-intrinsic signal dominates probe-extrinsic signals (token entropy, confidence) because it directly measures the classifier's own uncertainty, not the generative model's. This motivates training a specialized second-stage probe only on hesitant trajectories and activating it only when hesitation is detected at test time, decoupling accuracy from average cost. Methodology D²-Monitor operates in three stages. (1) Out-of-fold hesitation scoring: The training set is split into k folds; a linear probe trained on k−1 folds scores each held-out fold to obtain leakage-free signed margins {d_s} per sample. Steps with |d_s| < τ are flagged as hesitation steps; trajectories with n_τ > 0 form the hesitation subset. (2) Dual probe training: A linear base probe is trained on all training trajectories using cross-entropy over mean-pooled representations. An advanced probe (MLP: two-layer with hidden dim 256; or TimeAttn: additive attention over the hesitation window followed by an MLP) is trained only on hesitation trajectories, using only hidden states within the minimal contiguous hesitation window W^(i) as input. (3) Cascade inference: At test time, the base probe computes step-wise margins and counts n_τ. If n_τ ≤ λ, the majority-vote prediction from the linear probe is returned. If n_τ > λ, the hesitation window is extracted and the advanced probe classifies. Two critical design decisions are well-justified: choosing probe-margin over entropy/confidence (13.5% accuracy gap in the routed subset vs. 7.2% for entropy); and operating the advanced probe on the hesitation window rather than the full trajectory (supported by persistence analysis showing hesitation clusters in contiguous segments with slow temporal decay). Key Results Intra-dataset (WildGuardMix, 86.8k train / 1.7k test prompts): D²-MLP achieves 89.9%/89.7% Acc/F1 on LLaDA-8B-Instruct at ≤0.21M E[P], versus the best non-cascade baseline TimeAttn at 87.9%/87.5% with 1.59M parameters and LSTM at 87.8%/87.4% with 2.57M parameters. On ToxicChat (5.08k train / 5.08k test), D²-TimeAttn achieves 97.3%/89.9% Acc/F1 vs. LSTM 97.0%/88.9%. Cross-dataset (train WildGuardMix → test ToxicChat/OpenAI-Moderation): D²-MLP achieves 90.4%/75.0% Acc/F1 on LLaDA-8B-Instruct / ToxicChat vs. TimeAttn 88.9%/73.1% — a meaningful gain. However, on LLaDA-8B-Base / ToxicChat, D²-TimeAttn F1 71.0 vs. TimeAttn F1 70.8, essentially tied. Seed robustness on LLaDA-8B-Instruct (5 seeds): D²-MLP 89.3±0.1 F1 vs. best baseline 88.6±0.3. FLOPs: D²-MLP requires 0.68–0.97 MFLOPs per sample, compared to 35.7 MFLOPs for TimeAttn and 163 MFLOPs for LSTM. Inference time for D²-MLP is 0.56–0.66ms vs. 317–347ms for LSTM. A notable finding: the fraction of adversarial prompts in the routed subset reaches 86.3% for LLaDA-8B-Base and 71.6% for LLaDA-8B-Instruct, versus a dataset baseline of ~47%, demonstrating that hesitation severity acts as an implicit adversarial detector. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The bi-level cascade concept is adapted from prior AR-LLM work (Constitutional Classifiers, McKenzie et al. 2025), but D²-Monitor is the first to define and operationalize hesitation severity as a D-LLM-specific routing signal, the first to train the second-stage probe exclusively on hesitation trajectories using the hesitation window as input, and the first to show that probe-margin hesitation counts predict adversarial content. The advanced probe replacing an external LLM (as in AR-LLM cascades) with a lightweight parametric probe is a genuine contribution enabling sub-1M-parameter operation. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations cover: routing signal choice (margin vs. entropy vs. confidence), hesitation threshold sensitivity (τ varied to produce 30%–70% hesitation ratio with F1 variation <1 point), robustness to step length (1–8) and generation length (16–128) without retraining, and robustness across three remasking strategies (low-confidence, entropy, random). Seed robustness is reported on one model/dataset combination (5 seeds). Standard deviations are low (±0.1–0.4). No code or probe weights are released. All datasets and base models are publicly available. OOF scoring is methodologically careful. One concern: the hesitation signal discovery, validation, and routing are all evaluated on the same LLaDA model family trained with the same masked diffusion framework; generalization to D-LLMs with different architectures (e.g., continuous diffusion variants) is untested. For cross-dataset transfer to OpenAI-Moderation (no training split), 10% of test data is used for hyperparameter tuning — valid but worth noting. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A40 (48GB VRAM) for training and adds negligible post-generation overhead (<1ms per sample for D²-MLP); deploys alongside any D-LLM without modifying its weights or decoding procedure. - Efficient: D²-MLP at 0.68–0.97 MFLOPs and 0.56–0.66ms per sample matches single-step probe cost, achieving 2.4–6.6× speedup over MLP (Mean) and 35–150× over LSTM/TimeAttn at equal or higher accuracy; E[P] ≤0.36M parameters is 0.004% of LLaDA-8B. - Effective: Demonstrated F1 improvements of 1.7–2.7 points intra-dataset and 1.3–1.9 points cross-dataset over the strongest baseline; the adversarial enrichment analysis gives a concrete semantics beyond accuracy metrics. Cross-dataset margins are narrow in some cells (LLaDA-8B-Base / ToxicChat F1 difference: 0.2 points). - Scalable: Tested on models up to 16B parameters (LLaDA-2.0-mini-16B); no experiment at larger scale. The authors claim the trend extends to 100B-class models but provide no evidence. The hesitation window mechanism scales with denoising steps, not sequence length, which is favorable, but this is claimed not tested. No software artifact or trained model weights are publicly released. Strengths - The mechanistic grounding is rigorous: rather than asserting that multi-step trajectory helps and stopping there, the paper establishes a fine-grained, monotonic relationship between hesitation severity and probe failure probability (up to ~30% F1 gap), then connects this to the adversarial enrichment in the routed subset — making the cascade interpretable as a targeted adversarial detector. - The out-of-fold scoring for hesitation identification is a careful design choice that avoids optimistic bias in identifying hard training samples; it directly prevents the common pitfall in cascade training where difficulty is estimated on the very data the advanced probe will see. - The efficiency advantage is analytically verified via FLOPs derivation and empirically confirmed via wall-clock timing, not just reported as a secondary observation — D²-MLP at 0.7–1.0 MFLOPs vs. 163 MFLOPs for LSTM is a 150× reduction. - The temporal persistence analysis (showing hesitation clusters in contiguous segments with persistence probability well above the unconditional baseline for up to 30 steps) mechanistically justifies the hesitation-window design, adding depth beyond empirical validation. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly state: (1) experiments are limited to D-LLMs with ≤16B parameters due to computational constraints, and (2) activation monitors are vulnerable to adaptive adversaries who craft prompts to suppress hesitation steps, evading the second-stage probe. They recommend pairing D²-Monitor with complementary defenses. These limitations are confronted directly and honestly — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - All four D-LLMs tested (LLaDA-8B-Base, LLaDA-8B-Instruct, LLaDA-1.5, LLaDA-2.0-mini) share the same masked diffusion architecture from one research group. The paper provides no evidence that hesitation severity generalizes to architecturally distinct D-LLMs (e.g., Mercury, MDLM, or continuous-diffusion variants). - No comparison to any LLM-as-monitor baseline (Llama-Guard, ShieldGemma). The paper positions itself against probe-based baselines only, which are all trained on the same data and architecture. Practitioners deciding whether to use D²-Monitor vs. a lightweight LLM-as-monitor have no cost-quality tradeoff data. - The claimed "consistent" cross-dataset superiority is overstated: on LLaDA-8B-Base / ToxicChat, D²-TimeAttn F1 71.0 vs. TimeAttn F1 70.8 (0.2-point difference, within noise), while D²-MLP F1 70.7 is below TimeAttn F1 70.8 — the method does not win in this cell. - No adaptive attack experiment. The authors' security threat model (adversary suppresses hesitation to evade the heavy probe) is plausible and well-articulated, but the absence of even a preliminary evaluation (e.g., gradient-based optimization targeting hesitation counts) means the robustness claim against this specific threat is entirely unverified. - Layer selection for hidden state extraction is not ablated in the main paper; the choice of extraction layer may substantially affect hesitation patterns and is a design decision with no guidance provided for practitioners adapting the method to other D-LLMs. Bottom Line D²-Monitor makes a concrete, well-supported contribution to an understudied problem — external safety monitoring for diffusion LLMs — with an honest mechanistic story and strong efficiency gains; practitioners building D-LLM safety pipelines should read it, while treating the security robustness and scalability claims as forward-looking rather than evidenced.
Recommended 2605.25893 PDF
#76 — Innovative (64)

Adaptive KV Cache Reuse for Fast Long-Context LLM Serving

Fei li, Song Liu, Yan Liu, Jinhua Cui, Shiqiang Nie, Jinyu Wang, Weiguo Wu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces CacheTune, a serving system that recovers cross-chunk attention in non-prefix KV cache reuse by selectively recomputing the top-r% of low-frequency-dominant tokens per layer while sparsely transferring the rest from an external cache pool, and adaptively tuning r per storage tier via a roofline-warmstarted golden-section search. - Why it's important: Long-context LLM inference for RAG and multi-document workloads assembles prompts from independently precomputed chunks, making the Time-To-First-Token (TTFT) the dominant cost; naive chunk-KV concatenation silently degrades generation quality, and naive offloading to slow storage introduces a new I/O bottleneck that existing methods do not address jointly. - How it does it: KV cache tensors exhibit energy concentration in low-frequency spectral components; an offline real-FFT pass identifies the top-r tokens by low-frequency L2 norm energy, and online inference recomputes only those tokens in global context while async CUDA streams transfer the remaining (1−r)N KVs and apply deferred RoPE alignment. - The good: At r=15%, CacheTune retains 94.8% average generation quality across 5 datasets and 3 models while achieving 3.72×–4.86× TTFT speedup and 3.93×–6.21× throughput improvement over full recompute, outperforming 7 baselines including CacheBlend and CacheSlide. - The missing: The evaluation is bounded to 7B–32B models on 2 GPU platforms with no batching analysis; whether the low-frequency energy characterization and the r_min=15% quality lower bound hold at 70B+ scale or for reasoning-heavy tasks is untested. - Read it or not: Systems researchers building LLM serving stacks for RAG or multi-document summarization should read this; ML practitioners focused on model accuracy can skip it as the contribution is entirely in the inference serving layer.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (weights 15/15/10/15/15/30). Non-Obviousness (75) and Novelty (72) are the strengths: the frequency-domain insight — that KV low-frequency spectral energy encodes the cross-chunk dependencies disrupted by non-prefix reuse, and that a single offline index jointly drives quality recovery and I/O reduction — is genuinely novel and well-grounded empirically. Practical Impact (58) pulls the composite down hardest under systems weighting: no code is released, the system is not packaged for deployment, and CXL extensibility is claimed but unvalidated. Rigor (62), Completeness (62), and Resilience (62) are limited by point-estimate-only results (no seeds/error bars), an out-of-distribution-adapted CacheSlide baseline, no multi-request batching analysis, a 32B scale ceiling, and an r_min=15% bound derived from a single model. Composite: 0.15*72+0.15*62+0.10*75+0.15*62+0.15*62+0.30*58 ≈ 64.
systems Novelty 72 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 75 Completeness 62 Resilience 62 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Modern long-context LLM workloads—RAG, agentic reasoning, multi-document QA, multi-turn summarization—assemble prompts by concatenating multiple independent text chunks. The prefill stage over these long prompts dominates TTFT (not per-token decoding latency). KV cache reuse can eliminate redundant prefill, but existing prefix-caching systems (vLLM PagedAttention, SGLang RadixAttention, ChunkAttention) handle only strict-prefix matches and cannot reuse independently precomputed document chunks. When independently encoded chunks are naively concatenated, each chunk's KV cache lacks cross-chunk attention dependencies ("cross-attention loss"), degrading the attention distribution and generation quality. Prior non-prefix approaches are incomplete: CacheBlend requires an irreducible full first-layer recomputation to identify high-KV-deviation tokens; EPIC uses heuristic attention-sink positions that miss semantically critical tokens in chunk interiors; KVLink requires model pretraining; CacheClip requires an auxiliary model and fine-tuning. Separately, as deployments scale, KV caches overflow GPU memory to CPU, SSD, and HDD, making cross-tier I/O a new bottleneck that these methods ignore entirely. Key Idea KV cache tensors, treated as sequences, have energy strongly concentrated in low-frequency spectral components: the bottom 20% of the frequency spectrum accounts for the vast majority of total energy across multiple models and datasets. These low-frequency components encode the global contextual dependencies that are disrupted by non-prefix reuse, while high-frequency components are relatively unaffected. Consequently, recomputing only the tokens with the highest low-frequency energy scores—as measured by the L2 norm of the inverse-FFT-reconstructed KV—in the true global context is sufficient to restore near-full-recompute attention quality at a fraction of the total recomputation cost. This insight is both training-free and model-agnostic, and the same per-token importance index simultaneously determines which KV pairs to skip during external cache transfer, reducing I/O volume proportionally. Methodology CacheTune operates in two stages. Offline: For each reusable KV chunk, apply real-valued FFT (rFFT) along the sequence dimension to K and V tensors at each layer, apply a low-pass filter retaining the lowest α=0.5 fraction of spectral bins, apply inverse rFFT to reconstruct, and compute per-token importance as the average L2 norm of reconstructed K and V (Eqs. 5–6). TopK selection at ratio r yields a per-layer recomputation index set I_freq. This costs ~0.69ms per 3K-token chunk on GPU and is reused across requests. Online: Three CUDA streams run concurrently: the default forward compute stream, a Transfer stream that asynchronously prefetches only the complementary (1−r)N KV pairs from the external cache pool, and a Recompute stream that recomputes the rN selected tokens in the full global context and applies deferred RoPE with true global positions. Layers are categorized into collection, check (sparse attention on selected tokens), and fusion layers (scatter-fuse transferred and recomputed KVs into a unified per-layer buffer). Positional consistency is handled by storing pre-RoPE keys in the cache pool and applying RoPE online; this is folded into the async recompute path with no serial overhead. Hardware-aware r selection: Per-layer TTFT is modeled as max(r·N·t_c, (1−r)·N·t_i) + t_o (Eq. 9), yielding an analytical optimum r_0 = t_i/(t_c + t_i) (Eq. 11). This is warm-started into a golden-section search (GSS) over a 5–10 sample calibration set to account for nonlinear DMA startup and pipeline bubbles. Total one-time calibration cost is 3–4 minutes per hardware/storage configuration. Quality lower bound r_min=15% is imposed for high-bandwidth media to prevent accuracy collapse. Three design decisions stand out: (1) offline index computation avoids HKVD-style online first-layer overhead and enables I/O reduction by discarding to-be-recomputed KVs from cache transfer—well-justified. (2) Low-frequency selection over high-frequency or random—validated by ablation at identical r=15%: high-frequency and random selections fail to restore cross-attention, low-frequency selection substantially recovers it. (3) The r_min=15% lower bound is empirically derived from a quality-vs-r sweep on Mistral; adequate justification for that model but not extensively validated across other architectures. Key Results Models: Mistral v0.3-7B, Llama 3-8B, Qwen 2.5-32B. Datasets: SAMSum, MuSiQue, WikiMQA, HotpotQA, Multi-News (metrics: ROUGE-L and F1). Hardware: 2× A100-40GB (PCIe Gen3) with HDD offload (~205 MB/s read) and 2× RTX 4090-24GB with SSD offload (~535 MB/s read). Baselines: 7 methods including Full Recompute, Full KV Reuse, vLLM Prefix Caching, CacheBlend (r=15%), EPIC (first 16 tokens), CacheSlide (adapted), and KVLink (5 link tokens). Main result (CPU cache pool, r=15%): CacheTune achieves 3.72×–4.86× TTFT speedup over full recompute and retains 94.8% average generation quality. Throughput improvement: 3.93×–6.21× vs. full recompute (CacheBlend: 2.71×–5.16×; CacheSlide: 3.79×–5.94×). CPU offload pipeline outperforms GPU-resident cache on some tasks (Table 2: SAMSum TTFT 0.0817s CPU vs. 0.0899s GPU for CacheTune). CacheTune reduces TTFT 25.3% below CacheBlend and 11.9% below CacheSlide in CPU offload setting, and 74.4% below vLLM Prefix Caching. Adaptive recomputation: fixed r=15% on HDD/SSD gives only 1.92×/2.05× speedup; adaptive r* (36.4% HDD, 30.9% SSD) raises this to 2.36×/2.34×. Counterintuitive finding: CPU-offloaded CacheTune achieves lower TTFT than GPU-resident reuse due to sparse transfer benefits. Frequency ablation: at r=15%, low-frequency selection substantially outperforms both high-frequency and random selection on every tested model/dataset combination. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The frequency-domain importance scoring for non-prefix KV cache reuse is genuinely novel: no prior system characterizes cross-attention recovery in terms of spectral energy concentration. The hardware-aware roofline model for recomputation-ratio tuning across heterogeneous storage tiers is a practical and original engineering contribution. The underlying techniques (FFT, low-pass filtering, golden-section search) are standard; the novelty lies in their combined application to this serving problem. The use of the same offline index set to jointly drive semantic recovery and I/O volume reduction is a clean co-design insight absent from prior work. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and targeted: frequency selection strategy (low vs. high vs. random at fixed r), recomputation ratio sweep (r=5%–25%), storage tier comparison (GPU/CPU/SSD/HDD), and adaptive vs. fixed ratio under I/O-bound conditions. Error bars, significance tests, and multiple seeds are absent; all reported numbers are point estimates. No public code repository is mentioned. The comparison with CacheSlide uses an author-adapted version for a different use case than CacheSlide was designed for (RPDC agent prompts vs. independently precomputed RAG chunks), introducing uncertainty about whether the baseline is optimally configured. The KVLink configuration (5 link tokens) may not match the parameters that yield peak KVLink accuracy. The 94.8% quality retention figure is an average; per-dataset variance is visible in Figure 7 scatter plots but not quantified numerically. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.24022v1, May 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS (built system, evaluated by deployment on specific hardware configurations). - Practical: Runs on commercially available hardware (A100, RTX 4090); requires GPU+external storage setup standard in LLM serving infrastructure. - Efficient: Offline index construction costs ~0.69ms per 3K-token chunk; online transfers only (1−r)N KVs per layer; one-time 3–4 minute calibration per hardware/storage configuration; deferred RoPE adds no serial latency. - Effective: At r=15%, recovers 94.8% of full-recompute quality with 3.72–4.86× TTFT speedup; ablations confirm the frequency-selection mechanism is the decisive factor. Evidence directly addresses the stated cross-attention loss problem. - Scalable: Tested only on 7B–32B models; no experiments at 70B+ scale. Batching behavior under concurrent multi-request workloads is not analyzed. The claim of CXL extensibility is stated but not experimentally supported. The system is implemented in PyTorch/CUDA (PyTorch 2.2.1, CUDA 12.4) with custom CUDA stream management, but no code is publicly released. Strengths - The observation that KV cache low-frequency components encode global dependencies most disrupted by non-prefix reuse is well-grounded empirically (Figure 2 energy spectra, Figure 3 attention heatmaps) and conceptually connected to prior signal processing and image compression literature.
