Strengths - Fills a genuine, quantified gap: 14,056 research-level problems, MIT-licensed, orders of magnitude larger than any prior training-ready research-level corpus, with verified self-containment and resolution status metadata. - The finding that wrong-but-reasonable trajectories filtered for behavioral failures (fake citations, non-attempts) yield useful training signal without ground-truth solutions is practically important and provides a scalable path for future frontier data curation. - The behavioral analysis of citation hallucination across 8 models and 5 benchmarks is thorough and methodologically layered (rule-based counters + LLM judge + search-agent verification), and the cross-family trend that 2026-generation models hallucinate markedly more citations than 2025-generation predecessors is a surprising and practically actionable finding. - The two-stage agentic curation pipeline—with explicit self-containment auditing, resolution-status tagging, and conservative deduplication—is a careful engineering contribution with detailed prompt documentation (Tables 11–17) that enables replication. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The paper directly acknowledges that the 5,000-trace training set is budget-constrained and defers larger-scale tests to future work. It concedes that trajectories in RESEARCHMATH-REASONING are unlikely to be correct and that verifying correctness at research level is not scalable. It notes that conservative deduplication may remove distinct but closely related problems (and mitigates this by releasing the raw seed set). The paper calls for future work to clarify when correct traces remain necessary for reliable proof behavior. These are confronted honestly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The difficulty Elo comparison relies entirely on GPT-5-mini as judge without human mathematician validation; LLM-perceived difficulty likely conflates unfamiliar notation and surface complexity with genuine mathematical hardness, and the 400-Elo margin may overstate the gap. - No fine-tuning evaluation on held-out RESEARCHMATH-14K problems is presented; all evaluation benchmarks (AIME, HLE, SOOHAK) are external, making it impossible to distinguish transferable research-level reasoning from stylistic adaptation to the open-problem register. - The DASD-Thinking control is described as a format control, but its problem difficulty distribution relative to RESEARCHMATH-REASONING is not characterized—the observed gain over DASD in research-level benchmarks could partly reflect better difficulty matching rather than research-level content specifically. - The 94.2% self-containment rate is measured by an LLM judge (Codex), not by human mathematicians; for problems requiring highly specialized notation (Chow rings, Koras-Russell cubics, Schrodinger operators), automated self-containment assessment may substantially overestimate practical usability. - The hypothesis that agentic RL training with search tools causes the increase in citation hallucination across model generations is plausible and worth highlighting, but no causal evidence or ablation is offered—it remains speculation. - No analysis of training signal quality by problem status (open vs. partially solved vs. solved): solved problems with known solutions may provide stronger supervision, and the 8.3% solved problems in the corpus are never separated out in the fine-tuning experiments. Bottom Line A practitioner building training pipelines for frontier mathematical reasoning gains substantially from this paper: the dataset fills a real gap, the behavioral analysis of citation hallucination across model generations is a useful and surprising diagnostic, and the demonstration that imperfect research-level trajectories provide useful supervision over olympiad baselines opens a concrete path for scaling frontier math training data.
Recommended 2605.28003 PDF
#94 — Innovative (62)

Pantheon360: Taming Digital Twin Generation via 3D-Aware 360° Video Diffusion (16 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Pantheon360, a framework that generates temporally consistent, camera-trajectory-controlled 360° video from sparse or single 360° input images by conditioning a fine-tuned video diffusion model on an explicit 3D point-cloud cache rendered along the target path. - Why it's important: Perspective video generators hallucinate unseen geometry when traversing long or multi-directional camera paths, producing cross-view inconsistencies that make them unreliable for digital-twin and autonomous-agent simulation pipelines that need globally coherent scene representations. - How it does it: A 3D point cloud (the "3D Cache") is reconstructed at inference time from perspective crops of the 360° input using a 3D foundation model (PI3/VGGT), then rendered into an equirectangular geometry-only video Vgeo whose VAE-encoded latent is concatenated with the noisy generation latent at every diffusion step to enforce geometric consistency, while CLIP features from 8 perspective crops provide semantic conditioning via cross-attention. - The good: On the Web360 benchmark (100 in-the-wild outdoor sequences), Pantheon360 reaches PSNR 22.84, SSIM 0.746, LPIPS 0.065, and MET3R 0.284, versus the next-best baseline GEN3C at 20.73 PSNR, SSIM 0.583, LPIPS 0.145, and MET3R 0.350 — a consistent, multi-metric margin across both pixel quality and 3D geometric consistency. - The missing: All perspective baselines are adapted to 360° by feeding them the same Vgeo scaffold in 8 crops rather than in their native conditioning format, which may understate their potential; there is no quantitative comparison against the only native 360° controllable baseline (CamPVG), and no user study. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on scene generation, digital twins, or camera-controlled video synthesis who need a practical, in-the-wild 360° solution; practitioners building autonomous-driving or robotics simulation pipelines will find the Street View chaining and video stabilization applications directly applicable.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (65) is the strongest dimension: causally-grounded ablations (progressive 3D-Cache point-dropping shows PSNR 22.84→16.93 when Vgeo is removed), evaluation on MET3R that measures the geometric-consistency claim directly, multi-metric wins on two benchmarks, and in-the-wild data. Completeness (65) is helped by direct confrontation of failure cases (crowded/dynamic scenes, stitching artifacts). Novelty (62) is real but a combination/domain-transfer of the established 3D-cache conditioning paradigm to the 360° setting rather than a new primitive. Resilience (62) is dented because perspective baselines are adapted to 360° in a potentially unfavorable form and the only native 360° competitor (CamPVG) is compared qualitatively only; Impact (58) reflects single-A100 feasibility but no announced release and slow (199s/25-frame) inference. Weighted: 0.20*62 + 0.25*65 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*65 + 0.15*62 + 0.10*58 = 62.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 65 Non-Obv 58 Completeness 65 Resilience 62 Impact 58
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Building digital twins for simulation — particularly for autonomous driving and embodied AI — requires generating photorealistic video along arbitrary camera trajectories while maintaining global spatial coherence across all viewpoints. Perspective video generators are ill-suited for this because their narrow field-of-view forces long trajectories that repeatedly revisit geometry from new angles, causing temporal drift and cross-view contradictions each time the model must hallucinate unseen areas. Existing 360° generation approaches either limit control to coarse actions ("move forward," GenEX) or validate only on synthetic data (CamPVG), leaving a gap: precise, user-defined camera trajectory control for in-the-wild 360° content. Key Idea: The central insight is task decomposition: rather than asking a single neural network to jointly reason about 3D geometry and photorealistic appearance, Pantheon360 delegates geometric consistency entirely to an explicit 3D point-cloud cache reconstructed at inference time, and then asks the diffusion model only to perform texture and appearance synthesis conditioned on that deterministic geometric scaffold. This decoupling means the diffusion model never has to hallucinate structure — it sees exactly what geometry will appear at each frame before generation. Methodology: At inference, sparse 360° input frames are converted into 16 perspective crops (8 horizontal at 0° pitch + 8 floor-directed at 60° pitch, sampled every 45° in yaw) and fed into PI3 or VGGT to produce a dense point cloud with confidence scores. The cloud is filtered by confidence threshold (0.25), edge discontinuities (relative tolerance 0.03), and sky masking, then merged into a unified 3D Cache. Given a user-defined camera trajectory Ctarget, the cache is rendered in equirectangular projection (ERP) at 1024×512 to produce a geometry-only video Vgeo ∈ ℝ^{T×3×H'×W'}. This is passed through the frozen SVD VAE encoder to produce latent vequi, which is concatenated channel-wise with the noisy ground-truth latent at each of 25 denoising steps — the critical design choice that injects hard geometric constraints into the generation process. Simultaneously, CLIP features from 8 perspective crops of the first frame are concatenated and injected via cross-attention. The base model is SVD (initialized from Argus, which initialized from SVD-I2V-XL), fine-tuned with the standard diffusion loss λ(t)||ε − fθ(yequi,t, t, vequi, cimg)||²₂ at 512×1024 ERP resolution for 50K iterations on 4 A100 GPUs (~5 days each). Training data comes from the 360-1M dataset filtered to ~55K clips; camera poses and 3D caches are auto-annotated on-the-fly using ViPE (SLAM-based 360° pose estimation), with ground-truth trajectory set equal to the SLAM-estimated pose so the model trains to reproduce the actual video conditioned on its own geometry. A dual-anchor variant adds a second anchor frame by running forward and backward denoising passes conditioned on start and end frames respectively, averaging their latents at each step (latent fusion from Time Reversal Fusion), to enable interpolation. Key design decisions: (1) using perspective crops for CLIP because equirectangular distortion degrades CLIP features — well-justified; (2) using SLAM feature points rather than dense depth as the 3D Cache to ensure the model trusts the geometric condition — reasonable but not ablated directly; (3) not using the noise injection refinement from TRF to maintain inference speed — a deliberate tradeoff noted but not quantified. Key Results: On the Web360 dataset (100 in-the-wild outdoor sequences, single view-to-video): Pantheon360 achieves FVD 356.2, SSIM 0.746, PSNR 22.84, LPIPS 0.065, MET3R 0.284, versus GEN3C (best baseline) at FVD 380.1, SSIM 0.583, PSNR 20.73, LPIPS 0.145, MET3R 0.350. On Habitat (50 synthetic indoor sequences, sparse views-to-video): Pantheon360 achieves FVD 450.7, SSIM 0.756, PSNR 20.39, LPIPS 0.091, MET3R 0.303, versus GEN3C at FVD 511.0, SSIM 0.481, PSNR 17.31, LPIPS 0.195, MET3R 0.452. The 3D Cache ablation (progressively dropping cache points) shows monotonic degradation: removing Vgeo entirely degrades PSNR from 22.84 to 16.93 on Web360, confirming the geometric conditioning carries most of the quality gain. The interpolation ablation shows that Dual+Latent Fusion (28.95 PSNR) substantially outperforms Single (20.92 PSNR) and approaches the end-frame alignment quality of direct dual conditioning (27.86 PSNR) while maintaining better temporal consistency. Inference on a single A100 at 1024×512 takes 199s for 25 frames (34s reconstruction + 2s render + 163s diffusion, 30 GB), and 1365s for 100-frame long trajectories (41 GB). Baselines (ViewCrafter, TrajectoryCrafter, GEN3C) are adapted to 360° by feeding them the same Vgeo rendered into 8 perspective crops — they are current perspective-video methods, not native 360° competitors. The only native 360° controlled baseline (CamPVG) is compared qualitatively only. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuine novelty is extending the 3D-cache geometric-conditioning paradigm (established in perspective video generation by methods like GEN3C, ReconX) to the 360°/equirectangular domain with in-the-wild training and on-the-fly SLAM annotation. The individual components — SVD fine-tuning, point-cloud rendering as conditioning, latent fusion for interpolation — are each established; the contribution is their combination for the 360° controllable generation setting and the training pipeline that makes it robust to real-world data. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations are present for both the key component (3D Cache, Tables 4–5, progressive point dropping) and the dual-anchor mechanism (Table 3, four variants). Error bars and significance tests are not reported; multiple seeds are not mentioned. No code or model weights are released (a project page is provided at the URL listed in the abstract, but no GitHub repository is cited in the paper). The training data pipeline relies on ViPE for auto-annotation, but the quality of those annotations on the training set is not directly evaluated — if ViPE fails on a scene type systematically, the model may learn to ignore the geometric condition in those cases. Evaluation on Web360 uses 100 randomly sampled sequences and Habitat uses 50, which is small enough that variance could shift rankings. The baselines are adapted to 360° by giving them Vgeo in cropped perspective form — this is a reasonable but potentially unfavorable adaptation; it does not represent the baselines at their native best. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL (system built and evaluated with quantitative experiments). - Practical: Runs on a single A100 GPU (30–41 GB), which is commercially available; not deployable on consumer-grade hardware without optimization. - Efficient: Single-view 25-frame generation takes 199s total; diffusion denoising dominates at 163s (80% of wall time). This is 3–8× slower than real-time and not yet suitable for interactive or online simulation loops. - Effective: The method demonstrably improves geometric consistency (MET3R) and perceptual quality (LPIPS, SSIM) on two benchmarks over three adapted baselines; the 3D Cache ablation provides causal evidence that the geometric conditioning is the source of the gain. - Scalable: Long-trajectory synthesis (100 frames, 5 sparse inputs) is demonstrated via sequential chaining, but 3D Cache memory and reconstruction time scale with the number of input views; behavior at truly large-scale (full building interiors, multi-block street networks) is not tested. The paper does not test or claim 10×–100× trajectory length extrapolation. The work delivers a trained system (weights and pipeline), but no public release is announced. Strengths: - The task decomposition (geometry to 3D Cache, appearance to diffusion) is clean and causally validated — the progressive cache-dropping ablation (Tables 4–5) gives the strongest evidence that the geometric scaffold, not just fine-tuning on 360° data, drives the performance gap. - Training with on-the-fly SLAM annotation from unlabeled 360° video (360-1M) is a practical contribution: it removes the dependency on ground-truth camera poses in training data, which are scarce for in-the-wild 360° content. - Evaluating on MET3R (multi-view geometric consistency) alongside standard pixel metrics (PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS, FVD) is appropriate and non-trivial — it measures what the paper actually claims to improve. - The dual-anchor latent fusion for interpolation is a well-motivated addition that addresses a concrete failure mode (end-frame divergence when 3D Cache quality is poor), and the ablation in Table 3 cleanly isolates its contribution. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge that explicit control over object-level dynamics is not supported: the 3D Cache encodes only static scene geometry, and dynamic motion relies entirely on the diffusion model's learned priors. They also acknowledge failure cases in crowded dynamic scenes (motion blur artifacts) and inputs with stitching artifacts (artifact propagation). These are confronted directly in Section D.4 with visual examples, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The perspective baselines (ViewCrafter, TrajectoryCrafter, GEN3C) are adapted to 360° by splitting Vgeo into 8 perspective crops and using those as their 3D-aware condition — this is not the modality these models were designed for, and the adaptation may underestimate their capability. A fairer comparison would adapt them using their native conditioning (e.g., ERP latent concatenation as GEN3C is designed for). - CamPVG, the only concurrent method that also attempts exact trajectory control for 360° video, is compared only qualitatively (not on the same benchmarks), so it is impossible to assess whether Pantheon360's advantage over the one truly comparable prior is real or methodologically convenient. - Both main quantitative benchmarks are small (100 and 50 test sequences) and domain-specific (Web360 is predominantly outdoor, Habitat is indoor synthetic). Performance on indoor real-world scenes, dynamic urban environments, or scenes with large sky coverage is not quantified. - The 3D Cache reconstruction relies on PI3, which was designed for perspective images; the sliding-crop approach introduces seam artifacts at crop boundaries. The point-cloud filtering (confidence 0.25, edge tolerance 0.03) is empirically chosen but not ablated. - Inference time (199s for 25 frames) is not compared against baselines — if GEN3C or TrajectoryCrafter run in 20s, the practical tradeoff changes the recommendation for production use. - No user study evaluating perceptual realism, trajectory adherence, or temporal coherence is provided, which matters for downstream simulation tasks where psychophysical plausibility may diverge from PSNR. Bottom Line: Pantheon360 delivers a concrete and practically useful advance — precise trajectory-controlled 360° video generation from sparse inputs that works on real-world data — and the evidence for its geometric consistency advantage is causally grounded; researchers building digital-twin pipelines for robotics or autonomous-driving simulation should read it, though the comparison to the most relevant concurrent baseline (CamPVG) remains incomplete.

#95 — Innovative (61)

From Pixels to Words -- Towards Native One-Vision Models at Scale (56 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 28 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces NEO-ov, an encoder-free, monolithic vision-language model that unifies single-image understanding, multi-image reasoning, video comprehension, and spatial intelligence within a single autoregressive backbone trained end-to-end from raw pixels. - Why it's important: Modular VLMs (encoder + adapter + LLM) compress visual signals before reasoning begins, fragmenting pixel-level cues across frames and precluding KV-cache reuse for long video; a competitive native alternative removes these structural bottlenecks. - How it does it: NEO-ov replaces visual encoders with a two-layer convolutional patch embedder, decomposes attention heads into separate T/H/W axes with a THW-aware Native-RoPE, and applies a mixed attention mask that is bidirectional within each visual unit but causal across units, letting spatial and temporal reasoning emerge jointly from patch-level tokens at the earliest layers. - The good: NEO-ov (9B) scores 90.0 on the Mindcube spatial reasoning benchmark — surpassing the prior specialized 8B leader (Sensenova-SI, 85.7) and dominating general-purpose modular 8B models (InternVL3.5: 40.4, Qwen3-VL: 29.6) by a wide margin, providing the strongest single evidence that native architectures have a structural advantage in fine-grained spatial tasks. - The missing: The ablation study (Figure 4) reports only coarse aggregate-task accuracy bars comparing Pre-Buffer against image/video encoders; there are no component-level ablations isolating the contribution of THW-decoupled heads, Native-RoPE, or the mixed attention mask independently, leaving the source of the spatial intelligence gains ambiguous. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building or evaluating encoder-free VLM architectures, especially those interested in spatial or multi-image reasoning; practitioners primarily focused on OCR-heavy document tasks will find modular systems still ahead and may not benefit.
Why this rank: Empirical profile (per Deployability tag), though architecturally a systems contribution. Novelty (72) and Non-Obviousness (70) carry it: the first encoder-free native VLM evaluated across all four input regimes, with a general 9B model beating spatial specialists on Mindcube (90.0 vs 85.7) being a surprising, hard-to-predict result. Rigor (52) is the limiting factor — the ablation reports only coarse aggregate bars with no isolation of THW heads, Native-RoPE, or the attention mask, no error bars, unconfirmed weight release, and heavy first-author overlap with the baseline lineage, leaving the source of the spatial gains ambiguous. Composite: 0.20*72 + 0.25*52 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*60 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*60 = 61.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 52 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 60 Resilience 55 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Current vision-language models rely on pretrained visual encoders (CLIP, SigLIP) that compress images into semantically filtered representations before any language reasoning occurs. This design imposes three structural costs: (1) image encoders optimize for static per-frame semantics and generalize poorly to video or interleaved multi-image inputs, while video encoders do the reverse; (2) the encode-then-project pipeline prevents KV caching of visual context in long videos; (3) balancing encoder and LLM capacity during scaling is delicate. These issues are particularly acute in spatial intelligence settings, where cross-view and cross-frame correspondences must be computed over compressed semantic features rather than native spatial signals. Prior native VLMs (Fuyu, EVE, NEO) addressed some of these constraints but remained evaluated only on single-image tasks or single modality extensions; no prior native model unified all four input regimes (single-image, multi-image, video, spatial) in one backbone. Key Idea: The central technical insight is that encoding images as flat sequences of 32×32 patch tokens and applying an attention mask that is bidirectional within each visual unit but causal across units allows the backbone to build dense intra-image spatial structure while still supporting ordered cross-image and cross-frame reasoning — without any encoder bottleneck. Combined with THW-decoupled attention head dimensions (T inherits the LLM's sequential modeling; H and W add dedicated 2D spatial axes) and a Native-RoPE that assigns independent spatial frequency bases (θ=10K) to image coordinates while preserving the LLM's temporal frequency (θ=1M), the backbone simultaneously handles arbitrary-resolution images, video frame sequences, and multi-image interleaving within one unified sequence. Methodology: NEO-ov encodes images via two convolutional layers (stride 16, then stride 2) with GELU activation and 2D RoPE positional embeddings, producing one visual token per 32×32 image region at arbitrary resolution; text uses the original LLM tokenizer. The backbone is a decoder-only transformer initialized from Qwen3-1.7B or Qwen3-8B, with pre-buffer layers (12 layers for 2B, 6 for 9B) prepended. Attention heads are partitioned into T, H, and W channels; the dot product score decomposes as s_ij = <q^T, k^T> + <q^H, k^H> + <q^W, k^W>, enabling additive spatial-temporal interaction without changing model width. The attention mask is defined as M_ij=1 iff j≤i or (ui=uj>0), achieving bidirectional spatial attention within units and causal attention across units and text. Training proceeds in three stages: (1) pre-training on 20M image-text pairs, freezing all but patch embedding, pre-buffer, and new QK parameters; (2) mid-training on ~60M single-image, multi-image, and video samples up to 40962 resolution and 128 frames, thawing all layers and extending context from 16K to 36K tokens; (3) supervised fine-tuning on ~6M high-quality instruction samples. Design decision justification: the choice to freeze LLM weights in Stage 1 is well-motivated for preserving language priors; the THW head decomposition is justified by ablation showing Pre-Buffer outperforms encoder-based alternatives, though the decomposition itself lacks a dedicated ablation. The context-length progressive schedule (16K→36K) is stated but not ablated. Key Results: On image understanding (2B scale), NEO-ov scores 54.7 on MMMU (vs. best native peer NEO: 48.6; best modular at same scale Qwen3-VL: 53.4), 54.5 on HallusionBench (vs. NEO: 43.1; InternVL3.5: 48.6), and 91.2 on DocVQA. At 9B scale, NEO-ov reaches 68.1 MMMU (matching InternVL3.5 9B: 68.1; near Qwen3-VL 9B: 69.6). On multi-image and video (9B), NEO-ov achieves 62.8 BLINK, 67.4 VideoMME, 70.7 MVBench — competitive with InternVL3.5-9B (66.0 VideoMME, 72.1 MVBench) and trailing Qwen3-VL-9B (71.4 VideoMME). Spatial intelligence is the strongest differentiator: at 8B, NEO-ov scores 90.0 on Mindcube, 64.8 VSI-Bench, and 61.7 3DSR, outperforming spatial-specialist Sensenova-SI (85.7, 68.8, 55.5) on Mindcube and 3DSR while trailing on VSI-Bench. OCR performance lags: OCRBench 81.6 (9B) vs. Qwen3-VL 89.6; TextVQA 78.5 vs. Qwen2.5-VL 84.9. Baselines include current-generation competitors (Qwen3-VL, InternVL3.5), making the comparisons credible but noting these are all preprint-era numbers. The dominant Mindcube result (margin: +4.3 over the best specialist) is the single most surprising finding, suggesting native architectures learn qualitatively different spatial representations. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The core contribution is the first native (encoder-free) VLM evaluated comprehensively across all four regimes — single-image, multi-image, video, and spatial intelligence — at competitive scale. The THW-decoupled RoPE and mixed bidirectional/causal attention mask are incremental extensions of the NEO architecture (same first author) rather than independently novel primitives; the novelty lies in their integration and in empirically validating that the resulting monolithic model is competitive with strong modular systems across diverse benchmarks. Rigor & Reproducibility: The ablation in Figure 4 compares pre-buffer against image and video encoders in four task categories but reports only aggregate bar charts without numerical values, statistical significance tests, or error bars. Figure 5 shows one comparison (NEO vs. InternVL3.5 vs. Qwen3-VL on SI fine-tuning) and Figure 6 shows stage-wise accuracy trends — both informative but at the same coarse level. No per-component ablation isolates the contribution of THW head decomposition, Native-RoPE, or the bidirectional/causal mask separately. No error bars or multi-seed results are reported. The GitHub repository is listed (https://github.com/EvolvingLMMs-Lab/NEO) but the paper does not explicitly state whether model weights or training code are released. This is an arXiv preprint (May 2026) and has not undergone peer review. The heavy first-author overlap with cited baselines (NEO, EVE, EVEv2) is noted, though these are legitimate predecessors. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The model runs on standard 80 GB A100/H100 GPUs (128 GPUs used for training); inference requires only the monolithic backbone with no encoder preprocessing, enabling simpler deployment and KV caching for video — practically feasible for research and production settings with appropriate hardware. - Efficient: The patch embedder replaces a heavy visual encoder (e.g., SigLIP-400M), reducing total parameter overhead; however, long-video inputs at 4096² resolution with 128 frames generate very large token sequences, and the per-token compute cost within the unified backbone is not compared against modular alternatives at equivalent FLOPs. - Effective: Evidence shows competitive or superior performance on spatial and reasoning benchmarks versus strong modular baselines, though OCR-heavy tasks (DocVQA, ChartQA) remain below modular systems with specialized OCR pretraining, indicating partial effectiveness. - Scalable: Tested at 2B and 9B parameters, up to 4096² resolution and 128-frame video at 36K context length; no experiments beyond 9B, no throughput or latency numbers versus encoder-based competitors at equivalent scale are reported, so scalability is claimed but not stress-tested. The paper delivers a trained model architecture and training recipe; code and weights availability are unconfirmed at submission. Strengths: - The spatial intelligence results are the most credibly novel finding: a general-purpose 9B native VLM outperforms dedicated spatial-specialist models (Sensenova-SI, Cambrian-S) on Mindcube and 3DSR with no spatial-specific architectural components, suggesting native pixel-level interaction genuinely encodes richer geometric structure than encoder-compressed representations. - The mixed attention formulation (bidirectional within visual units, causal across) is a clean, principled design that naturally generalizes from single-image to multi-image and video without additional modules, and the THW-decoupled RoPE preserves the LLM's inherited temporal reasoning while adding spatial capacity without increasing hidden dimension. - The three-stage training curriculum with selective parameter freezing in Stage 1 is well-motivated and achieves stable multimodal adaptation without catastrophic forgetting of language priors — an ongoing practical challenge in native VLM training. - At the 9B scale, NEO-ov matches modular InternVL3.5-9B on MMMU (68.1 vs. 68.1) and HallusionBench (59.8 vs. 54.5) without any visual encoder, providing concrete evidence that the performance gap to modular systems is narrowing at commercially relevant scales. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors acknowledge three limitations directly: (1) a residual performance gap versus top-tier modular systems (Qwen3-VL) on some single-image and video benchmarks, attributed to training data scale and quality; (2) OCR-intensive tasks remain underexplored due to lacking diverse, high-quality OCR supervision; (3) broader scaling potential (more parameters, more data, longer context) is untapped. The authors confront these honestly without minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The ablation suite is the most significant weakness: Figure 4 shows that Pre-Buffer beats both image and video encoders across all four task categories when randomly initialized, but this is presented as one coarse result without ablating individual design components (THW decomposition, mixed attention mask, Native-RoPE). The dominant Mindcube result (+4.3 pts vs. spatial specialists) warrants deeper analysis — Mindcube is a very recent benchmark with a small comparison pool, and it is unclear whether the advantage reflects the architecture or the training data mixture. The paper does not report inference latency or throughput comparisons against modular baselines at equivalent parameter counts, which matters for the efficiency claim. Training data composition (web sources, OCR content) is described qualitatively with no dataset-level breakdown or contamination analysis relative to held-out benchmarks. The heavy first-author overlap across the cited NEO/EVE lineage makes it difficult to assess independence of the comparison; a fair ablation starting from a modular baseline and surgically removing the encoder would strengthen the causal claims. Bottom Line: NEO-ov is the most capable native (encoder-free) VLM at both 2B and 9B scale, and its spatial intelligence results are genuinely surprising; researchers working on unified multimodal architectures or fine-grained spatial reasoning should read it, but the limited ablation depth means its architectural claims should be treated as hypotheses to verify rather than established facts.
Recommended 2605.28820 PDF
#96 — Innovative (61)

Divide-and-Conquer Inference for Large-Scale Visual Recognition with Multimodal Large Language Models

arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Divide-and-Conquer Inference (DCI), a training-free, test-time inference framework that recursively partitions large candidate label sets into K-sized subsets to recover accuracy in MLLMs when performing large-scale closed-set image classification. - Why it's important: MLLMs collapse to near-random performance when asked to classify among thousands of categories — LLaMA 3.2-11B-Vision drops from 88.60% accuracy at 20 classes to 0.53% at 1000 classes — making them unusable for large-scale visual recognition despite possessing the underlying visual knowledge. - How it does it: By splitting the candidate list into K-sized groups, running parallel MLLM inference on each group (model outputs either the best-matching class or "None"), pruning away "None" branches, and recursing on the survivors until one candidate remains — keeping the effective attention over label tokens within the model's discriminative bandwidth at each step. - The good: On ImageNet-21K (21,841 classes), DCI lifts Qwen2.5-VL-7B from 0.21% to 37.87% accuracy (+37.66 absolute points) with no fine-tuning, and an 8B open-source model with DCI (71.32% mean across four benchmarks) outperforms GPT-4(v4.1) (70.43%) — achieving this while simultaneously reducing per-image latency below the flat-inference baseline at K=500. - The missing: The key theoretical lemma (Attention Dilution) assumes a "bounded and non-dominant logit regime" that modern trained transformers routinely violate through sharp, peaked attention; additionally, no comparison against hierarchical or taxonomy-guided classification baselines (e.g., WordNet-structured coarse-to-fine search over ImageNet) is provided. - Read it or not: Worth reading for anyone deploying MLLMs on large label-space recognition tasks or studying test-time scaling strategies; the practical gains are large, reproducible across architectures, and the code is released, though the theory is more illustrative than rigorous.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Impact (75) and Non-Obviousness (70) lift the score — DCI is training-free, model-agnostic, code-released, and delivers large reproducible gains (+37.66pp on ImageNet-21K), and the counter-intuitive dual win (higher accuracy AND lower latency at scale) plus the grouping-strategy ablation are genuine insights. Rigor (55) and Resilience (50) are the limiting dimensions: the Attention Dilution lemma assumes a bounded-non-dominant-logit regime that trained transformers routinely violate, no hierarchical/WordNet taxonomy baseline is provided for the exact problem DCI solves, Table 5 reports cherry-picked 'best values over multiple runs' with no variance, and the false-negative error compounding across recursion levels is unquantified. A strong engineering contribution with suggestive rather than conclusive theory.
empirical Novelty 68 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 75
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation MLLMs are increasingly applied to traditional computer vision tasks including image classification, yet their performance degrades catastrophically when the candidate class set grows beyond a few hundred entries. The dominant prior explanation attributes poor MLLM classification to insufficient domain-specific training data. This paper contests that framing: the authors show empirically that LLaMA 3.2-11B-Vision, Gemma 3-4B, and the full Qwen2.5-VL series all exhibit sharp, consistent accuracy collapse when the number of candidate classes is varied from 10 to 1000 on ImageNet-1K — across architectures with very different training corpora. The collapse is therefore not dataset-specific but a structural property of flat closed-set prompting with large label lists. The root bottleneck is the prompt length itself: as the label list grows, every candidate class competes for limited attention bandwidth in the softmax mechanism, drowning the correct label in textual noise. Key Idea The core insight is that attention dilution — the O(1/K) decay in expected attention weight per candidate as K grows — is a fundamental consequence of softmax normalization over long token sequences. The fix is to never present more than K candidates simultaneously. Recursively partition the full candidate set into groups of size ≤K, let the model screen each group (selecting the best match or "None" if none fit), prune all "None" branches, and recurse until a unique candidate survives. This coarse-to-fine search acts as a dynamic attention mask: at each level the model sees only a tractable candidate set, preserving local signal-to-noise ratio while systematically compressing the search space via branch pruning. Methodology DCI executes three phases per recursion level. In the Divide phase, the current candidate set C_t of size n_t is uniformly partitioned into M = ⌈n_t/K⌉ disjoint sub-sequences of size ≤K. In the Conquer phase, each sub-sequence is processed in parallel: a structured four-component prompt (task instruction, anti-laziness decision rule, output-format constraint, one-shot exemplar) is combined with the image and sub-candidate list; the MLLM returns either the best-matching class name or "None." In the Combine phase, all "None" responses are discarded (branch pruning) to form the next-round candidate set C_{t+1}; recursion terminates when |C_{t+1}| ≤ K (direct final inference), |C_{t+1}| = 1 (return the surviving candidate), or |C_{t+1}| = 0 (return "None"). Maximum recursion depth is O(log_K N). The theoretical analysis formalizes this via a Markov chain model: Lemma 1 (Uncertainty Scaling) establishes H(Y) ≥ log K − log β via Shannon entropy; Lemma 2 (Attention Dilution) derives E[α_k] = O(1/K) by applying the LLN and delta method to softmax logits under a bounded-logit assumption; Theorem 1 (Information Deficit) combines these via Fano's inequality to give Pe(K) ≥ 1 − (I_max + log(2β))/log K. Complexity is derived as T(N, K) ≈ (N/K + log_K N − 1)·K², yielding a "DCI advantage region" for large N where DCI undercuts flat O(N²) inference. Three critical design decisions: (1) K as the sole hyperparameter controlling the accuracy-latency tradeoff — ablated over K ∈ {2, 5, 10, 20, 50} on ImageNet-1K and K ∈ {50, 100, 500, 1000, 5000} on ImageNet-21K; (2) anti-laziness prompting to prevent degenerate all-"None" outputs; (3) random grouping preferred over semantic grouping, justified by an ablation showing <0.3% variance across random/most-similar/least-similar strategies. Key Results On ImageNet-1K (1000 classes) with K=10: Qwen2.5-VL-7B improves from 61.80% to 71.57% (+9.77pp); Qwen3-VL-8B from 67.56% to 71.16% (+3.60pp); Qwen3-VL-2B from 40.54% to 51.36% (+10.82pp). On ImageNet-21K (21,841 classes) with K=100: Qwen2.5-VL-7B from 0.21% to 37.87% (+37.66pp); Claude Opus 4.5 from 45.48% to 61.64% (+16.16pp); GPT-4(v4.1) from 36.36% to 45.10% (+8.74pp); Qwen3-VL-Plus from 10.56% to 42.20% (+31.64pp). Mean accuracy across four benchmarks (ImageNet-1K, CIFAR-100, CUB-200, Food-101): Qwen3-VL-8B+DCI achieves 71.32% vs. GPT-4(v4.1) at 70.43%, with no training. Speed: on ImageNet-21K, K=500 reduces latency from 7.96s (baseline) to 5.99s while boosting accuracy from 0.48% to 18.86%; parallel-mode DCI at K=100 reduces relative latency overhead to 1.32× vs. baseline at +38.36pp accuracy gain. Baselines CoT and Self-Consistency yield ≤0.82pp gains on ImageNet-1K and ≤0.82pp on ImageNet-21K — marginal compared to DCI's +38.36pp. Grouping strategy ablation (Table 6): accuracy variation across random/most-similar/least-similar groupings is ≤0.3% for both tested models on ImageNet-1K. The counterintuitive dual win — simultaneously higher accuracy AND lower latency than flat inference at scale — is the paper's most striking result. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The recursive label-partition-and-prune inference wrapper for MLLMs is a genuinely novel test-time strategy; the structured empirical characterization of PC-LSR (Performance Collapse in Long Sequence Recognition) as a model-agnostic, architecture-independent phenomenon is a meaningful empirical contribution. The information-theoretic framing applies standard tools (Fano's inequality, Shannon-Hartley analogy) to a new setting rather than deriving fundamentally new results. The divide-and-conquer pattern is classical, but its specific instantiation with branch pruning via "None" outputs and the anti-laziness prompt engineering is non-trivial and appears original. The simultaneous accuracy-and-latency improvement at large N is not obvious a priori. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present: group size K (Table 4), grouping strategy (Table 6), K vs. latency on ImageNet-21K (Table 3), and scaling across N=10–1000 classes (Figure 5). No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests are reported anywhere. Results in Table 5 are explicitly stated as "best values over multiple independent runs" without reporting variance, which is a cherry-picking concern given stochastic MLLM outputs with default temperature. ImageNet-21K evaluation uses 5,000-image subsets for closed-source models vs. 50,000 for open-source, making inter-model comparisons in Table 2 potentially affected by sampling variance. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/FourierAI/DCI. No trained model weights (framework is training-free). The theoretical proof of Lemma 2 (Attention Dilution) carries a material gap: it requires the "bounded and non-dominant logit regime" — that no single logit is strongly dominant — which trained transformers routinely violate through sharp, learned attention patterns; the O(1/K) result may significantly overstate dilution in models with focused attention. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper runs MLLM inference, measures top-1 accuracy and wall-clock latency. - Practical: Runs on 4× NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24 GB VRAM each) for open-source models; also validated via commercial API calls to GPT-4 and Claude. Commercially available hardware sufficient. - Efficient: Sequential-mode DCI adds 1.32–4.39× latency overhead vs. flat inference depending on N; at large N (21K) with optimal K, latency drops below the flat-inference baseline because shorter per-prompt context reduces quadratic attention cost. Parallel-mode DCI on ImageNet-21K reduces the overhead to 1.32× while delivering +38.36pp accuracy. - Effective: Evidence strongly supports the stated claim — consistent, large gains across 10+ model families and 5 datasets, including both open-source and API-based models. However, even with DCI, peak absolute accuracy on ImageNet-21K is 61.64% (Claude Opus 4.5), well below specialized classifiers; the paper does not compare against CLIP or fine-tuned classifiers on these benchmarks. - Scalable: Tested up to N=21,000 categories; the paper's own complexity analysis claims the DCI advantage grows with N, but behavior at N=100K or N=1M is not tested. A critical unquantified failure mode: false-negative "None" outputs at any recursion level permanently prune the correct candidate; error compounding across log_K(N) recursion levels is not characterized. The framework delivers a software artifact (GitHub code) but no trained model weights. It operates as a plug-and-play wrapper around any MLLM API. Strengths - The empirical characterization of PC-LSR is thorough and convincing: six+ architectures (including models from 2B to trillion-parameter scale) tested at controlled label subset sizes {10, 20, 100, 200, 500, 1000}, with consistent collapse regardless of model family or size — establishing this as a structural rather than model-specific problem. - The dual win on ImageNet-21K — DCI simultaneously improves accuracy by +38.36pp and reduces latency to 1.32× baseline in parallel mode — is counter-intuitive and practically significant; showing that test-time compute invested in search structure can buy both quality and speed. - The grouping strategy ablation (Table 6) yields a non-obvious insight: semantic organization within groups (most/least similar) produces negligible accuracy differences vs. random grouping, because hard inter-class comparisons are deferred to subsequent recursion levels rather than eliminated — a clean empirical falsification of an intuitive alternative. - Model-agnostic applicability is genuinely demonstrated across both locally-deployed open-source models and closed-source API models without code changes, demonstrating true plug-and-play practicality. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge (Section 6.4) that DCI introduces an accuracy-latency tradeoff in small-scale datasets: for small N, recursive reasoning increases wall-clock time vs. single-pass inference with no accuracy benefit. They note that high-stakes applications (e.g., medical imaging) may justify the latency cost and propose future parallelization work. The K hyperparameter requires scale-dependent tuning. These limitations are stated directly and without minimization. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Theoretical gap in Lemma 2: The attention dilution proof requires the "bounded and non-dominant logit regime" (no logit strongly dominates), which is violated by trained transformers that develop peaked sparse attention toward relevant tokens. In practice, a model may assign near-unit attention to the correct class token despite long lists, making the O(1/K) dilution claim empirically untestable from this analysis. The SNR formulation (E[α_k] divided by 1 − E[α_k]) conflates token-level attention over class-name tokens in the text sequence with classification confidence — the causal mechanism is asserted, not demonstrated. - Missing hierarchical baseline: ImageNet has a well-known WordNet synset hierarchy; coarse-to-fine taxonomy-guided classification (navigate the class tree, asking e.g. "animal or vehicle?" then "bird or mammal?" etc.) is the natural prior-art baseline for the exact problem DCI solves. Comparing against tree-structured search would substantially sharpen the contribution's positioning. - Error propagation unquantified: If the MLLM emits "None" for a partition containing the correct class (false negative), that class is permanently pruned. The per-level false-negative rate and its compounding across log_K(N) recursion levels is not reported. For ImageNet-21K with K=100, there are ~4 recursion levels; a 5% false-negative rate per level compounds to ~19% cumulative pruning of correct candidates, which would fully explain the gap between DCI accuracy and the theoretical ceiling. - Asymmetric evaluation in Table 2: Closed-source models (GPT-4, Claude Opus 4.5) are evaluated on 5,000 randomly sampled ImageNet-21K images while open-source models use 50,000. Without variance estimates, the 5K-sample results carry non-trivial sampling uncertainty that complicates direct comparison. - No absolute competitiveness framing: The paper does not compare against CLIP-based zero-shot classifiers or fine-tuned CNN/ViT models on the same benchmarks. At 61.64% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-21K (the best DCI result), the absolute performance likely remains well below fine-tuned task-specific models, a gap the paper does not characterize. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying MLLMs for large-scale closed-set recognition will find DCI immediately useful — training-free, model-agnostic, code released, and the gains at scale (particularly on 21K-class problems) are large and consistent — but the missing hierarchical baselines and unquantified error compounding mean this should be treated as a strong engineering contribution with suggestive rather than conclusive theory.
Search Results 2605.24799 PDF
#97 — Innovative (61)

Enhancing amharic news classification through ontology -based feature fusion and logistic regression

nature.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a hybrid Amharic news classifier that fuses a hand-built domain ontology (Amharic News Ontology, ANO) with TF-IDF features and trains a Logistic Regression model, achieving 97.0% accuracy on a 61,915-article six-class benchmark. - Why it's important: Amharic, Ethiopia's official working language with ~57M speakers, lacks NLP infrastructure, and news classifiers must handle rich morphological surface variation and semantic overlap between topically adjacent categories (Politics, Business, National News) that purely lexical representations cannot resolve. - How it does it: A 30-dimensional ontological feature vector is computed by aggregating TF-IDF scores of domain keywords mapped to OWL 2 DL ontology concepts (weighted by expert-assigned discriminativeness scores), then concatenated with a 20,000-feature TF-IDF vector using a fusion weight α=0.6 tuned via 5-fold cross-validation. - The good: The 3.2-point accuracy gain is statistically significant (McNemar's χ²=256.7, p<0.001, Cohen's d=1.87), and the XLM-R fine-tuned transformer achieves only 0.4pp more accuracy (97.4%) while requiring multi-GPU hours versus 42 seconds CPU training for this model. - The missing: The expert-assigned binary semantic weights (w_semantic ∈ {0.3, 0.7}) are never ablated against learned or uniform alternatives, making it impossible to isolate whether improvement stems from the ontology's hierarchical structure or simply from expert-guided soft feature selection. - Read it or not: Researchers building text classification tools for morphologically rich African languages in compute-constrained environments will find the documented ontology construction methodology and the TF-IDF fusion formalism directly reusable; those seeking advances in core NLP methods can skip it.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile (author-tagged). Rigor (72) is the top contributor: a strong statistical suite (McNemar's, paired t-test on 10×5-fold CV, bootstrap CIs, Cohen's d, MCC), present ablations (with/without ontology, α sweep), publicly released code + OWL ontology + deployed app, and peer review in Scientific Reports — though the missing learned-vs-binary weight ablation, an unanalyzed double-IDF term, and an identical-split AfriBERTa comparison cap it. Completeness (75) is high — the authors are unusually candid, explicitly warning against overinterpreting the minority-class recall and acknowledging the 30:1 imbalance and ~180-hour scaling ceiling. Novelty (50) is moderate: NEW METHOD plus an actionable empirical finding (CPU-only LR closes 87.5% of the gap to XLM-R at ~300× less compute), but ontology-augmented classification is an established technique. Non-Obviousness (40) is the main drag — the component combination is something a competent practitioner would try. Resilience (60) is solid but dented by the unexplained 80/20-vs-85/15 reversal and the double-IDF interaction; Impact (65) reflects a complete, deployed artifact. Weighted: 0.20*50 + 0.25*72 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*75 + 0.15*60 + 0.10*65 = 61. This work most resembles the positive calibration set in rigor and released-artifact discipline.
empirical Novelty 50 Rigor 72 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 75 Resilience 60 Impact 65
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Amharic remains severely underresourced in NLP despite its status as Ethiopia's official language and one of the largest Semitic languages globally. News categorization is a commercially and civically important task — for media monitoring, information retrieval, and content filtering — but Amharic's rich morphological structure causes surface-form explosion that bag-of-words representations handle poorly. Worse, news categories like Politics, Business, and National News share dense lexical overlap: terms for "government," "parliament," and "economy" co-occur across categories. Prior work splits into two unsatisfying poles: shallow statistical methods (Naïve Bayes, SVM with TF-IDF) that achieve 92-94% but ignore semantics, and deep transformer approaches (AfriBERTa, XLM-R) that are computationally inaccessible at Ethiopian academic institutions. The specific gap this paper addresses is a formal, reproducible mechanism for injecting domain semantic structure into a lightweight classifier without requiring GPU compute or large pre-training corpora. Key Idea The core trick is to project each document onto a hand-constructed 30-dimensional concept space — where each dimension aggregates the TF-IDF scores of all domain keywords belonging to a given ontology concept, scaled by a concept-level IDF and expert-validated discriminativeness weight — and concatenate this dense semantic vector with the standard sparse TF-IDF vector before logistic regression. The ontology acts as a fixed, human-encoded feature engineering step that compresses domain knowledge into a low-dimensional supplement, bypassing the need for large labeled data to learn semantic relationships implicitly. Methodology The pipeline has four main stages. (1) Ontology construction follows a four-phase protocol: RAKE-based corpus concept extraction (frequency threshold ≥50 across 61,915 articles); expert annotation by three native Amharic journalists/academics over four iterative sessions with structured inclusion criteria (Cohen's κ=0.85, 4.2% of cases requiring third-party arbitration); formal OWL 2 DL implementation with 6 top-level classes, 24 mid-level subclasses, 187 leaf instances, 4 object properties, and 156 logical axioms checked by HermiT reasoner; and semantic feature engineering. (2) The semantic feature formula is V_semantic(i) = Σ_{k∈K_i} TF-IDF(k)·IDF(c_i)·w_semantic(k), where w_semantic(k) ∈ {0.3, 0.7} is manually assigned. (3) Hybrid representation: V_hybrid = [V_lexical ⊕ α·V_semantic], α=0.6 from 5-fold CV grid search on training data only. (4) Logistic Regression (scikit-learn v1.4.0, lbfgs, L2 C=1.0, balanced class weights, multinomial). Three critical design decisions: expert-annotated ontology construction is well-justified for a low-resource language but limits domain scalability; binary manual semantic weights (0.3/0.7) are justified by expert validation but not tested against learned alternatives; LR classifier choice is justified by interpretability and compute constraints and validated by the XLM-R comparison. Evaluation uses three train-test splits, reporting accuracy, weighted precision/recall/F1, MCC, McNemar's test, paired t-test on 10×5-fold CV, bootstrap CIs (n=1000), and Cohen's d. Key Results Best split (80/20): 97.0% accuracy (CI: 96.8–97.2%), weighted F1=0.968, MCC=0.963. TF-IDF-only baseline: 93.8%, MCC=0.924. The +3.2pp improvement is statistically significant: McNemar's χ²=256.7, p<0.001; paired t-test t(9)=12.43, p<0.001; Cohen's d=1.87. XLM-R fine-tuned on the identical 80/20 split: 97.4% accuracy, weighted F1=0.976 — a gap of only 0.4pp at roughly 300× the training compute. Key error reductions: Politics→Business misclassifications fell 38% (187→116), Business→Politics fell 44%, Sports→Business fell 45%, National News recall improved +15.4pp (0.62→0.77). The minority class National News (137 test samples, 0.8% of dataset) remains the hardest: F1=0.86, recall=0.77, with acknowledged wide confidence intervals. Comparisons against prior Amharic work (SVM 92%, CNN 95%, NB+TF-IDF 93.8%, multi-domain fusion 94.2%) are approximate due to differing dataset splits and preprocessing pipelines. All baselines are drawn from the 2021-2025 literature and are reasonably current, though AfriBERTa — the most targeted multilingual African model — was not compared on an identical split. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The paper combines established components (TF-IDF, ontology-augmented features, Logistic Regression) but contributes a reproducible four-phase ontology construction protocol for a low-resource language with documented inter-annotator agreement and conflict resolution, a mathematically explicit feature fusion formulation addressing prior criticism of vague integration approaches, and the empirical finding that a CPU-only ontology-augmented LR model closes 87.5% of the performance gap between TF-IDF-LR and a fine-tuned XLM-R transformer. The ontology-augmented classification concept itself is not new; what is genuinely novel is its systematic application and formal specification for Amharic, with reproducibility artifacts publicly released. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation is present but limited: Table 3 compares with vs. without ontology; Table 4 sweeps the fusion weight α over [0.3, 0.8]. However, no ablation isolates the contribution of the ontology hierarchy versus the expert semantic weights, and no test compares learned weights against the binary 0.3/0.7 assignments. Error bars and CIs are reported throughout; the paired t-test runs on 10 repeated stratified 5-fold CV. Code, the ANO in OWL format, annotation guidelines, and a deployed HuggingFace application are publicly available (github.com/Beya2123/Amharic-News-Classification). The paper is peer-reviewed (accepted Scientific Reports, not a preprint). One internal inconsistency: Section 3.1 claims "the topics were even" while Table 1 reports a 30:1 class imbalance between Entertainment and National News — this factual error in the prose is not methodologically damaging but suggests incomplete editorial review. The suspicious result that the 80/20 split outperforms the 85/15 split by 1.7pp (rather than showing the expected tighter variance with more test data) is unexplained. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on any commodity CPU (demonstrated on Intel Core i7, 16GB RAM, Ubuntu 20.04); no GPU required for training or inference. - Efficient: 42-second CPU training time and ~5ms/sample inference versus ~50ms/sample for XLM-R; the one-time ontology construction cost (~180 person-hours) is the real bottleneck for new domains, not runtime compute. - Effective: The 3.2pp accuracy gain with large effect size (Cohen's d=1.87) and specific confusion reductions (Politics-Business -38%) support the claim that semantic integration genuinely helps, not just a proxy metric. - Scalable: Evaluated only on one dataset (61,915 articles, 6 news categories). The ontology construction cost per new domain (~180 person-hours) is a hard ceiling on scaling; cross-domain, cross-lingual, and larger-vocabulary behavior are entirely untested. The work delivers a complete software artifact (GitHub repo, OWL ontology, deployed classification app). Strengths - The four-phase ontology construction protocol — including RAKE-based seed extraction, structured inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.85), conflict resolution procedures, OWL 2 DL formal specification, and HermiT consistency checking — is documented with enough detail to be reproduced for other low-resource languages, directly improving on ad-hoc ontology construction in prior work. - The explicit mathematical formalization of semantic feature computation (V_semantic formula with named terms) removes ambiguity that plagued earlier ontology-NLP integration work. - The XLM-R comparison on the identical train-test split, delivering only 0.4pp more accuracy at ~300× the training cost, is a strong and practically actionable finding for deployment at resource-constrained African institutions. - The statistical validation suite (McNemar's, paired t-test on repeated CV, bootstrap CIs, Cohen's d, MCC for imbalanced evaluation) exceeds typical rigor in the Amharic NLP literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Authors explicitly acknowledge: ANO scope is restricted to news domain and will not transfer without substantial rework; manual ontology construction is labor-intensive (~180 person-hours) and limits domain scalability; the 30:1 class imbalance renders National News metrics statistically unreliable (authors explicitly warn against overinterpreting the 0.77 recall figure); no identical-split comparison with AfriBERTa or Walia-LLM; single-domain evaluation only; need for external independent validation; Amharic-only validation. The authors are unusually candid — directly cautioning readers against overinterpreting specific numbers — which adds credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The binary expert-assigned semantic weights (w_semantic ∈ {0.3, 0.7}) are never compared against learned weights, uniform weights, or information-gain-derived weights. Since these weights functionally act as a hard feature selection filter, the accuracy improvement could be partially or wholly attributable to expert-guided feature selection rather than the ontological hierarchy itself. - The formula multiplies TF-IDF(k) — which already incorporates a term-level IDF — by IDF(c_i), creating a double-IDF effect. This mathematical interaction is not analyzed or justified. - The abstract claims the dataset classes "were even" (balanced), directly contradicting Table 1's 30:1 ratio. This is an uncorrected factual error in the published manuscript. - The 80/20 split achieving 97.0% accuracy while the 85/15 split achieves only 95.3% is counterintuitive (larger training set with smaller test set typically yields equal or higher accuracy), and the paper offers no explanation for this 1.7pp reversal. - The ontological feature vector is fixed at 30 dimensions (matching 6+24 concept nodes). No analysis tests whether finer-grained ontology mappings (e.g., using all 217 nodes including leaf instances) improve classification or whether the mid-level granularity is specifically optimal. - AfriBERTa, the model most directly targeted at African languages and cited extensively in the introduction, is not compared on an identical split — the only transformer baseline is generic XLM-RoBERTa-base. Bottom Line A practitioner building news classification infrastructure for Amharic or other morphologically rich, low-resource African languages in compute-constrained settings will find actionable, reproducible methodology and a publicly deployed system; the broader NLP community will find the result confirmatory rather than surprising, as ontology-augmented classification is an established technique and the 3.2pp gain over TF-IDF leaves no gap that demands reading over the abstract.
Search Results
#98 — Innovative (61)

Contrast to Detect: Dynamic Graph Contrastive Regularization for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection in Multivariate Time Series

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces ContrastAD, an unsupervised MTS anomaly detection framework that treats inter-variable graph structural divergence across time windows as the primary learning signal, using a soft contrastive regularizer rather than enforcing view-invariance. - Why it's important: Reconstruction-based detectors fail because they reconstruct anomalies as faithfully as normal data, and existing graph contrastive methods assume stationary relational structure, generating false positives under the structural drift that characterizes real cyber-physical attacks and workload shifts. - How it does it: Per-window DTW distances build sparse graph snapshots; the maximally divergent pair (selected by symmetric KL divergence on degree distributions) is contrasted against a stable anchor via a negative-weight InfoNCE-style loss that separates embeddings without collapsing structural variation. - The good: Removing the DGCL module drops F1 by 14.4 points on SWaT and 28.86 points on SMD (AUC collapses from 98.66 to 69.51), confirming the contrastive signal is not redundant with reconstruction and is the dominant contributor to performance. - The missing: All five benchmarks have ≤55 variables; the O(N²) DTW construction cost at N=200+ is never empirically tested, and the headline F1 metric relies on the contested point-adjustment protocol, inflating apparent margins on datasets where they are under 0.13 points. - Read it or not: Read it if you work on unsupervised anomaly detection in non-stationary industrial or telemetry systems — the DGCL regularization concept is clean and the ablation is unusually thorough — but treat performance numbers with caution given preprint status and point-adjustment inflation.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Non-Obviousness (68) and Novelty (65) lead: the sign-flipped contrastive regularizer that treats structural divergence as signal rather than suppressing it is a clean, theoretically-grounded departure. Rigor (63) is comparatively strong for a preprint — module-level ablations, 5 seeds, a 21-value λ sweep, and CIs — but point-adjustment inflation and significance on only 2/5 datasets pull it down, and Resilience (55) suffers from the same PA concern plus untested scaling beyond N=55. Impact (45) is limited to released code without weights or a packaged library. Weighted composite ≈ 61.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 63 Non-Obv 68 Completeness 62 Resilience 55 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Industrial and telemetry multivariate time series (water treatment plants, server clusters, spacecraft sensors) require unsupervised anomaly detection because anomaly labels are rare or absent. Two structural challenges compound the difficulty: inter-variable dependencies drift as system states evolve (a coordinated cyber-physical attack changes actuator-sensor coupling without altering signal magnitudes), and spectral noise entangles periodic patterns across channels. Reconstruction-based methods minimize per-sample prediction error and therefore learn to fit any systematic pattern in the training distribution, including anomalous ones — they score anomalies as low-error events, directly contradicting the detection goal. Graph-based contrastive methods impose cross-view invariance, which trains the encoder to suppress exactly the structural change signals that betray anomalies in non-stationary settings. The result is false positives triggered by benign structural drift and false negatives on attacks that manifest as relational rather than magnitude deviations. Key Idea The single core insight is the sign flip on the contrastive objective: instead of minimizing divergence across views (enforcing invariance), use the divergence between the most structurally different pair of graph snapshots within a window as the regularization signal — aligning each divergent snapshot with a stable anchor while explicitly separating the two divergent snapshots from each other. Applied with a small negative coefficient λ, this makes the contrastive term a soft regularizer that sharpens the decision boundary around the normal manifold without overriding the reconstruction objective. Methodology ContrastAD has three jointly trained modules. Multi-Perspective Embedder (MPE): WaveNet-style dilated causal convolutions (4 layers, dilation rates 1/2/4/8) applied along the temporal axis yield z_seq; the same architecture applied to the transposed input yields z_fea encoding cross-variable patterns. A GAT-style temporal graph attention module over the fully-connected feature graph yields z_gat. The three embeddings are concatenated. Design choice: three perspectives in a single forward pass rather than two; justified by the argument that downstream modules can mix temporal, attribute, and structural evidence without a separate alignment stage. Frequency-Aware Attention Mixer (FAM): Real-valued FFT along the temporal axis → hard top-K frequency mask (K=6, zeroing all but the highest-magnitude Fourier bins) → inverse FFT → multi-head attention (2 layers, 128 channels, 4 heads) → gated fusion of two FFN branches. The critical design choice is placing the FFT mask before query-key projection, not after, so spectral noise never enters the similarity computation. Gradients flow through retained bins (straight-through estimator on a binary mask). Dynamic Graph Contrastive Learner (DGCL): Each sliding window (size 100) is partitioned into S=10 non-overlapping snapshots of length δ=10. For each snapshot, pairwise DTW distances between node segments are computed; the top-K most dissimilar pairs form a sparse adjacency matrix, where K follows a power-law edge budget K = ⌊N·E(X)/2⌋ (expected edges under Zipf degree distribution). The most structurally divergent snapshot pair (G_p, G_q) is identified by symmetric KL divergence on normalized degree distributions. GCN encodes G_p, G_q, and the mean of remaining snapshots (anchor z_a). The InfoNCE-style loss L_graph (Eq. 17) has two positive terms aligning each divergent snapshot to z_a and one negative term separating G_p from G_q. The overall loss is L = L_forecast + 0.1·L_reconstruction + λ·L_graph with λ < 0 (range −0.1 to −1.0 per dataset). The negative sign means the total objective minimizes by maximizing the contrastive score — keeping divergent snapshots anchor-compatible while separating them from each other. Key Results Evaluated on SWaT (51 vars, 475K/450K train/test), SMD (38 vars, 708K/708K), MSL (55 vars, 58K/74K), PSM (25 vars, 132K/88K), and SMAP (25 vars, 135K/428K), against 8 baselines (GDN, CSTGL, FuSAG, MTG, DTrans, MemStr, Catch, MSHTrans) over n=5 seeds. Point adjustment applied uniformly to all methods. ContrastAD achieves highest mean F1 on all five datasets. Statistically significant margins (non-overlapping 95% CI) on SWaT: F1 88.24±0.51 (+3.97 over Catch 84.27), AUC 93.60±0.22 (+1.66 over Catch 91.94); and PSM: F1 91.17±0.11 (+0.61 over MSHTrans 90.56), AUC 97.79±0.06 (+1.53 over MSHTrans 96.26). On SMD the F1 margin over MSHTrans is +0.04 and AUC margin +0.16 — not statistically significant. On MSL and SMAP, ContrastAD leads on F1 by 0.11 and 0.09 points respectively but trails MSHTrans on AUC by 0.16 and 0.63 points. Several baselines exhibit degenerate precision-recall tradeoffs that ContrastAD avoids: GDN on SMD achieves 86.39% Precision but only 3.41% Recall (F1 6.55%), and CSTGL on PSM achieves 99.95% Recall but only 27.77% Precision (F1 43.46%). All tests run on a single Nvidia RTX 3090. Training time for ContrastAD is 132.16 s/epoch on SWaT, versus 634.72s for MSHTrans and 9.53s for GDN. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The genuine novelty is DGCL: using the divergence between maximally dissimilar dynamic graph snapshots as a soft negative-weight contrastive regularizer, rather than enforcing cross-view invariance. This is a conceptually clean departure from prevailing graph contrastive learning design. MPE (DCN + GAT concatenation) and FAM (FFT-filtered attention) are incremental combinations of known components applied to a new setting rather than independently novel contributions. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation covers all three modules individually, with results over 5 seeds — and the DGCL ablation (λ=0) is replicated in the sensitivity table, cross-validating consistency. The sensitivity sweep over λ ∈ [−1.0, +1.0] at 0.1 intervals (21 values × 5 datasets × 5 seeds) is unusually thorough. Statistical significance is reported as non-overlapping 95% confidence intervals for F1 and AUC only — not for precision and recall, which are relegated to supplementary material. Code is available at an anonymous repository (anonymous.4open.science/r/CIKM26-ContrastAD-D29A). λ is selected per dataset via pilot experiments on train/validation splits without consulting test labels, which is appropriate. All baselines are run under identical point-adjustment settings and the same 5-seed protocol. The paper uses point adjustment uniformly and cites [15] (Kim et al. 2022) acknowledging the controversy, but retains it for comparability — this inflates F1 for every method and narrows the interpretive value of fine-grained margins. The anonymous repo URL implies this is under review for CIKM 2026; results are preprint and not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on a single commercially available RTX 3090 GPU; no distributed training or specialized hardware required. - Efficient: O(N_L) memory (linear in variable count × window length) versus O(N_L²) for Catch and MSHTrans; 132.16 s/epoch on SWaT is moderate — faster than CSTGL (384s) and MSHTrans (634s) but 14× slower than GDN (9.53s). DTW adds O(N²δ) per window, bounded at 1485 calls at N=55 with δ=10; Sakoe-Chiba banding is mentioned for larger N but not benchmarked. - Effective: Evidence across five real-world benchmarks consistently shows improvement over eight baselines, and ablation confirms the contrastive component is non-redundant. However, statistically significant margins appear on only 2/5 datasets, and point adjustment inflates F1 throughout. - Scalable: Tested only at N ≤ 55 variables and up to 708K time steps. No experiment at N > 55. Claims about DTW scalability via Sakoe-Chiba banding are theoretical; no empirical measurements are provided at larger variable counts. Delivers code and demo data but not pretrained model weights or a packaged library. Strengths - The DGCL conceptual contribution — treating structural divergence as signal rather than noise to suppress — is directly supported by the λ=0 ablation: removing DGCL causes F1 drops of 14.4 points (SWaT) and 28.86 points (SMD), and AUC collapse from 98.66 to 69.51 on SMD, ruling out the hypothesis that reconstruction alone suffices. - The sign of λ is derived from the alignment-uniformity theory of contrastive learning rather than selected arbitrarily; the sensitivity table confirms the sign matters far more than the magnitude, lending theoretical coherence to the empirical observation. - O(N_L) memory footprint and 132s/epoch training time put ContrastAD in the high-performance tier while competing methods in that tier (Catch, MSHTrans) require O(N_L²) memory — a meaningful practical advantage for longer windows. - The λ sensitivity sweep is unusually thorough (21 hyperparameter values fully evaluated at 5 seeds each) and genuinely demonstrates that any negative λ recovers most of the benefit, supporting the soft-regularizer claim. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify: (1) training assumes anomaly-free data — handling mixed training sets is left for future work; (2) DTW-based graph construction is not compared against correlation-based or learnable adjacency alternatives; (3) degree-distribution KL divergence as the snapshot divergence measure is not compared against spectral distances; (4) contrastive weight λ is fixed rather than adaptive to structural stability. These are acknowledged directly in the conclusion and future work, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The point-adjustment protocol inflates F1 for all methods and has been shown to make random detectors appear competitive (Kim et al. 2022, cited by the authors). F1 margins on MSL (+0.11), SMD (+0.04), and SMAP (+0.09) are within the PA-inflation regime and should not be interpreted as meaningful improvements on those datasets. - The claimed scalability via Sakoe-Chiba banding is not supported by any measurement. The DTW graph construction at N=200+ variables (common in network monitoring or financial applications) would produce 19,900 pairwise comparisons per snapshot vs. 1,485 at N=55 — a 13× increase that the paper does not address empirically. - The power-law edge budget is described as "power-law-inspired" but only determines the number of edges K, not their distribution. The actual degree distribution of the constructed graph is determined by DTW dissimilarity and is not guaranteed to be power-law. The theoretical motivation from Zipf/Barabási-Albert is loose and not empirically verified on the constructed graphs. - A GCN is applied to a dissimilarity graph (edges connect the most temporally divergent pairs), which inverts the standard homophily assumption. The paper acknowledges this but does not justify why GCN is more appropriate here than a simple mean-pooling readout or a graph transformer, leaving an architectural choice unmotivated. - SMAP shows a sharp F1 discontinuity at λ ∈ {-0.2, -0.1} (F1 ~48-49) versus the rest of the negative range (F1 ~70), which contradicts the claimed smooth robustness and suggests sensitivity to λ magnitude on at least one dataset. Bottom Line A practitioner working on unsupervised anomaly detection in non-stationary industrial telemetry will find genuine value in the DGCL regularization concept and the thorough ablation, but should independently verify performance without point adjustment and at variable counts beyond 55 before adoption.
Search Results 2605.23744 PDF
#99 — Innovative (61)

Macaron-A2UI: A Model for Generative UI in Personal Agents (67 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Macaron-A2UI, a system comprising a 14,245-sample training corpus, a 300-task benchmark (A2UI-Bench), and 30B/235B/754B models fine-tuned with LoRA SFT followed by GRPO reinforcement learning to generate executable structured UI actions interleaved with natural language in personal-agent dialogues. - Why it's important: Plain-text chat forces repeated clarification turns for tasks requiring structured input — parameter collection, option selection, booking confirmation — increasing cognitive load and interaction latency in deployed agent systems. - How it does it: A two-stage training recipe first applies LoRA SFT on a hybrid rule+LLM-annotated dialogue corpus to establish format and text–UI grounding, then applies GRPO with a composite reward that hard-gates on structural renderability before awarding partial credit for task construction quality and user experience, forcing the model to internalize the A2UI protocol without a long schema prompt at inference time. - The good: The 754B Macaron-A2UI-Venti achieves 75.6 overall on A2UI-Bench without any schema hint at inference time, surpassing GPT-5.4 given the full schema (74.1) — a schema-light trained model beating a schema-prompted frontier model is the headline finding. - The missing: There is no human user study measuring actual task completion rates, time-on-task, or preference vs. plain-text baselines; the entire UX benefit claim rests on LLM and VLM judges scoring outputs from a benchmark derived from the same data pipeline as the training set. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building conversational agent interfaces or studying reward-shaping for structured output generation; those wanting validated UX improvements over text-only interaction should wait for a follow-up with a real user study.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (70) is the strongest dimension — a new problem framing (agent-side Generative UI under a fixed protocol), a released corpus, a benchmark, and trained models, with a schema-light trained model beating a schema-prompted frontier model. Resilience (52) and Rigor (58) pull the composite down: the central UX claim (reduced user burden) rests entirely on LLM/VLM judges with no human study, A2UI-Bench is built from the same pipeline as the training corpus with unmeasured overlap, and the consequential GRPO reward weights go unablated. Composite: 0.20*70 + 0.25*58 + 0.15*62 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*52 + 0.10*65 = 61.
empirical Novelty 70 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 58 Resilience 52 Impact 65
Show full analysis
