By making this three-dimensional decomposition the organizing principle for both data generation and model training—and using preference-aware influence functions to identify which ~1k training examples most efficiently steer the model toward guardrail behavior in parameter space—the framework distills fine-grained safety judgment into compact models from a large noisy synthetic pool. The key trick is not the taxonomy itself (which extends prior AgentDoG work), but using the taxonomy gradient direction as a data selection criterion rather than as a post-hoc annotation. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. Stage 1—Taxonomy design: A three-dimensional schema with 15 risk sources, 21 failure modes, and 11 real-world harm categories is extended for OpenClaw (new categories: session-state contamination, approval bypass, cross-tool attack chaining) and Codex (repository artifact injection, dependency/MCP supply-chain compromise, unsafe shell execution) by adding new leaf categories while keeping high-level dimensions fixed, preserving cross-benchmark comparability. Stage 2—Data engine: A planner samples (risk source, failure mode, harm) tuples, constructs trajectory sketches with exact risk injection points, instantiates them as multi-turn safe/unsafe pairs using a three-stage pipeline (planning → synthesis → dual-layer validation by rule checker + LLM checker), and augments each with GPT-5.4-generated CoT rationales. The resulting pool covers 5,973 unique tools/MCP servers and all taxonomy dimensions. Stage 3—Influence-function purification: For each candidate training example z, the purification score sπ(z) = ĝz⊤ĝguard is computed, where ĝguard is the preference-weighted gradient contrast direction between correct risk-identifying responses and risk-missing responses over a small set of safety target prompts; only the top-scoring ~1k examples are retained. This is mathematically grounded in preference-aware influence function theory (Lin et al., 2026). Stage 4—Two-stage training: SFT on the purified CoT corpus (Qwen3.5-0.8/2/4B and Llama-3.1-8B; lr=1e-5), then GDPO RL—a multi-dimensional variant of GRPO that normalizes advantages per taxonomy dimension with weights (w1, w2, w3) = (0.3, 0.4, 0.3), applies a reasoning-block gate that zeros all rewards when the model omits a non-trivial analysis span, and uses asymmetric PPO clipping (εlow=0.2, εhigh=0.28). Critical design decisions: (a) per-dimension GDPO over scalar GRPO is well-justified because summing partial-credit rewards makes partially-correct rollouts indistinguishable from qualitatively different errors after normalization; (b) Pre-Reply guardrail placement over per-tool-call checking is well-justified for latency and broad framework compatibility; (c) influence-function purification to ~1k examples is empirically validated against full unfiltered training. Key Results On R-Judge binary classification, AgentDoG 1.5-4B achieves 92.2% accuracy and 92.7% F1, matching GPT-5.4 (93.3%, 93.7%) and exceeding all open-source and guard model baselines. On ATBench binary classification, the unified variant AgentDoG 1.5-4B-U achieves 78.4% accuracy and 77.7% F1, exceeding GPT-5.4 (73.7%, 76.7%) and all guard models (best prior: ShieldAgent 84.6% F1 but only on R-Judge, not ATBench). On ATBench fine-grained diagnosis, AgentDoG 1.5-4B achieves 75.2% risk source, 27.5% failure mode, 62.9% real-world harm (55.2% avg) versus GPT-5.4 at 25.8% average—a 29.4-point gap. On ATBench-Claw and ATBench-Codex after format-adaptation training, AgentDoG 1.5-4B achieves 84.0% and 87.6% respectively, with 4B outperforming Qwen3-235B and Qwen3.5-397B on Claw. For SFT application (Qwen3.5-4B base), AgentDoG 1.5-filtered data reduces AgentHarm harm score from 57.49% to 20.32% (versus 31.91% unfiltered) and increases refusal rate from 28.41% to 75.00%, while improving BFCL accuracy from 76.04% to 81.12%. The SFT+RL joint approach further recovers utility: 77.27% refusal rate, 59.32% AgentSafetyBench safe rate, 81.25% BFCL. For the Pre-Reply online guardrail on OpenClaw (GLM-5.1 backbone agent): ClawSafety ASR drops from 56.25% to 18.75% (4B), AgentHazard ASR from 41.92% to 26.92% (4B, while Qwen3-Guard and LlamaGuard show 0% reduction here), CIK-Bench ASR from 94.29% to 42.86% (4B). TTFT is sub-second across settings. The surprising finding is that adding fine-grained diagnostic supervision (AgentDoG 1.5-4B-U) improves coarse binary classification accuracy—an unexpected synergistic effect. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW METHOD. The three-dimensional taxonomy design is an incremental extension of prior AgentDoG 1.0 and ATBench (same group); the genuinely novel contributions are (1) preference-aware influence-function purification applied to agentic guardrail training data selection, (2) GDPO multi-dimensional RL for fine-grained risk diagnosis with per-dimension advantage normalization, and (3) ATBench-Claw and ATBench-Codex benchmarks covering new agent execution settings. The base model architecture (standard SFT+RL on open-source LLMs) is a conventional combination; novelty concentrates in the data pipeline and training recipe. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and well-structured: Table 4 isolates filtering vs. no filtering vs. base model across six benchmarks; Table 5 isolates SFT-only vs. RL-only vs. SFT+RL. No error bars, confidence intervals, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported anywhere in the paper. The online guardrail evaluation rests on n=16 (ClawSafety) and n=35 (CIK-Bench) cases—far too small to draw reliable statistical conclusions. All models and datasets are openly released (github.com/AI45Lab/AgentDoG, HuggingFace AI45Research/agentdog1.5). A major methodological concern is that ATBench-Claw and ATBench-Codex are constructed by the same research group that proposes AgentDoG 1.5, using the same taxonomy that guides training; performance on these benchmarks reflects alignment between the model and the group's own data generation process as much as genuine safety capability. GPT-5.4 is simultaneously used as a CoT annotation teacher during training and as a baseline comparator—learning to mimic its reasoning patterns while comparing against its raw performance without that training creates an asymmetric comparison. Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL + SYSTEMS. - Practical: All variants (0.8B–8B) run on commercially available hardware; the RL environment supports 10,000 concurrent instances on an 8-core machine with peak memory below 2.5GB, a 100× reduction versus Docker-level environments (SWE-Bench, AgentHazard). - Efficient: Pre-Reply TTFT is sub-second and TPOT ~0.02 s/token for the 4B model; the training corpus requires only ~1k samples. The finite-state Python simulator approach trades real-world fidelity for practical deployability, and this tradeoff is explicitly acknowledged. - Effective: The evidence shows the guardrail reduces unsafe final delivery rates across three benchmarks and that filtered SFT data outperforms unfiltered on every metric. However, Failure Mode accuracy of 27.5% on 21 classes renders the fine-grained diagnostic capability unreliable for incident investigation in practice. - Scalable: Concurrent environment scalability is demonstrated at 10,000 instances. Online guardrail behavior at scale is not characterized; the n=16 ClawSafety evaluation does not constitute a scale test. Behavior on agent architectures outside OpenClaw/Codex is uncharacterized. The work delivers trained models on HuggingFace, code on GitHub, ATBench family datasets, and prompt templates—a complete deployable artifact. Strengths - Influence-function purification achieving strong performance from ~1k training samples is a practically significant result: AgentDoG 1.5-4B matches GPT-5.4 on R-Judge trajectory classification, and even the 0.8B variant exceeds all closed-source models on fine-grained diagnosis—demonstrating that task-specific data selection is more impactful than raw model scale for this domain. - GDPO's per-dimension advantage normalization is a principled technical contribution to multi-dimensional RL reward design: the argument that scalar GRPO conflates partial-credit rollouts is well-motivated and the design choice is directly validated by the SFT+RL ablation. - The taxonomy extension mechanism (fixed high-level dimensions, customizable leaf categories) provides a practical framework for incorporating new agent platforms without fragmenting evaluation protocols or invalidating prior model training. - The Pre-Reply guardrail design is engineering-sound: a single intervention point with sub-second latency, trajectory-level context, and demonstrated reduction in unsafe delivery where per-output guardrails (Qwen3-Guard, LlamaGuard) show zero effect on AgentHazard. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) the guardrail operates only on text-based trajectories and cannot handle multimodal agent environments (GUIs, audio, video); (2) Pre-Reply intervention cannot prevent harms already realized through earlier external side effects before final reply; (3) a complete safety architecture must combine trajectory-level monitoring with selective tool-time checks, permission-aware execution policies, and human approval for high-risk actions; (4) the unified AgentDoG 1.5-4B-U model was not carefully tuned for fine-grained diagnosis due to resource constraints. These are substantive and credible acknowledgements of scope. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Circular benchmark construction: ATBench-Claw and ATBench-Codex are both built and evaluated by the same group using the same taxonomy that guides AgentDoG 1.5 training. Without independent construction or third-party reproduction, results on these benchmarks measure alignment with the group's own data generation methodology, not verified safety generalization. - Failure Mode accuracy near the actionability threshold: At 27.5% on a 21-category task (random baseline ~4.8%), the failure mode dimension is unreliable as a standalone diagnostic tool; the paper does not discuss whether this dimension is useful in practice at current accuracy levels. - Online guardrail evaluation sample sizes too small for inference: n=16 for ClawSafety and n=35 for CIK-Bench yield confidence intervals that encompass most claimed improvements; one or two trajectory re-labelings would reverse conclusions. - No false-positive rate characterization in online deployment: The fraction of safe trajectories incorrectly blocked by the guardrail (user-visible cost of deployment) is not reported in the online evaluation section. - Influence function compute cost not discussed: Preference-aware influence function computation requires gradient inner products over the full training pool, which is expensive for large models; whether a proxy model or approximation was used is not stated. - No evaluation outside the training taxonomy settings: All experiments use OpenClaw and Codex agent architectures. Generalization to novel agent frameworks not represented in the training taxonomy is uncharacterized. Bottom Line Practitioners deploying safety guardrails for OpenClaw or Codex agents gain a practical, lightweight, open-source system with demonstrated reduction in unsafe delivery rates and competitive trajectory-level classification; the circular ATBench benchmark construction and 27.5% Failure Mode accuracy require caution before treating the fine-grained diagnostic capability as production-ready.
Recommended 2605.29801 PDF
#105 — Innovative (60)

AI Research Agents Narrow Scientific Exploration (20 ▲ )

Yixuan Tang, Yi Yang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 28 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper demonstrates empirically that AI research agents—across four frameworks and six LLMs generating 37,802 ideas—produce ideas that are substantially more concentrated in semantic space than human-authored research from the same fields, and remain closer to their seed literature than later human follow-on work. - Why it's important: As AI research agents become integrated into scientific workflows at scale, a systematic narrowing of the explored idea space would create a self-reinforcing loop that crowds out unconventional directions and potentially suppresses the high-variance, high-impact research that drives scientific progress. - How it does it: Citation-defined research areas are constructed via bibliographic coupling and HDBSCAN clustering; AI ideas and human papers are embedded in a shared semantic space (Qwen3-Embedding-4B) and compared along four dimensions: within-area concentration, distance from seed literature, citation impact of nearest human papers, and a research-question vs. technical-method novelty decomposition. - The good: The narrowing effect is consistent across all four agent frameworks (zero-shot, AIScientist, ResearchAgent, AgentLaboratory) and all six LLMs spanning 0.8B to 35B parameters, showing that increasing agent sophistication does not resolve the exploration deficit. - The missing: AI ideas use full proposal text while human papers are represented only by title and abstract—this representation asymmetry almost certainly inflates within-AI-idea similarity and deflates AI-to-human similarity, and the authors do not address it. - Read it or not: Essential reading for researchers building or evaluating AI-assisted ideation systems; less useful for those seeking actionable design fixes, which the paper does not provide.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Novelty (72) is the high point — the first population-level, distributional characterization of AI-agent ideation (vs. prior per-idea quality judgments) is a timely and important NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING, and the research-question vs. technical-method decomposition (Non-Obv 70) is a useful lens. The factorial scale (37,802 ideas, 24 agent-LLM combinations, finding stable across 0.8B–35B) is a real robustness argument. However, Rigor (55), Resilience (55), and Completeness (52) are held back by a serious unaddressed confound: AI ideas use full proposal text while human papers use only title+abstract, which mechanically inflates the very intra-AI similarity gap the paper reports, and the citation proxy covers only 6.2% of ideas. No code is released and it is a preprint. Impact (50) is empirical-only with no design fix offered. The qualitative conclusion is credible but the quantitative magnitudes are upper bounds. Weighted: 0.20*72 + 0.25*55 + 0.15*70 + 0.15*52 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*50 = 60.
empirical Novelty 72 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 70 Completeness 52 Resilience 55 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation AI research agent frameworks (AIScientist, ResearchAgent, AgentLaboratory, and others) can now generate research ideas, design experiments, and draft papers at scale, and their prompts explicitly instruct models to produce novel, high-impact, unconventional ideas. Prior evaluations of these systems focus on whether individual generated ideas are interesting, feasible, or novel to human judges. What is missing is a distributional account: does repeated AI-assisted ideation broaden the range of scientific directions explored, or does it concentrate output around existing work? This matters because scientific discovery depends on covering diverse and sometimes peripheral directions—not just producing locally coherent proposals near known work. Key Idea The core technical insight is to reframe AI ideation evaluation from per-idea quality judgments to population-level distributional analysis: embed all AI-generated ideas and all human papers from the same research areas into a common semantic space, then compare the spread (pairwise similarity, centroid distance), the directionality (distance from seed vs. distance from follow-on human work), and the composition of novelty (new research question vs. new technical method vs. recombination of existing methods). This distributional lens reveals behavior invisible to per-idea evaluation. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. First, 34,698 papers from ICLR, NeurIPS, and ICML (2019–2025) are collected with DBLP citation data; research areas are defined via bibliographic coupling (papers sharing references are similar), IDF-weighted paper–reference matrix projected to 128 dimensions via truncated SVD, and clustered with HDBSCAN (min_cluster_size=15, min_samples=5), yielding 19 active citation-defined areas spanning GNNs, RL, fairness, Bayesian optimization, and related topics. Second, four AI agent frameworks (zero-shot single-pass, AIScientist iterative self-reflection, ResearchAgent three-stage planning + validation, AgentLaboratory multi-agent postdoc/PhD dialogue) are each run with six LLMs over 2,140 bootstrapped five-paper seed sets, producing 51,360 total runs and 37,802 valid ideas. Third, all ideas and human papers are embedded with Qwen3-Embedding-4B and analyzed via four measurements: (1) pairwise cosine similarity within research area, (2) cosine similarity of ideas to seed papers vs. later human follow-on papers (defined as papers citing ≥2 seed papers), (3) citation statistics of human papers with cosine similarity >0.9 to AI ideas, and (4) research-question and technical-method extraction via Gemma-4-31B-IT followed by semantic matching at a manually calibrated 0.87 threshold. Three critical design decisions: using only title+abstract for human papers but full proposal text for AI ideas (an asymmetry the paper does not justify), restricting seed sets to five papers due to context-window constraints, and using a manually calibrated rather than data-driven novelty threshold. Key Results Within-area pairwise cosine similarity is 0.82–0.84 for AI-generated ideas versus 0.77 for human-authored papers across all agent frameworks and all six LLMs—a gap that persists uniformly (Figure 2). The centroid-distance alternative confirms this: AI ideas sit at mean distance 0.091 from the area centroid versus 0.121 for human papers. For temporal distance from seed literature, AI ideas have mean cosine similarity 0.92 to their seed papers; later human follow-on papers have only 0.88 to those same seeds—and AI ideas and human follow-on papers differ most (0.82), indicating they diverge in different directions. For citation impact, papers matched to AI ideas (2,359 pairs, cosine >0.9) average 50.4 citations versus a same-area baseline of 54.9 (difference: −4.47, 95% CI [−6.41, −2.53], p<0.001); AgentLaboratory shows the largest deficit (−7.98, p<0.001). For novelty composition, 85.1% of AI-generated ideas contain a research question already present in the five seed papers, while only 62.6% contain technical methods already present—AI ideas vary more at the method level (recombination) than at the question level. The narrowing pattern is remarkably stable across model families, parameter scales from 0.8B to 35B, and all four agent architectures. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. Prior work (Si et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2024) evaluates individual AI-generated ideas for novelty, feasibility, and quality using human judges; this paper provides the first population-level, distributional characterization of AI ideation behavior across a factorial design of agents and LLMs. The bibliographic-coupling methodology for defining research areas and the research-question/technical-method decomposition lens are applied tools from scientometrics, not invented here, but their combination in this evaluation context is genuinely new. Rigor & Reproducibility The factorial design (4 frameworks × 6 LLMs × 2,140 bootstrapped seed sets) and the consistency of findings across all 24 agent-LLM combinations constitute a strong robustness argument. The centroid-distance analysis (Appendix D) replicates the pairwise-similarity finding. Threshold sensitivity analysis for the 0.87 novelty boundary shows qualitative stability. Confidence intervals and one-sided p-values are reported for the citation comparison. No code, data, or model weights are released (not stated in the paper). Three methodological concerns: (1) AI ideas are represented by full proposal text (hundreds of words) while human papers use only title and abstract—this representation asymmetry will mechanically inflate within-AI similarity and suppress AI-to-human similarity, and the paper does not control for it; (2) the citation impact analysis covers only 2,359 of 37,802 ideas (6.2% match rate at similarity >0.9), and the 93.8% of ideas without a close human analog are entirely excluded—if those ideas differ systematically (more novel or simply lower quality), the citation proxy is severely biased; (3) the 19 research areas total only 1,035 papers, are restricted to AI/ML, and skew toward narrow specialized niches, limiting generalizability. This is a preprint. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The study uses open-weight LLMs (0.8B–35B) run locally; the methodology is reproducible by academic groups with GPU access. - Efficient: 51,360 generation runs represent substantial compute; no wall-clock or GPU-hour figures are stated. - Effective: All four measurement approaches converge consistently, lending credibility to the core finding that AI ideas are more concentrated than human ideas; however, the representation asymmetry is an unaddressed confounder. - Scalable: The study is restricted to 19 AI/ML research areas; whether findings generalize to broader, faster-moving, or cross-disciplinary fields is untested and explicitly acknowledged by the authors as a limitation. The work delivers only empirical findings—no software artifact, dataset, or trained model is released. Strengths - The factorial design at scale (37,802 ideas across 24 agent-LLM combinations) makes the consistency of the narrowing effect convincing; it is not a property of any single model or framework. - The research-question vs. technical-method decomposition is a genuinely useful novelty lens that separates local method recombination from genuine question-level exploration—this distinction is underexplored in the AI ideation literature. - The finding that more sophisticated agentic designs (multi-agent deliberation, iterative self-reflection) do not improve exploration breadth is practically important and challenges the implicit assumption that adding more agentic machinery solves this problem. - Using citation-defined research areas (bibliographic coupling) rather than keyword or venue labels provides a principled, data-driven basis for comparison that is less susceptible to definitional disagreements about what constitutes a research area. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: (1) the corpus is restricted to AI/ML conferences (ICLR, NeurIPS, ICML), so findings may not generalize to biology, physics, or social science; (2) only the ideation stage is evaluated, not full experimental execution or paper writing; (3) the citation proxy is indirect—matched human papers, not actual AI-authored papers, are used; (4) context-window constraints forced five-paper seed sets, which may limit the breadth of input context available to agents. The authors confront these limitations clearly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The representation asymmetry (AI ideas: full proposal text; human papers: title+abstract only) is the most serious unaddressed methodological flaw. Longer, more repetitive proposal text will produce higher intra-corpus similarity for AI ideas regardless of true conceptual concentration. The paper makes no attempt to equate input text length or to test whether the gap persists when human papers are represented by their full text. - The citation-impact proxy covers only 6.2% of AI ideas (2,359 of 37,802). The large majority of ideas—those that do not closely match any existing human paper—are excluded. This is precisely the set that might be most novel (or most incoherent), and its exclusion makes the citation finding uninterpretable as a claim about AI ideation quality broadly. - The AIScientist's literature-search tool is served from a local corpus restricted to papers before time t—a setup that handicaps it relative to production use (where it would access Semantic Scholar). Results for AIScientist may not reflect its behavior in deployment. - No analysis of heterogeneity across the 19 research areas: do smaller, more mature niches show more concentration than broader active ones? If concentration is driven by seed-set diversity (i.e., areas with fewer seed papers force tighter clustering), this would be a confound rather than a property of AI ideation per se. - The paper compares AI ideas against human papers from the same research areas, but the human papers were written after the seed period and have the benefit of actual experimental results, conference peer review, and cross-pollination with other fields. The more appropriate comparison might be human-authored research proposals (grant applications, workshop position papers), which would share the same pre-experimental, text-only format as AI-generated ideas. Bottom Line This paper provides the most comprehensive population-level evidence to date that AI research agents concentrate rather than diversify scientific exploration—a finding robust across agents and LLMs—but the unaddressed representation asymmetry and narrow citation-proxy coverage mean the quantitative magnitudes should be treated as upper-bound estimates rather than precise measurements; nonetheless, the qualitative conclusion is credible and the paper is worth reading for anyone building or deploying AI-assisted research systems.
Recommended 2605.27905 PDF
#106 — Innovative (60)

Toward Native Multimodal Modeling: A Roadmap (30 ▲ )

Siyu An, Junru Lu, Junnan Dong, Qiufeng Wang, Yinghui Li, Weizhi Fei, Zichao Yu, Zheng Yuan, Biao Liu, Haopeng Wang, Renzhao Liang, Yixuan Yang, Yunhang Shen, Bo Ke, Keyu Chen, Linhao Luo, Difan Zou, Xiao Huang, Di Yin, Ruizhi Qiao, Xing Sun · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a formal taxonomy and end-to-end roadmap for Native Multimodal Modeling (NMM), organizing 40+ recent models along two orthogonal axes — fusion depth (mid-fusion vs. early-fusion) and input-output duality (Multi-to-Text / Multi-to-Target / Multi-to-Multi) — and systematically extracting regime-specific engineering prescriptions across architecture, data, training, inference, and evaluation. - Why it's important: The NMM design space has expanded explosively since 2023 but lacks principled formalization, making it impossible to rigorously compare architectural nativity, select appropriate training recipes, or identify which design decisions are fundamental versus incidental — a gap that hinders both research and industrial deployment. - How it does it: The authors define nativity through functional operators (F_late, F_mid, F_early), show that architectural fusion boundary is not merely structural but a training-regime determiner, and map the resulting engineering prescriptions (differential LRs for mid-fusion; QK-Norm, z-loss, and modality-mixture scheduling for early-fusion) to specific failure modes with primary-source evidence. - The good: The Training × Fusion grid (Figure 5) is the clearest single contribution — it establishes that each architectural transition introduces mandatory (not optional) engineering preconditions and co-emergent failure modes, grounding abstract architectural distinctions in concrete, practitioner-actionable consequences. - The missing: No new experiments are run and no quantitative controlled comparison between mid-fusion and early-fusion models on matched benchmarks is provided, so the paper's framing of early-fusion as the "optimal pinnacle" remains architectural aspiration rather than demonstrated empirical result. - Read it or not: Essential for ML engineers and researchers designing or evaluating multimodal foundation models; skippable for those outside the multimodal LLM space, who will find more targeted literature on individual sub-topics.
Why this rank: Scored using Theoretical profile (survey/formalization, no system built). Novelty (70) and Non-Obviousness (72) carry the score at 0.25 each — the two-axis taxonomy (fusion depth × modality flow), the 'architecture dictates training recipe' framing, and the structural derivation of three early-fusion RL failure modes are genuinely new organizing contributions, not a flat model enumeration. Rigor (50, theoretical sense = assumption honesty) is pulled down because the work runs no experiments, treats vendor self-reports and preprints identically to refereed results, draws heavily on institution-affiliated systems, and asserts early-fusion as the 'optimal pinnacle' while the actual SOTA models cited are mid-fusion. Resilience (50) suffers from that same unsupported superiority claim, which collapses under scrutiny. Completeness (55) is aided by directly confronted scope limitations but limited by the quantified cost-asymmetry gap and single-case OPD generalization; Impact (60, here = influence on follow-up and actionability) reflects a directly usable engineering-prescription grid. Weighted: 0.25*70 + 0.20*50 + 0.25*72 + 0.10*55 + 0.15*50 + 0.05*60 = 60.
theoretical Novelty 70 Rigor 50 Non-Obv 72 Completeness 55 Resilience 50 Impact 60
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation The rapid expansion of multimodal LLMs has produced a fragmented landscape spanning frozen-encoder late-fusion systems (LLaVA, DeepSeek-VL), joint-backbone mid-fusion models (Qwen-VL, InternVL, CogVLM), and shared-embedding-space early-fusion architectures (Chameleon, Transfusion, AnyGPT). Without a principled taxonomy, practitioners cannot rigorously evaluate "how native" a model is, cannot determine which training decisions are architecture-mandated versus freely tunable, and cannot identify whether apparent performance gaps stem from architectural choice, data curriculum, or post-training recipe. The field has accumulated dozens of concurrent systems with overlapping design claims and no common vocabulary for comparison. The problem is acute for industrial practitioners choosing between fusion regimes: the cost of architectural misjudgment at the pretraining stage is enormous. Key Idea The core insight is that architectural fusion depth is a training-regime determiner, not merely a structural property. Each fusion boundary (late→mid→early) imposes a distinct set of engineering preconditions that are failure-preventing requirements rather than optional refinements: mid-fusion mandates differential learning rates once gradients reach the encoder; early-fusion mandates QK-Norm and z-loss regularization (Chameleon diverges without them after ~20% of training) and treats modality-mixture scheduling as the analog of differential LRs. This "architecture dictates training recipe" framing, applied consistently across PT, SFT, RL, and OPD stages, distinguishes the paper from a flat model enumeration survey. Methodology The paper is a structured survey with formal definitions and no original experiments. Nativity is formalized via functional operators: F_late = G(LLM({P_i(E_i(m_i))})) where the backbone is blind to raw signals; F_mid = Backbone(C(E_1(m_1),...,E_n(m_n))) where cross-modal alignment injects features into a joint backbone; F_early = Transformer(⋃_i T(m_i)) where a single tokenizer maps all modalities into a shared embedding space from step zero. The output taxonomy applies FM2T: M→T, FM2G: M→y_k, FM2M: M_in→M_out. Analysis proceeds through five pipeline stages: (1) architecture — modality unification, multi-scale encoding, AR vs. diffusion, discrete vs. continuous tokenization; (2) data — four functional categories (understanding-, generation-, interaction-, preference-oriented) with stage-dependent mixture curricula; (3) training — a PT×SFT×RL×OPD × fusion-regime grid identifying regime-specific axes (progressive unfreezing, differential LRs, freezing rewiring privilege for mid-fusion; joint-from-start, unified NTP, mandatory stabilizers, modality-mixture scheduling for early-fusion; full-policy RL scope with three co-emergent failure modes for early-fusion RL); (4) inference/deployment — token compression, MoE routing, full-duplex streaming; (5) evaluation — benchmark landscape organized by modality and task type. All claims are derived from surveyed technical reports, most of which are preprints. Key Results There are no original quantitative results. Key empirical facts extracted from surveyed literature include: Chameleon's training diverges without QK-Norm after ~20% of pretraining; Emu3.5's Discrete Diffusion Adaptation delivers ~20× inference acceleration for single-image generation vs. serial AR decoding; ResAdapt eliminates >90% of visual tokens while processing 16× more frames and achieves >15% relative gains on complex long-video reasoning; Kimi K2.5's agent-swarm mode achieves 4.5× processing efficiency on long-video tasks; InternVL-3.5's Visual Resolution Router cuts token redundancy by 50% by assigning 256 tokens to semantically rich patches and 64 to backgrounds; Moshi targets 200ms latency for real-time full-duplex dialogue. The model comparison (Table 1) catalogs 40+ models from late 2023 to May 2026 across parameter counts from 0.8B to 1T+, input/output modality sets, and functional categories. Baselines are not compared directly; per-model numbers come from authors' own technical reports. Novelty Classification NEW FORMALIZATION + SURVEY. The two-dimensional taxonomy (fusion depth × modality flow) and the regime-specific training prescription grid are genuinely new organizing frameworks not previously consolidated in the literature. The identification of early-fusion RL failure modes (visual-grounding hacking, see-saw multi-objective regression, conflation of perceptual vs. logical errors under a unified softmax) and their structural derivation from architectural properties is a substantive intellectual contribution. All underlying techniques — transformers, tokenization, RL, diffusion — are existing; the novel element is the synthesis and the causal linkage between architectural choices and training failure modes. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations, no error bars, no code released. All empirical claims are sourced from cited works, the majority of which are arXiv preprints (many are Tencent-affiliated). The paper does not distinguish peer-reviewed results from vendor self-reports — a significant concern given that several key cited systems (Kimi K2.5, GLM-5V-Turbo, MiMo-V2.5, Ming-Flash-Omni-2.0) are commercial technical reports from the authors' institution or close collaborators, and their performance claims have not been independently verified. The formal definitions are internally consistent, but the claim that early-fusion is the "optimal pinnacle" is asserted rather than demonstrated: the highest-performing models on standard benchmarks as of mid-2026 (Qwen3-VL, InternVL-3.5, Kimi K2.5) are mid-fusion, not early-fusion. Selection bias in model coverage is possible given institutional affiliations of the 20-person author list. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: THEORETICAL (survey/formalization; no system is built or evaluated by the authors). - Sound: The formal definitions are mathematically clean; the regime-specific prescriptions are grounded in primary sources with specific failure modes cited (Chameleon divergence, Janus-Pro unfreeze schedule). No formal proofs are required or claimed. - General: The taxonomy covers text, image, audio, and video across comprehension and generation under a single framework. It does not yet extend to embodied sensing (proprioception, depth, tactile), structured modalities (code, graphs, 3D scenes), or continuous-time signals — the authors explicitly acknowledge this scope boundary. - Tight: As a taxonomy, tightness means coverage without overlap. The two axes appear to partition the existing model space cleanly, but the mid-fusion/early-fusion boundary blurs for hybrid-loss models (Transfusion, Show-o2) that use a shared transformer backbone with both AR and diffusion objectives. - Implementable: Yes — the engineering prescriptions are directly actionable: differential LRs (e.g., 1/10 base rate for encoders in mid-fusion), QK-Norm + z-loss (10^−5 · log² Z) for early-fusion stability, progressive resolution curricula (224→448→1344+ px), OPD as a terminal consolidation step. The paper stops at paper form; no library, code, or model weights are released. Strengths - The Training × Fusion grid (Figure 5) synthesizes regime-specific engineering prescriptions (differential LRs, freezing rewiring privilege, modality-mixture scheduling, full-policy RL scope) into a single actionable reference that makes previously implicit architectural necessities explicit. - The functional operator formalization (F_late, F_mid, F_early) gives the field reusable definitional vocabulary applicable to new models as they emerge — a useful foundation for rigorous future comparisons. - The identification and structural derivation of three early-fusion RL failure modes (visual-grounding hacking, see-saw regression, perceptual/logical error conflation) from the shared softmax architecture is a substantive analytical contribution beyond enumeration. - The coverage genuinely spans the full NMM lifecycle (40+ models, 5 pipeline stages, 3 modality groups) with regime-specific analysis rather than flat description — substantially more structured than prior survey papers in this space. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly flag: (1) the taxonomy does not extend beyond the four canonical modalities to embodied signals or structured data (§8.1); (2) aligned multi-stream data with synchronized audio, transcripts, and reasoning traces remains the hardest data bottleneck (§8.2); (3) modality-balanced optimization relies on heuristic mixture ratios with no theoretical grounding (§8.3); (4) existing evaluation benchmarks assess modalities in isolation and ignore response timing, compute cost, and streaming robustness (§8.5). The authors confront these limitations directly and frame them as future research directions, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The paper frames early-fusion as the "optimal pinnacle" of NMM but never demonstrates this empirically: the best-performing models on standard benchmarks (Qwen3-VL, InternVL-3.5) are mid-fusion, and no controlled comparison on matched benchmarks is provided. The superiority claim is architectural philosophy, not measured fact. - Vendor self-reports and preprints are treated identically to peer-reviewed results throughout. Many of the most favorably cited systems (GLM-5V-Turbo, MiMo-V2.5, Ming-Flash-Omni-2.0) are from Tencent or close industry partners, creating potential selection and confirmation bias in characterizing the state of the art. - The practical cost asymmetry between mid-fusion and early-fusion is not quantified: early-fusion requires training from scratch on a unified vocabulary (no warm-starting from a pretrained LLM), which is substantially more expensive than mid-fusion's progressive encoder unfreezing from existing checkpoints. This constraint is critical for practitioners but is absent from the analysis. - The OPD section generalizes from a single deployment (MiMo-V2.5, a Tencent model) to a proposed universal post-RL primitive. One case study is insufficient evidence for this generalization. Bottom Line Practitioners designing multimodal foundation model pipelines will find the training × fusion grid and regime-specific failure mode taxonomy the most structured and actionable single reference available as of mid-2026, making this a high-value read despite its lack of original experiments and some promotional framing of early-fusion architectures.
Recommended 2605.25343 PDF
#107 — Moderately Innovative (59)

PALS: Power-Aware LLM Serving for Mixture-of-Experts Models

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces PALS, a power-aware LLM serving runtime integrated into vLLM that treats GPU power caps as a first-class scheduling knob and jointly optimizes them with batch size via a 500ms closed-loop controller to maximize tokens/joule under throughput QoS constraints. - Why it's important: Data centers running LLM inference face facility-level power caps, real-time electricity pricing, and demand-response obligations, yet every existing serving system (vLLM, Orca, Sarathi-Serve, etc.) treats GPU power as a static external constraint, leaving a systematic efficiency gap that grows with MoE adoption. - How it does it: An offline random-forest model maps (power cap, batch size, parallelism) tuples to predicted throughput and tokens/joule; at runtime a PID feedback controller corrects for prediction error and selects the efficiency-maximizing feasible configuration every 500 ms via NVML power-cap actuation and vLLM scheduler hooks. - The good: On five MoE models across single- and multi-node A100 configurations, PALS achieves 1.26× normalized efficiency over a max-power baseline and captures 95% of the exhaustive oracle's headroom, while reducing QoS violation rates from 18.7%–35.2% (baseline) to 3.2%–5.3%. - The missing: QoS is defined as a throughput fraction threshold rather than per-request tail latency (p99 TTFT or TBT), the metric used in virtually all production LLM SLAs; it is unknown whether PALS's batch-size reductions worsen tail latency even when aggregate throughput is satisfied. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML infrastructure and data center power management researchers; the empirical characterization of how MoE compute-communication ratios interact with power caps is the most durable contribution, even if the control system itself is straightforward.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Practical Impact (62, the dominant 30% weight) reflects a clean plug-and-play vLLM integration on commodity A100 hardware with a directly actionable MoE power-knee result, but is capped by the absence of any public code/profiling-data release. Novelty (65) and Non-Obviousness (62) credit the genuinely new ~200W efficiency-knee finding for communication-bound MoE, though the offline-model + PID control architecture is a standard combination. Rigor (55) and Resilience (52) are the limiters: no error bars or seeds, a random forest trained on the same configs it is evaluated on (overfitting risk), and the critical tail-latency omission — QoS is defined as aggregate-throughput fraction rather than p99 TTFT/TBT, the metric real SLAs use, which a tough reviewer would attack directly. Weighted: 0.15*65 + 0.15*55 + 0.10*62 + 0.15*58 + 0.15*52 + 0.30*62 ≈ 59.
systems Novelty 65 Rigor 55 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 58 Resilience 52 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM inference has become a sustained, high-power workload in data centers, increasingly subject to facility-level power caps, real-time electricity pricing, and grid demand-response (DR) signals. In MoE architectures specifically, tokens are dynamically routed to a subset of experts, creating highly variable workloads where inference is often communication-bound (NVLink/PCIe all-to-all exchanges) rather than compute-bound. The gap PALS closes: all prior serving systems—Orca, vLLM, Sarathi-Serve, DistServe, AlpaServe—treat GPU power as a static provisioning constraint and optimize only software knobs (scheduling, batching, parallelism) within a fixed power envelope. As a result, they either over-provision power (wasting energy) or under-provision (degrading QoS), with no mechanism to dynamically trade performance for power in response to workload changes or external signals. Key Idea The core insight is that GPU power caps and batch size occupy complementary regions of the throughput-efficiency Pareto frontier: power-cap reduction alone trades throughput for efficiency (hardware-only), while batch-size increase improves both throughput and efficiency simultaneously but is bounded by the fixed power budget (software-only). The two frontiers do not overlap—they are additive. Jointly controlling both knobs (HW+SW) strictly dominates either alone by filling the gap between their respective frontiers. A secondary, model-specific insight is that the optimal power cap depends on each model's compute-communication ratio: for communication-bound MoE models (Qwen-MoE, OLMoE), increasing power beyond ~200 W accelerates NCCL all-to-all overhead rather than computation, so lower power caps actually improve tokens/joule. Methodology PALS operates as a plug-and-play layer above vLLM with three components. (1) Telemetry layer: collects GPU power (NVML/IPMI), throughput (tokens/s), and utilization at runtime. System-level power is estimated from GPU telemetry via a linear fit (P_sys = α·ΣP_GPU,i + β, with α≈1.05, β≈340–350 W, R²≈0.98, MAE 12–27 W). (2) Offline power-performance model: a random forest trained on a controlled parameter sweep over power caps (150–400 W in 6 levels), batch sizes (1, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64), tensor parallelism TP∈{1,2,4}, expert parallelism EP∈{1,4,8}, and data parallelism DP∈{1,2,3} nodes. The model achieves 6.8% MAPE for throughput and 4.5% MAPE for power on held-out configurations. Batch size is the dominant predictor, followed by power cap and TP. (3) Runtime control loop: every 500 ms, the controller evaluates all feasible configurations using the offline model, selects the efficiency-maximizing (tokens/joule) configuration satisfying a throughput target T_target, and falls back to the max-throughput configuration when no feasible configuration exists. A PID feedback controller corrects for steady-state prediction error, and reconfiguration is gated by a 5% error threshold to suppress oscillation. Critical design decisions: (a) restricting runtime-dynamic knobs to power cap and batch size only (TP/EP/DP are static, requiring model reload)—well-justified by deployment realism; (b) using a discrete configuration lookup rather than continuous optimization—justified by the small configuration space and fast inference; (c) the 500 ms control interval—asserted but not validated against workload variability timescales. Key Results All experiments use NVIDIA A100 GPUs (4×A100 per node, NVLink). Models tested span dense (GPT-2, Llama-2-7B, Mistral-7B) and MoE (OLMoE-1B-7B, Qwen1.5-MoE, DeepSeek-MoE, Phi-3.5-MoE, Mixtral-8x7B). Workloads use Poisson arrival processes on HellaSwag and GSM8K. Baselines are: Baseline (400 W, BS=64), Adaptive Batch (dynamic batch, fixed 400 W), Adaptive Cap (dynamic power cap, fixed batch), and Oracle (exhaustive offline optimum). Single-node efficiency: PALS achieves 1.26× normalized tokens/joule over baseline, capturing 95% of oracle headroom; Batch Only accounts for 1.14× of that gain, with power cap adding the remainder. Multi-node QoS (3 nodes, 4,800 W cluster budget, 60-minute runs): baseline QoS violation rates are 18.7%–35.2% across models; PALS reduces these to 3.2%–5.3%, a 4×–7× improvement, while improving aggregate efficiency by 12.1%. Demand-response tracking: over a 1-hour DR event on DeepSeek-MoE (3 nodes), PALS improves throughput by up to 22% at low power targets relative to a static-batch baseline, while tracking the power signal equally well. Counter-intuitive finding: for Qwen-MoE and OLMoE, efficiency peaks at 200 W and declines at higher power caps—running at maximum GPU power is actively harmful for communication-bound MoE models. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The claim to be the first LLM serving runtime to jointly optimize runtime GPU power caps with batch size under QoS constraints is credible—prior work (Zeus, BatchDVFS) optimized power for DNN training or single-GPU inference, not multi-GPU autoregressive LLM serving with MoE communication dynamics. The empirical finding that communication-bound MoE models exhibit declining efficiency beyond a ~200 W power cap is genuinely novel and practically important. The control system architecture (offline model + PID feedback) is a standard combination of known techniques applied to a new setting. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations comparing Cap Only, Batch Only, PALS, and Oracle directly quantify the contribution of each control dimension—this is the strongest methodological element. The Pareto frontier expansion plots (Fig. 3) cleanly demonstrate the HW+SW dominance claim. However, no error bars, multiple seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported for any result; all numbers are point estimates. The 60-minute experiment duration is reasonable but short for capturing diurnal load variation. No code or model weights are released (no repo URL mentioned). The offline profiling dataset is not released. A methodological concern: the random forest model is trained on the same hardware/model configurations used in evaluation, which risks overfitting to the profiling setup rather than generalizing to unseen workloads or hardware; the paper acknowledges this qualitatively in the Discussion but does not quantify generalization error. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.21427v1, May 2026) and has not undergone peer review. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — PALS is a built tool integrated into vLLM, evaluated by deployment on real hardware. - Practical: Runs on standard NVIDIA A100 multi-GPU nodes using NVML interfaces; requires no model retraining or API changes, so deployment is feasible on commercially available hardware without modification. - Efficient: The control overhead is claimed to be negligible (500 ms intervals, random forest lookup), but no measurement of the controller's own CPU/memory overhead or latency added to the inference path is reported. - Effective: On the tested models and workloads, PALS demonstrably solves the stated problem—maintaining QoS while improving energy efficiency—but QoS is measured as aggregate throughput fraction, not per-request tail latency (p99 TTFT/TBT), which is the metric actual LLM SLAs are written against. - Scalable: Tested up to 3 nodes (12 A100s); the paper claims the design is complementary to cluster-level systems like DynamoLLM for larger deployments but does not evaluate beyond 3 nodes, so scaling behavior at data-center scale (hundreds of nodes) is unverified. The work delivers a software artifact (modifications to vLLM) but no public release is mentioned. Strengths - The Pareto frontier analysis (Section 2.2, Fig. 3) is concrete and well-executed: constructing three separate frontiers (HW-only, SW-only, HW+SW) and showing strict dominance of the joint approach is a compelling and honest presentation of the core claim. - The model-specific finding that communication-bound MoE models exhibit declining efficiency above ~200 W is a non-obvious empirical result with direct operational implications—operators of Qwen-MoE or OLMoE clusters are actively wasting energy by running at maximum power. - The system integrates cleanly into vLLM without API or model changes, which dramatically lowers the adoption barrier; the plug-and-play property is real, not just claimed. - Covering five MoE architectures with very different compute/communication profiles (Mixtral as compute-bound, Qwen-MoE/OLMoE as communication-bound) gives the efficiency characterization meaningful breadth. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state three limitations: (1) the offline prediction model may mispredict when workloads deviate significantly from profiling conditions (long prompts, adversarial inputs, output-length distribution shifts), with PID correction only partially compensating; (2) PALS operates at node level and is not a replacement for cluster-level systems like DynamoLLM; (3) tensor parallelism is a static deployment-time knob, not dynamically adjustable at runtime, leaving residual efficiency headroom for some models under low power caps. These are confronted directly and honestly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Tail latency omission: QoS is defined as the fraction of time aggregate throughput falls below a target—a server-side metric. Production LLM SLAs are almost universally written in terms of per-request TTFT (time to first token) and TBT (time between tokens) at p99. Reducing batch size to save power may fragment batches and worsen tail latency even when aggregate throughput is met. This gap is nowhere addressed. - Profiling cost and staleness: The offline model is trained on a specific set of prompt distributions and sequence lengths. The paper does not state how large this profiling dataset is, how long profiling takes, or how frequently it must be refreshed as production traffic characteristics drift. For operators, this is a real maintenance burden. - Single GPU generation only: All experiments use A100 GPUs; it is unstated whether the model transfers to H100/H200 (different NVLink bandwidth, TF32/FP8 throughput curves, different power/compute knee points). The efficiency curves may look qualitatively different on newer hardware. - PID controller stability analysis: The 5% error threshold and control interval are stated but not validated; no analysis is provided of controller stability, oscillation frequency, or convergence time under step changes in load or power budget. - Missing comparison with DynamoLLM on per-node efficiency: DynamoLLM is the most directly related cluster-level system; Table 3 lists it but does not include a head-to-head experiment, making it impossible to assess whether PALS's node-level gains actually add beyond what DynamoLLM already captures via GPU frequency scaling. Bottom Line A practitioner running MoE LLM inference under power constraints—particularly with Qwen-MoE or OLMoE on NVLink-connected A100s—gains a directly actionable and deployable result: the paper's characterization of the MoE power-efficiency knee and the vLLM integration are worth reading, but the absence of tail latency analysis and the lack of a public code release limit immediate adoption.
Recommended 2605.21427 PDF
#108 — Moderately Innovative (58)

See What I Mean: Aligning Vision and Language Representations for Video Fine-grained Object Understanding (9 ▲ )

Boyuan Sun, Bowen Yin, Yuanming Li, Xihan Wei, Qibin Hou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SWIM (See What I Mean), a supervised fine-tuning strategy that aligns cross-attention maps for object noun tokens with ground-truth segmentation masks so that video MLLMs can ground user-specified objects from text prompts alone at inference, without requiring visual prompt inputs. - Why it's important: Existing fine-grained video understanding systems require users to supply masks, points, or bounding boxes at inference time, creating friction that diverges from how users naturally describe objects in text; eliminating this requirement lowers the barrier to deployment and improves usability. - How it does it: During training, the cross-attention weights from a tagged object noun token to visual tokens are aggregated across six evenly-spaced transformer layers, then supervised with a pixel-wise binary cross-entropy loss against the corresponding ground-truth instance mask. - The good: SWIM achieves 78.3% average on VideoRefer-Bench-Q and 3.78 on VideoRefer-Bench-D, surpassing the strongest specialist baseline DAM-8B (3.68) and GPT-4o (3.25) without requiring visual prompts at inference, while using only 235K training examples (less than one-third of VideoRefer's training data). - The missing: The method is evaluated on only one base model (Qwen2.5-VL-7B), and both the training data (NL-Refer, derived from VideoRefer-700K) and the evaluation benchmark (VideoRefer-Bench) originate from the same VideoRefer dataset ecosystem, raising a mild circular evaluation concern. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working on text-driven visual grounding or fine-grained video understanding; the training trick is simple and the results are credible, though practitioners should verify generalization beyond the single tested architecture.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. Solid, architecturally non-invasive NEW METHOD (Novelty 62) with thorough ablations across layer count, fusion, and loss, plus released code/data and a proactive synonym-robustness check. The composite is pulled to the moderate band by Completeness (52) and Resilience (55): the work is evaluated on a single base model (Qwen2.5-VL-7B), both training (NL-Refer) and the primary benchmark (VideoRefer-Bench) originate from the same VideoRefer ecosystem (circular-evaluation concern), there is no text-only-data ablation to isolate the attention loss from richer descriptions, and no explicit limitations section. Credible results, but generalizability beyond the tested architecture is unverified.
empirical Novelty 62 Rigor 58 Non-Obv 62 Completeness 52 Resilience 55 Impact 62
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Multimodal large language models excel at holistic scene understanding but fail to reliably attend to specific objects named in user text prompts. Current specialist systems address this by injecting explicit visual cues — pixel masks, point coordinates, or bounding boxes — at inference time. This approach increases system complexity, requires users to supply additional inputs, and deviates from natural language interaction. The gap is not merely behavioral: cross-attention analysis of Qwen2.5-VL reveals that attribute words (colors, textures) produce sharp, localized activations aligned to the correct visual region, while object nouns produce diffuse, scattered patterns. This misalignment is attributed to the distributional properties of large-scale pretraining data, where object nouns appear in diverse contexts and lose spatial specificity. Key Idea The core insight is that the poor spatial specificity of object noun cross-attention can be corrected by directly supervising those attention weights against segmentation masks during fine-tuning, without touching the model architecture. At inference the same model then grounds object nouns precisely from text alone, because the attention mechanism has been shaped to associate each noun token with its visual region. Methodology The pipeline has two components. First, the NL-Refer dataset is constructed by processing VideoRefer-700K: GPT-4o replaces the visual-prompt placeholder token `<region>` in each human turn with a concise natural-language referring expression derived from the GPT response describing the object. GPT-4o further identifies the single most representative object noun within the expression and marks it with `<ins>` delimiters, creating a deterministic link between token identity and the paired instance mask. The resulting dataset contains 125K video–text–mask triples. Second, during supervised fine-tuning on Qwen2.5-VL-7B (SIGLIP visual encoder + Qwen2.5 LLM decoder), cross-attention weights from the tagged noun token's query vector to all visual key vectors are extracted from a selected set of transformer layers S at each forward pass. The per-layer attention maps are spatially interpolated to match mask resolution and averaged across layers. This aggregated map is then supervised with pixel-wise binary cross-entropy (BCE) against the ground-truth mask. The total training loss is the standard autoregressive text loss plus the BCE alignment term. Key design decisions: (1) six evenly-spaced layers (e.g., [1, 6, 11, 16, 21, 26]) outperform both fewer layers and densely clustered late layers — justified by ablation (3.43 at single layer → 3.78 at six layers, plateau thereafter); (2) mean aggregation across layers outperforms addition, pooling, and element-wise product — justified by the argument that mean smooths noise without over-suppressing moderate activations; (3) BCE outperforms mIoU, Focal, and Dice losses — attributed to the sparsity of softmax-normalized attention maps, where BCE's per-pixel uniform penalty enforces suppression of diffuse activations. Key Results On VideoRefer-Bench-Q (198 videos, 1,000 multiple-choice questions from DAVIS-2017 and MeViS), SWIM achieves 78.3% average accuracy, outperforming VideoRefer-7B (71.9%), Qwen2.5-VL-7B (71.8%), and GPT-4o (71.3%). Gains are largest on Basic (+5.8% over Qwen2.5-VL-7B) and Sequential (+5.3%) subtasks. On VideoRefer-Bench-D (400 entries from Panda-70M, scored 0–5 on Subject Correspondence, Appearance Description, Temporal Description, Hallucination Detection), SWIM scores 3.78 average, beating DAM-8B (3.68, best prior specialist), VideoRefer-7B (3.46), and GPT-4o (3.25). On general video benchmarks, SWIM scores 62.1 on MVBench, 55.9 on VideoMME, and 55.6 on ActivityNet-QA, matching or slightly exceeding VideoRefer-7B (59.6, 55.9, N/A) without regression. Attention quality metrics versus Qwen2.5-VL-7B: AUC 0.62→0.67, NSS 0.39→0.50, Precision 0.28→0.39, AP 0.26→0.30. GamePoint@P-1 improves from 0.329 to 0.392 (+6.3%). Scalability holds monotonically from 30K to 125K mask-annotated training samples with no plateau, suggesting the method is data-hungry and would benefit from more annotations. Synonym perturbation robustness: SWIM drops from 3.78 to 3.74 under synonym substitution (marginal), compared to Qwen2.5-VL-7B at 3.43 under the same perturbation. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET. The contribution is a training-time attention regularization strategy applied to object noun tokens in a video MLLM, plus the NL-Refer dataset constructed to enable it. The observation that object nouns produce diffuse cross-attention is not entirely new (related work in visual grounding and VLM interpretability has documented related phenomena), but applying explicit mask-supervised attention regularization specifically to object noun tokens in video MLLMs during SFT, yielding inference without visual prompts, is a genuine methodological contribution. The NL-Refer dataset construction (GPT-4o-powered placeholder replacement) is an engineering contribution, not a fundamentally novel data collection method. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present and informative: layer count, layer positions, fusion methods, and loss functions are each varied. However, no error bars, confidence intervals, or multi-seed results are reported — all numbers are point estimates. The synonym robustness experiment (Table 8) is a positive addition. Code and data are stated to be released at https://github.com/HumanMLLM/SWIM. Methodological concern: NL-Refer is derived from VideoRefer-700K, and the primary fine-grained evaluation benchmark (VideoRefer-Bench) is also part of the VideoRefer ecosystem, raising a mild data-leakage-adjacent concern even though the benchmark itself is held out. Evaluation is on a single base model, so generalization to other MLLM architectures is undemonstrated. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.18018v1, May 2026); peer review has not occurred. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Training runs on 8× A100 GPUs with 235K examples, which is accessible for academic labs; inference requires no extra hardware beyond the base 7B model. - Efficient: SWIM adds a BCE attention supervision term during SFT only; at inference, no additional computation beyond standard MLLM forward pass is incurred, so runtime cost is identical to the base Qwen2.5-VL-7B model. - Effective: The evidence shows improvement on fine-grained video object description and QA benchmarks with consistent attention localization gains; the proxy (VideoRefer-Bench) is directly relevant to the stated task, though it originates from the same data ecosystem as training. - Scalable: Scaling tested only from 30K to 125K mask-annotated samples on a single 7B model; no test at larger model scales or with data beyond 125K; the absence of a plateau at 125K is cited as an indicator of further potential, but this is a claim, not a measurement. The work delivers a software artifact (code and dataset released) and a trained model (fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL-7B checkpoint implied but not explicitly confirmed as released). Strengths - The diagnostic finding — that object noun cross-attention is systematically more diffuse than attribute word attention — is well-supported visually and quantitatively, and motivates the proposed fix cleanly. - The inference-time simplicity is a genuine practical advantage: eliminating the need for visual prompts at inference reduces pipeline complexity without sacrificing performance relative to mask-dependent specialists. - Ablations across layer selection, fusion methods, and loss functions are thorough and provide actionable design guidance. - The synonym robustness experiment (Table 8) proactively tests a potential failure mode and shows minimal degradation, strengthening the credibility of the alignment claim. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors do not state explicit limitations in the main text or a dedicated limitations section. They note that performance on general benchmarks is "within a competitive range" rather than leading, implying a task-specialization tradeoff, but do not frame this as a limitation. The absence of a plateau at 125K is stated as suggesting untapped potential rather than as a limitation of the current work. The authors do not acknowledge the circular evaluation concern, the single-architecture evaluation scope, or the GPT-4o-induced noise in NL-Refer construction. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Single-architecture evaluation: all experiments use Qwen2.5-VL-7B as the base. Whether the attention regularization strategy transfers to architectures with different cross-attention designs (e.g., cross-attention in separate modules vs. unified self-attention over concatenated tokens) is untested and non-obvious. - Data circularity: NL-Refer is derived from VideoRefer-700K; VideoRefer-Bench is also from the VideoRefer ecosystem (Panda-70M and DAVIS/MeViS subsets selected by the same authors). While the held-out split prevents direct leakage, the benchmark distribution likely overlaps with training distribution more than an independently constructed benchmark would. - No text-only baseline ablation: the paper does not report a variant trained without the BCE loss but with the NL-Refer data alone (only natural language referring expressions, no attention supervision). This would isolate how much gain comes from richer text descriptions versus the attention loss. - GPT-4o NL-Refer quality: the referring expression generation process is described but no quality analysis (human evaluation, error rate, or coverage check) is provided. - The claim that the attention misalignment for object nouns is due to semantic distributional bias in pretraining data is a plausible hypothesis but not experimentally validated; alternative explanations (e.g., tokenization granularity, positional encoding) are not considered. Bottom Line SWIM delivers a practically useful, architecturally non-invasive improvement to video MLLM fine-grained object grounding, with solid ablations and credible benchmark numbers, but the single-model evaluation and the circular data ecosystem limit confidence in broad generalizability — researchers applying this to non-Qwen2.5-VL architectures should treat the results as provisional until confirmed.
Recommended 2605.18018 PDF
#109 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Eyes on VLM: Benchmarking Gaze Following and Social Gaze Prediction in Vision Language Models

Hengfei Wang, Anshul Gupta, Pierre Vuillecard, Jean-Marc Odobez · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces EyeVLM, a systematic evaluation framework that benchmarks general-purpose vision-language models (VLMs) on gaze following and social gaze prediction under zero-shot prompting and supervised fine-tuning, comparing them against specialized vision-only gaze models. - Why it's important: Gaze understanding is central to human behavior analysis (autism screening, HRI, social robotics), yet no prior work had systematically characterized whether modern VLMs can replace or complement specialized gaze models across both geometric localization and relational social reasoning tasks. - How it does it: Tasks are reformulated as VQA with JSON-structured outputs, four prompting strategies of increasing guidance (base, CoT, structured CoT, in-context CoT) are compared zero-shot, and LoRA fine-tuning (rank 8) on constructed QA pairs is applied to Qwen3-VL variants across three datasets. - The good: Structured CoT prompting cuts gaze-following Avg L2 from 0.332 to 0.215 on GazeFollow for zero-shot Qwen3-VL-8B, and LoRA fine-tuning further reduces it to 0.110 — approaching but not matching the MTGS-DINO-static specialist at 0.094; meanwhile, fine-tuned VLMs surpass the specialist on ChildPlay social gaze (SA F1 0.803 vs. 0.731). - The missing: The paper does not evaluate any VLM with a dedicated coordinate-regression head or hybrid architecture; all spatial predictions are extracted from text token sequences, leaving open whether the performance gap on gaze following is fundamental to VLM representations or an artifact of the text-output regression paradigm. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working at the intersection of VLMs and fine-grained spatial/social perception; practitioners expecting a drop-in VLM replacement for specialized gaze models will find the negative result instructive.
Why this rank: Empirical profile. The composite is pulled down primarily by Novelty (55) and Practical Impact (45): the contribution is a benchmark/characterization rather than a new method (VQA reformulation and LoRA are standard), and the paper releases no VLM weights, no public EyeVLM repo URL, and only an unlinked QA-construction pipeline. Rigor (62) is decent on ablation breadth (model size, data scale, prompting, sampling) but undercut by zero error bars, closed-source models capped at 1K samples, and a 50/50 balanced resample that inflates social-gaze F1 and confounds the comparison with the unbalanced-trained MTGS specialist. Resilience (58) suffers because the headline Best-of-N result (L2 0.055) is an oracle upper bound requiring ground-truth selection. Composite: 0.20*55 + 0.25*62 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*68 + 0.15*58 + 0.10*45 = 58.
empirical Novelty 55 Rigor 62 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 68 Resilience 58 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Gaze following — predicting the 2D image location where a person is looking — and social gaze prediction — inferring relational gaze behaviors such as mutual gaze (LAEO), looking-at-person (LAH), and shared attention (SA) — are foundational tasks for understanding human attention in computer vision. Specialized models for these tasks have matured over a decade, yet they remain narrow, requiring task-specific training and offering little semantic reasoning about why a person gazes in a given direction. VLMs, trained on internet-scale vision-language data, encode rich social and spatial knowledge that could in principle address these limitations. However, no prior work has systematically compared general-purpose VLMs against specialized gaze models under controlled protocols, studied how prompting strategies affect both geometric and relational subtasks, or investigated how LoRA fine-tuning closes the remaining gap. Key Idea: The core insight is that gaze following and social gaze prediction differ fundamentally in what capability they require from a VLM: gaze following demands precise sub-pixel geometric reasoning (head pose, 3D scene structure, spatial grounding), which benefits from structured chain-of-thought prompting; social gaze prediction relies more on high-level semantic and relational reasoning (eye contact, shared focus), which VLMs already partially encode from pretraining. This asymmetry predicts that prompting strategies should have opposite effects on the two tasks — a prediction the paper confirms empirically — and motivates separate treatment of the two tasks rather than a single unified prompting or fine-tuning recipe. Methodology: EyeVLM converts both tasks into a VQA interface: the input is an image plus head bounding box coordinate(s); the output is structured JSON. For gaze following, the output is an in-frame probability and a normalized (x, y) gaze point; for social gaze, it is a binary probability per relation type. Gaze following prediction is deliberately decoupled into two separate queries (in/out and gaze point) to avoid causal dependency artifacts in autoregressive generation. Four prompting strategies are tested: PRBASE (role + task + format), PRCOTBASE (base + "step-by-step" instruction), PRCOTSTRUCT (structured 7-step scaffold: describe scene → identify activity → infer gaze direction from head/eyes → enumerate candidate targets → describe spatial relations → synthesize), and PRINCONTEXT-COT (two manually written image+text in-context examples with CoT reasoning). Fine-tuning uses LoRA (rank 8, q/k/v/o and FFN projections, language head frozen, learning rate 4×10⁻⁴, batch 64) trained with LLaMAFactory on an H100 GPU for 2 epochs (gaze following) or checkpoint-selected on validation (social gaze). Images are resized to 448×448 pixels. Three training regimes are compared: FTspec (GF → target dataset, gaze following only), AllD/GFo (all three datasets, gaze following only), and AllD/GFo+SG (all datasets, both tasks jointly). Benchmarks are GazeFollow (122K images), VideoAttentionTarget (71K frames, video), and ChildPlay (120K frames, child-adult). Social gaze data is resampled to 50/50 positive-negative balance from VSGaze annotations to reduce class-imbalance artifacts. Metrics are Avg/Min L2 distance and angular error for gaze following, and precision/recall/F1 per relation for social gaze. A critical design decision — using per-task-type balanced resampling for social gaze — is well-justified given class skew in VSGaze, though it means the benchmark distribution differs from the specialist's training distribution (acknowledged). The image-resize-to-448 choice is justified by alignment with prior gaze work but limits native-resolution VLM capability. Key Results: On GazeFollow (zero-shot, PRINCONTEXT-COT): Qwen3-VL-8B achieves Avg L2 = 0.215, Qwen3-VL-32B = 0.184, InternVL3.5-38B = 0.184, Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview = 0.131, ChatGPT-5.4-Thinking = 0.164 — all well above the best specialist MTGS-DINO-static at 0.094. After LoRA fine-tuning, Qwen3-VL-8B drops to 0.110 (FTspec), still 17% worse than the specialist. On VideoAttentionTarget, the gap is larger: fine-tuned VLM achieves L2 = 0.133 vs. MTGS at 0.097. On social gaze (VAT, zero-shot PRBASE): Qwen3-VL-8B F1 averages 0.699/0.794/0.630 for LAH/LAEO/SA vs. MTGS at 0.867/0.896/0.809. After fine-tuning, Qwen3-VL-8B reaches 0.829/0.929/0.714 — matching or exceeding MTGS on LAEO, close on LAH, but still behind on SA. On ChildPlay, the AllD/GFo+SG fine-tuned VLM surpasses MTGS on SA (F1 0.803 vs. 0.731). CoT prompting reduces gaze-following Avg L2 from 0.332 (PRBASE) to 0.215 (PRINCONTEXT-COT) — a 35% reduction — while for social gaze PRBASE already achieves strong performance and PRINCONTEXT-COT degrades SA F1 from 0.630 to 0.538. Best-of-N stochastic sampling (N=16, T=0.7) reduces Avg L2 to 0.055 on GazeFollow — below all specialists — but this is an oracle upper bound requiring ground-truth oracle selection. Data scale ablation shows consistent improvement: Avg L2 drops from 0.191 at 1K samples to 0.110 at full training set (≈120K). Model size ablation shows 8B best for in-domain GazeFollow but 4B matches or beats 8B on cross-dataset VAT and ChildPlay, suggesting diminishing returns from scale alone. Novelty Classification: NEW BENCHMARK/EVALUATION FRAMEWORK + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The benchmark formulation (converting gaze tasks to VQA under unified protocol) is not technically novel, and LoRA fine-tuning of VLMs is standard practice. What is genuinely novel is the systematic characterization of the asymmetry between geometric gaze localization and social gaze prediction in terms of VLM capability, the comparative study spanning 9 model families (open + closed source) under 4 prompting strategies across 3 datasets, and the empirical finding that fine-tuned 8B VLMs can match domain-specific specialists on social gaze while remaining clearly below them on geometric gaze localization. Rigor & Reproducibility: The paper provides ablations on model size (2B/4B/8B), data scale (1K to full), prompting strategy (4 variants), in-context example type (text-only vs. image+text), sampling strategy (greedy, Best-of-N, Average-of-N), and training task combinations. No error bars or statistical significance tests are reported anywhere — all results are single-run point estimates, which is a meaningful concern given the variance in VLM outputs. Code is referenced as available in a codebase and the MTGS model checkpoint is at HuggingFace (Idiap/mtgs-static-vsgaze), but no public repo URL for EyeVLM itself is stated in the paper. Social gaze evaluation uses a custom balanced resample of VSGaze, creating a benchmark that differs from the MTGS specialist's training distribution — the paper acknowledges this in the limitations but does not disentangle the performance gap that arises from distribution mismatch vs. model capability. Closed-source model evaluation is capped at 1K samples per task due to API cost, which the authors acknowledge reduces robustness. The paper is a preprint; these results have not been peer reviewed. Deployability: EMPIRICAL profile. Practical: LoRA fine-tuning runs on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU; zero-shot inference runs via vLLM on any GPU that fits the model (Qwen3-VL-8B requires ~20 GB VRAM). Efficient: Autoregressive next-token text generation is slower than direct-prediction specialists (seconds per image in reasoning mode vs. milliseconds); the paper notes this explicitly as a limitation but reports no inference latency numbers. Effective: Fine-tuned VLMs demonstrably solve social gaze at specialist-level but leave a persistent ~15–20% L2 gap on geometric gaze localization that is not closed by fine-tuning; in-frame/out-of-frame classification is already nearly solved by zero-shot VLMs (F1in/out ≈ 0.80). Scalable: Model size scaling from 2B to 8B helps in-domain but not cross-dataset; no experiments beyond 8B parameters for fine-tuning; closed-source frontier models (Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview at 0.131 zero-shot L2) are better but still lag specialists, suggesting diminishing returns from scale without architectural changes. The paper delivers no software artifact beyond the QA pair construction pipeline (not publicly linked); no trained VLM weights are released. Strengths: - The task-asymmetry finding (CoT helps geometric gaze following but hurts social gaze) is a concrete, reproducible, and non-obvious insight with direct practical implications for prompt engineering in gaze-aware applications. - The cross-dataset evaluation (train on GazeFollow, test on VAT/ChildPlay) tests generalization rather than only in-distribution performance, revealing that fine-tuned 8B VLMs transfer reasonably (L2 = 0.130/0.119 on unseen datasets) while specialized models still lead. - The Best-of-N stochastic sampling analysis demonstrates that VLM representations contain sufficient information for accurate gaze localization if a good candidate can be selected — pointing toward future work on uncertainty estimation or self-consistency decoding rather than just better fine-tuning. - Evaluating both open-source and closed-source frontier models (GPT-5.4-Thinking, Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview) under a consistent protocol provides honest positioning of where the current model frontier stands.
