Critical design decision: using a smooth maximum rather than hard max ensures differentiability; the choice is justified by analogy to group-DRO worst-case objectives but not ablated. 3. Adapter-stability regularizer (L_stab): Using the local Lipschitz property of softmax and submultiplicativity of Frobenius norm, segment-level attention reallocation ||C^{l,h}(x') − C^{l,h}(x)||_F is upper-bounded by a term proportional to ||B^l_||_F ||A^l_||_F for LoRA projections ∗ ∈ {Q, K}. The regularizer L_stab = Σ_l Σ_{∗∈{Q,K}} ||B^l_∗||_F ||A^l_∗||_F penalizes this product. Under a Gaussian PAC-Bayesian prior centered at zero LoRA update and an isotropic posterior, this product is also connected to the dominant term of KL(Q||P), providing a complexity-oriented interpretation. This interpretation is explicitly labeled non-certifying. 4. Total objective: L = L_CE + λ_sem L_sem + λ_stab L_stab. Key Results All experiments use LoRA fine-tuning on BART-base, T5-base, and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2; baselines are R3F and SMART (both consistency-based). Perturbation types: typographical noise+deletion, synonym replacement, paraphrasing. Metrics: PDR (ROUGE-L degradation), 1−SB (BERTScore semantic consistency), Δed (Levenshtein edit rate), DKL (LoRA complexity), PAC-B (composite). - CNN/DailyMail, BART-base: S²R² PDR_avg = 0.0012 (81% below SMART's 0.0064), DKL = 78.006 vs. 202.554, PAC-B = 0.2626 vs. 0.2874. - XSum, BART-base: S²R² PDR_avg = 0.0185, slightly worse than SMART's 0.0125, though DKL is lower (90.775 vs. 149.155) and PAC-B is better (0.2924 vs. 0.3110). - PubMed, T5-base: S²R² PDR under paraphrasing is missing (dashes in the table, unexplained); for typographic/synonym conditions, 1−SB (0.1776) and Δed (0.1195) are dramatically lower than SMART (0.6089, 0.6461), though ROUGE-based PDR is high (0.3229). - PubMed, Mistral-7B: Comparable E-Risk to SMART (0.4419) with DKL reduced from 461.931 to 58.106. - Cross-dataset (CNN/DM → PubMed): E-Risk = 0.1939 vs. SMART's 0.3328. Cross-dataset (XSum → CNN/DM): E-Risk = 0.2695 vs. SMART's 0.3392. Baselines are reasonably current (SMART, R3F are standard choices) but limited to two methods. All experiments use base-scale or single 7B models. The high PDR on PubMed paraphrasing paired with low semantic shift is a counter-intuitive finding the paper partially explains as paraphrastic variation not captured by ROUGE. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD. The combination of optimal-transport segment alignment with a smooth worst-case robustness objective for generation-task fine-tuning is not found in prior work. The individual components — optimal transport, LoRA norm regularization, PAC-Bayesian analysis — are all standard; what is novel is their integration and the diagnostic framing of robustness as segment-level drift. This is a non-obvious combination applied to a real gap rather than a straightforward engineering combination. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablation table is provided separating the contributions of L_sem and L_stab — the paper explicitly defers norm-only and segment-only ablations to future work. No error bars, multiple seeds, or significance tests are reported. The T5-based segmentation validation runs on 1/8 of the data and is relegated to an appendix, limiting its evidential weight. The E-Risk metric (weight 0.8 on semantic stability, 0.1 each on PDR and edit rate) is authored by the same team and designed for a method optimizing semantic stability — this weighting choice is not independently motivated. The missing paraphrasing rows for T5-base/PubMed in Table 1c are unexplained. Code is stated to be released upon acceptance; no URL is provided in this preprint. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.01605v1). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Runs on commercially available hardware; BART-base and T5-base require standard single-GPU setups; Mistral-7B-Instruct requires 1–2 consumer/cloud GPUs with LoRA. - Efficient: The added cost per training step is OT alignment over typically <20 segments per output (computationally negligible) plus Frobenius norm computation over LoRA matrices (O(r·d) per layer). The punctuation-based segmentation is 60× cheaper than T5-based segmentation per the appendix. - Effective: Evidence for improved robustness is present on CNN/DM and cross-dataset transfer, but mixed on XSum (higher PDR than SMART) and partially occluded on PubMed (missing paraphrasing rows). ROUGE-based PDR conflates genuine semantic failure with valid paraphrastic variation, which the paper acknowledges. - Scalable: Tested on models up to 7B parameters. OT computation scales quadratically with segment count and linearly with batch size; no test or analysis at larger model scale or with outputs yielding many segments. No software artifact released at time of preprint. Strengths - The segment-level diagnostic framing — asking where generation breaks rather than whether it breaks globally — is a genuine conceptual contribution that exposes a blind spot in all prior sequence-level consistency methods. - Optimal transport is a principled choice for aligning segment sets of variable length and order, superior to position-aligned penalties that break under output-length changes. - The log-sum-exp smooth maximum in L_sem directly operationalizes worst-case segment robustness in a differentiable, training-friendly form. - Cross-dataset transfer results (two settings, both favorably for S²R²) provide evidence for generalization beyond in-distribution perturbation types. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors are direct about several scope boundaries: (1) punctuation-based segmentation is crude and the neuro-symbolic proposition-level segmentation is left to future work; (2) ablations separating segment-only and norm-only variants are deferred to future work; (3) the PAC-Bayesian analysis is explicitly framed as a complexity-oriented interpretation, not a certified robustness guarantee; (4) attention reallocation is described throughout as a diagnostic proxy, not a causal explanation of generation behavior. The authors also disclose LLM-assisted language polishing (GPT-5). This is an unusually honest set of self-qualifications for an ML paper. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The missing ablation is the central gap: without a segment-only run (L_sem, no L_stab) and a norm-only run (L_stab, no L_sem), it is impossible to determine whether the improvement is driven by the novel segment alignment, the standard norm penalty, or only their combination. - The E-Risk metric's 0.8/0.1/0.1 weighting is constructed to favor S²R²'s known strength (semantic stability) — a practitioner who prioritizes ROUGE-L preservation would read the XSum result as a regression. - The missing paraphrasing rows for T5-base/PubMed in Table 1c are never explained; paraphrasing is the hardest and arguably most realistic perturbation type. - The theoretical bound connecting L_stab to attention reallocation is loose (upper bound with unspecified constants Γ^{l,h}_{LoRA}) and is never empirically validated: the paper does not show that the segments with largest LoRA-mediated attention shift correspond to the segments with largest semantic drift. - All tested models are base-scale (BART-base, T5-base) or a single instruction-tuned 7B model; larger LoRA-tuned models (LLaMA-3 70B, etc.) where adapter rank and norm dynamics differ substantially are untested. - Evaluation is restricted to summarization; the claim that segment-level robustness generalizes to other generation tasks (QA, translation, dialog) is unsubstantiated. Bottom Line The segment-level framing of robustness is the paper's lasting contribution, but the absence of component ablations means a practitioner cannot determine whether a simple LoRA norm penalty (already known) accounts for most of the gain — a gap the authors themselves flag but leave unresolved in this preprint.
Recommended 2605.01605 PDF
#155 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Diversed Model Discovery via Structured Table Discovery

Zhengyuan Dong, Renée J. Miller · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces Structured Semantic Search, a table-driven model search pipeline that augments semantic retrieval with table discovery operators (unionability, joinability, keyword) applied to model-card tables, plus a nugget-based evaluation protocol measuring evidence coverage over retrieved candidate sets. - Why it's important: Semantic similarity over model card text collapses results around dominant model families, leaving users unable to compare task-aligned alternatives — a practical liability for ML practitioners selecting models from repositories like HuggingFace's 60K+ models. - How it does it: Starting from an anchor model card found via semantic search, the pipeline extracts its tables and applies three Blend table-discovery operators to a curated table lake, maps retrieved tables back to model cards (top-1 per table by semantic similarity), then reranks the union by query similarity under a fixed top-k budget. - The good: Unionable table search achieves mean nugget coverage of 9.4 (top-1), 28.8 (top-3), and 37.6 (top-5) versus sparse semantic baseline averages of 4.0, 11.9, and 19.8 — a 2–2.4× improvement at small budgets. - The missing: The evaluation queries are entirely adapted from a paper-recommendation benchmark (LitSearch) via lexical rewriting, not from real model-search logs; the ecological validity of this proxy for actual model search behavior is unestablished. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers working at the intersection of data lake table discovery and ML model repository search; practitioners seeking production model-selection tooling will find the system too early-stage and evaluation too proxy-dependent.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile (built table-driven model-search pipeline on a real 60K-card model lake). Rigor (30) and Resilience (35) pull the composite down hardest: there are no ablations decomposing component contributions, no significance tests or variance, the nugget metric is an unvalidated raw recall-like count produced by un-audited LLM extraction, and the entire query corpus is a lexically-rewritten proxy from a paper-recommendation benchmark (LitSearch) with no validation against real model-search logs — under adversarial review the quantitative gains would not survive. Novelty (55) and Non-Obviousness (55) are moderate — applying data-lake table-discovery operators to model cards is a natural, previously unexplored connection, but the operators themselves (Blend, ALITE) are existing systems and the contribution is integration/domain adaptation. Impact (50) reflects a released code+data+prompt artifact, but it is explicitly workshop/proof-of-concept stage with no latency, no user study, and an undisclosed anchor-card selection bias. Weighted: 0.15*55 + 0.15*30 + 0.10*55 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*35 + 0.30*50 = 45.
systems Novelty 55 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 45 Resilience 35 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Machine learning model repositories ("model lakes") such as HuggingFace host tens of thousands of models, each documented via a model card that mixes narrative text with structured tables (benchmark results, configuration tables, dataset summaries). Existing search systems treat model cards as unstructured documents and apply semantic or keyword retrieval, optimizing for textual proximity. Because model cards within the same family or institution share writing templates and narrative conventions, this collapses results into homogeneous clusters around dominant model families — even when those models differ empirically. ML practitioners who want to compare competing architectures, evaluate performance trade-offs, or find models benchmarked on specific datasets have no mechanism to surface this structured evidence systematically. The gap is that no prior system applies data-lake table discovery techniques — which exist for general tabular data — to the model-card domain to support comparative, diversity-oriented retrieval. Key Idea: The core insight is that model-card tables (benchmark results, configuration tables, evaluation summaries) are already normalized, low-stylistic-variance evidence units that can be searched using existing data lake table discovery operators. By routing a query through a semantic anchor card to seed table discovery, the pipeline escapes the text-similarity gravity well without abandoning task alignment: unionable tables share schemas (hence same benchmarks), joinable tables share entity columns (hence same models/datasets), and keyword search over headers finds semantically adjacent tables. Table discovery thus acts as a structured diversification mechanism layered on top of, not replacing, semantic retrieval. Methodology: The pipeline has two main paths. Unstructured Semantic Search (NL2Card) implements three semantic baselines: dense (Sentence-BERT + FAISS), sparse (Pyserini/BM25), and hybrid (sparse candidate pool + dense reranking). Structured Semantic Search (NL2Card2Tab2Card) operates as follows: (1) select the top-1 anchor model card by semantic search, constrained to cards with at least one associated table; (2) for each anchor table, apply three Blend operators — keyword search over table headers and first-column values ranked by matched-token frequency; joinable search using the first column as query column ranked by join-column overlap; unionable search ranked by number of alignable anchor columns; (3) map each retrieved table to a single representative model card via top-1 semantic similarity re-ranking; (4) deduplicate and take top-k by semantic similarity. An orientation-aware integration step (Algorithm 2) detects and corrects transposed tables before feeding into ALITE for union-based integrated views. The evaluation protocol defines nuggets as 6-tuples (model, base model, model variant, dataset, metric name, metric value), extracts them from model cards via LLM prompts, maps queries to nugget constraints via a second LLM prompt, and scores candidate sets by the count of distinct query-matching nuggets covered. Critical design decisions: (a) one-representative-card-per-table selection avoids inflating the candidate set — justified; (b) prompt-based nugget extraction and query mapping rather than exact schema parsing — partially justified by heterogeneous model-card formatting but introduces unvalidated LLM noise; (c) constraining the anchor step to cards with tables — introduced to enable table discovery but silently excludes table-sparse cards from being anchors, creating selection bias not discussed in the paper. Key Results: On 597 adapted LitSearch queries against the ModelTables corpus (60K+ HuggingFace model cards), mean per-query nugget counts are: Unionable 9.4 / 28.8 / 37.6 / 45.5, Joinable 8.8 / 24.0 / 29.1 / 34.1, Keyword 8.7 / 25.6 / 31.1 / 37.9, versus Sparse 4.0 / 11.9 / 19.8 / 39.6, Dense 2.5 / 8.4 / 15.0 / 34.7, Hybrid 3.4 / 10.5 / 17.9 / 39.8 (top-1 through top-10). The structure-aware advantage is most pronounced at small k (2–2.4×) and narrows substantially at top-10, where hybrid and sparse semantic become competitive with joinable and keyword methods. At top-10, unionable (45.5) still leads sparse (39.6) and hybrid (39.8) by ~15%. Query-level rank distribution shows unionable ranks first among all six methods for ~27% of top-1 queries but only ~15–17% at top-10. No statistical significance tests are reported. No error bars or multi-run variance is given. Evaluation uses only one query corpus and one model lake. Novelty Classification: NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The technical contribution is genuine: applying data-lake table discovery operators (unionability, joinability from the Blend system) to model card search is a novel application not previously studied. The nugget-based evaluation adapted to model cards is also a contribution. However, the core retrieval operators (Blend, ALITE) are existing systems, Sentence-BERT + FAISS is standard infrastructure, and nugget-based evaluation is borrowed from TREC RAG. The novelty lies in the integration and domain adaptation rather than in any new algorithm. Rigor & Reproducibility: Code, prompts, data, and outputs are released at https://github.com/RJMillerLab/ModelSearch (stated in footnote 4). The paper provides two algorithms but no ablation decomposing the contribution of individual components (e.g., orientation-aware integration vs. raw union, or the effect of anchor-card selection quality). Nugget extraction accuracy is not validated — both extraction and query-to-nugget mapping rely on LLM prompts with no inter-annotator agreement or error analysis, meaning the evaluation metric itself may carry substantial noise. The nugget score is a raw count (no normalization by total possible nuggets), so it functions as a recall-like measure with no precision counterpart; a method that retrieves many irrelevant models with large tables would score artificially high. The query corpus is derived from LitSearch paper-recommendation queries via minimal lexical rewriting — ecological validity for actual model search is assumed but not validated against real search logs. The query distribution is severely skewed: 540 of 597 queries are evidence-based, with comparison (17), experience (6), reason (19), instruction (13), and debate (2) representing tiny minorities; claims about general model search coverage are not supported. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — the paper presents a built pipeline demonstrated on a real model lake. - Practical: The pipeline runs on commodity hardware using Sentence-BERT, Pyserini, FAISS, and the Blend system; no GPU inference required at retrieval time. - Efficient: Structured search adds table discovery on top of semantic retrieval; per-query latency is not reported, and Blend's join/union operators over large table lakes may be expensive at scale — not evaluated. - Effective: Evidence favors table-based retrieval for nugget coverage, but the evaluation measures only evidence count, not whether users actually find better models; no user study or downstream task performance is reported. - Scalable: The ModelTables corpus covers 60K model cards, but behavior at 600K or 6M models — likely over the next few years on HuggingFace — is not assessed; the anchor-card constraint (requires at least one table) may become more binding as the long tail of sparse-table cards grows. The work delivers a code artifact with prompts and data. It is preprint/workshop paper form — the venue header "Conference'17, July 2017" is a placeholder template artifact, and the paper explicitly states the system "will be demonstrated at the workshop." Strengths: - The framing of model search as inherently comparative and the diagnosis of why text similarity fails (shared templates, family clustering) is well-argued and supported by concrete examples. - Adapting data-lake table discovery primitives (unionability, joinability) to model cards is a natural but previously unexplored connection that produces concrete empirical gains. - The nugget-based evaluation framework — defining atomic evidence units as 6-tuples derived from leaderboard conventions — is a practical contribution for the model lake evaluation gap (no static ground-truth benchmark exists for model search). - The qualitative examples (Figures 4–5) concretely illustrate where semantic retrieval returns cards with no usable tables while table-based retrieval returns benchmark-comparison-ready integrated views. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors explicitly identify three limitations: (1) ALITE fails when tables are transposed or exhibit schematic heterogeneity (rows/columns swapped between tables), which is common in model lakes; (2) many model cards remain incomplete or table-sparse, limiting table-based retrieval coverage; (3) the query corpus is dominated by evidence-based intents, and whether performance trade-offs differ for comparison, instruction, or reason queries is unexplored. They also note that nugget scores were used comparatively rather than as absolute precision/recall, and suggest future work could convert them to ground-truth labels. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - The LitSearch-to-model-search query adaptation via lexical rewriting is a fragile proxy. Paper-recommendation queries ask about research contributions; model-search queries ask about deployment properties, hardware constraints, and performance trade-offs — structurally different intents. The claim that "adapted queries remain close to the original retrieval intent" is asserted, not validated. - Nugget extraction quality is unverified. Both the extraction LLM call and the query-to-nugget mapping LLM call are treated as noiseless; if extraction recall is ~70%, the entire comparative evaluation is measuring noise on top of noise. A 50-query human validation of extraction quality is missing. - The anchor-card constraint silently introduces selection bias: only queries whose top-1 semantic hit has ≥1 associated table proceed through the structured pipeline. Queries where the top-1 card has no tables are excluded without analysis. This affects an unknown fraction of queries. - At top-10 the advantage largely evaporates for sparse and hybrid baselines, which is noted in passing but not adequately explained. The mechanism by which budget sensitivity interacts with the structured pipeline is not analyzed. - No comparison to strong non-semantic baselines: metadata-filter search (pipeline, architecture, dataset fields from HuggingFace's own structured metadata) is the obvious competitive baseline and is absent. - The paper claims table integration produces "comparison-ready" candidate sets, but this is shown by two cherry-picked qualitative examples, not a systematic study of integration quality or failure modes. Bottom Line: This is a workshop-stage paper with a legitimate and well-motivated idea — applying table discovery to model lake search is a natural and underexplored direction — but the evaluation relies on an unvalidated proxy query corpus, unvalidated LLM-based metric extraction, and cherry-picked qualitative examples, making the quantitative gains difficult to trust at face value; it is worth reading for researchers building model lake infrastructure but should be treated as a proof-of-concept pending more rigorous evaluation.
Citations 2605.22766 PDF
#156 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Unveiling AI-powered ERP systems: a systematic review of trends and future directions

link.springer.com · genai manufacturing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents a systematic mixed-method review of 183 academic publications and 35 industry grey literature sources covering the integration of generative AI, automation AI, and analytics AI into ERP systems, combining bibliometric analysis, LDA topic modeling, VADER sentiment analysis, and qualitative grey literature synthesis. - Why it's important: ERP systems are the operational backbone of most large organizations, and the literature is fragmented across narrow technology silos — no prior review has jointly mapped all three AI categories (generative, automation, analytics) within ERP, leaving practitioners and researchers without a consolidated picture of adoption trends, gaps, and risks. - How it does it: PRISMA-guided corpus construction from Web of Science (183 papers), bibliometric mapping via R/Bibliometrix, coherence-tuned LDA (8 topics), VADER sentiment scoring on abstracts, Louvain keyword clustering, thematic evolution indices, and thematic coding of 35 grey literature sources. - The good: The structural finding that governance/ethics topics account for under 2% of the discourse — despite ERP systems handling payroll, financial transactions, and HR records — is a credible and actionable observation about a systematic blind spot in the field. - The missing: Sentiment is measured on abstracts only (not full text), using VADER (designed for social media), on a corpus of only 183 documents — a combination that likely inflates the headline 93%-positive figure and is not acknowledged as a validity threat. - Read it or not: Worth reading for enterprise IS researchers or ERP practitioners wanting a structured landscape map of AI-ERP research as of early 2025; not a reliable source for quantitative claims about sentiment or topic prevalence given the methodological weaknesses.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (45) and Resilience (40) pull the composite down: the headline 93%-positive finding rests on VADER (a social-media tool) applied to abstracts only over a 183-document single-database corpus, with no manual cross-validation and no released code — a chain of weaknesses that would not survive a tough reviewer, and the abstract-only validity threat is not even flagged. Novelty (50) is modest — a NEW SURVEY/SYNTHESIS that is first to jointly map all three AI categories in ERP, with no new algorithm. Completeness (50) is helped by candid acknowledgment of corpus and parameter limitations but hurt by the unaddressed sentiment-validity threat and the structurally-biased grey-literature sample. The work stops at paper form with no artifact (Impact 35).
empirical Novelty 50 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 50 Resilience 40 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Enterprise organizations depend on ERP platforms that integrate finance, HR, supply chain, and manufacturing. AI is being embedded into these systems at accelerating pace — the review documents a 375% growth in AI-ERP publications over five years and an 11.3% compound annual growth rate from 2010 to 2025. Yet the academic literature remains fragmented: prior reviews address RPA (Ribeiro et al. 2021), generative AI (Schneider 2024), or analytics (Zhang et al. 2021) in isolation. No prior review simultaneously covers all three AI categories within ERP contexts. The gap is practically significant because organizations making AI-ERP adoption decisions encounter conflicting narratives from vendors (uniformly optimistic), academics (largely optimistic but fragmented), and implementers (concerned about data readiness and change management). Key Idea The core organizing insight is that triangulating multiple quantitative text-mining methods — bibliometrics, LDA topic modeling, VADER sentiment analysis — against grey literature qualitative synthesis can reveal not just what topics are covered but how they are framed, how coverage evolves over time, and where systematic silences exist. There is no novel algorithmic contribution; the "trick" is applying standard NLP and bibliometric tools jointly to a domain-specific corpus and using the divergence between academic framing and industry framing as an analytical signal. Methodology Data collection followed PRISMA: three Web of Science queries combining AI/ML terms with ERP/IS terms and one of three AI-category discriminators (generative/NLP, automation/RPA, analytics). Starting from 502 hits, deduplication and language filtering yielded 395, then title/abstract/keyword screening reduced this to 183 relevant documents (2010–Feb 2025). Excluded: non-English, literature reviews, off-domain papers, and papers where ERP acronym carried a different meaning. Grey literature: 35 sources selected from ERP vendors (SAP, Oracle, Microsoft Dynamics, NetSuite, IFS, Epicor), consulting/industry reports (Gartner, McKinsey, Deloitte, PwC, Accenture, IDC, Oxford Economics), and business media (Forbes, HBR, MIT Sloan, ERP Today), following Adams et al. (2017) grey literature guidelines. Bibliometric analysis used R/Bibliometrix (Biblioshiny) to extract publication counts, CAGR, authorship metrics, and keyword co-occurrence networks with Louvain community detection. Topic modeling used Python/gensim LDA on preprocessed abstracts+titles+keywords; coherence score maximized over 2–10 topics yielding 8 topics (parameters: passes=10, chunksize=100, alpha/eta='auto'). Sentiment analysis used VADER (NLTK SentimentIntensityAnalyser) on abstracts; compound threshold ±0.05 for positive/negative/neutral. Qualitative grey literature synthesis used thematic coding. Critical design decisions: (1) abstract-only text mining rather than full text — limits sentiment depth; (2) single-database corpus — limits coverage; (3) three-category AI taxonomy — imposes structure but boundaries between categories are fuzzy in practice. Key Results Publication growth: 11.3% CAGR over 2010–2025; >65% of the 183 papers appeared in 2022–2024; annual growth exceeded 40% in 2022–2024. Collaboration metrics: mean 4.3 authors/paper; 33.9% international co-authorship; mean 28.86 citations/paper. Six keyword clusters identified via Louvain: AI & Enterprise Systems; Data Analytics & ML; Process Automation; Predictive Analytics; Deep Learning & Intelligent Automation; Generative AI. Motor themes (high centrality + density): deep learning, RPA, artificial intelligence. LDA topic distribution: Topic 5 "General AI in ERP and Automation" dominates at 40.98%; Topic 1 "Supply Chain/Manufacturing" at 18%; Topics 2–8 cover ML techniques, BI/decision support, user interaction/trust, generative AI/NLP, data quality, and surveillance/digital control (the last at <1%). Sentiment: 93% of abstracts classified as positive, ~3% negative, ~1.6% neutral; no statistically significant sentiment difference between IT and Business journals (T=1.69, p=0.094), though Business journals cluster more tightly at high positive scores (compound >0.90). Ethical/governance discourse: <2% of papers. Grey literature: SAP planning >400 AI-driven scenarios by end 2025; 64% of businesses in one industry survey expect AI to improve productivity. Baselines are prior literature reviews (Schneider 2024, Ribeiro et al. 2021, Zhang et al. 2021), not empirical comparator systems. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING + NEW SURVEY/SYNTHESIS. The genuine novelty is being the first systematic review to jointly cover all three AI categories (generative, automation, analytics) within ERP, and the empirical finding of a structural optimism bias with near-total absence of governance discourse is a real contribution. The text-mining methods are standard combinations of existing tools applied to a new corpus; there is no new algorithm, dataset, or benchmark. Rigor & Reproducibility No code or dataset is released ("No datasets were generated or analysed during the current study"). PRISMA diagram and inclusion criteria are stated. LDA coherence is used to select topic count — standard practice — but no inter-rater reliability is reported for topic label interpretations. VADER is a general-purpose social-media sentiment tool; no validation study for its use on academic abstracts is cited, and no alternative instrument or manual annotation is used to cross-validate the 93% positive finding. Readability analysis (Flesch, Flesch-Kincaid) is computed on abstracts only and explicitly acknowledged as a proxy. The sentiment-on-abstracts limitation is self-acknowledged but not treated as a validity threat to the headline statistic. Single-database corpus (Web of Science) is acknowledged as a scope limitation. The grey literature corpus (35 sources) is drawn entirely from vendor marketing, consulting reports, and trade media — sources with strong structural incentives toward positive framing — and this selection bias is not quantitatively controlled. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) Profile: EMPIRICAL — the paper runs text analysis on a document corpus and synthesizes results. - Practical: The methodology runs on standard academic computing (R, Python, NLTK, gensim); no specialized hardware required; reproducible given Web of Science access. - Efficient: Text mining on 183 abstracts is low cost; the grey literature thematic coding is manual and does not scale automatically. - Effective: The review surfaces structural patterns in the AI-ERP literature that would be difficult to obtain by reading papers individually; however, the specific 93% sentiment claim rests on abstract-only VADER scoring, which is a weak proxy for actual author stance. - Scalable: Extending to Scopus and Google Scholar is technically straightforward; the authors explicitly call for this. Grey literature synthesis would require systematic qualitative coding at larger scale. The work stops at paper form; no software artifact, dataset, or trained model is released. Strengths - First review to jointly cover generative, automation, and analytics AI within ERP, documented against a specific set of narrower prior reviews; the scope gap it fills is real and well-evidenced. - The structural finding that ethics/governance represents <2% of the AI-ERP discourse — despite ERP systems handling payroll, HR decisions, and financial transactions — is a credible, specific, and actionable research gap. - Thematic evolution analysis using Weighted Inclusion Index and Stability Index is a methodologically appropriate and underused tool for tracking conceptual migration across time periods; the 2010–2021 vs. 2022–2025 transition mapping is interpretable and informative. - Triangulation of academic corpus with grey literature surfaces the academia-industry divergence on implementation prerequisites (data readiness, change management) that pure academic reviews miss. Author-Acknowledged Limitations Authors directly acknowledge: (1) reliance on Web of Science alone, excluding Scopus and Google Scholar; (2) restriction to business organizations, limiting generalizability; (3) corpus cutoff at early 2025 in a rapidly evolving field; (4) small corpus size (183 documents) limiting LDA/sentiment reliability; (5) sensitivity to keyword selection and LDA parameters. These are confronted straightforwardly and proportionately, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The sentiment-on-abstracts design is a fundamental validity problem that the authors do not flag: abstracts are structurally positive (they are the "sales pitch" of a paper), so measuring sentiment on abstracts rather than full text likely inflates the 93% positive figure regardless of actual paper content. This is not acknowledged as a validity threat, only as a scope note. - The grey literature sample (35 sources: all vendor, consulting, or trade media) is inherently positively framed; the claim that grey literature "triangulates" and validates academic findings is questionable when both source pools have independent structural incentives toward optimism. - No inter-rater reliability coefficients are reported for any qualitative step (grey literature thematic coding, LDA topic label assignment); the robustness of topic labeling is asserted but not measured. - The exclusion of prior literature reviews from the academic corpus is stated in the methodology but not justified; this means the paper misses existing meta-level synthesis and may overstate the novelty of its own synthesis. - VADER's validity for academic text is not established; no manual annotation sample or cross-validation against human judgments is provided to anchor the 93% positive claim. Bottom Line A practitioner or researcher mapping the AI-ERP landscape as of early 2025 will find this review useful as a structured starting point, but its core quantitative claims — particularly the 93% positive sentiment finding — rest on methodology (VADER on abstracts, single database, 183-document corpus) too weak to treat as reliable evidence; the paper is better read as a well-organized descriptive synthesis than as rigorous empirical analysis of field-wide sentiment.
Search Results
#157 — Moderately Innovative (44)

The Vocabulary Hub as a Catalog for Semantic Artifacts for Discovery and Alignment of Datasets

pietercolpaert.be · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper proposes extending the IDS-RAM Vocabulary Hub to catalog two new first-class artifact types — dataset profiles (W3C Profiles Vocabulary descriptions linking SHACL shapes, ontologies, and specifications to a dataset) and profile alignment artifacts (directional SPARQL Construct or RML mappings between profiles, annotated with provenance and DQV quality metadata) — and provides a prototype web UI demonstrating these interactions for cross-border traffic measurement data. - Why it's important: EU data spaces cannot achieve automated semantic interoperability because current vocabulary hubs govern only ontologies, leaving consumers to manually discover, create, and duplicate dataset-level structural alignments with no governance or reuse infrastructure. - How it does it: A minimal `pmap:` RDF vocabulary (two properties: `pmap:sourceProfile`, `pmap:targetProfile`; one class: `pmap:ProfileAlignment`) is layered onto existing DCAT + W3C Profiles Vocabulary to make alignment artifacts directional, governable catalog entries linked to dataset entries via `dct:conformsTo` and `dct:relation`. - The good: A working Docker-based demonstrator integrates real traffic streams from Hamburg (OGC SensorThings API/LDES) and Flanders (OSLO Verkeersmetingen/LDES) via two published SPARQL Construct alignment queries, showing the end-to-end discovery-to-integration workflow is technically feasible. - The missing: No quantitative evaluation of any kind — no alignment quality measurements, no discovery precision/recall, no scalability benchmarks; the governance and trust model for third-party-published alignments, the hardest unsolved problem, is explicitly deferred. - Read it or not: Practitioners designing EU data space interoperability components or semantic catalog infrastructure should read it; for anyone outside this specific niche, the paper lacks empirical results and leaves its central open problem (governance of decentralized alignments) unresolved.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile (workshop paper with Docker prototype). Impact (50) at 0.30 weight dominates: a working demonstrator, published pmap: vocabulary, and SPARQL Construct alignment queries are released, but the contribution is niche and the pmap: namespace has no standardization pathway. Rigor (30) and Resilience (40) pull it down hard — there is no quantitative evaluation of any kind (no alignment quality, discovery precision/recall, or scalability), and the demonstrator assumes all catalogs are writable by all actors, which eliminates the central trust/authorization constraint of real data spaces. Novelty (50) credits the genuine governance framing (directional, cataloged, provenance-bearing alignment artifacts), but it is a composition of existing standards. Weighted: 0.15*50 + 0.15*30 + 0.10*45 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*40 + 0.30*50 = 44.
systems Novelty 50 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 45 Resilience 40 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation In EU Common Data Spaces, participating organizations publish datasets under divergent schemas and ontologies (e.g., OGC SensorThings API, OSLO Verkeersmetingen, SSN/SOSA), forcing each data consumer to independently discover, create, and maintain dataset-level structural and semantic alignments. Current vocabulary hub implementations (VocBench, OntoPortal, PoolParty, Semantic Treehouse) govern ontologies and support SKOS/OWL conceptual mappings, but cannot capture structural transformations between distinct uses of data models, nor provide a governed, discoverable catalog of executable alignment queries. This gap means alignment effort is duplicated across consumers, transformation artifacts are siloed, and no trust signal exists to allow consumers to select among competing alignments. Key Idea The core insight is that dataset profiles (machine-readable declarations of which ontologies, SHACL shapes, and specifications a dataset distribution conforms to) and alignment artifacts (directional, executable mapping queries between two profiles) should be treated as governable, cataloged semantic artifacts in the vocabulary hub, co-equal with ontologies — making them discoverable via standard DCAT catalog patterns and enabling consumers to locate and apply already-published transformations rather than creating new ones. Methodology The paper defines three artifact layers. First, existing DCAT dataset entries are extended with `dct:conformsTo` links pointing to profile URIs. Second, dataset profiles are modeled as `prof:Profile` resources (W3C Profiles Vocabulary) carrying `prof:hasResource` descriptors with typed roles: `role:vocabulary` (ontology URI), `role:validation` (SHACL shape file), `role:specification` (standards document), etc. Third, alignment artifacts are modeled as `pmap:ProfileAlignment` (a new class in the `pmap:` namespace) which inherits from `prof:Profile` and adds `pmap:sourceProfile` and `pmap:targetProfile` to make directionality explicit; alignment artifacts carry `dqv:hasQualityMeasurement` annotations and reference executable SPARQL Construct (or RML) queries as `role:mapping` artifacts. The vocabulary hub hosts a DCAT profile catalog and a DCAT alignment catalog; datasets link to profiles via `dct:conformsTo`; consumers query these catalogs to discover compatible sources and applicable alignments. Execution uses an RDF-Connect pipeline (Docker) that fetches an LDES stream, applies the SPARQL Construct query via a SPARQL engine, and loads results into a local graph store. No formal objective function or algorithmic framework is defined; the design choices are: (1) re-use existing W3C standards (DCAT, Profiles Vocabulary, DQV) and introduce minimal new vocabulary — justified as reducing adoption friction; (2) separate profile definitions from alignment artifacts to support decentralized publishing by third parties — well-justified for governance; (3) host profiles in the vocabulary hub rather than connector catalogs — a pragmatic choice the authors acknowledge is debatable. Key Results The paper presents no quantitative evaluation. The deliverable is a proof-of-concept demonstrator: a Docker Compose setup hosting a DCAT metadata broker and vocabulary hub, plus a four-panel web UI (Data Portal, RDF Portal, Alignment Pipelines, Dataset Profile Registry) deployed at a live GitHub Pages instance. The use case covers traffic count data from Hamburg (SensorThings API → custom `stapi` ontology → LDES) and Flanders (Straatvinken and Telraam, OSLO Verkeersmetingen → LDES), with two published SPARQL Construct alignment queries (stapi→SSN/SOSA and OSLO Verkeersmetingen→SSN/SOSA). The 0.87 completeness value appearing in a code listing (Listing 3) is a fictional example in the metadata model, not a measured alignment quality score. No baselines are compared against. The scale tested is a small fixed dataset; the authors state explicitly that scalability was not implemented. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW FORMULATION. The genuine novelty is the governance framing: treating directional, executable profile alignments as first-class, cataloged, provenance-bearing semantic artifacts with the `pmap:` extension to the W3C Profiles Vocabulary. All individual technologies (DCAT, W3C Profiles Vocabulary, SPARQL Construct, SHACL, RML, LDES, DQV) are existing standards; the contribution is their composition into a specific architectural pattern for data space alignment governance. Rigor & Reproducibility This is a workshop paper with a prototype demonstrator. No ablations are present. No error bars, significance tests, or performance benchmarks are reported. Code and a live instance are publicly available (GitHub: IDLabResearch/vocabuary-hub; GitHub Pages instance). The alignment SPARQL queries are published on GitHub (deployEMDS/vocabulary-hub-demo). The demonstrator explicitly assumes all datasets and catalogs are accessible to all actors and defers governance and access control, which removes the hardest real-world constraints from the evaluation. The `pmap:` vocabulary is proposed but not submitted to any standards body, so its stability is uncertain. Deployability (4-property check) SYSTEMS profile. - Practical: The demonstrator runs via Docker Compose on commodity hardware using standard RDF/SPARQL tooling. - Efficient: No compute or memory benchmarks are reported; the authors acknowledge that full client-side catalog loading becomes infeasible at scale and propose SPARQL endpoint services as a mitigation, but do not implement or measure this. - Effective: The system successfully demonstrates the discovery-to-integration workflow for two cross-border traffic datasets using two SPARQL Construct alignments, but no formal evaluation of integration correctness or completeness is provided beyond the working demo. - Scalable: Not tested; the authors state explicitly that scalability was not addressed in the demonstrator and leave it for future work. The work delivers a software artifact (Docker-based prototype, web UI, published ontology, SPARQL queries) and a data model specification (`pmap:` vocabulary). Strengths - Clean architectural separation of dataset profiles from alignment artifacts, with alignment directionality (`pmap:sourceProfile`/`pmap:targetProfile`) filling a genuine gap in the W3C Profiles Vocabulary, which explicitly deferred this (referenced GitHub issue #15). - Disciplined reuse of existing W3C standards (DCAT, Profiles Vocabulary, DQV) minimizes the new vocabulary surface to two properties and one class, lowering the barrier for adoption by existing vocabulary hub implementations. - The dataset profile concept generalizes naturally to support LLM-agent data discovery (analogized to MCP context injection), a forward-looking connection that identifies a concrete future use case. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly acknowledge: governance and trust challenges for decentralized alignment artifacts are out of scope for the demonstrator; scalability under realistic data space conditions is not implemented or tested; the integration of the extended vocabulary hub with actual data space connector implementations (e.g., Eclipse Dataspace Components) is future work; and how consumers make informed decisions when multiple conflicting alignments exist for the same source→target pair is noted as beyond the scope of this contribution. These admissions are clear and credibility-enhancing for a workshop paper. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 0.87 completeness figure in Listing 3 appears only as a placeholder example value in the RDF model, not as an actual quality measurement; a reader scanning quickly could mistake it for an empirical result. - No comparison against existing systems (PoolParty, Semantic Treehouse) is provided on expressiveness, alignment coverage, or integration effort; the claim that this approach surpasses current tools is asserted but not demonstrated. - The demonstrator assumes all catalogs are writable by all actors — a condition that eliminates the primary trust and authorization challenge of real data spaces and makes the evaluation scenario unrealistic. - The `pmap:` namespace is proposed without any standardization pathway; without community adoption or standards-body backing, the vocabulary is unlikely to achieve the interoperability it targets. - Conflict resolution — selecting among multiple published alignments for the same source→target pair — is architecturally central to the governance vision but entirely unaddressed, even at a conceptual level. Bottom Line This is a credible workshop-level proposal that correctly identifies a gap in current data space architectures and provides a working prototype, but it stops well short of a research contribution: no quantitative evaluation, no scalability evidence, and the hardest unsolved problem (decentralized alignment governance and trust) is deferred entirely.
Search Results
#158 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Building a privacy-preserving Federated Recommender system for mobile devices

arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a two-stage privacy-preserving mobile recommendation pipeline that combines a centralized Correlated Cross-Occurrence (CCO) candidate generator with a federated AutoInt CTR model for on-device ranking, implemented end-to-end in Kotlin Multiplatform. - Why it's important: GDPR and the deprecation of third-party trackers have stripped mobile apps of the behavioral data they need for personalization; federated learning enables hyper-personalization using sensitive on-device sensor data without it ever leaving the device. - How it does it: The first stage uses a log-likelihood ratio co-occurrence algorithm (Universal Recommender) on non-sensitive app-context data; the second stage uses FedAvg to train an AutoInt self-attention CTR model locally on each device, sharing only embedding layer weights for communication efficiency. - The good: AutoInt in the federated non-IID setting achieves AUC 0.772 vs. the company's prior logistic regression baseline of 0.671—a ~15% relative improvement—and the ablation finding that federating only the embedding layer (AUC 0.797) outperforms full federation (AUC 0.772) is a practically useful result for bandwidth-constrained edge deployments. - The missing: The entire federated pipeline is evaluated on simulated client splits of MovieLens 1M, not on real mobile devices; the end-to-end system was never tested with live federated training, leaving the central claim of on-device privacy-preserving learning unvalidated. - Read it or not: Skip for research purposes—the novelty is minimal and the evaluation is simulation-only; read only if you need an engineering blueprint for a two-stage federated mobile recommender built on Kotlin Multiplatform, as it documents practical integration decisions not found in academic papers.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Honest limitation disclosure (Completeness 65) is the main positive, but Novelty (30) and Rigor (45) drag the composite down: FedAvg, AutoInt, and CCO are all prior methods, the entire federated pipeline is evaluated on simulated MovieLens splits rather than real devices, and the differential-privacy selling point is never quantified. Impact (45) is limited because the central live on-device claim is unvalidated and the Kotlin library is unreleased. Weighted to 44.
systems Novelty 30 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 30 Completeness 65 Resilience 40 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mobile app developers rely on personalized recommendations to reduce churn and boost conversion, but have historically used third-party trackers and centralized data pooling to do so. GDPR and user privacy awareness make this approach increasingly untenable. Sensitive mobile context data—sensor readings (accelerometer, gyroscope), location, app usage habits, device state—has high value for hyper-personalization but cannot be sent to a central server without violating user privacy. Existing federated recommendation systems either ignore this sensor-rich context entirely or require a single monolithic federated model that raises friction for app developer adoption. The gap is a modular, privacy-preserving pipeline that can slot into existing app ecosystems and exploit on-device sensor data while keeping it on device. Key Idea: The central insight is architectural: split the recommendation pipeline at the privacy boundary. Non-sensitive app-context data (user interactions, item metadata, demographics) can safely feed a centralized collaborative filtering stage for coarse candidate generation, while sensitive mobile-context data (sensor embeddings, device state) exclusively feeds a federated CTR ranker that never ships raw data off device. Within the federated stage, a secondary insight from the ablation experiments is that sharing only the embedding layer weights—building a shared item vocabulary across clients—suffices for coordination, while keeping the attention and output layers local allows per-client personalization and reduces communication overhead. Methodology: The pipeline has four functional components. (1) Activity recognition pre-processing: raw accelerometer and gyroscope time-series (30 observations at 0.5 Hz over 60-second windows) are converted to 112-dimensional hand-crafted feature embeddings (time-domain statistics + FFT frequency components) for downstream use; an LSTM classifier also achieves 97.66% macro-F1 on the Real-life HAR 4-class dataset for label extraction. (2) Centralized candidate generation: the Universal Recommender system applies a Correlated Cross-Occurrence (CCO) algorithm using Apache Mahout's log-likelihood ratio to score item pairs across primary (liked) and secondary (disliked, neutral, item/user properties) interaction events; this stage is deployed on cloud servers using ActionML. (3) Federated ranking: the AutoInt model (embedding layer → 2-head multi-head self-attention with hidden dim 32 → MLP → sigmoid) is trained locally on each mobile client using binary cross-entropy loss on binarized ratings (≥4 → positive). FedAvg aggregates client models via weighted averaging (weights proportional to local dataset fraction) over multiple rounds. (4) Kotlin Multiplatform implementation: all ML components—forward/backward passes, FFT, embedding layers, attention—are implemented from scratch in Kotlin due to the absence of mature ML libraries in that ecosystem. Critical design decisions: (a) session-level feature embeddings rather than raw time-series as input to the LR ranker (justified by pilot study results, though gains are marginal); (b) FedAvg rather than FedSGD for communication efficiency despite weaker convergence guarantees; (c) using the MovieLens 1M dataset as a proxy for the full sensor+interaction+item pipeline due to the absence of a suitable public dataset with all three data categories. Key Results: On the Real-life HAR 4-class activity recognition task, the LSTM model achieves macro-F1 = 97.66% vs. SVM+RBF at 84.79% and Linear SVM at 57.49%. On the pilot company dataset (app-category prediction, 39 users), the best session-activity-embedding approach achieves 15.13% balanced accuracy versus a dummy baseline of 12.5% and a prior XGBoost best of 19.60%—all models perform near chance on this 8-class task, indicating the problem is intrinsically underdetermined. On the centralized recommendation stage (MovieLens 1M, leave-one-out, k=10), CCO with all secondary indicators achieves Hit Rate@10 = 0.614 vs. NCF baseline of 0.730; the gap is attributed to the absence of matrix factorization in CCO. On the federated ranking stage, AutoInt centralized achieves AUC = 0.848 / LogLoss = 0.367, degrading to AUC = 0.796 (IID) and 0.772 (non-IID) under FedAvg, compared to the company's prior LR+Raw baseline at AUC = 0.671 (non-IID). Ablation over which layer to federate: embedding-only federation achieves AUC = 0.797, outperforming full federation (0.772); attention-only and output-only federation diverge in log loss. All federated experiments use 10 simulated clients, 20 aggregation rounds. Novelty Classification: ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The two-stage privacy boundary split is a reasonable engineering architecture but not a novel algorithmic idea—FedAvg, AutoInt, Universal Recommender/CCO, and LSTM-based HAR are all prior published methods. The ablation finding on layer-wise federation selectivity is an empirical finding with modest originality. The Kotlin Multiplatform ML library is a genuine engineering contribution. Nothing here constitutes a new problem formulation or new method. Rigor & Reproducibility: The HAR experiments include a clear train/val/test 80-10-10 split and macro-F1 reporting, but no error bars or multiple seeds are reported. The federated experiments compare three models across three settings with consistent metrics (AUC, LogLoss), which is adequate for the scope, but again no statistical significance testing is performed. Code is described as forthcoming open-source but is not released at submission time. The pilot company dataset is proprietary and unpublishable. Critically, the federated evaluation uses simulated splits of MovieLens 1M rather than actual mobile devices—this is an explicit acknowledged limitation. The centralized recommendation stage has no published baseline from ActionML authors; comparisons against NCF/BPR/ItemKNN are reproduced from the NCF authors' own code, which is acceptable. The differential privacy and secure multi-party computation components mentioned in the system architecture are never experimentally characterized in this report—their privacy guarantees are asserted but not measured. No ablation isolates the contribution of the activity embedding over using raw sensor features in the full recommendation task, because no end-to-end test exists. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SYSTEMS — a built tool evaluated by partial deployment (Kotlin library deployed to beta devices, but federated training not yet live). - Practical: The Kotlin Multiplatform library targets Android/iOS smartphones, which are commercially available; the server side uses standard cloud infrastructure (AWS EC2 T4 GPUs, MongoDB, Elasticsearch). - Efficient: The AutoInt model is small (57,600 embedding + 38,015 attention + 441 output = ~96k parameters total), making it plausible for on-device inference, but training latency on mobile hardware is not benchmarked—the authors flag this as future work. - Effective: The federated CTR model shows consistent ~10% AUC improvement over the prior LR baseline on MovieLens 1M simulation; however, this does not validate that the system solves the actual problem (personalized mobile recommendations using real sensor data), since no end-to-end evaluation on live devices exists. - Scalable: Tested only with 10 simulated clients; the authors explicitly list generalization to larger client counts as future work. The centralized CCO stage scales linearly in users via MapReduce (claimed, not tested in this work). The work delivers a software artifact (Kotlin ML library, with open-source release pending) and a deployed centralized recommendation stage (ActionML in production); the federated stage is implemented but not live. Strengths: - The two-stage pipeline architecture sensibly partitions privacy-sensitive from non-sensitive data, and its modularity (the first stage is hot-swappable with any existing system) is a practical design decision that reduces adoption friction. - The ablation finding that federating only the embedding layer outperforms full federation on non-IID splits (AUC 0.797 vs. 0.772) is a concrete, actionable result for practitioners balancing communication cost against personalization, and required non-trivial custom extension to the Flower framework. - The Kotlin Multiplatform implementation—including FFT, autodiff-capable embedding and attention layers written from scratch—is a substantive engineering contribution filling a real gap in the mobile ML ecosystem. - The non-IID split strategy using SVD embeddings + kNN clustering to simulate realistic user heterogeneity is more ecologically valid than the standard label-imbalance approach. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors directly acknowledge: (1) no public dataset exists combining mobile sensor data with item interactions and metadata, forcing evaluation on MovieLens 1M as a proxy; (2) no end-to-end live federated testing on actual mobile devices was completed; (3) the ablation study results require further experiments to be conclusive; (4) wall-clock training benchmarks on edge devices are missing; (5) the Kotlin ML library is not yet released. These are confronted honestly and are not minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps: The pilot study results (15.13% balanced accuracy vs. 12.5% dummy baseline) reveal that activity-context features contribute almost nothing to the proxy recommendation task, yet this negative signal is not fully interrogated—the authors proceed to use activity embeddings in the pipeline regardless. The differential privacy component (which is a core selling point of the described system architecture) receives no quantitative treatment: no epsilon/delta bounds are stated, no privacy-utility tradeoff is analyzed, and no membership inference or gradient inversion attack experiments are run. The CCO stage is substantially weaker than NCF on HitRate@10 (0.614 vs. 0.730) and dramatically weaker on NDCG@10 (0.2193 vs. 0.4470); the justification that "sufficient" performance is acceptable for the pipeline is not backed by any downstream evaluation showing that the CCO candidates are good enough for the ranker to recover quality. The study evaluates only 10 simulated clients over 20 rounds—neither the number of clients (real mobile deployments involve thousands) nor the number of rounds has been stress-tested. The "preprint" label applies (arXiv:2605.22924); this is an M.Sc. internship report submitted to arXiv, not peer-reviewed. Bottom Line: This is an earnest engineering internship report documenting a proof-of-concept federated mobile recommender system at Lerna AI—practitioners building a similar system in Kotlin will find the architectural decisions and Flower extension useful, but researchers seeking validated algorithmic contributions will find no novel methods, no differential privacy analysis, and no live system evaluation.
Search Results 2605.22924 PDF
#159 — Moderately Innovative (43)

Operationalizing Document AI: A Microservice Architecture for OCR and LLM Pipelines in Production

Yao Fehlis, Benjamin Bengfort, Zhangzhang Si, Vahid Eyorokon, Prema Roman, Patrick Deziel, Devon Slonaker, Steve Veldman, Ben Johnson, Joyce Rigelo, Michael Wharton, Steve Kramer · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a three-microservice architecture (Gateway, Worker, Inference Service) for deploying OCR and LLM-based document extraction pipelines at production scale, along with operational lessons from processing thousands of multi-page documents per hour. - Why it's important: Practitioners deploying document AI find almost no published guidance on production engineering—the gap between a model checkpoint and a reliable, cost-controlled system processing thousands of forms per day is substantial and nearly undocumented in the literature. - How it does it: The core mechanism is separating GPU-bound inference from CPU-bound orchestration into independently scalable microservices coordinated through message queues, combined with a hybrid CLIP-KNN + VLM classification strategy that achieves near-VLM accuracy at near-zero API cost. - The good: The finding that OCR (not LLM parsing) dominates end-to-end latency—roughly two-thirds of wall-clock time for an 8-page document—is a counter-intuitive, operationally validated result that directly redirects where practitioners should invest optimization effort. - The missing: All profiling results are explicitly described as qualitative; specific saturation thresholds, throughput figures, and quantitative scaling curves are deferred to future work, leaving the architectural claims supported by direction but not measurement. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML engineers building or scaling document processing pipelines; can be skipped by model researchers or readers already fluent in standard microservice patterns.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. The hybrid CLIP-KNN + selective-VLM cascade and the OCR-dominates-latency observation are reusable, non-obvious operational insights (Non-Obviousness 50, Impact 48), but Rigor (30) collapses the composite: the paper itself states all profiling is qualitative, with no throughput numbers, no saturation thresholds, no ablation against simpler architectures, no released artifact, and accuracy self-reported on the authors' own documents with no standard benchmark. Completeness (45) and Resilience (38) are low because the architectural claims are supported by direction rather than measurement. Weighted: 0.15*42 + 0.15*30 + 0.10*50 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*38 + 0.30*48 = 43.
systems Novelty 42 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 50 Completeness 45 Resilience 38 Impact 48
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation The document AI research community publishes model innovations (LayoutLM, Donut, VLMs) but almost nothing on deploying them into production systems that handle thousands of heterogeneous multi-page documents per day. Practitioners face a composite engineering challenge: normalizing heterogeneous inputs (multi-page TIFFs, scanned PDFs, photographs), routing each page to the correct extraction pipeline, running OCR on degraded scans, extracting structured fields via LLM, managing stochastic failures and retries, ensuring auditability, and operating within cost and latency budgets—all while balancing CPU-bound orchestration, GPU-bound inference, and IO-bound data movement. Commercial platforms (AWS Textract, Azure Form Recognizer, ABBYY) are proprietary and inflexible; general ML platforms (TFX, Michelangelo) do not address document-specific pipeline concerns. Key Idea The central insight is that the computational profiles of inference (GPU-bound, high memory) and orchestration (CPU-bound, IO-heavy) differ enough that coupling them in a monolith wastes both resource types and blocks independent scaling. Separating them enables "computational staggering"—overlapping GPU inference for one document with CPU/IO work on another—which improves utilization beyond what batching alone achieves, because using heterogeneous models across pipeline steps (classifier, OCR, parser) creates natural opportunities for GPU–CPU overlap without explicit batching coordination. Methodology The architecture comprises three independently deployable microservices: (1) a Gateway that ingests documents via REST or asynchronous queue, stores page images in object storage, tracks records in a relational DB, and enqueues document IDs; (2) Workers that orchestrate per-document pipelines asynchronously using Python coroutines (lazy-loading images, calling the Inference Service, uploading results), with horizontal scaling via Kubernetes and vertical concurrency via async tasks (default: 5 tasks per pod); (3) an Inference Service isolating GPU-bound OCR behind FastAPI REST endpoints, enabling independent GPU provisioning and model swapping without touching worker code. Three message queues (ingestion, worker, status) provide backpressure, retry semantics, and decoupled scaling. The per-document pipeline runs five steps: (1) hybrid CLIP-KNN + VLM classification—CLIP-KNN runs locally at 0.5–1s/page achieving 92% accuracy; pages with confidence below 0.7 fall back to Claude Sonnet at $0.010/page; the hybrid achieves 96% at $0.001/page with VLM fallback triggered on ~4% of pages; (2) optional RF-DETR or Claude Sonnet auxiliary metadata extraction; (3) DocTR OCR (db_resnet50 + crnn_vgg16_bn) at 1–2s/page on GPU, ~800 MB GPU memory; (4) per-page text concatenation (text stitching, <1s, trivial); (5) Claude Sonnet structured parsing via Anthropic API (~4,500 input tokens, ~400 output tokens, ~$0.03/document, ~3s latency), with schemas dynamically generated from Pydantic models. Three design decisions are central: inference–orchestration separation (justified by resource profile mismatch quantified at 3–5× CPU OCR slowdown vs. GPU), hybrid classification (justified by 10× cost reduction with ~2% accuracy loss), and async workers (justified by the predominantly IO-bound workload character). Key Results The system reduced per-page processing cost from $0.010 to $0.001 while maintaining 96% accuracy (self-reported, single deployment). Hybrid classification achieves 96% accuracy at $0.001/page vs. 98% at $0.010/page (VLM-only) and 92% at no API cost (CLIP-KNN only); VLM fallback fires on 4% of pages, reducing API cost ~10× relative to VLM-only classification. For a representative 8-page document, OCR consumes ~two-thirds of wall-clock time; LLM parsing is a distant second because it runs once per document while OCR scales linearly per page. Under increasing concurrency, throughput improves then plateaus once GPU inference capacity saturates; P95 latency remains stable below the GPU ceiling and degrades above it. Profiling was conducted on "several hundred synthetic multi-page documents." No quantitative throughput figures, saturation breakpoints, or speedup numbers are provided—the paper explicitly describes all profiling results as qualitative and defers quantitative benchmarks to future work. No comparison against alternative architectures on a common benchmark is presented. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The microservice architecture is a composition of standard patterns (message queues, containerized inference, async workers) applied to the document AI domain; what is genuinely novel is the hybrid CLIP-KNN + selective VLM fallback classification strategy (a reusable, cost-effective design with measured accuracy–cost tradeoff) and the empirical finding—counterintuitive to practitioners who assume LLM parsing dominates—that OCR determines end-to-end latency at document-processing scale. Rigor & Reproducibility No controlled ablations compare the three-service architecture against a two-service or monolithic alternative on matched hardware and workloads. No error bars, confidence intervals, or statistical tests are reported. No code, model weights, or datasets are released. Accuracy figures (96% classification, 96% end-to-end) are self-reported on the authors' own production documents of unspecified type and quality; no evaluation on standard benchmarks (DocVQA, FUNSD). The synthetic batch profiling workload is not characterized (page-count distribution, document type, image quality). Section 7 explicitly states that saturation thresholds and cost tradeoffs are workload-dependent and should not be treated as universal constants, and that re-profiling with corrected instrumentation is required before quantitative claims can be made. The paper itself acknowledges the current case study scope. This is a preprint (arXiv:2605.18818v1). Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built production system evaluated by deployment. - Practical: Runs on Kubernetes with standard GPU nodes (DocTR requires ~800 MB GPU memory; Claude Sonnet parsing uses the Anthropic cloud API); commercially available hardware suffices. Self-hosted or private endpoints are listed as an option for sensitive deployments. - Efficient: OCR at 1–2s/page on GPU with ~1 GB peak memory; parsing at ~$0.03/document; total direct API cost ~$0.038 for an 8-page document. Infrastructure costs (GPU cluster, object storage, Kubernetes, MLflow) are explicitly excluded from the cost figures, which materially understates total cost of ownership. - Effective: Achieves 96% accuracy on the authors' own production document types; no evaluation on a standard benchmark or comparison to commercial platforms on matched data, so generalization of the accuracy claim is unverifiable. - Scalable: Horizontal worker scaling is demonstrated qualitatively; the GPU Inference Service saturates first; adding Inference Service replicas shifts the bottleneck to downstream APIs and object storage. The paper claims "thousands of documents per hour" throughput but provides no quantitative scaling curve. The break-even worker-to-inference pod ratio is explicitly stated as workload-dependent and not yet quantified. The system delivers no public software artifact; the architecture is described at the pattern level only. Strengths - The hybrid CLIP-KNN + VLM fallback classification is a concrete, immediately reusable pattern: it demonstrates that a confidence-gated cascade achieves 96% accuracy at ~10× lower cost than VLM-only, with the fallback rate (4%) low enough that the cost savings dominate. - The empirical observation that OCR dominates latency because it scales per-page while parsing scales per-document is a non-obvious, operationally validated finding that will redirect optimization effort for any team that assumed LLM calls are the bottleneck. - The lessons-learned section (§7) contains specific, concrete failure modes with identified root causes and fixes—message queue visibility timeout too short, stale detection clocked from wrong event, model accuracy not equal to system reliability—making this section unusually actionable for a systems paper. - The explicit discussion of when this architecture is and is not appropriate (§3.6) honestly acknowledges that managed platforms or monolithic workers may be cheaper for low-volume or homogeneous workloads, avoiding overselling. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state in §7 that results are "intentionally scoped as an operational case study rather than a fully controlled systems benchmark" and that saturation thresholds, worker-to-inference ratios, and cost tradeoffs depend on workload composition, page count distribution, document quality, queue settings, model choice, and downstream API behavior. They acknowledge that quantitative benchmarks are deferred pending corrected instrumentation. Cost figures explicitly exclude cluster infrastructure, storage, observability, and human-review labor. The authors confront these scope limitations directly rather than minimizing them, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 96% accuracy figure is self-reported on the authors' own document types with no holdout benchmark; evaluation on DocVQA or FUNSD would enable meaningful comparison to the model-level baselines cited in §2. - Infrastructure costs (GPU nodes, Kubernetes, object storage, MLflow registry) are excluded from the per-page cost figures; the true total cost of ownership may narrow the claimed 10× advantage over managed platforms significantly. - The paper does not characterize failure rates, retry frequencies, or the distribution of failure modes under sustained load—information essential for reliability assessment in SLA-bound deployments. - There is no measurement comparing the three-service decomposition against a simpler two-service design (monolith worker + separate inference) on a matched workload; the benefits of the Gateway separation are argued but not empirically demonstrated. - The CLIP confidence threshold (0.7) is presented without sensitivity analysis; the accuracy–cost tradeoff curve across threshold values and the propagation of classification errors to downstream extraction accuracy are not characterized. Bottom Line ML engineers planning to build or scale document extraction pipelines will extract concrete, actionable architectural patterns and non-obvious operational lessons; readers focused on model accuracy research or already fluent in microservice design will find the engineering familiar and the empirical support too thin to shift their priors.
Search Results 2605.18818 PDF
#160 — Moderately Innovative (43)

Adversarial Reframing: A Framework for Targeted Generation in Language Models

arxiv.org · Recommended articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces THREAT (Targeted Harmful generation via Reframing and Exploitation of Adversarial Tactics), an iterative, LLM-guided jailbreak framework that combines template-based prompt reframing, BERT semantic similarity filtering, and a safety classifier score to find prompts that bypass aligned LLMs' safety mechanisms. - Why it's important: Deployed LLMs are widely used in high-stakes settings, and even heavily aligned models like GPT-4o remain exploitable through semantically disguised prompts, which creates real risks of misuse for discrimination, information hazards, and social engineering at scale. - How it does it: Each iteration generates L reframings of the current prompt using an attacker LLM guided by randomly selected templates, filters out refusals and out-of-band semantic drifts via BERT cosine similarity, and selects the candidate with the highest safety-classifier score, iterating until the score exceeds a threshold λ. - The good: Across four datasets, THREAT reduces GPT-4o refusal rates from 37–61% to below 1%, and on white-box targets achieves 72.81% ASR on LLaMA-2-7B vs. 60% for the closest baseline (Adversarial Reasoning), while requiring fewer concurrent LLM calls. - The missing: The paper's refusal-rate experiments on GPT-4o include no baseline comparisons under the same setting — the dramatic refusal reductions are measured against raw, unmodified prompts, not against peer jailbreak methods applied to the same model and datasets. - Read it or not: Security practitioners building red-teaming pipelines will find the ablation on judge prompt framing (execution-focused vs. stance-focused) practically valuable; researchers looking for methodological advances beyond existing iterative reframing methods (PAIR, TAP-T) will find the contribution thin.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The composite is pulled down primarily by Rigor (42) and Resilience (38): the main GPT-4o refusal-rate experiments omit peer-method baselines (compared only to unmodified prompts), the f_safe classifier both guides selection and declares success (a Goodhart circularity the paper never acknowledges), and no error bars are reported despite M=5 repetitions. Novelty (45) is modest — the iterative LLM-guided reframing loop is a narrow advance over PAIR/TAP-T, and the non-convexity proof is operationally inert. The released code and the useful execution-vs-stance judge ablation keep it from scoring lower. Composite: 0.20*45 + 0.25*42 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*38 + 0.10*55 ≈ 43.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 42 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 40 Resilience 38 Impact 55
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: Aligned LLMs deployed in consumer and enterprise products can be manipulated into producing harmful outputs through carefully crafted prompts that reframe harmful intent in innocuous-sounding language. Safety engineers and red teams need automated methods to systematically discover such prompts across diverse harm categories (hate speech, identity theft, system risks, information hazards) and model architectures. Existing black-box methods either require high query volumes that can be flagged by monitors, rely on brittle hand-crafted templates that fail to generalize, or incur high cost through multi-model pipelines. White-box methods require gradient access unavailable against proprietary APIs. The gap is an efficient, transferable, black-box jailbreak method that produces semantically fluent prompts at scale. Key Idea: The core trick is using the target model's own safety classifier signal (or a surrogate classifier) as a reward signal to greedily select, from a pool of LLM-generated semantic paraphrases, the prompt that most raises the perceived safety score while remaining semantically tethered to the original harmful intent via bounded BERT cosine similarity. The insight is that semantic disguise and apparent safety-alignment can be simultaneously maximized if an adversarial LLM generates candidate rewrites guided by role-playing red-team system prompts, and a safety score ranks them. Methodology: The pipeline has four steps per iteration, formalized as nonconvex optimization. Step 1: given current prompt x_{i-1}, use an attacker LLM (e.g., Mixtral) with a red-team system prompt and a randomly drawn reframing template to produce L=5 candidate variations. Step 2: discard candidates that elicit a refusal from the target model, then filter by BERT cosine similarity against x_{i-1} within [ε1=0.05, ε2=0.98]. Step 3: score remaining candidates using f_safe (either a dedicated safety classifier or a second LLM call), select the highest-scoring candidate as x_i. Step 4: repeat up to T iterations or until f_safe(x_i) ≥ λ=0.95.
