The optimization is formalized as maximizing reward safety gain R(x_{i-1}, x_i) = f_safe(x_i) - f_safe(x_{i-1}) subject to the semantic similarity constraint. Non-convexity is proven (trivially, since BERT embeddings are nonlinear and cosine similarity is non-convex). Complexity is O(TK) per prompt. Three design decisions: (1) random template selection adds diversity but is unjustified over learned selection; (2) ε1=0.05 lower bound is essentially vacuous — any coherent English paraphrase will exceed 5% cosine similarity with any other; (3) using f_safe both to guide prompt selection and to declare success creates a circular scoring loop that could be gamed by the same model being used to score. Key Results: On GPT-4o with four safety-benchmark datasets, THREAT reduces refusal rates from 37.06% → 0.25% (Discrimination), 60.86% → 1.08% (Information Hazards), 59.02% → 0.44% (System Risks), and 40.97% → 0% (HarmfulQA). On white-box targets using HarmBench ASR metric and Mixtral as the attacker: LLaMA-2-7B 72.81% (vs. Adversarial Reasoning 60%), LLaMA-3-8B-RR 67.81% (vs. 44%), Mistral-7B-v2-RR 70.31% (vs. 70%). Additional models: DeepSeek-7B 68.44%, Zephyr-7B 75.62%. Ablation on judge prompt framing shows that execution-focused scoring (a response must implement the harm) yields lower violation counts than stance-based scoring (a response is a violation if it fails to challenge the intent). Ablation on model choice for f_safe shows using GPT-5 as safe function increases refusals from 2 to 22 vs. GPT-4o — a counterintuitive finding the authors attribute to GPT-5's stricter evaluation steering prompts toward paraphrases that GPT-4o then rejects. No error bars are reported despite M=5 repetitions. The GPT-4o refusal-rate results have no peer baseline comparison under the same conditions. Novelty Classification: NEW METHOD. The iterative, LLM-guided reframing loop is a genuine engineering contribution with working code, but the conceptual advance over PAIR [Chao et al., 2025] and TAP-T [Mehrotra et al., 2024] is narrow — both also use iterative attacker LLMs with feedback signals. What is new is the explicit BERT-bounded semantic similarity constraint, the safety-gain reward formulation as a named optimization problem, the stance-vs-execution judge ablation, and the reduction in LLM calls per iteration. The non-convexity proof is mathematically correct but contributes nothing operationally — the greedy algorithm does not use this structure. Rigor & Reproducibility: Ablations cover judge prompt choice and safe-function/engine choices, but do not isolate the semantic similarity constraint's contribution (what happens if ε1=0, ε2=1?), the template pool size, or the number of iterations T. M=5 repetitions are stated but no standard deviations are reported on any metric. Code is publicly released at https://github.com/Shahanewaz/THREAT_Codes. The key methodological concern is Goodhart's Law: f_safe guides prompt selection and also serves as the stopping criterion — both roles use the same (or closely related) model, creating the risk that the attack optimizes for classifier perception of safety rather than actual semantic disguise of harm. The red/blue scoring using reference-response cosine similarity is a non-standard proxy for harm that has not been independently validated. Baselines in the GPT-4o experiments are absent; baselines in the white-box comparison are reasonable (GCG, PAIR, TAP-T, AutoDAN-turbo, Adversarial Reasoning) and current. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: The framework uses off-the-shelf LLM API calls and BERT embeddings; it runs on any hardware with internet access to GPT-4o or similar, and BERT inference requires at most a standard GPU. - Efficient: The authors claim as few as two LLM calls per iteration (one attacker, one safe-function), which is more efficient than PAIR (~20 queries) and Adversarial Reasoning (≥3 calls per step), though wall-clock runtime figures are not reported. - Effective: The evidence shows refusal bypass at high rates on GPT-4o and multiple open-source models; the stance-based judge ablation reveals that the generated prompts elicit socially engineered content even when they do not technically execute specific harmful steps, suggesting the harm produced is real rather than cosmetic. - Scalable: The framework was tested on datasets of up to ~1,390 prompts across five model targets; behavior at 10×–100× scale (e.g., fully automated mass red-teaming of a production API with rate-limiting and monitoring) is not evaluated and likely faces practical constraints from model API quotas and monitoring. The paper delivers a working codebase (Python, GitHub) and result dataframes. Strengths: - The judge-prompt ablation (execution-focused vs. stance-focused) is a substantive contribution to evaluation methodology for jailbreak research: the finding that a response can be a usable social-engineering artifact without technically implementing the harmful action has direct implications for how safety teams should define violation criteria. - Testing on five distinct white-box model targets including circuit-breaker-enhanced variants (LLaMA-3-8B-RR, Mistral-7B-v2-RR) provides evidence of cross-architecture generalization that many prior papers lack. - The public code release with result dataframes enables direct replication and extension. - The ablation on safe-function and engine choice (Table 7) yields a non-obvious finding: using a stricter evaluator (GPT-5) as the safe function actually degrades attack success, because it steers the attacker toward prompts that a more cautious generator also declines — a useful empirical insight about adversarial search dynamics. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not explicitly state limitations in a dedicated section. Implicit scope boundaries include: (1) all experiments use English-language prompts; (2) the framework is tested on a fixed snapshot of model versions (GPT-4o as of 2024, not later safety-updated versions); (3) the stance-based judge criterion acknowledges that violation definitions affect measured rates. The paper presents its results positively throughout and does not discuss the risk that prompts could be detected by query-pattern monitoring. Analyst-Identified Gaps: 1. The GPT-4o refusal-rate experiments compare THREAT to unmodified original prompts, not to peer methods (PAIR, TAP-T, Adversarial Reasoning) applied to the same datasets — making it impossible to assess whether the dramatic reduction (e.g., 60.86% → 1.08%) represents an advance over existing methods in the black-box setting or merely restates the known fact that any iterative reframing reduces refusals. 2. The semantic similarity lower bound ε1=0.05 is effectively meaningless; the paper does not ablate this parameter or show it excludes any candidates in practice. 3. The fsafe circular role (guide + judge) is not acknowledged. If f_safe is implemented as GPT-4o moderation on GPT-4o outputs, the method is optimizing for GPT-4o's own internal consistency rather than an independent harm signal. 4. No latency or API-cost numbers are reported, so the claimed efficiency advantage over PAIR/TAP-T rests on LLM-call counts alone, which ignores prompt length differences. 5. Adaptive defenses are not considered: a production system that adds slight paraphrase detection or query monitoring would plausibly catch the iterative reframing pattern, but this is not evaluated. Bottom Line: This preprint delivers a working red-teaming tool with a useful judge-prompt ablation, but its claimed advance over PAIR/TAP-T-style iterative reframing is not substantiated because the main GPT-4o experiments omit peer-method baselines, and the formal optimization framing adds no algorithmic insight beyond a greedy threshold search.
Recommended 2605.21674 PDF
#161 — Moderately Innovative (42)

ModeSwitch- LLM : A Lightweight Phase-Aware Controller for Cross-Mode LLM Inference on a Single GPU

Aman Sunesh, Ali Alshehhi, Hivansh Dhakne · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a deterministic rule-based request-boundary controller that routes each LLM inference request to one of five pre-characterized serving modes (GPTQ 4-bit, INT8, speculative decoding, GPTQ+prefix caching, INT8+continuous batching) using six cheap workload features, achieving 2.10× mean latency speedup and 51.7% energy reduction over FP16 on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU. - Why it's important: Production LLM serving deployments routinely apply one static inference configuration across heterogeneous workloads — short interactive, decode-heavy, shared-prefix, memory-constrained — even though no single mode is Pareto-optimal across all request types, leaving substantial efficiency potential unrealized. - How it does it: The controller benchmarks ten inference modes empirically, derives a 7-rule priority policy from those results, and classifies each incoming request by prompt length, expected output length, shared-prefix status, memory pressure, batch pressure, and workload tag — all available before generation begins — with 0.0096 ms CPU overhead per routing decision. - The good: The rule controller achieves 2.38× latency speedup on the collapsed workload-family evaluation, capturing 88% of the 2.41× constraint-aware oracle speedup while adding negligible overhead — outperforming all learned router variants (decision tree and logistic regression: 2.26×; random forest: 2.22–2.23×). - The missing: The routing policy is derived from and evaluated on the same synthetic workload taxonomy, with no held-out distribution or real production traffic traces, making the 2.10× headline figure an in-distribution result whose generality is undemonstrated. - Read it or not: Worth reading for ML systems engineers who deploy Llama-scale models on single-GPU servers and want an actionable inference routing recipe, but the narrow evaluation scope and absence of variance reporting reduce confidence in the generality of the gains.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile (a routing controller over an existing vLLM stack). Rigor (35) and Resilience (35) pull the composite down hardest: the 7-rule policy is derived from and evaluated on the same synthetic workload taxonomy (circular evaluation, no held-out distribution), no variance is reported anywhere so the rule-vs-learned-router gap (2.38× vs. 2.26×) is statistically indistinguishable, and the quality-preserving behavior depends on a 'workload tag' feature unavailable in real deployments. Novelty (40) is low — a dispatch layer over well-established modes — though the negative result that a 0.0096 ms rule policy beats learned routers is a useful empirical point. Impact (50), weighted heaviest under the systems profile, reflects a reproducible open-source single-GPU recipe but is bounded by one model and one GPU. Composite: 0.15*40 + 0.15*35 + 0.10*35 + 0.15*45 + 0.15*35 + 0.30*50 = 42.
systems Novelty 40 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 45 Resilience 35 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation LLM inference workloads are heterogeneous along at least four dimensions: latency sensitivity (short interactive requests), decode time (long-generation requests), prefix reuse (repeated system-prompt chat), and memory pressure (long-context). Despite this heterogeneity, dominant serving frameworks — vLLM, SGLang, Orca — apply one fixed global configuration. A quantized mode that reduces latency for short prompts may degrade output quality on reasoning benchmarks; speculative decoding that helps decode-heavy requests adds overhead for short ones; prefix caching is only beneficial when shared context is present. The gap is that no serving system selects the inference mode per-request based on request-level signals, even though those signals are available before generation begins. Key Idea The core insight is that existing inference optimizations are complementary rather than universally dominant: each mode has a workload regime in which it is Pareto-superior on latency, energy, and quality jointly. A router that classifies requests into these regimes — using only cheap pre-inference features — can recover most of an oracle's efficiency benefit without any model modification, retraining, or mid-request mode switching. The secondary insight is that a hand-written rule policy derived from empirical mode benchmarking outperforms supervised learned routers in end-to-end latency because routing overhead and constraint violations cost more than the learned policy's marginal classification accuracy gains. Methodology All experiments run on Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct served via vLLM on a single NVIDIA A100 40GB GPU. Ten candidate single modes are benchmarked (FP16, INT8, GPTQ 4-bit, AWQ 4-bit, speculative decoding, prefix caching, chunked prefill, continuous batching, CUDA graphs, KV-cache compression) plus two hybrid modes (GPTQ+prefix caching, INT8+continuous batching). Synthetic workloads use a 2×2 grid of prompt length (128 or 1024 tokens) × output length (32 or 128 tokens), plus shared-prefix chat and memory-pressure long-context variants. Quality is gated using five automatic benchmarks (MMLU-Pro, GSM8K, TruthfulQA, GPQA, MLU) with a ±1.5 percentage-point threshold relative to FP16. Energy per token is estimated by polling NVML at 50 ms intervals, integrating power, and dividing by generated tokens. The online controller extracts six request-level features and applies a fixed 7-rule priority policy. Three learned routers (decision tree, random forest, logistic regression) are trained on 605 workload rows to imitate a constraint-aware oracle. Critical design decisions: (1) Fixed-mode-per-request rather than mid-request switching, avoiding KV-cache compatibility issues — well-justified; (2) Rule-based over learned routing — empirically justified by the learned router comparison; (3) ROUGE-L as a quality proxy for synthetic generation workloads — weakly justified, as surface overlap does not reliably measure semantic correctness. Key Results On synthetic deployment-style workloads, the controller achieves 2.10× mean latency speedup and 0.48× mean energy ratio (51.7% lower energy/token) over FP16. On automatic benchmark workloads, it achieves 1.30× latency speedup, 0.71× energy ratio, and +0.17 percentage-point mean accuracy delta — all benchmarks within the ±1.5 pp quality threshold. GPTQ 4-bit is the best single mode for most synthetic workloads (speedups of 2.10×–2.87×, energy ratios of 0.39×–0.55×) but degrades benchmark accuracy on MMLU-Pro (33.0% vs. 37.0% FP16), GSM8K (79.0% vs. 83.0%), and MLU (39.0% vs. 47.0%). GPTQ 4-bit shows severe ROUGE-L degradation on memory-pressure long-context generation (0.249 → 0.090) despite strong efficiency gains. INT8 quantization preserves or improves accuracy on most benchmarks while achieving 1.08×–1.14× speedup. Against the collapsed workload-family oracle (1.97× speedup), the rule controller reaches 1.74× — an 88% capture rate on latency, and near-parity on energy (41.3% vs. 41.7% reduction). The random forest achieves 60.0% oracle-match accuracy but only 2.22–2.23× speedup due to ~50 ms routing overhead; decision tree and logistic regression reach 2.26× with 4.5–4.8 ms overhead — both inferior to the rule controller's 2.38× with 0.0096 ms overhead. Evaluation is restricted to one model size (8B parameters) and one GPU (A100 40GB); no results on larger models, multi-GPU setups, or production traffic traces. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The routing framework itself is a straightforward composition of existing inference optimizations (GPTQ, AWQ, speculative decoding, prefix caching, continuous batching) with a new dispatch layer — the individual modes are well-established. Genuinely novel is the empirical demonstration that a 7-rule deterministic policy essentially matches a constraint-aware oracle in end-to-end latency while beating all learned router variants, and the systematic joint evaluation of latency, energy, and quality across ten mode-workload combinations. Rigor & Reproducibility No error bars or standard deviations are reported on any efficiency metric — the 2.10× and 2.38× figures are means with no variance characterization, making it impossible to assess whether differences like 2.38× (rule) vs. 2.26× (decision tree) are statistically meaningful. Benchmark sample sizes are not stated; the accuracy figures (GPQA: 25.0% FP16, MMLU-Pro: 37.0%) are consistent with small subsamples, and the ±1.5 pp quality threshold is arbitrary without significance testing. The 605-row learned-router training set is small; no train/validation/test split methodology is described. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/ModeSwitch-LLM/ModeSwitch-LLM. No concern about benchmark contamination, but the routing policy is derived from the same measured workload taxonomy it is then evaluated on — the headline gains reflect in-distribution performance. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a controller deployed over an existing vLLM serving stack. - Practical: Runs on a single NVIDIA A100 40GB (commercially available), requires vLLM, pre-quantized GPTQ/AWQ model weights, and a speculative decoding draft model — all obtainable, though setup effort is non-trivial. - Efficient: CPU routing overhead is 0.0096 ms per request (negligible); efficiency gains come entirely from the underlying modes, not from additional computation introduced by the controller. - Effective: Achieves 2.10× latency speedup and 51.7% energy reduction on synthetic workloads while preserving benchmark accuracy within ±1.5 pp; however, the quality gate uses a coarse threshold and synthetic workloads may not represent real deployment distributions. - Scalable: Evaluated only on one 8B-parameter model and one GPU; the paper does not test multi-GPU deployments, larger models, or real production request traces, so scalability is asserted but not measured. The artifact is an open-source codebase including benchmarking scripts, controller implementation, and result-generation pipeline. Strengths - The finding that a 7-rule, 0.0096 ms-overhead policy captures 88% of the constraint-aware oracle's latency benefit — and outperforms all trained classifiers — is a practically important negative result against learned routing complexity for this problem size. - The joint multi-objective evaluation (latency + energy + quality) with explicit quality gating is more rigorous than latency-only serving system benchmarks, and the decision to separate quality checks from efficiency selection is architecturally sound. - The hybrid mode analysis (GPTQ+prefix caching: 1903→942 ms latency, 3.26→1.36 J/token; INT8+continuous batching: 205.8→279.3 tokens/s) provides concrete, reproducible engineering guidance for specific deployment patterns. - The distinction between oracle-match classification accuracy and end-to-end deployment utility (random forest wins on the former, loses on the latter) is a useful methodological contribution to the learned routing literature. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state that evaluation is limited to one model (Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) and one GPU type (A100 40GB), and that generalization to other model families, GPU types, and real production request traces is unvalidated. They identify learned routing with richer online system signals and finer-grained phase-aware control (separate prefill and decode adaptation) as future directions. These are confronted clearly rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The routing policy is derived from the same benchmark results used to evaluate it — a circular evaluation. No held-out workload distribution is tested, so the 2.10× figure is an in-distribution result. A practitioner with a different request mix would likely see different gains. - The "workload tag" feature (identifying whether a request belongs to a known benchmark family like GSM8K or GPQA) would not be available in real deployments without explicit user labeling, undermining the generality of the quality-preserving routing behavior on benchmark workloads. - No variance reported anywhere. The difference between the rule controller (2.38×) and learned routers (2.26×) could plausibly collapse with noise characterization — the paper cannot distinguish these as statistically meaningful. - Benchmark sample sizes are not disclosed. GPQA at 25.0% FP16 accuracy and GPQA with GPTQ at 32.0% (a 7 pp swing that exceeds the ±1.5 pp quality threshold) suggest small samples where individual question variance could drive the result; this anomaly is not discussed. - The 50 ms NVML polling interval for energy estimation is coarser than many short requests' total inference time, introducing systematic energy measurement error for short-prompt/short-output workloads. - AWQ 4-bit, despite being a competitive alternative to GPTQ 4-bit, does not appear in the final controller's mode pool; the paper does not explain or ablate this exclusion. Bottom Line A practitioner deploying Llama-3.1-8B on a single A100 for mixed workloads gains a reproducible inference routing recipe and a useful empirical argument for GPTQ 4-bit on throughput-sensitive tasks, but the circular evaluation design, absent variance reporting, and restriction to one model and one GPU mean the 2.10× headline speedup should be treated as a best-case estimate rather than a generalizable result.
Search Results 2605.23057 PDF
#162 — Moderately Innovative (42)

SciAtlas: A Large-Scale Knowledge Graph for Automated Scientific Research (46 ▲ )

Shuofei Qiao, Yunxiang Wei, Jiazheng Fan, Bin Wu, Busheng Zhang, Mengru Wang, Yuqi Zhu, Ningyu Zhang, Keyan Ding, Qiang Zhang, Huajun Chen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 25 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces SciAtlas, a heterogeneous academic knowledge graph of 43M papers, 157M entities, and 3B triplets across 26 disciplines, paired with a neuro-symbolic tri-path retrieval algorithm that fuses keyword matching, semantic embedding, and title matching via Random Walk with Restart over the graph. - Why it's important: Current academic retrieval tools do flat keyword or vector-space comparisons that cannot traverse logical connections across citation, authorship, and concept co-occurrence networks, while iterative LLM agent approaches that try to compensate incur high inference costs and produce hallucinations due to lack of deterministic structural grounding. - How it does it: Three retrieval paths (LLM-extracted keyword matching, dense embedding over pre-indexed paper titles/abstracts, GROBID-extracted title matching) seed a 2-hop heterogeneous subgraph, over which Random Walk with Restart propagates scores weighted by edge type and citation importance, then combines initial relevance, graph score, and citation importance into a final ranking. - The good: The scale is genuinely large — 43M papers and 3B triplets deployed in Neo4j — and the retrieval pipeline completes within 2 minutes, which is materially faster than iterative LLM-based deep research loops. - The missing: The paper contains zero quantitative retrieval evaluation: no precision, recall, NDCG, or MRR on any benchmark, making it impossible to verify that the topological retrieval outperforms a well-tuned BM25 or dense-retrieval baseline. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers building AI-for-science infrastructure who want to understand schema design for academic KGs and graph-based retrieval pipelines, but the complete absence of quantitative benchmarking limits confidence in the claimed advantages over vector search.
Why this rank: Scored using the Systems profile (Novelty 55, Rigor 28, Non-Obviousness 45, Completeness 40, Resilience 30, Impact 50). Rigor and Resilience are the decisive negatives: the paper reports zero quantitative retrieval evaluation — no precision/recall/NDCG/MRR, no baselines (Semantic Scholar, OpenScholar, OmniScientist dismissed qualitatively), no ablations isolating the tri-path or RWR contributions, hand-set edge weights with no sensitivity study, and all qualitative demos anchored on the authors' own papers, so the central claim that topological retrieval beats dense search collapses under the first reviewer question. Novelty and Impact (Systems-weighted 30%) keep it in the Moderately Innovative band — a genuinely large 43M-paper / 3B-triplet heterogeneous KG with a principled LLM-keyword layer is real infrastructure, and the authors' direct, non-minimizing acknowledgment that the work 'remains at the qualitative analysis level' is a Completeness positive — but Impact is itself capped because the KG data is not clearly downloadable (only interfaces released). Weighted: 0.15*55 + 0.15*28 + 0.10*45 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*30 + 0.30*50 ≈ 42.
systems Novelty 55 Rigor 28 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 40 Resilience 30 Impact 50
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Researchers and AI agents trying to navigate the literature face two compounding problems. First, research outputs are scattered in unstructured text with no relational organization — citation networks capture only one dimension, leaving author networks, concept co-occurrences, and disciplinary hierarchies latent and inaccessible. Novice researchers and AI agents cannot perceive global topological structure, which is particularly acute for interdisciplinary problems. Second, existing retrieval tools — Google Scholar keyword search, Semantic Scholar dense retrieval, arXiv/PubMed full-text search — perform flat feature comparisons and cannot support multi-hop reasoning: finding papers connected through shared authors, co-occurring concepts, and intermediate citing papers simultaneously. Iterative LLM agent frameworks (e.g., deep-research pipelines) attempt to compensate through repeated queries but incur high latency and, without a deterministic structural anchor, hallucinate connections in complex exploratory trajectories. Key Idea The core insight is that organizing academic literature as a heterogeneous graph with 9 entity types (Paper, Author, Institution, Keyword, Topic, Subfield, Field, Domain, Source) and 12 typed relational edges, then applying weighted Random Walk with Restart seeded by fused lexical and semantic matches, enables deterministic multi-hop association discovery that flat retrieval cannot achieve without iterative LLM inference. The key technical move enabling this is the construction of a purpose-built Keyword entity layer derived from LLM extraction (rather than reusing OpenAlex's sparse 65K Concept entries), which creates a dense conceptual substrate connecting papers at the terminological level and allows the RWR to propagate through concept co-occurrence paths as well as citation and authorship paths. Methodology SciAtlas construction proceeds in three stages. Data is sourced from OpenAlex's 480M-paper corpus and filtered to English papers with abstracts and PDF URLs, retaining 43.3M. Keywords are extracted using Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 prompted to return 3–8 canonical, reusable concept phrases per abstract (explicitly avoiding paper-specific system names), with a per-keyword importance score; COOCCUR edges between co-appearing keywords carry co-occurrence frequency as weight. Semantic embeddings (1024-dim via bge-large-en-v1.5) are precomputed for paper titles, abstracts, and keyword text, stored as node attributes. The full graph is deployed in Neo4j with RANGE, FULLTEXT, and COSINE vector indexes. Retrieval operates in two phases. In the node-matching phase, three paths run in parallel: (1) keyword path — an LLM extracts keywords from the query with importance scores, then exact text matching and vector similarity (threshold θ=0.7, top-3 per keyword) identify keyword seed nodes; (2) semantic path — the query is embedded, top-60 papers retrieved by title and abstract cosine similarity, reranked by bge-reranker-large, top-15 retained per field, combined as s_emb = (0.4·s_title + 0.6·s_abs)/normalizer; (3) title path — GROBID extracts all titles from the input, LLM assigns confidence scores, top-10 titles are matched by exact text or fuzzy similarity using 0.65·LCS + 0.35·Jaccard-overlap (threshold 0.88). Seeds are merged with a unified pre-graph score s_pre = 0.3·s_emb + 0.8·s_title + bonus (0.35 exact hit, 0.10 fuzzy). In the graph-propagation phase, 2-hop subgraph expansion from all seeds (max 500 nodes per hop per entity type) constructs a local subgraph; edge weights are hand-set constants by type (HAS_KEYWORD=1.20, CITES=1.00, RELATED_TO=0.90, AUTHORED=0.80, COAUTHOR and COOCCUR=0.60 with logarithmic frequency scaling). Random Walk with Restart runs until ‖r^(t+1)−r^(t)‖₁ < 10^-6 or T_max=50 iterations. Final score: s_final = min(1, 0.35·s_pre + 0.45·s_graph·g_p + 0.20·imp_final), where g_p = max(0.25, s_pre) gates graph-discovered papers. Top-20 papers are returned with score breakdowns and path-based explanations. Three critical design decisions: (i) LLM keyword extraction over OpenAlex Concepts — well-justified, as Concepts cover only 65K distinct entries for 480M papers and remain too coarse; (ii) the graph support factor g_p that floors at 0.25 to prevent semantically-irrelevant graph-discovered nodes from dominating — reasonable but sensitivity to this floor value is unstated; (iii) hand-tuned edge weights rather than learned weights — unjustified, no ablation. Key Results No quantitative retrieval evaluation is reported. The paper presents: (1) scale statistics — 43.30M papers, 109.70M authors, 3.76M keywords, 0.12M institutions, 3B triplets; (2) a latency claim that retrieval completes within 2 minutes; (3) one qualitative idea-grounding example using the authors' own prior paper (InnoEval); (4) one qualitative idea-generation example ("Federated Knowledge Editing" from a "Knowledge Editing" query); (5) one qualitative trend-prediction example for spiking neural network learning; (6) one anonymized researcher background summary. No precision, recall, NDCG, MRR, or Hit@K is reported on any IR benchmark. No comparison to Semantic Scholar, Google Scholar, OpenScholar, or OmniScientist on any quantitative retrieval metric. OmniScientist is dismissed qualitatively (lacks keyword integration and performs only shallow citation propagation) without numeric evidence. All baselines are absent. Novelty Classification NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The KG itself (43M papers, 3B triplets, 9-type heterogeneous schema) is the primary contribution; the tri-path RWR retrieval algorithm is the secondary contribution. The underlying components — Random Walk with Restart (Tong et al. 2006), bge embeddings, GROBID, Neo4j, OpenAlex data — are all existing. What is genuinely novel is the specific schema design (4-level organization plus LLM-extracted keyword layer) and its combination with tri-path seeding; applying heterogeneous RWR to academic retrieval is a known idea applied here at unprecedented scale. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations are present — there is no comparison of keyword-only vs. semantic-only vs. title-only vs. combined paths, and no comparison of RWR vs. simple seed-set retrieval. No error bars, significance tests, or multiple trial results appear anywhere. The GitHub repository (https://github.com/zjunlp/SciAtlas) releases retrieval interfaces and downstream task code, but the full KG construction pipeline is described as forthcoming ("will open our KG construction code"). The KG data itself — 3B triplets — is not stated to be publicly downloadable; users are directed toward building it themselves from OpenAlex. Two methodological concerns: (1) all demonstrated examples use the authors' own recent papers as queries, creating a selection bias in qualitative evidence; (2) the retrieval hyperparameters (λ_pre=0.35, λ_graph=0.45, λ_imp=0.20; β_hk=1.20; g_p floor=0.25) appear to have been set by intuition with no tuning study. This is a preprint (technical report), not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS. - Practical: Running the full system requires deploying Neo4j with 157M nodes and 3B edges, likely requiring >32GB RAM, several TB of disk, and a server-class machine; feasible for a well-resourced research group but not a commodity workstation. - Efficient: Retrieval is claimed to complete within 2 minutes end-to-end; the 2-hop cap at 500 nodes per hop per type prevents subgraph explosion; bge embedding inference and RWR are computationally modest relative to iterative LLM calls, making the design sensible for latency-sensitive use. - Effective: Not demonstrated beyond anecdotal examples; whether graph-augmented retrieval actually returns more relevant papers than dense retrieval alone is an open empirical question that the paper does not close. - Scalable: The system ingests periodic OpenAlex changefiles (every 2 months); real-time automated updates are listed as future work. The 43M-paper scale is the only scale tested; no stress-scaling experiment to 2× or 10× is reported. The system delivers retrieval interfaces and downstream task scripts; the trained embedding model (bge-large-en-v1.5) is an externally released artifact reused here; the KG dataset itself is not clearly publicly downloadable at time of writing. Strengths - Scale and coverage: 43M papers across 26 disciplines with 3B relational triplets is among the largest structured academic KGs described in the literature, and broad disciplinary coverage from Medicine to Veterinary enables cross-disciplinary path finding that domain-specific resources cannot. - Schema expressiveness: The 4-level organization (semantic/conceptual/direction/social) and LLM-extracted normalized Keyword entity layer — deliberately stripped of paper-specific terminology to be reusable — is more semantically principled than raw OpenAlex Concepts or simple citation-only graphs. - Honesty about evaluation gap: The authors explicitly state in the Limitations section that the paper "merely presents running examples of downstream tasks, remaining at the qualitative analysis level" and commit to developing quantitative benchmarks. This is a credibility-positive acknowledgment that reviewers should weight. - Retrieval latency design: The architectural choice to precompute embeddings, cap subgraph expansion, and use convergence-bounded RWR rather than iterative LLM calls is sound engineering that produces a principled response-time guarantee. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors identify four explicit gaps: (1) CLI and agentic skill wrappers are missing — users must write raw Cypher queries; (2) the KG covers only paper-centric entities, missing atomic knowledge units, theorems, datasets, and code, which limits its coverage of the full research workflow; (3) no benchmarks or quantitative metrics exist for downstream tasks — all results are qualitative examples; (4) dynamic update relies on manual periodic scripts rather than automated real-time ingestion. The authors confront limitations directly and propose concrete future directions for each, which lends credibility. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Author deduplication is explicitly skipped due to name ambiguity, meaning the 109.70M author nodes likely contain substantial duplicates. Corrupted COAUTHOR paths propagate errors through the RWR, but this is not analyzed or bounded. - All edge type weights (HAS_KEYWORD=1.20 through COOCCUR=0.60) are hand-set defaults with no empirical justification or sensitivity analysis. A different weight regime could produce meaningfully different retrieval rankings, and the current settings are not reproducibly derived. - No quality validation of the underlying OpenAlex data is reported. OpenAlex is known to have incorrect author-paper assignments, missing reference edges for pre-2000 papers, and noisy institution linkages — none of which are addressed in the construction pipeline. - The only quantitative claim (2-minute retrieval) is not benchmarked against comparable systems (Semantic Scholar API, OpenScholar, OmniScientist) under controlled conditions, and it is unclear whether this includes the LLM keyword extraction step, which adds latency. - All qualitative demonstrations use the first author's own prior paper (InnoEval, Qiao et al. 2026) as the query anchor, a selection that cannot rule out favorable example-picking. - The paper does not address how keyword normalization quality degrades for highly specialized or emerging sub-disciplines where Qwen3-30B may lack domain grounding. Bottom Line SciAtlas is a substantial infrastructure contribution that practitioners building AI-for-science pipelines should monitor and potentially deploy, but the complete absence of quantitative retrieval evaluation means the claimed advantage of topological reasoning over dense vector search remains an unverified hypothesis rather than a demonstrated result.
Recommended 2605.22878 PDF
#163 — Moderately Innovative (42)

Foundation Protocol: A Coordination Layer for Agentic Society (59 ▲ )

arxiv.org · Daily papers of 26 May 2026
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces the Foundation Protocol (FP), a graph-native coordination layer that unifies identity, organizational structure, economic primitives, and governance oversight across heterogeneous multi-agent systems into a single 7-object protocol vocabulary with a working reference implementation. - Why it's important: Existing agent protocols (MCP, A2A, A2UI, DIDComm, ANP, UCP) each solve a slice of agentic coordination but fragment identity, session state, authority, and provenance at their boundaries, creating integration overhead that pushes toward vertical platform lock-in rather than open, governable interoperability. - How it does it: FP defines four composable planes — Entity & Trust, Transport & Routing, Interaction & Organization, and Regulation & Oversight — sitting beneath existing protocols as a shared control-plane substrate rather than replacing them, with variability pushed into profiles, extensions, and bridges. - The good: The reference implementation includes a working contract/settlement subsystem with a hash-chained arbiter-signed audit trail, a pluggable checkpoint pipeline supporting human-in-the-loop escalation, and an MCP bridge that wraps existing tool servers without modification — making this more than pure protocol speculation. - The missing: The paper contains zero empirical evaluation: no latency or throughput numbers, no comparison of coordination overhead against baselines, no deployment at scale, and no formal security analysis or threat model. - Read it or not: Worth reading for architects designing multi-agent infrastructure who want a structured vocabulary for the governance and economic layers that MCP/A2A leave undefined; skip if you need measured evidence of correctness, performance, or adoption at scale.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Resilience (30) and Rigor (30) are the dimensions that pull the composite down hardest: there is zero empirical evaluation (no latency, throughput, or overhead numbers), no security threat model, and a single-arbiter contract subsystem that is an unanalyzed single point of trust and failure. Impact (45) and Novelty (58) keep it off the floor — the unified entity model is a real design contribution and the open-source reference implementation is a credible proof-of-concept — but with an explicitly incomplete, non-normative spec and no adoption evidence, this remains an early-stage proposal. Composite: 0.15*58 + 0.15*30 + 0.10*45 + 0.15*40 + 0.15*30 + 0.30*45 = 42.
systems Novelty 58 Rigor 30 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 40 Resilience 30 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation As autonomous agents move from single-task tools to persistent actors that browse, purchase, deploy software, and transact on behalf of users and organizations, the bottleneck shifts from model capability to coordination infrastructure. Existing protocols handle specific boundaries well — MCP for tool access, A2A for agent delegation, DIDComm for secure messaging, UCP for commerce — but each carries its own notion of identity, session state, authority, trace, and evidence. When a single real workflow spans multiple protocols, provenance breaks at protocol boundaries, oversight becomes a patchwork of logs and prompt-level conventions, and governance becomes an afterthought assembled from disparate application-layer heuristics. The authors argue this fragmentation will either push toward vertical integration (one platform owning identity, routing, memory, and settlement end-to-end) or produce open but fragile networks that are hard to audit and easy to abuse. Key Idea The core insight is that agents, humans, tools, resources, and organizations should all be treated as nodes in a typed graph — addressable, identity-bearing, and policy-governed — with a minimal 7-object vocabulary (Entity, Session, Activity, Envelope, Event, Receipt/Settlement, Provenance) providing a common control-plane substrate beneath existing domain-specific protocols. This lets the same envelope, routing, checkpoint, and audit infrastructure handle tool invocations, agent delegation, organizational workflows, and economic settlements without requiring separate coordination mechanisms for each interaction type. Methodology This is a protocol specification and reference implementation paper, not an algorithmic paper with an objective function. The architecture organizes into four planes: (1) Entity & Trust — a unified addressable entity model with progressive disclosure of capabilities (capability summaries first, full schemas on demand) and pluggable trust signals including attestations, stakes, and reputation hooks; (2) Transport & Routing — transport-agnostic semantics with concrete bindings in profiles, currently defaulting to WebSocket for host-to-host and HTTP REST for client-to-host; (3) Interaction & Organization — sessions as scoped multi-party containers with role/budget bindings, typed event streams with ordering and backpressure, and ledger-agnostic economic primitives (metering records, signed receipts, settlement references, dispute signals); (4) Regulation & Oversight — a pluggable checkpoint pipeline through which every inbound message passes ordered policy enforcement points before reaching its handler, producing structured traces of decisions including rejections and human escalations. Three design decisions are central: (a) progressive disclosure over prompt stuffing — entities exchange lightweight EntityCards by default and reveal full schemas only after counterparty selection, justified by token overhead in current MCP integrations (reasonable, but not quantified); (b) small core plus profiles/extensions/bridges — variability in transport, identity method, and domain patterns is kept out of the core to maintain stability across protocol versions (reasonable); (c) flat entity model with behavioral specialization via handlers rather than type hierarchies — a new entity kind requires a new handler, not a new protocol path (clean engineering, but abstraction cost is unstated). The reference implementation (Python: foundation-protocol + ai-link-net) includes a contract state machine (DRAFT → PENDING → ACTIVE → COMPLETING → SETTLING → SETTLED, with CANCELLED and DISPUTED branches), hash-chained signed snapshots per state transition, escrow and direct payment modes, a 5-dimensional reputation system derived from closed-loop contract facts, an MCP bridge translating FP INVOKE to JSON-RPC tools/call, and a CLI adapter for external AI providers (Claude Code, Codex CLI). Current topology is a tree (parent/child Hosts); the paper asserts peer-to-peer extension is possible without core changes but does not implement or test it. Key Results There are no quantitative results. The paper demonstrates feasibility through a worked scenario (an AI company with human oversight exercising all five planes) and a capability comparison table against six existing protocols. The comparison table is self-assessed and documents which capabilities FP claims to support versus which existing protocols partially or fully address — it is a boundary map, not a performance measurement. No benchmarks, no deployments, no user studies, no comparisons of actual integration overhead. The specification is explicitly stated to be incomplete: the authors call for turning the architecture into a precise specification and reference bindings as future work. Novelty Classification NEW SYSTEM/TOOL + NEW FORMULATION. The genuinely novel contribution is the unified entity model that places organizations, humans, agents, and tools on the same addressing, identity, and policy surface as first-class protocol objects — not application-layer conventions — combined with a governance-first design that embeds checkpoint-based policy enforcement and cryptographic provenance into the protocol's normal flows. The individual components (DID-based identity, checkpoint pipelines, event streams with backpressure, economic attestation, hash-chained audit trails) are known ideas. The specific combination and its application to cross-protocol multi-agent coordination as a society-level substrate is novel in framing, though not yet validated. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations, no controlled experiments, no error bars, no significance tests. The reference implementation is released open source at github.com/FoundationAgents/foundation-protocol and github.com/FoundationAgents/ai-link-net, which enables inspection and independent evaluation of the design. However, the specification itself is non-normative — the appendix explicitly labels the implementation as such. There is no formal protocol specification document, no test suite coverage report, no interoperability test results. The paper is an arXiv preprint (arXiv:2605.23218v1, May 2026); it has not been peer-reviewed. The security properties (Ed25519 signatures, X25519 + AES-256-GCM encryption) are stated but not formally analyzed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SYSTEMS — a built protocol stack and reference implementation, evaluated by design walkthrough rather than deployed measurement. - Practical: The Python reference implementation runs on standard infrastructure (WebSocket, HTTP REST) with no exotic hardware requirements; a developer can stand up a local FP node and run cross-host interactions with existing MCP tool servers. - Efficient: No performance numbers of any kind — no message latency, no checkpoint pipeline throughput, no overhead of cryptographic signing per message, no memory cost of the append-only mailbox at scale; cost claims are entirely absent. - Effective: Feasibility is demonstrated through a walked scenario, not empirical measurement; no real deployment is reported, and no evidence is presented that the protocol reduces actual integration or governance overhead relative to alternatives. - Scalable: The tree topology creates a single-root bottleneck; the paper asserts peer-to-peer extension is possible by adding lateral routing entries without core changes, but this is an architectural claim only — no implementation, no analysis of routing table size, no test of the hash-chained audit trail at large contract volumes. The work delivers a software artifact (Python library and application-network server) plus a protocol design document; the artifact is non-normative and the protocol specification is explicitly incomplete. Strengths - The unified entity model that gives organizations, humans, agents, and tools identical addressing and identity surfaces is a concrete design contribution that closes a genuine gap: existing protocols represent organizations only as application-layer conventions, making cross-organizational policy enforcement and provenance fragile. - The checkpoint pipeline with human-in-the-loop escalation built into the protocol layer (not wrapped around it as an application concern) is a deployable governance mechanism — any checkpoint can defer to a human owner with the same ordering, tracing, and audit guarantees as automated checks. - The hash-chained, arbiter-signed contract audit trail with participant EntityCard capture at contract creation time provides a cryptographic evidence spine that survives entity metadata changes and supports third-party verification without payload exposure — a practical advance over log-based approaches. - The progressive-disclosure principle (EntityCard first, full capability schema on demand) directly addresses a real cost in current MCP integrations where full tool specifications are injected into model context before need, and the design provides a concrete mechanism (reference fetch post-authorization) rather than just a principle. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors are explicit that the paper stops at architecture: "The next step is to turn this architecture into a precise specification and a set of reference bindings, so that the ecosystem can evaluate, implement, and refine FP in practice." They label the reference implementation non-normative and defer transport binding, identity method, and payment rail to profiles and deployment environments. They do not discuss performance, security threat models, or failure modes of the arbiter role. The confrontation of limitations is honest in scope but brief — the paper does not systematically characterize what remains unresolved. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The arbiter in the contract/settlement subsystem is a single point of trust and availability: arbiter compromise, collusion, key loss, or downtime can stall or corrupt every contract. The paper provides no arbiter redundancy mechanism, no Byzantine fault tolerance analysis, and no key rotation procedure. - Trust bootstrapping in an open network is not resolved: FP provides hooks for trust signals but specifies no mechanism for establishing initial trust between previously unknown entities — the hardest part of decentralized identity — leaving this to deployment-specific conventions. - No security threat model is present: prompt injection via malicious EntityCards, Sybil attacks on the entity model, replay attacks on signed envelopes, and checkpoint bypass via protocol downgrade are not analyzed. - The 5-dimensional reputation system is derived from contract facts but is not calibrated against any outcome data; whether quality, reliability, collaboration, efficiency, and integrity dimensions actually predict future counterparty behavior is entirely unvalidated. - Economic incentives for operating Hosts and arbiters are unaddressed: who runs the infrastructure and under what incentive structure is not discussed, which matters for decentralization claims. - The comparison table (Table 2) is self-assessed with no independent verification; the "partial/indirect" ratings for competing protocols may understate their coverage of FP's claimed gaps. Bottom Line A practitioner designing multi-agent infrastructure will find FP's four-plane architecture and checkpoint pipeline a useful design vocabulary and the reference implementation a credible proof-of-concept, but the absence of empirical evaluation, a complete specification, security analysis, or evidence of adoption makes this an early-stage design proposal rather than deployable infrastructure.
Recommended 2605.23218 PDF
#164 — Moderately Innovative (42)

Energy-Aware Computing in the Year 2026

arxiv.org · genai manufacturing - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents a comprehensive survey and holistic taxonomy of energy-aware computing across the Cloud-Edge-HPC continuum, synthesizing ~250 references spanning hardware design, profiling tools, software optimization, generative AI energy footprint, cooling systems, and regulatory perspectives through early 2026. - Why it's important: ICT energy consumption has become a macro-environmental crisis: Exascale systems draw 15–40 MW and cost $4,000–$5,700/hour in electricity, LLM training clusters consume 100–150 MW, and inference now accounts for the majority of the total AI energy footprint, making the field-wide lack of a unified, cross-stack reference map a real gap for researchers and practitioners. - How it does it: The authors apply a multi-stage hybrid Systematic Literature Review (SLR) that combines Q1-journal and CORE A/A* conference papers (SC, ISCA, MICRO, ASPLOS, EuroSys) with high-impact grey literature (vendor whitepapers, IEA reports), filtered to ~250 post-2023-preferenced references, supplemented by a keyword-frequency bibliometric analysis of 2,133 extracted author terms. - The good: The taxonomy (Figure 2) is genuinely holistic — bridging IoT milliwatt inference through Exascale supercomputers and explicitly including regulatory/governance dimensions — and the quantitative bibliometric breakdowns (e.g., 42% of hardware papers now focus on AI accelerators; 45% of profiling papers use software carbon estimators) provide concrete, field-wide evidence of research-community pivots rather than mere assertion. - The missing: The survey cites efficiency figures from primary literature (e.g., "37–51% carbon savings from Carbon Elasticity," "78% emission reduction from CarbonEdge") without any meta-evaluation of experimental validity, comparability of measurement methodology, or generalizability, so the reported numbers cannot be meaningfully compared across cited works. - Read it or not: Worth reading for HPC systems researchers, data center architects, and Green AI practitioners who need a current, well-organized map of the 2025–2026 energy-aware computing landscape; can be skipped by those seeking critical synthesis or quantitative meta-analysis.
Why this rank: Profile: empirical-default (survey, no system built). Pulled down primarily by Rigor (45) and Resilience (40): a multi-stage SLR lacking PRISMA diagram, inter-rater reliability, or replication package, defending small sub-corpora (N=33-52) as representative, and propagating non-comparable efficiency figures (37-51% carbon, 78% cuts) from heterogeneous works without meta-evaluation — even while noting NVML underestimates ~75% on loaded GPUs. Novelty (50) is modest: currency and regulatory/embodied-carbon framing are fresh, but the taxonomy recombines existing schemes on a new snapshot. Practical Impact is low (35) as a paper-only artifact. Completeness (55) is helped by honest scoping ('navigation guide, NOT authoritative synthesis'). Non-peer-reviewed preprint without critical meta-analysis.
empirical Novelty 50 Rigor 45 Non-Obv 38 Completeness 55 Resilience 40 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation The end of Dennard Scaling has made energy the primary operational, financial, and environmental bottleneck in computing. Exascale systems (El Capitan at 29.68 MW, Aurora at 38.69 MW, JUPITER at 15.87 MW) impose electricity costs of $3,400–$5,700/hour and carbon footprints of 6,000–12,400 kgCO2e/hour (the outlier being LUMI at ~355 kgCO2e/hour on near-100% renewables). LLM training on 100,000-GPU clusters consuming 100–150 MW has shifted from an operational concern to a macro-environmental crisis. Prior surveys have addressed siloed sub-domains — HPC scheduling, or edge computing, or AI model compression — without integrating the full IoT-to-Exascale continuum or explicitly treating the emerging embodied-carbon and regulatory dimensions. Inference, not training, now dominates the AI energy footprint, yet most academic literature trails commercial reality by 2–3 years. Key Idea This is a survey, not a methods paper, so there is no single novel technical trick. The central intellectual contribution is the organization of the field into a four-pillar taxonomy: (1) Key Metrics & Hardware (from milliwatt MCUs to Exascale accelerators), (2) Software-Level Energy Optimization (compilation, DVFS, VM consolidation, carbon-aware scheduling), (3) Generative AI & Green Workloads (training, inference, RAG, federated learning, compression), and (4) Infrastructure & Perspectives (cooling, embodied carbon, regulation, energy reuse). The authors additionally provide an explicit "Red AI vs. Green AI" framing and argue — with supporting bibliometric evidence — that the field is shifting from operational carbon as the dominant concern to embodied (manufacturing) carbon as grids decarbonize. Methodology The SLR pipeline operates in two stages. Stage one defines a starting pool of highly cited papers explicitly focused on sustainable HPC/cloud/edge computing, AI workloads, power capping, and hardware telemetry, then applies two filters: (a) publication venue ranked Q1 (SCImago/JCR) or CORE A/A* at time of publication; (b) for theoretical energy models, empirical validation on real devices or validated simulators is required. Stage two integrates grey literature — vendor whitepapers (NVIDIA Blackwell, AMD MI300X, Google TPU Trillium), IEA data center forecasts, Paris Agreement targets — to capture practitioner-established state-of-the-art that precedes formal academic recognition. The resulting corpus of ~250 references is analyzed via keyword frequency (2,133 terms, 949 unique), yielding the Figure 3 word cloud and the quantitative bibliometric distributions cited throughout. The taxonomy itself (Figure 2) is constructed post-hoc by the authors to organize these contributions into 12 leaf categories under 4 branches. Critical design decision: the authors explicitly exclude less-recent papers, books, and below-threshold conference/journal publications as a deliberate, acknowledged scope choice. Key Results The paper reports no original experimental results. Its primary quantitative contributions are bibliometric distributions from small sub-corpora: 42% of N=43 hardware papers focus on AI super-accelerators; 33% on Edge/IoT; 25% on CPUs/networks. For software optimization (N=52 papers): 46% on VM consolidation and carbon-aware scheduling; 31% on DVFS/power capping; 23% on code/compiler optimizations. For profiling methodology (N=33 papers): 45% focus on software carbon estimators; 34% on in-band silicon/eBPF; 21% on out-of-band physical monitoring. For Green AI (N=45 papers): 42% target inference optimization; 31% decentralization/federated learning; 27% training. Quantitative claims from cited primary literature include: COUNTDOWN recovers 37% power savings at node level during MPI wait times; Carbon Elasticity saves 37–51% carbon without synchronization penalty; CarbonEdge achieves 78% emission reductions with <5 ms latency overhead; MCI-based deliberate pausing can reduce carbon by >80%; migrating GenAI inference to edge saves >90% energy vs. cloud. Hardware cost data: Aurora consumes 38.69 MW producing ~12,380 kgCO2e/hour on a high-fossil grid vs. LUMI at 7.10 MW producing ~355 kgCO2e/hour on near-100% renewables — the starkest quantitative finding in the paper, illustrating that grid carbon intensity dwarfs PUE optimization. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING (bibliometric analysis of 2024–2026 research-community trends) + SURVEY. The primary contribution is organizational synthesis, not a technical advance. Genuinely novel aspects versus prior surveys: (1) explicit, current (2025–2026) coverage including SC'25, ASPLOS'25, HPCA'25, MICRO'25, and ISCA'25 publications — rare for a survey; (2) explicit treatment of regulatory compliance (EU AI Act, CSRD), embodied carbon, and energy reuse factor as first-class topics; (3) quantitative evidence of the inference-over-training shift in Green AI literature. The taxonomy itself is a standard combination of existing categorization schemes applied to a new temporal snapshot. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations, error bars, or significance tests apply (survey format). The SLR methodology is described with explicit selection criteria, which is a credibility positive, but several gaps undermine rigor. The bibliometric sample sizes (N=33 to N=52 papers per sub-corpus) are small and not defended as statistically representative; different paper selection could shift percentages substantially. No PRISMA flow diagram, inter-rater reliability metric, or replication package is provided. The authors acknowledge (Section 2.3) that their popularity-driven, venue-ranked filter may exclude "genuine/valuable contributions" — this is honest but means the bibliometric distributions measure field visibility, not field completeness. Primary literature efficiency numbers (37%, 51%, 78%, 80%, 90%) are cited without any assessment of experimental controls, hardware platforms, workload coverage, or comparability of measurement instrumentation across cited papers. The paper is a preprint submitted to Elsevier and has not yet been peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: SURVEY — no system is built, deployed, or evaluated by the authors. - Practical: The paper produces no software artifact, benchmark, or tool; all deployability assessments belong to the primary literature cited, not this survey. - Efficient: Not applicable; the survey's computational cost is the SLR process itself. - Effective: As a navigation aid, the taxonomy and bibliometric analysis are genuinely useful for orienting a researcher entering the field, but no experiment validates whether the survey correctly captures field consensus. - Scalable: The survey discusses scalability limits (RAPL observer effect above 100 Hz, TSDB bottlenecks at 100,000 GPUs, 15-year vs. 18-month upgrade cycles) but tests none of them. The work delivers a paper-form artifact only — no code, dataset, model weights, or benchmark suite is released. Strengths - The taxonomy (Figure 2) explicitly bridges the milliwatt-to-megawatt continuum and includes regulatory/governance and embodied-carbon dimensions routinely absent from systems surveys, making it more actionable as a research roadmap than most prior work in this space. - Currency is a genuine strength: the inclusion of SC'25, ASPLOS'25, HPCA'25, MICRO'25, and ISCA'25 references — alongside 2026-published journal papers — makes this among the most temporally current surveys in the field. - The explicit identification of field-wide pathologies — the PFAS regulatory crisis ending two-phase immersion cooling viability, the "sparsity trap" invalidating unstructured pruning on systolic arrays, the MCI optimization congestion failure mode, the Jevons Paradox undermining efficiency gains — demonstrates critical engagement rather than mere annotation. - The quantitative bibliometric distributions, while based on small N, provide concrete directional evidence of research-community pivots (e.g., the inference-over-training shift in Green AI, the software estimator displacement of physical out-of-band monitoring) that are not available elsewhere in such synthesized form. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly acknowledge (Section 2.3) that the popularity-driven, venue-ranked selection filter may have excluded genuine contributions that are less recent, appear in books or blogs, or were published in lower-ranked venues. They note that "most of the aforementioned papers are cited in those selected" — a reasonable but unverified claim. The survey also explicitly scopes itself to the IT-related activities phase of the energy lifecycle (not manufacturing/disposal), which is noted as a deliberate boundary. These are confronted directly and credibly. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No meta-evaluation of evidence quality: efficiency figures from disparate primary works (different hardware, workloads, measurement instruments, evaluation scales) are cited side-by-side as if comparable, which they are not — a 37% power savings on a single Frontier node may not generalize to a different system or workload. - Small bibliometric N (33–52 papers per sub-corpus) is not defended as representative; the survey does not test sensitivity of its percentage-based conclusions to inclusion/exclusion of borderline papers. - No comparison to prior energy-aware computing surveys (e.g., Lin et al. 2024, Hasan et al. 2025 are cited but not compared as prior surveys) to establish what this synthesis adds over existing coverage. - The forward-looking Section 9 conflates laboratory prototypes (photonic computing with claimed 229× EDP reduction, AMS in-memory computing at 490 TOPS/W) with operationally deployed systems (eBPF-based monitoring, liquid cooling at Exascale sites) without distinguishing maturity levels. - The paper does not address measurement methodology heterogeneity across cited works: RAPL, external wattmeters, NVML, and vendor self-reported TDP numbers have known systematic biases (NVML underestimates by ~75% on heavily loaded GPUs per cited reference [13]), making the cited aggregate efficiency numbers potentially incomparable even within the survey. Bottom Line A practitioner or researcher who needs a well-organized, genuinely current entry point to the 2025–2026 green computing landscape — spanning IoT to Exascale, hardware to regulation — will find this survey useful as a navigation guide, but its absence of critical meta-analysis and its small bibliometric sample sizes mean it should not be treated as an authoritative synthesis of which techniques actually work at scale.
Search Results 2605.24569 PDF
#165 — Moderately Innovative (42)

When Interpretability Becomes a Liability: Adversarial Attacks on CBM Concept Layers

Aditya Sridhar · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a framework for concept-level adversarial attacks on Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) and proposes SPECTRA, a stability regularization defense that hardens the concept space against such attacks. - Why it's important: CBMs are deployed in safety-critical domains (medical diagnosis, autonomous driving) where an adversary who can flip semantic concept activations via imperceptible pixel perturbations could cause clinically plausible but catastrophically wrong predictions without triggering standard anomaly detectors. - How it does it: The attack is cast as a constrained quadratic program over the linear concept-to-class mapping, yielding a closed-form minimal-norm perturbation; SPECTRA adds a stability loss term (-log(1 + ||δ_min||²)) to the training objective that directly penalizes small decision margins in concept space. - The good: SPECTRA induces a sharp phase transition at λs = 0.083 where the required attack perturbation norm jumps from 1.97 to 14.30 with only a 2% accuracy drop, and reaches norms exceeding 4,200 at λs = 0.100. - The missing: The entire experimental evaluation is conducted on a 15-class subset of CUB-200-2011 under white-box conditions with a linear classifier; no comparison to alternative defenses (e.g., adversarial training) is provided, and no evaluation confirms that concept semantics survive regularization. - Read it or not: Worth reading for researchers interested in interpretable ML security or CBM robustness, but treat experimental claims cautiously — the evaluation is too narrow to support broad deployment recommendations.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (28) is the dominant drag: evaluation is a self-selected 15-class CUB subset on one backbone (ResNet-18), white-box only, with no seeds, no error bars, no released code, no comparison to the obvious adversarial-training baseline, an unexplained baseline-attackability discrepancy (3.8054 vs 2.196), and a self-reported >85% transfer claim with no supporting table. Resilience (32) and Completeness (38) are also low — the defense is never tested for concept-semantic fidelity (its whole purpose), and the white-box assumption is the strongest possible threat model. Novelty (65) is the redeeming dimension: the concept-level attack surface for CBMs is a genuine NEW PROBLEM and the closed-form QP attack is clean (Non-Obv 55), but Impact (30) is just paper-form methodology with no artifact. Weighted: 0.20*65 + 0.25*28 + 0.15*55 + 0.15*38 + 0.15*32 + 0.10*30 = 42.
empirical Novelty 65 Rigor 28 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 38 Resilience 32 Impact 30
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Concept Bottleneck Models decompose prediction into a concept-encoder (f: X → C) and a concept-to-class classifier (g: C → Y), where concepts are intended to be human-interpretable attributes (e.g., "curved beak," "irregular border"). This explicit semantic bottleneck, while valuable for interpretability and intervention, creates a new attack surface: an adversary who can manipulate concept activations via pixel-level perturbations can cause semantically coherent but incorrect predictions — a malignant lesion misclassified as benign while the explanation remains plausible. Prior adversarial robustness research focuses on input-space perturbations and has not systematically examined concept-space vulnerabilities in inherently interpretable architectures. Key Idea Because the concept-to-class head is linear (g(c) = Wc + b), the minimal-norm concept perturbation that flips a prediction to any target class t reduces to a constrained quadratic program with a closed-form solution via the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse. This analytical tractability enables both exact attack computation and a differentiable stability loss whose gradient can be propagated back through training to increase the margins explicitly. The monotonic robustness transfer theorem (Theorem 1) then guarantees that hardening concept space provably hardensthe input pixel space, at a rate inversely proportional to the Lipschitz constant L of the concept encoder. Methodology The pipeline has four components. (1) Attack formulation: given a concept vector c and linear classifier W, b, the minimal-norm perturbation δ satisfying all C-1 class-margin constraints is computed via δ_min = A†b (Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse, O(CK²)), where A has rows (w_t - w_k)ᵀ and b encodes classification margins. A single-constraint O(K) variant also provided. (2) Robustness metrics: attackability A(c, y*) = 1/(||δ_min||₂ + ε_stab) and its dataset-level average; relative perturbation norm is the primary safety indicator. (3) SPECTRA defense: adds L_stability = -log(1 + ||δ_min||²) to the combined BCE/CE training loss with weight λs, with 5-epoch linear warmup. Theoretical bound (Eq. 8, via Jensen's inequality) shows E[||δ_min||²] scales exponentially with λs. (4) Extension to nonlinear classifiers via local Jacobian linearization; the paper claims >85% attack transferability but this figure is self-reported. Critical design decisions: focusing on the linear head (well-justified for interpretability, but restricts the threat model); using log(1 + ·) stability loss (prevents gradient explosion); domain-agnostic training objective requiring only concept-annotated supervision. Key Results All experiments use a 15-class subset of CUB-200-2011 (selected from 200 classes for "computational efficiency") with ResNet-18 backbone. Baseline CBM (λs=0): attackability 2.196, relative perturbation norm 0.46, top-1 accuracy 72.2%. At λs=0.083 (identified as optimal): attackability 0.070 (86% reduction), perturbation norm 14.30, accuracy 70.2% (2% drop). At λs=0.100: attackability ≈0, perturbation norm 4,250, accuracy 61.6%. At λs=0.300 the norm reaches 49 million but accuracy falls to 54.9%. The paper identifies a sharp phase transition at λs=0.083. Sparsity loss drops 99% from baseline to λs=1.0. Qualitative examples (Figure 2) show PGD and noise attacks flipping predictions on M1 (λs=0) while SPECTRA remains stable. No comparison to adversarial training or other defenses is made. There are internal numerical inconsistencies: the abstract cites a baseline attackability of 3.8054 and the conclusion refers to "attackability scores exceeding 3.8," but Table 1 shows 2.196 as the baseline — this discrepancy is unexplained. Novelty Classification NEW PROBLEM + NEW METHOD. The concept-level adversarial attack framing for CBMs is genuinely new; prior feature-space attacks do not target interpretable concept bottlenecks. The SPECTRA defense and associated robustness metrics are novel in this setting. The core mathematical machinery (constrained QP attacks, pseudoinverse solutions, Lipschitz-based transfer bounds) applies standard optimization tools to a new structure — the novelty is primarily in problem identification and application rather than fundamental algorithmic invention. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations over λs are provided (Table 1, 8 values) and reveal the phase transition, but no error bars, multiple random seeds, or statistical significance tests are reported anywhere. No code, model weights, or data splits are released (no repository mentioned). The evaluation is conducted on a self-selected 15-class subset with no justification for which species were chosen or whether results generalize to the full 200-class problem. The claim that linear attacks transfer to nonlinear classifiers with >85% effectiveness is stated without experimental detail — no table, no architecture variants, no controlled comparison. The paper does not evaluate whether SPECTRA preserves concept semantic fidelity (e.g., concept prediction accuracy or concept alignment metrics), which is essential for an interpretable ML defense. The numerical inconsistency between the abstract's "3.8054" and Table 1's "2.196" baseline attackability is a reproducibility flag. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: SPECTRA adds only a closed-form regularization term to standard CBM training and runs on ordinary GPU hardware with ResNet-18; the compute overhead scales as O(CK²) per batch, which is manageable for typical CBM concept counts (K ≤ 500). - Efficient: Training cost increases by the stability loss computation, roughly proportional to number of classes × concepts squared per sample; not benchmarked against baseline training time. - Effective: Within the tested 15-class CUB setup under white-box conditions, SPECTRA demonstrably hardens concept margins, but effectiveness against black-box or physically realizable attacks is untested and not claimed. - Scalable: Not tested at the full 200-class CUB scale, let alone larger concept inventories or other domains; scaling behavior of the phase transition threshold (λs=0.083) to different K and C is unknown. The work delivers methodology and algorithms in paper form; no software artifact is released. Strengths - The problem identification is sharp and well-motivated: CBMs' interpretability genuinely creates a novel and underexplored attack surface that is distinct from both input-space and post-hoc explanation attacks. - The closed-form attack via constrained QP is analytically clean and computationally efficient, enabling exact minimal-norm perturbations rather than iterative approximations used in most adversarial attack literature. - Theorem 1 (monotonic robustness transfer) provides a formal link between concept-space hardening and input-space security, grounding the defense in theory rather than heuristics. - The empirical phase transition (attackability collapsing by 86% over a narrow λs range) is a striking and practically useful finding — it simplifies hyperparameter selection for practitioners. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge: (1) the linear classifier assumption restricts exact closed-form analysis, though they argue local linearization extends coverage to nonlinear classifiers; (2) evaluation is limited to CUB visual concepts, and generalization to medical or textual CBMs requires further work; (3) black-box and physical attacks are not evaluated and are left as future work. These limitations are stated directly, which is a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The 15-class subset selection is a critical methodological weakness: with no stated inclusion criteria, results may not generalize to the full 200-class problem where decision boundaries are denser and concept overlap higher. - No comparison against alternative defenses — adversarial training is cited repeatedly as a baseline approach but never implemented or compared, leaving SPECTRA's relative advantage unmeasured. - The paper does not measure whether SPECTRA degrades concept interpretability (e.g., concept prediction MSE, concept alignment, or intervention accuracy), yet preserving interpretability is the stated purpose of CBMs; a defense that makes concepts sparser (99% sparsity reduction) may undermine the very property it is protecting. - The numerical inconsistency between the abstract's baseline attackability (3.8054) and Table 1 (2.196) is unexplained — either there is a data discrepancy or two different experimental settings are being reported. - The white-box threat model assumed throughout (adversary knows W and b) is the strongest possible assumption; in practice, concept-to-class weights may not be accessible, and the paper gives no guidance on how vulnerability changes under partial knowledge. - The Lipschitz constant L appears in the robustness transfer bound but is never estimated for the specific ResNet-18 architecture used, making the quantitative guarantee in Theorem 1 uninformative in practice. - The paper is a preprint from an independent researcher with no institutional affiliation and no peer review indicated; the "thoughtful reviewer" acknowledgment is unexplained given the arXiv-only status. Bottom Line The problem framing is legitimate and the analytical attack construction is sound, but the evaluation — one dataset subset, one architecture, no defense baselines, no code, no seeds — is too thin to support the deployment recommendations the paper makes, and a practitioner needs to see results at full CUB scale and against adversarial training before adopting SPECTRA.

#166 — Moderately Innovative (42)

Personalized Marketing Through Artificial Intelligence: Architectures, Techniques, and Emerging Frontiers

ijsrem.com · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a five-layer production architecture for AI-driven personalized marketing that combines ML-based customer clustering, BERT-based NLP, an LSTM recommendation engine, and a contextual bandit re-ranker, along with a novel Model Staleness Index (MSI) metric for drift-triggered retraining, evaluated on a 24-month e-commerce dataset of ~2.4 million records. - Why it's important: Deployed AI marketing systems fail in underdocumented ways—asynchronous model staleness, multilingual NLP degradation, privacy-accuracy tradeoffs—that isolated benchmark papers do not address, and practitioners lack honest accounts of these failure modes. - How it does it: Three parallel model subsystems share a centralized Feast feature store to maintain feature consistency across asynchronous update cycles, with a contextual bandit re-ranker incorporating real-time session signals unavailable at training time. - The good: The paper reports concrete deployment failure modes with specific mitigations (MICE imputation bottleneck, Redis sync lag, Hindi/Marathi NLP degradation to F1 ≈ 0.79/0.74) and honestly acknowledges that the differential privacy budget (ε = 1.0) is permissive and that the CLV metric is causally confounded. - The missing: The paper compares against a rule-based baseline and a basic Random Forest, but omits modern transformer-based recommendation baselines (e.g., BERT4Rec, SASRec); the MSI metric is described qualitatively but never formally defined or validated on held-out drift events. - Read it or not: Worth skimming for practitioners building production marketing ML pipelines in regulated South Asian markets who want a candid deployment account; too thin on formal rigor and baseline competitiveness to be useful to ML researchers.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. The candid deployment account and measured DP tradeoff are genuine but modest contributions (Completeness 58).
