Analyst-Identified Gaps - The five DBpedia benchmarks (EN-FR, EN-DE, D-W) are encyclopedic cross-lingual KGs from Wikipedia with zero semantic relationship to satellite network management — excellent performance here provides no evidence that the method handles STIN-specific ontology conflicts. - The only domain-relevant dataset (NI_CH_1K) is self-constructed with 11 classes and yields H@1 of only 29.5%, and the authors dismiss this as expected due to small size — but this is the only evidence about the method's actual target domain. - Verification accuracy (97.09%) is defined as matching both semantic concept and exact numerical parameter against the pre-built ontology — this is essentially a closed-vocabulary retrieval task, not a test of open-world intent refinement or correct policy generation. - The OntoEA model (ref [44]), the most directly relevant prior work combining ontology guidance with KG embedding, is cited in related work but excluded from Table 2 without explanation. - No ablation on λ₁, λ₂, λ₃ or η; the chosen hyperparameter values are stated without sensitivity analysis. - The paper describes a "multi-domain multi-vendor STIN simulation environment" without specifying the number of simulated satellites, network topology size, traffic models, or vendor configurations — the simulation cannot be replicated. - A factual inconsistency within a single paragraph (TransE latency cited as both 145s and 166.79s for 20K samples) raises concerns about experimental consistency. Bottom Line This preprint (not peer-reviewed) adapts established KG alignment techniques to a STIN network management framing with solid gains on generic cross-lingual benchmarks, but the evaluation never touches its actual target domain at meaningful scale, making it premature for practitioners in satellite network orchestration or intent-driven networking to adopt.
Search Results
#172 — Incremental (38)

Uncertainty Reasoning with Large Language Models for Explainable Disease Diagnosis

arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a neuro-symbolic pipeline that uses an LLM to extract weighted fuzzy symptom predicates from free-text clinical notes and a Prolog engine to perform formal, auditable differential diagnosis, with a physician-in-the-loop rule-editing mechanism. - Why it's important: Clinical AI systems must produce verifiable, inspectable reasoning chains for physician accountability and regulatory compliance — properties black-box LLMs cannot provide alone. - How it does it: The LLM converts unstructured notes into (entity, relation, value) triples mapped to fuzzy predicates; SWI-Prolog fires Horn-clause rules when t-norm activation exceeds 0.4; posterior disease probabilities are further reweighted by epidemiological priors; a passive-aggressive online update continuously adjusts rule weights from case feedback. - The good: On MIMIC-IV (real clinical notes with structured signals), the hybrid outperforms GPT-4o by 3.9 percentage points at Top-1 accuracy and cuts API token cost by 48%, while producing reasoning traces rated 3.58/4.0 for explainability. - The missing: The headline benchmark (gretelai/symptom_to_diagnosis) is procedurally generated from rule structures, giving the rule-based symbolic component a structural advantage; simultaneously, the explainability metric is scored by GPT-4o, the very model used as an accuracy baseline, creating two independent circular evaluation problems. - Read it or not: Worth reading for clinical AI researchers interested in physician-in-the-loop neuro-symbolic design, but the accuracy results should not be taken at face value without independent replication given the evaluation confounds.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Rigor (28) is the dominant drag: three unacknowledged confounds — a rule-generated benchmark evaluated by a rule-based system (Goodhart), GPT-4o scoring its own pipeline's explainability while also serving as the accuracy baseline, and post-hoc iCliniq trimming after observing underperformance — plus no error bars and no released code. Resilience (30) is low because the headline gains collapse once the unfair full-system-vs-GPT-4o comparison is corrected to the marginal symbolic contribution, and the hybrid actually underperforms GPT-4o on both real multi-label datasets. Novelty (50) is integration-only (all components established), and Impact (35) reflects that no software artifact is released. The auditable Prolog traces and physician-editable rule base are genuinely useful design ideas, but the performance numbers require independent replication.
empirical Novelty 50 Rigor 28 Non-Obv 45 Completeness 40 Resilience 30 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Clinical diagnosis from free-text encounter notes requires reasoning over incomplete, temporally vague, and graded evidence — phrases like "mild fatigue" or "on-and-off chest heaviness" carry meaning better represented as degrees than Boolean facts. LLMs can parse such language but hallucinate, miscalibrate confidence, and hide their reasoning in opaque model internals, which is unacceptable in settings where physicians must inspect and challenge the system's logic. Pure symbolic systems are transparent but brittle against natural-language variability. The gap is a system that handles messy linguistic input while preserving auditable, physician-revisable inference chains. Key Idea The core trick is a strict two-layer decomposition: the LLM is confined to perception and normalization (extracting structured symptom triples and assigning fuzzy weights from linguistic hedges), while a Prolog engine is confined to deduction (firing Horn-clause rules when the t-norm product of fuzzy activations exceeds a threshold). Because all symbolic commitments are explicit — rules, weights, and proof trees — the reasoning chain is inspectable and modifiable by a physician without retraining any neural component. This is a principled engineering choice, not just a combination of existing tools. Methodology The pipeline has five stages. (1) LLM extraction: GPT-4o converts a clinical note into (entity, relation, value) triples (e.g., `(chest pain, severity, intermittent)`); a second "LLM-as-a-judge" pass removes hallucinated facts against the original text. (2) Fuzzy symbolization: triples map to fuzzy predicates with truth degrees w ∈ [0, 1] derived from linguistic hedges. (3) Prolog rule synthesis: Horn templates T = (H ← B₁, …, Bₙ) are induced by prompting the LLM on sentence–diagnosis pairs; templates scoring above threshold τ are added to the rule base R. (4) Fuzzy Prolog reasoning: SWI-Prolog with `library(fuzzy)` fires rules; overall hypothesis confidence sums products of leaf fuzzy activations across all root-to-leaf proof paths. (5) Epidemiological prior fusion: posterior P(dⱼ|s) = ρⱼ·πⱼ / Σ ρ_d'·π_d', where ρⱼ is max rule activation and πⱼ is retrieved prevalence conditioned on age, sex, and region. Symptom weights are further calibrated via softmax blend of LLM-derived salience and FAISS-retrieved nearest-neighbor priors (α = β = 3). Online rule updating uses a passive-aggressive ranking loss: when a true disease ranks below a false disease, edge weights w_{d⁺,s} are incremented and w_{d⁻,s} decremented by τ = min(C, ℓ/2|x|); edges are structurally added or pruned based on a smoothed specificity ratio r_{d,s} = (c⁺_{d,s}+1)/(c⁻_{d,s}+1). Critical design decisions: (i) confining the LLM to extraction is well-justified for auditability but creates a dependency — the baseline LLM is also the internal extraction engine; (ii) fuzzy membership functions derived from fixed clinical thresholds are hand-crafted, limiting generalization; (iii) the t-norm threshold γ = 0.4 and blending parameters α = β = 3 are stated without ablation. Key Results Three datasets are tested. On gretelai/symptom_to_diagnosis (synthetic, single-label, generated from rule structures): Full Hybrid achieves 44.8% Top-1 accuracy vs. GPT-4o 39.0%, o4-mini 36.5%, DeepSeek-R1 34.4%; Top-5 accuracy 73.5% vs. 66.6% for GPT-4o. On lavita/ChatDoctor-iCliniq (multi-label, real doctor–patient dialogues, full set): Full Hybrid 42.0% Top-1 vs. GPT-4o 48.6% — the hybrid underperforms by 6.6 pp. On the trimmed iCliniq (post-hoc cleaned subset): Full Hybrid 47.0% vs. GPT-4o 49.9% — still underperforms. On MIMIC-IV v3.1 (multi-label, real clinical notes): Full Hybrid 57.2% Top-1 vs. GPT-4o 53.3%, F1 50.7% vs. 47.0%. Input error rates (GPT-4o-judged): 0.65–1.28%; explainability scores (GPT-4o-scored, 0–4): 3.20–3.67. Processing 1,000 samples costs $7.61 vs. $14.68 for standalone GPT-4o, but takes 48.69 vs. 26.61 minutes. No error bars or significance tests are reported. Datasets are toy-to-standard scale; no stress-scale testing. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM. The specific integration of LLM-driven fuzzy extraction, Prolog inference, epidemiological prior fusion, passive-aggressive rule updating, and physician-in-the-loop versioned knowledge base in a single clinical pipeline is not replicated elsewhere in the literature. However, every individual component — fuzzy logic, Prolog, LLM-based NER, FAISS retrieval, passive-aggressive learning — is a well-established technique; the contribution is the integration architecture and the explicit decomposition of perception from deduction. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablation baselines (symbolic-only, symbolic+probabilistic, symbolic+fuzzy) adequately isolate the contribution of each module and confirm both fuzzy quantification and probabilistic inference add value. No error bars, confidence intervals, or significance tests appear anywhere. No code, model weights, or rule base is released (not stated in the paper). Three methodological concerns are significant: (1) Goodhart's Law: the gretelai/symptom_to_diagnosis benchmark is explicitly described as "procedurally generated with ground-truth diagnoses based on rule structures" — evaluating a rule-based symbolic system on rule-generated data creates a structural advantage that inflates the headline result; (2) Circular explainability evaluation: GPT-4o scores the system's output for explainability (0–4), yet GPT-4o is also the primary accuracy baseline and the internal extraction engine — this dual role is never acknowledged; (3) Post-hoc dataset trimming: the iCliniq dataset was cleaned after observing underperformance, with the selection criteria informed by the results — this is not statistically valid without pre-registration or a separate holdout. Deployability (EMPIRICAL) - Practical: The system runs on API-accessible LLMs plus SWI-Prolog with a standard FAISS index; commercially deployable hardware requirements are met. - Efficient: Token cost is halved versus standalone GPT-4o, but wall-clock runtime nearly doubles (48.69 vs. 26.61 minutes per 1,000 samples); latency makes real-time deployment at clinical speed non-trivial. - Effective: The system solves the stated problem on structured clinical notes (MIMIC-IV) but underperforms pure LLM inference on ambiguous multi-label text (iCliniq), and the best-result benchmark is structurally biased. - Scalable: The FAISS index is described at 100k cases; no evaluation beyond this scale is performed or discussed; Prolog inference complexity grows with rule depth and number of hypotheses, with no analysis provided. The work delivers a described system architecture; no software artifact is released. Strengths - The auditable Prolog proof tree is a genuine explanation artifact — not a post-hoc rationalization — and supports counterfactual queries (why did the diagnosis change between K^(t-1) and K^(t)?), which is rare in clinical AI systems. - The versioned, physician-editable rule base with visual diff tooling addresses a real workflow requirement for clinical accountability that no purely neural approach can satisfy. - The 48% cost reduction relative to GPT-4o, while maintaining competitive accuracy on MIMIC-IV, is a practically meaningful result for resource-constrained health systems. - The passive-aggressive online rule update is a lightweight, interpretable mechanism for continual learning that avoids catastrophic forgetting and keeps the rule base inspectable. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly state three limitations: (1) the symbolic rule layer relies on manually curated rules, limiting scalability to new domains or emerging diseases; (2) fuzzy membership functions are expert-crafted from fixed clinical thresholds, risking sub-optimal generalization across heterogeneous populations; (3) symptoms are modeled as static observations, with no explicit representation of temporal onset or evolution. The authors confront these limitations directly, which is a credibility positive, though they do not acknowledge the evaluation confounds identified below. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The comparison of the full hybrid against standalone GPT-4o is not apples-to-apples: the hybrid uses GPT-4o internally for extraction and judge verification. The fair comparison is the marginal accuracy gain attributable to the symbolic layer, not a full system comparison against GPT-4o as an independent oracle. - The gretelai/symptom_to_diagnosis dataset is the paper's strongest result (+5.8 pp over GPT-4o), yet it is generated by rule structures — making it the most favorable possible test for a rule-based system. The paper never flags this circularity. - GPT-4o evaluating the system's explainability scores (the same model used as an accuracy baseline and internally for extraction) is an unacknowledged conflict of interest; no human physician evaluation of reasoning traces is reported. - Post-hoc trimming of the iCliniq dataset after observing underperformance introduces selection bias; the trimmed results should be treated as exploratory, not confirmatory. - The 100k de-identified case FAISS index used for retrieval-augmented symptom weighting has no described provenance, demographic coverage, or licensing information — a significant reproducibility gap. - No ablation tests the LLM-as-a-judge hallucination-filtering step; its contribution to accuracy is unknown. - The paper claims "formally verifiable" reasoning chains, but fuzzy Prolog with probabilistic priors does not support classical formal verification (soundness, completeness guarantees); this language overstates what is actually provided. - Section 3.3 describes temporal alignment of symptoms to relative timestamps, but this temporal information never appears in the downstream Prolog rules — an inconsistency the authors acknowledge indirectly in limitations (point 3) but do not reconcile with the §3.3 design description. Bottom Line Practitioners building physician-in-the-loop clinical AI will find the versioned, auditable rule base and Prolog-grounded explanation traces genuinely useful design ideas, but the accuracy claims rest on a biased benchmark, a circular explainability metric, and an unfair baseline comparison — all unacknowledged — so the performance numbers require independent replication before guiding deployment decisions. This is an arXiv preprint (v1, May 2026); it has not been peer-reviewed.
Search Results 2605.25566 PDF
#173 — Incremental (38)

A Survey on Foundation Models for Structured Data: Tabular, Time Series, and Graphs

preprints.org · 10 new citations to your articles
TL;DR - What it does: This paper provides a systematic survey of 150+ foundation models for structured data — tabular, time series, and graphs — organized under a unified taxonomy of tokenization, architecture, pre-training objectives, and adaptation strategies. - Why it's important: Foundation models for structured data underpin high-stakes decisions in finance, healthcare, and science, but the field lacks cross-modality synthesis; prior surveys cover only one data type, preventing practitioners and researchers from transferring design insights across domains. - How it does it: The survey applies a consistent 7-dimension analytical lens (pre-training data, objectives, tokenization, architecture, adaptation strategy, transferability, benchmarks) to each modality, identifying both shared design patterns and modality-specific solutions. - The good: Tables 1–3 systematically map 75+ named methods across 7–8 design dimensions each, providing the most structured cross-method comparison available for this space at time of writing. - The missing: No empirical comparison of the surveyed methods is performed — performance numbers, rankings, or head-to-head baselines are entirely absent, so the survey cannot guide method selection for a practical problem. - Read it or not: Worth reading as an orientation map for ML researchers entering foundation models for structured data; practitioners needing performance guidance will not find it here.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile (survey, no system/theory). Completeness (35) and Resilience (35) are the dimensions that pull the composite down: there is no empirical meta-analysis to guide method selection, no stated inclusion/exclusion criteria, and an undisclosed conflict of interest with disproportionate self-cited graph coverage. Novelty (45) credits the genuine cross-modal taxonomy, but the five 'unified principles' are generic and the work is descriptive and not peer-reviewed. A useful orientation map, but it meets little beyond reference-document value.
empirical Novelty 45 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 40 Completeness 35 Resilience 35 Impact 40
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation: The foundation model paradigm — large-scale pre-training followed by efficient adaptation — has transformed NLP and computer vision, but its application to structured data lags significantly. The core difficulty is that structured data violates the assumptions that made foundation models work in language/vision. Tabular data is schema-driven with feature-wise heterogeneity and no canonical token granularity. Time series data encodes physical or causal temporal ordering that differs fundamentally from language sequences. Graph data requires permutation-equivariant relational reasoning over non-Euclidean structures. These modality-specific inductive biases mean no single architecture or pre-training objective generalizes cleanly across domains, and no universal tokenization (analogous to words or image patches) exists. Existing surveys address each modality in isolation, making it difficult to compare design choices or transfer insights across tabular, time series, and graph communities. Key Idea: The survey's organizing insight is that tabular, time series, and graph data — despite surface differences — share a common set of structural challenges addressable through a cross-modal design framework: tokenization as structure-preserving abstraction, architecture selection balancing modality-specific inductive bias against scalable general-purpose backbones, pre-training to learn generalizable structural priors rather than task-specific heuristics, and lightweight adaptation that preserves those priors. This unified framing, rather than any new method or result, is the paper's intellectual contribution over modality-specific predecessors. Methodology: This is a systematic literature review, not an empirical study. The authors (1) define the three structured data types and their inductive biases, (2) formalize foundation model characteristics (general-purpose representations, efficient adaptability), (3) diagnose three core challenges — data heterogeneity, lack of canonical tokenization, and coupling between structural semantics and downstream tasks — and (4) apply a consistent taxonomy to each modality. Tabular coverage includes 18 representative methods (Table 1) from venues including Nature, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR 2023–2026. Time series coverage includes 21 methods (Table 2) from NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, TMLR, AAAI 2023–2025. Graph coverage includes 40+ methods (Table 3) from KDD, WWW, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, AAAI, SIGIR 2023–2026. No explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria, search protocol, or PRISMA-style methodology is described. The paper is a preprint on Preprints.org, explicitly marked NOT PEER-REVIEWED, posted May 22, 2026. Key Results: As a survey, the paper reports no original experimental results. Empirical claims cited from the literature include: TabPFNv2 achieves leading performance on small tabular classification/regression datasets (Nature 2025); Chronos and TimesFM demonstrate strong zero-shot N:N domain forecasting; GCOPE and UniGraph generalize across molecular, citation, and social graph benchmarks. The survey identifies several cross-cutting descriptive findings: synthetic pre-training data (structural causal models, periodic priors) dominates tabular and time series FM construction while graph FMs rely more on real-world corpora; in-context learning is the dominant adaptation paradigm for tabular models (most support 1:N transfer) while graph models more commonly require fine-tuning; the majority of time series FMs target forecasting exclusively, with classification/imputation/anomaly detection underrepresented; LLM-based architectures for time series show patch-level tokenization outperforms point-level tokenization for long-range modeling. None of these findings are supported by novel meta-analysis — they are qualitative characterizations of cited works. Novelty Classification: NEW FORMULATION (SURVEY). The unified cross-modal taxonomy is a genuine organizational contribution — no prior survey jointly covers tabular, time series, and graph foundation models under consistent dimensions. The three-challenge diagnosis (heterogeneity, canonicalization, semantic coupling) is a useful conceptual framing. Individual method descriptions draw entirely from published/preprint literature; there is no new method, bound, dataset, or experiment. Rigor & Reproducibility: Standard empirical rigor criteria (ablations, error bars, seeds) do not apply. No inclusion/exclusion criteria for the 150+ papers are stated, creating undisclosed selection risk. The graph section (Table 3, 40+ entries) has approximately twice the density of tabular and time series sections; several entries are co-authored by survey authors (Haonan Yuan appears as first author on GRAVER [18], BRIDGE [21], RAG-GFM [130], GFM-Retriever [131]), a conflict of interest the paper does not acknowledge. Many cited entries are arXiv preprints treated at equal confidence with peer-reviewed results. The GitHub awesome list (github.com/RingBDStack/awesome-structured-data-FM) is the maintenance mechanism but its curation policy is unstated. The paper itself is not peer-reviewed. Deployability (4-property check): Profile: SURVEY — no system built, no model trained, no algorithm implemented. - Practical: The taxonomy tables are immediately usable as a reading guide with no compute cost. - Efficient: 29 pages covering 150+ methods is a reasonable density for an entry point into the field. - Effective: The taxonomy describes what methods do but, absent empirical comparison, cannot guide method selection for a practitioner facing a real problem. - Scalable: Coverage through May 2026 will date quickly in a fast-moving area; the awesome list is the stated maintenance mechanism, but its update cadence is unknown. The paper delivers a curated reference list and taxonomy in paper form only; no software artifact, trained model, or dataset is released. Strengths: - The three-table taxonomy is the paper's most concrete deliverable: consistent 7–8 dimension comparison across 75+ named methods makes cross-method navigation tractable, a service no modality-specific survey provides. - The cross-modal framing explicitly connects tabular, time series, and graph challenges through shared principles (tokenization, pre-training objectives, adaptation), which is a genuine organizational novelty over prior single-modality surveys. - Coverage breadth spanning NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR/KDD/WWW 2023–2026 including very recent arXiv works indicates genuine contemporaneity at time of writing. Author-Acknowledged Limitations: The authors do not explicitly acknowledge any limitations of the survey itself. Section 7 identifies open research problems in the field (scaling laws unknown for structured data, no unified cross-modal FM, LLM integration underspecified) but these are presented as future directions rather than gaps in the survey's own coverage or methodology. The absence of self-critical reflection on selection bias, coverage gaps, or the lack of empirical comparison is a credibility concern. Analyst-Identified Gaps: - No quantitative meta-analysis: the survey never reports which approaches perform best on shared benchmarks (TabArena, TSFM-Bench, GFMBench), so a practitioner cannot use this paper to choose a method. - Likely selection bias toward graph methods: Table 3 contains ~2× the entries of Tables 1 and 2, and multiple entries are co-authored by survey authors without disclosure. - No paper selection methodology: the 150+ claim is unverifiable and search/exclusion criteria are absent. - Mixed confidence treatment: arXiv preprints and Nature papers are cited at equal standing without flagging differential confidence levels. - The "unified framework" framing is partly cosmetic: the five common principles in Section 3 are generic enough to describe any deep learning survey; the genuinely cross-modal insights are confined to a few paragraphs in Section 7. Bottom Line: The three-table taxonomy makes this a useful reference document for researchers entering the structured data foundation model space, but the analysis is descriptive rather than critical, graph coverage is disproportionate with an undisclosed conflict of interest, and the total absence of empirical comparison limits utility for anyone who needs to choose or compare methods.
Citations
#174 — Incremental (35)

LLMs as Knowledge Workers

ojs.aaai.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces a neurosymbolic knowledge acquisition method that uses Claude 4.5 as a "knowledge worker" to populate and extend OntoAgent's formal lexicon and ontology by combining LLM distributional reasoning with automatic semantic validation against ontological constraints. - Why it's important: Building the typed lexicons and ontologies required for trustworthy, inspectable, causally explainable AI agents has been prohibitively expensive (the "knowledge bottleneck"), and no prior approach has leveraged LLMs as structured acquisition tools operating over a richly constrained symbolic knowledge base. - How it does it: A symbolic semantic analyzer produces partial meaning representations (MRs) encoding precisely what is unknown and what selectional constraints the unknown item must satisfy, then passes this structured specification to an LLM that proposes lexicon entries or new ontological concepts, which are validated against formal ontological type constraints and inheritance rules. - The good: The full acquisition cascade—gap detection, five-strategy concept search, constraint-based validation, and new-concept creation—worked end-to-end on a short procedural text, with all 9 lexicon entries and 2 new ontological concepts passing automatic semantic validation. - The missing: The experiment covers 8 acquisition events from a single 70-word shipboard maintenance text; there is no accuracy measurement, no baseline comparison, no human evaluation of entry correctness, and the system depends on proprietary 30-year-old infrastructure that is not publicly available. - Read it or not: Researchers specifically working on neurosymbolic cognitive architectures or knowledge acquisition will find the architectural position and trigger mechanism design valuable, but anyone expecting quantitative evidence of efficacy will be disappointed by what is essentially a system design paper with a single illustrative example.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. The architectural inversion — using a symbolic analyzer's partial meaning representations as typed acquisition specs that constrain LLM output, then validating against ontological constraints — earns real credit on Novelty (50) and Non-Obviousness (55). But Rigor (18) and Impact (25, the dominant systems weight) collapse the composite: the evidence is a single 70-word text with 8 acquisition events, no accuracy metric, no baseline (e.g. direct prompting without OntoAgent), circular validation against the system's own ontology, and dependence on unreleased 30-year proprietary infrastructure that makes replication impossible. Completeness (50) is rescued by candid self-acknowledgment that this is only a viability test. Composite: 0.15*50+0.15*18+0.10*55+0.15*50+0.15*30+0.30*25 = 35.
systems Novelty 50 Rigor 18 Non-Obv 55 Completeness 50 Resilience 30 Impact 25
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Knowledge-based AI systems capable of transparent reasoning, causal explanation, and targeted error correction require richly structured formal resources: typed ontologies, lexicons mapping word senses to ontological concepts with selectional constraints, and meaning representations grounded in those concepts. Constructing these resources by hand has been prohibitively expensive; decades of knowledge engineering projects achieved only partial coverage before the field shifted to statistical ML. The resulting generative AI paradigm scales but is opaque: when an LLM hallucinates, there is no knowledge base or reasoning trace to inspect and correct. The specific gap this work addresses is whether LLMs, which encode distributional knowledge from large corpora but lack formal structure, can serve as knowledge acquisition subcontractors for symbolic systems—reducing the human labor cost of building those systems rather than replacing them. Key Idea The core insight is an architectural inversion: instead of using symbolic structures as post-hoc filters on an LLM core (the prevailing neurosymbolic pattern), make the symbolic analyzer the primary processor and the LLM a constrained hypothesis generator. The symbolic analyzer produces partial meaning representations (MRs) that encode exactly what is unknown and what formal constraints the unknown item must satisfy—unilateral selectional restrictions derived from surrounding analyzed context. The LLM receives not raw text but a structured acquisition task: "find or create an ontological concept such that this property role is satisfied, subject to these inherited constraints." This transforms LLM generation from unconstrained text production into constrained search within a formal hierarchy, enabling automatic semantic validation of the result. Methodology The pipeline has three stages. First, gap detection: OntoAgent's symbolic language analyzer processes text and produces partial MRs; when it encounters an unknown word/sense or an underspecified noun-noun compound (flagged by the generic RELATED-TO property), it triggers acquisition and passes the partial MR as context. Second, candidate generation: the LLM, guided by narrative algorithm descriptions, executes five sequential search strategies—(a) direct concept name lookup, (b) semantic field expansion via synonyms and hypernyms, (c) inverse lexical lookup in existing sem-struc zones, (d) distributional similarity over collocational patterns, (e) property-based matching against contextual expectations from the partial MR. A "good-enough principle" suppresses ontological proliferation: if an existing concept adequately supports required inferences, no new concept is created. Third, validation: proposed entries are checked against three formal conditions—the anchoring concept exists in the ontology; the concept's properties support required contextual inferences (selectional restriction compatibility); local constraints do not violate inherited ones. When candidate search fails entirely, ontology acquisition is triggered: a new concept is positioned in the hierarchy by constraint filtering, given properties derived from corpus MRs and dictionary definitions, and validated for non-circularity and property compatibility. Knowledge resources are: ~9,000 ontological concepts with typed property-facet-filler triples and inheritance, ~30,000 lexicon entries, and 1,780 validated MRs used as in-context examples. Three critical design decisions: (1) triggering from partial MRs rather than raw text is well-justified as it produces precise typed specifications; (2) using narrative algorithm descriptions as prompts rather than fine-tuning is motivated by inspectability and anticipated transfer to symbolic code, but is not ablated; (3) the good-enough principle for concept reuse is well-motivated but has no quantitative characterization of when it succeeds or fails. Key Results The system was tested on a single 70-word procedural text about shipboard fuse replacement. The semantic analyzer flagged 6 unknown words (amperage, bin, fuse, holder, rating, retrieve) and 2 missing word senses (store-n2, pull-v7). Results: 3 entries mapped directly to existing concepts; 4 entries mapped to existing concepts with local constraints; 2 entries required new ontological concept creation (AMPERAGE, POWER-PANEL); 1 nominal compound entry acquired. All 9 lexicon entries and 2 new ontological concepts passed automatic constraint validation. No accuracy metrics are reported against a gold standard. No baselines are compared. No human evaluation of entry correctness is presented. The paper explicitly characterizes this as an "initial viability test" not a quantitative evaluation. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW SYSTEM/TOOL. The genuinely novel element is the specific integration mechanism: using runtime partial MRs from a symbolic analyzer as typed acquisition specifications that constrain LLM output, combined with semantic (ontological constraint) validation rather than task-completion or syntactic execution validation. The five candidate search strategies and the good-enough principle are explicitly drawn from decades of prior knowledge acquisition research (not novel in themselves), instantiated in a new configuration. The architectural position (symbolic-primary, neural-supporting) is not new as a stance, but the specific trigger mechanism and validation approach are meaningfully differentiated from comparable SOAR/ACT-R + LLM integrations. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations are present. No error bars, multiple runs, or significance tests—the experiment is a single text with 8 acquisition events. No code, data, or model weights are released; OntoAgent's resources were built over three decades and are not publicly available, making replication impossible. The paper is published at AAAI Spring Symposium 2026, which typically carries lighter peer review than main-track AAAI. Methodological concerns: correctness is validated only by automatic constraint checking against the system's own ontology—there is no independent ground truth, so the validation is circular (Goodhart's Law); the worked examples may be cherry-picked; the false-positive and false-negative rates of constraint checking are completely uncharacterized; and there is no comparison against a simpler baseline (e.g., direct LLM prompting without OntoAgent scaffolding) to isolate the contribution of the symbolic infrastructure. Deployability Profile: SYSTEMS (a cognitive architecture subsystem evaluated by a worked example). - Practical: Cannot run on commercially available hardware in any replicable form—the system depends on OntoAgent's proprietary ~9,000-concept ontology, ~30,000-word lexicon, and 1,780 validated MRs accumulated over 30 years, none of which are publicly available. - Efficient: Compute cost is not characterized; the number of LLM calls per acquisition event, latency, and token cost are not reported. - Effective: The 8-event worked example shows coherent outputs that pass constraint validation, but correctness is evaluated only against the system's own ontology—no independent measure of semantic accuracy exists. - Scalable: Scaling beyond 1 text and 8 words is not tested; the analogy to GPUs enabling neural scaling is an aspiration stated in the conclusion, not a supported experimental claim. The paper delivers no software artifact, no trained model, no dataset, and no public code; it stops at paper form with internal illustrative examples. Strengths - The trigger mechanism—gap detection from partial MRs encoding typed selectional restrictions—produces a structurally precise specification of what the LLM must learn, substantially more informative than raw text prompting and enabling validation that would otherwise be impossible. - Semantic validation against formal ontological constraints (type hierarchy, property inheritance, constraint compatibility) catches a genuine class of errors—semantically incoherent entries that are syntactically valid—that task-completion and execution-only validation miss, as illustrated by the POWER-PANEL example. - The modular separation of inherently distributional steps (distributional similarity, synonym expansion) from inherently symbolic steps (constraint checking, inheritance computation) is a principled design that supports progressive migration from LLM to deterministic symbolic execution as the system matures. - The architectural distinction from prevailing neurosymbolic work (LLM as knowledge subcontractor, not as engine) is clearly articulated and meaningfully supported by comparison to SOAR/ACT-R integrations and CoALA. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors directly and honestly acknowledge: (1) no quantitative evaluation at scale—the experiment is explicitly a viability test; (2) no benchmarks exist and constructing them is future work; (3) only a narrow concrete-vocabulary domain (shipboard maintenance) was tested, with abstract concepts, metaphorical language, and knowledge-sparse bootstrapping scenarios untested; (4) the substantial infrastructure investment required may be a practical barrier for other researchers. These are confronted with notable candor rather than minimized. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Circular validation: All correctness assessment is performed against OntoAgent's own ontology; whether proposed entries correctly represent word meaning is never independently verified by human linguistic experts, making it impossible to distinguish "entry passes formal constraints" from "entry is semantically correct." - Insufficient experimental scale: 8 acquisition events from one 70-word text cannot support any statistical characterization of accuracy, coverage, or failure modes; even 50 texts with gold-standard entries and inter-annotator agreement would be a minimum credible evaluation. - No error analysis: The paper presents only successful acquisitions; whether the system produces incorrect candidates that pass validation, or correct candidates that fail, and at what rates, is entirely unknown. - Proprietary infrastructure as barrier: The dependence on a 30-year proprietary knowledge base is understated as a limitation—it makes independent replication impossible and renders the viability claim conditional on resources that no other research group can access. - LLM prompt content not shown: The narrative algorithm descriptions that guide the LLM are described but not reproduced; whether acquisition quality is sensitive to prompt formulation is not investigated. - No ablation of symbolic scaffolding contribution: No comparison against prompting the LLM directly (without OntoAgent context) quantifies what the symbolic infrastructure actually contributes. Bottom Line A practitioner in neurosymbolic AI will find a well-reasoned architectural position and a genuinely interesting trigger-and-validate design, but the single-text viability demonstration, absence of quantitative evaluation, and dependence on unreleased proprietary infrastructure make this a credible research direction proposal rather than a demonstrated advance.
Search Results
#175 — Incremental (34)

Multivariate Financial Forecasting using the Chronos Time Series Foundation Models

Sanjiv R Das, Tarang Goyal, Mohini Yadav · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper evaluates whether multivariate (MV) inputs to Chronos-2, an open-source zero-shot time-series foundation model, improve forecast accuracy over univariate (UV) baselines for Magnificent-7 equities and U.S. Treasury rates across rolling monthly evaluations from 2000–2025. - Why it's important: Financial practitioners face an unresolved question about when adding cross-series context introduces signal versus noise — the answer directly determines whether foundation models offer practical gains over per-series approaches already in use. - How it does it: Chronos-2's group attention mechanism shares information across co-evolving series at inference time without fine-tuning; the paper isolates the MV vs. UV effect by holding the model constant and varying only input scope across two within-domain panels and one cross-domain combined panel. - The good: MV outperforms UV on every one of the 17 individual series, with rates MAPE dropping from 0.1233 (UV) to 0.0497 (MV) and RMSE from 0.1865 to 0.0418 — a 60% and 78% reduction respectively. - The missing: No external baselines (ARIMA, VAR, random walk, Nelson-Siegel) are reported, so the paper cannot establish whether Chronos-2 MV beats the simpler tools practitioners already use — a critical omission given that co-author Das's own prior work (Singh et al., 2025) showed classical econometric models outperform many ML approaches on yield curves. - Read it or not: Worth a skim for practitioners curious about conditions under which Chronos-2's multivariate mode helps, but too methodologically thin to serve as a deployment recommendation.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. Resilience (30) and Rigor (35) are the decisive weaknesses: the central practitioner question — whether Chronos-2 beats simpler tools (ARIMA, VAR, Nelson-Siegel, random walk) — is unanswerable because no external baseline is reported, there are no significance tests or error bars, and the leakage check is logically weak. Novelty (35) is low as a NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING application study of an existing model. The clean rolling protocol, code release, and the genuine cross-domain-degradation negative result keep it above the routine floor, but methodological thinness limits it to incremental.
empirical Novelty 35 Rigor 35 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 35 Resilience 30 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Financial time-series forecasting is contested ground for machine learning. Classical econometric baselines (ARIMA, factor models, no-arbitrage yield-curve approaches) regularly match or outperform deep-learning methods across long evaluation windows, especially for U.S. Treasury yields. The unresolved practitioner question is: under what conditions do multivariate models reliably add value over simpler per-series forecasts? Adding correlated series can inject signal from shared dynamics, or it can inject noise if the context is misaligned — the signal-to-noise trade-off is the central empirical question. The specific gap targeted is characterizing this trade-off for a modern foundation model (Chronos-2) across two economically meaningful domains: large-cap equities and the U.S. Treasury yield curve. Key Idea The core insight is that Chronos-2's group attention mechanism can exploit within-domain covariance structure — e.g., yield curve co-movements across maturities — to reduce forecast error at inference time, without retraining, but that feeding context from a qualitatively different asset class (equities mixed with rates) introduces noise that degrades rather than improves accuracy. Domain homogeneity of the multivariate context is the operative condition for gains. Methodology The study is a pure empirical evaluation using Chronos-2 (Ansari et al., 2025) in zero-shot mode — no fine-tuning is performed. Three panels are evaluated: Magnificent-7 stocks (K=7: AAPL, AMZN, GOOGL, MSFT, NFLX, NVDA, TSLA), U.S. Treasury rates for ten maturities DGS3MO through DGS30 (K=10), and a combined panel (K=17). Input window lengths: n ∈ {126, 252, 504, 756} trading days (0.5–3 years); forecast horizons: m ∈ {21, 63} trading days (1–3 months). Rolling monthly re-estimation over 2000–2025 with predictions starting three years after dataset start. Error metrics are MAPE and RMSE, aggregated across window/horizon combinations and reported per-series. Chronos-2's architecture uses time attention (with RoPE) for within-series temporal structure and group attention for cross-series information sharing; it produces probabilistic forecasts across 21 quantiles (0.01–0.99) via quantile regression. Three critical design decisions: (1) holding the model constant isolates the MV vs. UV effect cleanly; (2) a pre/post-2023 accuracy split is used as an informal leakage check; (3) the combined panel test uses a different time window (July 2010–December 2025) than the within-class tests (2000–2025), which complicates direct comparison. Key Results Rates MV MAPE: 0.0497 vs. UV 0.1233 (60% lower); Rates MV RMSE: 0.0418 vs. UV 0.1865 (78% lower). Stocks MV MAPE: 0.0706 vs. UV 0.0844 (16% lower); Stocks MV RMSE: 3.9619 vs. UV 5.0395 (21% lower). MV outperforms UV on all 17 individual series without exception. Forecast horizon matters — 63-day errors exceed 21-day errors as expected — but input window length (n ranging 0.5–3 years) has negligible impact on relative accuracy. The combined stocks+rates panel shows MV still beats UV per series, but cross-domain MV performs slightly worse than within-domain MV for most series (e.g., DGS3MO MAPE under combined MV: 0.2392 vs. within-rates MV: 0.2304). Post-2023 forecasts are more accurate than pre-2023 (especially for rates), which the authors interpret as evidence against leakage. No external baselines are reported at any point. Novelty Classification NEW EMPIRICAL FINDING. The contribution is documenting the conditions — within-domain homogeneity — under which Chronos-2's group attention produces reliable MV gains in financial forecasting. The Chronos-2 model itself, the rolling evaluation protocol, and the error metrics are all existing tools applied to a new domain question. No new method, dataset, or formal result is introduced; the paper is an application study. Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations are performed — the study cannot decompose how much of the MV gain comes from the group attention architecture versus from the specific panel compositions. No statistical significance tests are reported; all comparisons are point estimates of means with standard deviations, leaving the reader unable to assess whether differences are reliable across rolling windows. No error bars on per-series estimates. Code and data are publicly released (GitHub: https://github.com/srdas/timeseries-fm/tree/main), which enables replication. The leakage check is informal and logically weak: the claim that better post-2023 accuracy disproves leakage conflates absence of memorization with absence of distributional correlation, and lower post-2023 error could equally reflect a lower-volatility rate regime. The paper is a preprint (arXiv:2605.21504v1, May 2026); peer review has not been applied. Appendix A discloses that GPT-5.2 (via OpenAI Prism) drafted the Results, Introduction, and Concluding Discussion sections, and Gemini drafted the Methodology — a notable transparency disclosure that nonetheless raises concerns about analytic depth in those sections. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL - Practical: Chronos-2 is open-source and the paper reports no unusual compute requirements; standard hardware suffices for the panel sizes tested. - Efficient: Not stated — no inference time, memory footprint, or throughput comparisons between MV and UV modes are reported. - Effective: MV Chronos-2 consistently outperforms UV Chronos-2 within domain, but effectiveness relative to the practitioners' existing tools (VAR, ARIMA, Nelson-Siegel, random walk) is entirely undemonstrated — the comparison that would matter most is absent. - Scalable: Tested on K=7, K=10, and K=17 series over 25-year rolling windows; behavior at larger panel sizes, daily frequency, or non-U.S. markets is untested and not claimed. The paper delivers a public code repository and reproducible evaluation artifacts. Strengths - Rigorous rolling evaluation protocol (monthly re-estimation, 2000–2025) avoids look-ahead bias that plagues many financial backtest studies. - Isolating the MV vs. UV comparison within a single fixed model is a clean design choice that produces an internally valid causal attribution. - The cross-domain degradation result (combined stocks+rates MV underperforms within-domain MV) is a genuine actionable negative finding that would not be obvious a priori and has direct practical implications for context construction. - Full code release enables reproduction and extension. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors acknowledge: (1) single foundation model evaluated; (2) fixed window and horizon choices, with higher-frequency data or different rolling cadences untested; (3) no tail risk, directional accuracy, or economic value metrics reported. These are stated directly and concisely, though the most consequential gap — the absence of external baselines — is not acknowledged. Analyst-Identified Gaps - Most serious: No comparison to any external baseline (random walk, ARIMA, VAR, Nelson-Siegel, or the classical methods from Singh et al. 2025 — co-authored by Das). Demonstrating MV > UV within Chronos-2 says nothing about whether Chronos-2 MV is useful relative to simpler approaches; this makes the practical takeaway unsubstantiated. - No statistical significance testing anywhere: all improvements are reported as arithmetic differences of means with no inference on whether MV gains are robust across the rolling window distribution. - The leakage argument is logically insufficient: the post-2023 accuracy improvement could reflect a lower-volatility rate environment (rates converged and stabilized post-2023 hiking cycle) rather than absence of training-data overlap. - The combined panel uses July 2010–December 2025, while within-class tests use 2000–2025; this different time window confounds the cross-domain vs. within-domain comparison. - Only 2 forecast horizons and 4 window lengths are tested; no stress-period sub-analysis (2008 financial crisis, 2020 COVID shock) is reported, leaving tail-regime behavior unknown. - The AI-written prose (per Appendix A) for Results, Introduction, and Conclusions warrants extra scrutiny of interpretive claims — the GPT-generated write-up may overstate findings or smooth over nuance. Bottom Line This paper cleanly demonstrates that within Chronos-2, domain-homogeneous multivariate context reduces forecast error substantially for Treasury rates and modestly for equities, but the absence of any external baseline leaves the central practitioner question — whether to replace existing tools — entirely unanswered.
Search Results 2605.21504 PDF
#176 — Incremental (33)

Semantic architectures for domain-specific programming AI agents: lessons from a JavaScript semantic assistant

nature.com · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces an end-to-end pipeline that fine-tunes LLaMA-3B-Instruct on a ~1,500 Q&A dataset derived from a hand-crafted JavaScript OWL/RDF ontology using BitFit (updating 0.1% of parameters), augmented by a Pellet OWL reasoner at inference time that flags ontology-constraint violations and prompts the model to revise its output. - Why it's important: Generic LLMs hallucinate on domain-specific programming tasks, producing syntactically valid but semantically incorrect JavaScript; a domain-grounded assistant that understands scoping rules, async patterns, and error semantics could reduce developer debugging burden. - How it does it: SPARQL queries extract class hierarchies, property relations, and axioms from the OWL ontology and mechanically convert them into natural-language Q&A pairs, which are then used to fine-tune a 3B-parameter instruction-tuned LLM; at inference, the Pellet reasoner parses model output, identifies constraint violations, and triggers a revision pass. - The good: The proposed system achieves 92% pass rate on ~100 author-constructed Jest unit tests versus 70% for the untuned baseline, with 95% confidence intervals and paired t-tests (p < 0.01) reported across four metrics. - The missing: Every evaluation benchmark — the ~100 coding tasks, ~50 bug snippets, ~30 adaptability scenarios, and all extended code-quality metrics — is entirely self-constructed by the authors; no standard code benchmark (HumanEval, MBPP, SWE-bench) is used, making independent verification of the claimed gains impossible. - Read it or not: Researchers interested in ontology-augmented PEFT pipelines may find the architecture template useful, but the absence of standardized benchmarks and the sole comparison against a weak, untuned 3B baseline severely limits trust in the headline numbers; read the related work section but not the results section uncritically.
Why this rank: Scored using the Empirical profile. Rigor (25) and Resilience (25) collapse the composite: every benchmark is small and author-constructed with no standard comparison (no HumanEval/MBPP), the logical-consistency metric is circular (Pellet-checked against the same training ontology — Goodhart's Law), the sole baseline is the same untuned LLaMA-3B, and the extended quality metrics (security, runtime, memory) are reported with no methodology and 'appear manufactured.' Novelty (40) is limited to composing established components (BitFit/LoRA, OWL reasoning), and the headline gains would not survive adversarial review. The released Zenodo artifacts lift Impact (45) slightly, but the structural evaluation flaws place this firmly in the Incremental band.
empirical Novelty 40 Rigor 25 Non-Obv 38 Completeness 35 Resilience 25 Impact 45
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation General-purpose LLMs produce JavaScript code that is syntactically correct but semantically wrong — violating scoping rules, mishandling async/await patterns, or failing to propagate errors — because they lack an explicit representation of the language's formal constraints. Developers using LLM-based coding assistants must manually verify semantic correctness that goes beyond syntax, a cognitive burden that grows with code complexity. Prior approaches split into: (a) large-scale pretraining on code corpora (CodeLlama, Codex), which embeds statistical patterns but no formal semantics, and (b) retrieval-augmented generation, which appends relevant context at inference but does not internalize domain rules. Neither approach encodes JavaScript-specific constraints such as lexical closure scope, async function return types, or prototype inheritance rules as enforceable axioms. Key Idea The core insight is to invert the typical relationship between LLMs and ontologies: rather than using an LLM to help build an ontology, use a formal OWL ontology as the authoritative source for generating a fine-tuning corpus, then enforce that ontology's constraints at inference via a description-logic reasoner. The "trick" is that SPARQL queries over the OWL graph mechanically produce semantically grounded Q&A pairs that encode every class relationship, property domain/range, and cardinality constraint — converting formal axioms into natural-language training signal without human annotation at scale. Methodology The pipeline has four stages. (1) Ontology construction: a JavaScript OWL/RDF ontology is built in Protégé with ~130 classes, 55 object properties, 30 data properties, and ~300 axioms encoding scoping rules, async patterns, error types, and prototype hierarchies; class hierarchy averages 3–4 levels deep (e.g., ForLoop → Loop → ControlStructure). GPT-4 API suggestions are used to propose additional relations, which a JavaScript expert then validates; Pellet enforces consistency. (2) Dataset generation: SPARQL queries extract subclass relations, property domains/ranges, and axiom constraints and map them to Q&A templates ("What is X in JavaScript?", "Does every AsyncFunction return a Promise?"), producing ~1,500 Q&A pairs stored as JSON; at least one pair per class or property. (3) Model fine-tuning: LLaMA-3B-Instruct is fine-tuned on this corpus using BitFit (only bias terms, ~3M parameters) as the primary method, with LoRA (rank 4, ~8M parameters) and standard full fine-tuning for comparison; training uses lr=1e-5, batch=16, 3–5 epochs, 80/10/10 train/val/test split; multi-task prompts mix code generation, debugging, and conceptual Q&A; contrastive learning pairs correct ontology-based answers with plausible-but-incorrect distractors; incremental layer unfreezing mitigates catastrophic forgetting. (4) Inference reasoning: the Pellet OWL reasoner parses model-generated code into a semantic form, checks against ontology constraints, and prompts revision when violations are detected; the reasoner intervenes in approximately 30% of execution paths. The objective is supervised next-token prediction on the Q&A corpus with no explicit formal loss stated for the contrastive component. Three critical design decisions — using BitFit over full fine-tuning (justified by resource efficiency), deploying the reasoner only at inference not training (justified as pre-training phase), and generating Q&A from SPARQL rather than manual annotation (justified for coverage) — are plausible but incompletely ablated. Key Results On four author-constructed metrics, the ontology-augmented model (BitFit) outperforms the untuned LLaMA-3B baseline: code correctness 92% ±2% vs. 70% ±3% (100 Jest-tested tasks); logical consistency 87% ±3% vs. 65%; error detection F1 0.88 (precision 0.90, recall 0.86) vs. 0.60 (50 buggy snippets); adaptability 82% vs. 55% (30 novel scenarios). LoRA achieves ~90% precision (marginally below BitFit). Disabling the inference reasoner drops correctness and consistency approximately 4 points. A comparison to CodeLlama-7B is limited to code precision only (~78%), without reporting other metrics. The paper also reports extended quality metrics (cyclomatic complexity 7.2 vs. 12.8, maintainability index 84.5 vs. 61.3, code duplication 4.1% vs. 15.7%, security vulnerability score 1.2 vs. 8.9, runtime 125ms vs. 210ms, memory 48MB vs. 85MB) with no description of the tool, codebase, or methodology used to obtain them; these numbers are not independently verifiable and appear implausibly clean. All primary improvements are statistically significant by paired t-test (p < 0.01) over 3 random seeds. The baseline throughout is the same untuned LLaMA-3B-Instruct — a weak comparator for a 2026 paper. Novelty Classification NEW METHOD + NEW DATASET/BENCHMARK (self-constructed). The genuine contribution is the end-to-end pipeline combining OWL ontology construction → SPARQL-driven Q&A generation → PEFT fine-tuning → OWL reasoner inference validation, applied to programming assistance. Individual components — PEFT with BitFit/LoRA, ontology-augmented RAG, OWL reasoning with Pellet — are established. The novel element is their composition into a code-generation pipeline and the "domain-specific IQ" evaluation framing. This is a competent application of known techniques to a new setting rather than a new method in the algorithmic sense. Rigor & Reproducibility Ablations are present but narrow: the reasoner on/off comparison shows a ~4-point drop; fine-tuning method comparisons (BitFit vs. LoRA vs. full) show marginal LoRA benefit. Error bars (95% CI) and paired t-tests over 3 seeds are reported. Code, the OWL file, SPARQL queries, and model weights are publicly available on Zenodo (https://zenodo.org/records/17284586). However, three serious methodological concerns undermine confidence in the results: (1) every benchmark is author-constructed — the ~100 coding tasks, ~50 bug snippets, and ~30 adaptability tasks are not publicly standardized, making replication dependent on releasing the exact test suite (which is not explicitly stated as released); (2) the logical consistency metric uses automated Pellet checks against the same ontology used in training, creating circular validation — a model trained to satisfy these axioms is evaluated by whether it satisfies these axioms; (3) the expanded code quality metrics (security, runtime, memory) are reported with no methodology description whatsoever. The paper is in press and unedited, which may explain some of these gaps, but the concerns are structural. Deployability (4-property check) Profile: EMPIRICAL. - Practical: Training requires a single 8-GPU machine and completes in ~10 hours with BitFit; inference adds Pellet reasoner overhead; commercially feasible at this scale. - Efficient: BitFit updates only ~3M of ~3B parameters; LoRA adds ~8M; both are lightweight relative to full fine-tuning; the Pellet reasoner adds latency on the ~30% of outputs that trigger revision, but overhead is not quantified. - Effective: The system improves over the untuned LLaMA-3B baseline on four self-constructed metrics, but comparison to CodeLlama-7B covers only one metric and shows a 14-point gap (92% vs. 78%) that is not contextualized against model size differences (3B vs. 7B parameters); no comparison to instruction-tuned code models at parity scale. - Scalable: Not tested beyond 3B parameters; the authors explicitly acknowledge that Pellet does not scale to large ontologies; 1,500 Q&A pairs is a tiny corpus; behavior on industrial-scale JavaScript codebases is untested. The paper delivers an open-source artifact (ontology OWL file, SPARQL scripts, fine-tuning code, model weights) on Zenodo. Strengths - The ontology-to-SPARQL-to-Q&A pipeline is a concrete and reproducible recipe for injecting symbolic domain knowledge into LLM fine-tuning, and the full artifact release (OWL, queries, code, weights) enables follow-on work. - The multi-faceted evaluation framework (correctness, semantic consistency, error detection, generalization) is more informative than single-metric code benchmarks, even if the specific instantiation is weak. - The ablation isolating the reasoner contribution (4-point drop without reasoner) is direct evidence that the inference-time symbolic layer adds value beyond fine-tuning alone. - The comparison of BitFit, LoRA, and full fine-tuning on the same task provides a practically useful data point for the PEFT community, showing BitFit is competitive at this scale. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify: (1) ontology construction is time-consuming and requires domain expertise and iterative refinement; (2) the JavaScript ontology struggles to capture all evolving idioms (e.g., TypeScript extensions); (3) Pellet does not scale to large ontologies; (4) the approach does not handle entirely new programming paradigms without ontology updates; (5) only 18% of adaptability cases fail, typically involving entirely novel paradigms. The authors frame these honestly as future work directions rather than minimizing them — a credibility positive. Analyst-Identified Gaps - No standard benchmark: The complete absence of HumanEval, MBPP, or any other established code generation benchmark is the single most damaging omission. A 3B model fine-tuned for 10 hours on 1,500 Q&A pairs achieving 92% on author-constructed tests is not comparable to any published result in the literature. - Circular logical consistency: Evaluating semantic correctness using the Pellet reasoner against the same ontology used to generate training data is Goodhart's Law — the model optimizes for what the ontology says is correct, and correctness is measured by what the ontology says is correct. An independent JavaScript static analysis tool (ESLint, TypeScript compiler) would provide non-circular evaluation. - Weak baseline: The primary comparison is against the same untuned model (LLaMA-3B-Instruct). A fine-tuned baseline on a general JavaScript Q&A corpus of comparable size to the ontology-derived corpus is mentioned but the results for it are only partially reported (80% precision on one metric). Comparing to Phi-3-mini or a similarly sized instruction-tuned code model would be far more informative. - Extended quality metrics are unverifiable: Cyclomatic complexity, maintainability index, runtime, memory, and security scores appear in Table 4 with no description of the tool used, the codebase measured, or the sampling methodology. These numbers look manufactured. - Small test sets: 100 tasks for correctness, 50 bugs for error detection, and 30 scenarios for adaptability are insufficient to estimate population performance with the precision implied by ±2–3% confidence intervals across only 3 seeds. - The paper inconsistently reports the dataset size as "~1,500 Q&A pairs" (abstract, Section 1) versus "thousands of QA pairings" (Section 3.2) — a factual inconsistency that was not caught in peer review. Bottom Line The ontology-to-fine-tuning pipeline is a reproducible and interesting architectural pattern with publicly released artifacts, but the claimed performance gains are evaluated entirely on self-constructed, small-scale benchmarks with circular validation, making the headline numbers untrustworthy for anyone deciding whether to adopt this approach over a stronger code-specialized baseline.
Search Results
#177 — Incremental (31)

A Review of Structure Learning in Markov Logic Network

researchsquare.com · neural symbolic - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper presents a systematic survey of 63 Markov Logic Network structure learning (MLN-SL) algorithms spanning 1997–2025, organized by a proposed three-dimensional analytical framework: optimization objectives, constraint forms, and solution strategies. - Why it's important: The MLN-SL research landscape is highly fragmented — no prior unified framework for comparing algorithms by optimization paradigm exists, making it difficult for researchers to evaluate trade-offs between scalability and theoretical guarantees. - How it does it: A PRISMA-guided literature search (573 → 63 papers) classifies algorithms into nine temporal/conceptual categories, each receiving pseudocode, an evolution narrative, a performance summary table (AUC/CLL/time), and a complexity/applicability/bottleneck table. - The good: The curated benchmark dataset index (33 datasets with URLs, scale statistics, and typical uses) is the most practically reusable artifact the paper produces. - The missing: No new experiments are run; reported performance numbers across tables are drawn from heterogeneous hardware and dataset conditions and are explicitly acknowledged as non-comparable, yet no normalization or meta-analytic synthesis is attempted. - Read it or not: Worth reading as a structured entry point for researchers new to MLN-SL who need a map of the algorithm families; established practitioners will find the per-table bottleneck summaries useful but the analysis too shallow to drive research decisions.
Why this rank: Scored using Empirical profile. The composite sits in the incremental band, driven down by Rigor (25) and Completeness (28): a survey with no new experiments, performance tables explicitly non-comparable yet presented without any meta-analytic normalization, an informal/heuristic complexity framework, a garbled table (Table 8), and broken abstract promises (no implementation index despite the claim, only two of three advertised challenges delivered). Resilience (28) is low because the 'first comprehensive 3D-perspective review' claim is unsubstantiated against prior partial surveys. Novelty (40) credits a coherent organizing taxonomy, but it is never formalized into a scoring rubric or used to generate testable predictions. Impact (35) reflects the one genuinely reusable artifact — the curated 33-dataset index. Weighted: 0.20*40 + 0.25*25 + 0.15*35 + 0.15*28 + 0.15*28 + 0.10*35 = 31.
empirical Novelty 40 Rigor 25 Non-Obv 35 Completeness 28 Resilience 28 Impact 35
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Markov Logic Networks (MLNs) unify first-order logic with probabilistic graphical models by assigning real-valued weights to logical clauses, defining a Gibbs distribution over possible worlds. Structure learning — automatically discovering the set of weighted clauses from data — is harder and less mature than weight learning or inference. The field has produced a large number of algorithmically heterogeneous methods since 1997, but no unified comparison framework exists that cuts across objective function type, constraint handling, and search strategy. Researchers working on new algorithms cannot systematically position their work or understand which design axes prior methods have explored. This review targets that gap. Key Idea The paper's organizing insight is that any MLN-SL algorithm can be characterized along three axes simultaneously: (1) what objective it optimizes (joint likelihood, conditional likelihood, pseudo-likelihood, etc.), (2) what form the constraints take (hard logical clauses, soft weighted rules, lifted representations, neural embeddings), and (3) what computational strategy it uses to search clause space (greedy beam search, random walk on partial-order graphs, functional gradient boosting, online ILP, federated learning). This "3D lens" is the paper's primary conceptual contribution; it is not itself formalized or operationalized into a scoring rubric, but serves as an organizing schema for narrative comparison. Methodology The review follows PRISMA guidelines. A multi-database search yielded 573 papers; after filtering on criteria including focus on algorithm cores, presence of key optimization steps, and completeness of method descriptions, 63 papers were retained. These are classified into nine families presented chronologically: generative structure learning (1997–), discriminative structure learning (2005–), transfer learning (2007–), directed relational models (2011–), random walk methods (2010–), online learning (2011–), logistic regression-based methods (2010–), lifted structure learning (2014–), and deep learning-based methods (2017–). Each family receives pseudocode (abstracting shared implementation patterns), an algorithm evolution narrative citing individual papers, and summary tables reporting time, AUC, and CLL as collected from original papers. Section 4 then applies a unified symbolic complexity framework (parameterized by N, C, d, T, E, K) to each algorithm category and collects per-category applicability and bottleneck characterizations into tables. Two open challenges are discussed: limitations of local dependency assumptions in high-dimensional sparse data, and reusability of domain-specific knowledge across domains. Key Results No new experimental results are produced. Reported numbers are aggregated from 63 source papers under heterogeneous conditions — the paper explicitly notes these "lack precise comparability." Representative reported figures: generative methods take 4–1597 minutes on UW-CSE/Cora with AUC 0.22–0.87; discriminative methods achieve AUC up to 0.98 on IMDB (DMSP, [21]) in 24–2265 minutes; random walk GNS [40] reaches AUC 0.85–0.96 on UW-CSE/WebKB in 3.4–3.9 minutes; deep learning NMLNs [60] achieve AUC 0.97–0.99 on Nations/Kinship/UMLS; online OSL [44] achieves AUC 0.96 on CiteSeer in ~103 minutes. Table 8 (lifted structure learning) appears to have a rendering/layout error — columns are misaligned and some entries are truncated or garbled. The paper collates 33 publicly available benchmark datasets with URLs and scale statistics. Novelty Classification NEW FORMULATION (the three-dimensional taxonomy of MLN-SL optimization paradigms). The survey itself is a genuine intellectual synthesis — 63 papers organized coherently — but the analytical framework is not formalized, validated, or used to generate testable predictions. The paper claims to be "the first comprehensive review" to apply this optimization-centric perspective to MLN-SL; this claim is plausible but not independently verified against prior partial surveys (e.g., the SRL textbook literature). Rigor & Reproducibility No ablations are possible (survey paper). No error bars or significance tests — they would require new experiments. No code or reproducibility artifact is released despite the abstract promising "reproducible research resources"; the deliverable is a dataset URL table, not an implementation index. The complexity framework is informal and heuristic: expressions like O(T·N·C) are stated without derivation from algorithm analysis. A methodological concern: the paper claims the abstract's "three key open challenges" but Section 6 presents only two challenges, with bullet-point descriptions rather than deep analysis. The reported performance numbers cannot support cross-algorithm ranking because hardware, datasets, evaluation protocols, and years differ throughout. This is an unreviewed preprint (Research Square, posted May 2026). Deployability Profile: EMPIRICAL (survey of empirical methods). The paper's own contribution is a taxonomy, dataset index, and challenges synthesis — it stops at paper form with no software artifact, trained model, or reusable proof technique. Practical: the survey itself requires no hardware to use. Efficient: trivially so as a document. Effective: the framework helps situate algorithms conceptually but does not support quantitative algorithm selection. Scalable: the taxonomy is extensible to future methods without modification. Strengths - The 3D framework (optimization objective × constraint form × solution strategy) cuts usefully across heterogeneous literature and gives researchers a positioning tool that prior surveys lack. - The curated dataset index (Tables 19–21) with URLs, scale statistics (predicates, constants, atoms), and typical use cases is immediately reusable by practitioners. - The per-category complexity and bottleneck tables (Tables 10–18) compress a large body of scattered findings into scannable reference material. - Coverage is broad (63 papers, 1997–2025, 9 algorithm families) and the evolutionary narratives within each family accurately trace algorithm lineage. Author-Acknowledged Limitations The authors explicitly identify: (1) computational inefficiency on large-scale problems; (2) poor robustness to noise; (3) weak generalization across domains; (4) reliance on local dependency assumptions that miss global patterns; (5) inability to reuse domain-specific knowledge across domains. These are framed as future directions rather than limitations of the survey itself. The authors also acknowledge that reported runtime figures "lack precise comparability" across different experimental conditions. Analyst-Identified Gaps - The claim of being the "first" 3D-optimization-perspective review of MLN-SL is made without citing and ruling out prior partial surveys; the novelty claim is unsubstantiated. - The performance tables cannot support any comparative conclusion, yet the paper presents them without a meta-analytic synthesis (effect sizes, dataset-normalized rankings, or even explicit warnings against cross-row comparisons). - Table 8 (Lifted Structure Learning) contains apparent rendering errors — column headers and data appear misaligned — making its reported numbers unreliable as presented. - The abstract promises "three key open challenges" but Section 6 delivers only two (with a third apparently dropped). Section numbering is also inconsistent (Section 7 is labeled in the introduction's roadmap where Section 4 appears in the main text). - The deep learning category (Section 3.9) conflates architecturally dissimilar approaches (RelNN, NMLNs, federated MLN, cumulative learning, neuro-symbolic imitation learning) under a single workflow and pseudocode, obscuring meaningful distinctions. - No survey of available open-source MLN implementations is provided, despite the abstract's promise — only dataset URLs are compiled. - The challenges section offers no quantitative evidence for the severity of the identified problems, weakening their prioritization for future research. Bottom Line This preprint provides a competent taxonomic map of MLN structure learning for newcomers, but the non-comparable performance aggregation, thin challenges analysis, garbled table rendering, and absence of any implementation index or cross-algorithm empirical synthesis limit its value to researchers who already have working knowledge of the field.
Search Results
#178 — Incremental (28)

Ontology -based decision support for young agripreneurs in organic agriculture using Semantic Web Rule Language

library.acadlore.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
TL;DR - What it does: This paper introduces an OWL ontology (127 classes) paired with five SWRL rule sets that automatically recommend organic certification standards, certifying agencies, regulatory conditions, certification services, and supporting documents to young farmers navigating Thai, EU, and USDA certification pathways. - Why it's important: Young organic agripreneurs face fragmented, knowledge-intensive certification requirements across multiple competing standards and agencies, with no integrated, explainable decision tool that links product type and target market all the way through to required paperwork. - How it does it: Organic certification knowledge is encoded as explicit IF–THEN Horn-logic rules in SWRL, executed by the Pellet reasoner inside Protégé, with results retrieved via SPARQL queries against the post-inference ontology — no statistical model is involved. - The good: The system achieves 100% precision (zero false positives) across all 66 case-based test scenarios and 93.03% average recall, with every recommendation traceable to an explicit rule. - The missing: The entire evaluation was conducted by the same researchers who designed the rules and defined the expected outcomes — the 100% precision result is expected by construction and carries no independent evidential weight. - Read it or not: Worth reading for knowledge engineers targeting regulatory compliance domains where explainability is mandatory; practitioners expecting empirical validation or a deployable tool will find the evidence base too thin.
Why this rank: Scored using Systems profile. Rigor (20) and Resilience (25) pull the composite down hard: the evaluation is self-referential — the same team wrote the SWRL rules, defined the expected outcomes from the same certification documents, and ran the SPARQL queries, so the 100% precision is structurally guaranteed and carries no evidential weight. Non-Obviousness (20) is low — the {product,market}→standard→agency→document chain is a deterministic lookup, and the OWL+SWRL+Pellet+SPARQL stack is standard. Impact (25, the dominant systems weight) reflects an unreleased prototype with no end-user interface. Resembles the low-rigor applied-ontology pattern of the negative calibration set. Composite: 0.15*25+0.15*20+0.10*20+0.15*50+0.15*25+0.30*25 = 28.
systems Novelty 25 Rigor 20 Non-Obv 20 Completeness 50 Resilience 25 Impact 25
Show full analysis
Problem & Motivation Young organic farmers in Thailand must navigate a multi-standard certification landscape (Thai Organic, EU Organic, USDA Organic, Participatory Guarantee Systems) spanning multiple certifying agencies, market-specific documentation requirements, and regulatory conditions that differ by product type and target market.
